title: Bulgogi Sliders With Scallion Salsa Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
Delicious! I would suggest maybe letting the meat drain in a colander as there was no way the meat was ever going to crisp in all that liquid. The cucumbers are a must here- the tartness balances the overall sweetness of the marinade and sauce.

For those who harp on the calorie count: look at the ingredients, figure out how to make it work for you, tell us the tweaks. All the mayo will not get used. Skip some oil. It is so tiresome to constantly read comments about calorie heavy recipes.
Thank you and ditto!
thanks. I agree that it's easy to find low calorie recipes online so go look already.
You can also use vegenaise instead of mayonnaise. Some people even prefer it.

I cooked the brisket, with all the Bulgogi ingredients listed, in my slow cooker for 4 hours on high and it was amazing. Also, I highly recommend making the Light Brioche Buns, by Jane Sigal if you have the time!
Did you cook it whole, or sliced?
Did you keep the proportions the same as the recipe? I have such little luck with my slow cooker, and I would love to change that!
Did you slice the brisket per the recipe?

I used flank steak b/c the Wegman's I went to had gigantic briskets & I was only cooking for my husband & me. I half-froze it, sliced it very thinly against the grain, then marinated it according to recipe. Used fat-free plain Greek yogurt w/ a dollop of mayo, the soy sauce & Sriracha. Added a packaged salad mix of shredded red cabbage, green cabbage, brussels sprouts, etc. to the scallion salsa, creating a 'slaw' effect. Fast and easy recipe that was very good.

For my vegetarian household, I substituted seitan for the brisket, and used a bit of sesame oil for browning it.  Everything else as specified in the recipe... The results were fabulous, and got even better after the leftovers sat in the fridge for a few days, seeping in the pleasantly warm flavors.  Our new favorite!
Try mushrooms for vegan/vegetarians.  We find that most of what the carnivores eat can be me made for the vegan similarly with oyster or shitake mushrooms.

Yes to subbing mushrooms! I created a recipe called Sloppy Moes that uses mushrooms, a little black beans, and a little rice in place of ground beef. The trick is to slice the mushrooms thick and fry them until they are nice and crispy before mixing with the rest of the cooked ingredients. Really yummy.
Put the whole piece of brisket in the freezer for an hour or so, making it quite firm. It will make slicing it thin much easier.
Is the brisket pre-cooked?
Nope, it gets cooked in Step 3
You cook it in step 3
See Step 3. The brisket is not precooked. If it is thinly sliced, it cooks quickly. If I want to thinly slice beef of any kind, I partially freeze it first and that makes it easier.
The Asian pear is the tenderizer.  Could also use a kiwi.

Made it with brisket after all. No problem slicing thin enough when slightly frozen. But brisket isn't a great choice for this - too tough and dry when seared over high heat. Love the flavors of the marinade, salsa and mayo, and will certainly do this again using boneless sirloin or rib steak (which isn't much different in price anyway). I used Aleppo pepper flakes instead of gochugaru; OK substitute but maybe not quite enough heat.

I would recommend using a quarter cup of sprite or 7up in the marinade to tenderize the meat.  That's a Korean secret that my grandmother and mom always used - also be sure to let the meat marinade for as long as possible - at least 6 hours if not overnight.

Thanks for the tip. Hadn't considered 7up but was thinking about papaya or pineapple juice instead of apple juice to tenderize.  I did marinate the brisket for 24 hours, though - and still think using another cut would be better.
If you are overwrought by the soy use braggs amino , I quit soy years ago and it tastes just like kikkoman
Braggs Aminos is soy, how do you mean you "quit soy"?

This is a great recipe. I used top sirloin. The cut of meat isn't what makes this. You can even use ground beef, pork or turkey. It's the marinade and the salsa that makes this so good. Before you start complaining about the cut of meat, stop and think.

If you think it is all too calory laden, then you are just looking for something to complain about.
I froze tofu, then let it thaw before marinating.  For we non-meat eaters it was perfect.
Such a great trick! Makes it like a marinade sponge. We do that often as well. :)

Wednesday's we re-purpose leftovers, and don't buy new stuff. In this case, Google in hand, hacked the recipe to use up 1 lb tender smoked brisket left from weekend. Half recipe marinade became sauce to simmer chopped beef. No sake: use rice vinegar+water. No mirin: dry vermouth+tsp sugar. No Asian pear: Bartlett, + kiwi, per 126tom suggestion. No gochagaru: use chipotle flakes. Don't know if as good as original, but quite wonderful, 3 adults ate well and very little remained.
Well worth the effort!

I cheated and used TJ’s shaved beef.  This was very delicious.  After draining the beef well, it still exuded a LOT of moisture when starting to cook.  Simple fix:  drain again, and it crisps up nicely.  I did add back some of the marinade, reduced it to a nearly sticky glaze at the end.  Yum.  Scallion salsa was delicious.  Served with NYT recipe for sweet potatoes with tahini butter (that butter would make a shoe delicious), but roasted wedges instead of steaming whole.
Hello, I see the note about cucumbers being a must, but I don't see cucumbers in the recipe.
Link is in the article. See https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1013830-cucumber-kimchi It's really good and really easy.

This was delicious, though we could have halved the recipe for the spicy mayo and scallion salsa and still had plenty. That said, the scallion salsa is amazing and we can use the leftovers on just about everything: eggs, dumplings, rice, etc.

This is delicious. It's a little challenging to slice the brisket really thin. Put it in the freezer for a few minutes before slicing helps. I really don't know why this should have as much fat as the nutritional information indicates per serving.  Sure there's fat in the beef but it's only a sandwich so it's not like eating a big steak.  And you'd only put a tablespoon or so of the mayo on your sandwich. Same for the scallion salsa. And do make the kimchi!

Made these with my kiddo this weekend.  Amazing.  Started with ultra-thin slices from H Mart.  We marinated overnight, then strained meat before cooking. Glad we read that tip below, or it would have been soggy. Be sure to cook in a single layer until meat is caramelized and browned, not just cooked through.  Kept warm in 200 degree oven while I had two pans going on the stove.  We doubled the recipe for two families with 5 growing boys.  Not much left for my lunch the next day
More info on the brisket! Is it sliced raw and cooked?

And where (outside NYC) would one get said brisket?  Such things are not casually obtained at a grocery chain store in the midwest.  Would a top quality sliced roast beef deli meat taste good in this?

"And where (outside NYC) would one get said brisket? Such things are not casually obtained at a grocery chain store in the midwest."

Yosa, you have got to be kidding. You can find beef brisket at ANY grocery store chain's buther counter and/or meat department in any state in the midwest. Not difficult at all.
Brisket is a cut of beef. It is raw. The recipe states to chill it, and then slice it when cold. In step 3 you cook the meat. I don't think it's unclear from the recipe.

Omitted the sugar in both the marinade and the salsa, and still turned out delicious. I also served with basmati rice instead of on hamburger buns. This will go on regular rotation, especially that scallion salsa <3

We made this and enjoyed it but the meat was a little tough. May have not cut it thin enough or purchased the wrong cut from the butcher but,  did reuse the marinade on some chicken thighs afterwards with great results.
If, by chance, you have any meat left over it makes a great taco!

This evening I made this recipe mashed up with https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1017444-bulgogi-korean-grilled-beef.
The other marinade is a little simpler and calls for sirloin (easier to downsize than a brisket). I used ingredients from this marinade (no juice) and the method from the other recipe, grinding the fruit and vegetables in my mini prep. One comment over there suggests pineapple or kiwi rather than pear and that'll be my next experiment.

This is excellent, and as one married into a Korean family for nearly 45 years, I assure you the bulgogi is quite authentic.  Do be sure to use the Asian pear—it’s the meat tenderizer.  Although I had sake, I subbed in soju (which is Korean).   

And why not try this recipe over fries?  Bulgogi fries are delish!
This dish was amazing! Must do again for a party.

I had all the ingredients, sliced the brisket thinly across the grain, marinated it overnight, and seared it off quickly in a very hot pan.  The flavors were good, but the meat was too tough and chewy.  Maybe a skirt steak would be a better choice?

See my response to Mark above.  Actually instead of apply juice, I would substitute 1 cup 7up or Sprite - the carbonation and sugar breaks down the meat and tenderizes it.  That's a secret that all Koreans know - not sure why this recipe calls for apple juice, but it's probably a good substitute - it just won't tenderize the meat as well.

Delicious!  I followed the recipe nearly exactly (except used regular red-pepper flakes, toasted sesame oil).  Marinated the brisket 24 hours.  The key to slicing brisket very thin is, of course, a very sharp knife. I seared the brisket very quickly (10-15 seconds per side) in a thin layer of canola oil in non-stick pans.  It came out very tender.  I used brioche-style buns and didn't butter them.
Brisket is best sliced against the grain, probably also best sliced after it sits for a while or has been refrigerated (after cooking).
Delicious! Left out the sake because I did not feel like opening a bottle for just a few tablespoons. Even the picky teenager went back for seconds.

This is a great recipe for post-Passover leftover brisket. I assumed the brisket was already cooked since quick cooking such a cut yields a tough and too chewy result. The recipe specifies chilled not slightly frozen. A chilled, properly braised brisket can be sliced quite thinly. Other cuts of beef can be used from raw.

A bit too sweet for me. My wife loved it.
Am freezing cooked meat to see if I could do a lot of the work in advance. I could toast, butter, and mayo the buns, pile on the rewarmed meat and keep everything warm in a low oven, then just add the scallion salsa to serve.

I would recommend using a quarter cup of sprite or 7up in the marinade to tenderize the meat. That's a Korean secret that my grandmother and mom always used - also be sure to let the meat marinade for as long as possible - at least 6 hours if not overnight.

I cooked the brisket, with all the Bulgogi ingredients listed, in my slow cooker for 4 hours on high and it was amazing. Also, I highly recommend making the Light Brioche Buns, by Jane Sigal if you have the time!

TJ’s I cheated and used TJ

I'm going to cut the meat into 2" cubes and braise it in the Dutch oven after marinating. I will serve with cauliflower rice since my husband is bun-averse. Didn't have apple juice, sprite or 7-up so (gasp!) used coca cola.

This was very easy and had great flavor. I used slider buns to serve as appetizers and followed another reader's advice to use top round steak instead of brisket and 2 kiwis grated instead of an Asian pear. Definitely adding to my rotation.

Love this recipe, however even partially frozen could not slice the brisket thin enough. It ended up to chewy and hard to eat. Will use rib eye next time; it does not have all the connective tissue that brisket does. The salsa ingredients are insanely yummy!

I used flank steak, omitted the added sugar in the marinade, and replaced the mirin with Chinese rice wine. Marinated overnight. It was still plenty sweet, but not cloying. The meat was tender and tasted wonderful. 

Even drained in a colander, the beef still released a lot of liquid and didn't really crisp in the skillet, but honestly that was nice on toasted potato rolls.
26, count them, 26 ingredients and some of them are not readily available and require trips to a Korean or Asian market in addition to a butcher and a green grocer.  Not fun!

You can find nearly all of these ingredients in many grocery stores. Heck, I just Googled mirin (which I would suspect would be the most second most difficult to find; I've seen all of these other ingredients except gochugaru at the local grocery stores).

Turns out Walmart sells mirin and Asian pears. Walmart!

Amazon.com com sells gochugaru. Maybe try some plain red pepper flakes and see how it works if you don't want to trek to an Asian grocery or order online.

With good food, as with so many worthwhile objectives in life, the same maxim applies: if you're not willing to do what it takes, then no one can help you, and you can't complain when nothing happens. I live in a small town, away from a major city, and every ingredient in this recipe is available at both of our local markets. Of course, I've been known to drive 50 miles for a good sandwich..
Some people find exploring new markets and ingredients fun!

I made a few changes but love the results. In the marinade I left out the carrot because I couldn't believe it mattered. I didn't have any sake, so I increased the mirin. Instead of slicing the meat I marinated it whole, then grilled it. Fantastic flavor! I made the mayo and salsa as written and love them, too.

I'm assuming the marinade needs "Toasted Sesame oil."

For recipes requiring sesame oil, can you please specify whether toasted or regular? Toasted sesame oil is readily available almost everywhere. Regular sesame oil is usually only found in Asian markets, and is ferociously expensive.
Regular sesame oil is what is called for - I'm Korean-American and we always used regular sesame oil - the good news is that a very small amount goes a long way!

I have to disagree with Sue -- In Korean cooking, "sesame oil" always means toasted. We don't use regular (untoasted) sesame oil. If not clearly labeled, the most obvious way to tell them apart is by color: Toasted sesame oil is dark brown, and regular sesame oil is vegetable-oil colored.

Tough old piece of brisket isn't used in this dish. If you go into a Korean market, the bulgogi meat is razor thin sliced sirloin or rib eye. If you don't want to pay the price of pre-sliced meat, buy a nice cut of steak and place in the freezer until almost frozen. almost frozen meat slices nicely.

I don't not use ribeye, soy sauce, brown sugar, onion, garlic, sesame seed oil and black pepper. With razor thin slices, both marinating and cooking is quick.

If you have access to a Korean grocery store, such as HMart (ours is an hour away), you can also buy the bulgogi meat (they use ribeye) already prepared. Then it is already tenderized, sliced razor thin, and marinated, and it costs about $5 a pound. I just added the carrot and onion to it.
@Joyce but why bother making the recipe? If you’re just buying the marinated meat from the store?

The salsa is excellent. I made a version with ground turkey to effect an old school sloppy joe. This required skipping the marinade, adding ginger and garlic to the ground turkey, a corn starch slurry when the meat released its water and a few tablespoons of tomato paste and half the salsa.

The marinade flavor is great, the scallion salsa is great! But the cut of meat is wrong for this dish, I think...
Doesn't brisket take a long slow cook? I say this because, my meat was like chewing gum. I sliced it very thin and marinaded it for the whole time and yet it was SUPER chewy. I'm thinking it would be better to use something like a flank.

last night i did a quick cook on sliced pork shoulder, another meat that is supposed to take a long slow cook and it came out fantastic. so, it could be the same here. that said, brisket is extremely expensive where i live so i usually sub a chuck roast, but in this case since it's a quick cook i'm going to sub stir fry beef strips. :)

I wish that you didn't think we are all professional chefs. More detailed instructions could have been helpful  (I.e how long to cook the meat, more photos or preferably a video). I spent so much money purchasing all the ingredients and the result wasn't what I was hoping for. I will never cook it again

I wrote the arm roast suggestion. Sometimes some 'cuts' you'll have to cook awhile.... until they're tender. I'd suggest making ahead so you have enough time, or even a slow cooker overnight if you're concerned about tenderness.
Would boneless beef ribs work..I use them a lot for Korean BBQ
Followed the recipe, tasted amazing, but a bit too salty (marinated meat, scallion salsa and Kinchi). I will find a way to reduce the salt for the future.
Much less soy sauce would do the trick!
Quarter marinade for 1lb steak

Getting the meat thin enough is really important.  I did not, and the meat came out in every bite of the sandwich.  I might try this as a burger instead by mixing a smaller amount of marinade with ground beef.

Loved this. Wegmans didn’t have brisket or Asian pears when I did my shop. Bought sirloin instead and it came out well (froze it briefly to slice thin). I used a Bartlet pear, and it chopped rather than
pureed in the food processor. The meat was tender so I guess it worked ok? I also put the meat mixture over a colander to drain, and it seemed to help crisp the edges. As others have said, the meat gives up more liquid in cooking. When I make again, I’ll halve both sauces—a lot went to waste.
Unfortunately I didn't understand how necessary it was to chill the meat in the freezer before cutting it. I don't think Brisket is the right cut of meat for this recipe. Flavor is excellent.

My take on sesame oil, is you use untoasted if your heat is on for long periods (which will toast it), and toasted if you’re drizzling over a finished product. Cooking toasted sesame oil, especially in a wok, gives it a burnt taste (IMO). Any other opinions?

The flavors in this recipe are amazing. However, beef brisket is a poor choice for meat. I froze slightly, sliced thin and marinated for 24 hours. It was still tough. I’ll make it again but definitely not with brisket.
I made this as directed.  It was DELICIOUS but the brisket was almost inedible,  it was so tough! Ugh.  I'm going to use leftover marinade on chicken and try that.
We don’t eat beef, would a turkey breast work?

Sliced brisket isn’t easily found in my small town but the local Costco has sliced NY steak that’s wonderful to keep in the freezer. They slice it razor thin and it works for a myriad of recipes. Just an FYI for folks looking for a reliable, quality substitute for brisket. I was considering buying a small hunk of brisket and putting it on a meat slicer as an option.
Costco also has bulgogi, ready to cook, which is quite nice if you don't have time to do those steps of this recipe.

Here’s a dumb question. I read and reread the steps and it reads as if the marinade is not used, which is expected after exposure to raw meat. However, when I look at the photo, it looks like it may be on the sandwich since the salsa only has scallions and I’m seeing carrot and julienne onions on the sandwich. Am I missing a step or misreading?
Any thoughts on using flank steak v. brisket? Easier to get smaller pieces of flank, and I'm only cooking for 2.  Guessing it should be fine....

Mark,
I've made Bulgogi in the past with Flank steak. It came out great. Its easy to slice thin across the grain and it takes the marinade great.
Are you supposed to cook the meat in a dry pan? There's no mention of oil. I'm afraid the meat will stick, even at high heat.
Is the rice vinegar for the scallion salsa supposed to be seasoned or unseasoned?

Can you cook the beff ahead of time? How would you hold it? Want to bring this to Super Bowl party, but don't want to cook there.
Also the price of brisket has skyrocketed here in SF--butcher suggested substituting tri-tip. Comments?

I can't see that cooking the meat ahead of time and then warming it up later in a microwave would harm it. Maybe just keep it in a covered glass container, and the sauces in jars, in a cooler on ice til you get there. the buns don't have to be toasted, but it does keep them from getting (as) soggy from the salsa. For the cucumber kimchi I also added some shaved white onion and very thinly sliced radishes. Very tasty.

I used tri-tip, substituted ginger ale for the apple juice and srirachi sauce for the marinade. Delicious!  Also served on brioche rolls. I have a lot of beef left over and did not cook.  Can I freeze it in the marinade?   I also substituted plain red pepper flakes for the salsa sauce--didn't seem to make a difference.  I recommend tasting the mayo as you add the sriracha--it can pack a wallop!  I used regular sesame oil, but think toasted sesame oil would be better for salsa.
Is the brisket eaten raw?
See step 3
Read the recipe again. Where does it indicate raw?
Thank you Sam for sharing the music video in your introduction. Loved it!
Anyone tried grilling this?

Yes, and i'll do that going forward.  The first time around, I really smoked up the house and crusted up the skillet trying to get those "crispy edges" with all that liquid.  Skewered the leftover uncooked meat the next day and grilled for maybe 5 minutes.
Can you cook the meat in a crock pot?
Wondering - do you fry the onion and carrots up with the meat? Somehow I'm missing this. Seems wasteful to discard.
I wondered the same.  I did fry some of them up and my husband commented how much he liked it that way.

Anna brisket is highly available in the Midwest. For much of the year I live in a very small town (population 5,200) and the small grocery store in town carries it. In fact, I don't know of one that doesn't. Ask the butcher where you shop.
yo- I always like an arm roast for any pot roast. I'm sure brisket would be more fatty which Can be a wonderful thing.
This is one of the best recipes I've made in a while. I'll definitely make it again!
Could someone tell me how to really dial down the heat (hot spice) enough to make it "child-worthy?"
Just give them regular mayo. No biggie.
The salsa has a whopping 2 tablespoons of red pepper, so dialing down the mayo isn't going to reduce the heat much. I would cut the red pepper in the salsa down to 1 tablespoon or less.
Don't put the hot salsa on the child's serving? The bulgoggi itself is prett mild...actually a bit sweet.
My daughter is allergic to pears.Is the pureed pears used for flavor or a as a tenderizer? What could I substitute for the pear?
Under the traditional bulgogi recipe, a Korean reader suggests using pineapple or kiwi.

According to my Korean friend and food guru, one could use an apple or even apple sauce. It is essentially a tenderizer.
Other fruits work too, others have mentioned.

Delicious!  I was not confident of my ability to slice the brisket thinly enough, so I opted for flap meat which turned out great.  Also added cucumber matchsticks in with the scallion salsa. Otherwise followed the recipe as written.  Will definitely make this again!
Will try this as-is tonight. Brisket is available in most chain stores in the Midwest. And at Sam’s Club and Costco. It’s a staple for many midwestern cooks!
Is there another type of meat that can be used besides beef brisket?  I am not a fan of brisket, but would like to try this recipe.

What kind of cut would you like to use?  You can even use pork chops. 
Just do it.  Unless you've never cooked before in your life.  Then get a cookbook.

Denise 

I  think sirloin is a great choice, some people go all out and use ribeye. Just about any kind of steak/roast will work as long as it is thinly cut against the grain.
This sounds delicious, but any suggestions for substitutions for the Asian pear called for in the marinade? I’m allergic to pears.
Saw that Billie did a slow cooker version, so I'm just wondering if it wouldn't also work to cook it in a traditional Dutch oven, stove-top and/or oven. Anyone have any experience with that?
I made a napa cabbage slaw using the "salsa" ingredients. Balanced the meatiness and fattiness of everything so well.
I substituted the mayo sauce for chopped jalapeños and avocado.

Out of cheapness and laziness regular crushed red peppers like you put on a frozen pizza for the korean ones. Still delicious, saved a trip. I cooked mine in the crockpot on low for 7 hours. Even my very picky friend who hates onions was happy to pick around them and loved the flavor. I'm excited for my lunch leftovers!
Excellent. Had small dinner party and everyone raved. I overdid by putting Mayo both sides of the buns and would keep to one going forward but this is on my rotation for sure!

These were amazing…sooo good.  Went to the Korean grocery store where they had thin cut beef slices (sirloin) which made everything easy!  Given how thin they were they cooked in a flash so since everything was made in advance, dinner was quick on the table.  Don’t skip the cucumbers - the acidity was great for the meat.

What cucumbers? Reason I ask is I am wondering if you buy prepared beef, what else if anything  needs to be added that would otherwise have come from the marinade? Would appreciate advice from anyone.
Very tasty, but the brisket that I used was extremely tough, even when thinly cut and marinated according to the recipe.  Next time I will use a more tender cut of beef.

DO NOT USE BRISKET. IF YOU COOK THIS, USE A MORE TENDER CUT OF MEAT. Total dud for my family. Nobody wanted to eat the final product because the brisket was too tough, so the dogs were the only winners. I was actually worried about this. Normally, brisket is slow-cooked for hours to break down the meat. Flash cooking just does not break down the meat enough.

Why brisket though? 24 hrs marinated and still tough. Also: way too salty. Otherwise the flavors were excellent, made with the cucumber kimchi as suggested. Didn’t butter the buns, they get spicy Mayo on them anyway.

Assembled the marinade in the blender with the pear, everything except the carrot and half the onion (yes, I blended half the onion because I like onion flavor and wanted more body in the marinade - this also left equal amounts of the julienned carrot and onion). Reserved 1 tbsp marinade and used it in the mayo, in place of the soy sauce.

I made this with a small brisket from my meat share, and my slicing wasn't sufficiently skilled to get the paper-thin slices that are necessary to ensure tender bites. Next time, I'll buy pre-cut thin-sliced meat from the Korean grocery store. 

All that said -- even though the meat was chewy, this was absolutely to die for. I was so excited for the leftovers all week. The spicy mayo and bulgogi sauce both make more than you need. If you're not a sauce fiend like me, halve both.

The marinade is a bit of work but well worth it. The flavor of the meat is outstanding. As good as the best I had when I lived in Korea. I recommend using sirloin; I used Picanha steak (rump cap/ sirloin cap/culotte). For the spicy mayo, used Gochujang and sesame oil with the soy sauce. Scallion salsa also worthy. Served over Claire Saffitz's All-Purpose Enriched Bread (also work but well worth it). Made the Cucumber Kimchi; a great accompaniment. Will do this again for delight and to impress.
Potentially dumb question: would Beyond Meat's steak tips work in this recipe?
TJ's has a vegetarian version of Bulgogi.  I think it's delicious but I haven't used it in this recipe yet.
Another quick fix is to buy Trader Joe's bulgogi in the freezer section. They are ribs but you can cut the meat off. Already marinated.
The photo show carrot and white onion on the slider- should that part of the marinade be cooked with th beef?
Had top round and it worked perfectly- marinated right about 6 hours.  Used dark soy sauce, which made it too soy-y and salty.

This is very good.    5 stars for the idea and for the scallion salsa.     I just use my own bulgogi recipe.    Scallion salsa is excellent although I skip the hot pepper flakes and keep it cooling rather than spicy.    The bun topping I also kept mild.   The bulgogi was spicy enough for a few of my diners, thus the tone down of the heat.     Planning on trying this over homemade buns or even in an Arepa or crepe

I was unable to source an Asian pear after two stores, so I pivoted to bosc. I also substituted coconut aminos for soy because I was intent on using what I had on hand. For real though, Sam Sifton, marinade in the fridge at least six hours! We ordered a pizza. 
Fast forward to the next day now, and those “sliders” were out of this world delicious. Not to mention, I might put that spicy mayo on everything. Take the time to make this and you will have bulgogi, spicy mayo and a giant smile all over your face.
Can I substitute red pepper flakes for the Korean red pepper flakes?
These were so so good! We used hero sweet Hawaiian rolls for the sliders and a cold peanut noodle salad rounded  out a great dish
@Jacki Cucumbers?

Incredible flavors, and even lends itself to a slow cooker variation. Brisket was insanely expensive at my grocery store so I swapped it out for 2.5 lbs short ribs. I threw them in a slow cooker with most of the marinade ingredients (except for the carrot) for 8 hrs and they fell off the bone. I used the carrot in the slaw with some thinly sliced cucumbers. Topped it off with mayo and gochujang and it was delicious!

If you’re going to use brisket are we talking flat or point?  And I know the marinade tenderizes the meat but even tenderized, is it feasible to cook brisket for just a few minutes and have it be tender?  Jewish moms and Texas pitmasters might beg to differ.
Will make again, probably better than this time. I did not get the meat very tender so it was a tough chew.
My friend the former chef taught me to rub the baking potato with a light coating of bacon fat, then salt and pepper it. I use the applewood-smoked variety. So tasty.

Sam is a way better cook than me, but: If you already have the oven at 400 for an hour, why would you cover the stovetop with bacon fat? Spread the bacon over a foil-lined baking sheet and put it on a rack below the potatoes. Keep an eye on it, the bacon should be done in 15-20 minutes. The foil allows you to save the bacon fat, too. Plus, you just saved energy.
@Maura McGrath maryellin The recipe says to bake the potatoes for an hour, not preheat the oven for an hour.
These would be great with some roasted broccoli - we can pretend it's healthy by adding a veggie!
Not necessary--potato is already a vegetable!
A potato is a vegetable.

Updated pic is nice, but you might want the food stylist to talk to the recipe developer. Pic shows potato halves - recipe calls for potato quarters. Since many people do look to the pics to see if they're on the right track, it should at least match the recipe instructions.
@Maty,  seems like quarter vs 1/2s can be quite the discriminator here. The logistics of ‘load’ and handling for dipping (maybe 1/4s are easier for dipping in ranch) could sway the execution.

I will attempt the vegetarian version: Olive oil instead of bacon fat, and a little sour cream on top of the bubbling cheese.  When we were kids, Dad used to fill baked potato skins with butter, roll them up and call them "cigars."  A high point of childhood.  Those were the days.

LightLife's Smoky Tempeh Strips (formerly known as "Fakin' Bacon" is a great veg alternative. You could use smoked paprika or smoked cheese but the crispy texture of the fried tempeh is helpful here - plus it's extra protein!

@Lori L

Oh yeah!! My Mom used to cut the potatoes the short way(across) and put a small pat of butter, S&P and put back in oven for a few min and they were Awesome! I still do it occasionally 😍! Thanks for reminding me Lori!
From someone: My friend the former chef taught me to rub the baking potato with a light coating of bacon fat, then salt and pepper it.
Gruyere cheese and roasted cauliflower w/ prosciutto. Yummy.

Don’t let the potatoes sit in their skins after baking. Split them (partially or fully) and let the steam out! Small pricks with forks or a knife won’t do. The captured steam softens the lovely, crisp skins.

Suggesting a variation.
I take Yukon Gold's and cut them in half. Grease the outside with bacon fat, put them in a 350F countertop convection oven for 30 to 40 minutes depending on the size. Using a melon baller, I scrape out some of the insides, put cheese and bacon in them 
and cook for 3 more minutes. Then I take a crock of sour cream and dip the potatoes in there as I eat them. Delicious as a snack or dinner and faster than one and a half hours. Could be done in a toaster oven too.
Read the source article for atmosphere. Sam Sifton is a great writer.

Made this tonight and it was terrific!  Served with the caramelized onions and added fried eggs. The bacon fat brushed on the insides before broiling makes this special. Be sure to liberally salt and pepper.
Loved these! Just added chopped and pickled pepperoncini for a dose of vinegar and heat.
I always scrub the potato, prick with a fork all over and spray with cooking spray. Then bake it. It comes out crispy and solves the bacon fat concern.
Pretty please. Just make the bacon in the oven. Perfect temp control. No splatter. Easy cleanup.

Hits the spot. Bar food without the bar. Thank you. 
Scooped-out potato insides might become tomorrow's home fries in the extra bacon fat - just going to leave it there in the pan.
Any suggestions to make this without the bacon fat?  I'm cooking with a vegetarian in mind.
Butter
Any fat~ avocado lol, ghee, olive oil

LightLife Foods "Smoky Tempeh Strips" (formerly known as "Fakin' Bacon") is a great substitute. They need to be shallow fried in oil til crispy, and the oil can then sub for the bacon fat because the spices will  have permeated it as the tempeh cooked.

Just made this substituting russet potatoes with bacon grease rather than olive oil, sprinkling habanero seasoning salt & seasoned pepper before adding cheese, and using 2/3 freshly fried cut bacon pieces and 1/3 store bought jalapeño bacon peices. Amazing! Crispy on the outside and soft/melty on the inside with lots of flavor. Turned the potatoes every 10 minutes to equally distribute the bacon grease coating and cut in halves rather than quarters (used kitchen shears for the crispy skin).

To speed the process, I used a method similar to making twice-baked potatoes. I chose flatter potatoes from the bag, sliced them lengthwise to make 2 long pieces of each, then oiled, salted and cooked open-faced. When ready, I hollowed out a shallow amount of potato flesh and put toppings in, then broiled until crispy. Yum!
Dont do quarter wedges. Do halves. Can load more, and easier to handle. Lessens the quantity, so add accordingly.
I agree with Suzanne (comment from 3 years ago). Better to salt & pepper the potatoes before adding the bacon & cheese.
When baking potatoes, if you first put them in the microwave until they're hot (but not cooked), you can reduce the time in the oven by half or more.
I used a melon baller to scoop out the flesh which we roasted with olive oil and salt the next day in a very hot oven to fantastic results.

Everyone loves these. I bake potatoes ahead and scoop out while still warm. Later in the week, I rub skins with oil and reheat in the oven, bottom rack, for 10-15 min while I cook the bacon. Then top with cheese and finish with the broil.
When you can find them, use garlic scapes instead of (or with) scallions.

That would make them terrific. But a word of caution to people who go out in the woods and pick them, they are being depleted that way. So if you do pick them please don't pick the entire plant and leave some to propagate.
Instead of broiling, I put these in the air fryer at 390 for 6 minutes. The skin was super crispy and the cheese was perfectly melted.

These were a huge hit at my Super Bowl party for 4 last night. I made several vegetarian, leaving off the bacon and bacon fat. Our vegetarian loved them, just as well as the meat eaters. I was worried they would not be crispy enough, but the broiler did the trick, and they were perfect. For variety, I suggest using different cheeses or other toppings such as Canadian bacon, black olives, or veggies such as steamed broccoli or sautéed mushrooms. Definitely will make this again, and again.
This is my go-to dish for useing skins leftover skins after making twice baked potatoes.  However, somehow you forgot to include the diced jalapenos.

Yum. Second time for was twice as good b/c I added caramelized shallots. Added garlic powder to the salt & pepper on the skins. Yesss! Oh, my! Next time I'm going to go with smaller potatoes, though hesitating on Yukon golds b/c the skin is thin. Any feedback on that?

Wash & dry your potatoes; prick them all over with a fork or a skewer; place them in a microwave in a kind of star pattern (radiating outwards) and cook on high for 5 minutes; turn them around and cook for another 3 - 5 minutes (depending on how big they are).  Take them out and let them cool a bit (Don't cover them and let the skins steam!) then continue on your way; I've just saved you an hour or more. Perfect baked potatoes ..  You're welcome :-)
Nice recipe. You can custom size the potato skins...used the air-fryer oven.

This is not what I crave, but it is football season and my family wants these again today. They are easy enough to make and the kids eat them.  The seven year old is the only one who adds hot sauce, we are in Louisiana after all.
I made these a couple of month ago and even though they were just a bit time consuming, they are worth the effort.  They also freeze well for another time.
Made these but just halved them and left in the meat of the potato. Turned out great !
Butter also works great instead of bacon fat! You can also used clarified butter on the insides for a more nutty flavor.

Question:  Any suggestions on making these into bite sized appetizers using very small potatoes?  How long should baby potatoes (1 inch diameter) bake?
Thanks

Hey its your recipe. You do anthing you want to change it. The recipe police aren't going to bust into your kitchen. In terms of how long to cook the smaller potato's. Set a timer and check them occasionally for doneness. There's no definitive answer. Depends on the size entirely.

Easy to make, good, but not remarkably better than other recipes.  But everyone likes potato skins so they were the 1st app gone!  I had 5 potatoes I wanted to use up so used the whole 8 oz pkg of cheddar & 12 oz package of bacon.  No such thing as too much cheese or bacon.
Washington potatoes are just as good!
Tip: Use a paring knife instead of a spoon. A lot easier to cut out the potato meat then to scoop or scrape. Also you can mash it up and make twice baked skins.
Brine those potatoes - prick them with a fork and roll them in some salted water. Check out ATK for all the details
And I'm wearing my Hold Steady tshirt already!!
Using grapefruit spoon makes the scooping fast and easy.
Let us dice a jalapeño.
Took to Superbowl Party 2017. I might try finishing these in a very hot oven instead of a broiler but guests ate every, even-less-than-crisp piece.

How many actual potatoe skins do you have total once they are ready to serve? No judgements but 4 Idaho Potatoes makes four servings but since everyone eats different amounts and some people eat multiple servings to themselves. 

Let me know! Thanks!

Hi Harley - 

Recipe says quarter each potato. So 4x4=16 potato skins. Hope that helps.
Can you make the potatoes ahead of time and then cut and broil the wedges or will it lose all the crisp?
Unfortunately, it loses all the crisp.
For our Yukon golds, we cut in half lengthwise. Did not brush with bacon grease and they were still good. Added sliced green onions.

My husband made these yesterday w/o bacon for a stay at home pub afternoon. Honestly some of the best potato skins I’ve ever had! A focused approach to a simple snack can really elevate flavors and enjoyment!
These are the best potato skins I've ever eaten. Yum.
Great recipe. Went with 2 large potatoes instead of 4 to feed four people and it was plenty for an appetizer.
"Beyond expectations" -- 23 year old son

I make a quick potato salad using baked Russet potatoes (even cheat sometimes and do them in the microwave) Half the potato, cross hatch the flesh and pop that into a bowl for the salad. Then slice the leftover skins into wedges.  You can also freeze the wedges and have them ready in no time if you're suddenly faced with company and need an appetizer.  Load 'em up and pop them into a hot oven, broil last few minutes.  Serve with sour cream and fresh scallions.
Rub the potatoes w ghee instead of olive oil before baking. Buttery deliciousness

I'm not a fan of potato skins, but even I had to admit that these were good. Next time, I'll try broiling the bacon fat-painted skins before filling and broiling again. Hot sauce would also be good on these.

What do you do with the rest of the potato? 

What if you made potatoes one night and had skins left over? What is best way to preserve for the next day or later?
that's what i did.  when i make baked potatoes, i save the skins.  i freeze them and when i have enough, i defrost them.  piled with bacon and cheese and then sour cream--delish

@Paul Banas

I never make potato “skins” - I make twice baked potatoes.  Cobine all that spooned-out potato with bacon, green onions, butter, sour cream, parsley, crispy onions, shredded cheese, then spoon back into the skins and rebake until the cheese is melted.  No need for “skins.”

I baked these tonight.  So delicious!  I think that cutting the largish potatoes in half, instead of quarters, would be just fine.  I put the scallions and prosciutto on the potatoes, with the cheese on top.  (I was out of bacon).

saved up potato skins from baked potatoes (froze them to get enough for 2 people--approximately 3 each)
added bacon and cheese and put under broiler.  added sour cream

what's not to like.  yummy
I used white cheddar.  Wouldn’t do that again.  Not sure these are worth the effort.

I was never a fan of potato skins. The ones I had experienced were mainly chewy bar food. When my husband requested these, I was skeptical. But … after following this recipe, I am a convert!  These are absolutely delicious. My husband added a little salt, but I wouldn’t change a thing. And the drizzle of hot sauce really added to it. Thank you!
Can we just all agree that Sam should just cook and also eat? Let him already.

Yummy Yum I really like this one. In fact, it is my late hobbies faite he demanded they added it to the weekly rotation so that every Sunday could be the only difference is that hates bacon, and so I had to swap it out for his favorite meat of the skinned hotdog meat, a couple of little tricks that I learned when I was in culinary school is to brush the potato skin with a lead seasoning of cornstarch about 1 teaspoon per potato skin by doing this. You really get an extra crisp when you put it in the oven because the cornstarch acts like it adhesive to the potato skin and then crisps up like a fry very yummy. Highly recommend sometimes though if you’re not careful, get a glue texture which my hubby really liked but my three sons did not so be careful when you do it thank you.
Not sure about lead seasoning ... doesn't sound healthy
The problem is what do I do with all of that potato ...

For the bacon: 5 mins in the microwave, between paper towels, gives you crispy bacon,  ans you can use the paper towels to grease your potatoes before baking/broiling if you like.  

Also, you can prepare these in advance of a party up to topping with bacon and cheese—just throw them in the oven at 400 for a bit before you serve.

Try to find a bag of russets that are a bit on the smaller side. Just do halves instead of quarters, easier to cut and scoop. When it comes time to scoop, you need to do it while the potato is hot, so you have your tools ready.  Two things I wished I had, an oven glove and a proper scooper.  A heat insulated glove in order to properly hold the hot potato would've been nice.  And, a melon scoop would be the best to remove the desired amount of tater meat. I couldn't find mine, so I used a  spoon but it wasn't the best. When you scoop be sure to start shallow and leave enough substance on the skin. I dug a little deep on a few and there was nothing left to really bite into. Have your knife sharpened. Skins can be tough after initial cook. Some of my potatos practically disintegrated under pressure because my knife betrayed me and couldn't complete the cut. If you wait to cook the crispy bacon while the potatos are in their initial baking phase then you will have easily brushable bacon grease ready to spread on your scooped out potato halves. I thought I was being clever by having prepared chopped bacon pieces earlier in the day when cooking a different dish. Then I realized I didn't have the bacon grease ready to spread. Not so clever.

Another app I prepared for a Superbowl party. They were good, but felt like there was an ingredient or 2 missing, even with the bacon and scallions. This is a fairly easy and straightforward app to make, so no complaints. Will find an extra topping or 2 for the next time.
The name made me smile. I suspect these might be gloriously frivolous potato skins.

stats.Total Time: 15 minutes, plus 1 hour to heat oven

comments.commentBody: I'm always a bit confused abut the term fresh mozzarella. Is this the buffala type sold in water (sometimes also sold as fiore di latte, not buffal but less expensive) or like boccancini? Or the firmer type sold in plastic?

I use grated low-moisture mozzarella you can buy in the dairy case in the supermarket, TJ's etc. You could get fresh mozzarella from a cheese shop and grate it yourself at home. Not the buffala, or bocconcini.
Yes, the buffala-like.
Yes, it's the one sold in water.  Cut think slices and place them sparingly on the pizza.  it's goood

DO NOT shy away from making the dough recommended here (Roberta's).   Do not give in to the temptation to use store-bought dough.  I've made this pizza with both, and the difference between the two can be measured in light years.  Make it.  You can do it.  It's not hard.  And it is A-MAY-ZING.
Roberta's dough is easy and very good.  I made it with 1/2 WW flour and it still was very elastic.

Agreed! I made it with half 00 flour and half Gold Medal All Purpose. Refrigerated for three days then made my first pizza tonight. Started watching it at six minutes and we started smelling it around eight. Removed it at nine minutes and wow, it was a great first try. I'm inspired to try a number of different toppings so, I'm looking through NYT recipes and the helpful notes posted by fellow readers.
No!...don't buy the dough from the store! CFXK is correct!! I don't use Roberta's recipe, but mine is very similar. Make sure you do at least 24 hours in the fridge; it makes a big difference.

I followed this recipe to the letter, and I have to tell you, it was pretty bland.  If you really want to do pizza sauce the right way, you must add oregano -- along with chopped onions and garlic.  Otherwise, the sauce has no kick at all.
Matter of taste, but the above recipe is exactly like the pizza I ate in Italy and the pizza my grandmother made. All you need is tomatoes, olive oil and salt.

In Italy, I found that pizza's vary tremendously, however, the standard Pizza Margarita, is bland: a light tomato sauce, light mozzarella and not heavily seasoned. I'm going to make the fresh sauce myself soon, but I agree, definitely garlic, Italian herbs, salt and pepper and olive oil in the sauce, then the rest can be very creative. I eat gluten free pizza crust that I buy at the supermarket which is tasty, but will be making my dough from scratch from the GF recipes of the NYT.
I love the taste of Basil on my pizza, so I recommend adding some fresh Basil or dried.

For those of you saying the sauce is bland it really isn't for those of us who lived in Naples. They cook it just like this and it's delicious. You can add your spices and all you're doing is americanizing it. I use San Marzano tomatoes, put them through a mill to remove the seeds and just bake it on the pie. The best pizza is the one where the taste of tomatoes linger on your palate insead of confusing it with so many different tastes.

100% correct!

Sugar, oregano, garlic,  all these spices for pizza sauce AKA MARGERITA SAUCE, is a big No!

That’s a pasta sauce not pizza sauce..

A lot of shops make these in Wood Burning ovens  which of course adds flavor also ..
If you can find it which is hard...
try using with (smoked Mozz... fresh )or add slight amount of smoked mozzarella. SLIGHT.... 

Other wise this recipe is spot On!

Since home ovens don't get hot enough, precook your crust on the stone for about 8-10 minutes before adding sauce and cheese. This will eliminate the chance of a soggy or underdone crust.

Preheat for 30 minutes, you don't need an hour.

Use whole, canned San Marzano tomatoes and crush them by hand.

True "fresh" mozzarella floating in water is too bland and doesn't melt properly. Use the packaged fresh whole milk style in "balls" or "logs".

I made the dough yesterday and the pizza today. One I cooked as described the second I stretched out and let it rise 15 minutes, then baked it for 5 minutes, then topped it and baked until light golden brown. I loved both but preferred the extra puffiness and crisp chewy bottom of the second method, as suggested by Jeff on above post.

So, I have yet to perfect the technique of sliding the pie off the peeler onto the stone. This caused some off the topping to spill onto the stone which, of course, started to smoke as the pizza was cooking, setting off my fire alarm and scaring the poop out of my poor dog. Otherwise, the pizza was delicious.
If you work fast enough flour the peel then place dough on it then add toppings.  Put the dough at the edge of the floured peel. Should keep things in place as you transfer.
I have had great success using a bit of corn meal on the peel.  It acts like little ball bearings, and adds to the flavor.

Also a sheet of parchment paper put on the peel and then flatten the dough.  the paper and peel will then slide easily off the peel onto the stone.  You can trim the paper to a circle shape around the pizza befor putting it into the oven.

1. If you have a cast iron pizza stone, try heating the stone over a stove burner after spreading the dough on the (unheated) cast iron stone. Wait until you start to see cheese on top melting from the heat below to put pizza in oven

2. Some herbs, especially oregano, + fresh garlic, are essential to give the tomato sauce flavor

3. Use best canned tomatoes possible. Try either imported Italian, or fire roasted

4. I add basil leaves after cooking pizza; otherwise they shrivel

Use Cento San Marzano canned tomatoes. I found them at walmart and Publix supermarket which are just regular chain supermarkets. Use a mill to get the seeds out ,the result will be pure sauce minus the seeds. I add just a little salt no oil. I lived in Naples Italy for three years so I adopted the Neapolitan way of doing this. Sprinkle Parmesan cheese on the dough, put the sauce, mozarella, and basil as directed and sprinkle again with parmesan.
Any recomendation for the flour brands?
And Costco carries them too.  My go to tomatoes.
I wouldn't put the basil on until after the pizza cooks. I think its better when it's fresh and unburnt. Otherwise, the recipe sounds delish!

Definitely refrigerate the dough for 24 hrs to develop the taste, light texture, and bubbly crust. My grandma used to add 1 clove of slightly crushed garlic to her sauce to infuse the garlic flavor without overpowering the sauce, giving it a more savory flavor. I agree with others who commented that the sauce needs this addition of garlic and 4-5 tablespoons on each pizza.

We tried the recipe for the first time today. 
Pretty good but still needs tuning to be perfect.
After cooking it, we though the crust was to hard/tough on edges.
My question is: is this due to the flour since only used multipurpose, lack of kneading the dough, oven temperature (being too long inside it dehydrates) or dough needs more hydration?
Any suggestions? Thank you!

Knead it gently, don't over knead.
Always keep it covered while the dough is rising. I use a damp "tea towel" whether I am letting it rise in the refrigerator or letting it rest on my countertop.
When stretching it, again be gentle.

1.  Make sure dough is properly hydrated...just dry enough to keep from sticking to hands and countertop.  This is particularly important in a home oven where a maximum 550 degree temp means a longer bake time than in a high-temp commercial oven.

2.  When stretching the dough, DO NOT FIGHT IT.  I cannot stress this enough.  If it does not stretch very easily, cover it and come back to it.  You can proof in oven for a short while just before working.  A warmer dough will be easier to work.
A light drizzle of really good balsamic is a wonderful addition.

Four of us just finished eating these pizzas, made by following the instructions to the letter.  It was my first time making my own dough.  I handled the dough gently, as they said to do on the video, and it formed easily into rounds.  No fighting the dough this time!  I'm through with store-bought dough.
Pizza was very good, but the glass on my oven door shattered!  Time for a new stove, I guess.
Sorry to hear about the glass in your oven's door. Suggestions for turning the oven temperature to its highest setting (before broil), intimidates me. I don't want to crack the glass :-|

I form the dough on a cookie sheet with a little cornmeal.  Then I put  the whole thing in the freezer for about 15 min.   It slides right off the cookie sheet onto the pizza stone with no spilling.  Since the dough is cold it takes just a bit longer to cook which allows the toppings to cook thoroughly.

I have made the Roberta's dough and the Margherita pizza several times and it's very easy to make and tasty.  I would, however, recommend the following that I picked up from Ken Forkish's The Elements of Pizza book.  Add the mozzarella cheese to the pizza after its baked for about four minutes.  Otherwise, the mozzarella cheese will liquify and it won't be like a true Naples style Margherita pizza - enjoy.

Step 1: Baking steel on oven rack placed at second highest position.
Step 2: Sprinkle pizza peel with generous amount of cornmeal. 
Step 2: Place stretched dough onto peel before adding sauce. 
Step 2: Sauce = 3 T. Pomi Tomato Sauce (Passata) + splash olive oil +      sprinkle salt.
Step 3: Very slight drizzle of olive oil, so it doesn't soak through the thin uncooked dough.
Step 4: Slide onto Baking Steel. Turn on oven fan. After 4 minutes, rotate pizza.

When I tried this I was skeptical that canned tomatoes and barely any ingredients would match the Margherita pizza I’ve had at any restaurant. However, I was blown away by the rich, deep flavors of the sauce and the simplicity of basil and mozzarella. Wouldn’t change a thing except now I want to build a brick pizza oven in my backyard to get a more authentic crust. Perfect recipe and really opened my eyes to the perfection of San Marzano tomatoes.
Check out the Pizza Pronto or the Ooni 3 wood pellet stove. Both around $300. I have the pronto and LOVE it.

Cook the rolled out dough on the stone for a minute or two to firm it up, then take it out and add all the toppings. It will then slide easily off the pizza peel back into the oven. No sagging, flopping, dripping over the side, etc.

I was about to give up on this recipe forever, since the first pizzas I made were not great and the second one, made the same day I made the dough, had a tough crust.  I froze the other ball of dough after it rose, though, and made that one a week later.  And despite being hopeless at getting the pizza to be round, the crust was everything it should have been - crispy, chewy, not at all tough.  Moral of the story:  don't skip that 12-18 hours in the fridge!

You can make dough fresh and use same day if given a few hours to rise, yes longer rise more time the yeast has to produce the gasss s from sugar consumption and it increases the flavor . Biggest problem I see in Homemade dough is people do not use enough sugar therefore the yeast have nothing not enough to consume to develop the flavors

Keenan’s  comment is absolutely correct I worked in pizza shops for 15 years the recipe for authentic margarita is spot on if you’re going to make pizza at home you need to get a stone for your oven .  FOIL, parchment paper??? What...

Homemade dough YES!! Fresh mozzarella.  Yes!!! Typically it’s very wet almost like sitting in water.
Oregon , garlic no!!! This is not spaghetti sauce it’s pizza sauce!
Proper technique with pizza piel & SAN MARRANZANO TOMATOES!!!

i really like and enjoying this lovely and delicious pizza where i usually make it using mozzarella cheese as the deep and and amazing cheese to be used in preparation of a magharita pizza,,wow  i do advice for the users to follow this correct recipe ,by having this all you've pizza in your hands. now enjoy.

I am from Naples, just like my granfather. Love this recipe but never forget we usually use FIOR DI LATTE as bufala leaves too much water. Another tip is the actual pizza types. We only consider Margherita, Marinara (its margherita with no formage but some origan instead of basel) and Calzone or Pizza fritta.
Anything else is not really part of our tradition. of course nowadays Gourmet Pizzaoili are all over the world and with some great results but to judge a good pizzeria always ask for M&M.

In my experience many pizzerias in Rome (where I live) and Naples (where I work once a month) offer bufala on pizza--but the difference is that pizzeria ovens are extremely hot and the water evaporates quickly. This is not really possible at home. But you can slice bufala and spread on paper towels for an hour to absorb excess water.

I use this recipe almost weekly and while it never comes out exactly the same way twice, it never fails. I don't bother with the fuss of a heated stone or tiles or a peel, but rather use the grandma method of stretching the dough in a sheet pan or cast-iron skillet doused with olive oil. Oven at 500. Regarding the sauce, I prefer this simple, no-garlic, no-herbs approach, which is more Naples or Rome than Brooklyn or Chicago, but you should have fun with it and jazz it up anyway you like.
I add the basil before the cheese and wedge into the sauce so it doesnt char and tastes like basil. Highly recommended.
It's 110° today and preheating the oven for one hour doesn't make sense to me! I put my pizza stone on my BBQ and turned it up! It took less than 5 minutes, keep an eye on it, it goes fast!
I agree. I cook mine on charcoal BBQ sometimes. It adds a little extra smokiness to the pizza.
2-¾ oz. of mozzarella = 78 gr
Use San Marzano Tomatoes and 00 Flour to make the dough...  Also, use good mozzarella cheese, not the bag of shredded cheese.  Big difference.

I love pizza, especially Roberta’s, so I was excited to make this during lockdown. It was all user error but it was a disaster. I’m leaving this comment so others feel less alone if they also stuff it up. Couldn’t find proper 00 flour, had to use a spelt thing. Then my base ended up oblong, not circular. No pizza tile or slider thing. Used inverted baking sheet. Couldn’t transfer pizzas from one surface to the next. Spatulas didn’t help. Have now ordered pizza from our local place — and wine.
Oil the pans then sprinkle corn flour before piling on the dough.

Mr. Softon:
I have used Roberta's recipe for pizza dough many times. On occasions I have used bread flour in place of AP and really don't notice much difference in the end product. I find that your suggest -ed sauce comes as close as it can to that used in Italy . The use of onions, garlic, and other spices is best left to rague.
A few people on here mention the sauce is bland without adding a bunch of herbs. You know who adds a bunch of herbs? Pizza Hut, Dominos, etc. I guess I like my pizza bland.
No herbs just basil but you have to use true San Mariano tomatoes. They just have better flavor. H and garlic with olive pol of course.

I have used the Roberta's dough recipe for years with a stone and have enjoyed every pie.  

I use a thin round aluminum pizza dish that I lightly oil to assemble the pie and then directly on to the hot stone.  No mess or fuss going in and out of the oven and the pie slides right off to the cutting board when done.
The tips on using parchment paper to slide it off the pizza peel were really helpful!

In tomato season, I slice tomatoes & set slices on paper towel for 10-15 mins., then distribute them along with so much more fresh basil than in the photo. My go-to pizza sauce follows the tip, but I add fresh thyme, oregano & roasted garlic for richer flavor.
Preheat oven/pizza stone for 1h for 5 min cooking? What a waste of energy. Do 15-20 min

The trick to dealing with sticky pizza dough is to use a lot of flour in handling it while stretching, and then placing it on a sheet of parchment paper. Put your toppings on and then slide the pizza and parchment paper onto your peel and then slide the whole thing (paper and pizza) into your heated oven.  It works great with home ovens, but not sure how well the parchment paper would hold up in a true pizza oven with high temps. Cooked pizza slides right off of the parchment!

I second the suggestion to form and bake the pizza on a piece of parchment paper. It completely eliminates problems with the pizza sticking to a peel or inverted sheet pan as well as the oven mess and smoke if you use semolina or cornmeal to try to solve that problem. The parchment paper does not inhibit the browning and crisping of the underside of the pizza.

I am surprised to see how few people know the “trick” of putting parchment paper under the dough. It slides on and off the pizza peel very easily. Then the pie slides easily off it onto the cutting/serving board. It only chars a small bit in a hot oven and leaves no residue.

I thought your trick would be good and was looking forward to try it.  Well I tried and it didn't go well at all.  The parchment paper just slid with the pizza onto the stone and caught on fire. It was stuck to the pizza no matter how much flour I added at the bottom.  I usually put cornmeal on the bottom and then slide it on the stone with pizza peel.  So I didn't have luck with your method so I had to ditch the pizza.  No matter it was worth a try.

The key is a good parchment paper.  I use parchment paper every time I make pizza, as well as underneath my sourdough bread to create a sling.  I found out the hard way that generic grocery store parchment paper will do the opposite of what you are using the paper for.  Picking bits of paper out of my sourdough was not an exercise I ever want to repeat.  Went with more expensive parchment sheets and have been sliding sourdough and pizza in and out of the oven ever since.

This was 1st time I made Roberta’s Pizza Dough & had no issues using Reynolds parchment in a 475F oven. No sticky dough, no parchment stuck to dough, and no fire. The parchment will brown but did nothing worse. I baked the pizza dough, without toppings, on a sheet of Reynolds parchment placed on the stone x 1 minute to get it slightly firm. Removed, added toppings, & returned to the oven by sliding the pizza off the parchment onto the stone. Baked directly on stone x 10 mins @ 475F.

Important to NOT keep tomato sauce in fridge for weeks! Someone I know didn’t realize this and ended up with Botulism poisoning. 

Freeze it instead.

dash red pepper flakes (adjust to taste)

I don't know why, but this recipe says to add basil when the pizza is put in the oven.  Basil is delicate and I would recommend adding after it comes out of the oven or adding when there are only a few minutes left.  It surprises me that the recipe would suggest adding basil at the start of cooking.

This was delicious. Used the Roberta's pizza dough recipe and made it two night in advance, letting it rise in the refrigerator. Made the pizza sauce as directed in the note but found it way too watery and bland (even though I drained the tomatoes and added salt), so I added almost an entire small can of tomato sauce plus a bit more salt and blended that together. The sauce was delicious and I froze the leftovers for the next time we make pizza.

i made my first ever pizzas last night using the dough and sauce recipe here. didn’t have any basil due to quar but i was honestly really pleasantly surprised by how well they turned out. used bread flour in place of both flours on the dough and it turned out great. i added a little grated garlic, pepper, and aleppo pepper to the sauce and it was delicious. also used an inverted sheet pan in place of a pizza stone and it worked great. i’m not afraid of pizza anymore !

I use parchment paper to transport the pizza to the oven. After about 30 seconds, you can easily pull the parchment paper out from under the dough.
Definitely refrigerate the dough for at least 24 hrs. to develop the taste, light texture, and bubbly crust.

Love this quick pizza sauce.  I don't find it bland at all. Perfect! I followed the recipe pretty precisely, using the mentioned dough recipe (it has a ciabatta like taste and turned out perfectly bubbly!). However, for the sauce I used the sauce recipe of Sam Siftons Pan Pizza, which has garlic in it and turned out wonderful.

5/5 will make again!

Always use fresh mozzarella in water. In America it can be hard to find buffalo milk mozzarella but cow's milk is fine so long as it is fresh and in water. In fact, here in Italy the cow's milk version ("fior di latte") is the norm in most neighborhood pizzerias; pizzas with buffalo milk cheese are usually a separate menu item and cost a few more euros per pizza.

If lucky enough to have an outdoor grill, use it. Heat it up, put the dough on the racks naked and cook for about 3 mins, then turn over for 2 mins. Remove from heat it will be firm and not sticky at all. Top as you like and return to the heat for 5 to 9 mins depending on the grill. Best pizza ever.

A solid recipe ingredients-wise, but I find the technique lacking. Salt retards water absorption into the flour so don't add it until after the first 15 minute rest, before the second kneading.  Leaving dough in the fridge for 48 hours increases flavor tremendously.  Use 2 stones to be replicate a commercial pizza oven - one stone on the bottom rack (pizza goes on this one) and the other stone on the top rack by the broiler to help trap heat.  Heat stones at highest oven temp for at least 45 min
The “parchment paper for 5 minutes” tip is brilliant. Especially if, like me in July, you use an outdoor grill and a Lodge cast iron pizza “stone”.
Don’t bother buying a pizza stone. Use your cast iron frying pan. heat it up to 450 degrees and  dust it with flour before placing pizza dough on surface. Idea from Rachel Ray.
Pizza stones will eventually break, and stainless pans do not get hot enough, so place an uncoated cast iron skillet, upside down, on the lowest rack in the oven, preheat to 500, 7 minutes, done.

I make my pizza sauce alla Marcel Hazan. Put hand squished San Marzano tomatoes (28 oz can), one clove minced garlic, 1/2 tsp oregano, 1 tsp salt, dash of red pepper flakes and a twist of freshly ground black pepper. Add one small onion, cut in half, pole to pole and immerse in the tomatoes. Bring to a simmer and cook until the onion is soft. The onion gives up it's flavor without diminishing the textureof the sauce. Use the cooked onion sliced on your pizza or use in soups or stews.

Loved the crust from this dough. I would suggest adding two garlic cloves to the sauce it made a huge difference to the flavor. Also sprinkle a few red pepper flakes on the dough before topping with the sauce.

Who's bright idea was it to use wet/fresh/buffalo mozzarella on a pizza?  Firstl, I learned while in Naples that pizza was invented to use up old dried up mozzarella that's sat in the frig too long.  Secondly, the "wet" mozzarella leaves the pie soggy with the large slices of cheese prone to slip off the slice and onto your plate, if you're lucky or  if not on to your lap if not.  Store bought low moisture mozzarella from the deli case works far better.

It depends on what style of pizza you're going for. Neapolitan pizza is generally topped with fresh mozzarella, comes out gooey -- even soupy in the middle -- and is eaten with a knife and fork. New York style pizza uses the low-moisture mozzarella, which is certainly more common in much of this country.
Need help! I make my own dough and my sauce. I love it! But I need tips on how to move my pizzas into the oven without making a mess.

I assemble my pizza on a cornmeal dusted pizza peel. Sprinkle the cornmeal all the way to the outside edge of the peel, the edge that will head first into the oven and onto the pizza stone (or I use a lipped cookie sheet). If you gently urge the pizza off the peel and onto the preheated and hot stone, it will attach itself and then slide fairly easily off. The cornmeal acts like ball bearings and adds a bit of crispness to the crust, too.

I use parchment paper sprinkled with semolina under the dough. I build the pizza on the paper then slide it directly onto the pizza stone. I usually hold a corner of the paper while sliding it into the oven so it doesn't slip off the back side. That is no fun. Practice makes perfect... well close enough.

I recently read a recipe that suggested freezing the fresh mozzarella for about a half hour or until pretty solid. This process helped limit the release of fluid during cooking.  kept the crust crusty!! I use the firmer type sold in plastic not ones stored in water but both work. As for the sauce, dice 1 fresh fennel bulb, 1 sweet yellow onion, 2-3 tlb garlic, and sauté' them until tender. When cooled, puree them and add to 16 oz crushed tomatoes, fresh oregano, salt and pepper.

I made a quick and easy pizza sauce with a couple of tablespoons of canned tomato paste, stirred in a little water to make it thinner, and added dried oregano, onion powder, minced garlic, and ground pepper and salt.  It's good in a pinch.
Putting fresh basil at the end after cooking is a must. Should still be green. Do not put it on and then let it bake. Looks like burned basil in the photo. And don’t over cheese.

I would try it that way if you don't have a stone. The main thing about a stone (or tiles) is that they absorb and retain heat, enabling your crust to crisp up nicely. Another option would be cast iron.

No, you really need a stone or a pizza steel for retained heat. Home ovens  just don’t get hot enough under the pizza dough to cook it well. Your top will cook but not your bottom.

It's definitely a skill. Use semolina or fine cornmeal to dust the peel before you put the stretched dough on it. Then, jiggle the handle to loosen the dough - both while you're applying the sauce and cheese chunks, and again right before you open the oven door to slide it onto the stone. Use short, quick shakes with your wrist.

I agree with Eric. Corn meal makes all the difference in pizza dough.  It gives it a very nice crunch and is great for both deep dish and thin crust pizzas.  I'm originally from Chicago, the home of deep dish, and most of the popular deep dish pizzerias there use corn meal in their dough recipes.  It is also fun to experiment with using beer instead of water when making the dough.  Warm it a bit to just above room temperature to keep the yeasties happy.
If you use cornmeal in an oven at high temp. it tends to burn.  Use a little semolina instead.

Sorry to say this recipe was not as I hoped. I followed it without alteration. The dough was tough and my pizza stone smoked us out of the kitchen for the evening, before the pizza was even in the oven. I make scratch pizza often and understand how to make it great. I was craving that simple, margarita flavor. Won’t repeat this one.
What temperature to preheat pizza stone?
As high as your oven will go. For most home ovens this is 500 degrees F (although some "pro-style" ovens will get up to 550 degrees F).

I don't know how to make my dough to look like the picture (thin and crust with a bit puffy on the side). Does it have anything to do with the temperature of the oven? 350 is enough but preheated 30min or an hr or should I go higher? 
Does it have anything with the dough? what should I add to it or how thin should I make it- maybe on the side it should be thicker? does it make any different if I use pizza stone instead of pan?

Turn your oven as high as it will go, which will still not be as hot as a real pizza oven. Pizzerias in Italy have ovens hotter than 700F, plus wood burning inside. The pizza takes literally five minutes to cook. For this reason Italians rarely make pizza at home; there's a pizzeria on almost every corner, and it costs 6 euros.
A little chiffonade basil on the bottom, oh la la! Uncharred basil on top.

Put on parchment, remove paper after 30 secs then Pre bake 3 mins
Then cook 5 mins
Chiffonade the basil and sprinkle it on top after you pull your pizza from the oven.
It wouldn't be the classic Italian presentation, but of course you can do as you wish...
After reading many of the comments I decided to use parchment paper!! What a mistake. It started clean to the crust like 100 yr old wallpaper. We could not eat the pizza at all.

I put the pie in the over on parchment. After 5 minutes, I lift the pie slightly with a metal peal or large spatula to remove the parchment for the rest of the bake, which results in a perfectly crisp crust.

I've found that the "store brand" rolls of parchment sold in most supermarkets are prone to sticking as you describe. The more expensive Reynolds parchment, also widely available, never sticks in my experience. Alternatively, you could use non-stick aluminum foil.

I didn’t find the sauce to be bland — it was actually really wonderful, very bright and fresh. I put cento san marzano whole peeled tomatoes through a food mill, then added salt and olive oil to taste. I also threw in two crushed garlic cloves and let it all sit in the fridge for a few hours.

If you’re using a pizza stone like I did, definitely let the crust cook for a few minutes and then apply the toppings. Otherwise the cheese cooks too much or you have underdone crust.
Our solution to burnt/dried basil is to toss the basil with olive oil, which we prefer to adding fresh basil after cooking.

Ordinary home ovens do not heat to much more than 500 degrees, and CANNOT cook this pizza in 4-6 minutes.  A pizza steel will help, and either that or a pizza stone, is a necessity.  Put the stone 6 inches from the top element of the oven.  Heat to temp for >30 minutes.  Prior to placing the pizza on the stone or steel, turn on the broiler element and allow to heat fully.  Leave the broiler on for the first 4-6 minutes, then return to baking at 500 degees or more.  Monitor to avoid scortching.
Why doesn’t this recipe say what temperature to preheat the oven to?! Seems like a basic instruction is missing.
Always bake a New York-style pizza at as high a temperature as your oven will go -- 500 - 550 degrees.
Writing this in summer - I heat my pizza steel and cook the pizza on the grill - really keeps the house cooler. I love this pizza recipe as written!
What should I do if I do not own a pizza stone or cast iron pan, will I still be able to make the pizza?

You could bake the pizza on a cookie sheet or on an inverted half-sheet (13" x 18") pan. Or you could approximate a "Chicago style" pan pizza (a.k.a., deep-dish pizza) by forming the pie in a nine- or ten-inch round cake pan.
What temperature is the oven? It doesn't say.
We bake ours at 500 for about 7 minutes if just making Margharita!

The instructions say to set the oven temperature as high as it will go and let it heat up for at least an hour before baking the pizza. In most home ovens the maximum setting on the temperature dial (or digital display) is either 500 degrees or 550 degrees. Some specialty home pizza ovens claim to achieve temperatures of 900 degrees or more. If you have one of those, it's best to consult the included recipe guide (or app) for how to bake your pizza.
Can I make it without a pizza stone?

After several tries I got a very nice result using the dough recipe referenced here.  Cooking time was 10 minutes for me. I am using a 1/4 inch pizza steel and the surface temperature of the steel gets to about 520 degrees in my oven. Also added some chorizo sausage crumbles that I fried in advance. The crust was very crunchy and nice.

This pizza is simply delicious. I have tried several pizza dough recipes and Roberta's is the best for Neapolitan style pizza. The toppings are minimal but perfect. For a crispier crust, parbake the crust for just a few minutes.

Excellent recipe!

- If worried about sliding pizza onto the pizza stone or steel, place the stretched dough onto a pizza pan or baking sheet that has been very lightly coated with cornmeal or seminola, bake it that way for a few minutes, then slide it onto the stone or steel.
- Do not use more cheese than suggested.

I’ve been making pizza for years.  It’s always been good but never tasted like the restaurant.  That’s all changed thanks to this recipe.  Stretching the dough rather than rolling it and an extremely hot pizza stone made all the difference.  If I’d had a pizza peel instead of a flattened cereal box, then it would have been perfect.  Regardless, it was excellent.  Make this pizza.

The "tomato sauce" recipe included here is not a "sauce." It's a can of pureed tomatoes.  And the 3 Tab. amount called for is not adequate!  The tomatoes need to be cooked in some olive oil, in which you've first browned a clove of garlic and smashed a can of anchovies and cooked for a few minutes. Then add the mashed tomatoes and cook that for about 30 min. Add oregano or whichever herbs you want.

San Marzano tomatoes are bursting with flavor. Sadly I can't eat them any more because they trigger a migraine. I can eat regular tomatoes just fine. Anyway make sure your simple ingredients are the best and you won't need to "doctor" it with other ingredients.
