Has anyone ever put tofu in the freezer overnight?
The ice crystals that form when frozen -thaw- and extract the water from within the tofu. 
You can squeeze the tofu like a sponge and it will expel the majority of the water.
@Desi1999 tes, I have done this successfully. You can't quite 'wring' it out, but pressing with palm of your hand works well.
Do you put it in the freezer in its sealed carton or do you take it out? @Des
@debbi It's generally packaged in liquid so remove from container.

I read in notes for another recipe about microwaving tofu to get the water out. I just tried it! I put slices of tofu on high in the microwave for about 4 minutes and it squeezed out a lot of water so no pressing required.

This was lovely. I made a large batch of marinade and divided it between tofu and shrimp to please multiple eating styles. Served in butter lettuce cups over mango quinoa with cilantro. Served with kimchi and sriracha Mayo, and spring rolls. Chilled soju.

You really don't. Think about how each of the marinade ingredients tastes separately. Think about what you like. Ginger is pungent and powerful, so maybe not much of that, but a bit more if you really love ginger. A small spoonful of brown sugar if you don't want much sweetness; a bigger spoonful if you like sweet. Taste as you go. It will be good. You'll learn to combine flavors. Worst-case scenario: it's not exactly what you want and you do it a bit differently next time.

I make this mixed with roasted brussel sprouts (sliced in thirds), 1-inch tofu, and use the CONVECTION setting of my oven at 375F for 25 minutes to roast them together. I've never found cornstarch necessary when frying or baking tofu. Quite the opposite, as soon cornstarch-covered cooked tofu hits any sort of sauce, that "crispy" coating becomes slimy and unappealing. Just cook the tofu on high heat in a frying pan or the oven. It will brown nicely without the need for cornstarch.

Incredible.  I brushed the marinated tofu (only did 30 m marinade and next time will try longer bc WOW) with a little extra cornstarch before broiling high for 25 m to crisp it. Oh wow, this was good. Best tofu marinade ever. We didn’t measure anything, baked it at 425 and served it on lightly sautéed broccoli, bok choy and carrot.

Delicious!! I hope people can use this no-recipe recipe as an opportunity to learn and experiment with the sauce to fit their preference! It took me a while to crisp up my tofu cubes to what I wanted (probably because I didn’t squeeze out enough water before tossing in cornstarch).

I cut a 14 oz package of tofu into three slices and then 4X5 squares.  Set that on a tea towel with cutting board and can of tomatoes to drain the water for 1 hour.  Then I combined 1tbs soy sauce, 1 TBS sesame oil, 2 TBS grapeseed oil and 1 TBS gochujang and 1 TBS brown sugar in a shallow dish.  I added the tofu and marinated it for 2-3 hours.  Set oven to 425 and cooked for ~35 minutes, flipping the tofu half way through. Served on rice with kimchi, picked cucumber and blanched spinach.

This was very good, easy. The addition of fat scallion pieces was a plus. I made two batches — one with, and one without, cornstarch as per comments. Next time I would skip the cornstarch, which added nothing good to the outcome.

Made this for dinner tonight. Easy, delicious, and fun to eat. Romantic even. Licking of fingers. Smacking of lips. Sauteed a Bosc pear in the pan after the tofu. Using what I had, I followed the process with soft tofu and it worked brilliantly. then I laid it on top of a salad layered with garden fresh lettuce, cherry tomatoes, sliced cucumber, leftover fried rice with pork belly, and then the tofu on top. It was delicious! The tofu marinade plus the dipping sauce really made the recipe, whatever is done with it.

My best attempt at tofu yet! I will always bake/roast it in the future. Now the "no recipe" recipe was a struggle. I had to google cooking times and temps and as someone else wrote I added a bit of cornstarch which supposedly makes it crispier. I did just wing the amounts in the sauce but it turned out great and the leftover marinade I sautéed to thicken it and put in over rice. Overall a pretty easy and delightful meal.
I cooked for 25 minutes at 375 and it still was not "crisp". I set to 500/broil for a couple minutes and it helped. It was delicious and reminded me of eating at Korean restaurants in Japan.

Excellent recipe. I used palm sugar in place of brown sugar. I also added some diced shallots and a dash of black vinegar. I used a comparable amount of soy sauce from a recipe for beef bulgogi, which is about 1/3 cup. I used a 1/4 cup of the palm sugar. I also added Sichuan pepper corns ground. I added some soy lecithin to emulsify the oil and soy sauce mixture. It was perfect.

Excellent recipe. I used 1/3 of a cup of soy sauce which is comparable to a beef Bulgogi recipe and I used a quarter of a cup of palm sugar in place of brown sugar. I also added diced shallots and some Sichuan peppercorns ground up. I also added some soy lecithin to emulsify the soy and oil mixture so that it would absorb into the tofu better. Wonderful recipe I really liked it
More effort than it's worth.  Order shaking tofu from Lua instead.
diced pressed tofu and portobellos, follow sauce proportions given in notes, cool at convection 375 for 30ish, toss around a few times

Made this twice and froze/unfroze the tofu both times. After it’s unfrozen and pressed it’s so porous it absorbs the sauce immediately. I like to add hot chili sesame sauce and three TBS apple cider vinegar to the sauce mix. Super simple, adaptable, delicious dinner!

This is a great bulgogi marinade recipe
1/4 cup low sodium soy sauce 
1 Tab minced garlic 
1Tab minced fresh ginger
2Tab brown sugar
2 Tab sesame oil
Sautée tofu in 1 tab canola or veg. oil
Finish stir fry with 2 sliced scallions 
and 1 tsp toasted sesame seeds

I forgot 1 Tab gochujang 
See my post below
Made this and roasted the tofu with some fresh pineapple chunks and a bit of sliced red onion that I marinated all together. Served over rice with fresh chopped cilantro on top. Delicious!

This was my first venture into cooking tofu. Thanks to DG, who shared his measures for this recipe. I followed it but doubled everything -- except the gochujang and sugar, only added an extra tablespoon of each -- and it was perfect, tasty with some sauce left over for serving. I roasted it in a 400 degree oven for about 16 minutes. Definitely use a foil-lined sheet if you choose to do this. Served with steamed rice, steamed green beans and kimchi-stuffed dumplings. Delicious!
Could add some grated pear. A good way to roast tofu.
Eric added the juice of half a lemon and we added half a tablespoon of cornstarch to make it crispy and it was delicious. We cooked it at 400°

Made this tonight no-recipe style with what I had in my fridge.  Baked at 400 after tossing marinated tofu with arrowroot powder for crispiness, and served with roasted carrots and kale (rubbed and marinated with sesame oil and lemon juice) and sauteed with onions and sesame seeds.  It was a hit,
How would this be with Housin sauce?
Meera Sodha’s honey soy ginger braised tofu is similar and is incredible, if you’re looking for a bit more guidance on amounts etc.

I used the marinade link and threw everything (cept the tofu) in my Ninja, while pressing the tofu, The marinade & tofu went in a corning bowl and I waited for dinner. Oven at 400, I sprayed a pza pan, arranged the tofu on it, did the 30 minute roast, flipping at 15". The pre-baked sauce was incredibly hot but after the bake the tofu was fine. Steamed broccoli and a green salad helped the tofu temperature.
Freeze the tofu and then thaw and squeeze the moisture out.  bake on parchment paper and smear with bbq sauce.  then cut into cubes if you like.

So amazing!!! 

Marinated and then sprinkled with seasame seeds prior. Cooked at 400 in air fryer for 20 min. Cooked some in a pan with oil, coated the marinated tofu with some panko crumbs. Really good way to cook as well!!

Served with lettuce wraps, rice, avo, sautéed jalapeño and red pepper and topped with yum yum sauce

So good. Cooked in air fryer for 20 min. Tossed with oil prior and seasame seeds. 

Also cooked some on a pan and was also really good. Tossed with panko prior 

Really amazing. Served with peppers, rice and yum yum sauce
I really love this food and my children love this and I would add it to my cooking recipe and my grandparents love it and my grandchildren love it too thank you for making this recipe ^•^
I love this recipe! So easy and so delicious

use less soy sauce and replace with crushed pear and onion?

or maybe just use aaron’s bulgogi marinade

definitely flip halfway through and maybe do a couple extra minutes in the oven total

Try it without the roasting/oven treatment, cutting back on the sauce treatment.  Ditto leave out the pressing of the tofu, it's fine as-is.  Tofu does not need to be pressed or crisped or fried or roasted - or even "cooked" - to be delicious.

Tofu marinade can be dicey. I prefer to pressure squeeze under a cast iron pan, e.g., then toss with arrowroot, smoked paprika, salt, and whatever suits me. Bake until outer layer is firm to crispy. Use as croutons, sauce up, etc. I do like with mango quinoa too!!
Cut really thin tile like squares. Baked at 350 for 35 min.

Not big on tofu, but sounded promising. 
Didn’t have gochujang so sub’d 2T Hoisin, 1t Thai sweet red chili, 1t Thai red curry paste. Used 1/4c low sodium soy sauce   1T dark soy sauce, 2T turbinado sugar, 1T grape seed oil, 2T black sesame oil, 1 clove minced garlic, 1T minced ginger, 1 sliced scallion for 2h marinade.
Baked 30m at 400F in marinade without turning.
Served over Jasmine basmati, topped w/toasted sesame seeds, braised bok choy.
Ok. Might be better if tofu was crispy.
Does anyone else have trouble extracting liquid from tofu? I've followed many tips and techniques but each time I end up with crumbly bits instead of a solid slab.
Wrap a firm block in a clean flour bag towel, put a cast iron frying pan on top for an hour.  Unwrap and slice in 1/2 inch slices across the short side.  Works for me every time.
If you eat tofu frequently, invest in a tofu press. It makes extracting the water effortless.
I can only see a photo and notes, ingredients and preparation instructions are not populating.

How about an actual recipe? Air Fryer instructions would be even better. 

I often have to change online recipes to accommodate our food allergies, but without the original proportions it’s not at all useful.
Couldn't stop eating these even before they reached the oven! So quick and easy and addictive!
Buy Wildwood organic high protein super firm tofu. Doesn't need pressing, very solid, keeps its shape. Good for grilling.

Just pointing out this would work equally well with soy curls, which don’t have all the extra ingredients tofu has. They’re on Amazon. 

I make something similar to this with roasted eggplant and soy curls (chopped into short lengths. Delicious!

The ratio of ingredients from prior commenter's was great. However, even with a coating of cornstarch the tofu stuck badly to the oiled foil. The result was a bit messy but delicious. I could not get butter lettuce at my local so I layered the tofu onto crisp bed of  lettuce, added diced chives, sautéed mushrooms. Garnish w chopped mint and a little avocado. It was a great main course salad.

-Next time I'll add cornstarch for crispiness.
-I made it twice (reused marinade from first time). The dipping sauce (I didn't have ssamjang so used gochujang, sherry vinegar, sesame oil) really punched it up the second time (I didn't make it the first time)
-I added some gochugaru (Korean red pepper flakes) to the marinade in addition to gochujang.
It doesn't seem like the amounts given would cover 1 package of tofu.
Delicious however I wish NYT was more specific than 'hot oven' and for how long in said hot oven.
@pfw I baked in a 375 degree oven for about 20 mins flipping a few times along the way.

I used this as a marinade for grilled pork tenderloins. I simmered the excess in a small saucepan on the grill for a couple of minutes, then brushed it on the loins when almost finished at the end. Raves all around.

Sam and Melissa--thanks for the liberating no-recipe recipe, alllowing so many of us who know what we like on our tastebuds and know ways to tweak a recipe to explore or push our tastebuds further or just let the familiar lay comfortably among them. Moderation with seasoning is one of the rules I've used during my life. From the moderation point, it's more fun to add a bit more of a tweak to render another take, another taste. Cooking is chemistry, fun, creative; eating what is made is terrific!

How is this not a recipe? It just seems perverse and maybe a bit pretentious to deprive us of an ingredient list and numbered steps. To encourage improvisation and creativity, it could perhaps have been prefaced with a statement about your intention for the recipe to be flexible—-which, honestly, many home cooks take for granted anyway.
I like to follow recipe recipes at least the first time! :)

For more authentic and delicious flavor and more nutrition, toast the sesame seeds and then grind them with a pinch of salt: if you toast a cup at a time, you can use the blender, then freeze what you don't use. Add to almost anything by the spoonful: it's a seed, not a spice or an herb.
Followed DG's measurements. Doubled everything. Pan seared the tofu first in ghee then tossed in the sauce that I had heated in a small pot. Delicious.

I don’t measure anything when I cook. Reading a prompt like this taught me something about myself. When it’s a new cuisine or dish, I like seeing the references in recipe format. I typically research recipes from several sources (videos made in the country of origin, ATK, YouTube and of course NYT) before I create a version that sounds best to my experienced ear. Apparently it’s my EYE that’s doing the heavy lifting. Made this as “written” with crumbled extra firm tofu instead of cubed and it was delicious over rice.

Yield:
4 servings
1pound well-marbled, boneless sirloin, tenderloin or skirt steak
4large garlic cloves
1cup peeled, chopped ripe Asian or Bosc pear
¾cup finely chopped onion
1teaspoon finely chopped ginger
1scallion, chopped
2tablespoons soy sauce
1tablespoon roasted sesame oil
1tablespoon light brown sugar or honey
½teaspoon black pepper
½teaspoon sesame seeds, toasted

Having just purchased one lb. of very fresh filet of sole, I had to try this. The result may have been a work of art. However I did triple the amount of shallots, garlic, minced mushrooms, (green onions), and parsley. I doubled the amount of wine (used dry sherry) and vinegar. The use of minced mushrooms clued me in that this be something special on the order of a Beef Wellington. I cook every day. One of the best dishes I’ve made in years.
An easy way to dot bread crumb topping with butter is to grate a frozen stick of butter over the topping.
Au gratin is traditionally breadcrumbs AND cheese, and I would definitely add a little Parmesan.

Au gratin can be grated cheese, or breadcrumbs, or both.  It's properly used in this recipe.  I love the simplicity and purity of the ingredients.  It's a wonderful entree.  If anyone wants to add some grated cheese, nothing wrong with that...go ahead, but most French cooks would not.

Just tried with boneless skinless chicken breasts. Bread crumbs only as in recipe. Totally delicious. 
Thanks Sam for rediscovering.
Delicious, elegant and relatively easy. I doubled the sauce because I've learned that's pretty much a given with NY Times recipes. Always double the sauce.
(Almost) always double the sauce, yes, and certainly with this dish.

I don't think fish should be topped with Parmesan or any cheese. Bread crumbs topped with butter flakes and a sprinkle of salt and pepper make it au gratin. Sprinkle with chopped parsley when serving.

@Monika many cuisines include cheese with seafood , including Peruvian https://www.chefyerika.com/en/post/shells-parmesan-peruvian-style-seafood-flavor#:~:text=Conchitas%20a%20la%20parmesana%20%2D%20Shells,is%20fine%2C%20less%20is%20more. This is not an Italian dish. Italian “rules” don’t rule.

Quick, easy and delectable.
As noted by others, adding cheese is definitely doable and even yumsome but takes the dish in a different direction.
Capers in my opinion are a necessity, and I would drop the vinegar next go-around.
A+

Great idea on the capers, will try that next time, and if you have the fluke filet's do the Nearly Naked  Fluke Recipe as the appetizer, with a summer salad, and the Crepes for desert.... high life living :)
Sometimes cheese, not always. Do what you like.
Seems like substituting thin fillets of chicken breast would be a delicious dish. Cooking times might be similar too if monitored with a thermometer. Anyone try this yet?
Gratin not always includes cheese. Personally am not a fan of fish and cheese

Next time I make this satisfying dish, I’ll reduce the sauce more thoroughly. If there’s a bit too much liquid in the dish, the crumbs (I used panko) won’t brown properly. I prepared the recipe with haddock, beautifully complemented by a very dry Alsatian Reisling.

Just made this for our Father's Day dinner and it was a hit. The person at my fish store said halibut would be the closest match to fluke and it worked out well. I will make even more of the "sauce" next time--there was barely enough to cover 3 fillets. Also, the crumbs didn't become very "golden", so will try baking higher up in the oven next time.
Mixing a little oil or melted butter with the breadcrumbs will result in the "golden" color after cooking.
All I would change are the mushrooms: cremini or oyster if you get get them would really crown the fish.

As suggested by others, I cut the vinegar and substituted cremini mushrooms for white. What really elevated the dish was the addition of some reconstituted dried wild mushrooms to the initial saute. The recipe is good as written but terrific with cremini and dried mushrooms.

Dumb question that I've always wanted to ask:  is there any reason to dot cold butter over the top of a dish that is going into an oven, where the butter will quickly melt, rather than melting the butter first and drizzling it over the top?  The latter is much easier, so I have usually done it that way, but I have wondered whether I'm missing out on some effect that cold butter bits would give to the finished dish.
The mushroom mixture is so good, I would double the amount next time! Excellent and easy!
Add a little olive oil to the bread crumbs to avoid burning.

To Peejay:  Your comment reminded me of a just-about-perfect Italian restaurant across from the Elmwood Theater in Berkeley.  If you asked the waiter for a sprinkling of cheese on your puttanesca or scampi the answer was a firm "no!"  When a friend asked if they might divulge their dipping olive oil recipe (you had to check the impulse to guzzle); also "no!"  And then the waiter plunked in front of her a full quart-size container of their imported o.o. with all the tidbits.
A lovely dish, proving that fresh ingredients and a light, expert touch is all that is needed.

Delicious and simple to make.

If you have any dried mushrooms ( shitake worked well )  grate a bit (1/4 teaspoon) with a very fine holed grater and sprinkle over the top before baking. Adds a lovely flavor.

Story about dumb substitutions: Hungry, desperate. Wanted to make this. Went to Walmart next door; no fresh fish! Chicken? Why not. Liquor store closed now; got $8 bottle of non-alc white wine. Came home. Forgot fresh herbs!! Bet Walmart didn't have them anyway. What's green in my fridge? Old spinach, nah. Cupboard? Old jar of summer savoury... smells chivey, why not. Seven kinds of vinegar on hand, no problem. Assembled, cooked, ate... Oddly all right. Can't wait to try with right ingredients.
Delicious. I omitted the vinegar and next time I would add some capers. 4 stars

Just made this last night.  My mushrooms did not cook down as much in the photo, and I ended up turning on my convection to try to get more browning.  But it was delicious anyway.  Rich, but light and elegant.
My husband and I loved this--made with flounder.  I went heavy on the garlic, shallot, herbs and wine.     Will definitely make again.

Any thoughts on type of breadcrumbs - old fashioned blue cardboard container breadcrumbs or panko. I blitz my panko in the food processor to make them finer, I think they adhere better and taste better too.
@Kate M I just used leftover garlic bread that I toasted a bit and then whirred in the blender. I’ve also made this recipe using breadcrumbs from stale sourdough bread and they were terrific.
Made the full recipe as written (no cheese) with 1 lb of flounder for two people and we ate it all, including a green salad and the Times' recipe for smashed and fried potatoes. Excellent.

This recipe is quite quick and easy AND absolutely delicious. The only thing I do differently is melt the butter in a small bowl in the microwave for about 15 seconds and then I stir in the breadcrumbs. I find this approach is easier and yields more uniform browning of the breadcrumbs than trying to evenly distribute little bits of cold butter. Also note I use the panko iteration of breadcrumbs for perfect crunch when they turn golden brown as the fish perfectly cooked.
Used 1.5 lb.  Petrale Sole in 9 by 13 pan. Should have used larger pan -- Petrale fillets are thin. . Would increase entire sauce/ bradcrumb recipe a bit next time too.
This recipe is perfect for snapper. The amount is better for two servings. I would double for four servings.

SPECTACULAR!!
We have many friends/ family who don’t eat meat, so I am always looking for fish recipes that I can prepare ahead. This can be assembled ahead of time, up to adding the fish and popping into the oven. 
Very elegant and so good.
Has anyone tried this using olive oil instead of butter?

I Made this tonight with 6 0z chicken breasts pounded lightly. 
It was so delicious!! Every taste was perfect. Thank you and will try with fluke/flounder next.  Wow is all I can say.
Used grey sole and cooked as written. No cheese. Best recipe for a fish like this I've ever made. Thanks, Sam.
Roasted as written and two days later guests are still talking about it.  EASY and impressive.  Use quality White Wine vinegar - adds the perfect acidic touch.
Even better with cod

Made this last night with trout filets. Added rehydrated dried shiitakes to both the minced and sliced mushrooms. Cooked on a sheet pan with haricots verts tossed in olive oil surrounding the fish to make it a sheet pan dinner. Otherwise followed the recipe. Delicious!

Fabulous! Mine didn’t look anything like the picture, the breadcrumbs overwhelmed it visually but it was still a winner. I used the fish i had on hand: some frozen white fish of uncertain species, and salmon. The salmon won by a nautical mile.

Made this last night with halibut, which is in season here in the Pacific Northwest, and crimini mushrooms. Apart from those two substitutions, I followed the recipe exactly and it was delicious.  Next time, I'll make it even more delicious by adding considerably more garlic and I think I'll also saute the sliced mushrooms in a little butter before layering them on top of the fish.
Cheese is great on burgers, in omelets, with macaroni, etc., but with things from the sea, tread carefully.
There is no cheese in this recipe
Delicious - I followed the recipe exactly.  Very flavorful without overwhelming the delicate taste of the fish.  I lover Parmesan but I think it would be too much here.

I made this tonight. Followed the recipe exactly except I omitted the vinegar, used a bit more wine, and subbed half of he breadcrumbs for Parmesan. The parm was a great add, added some saltiness to the dish. Turned out wonderfully. I also love fluke and will be cooking this recipe regularly. 

Question - where is the best place to buy fresh fluke in NYC? I used to got Blue Moon at the Tribeca Greenmarket, but they retired. I now use Citarella but wondering if there is something better
Added tarragon, cream, and parmesan to the mushroom sauce. added some macadamia nut/lemon myrtle oil to the breadcrumbs. Made this with halibut and it was amazing. Disappeared fast.

DELICIOUS.  I had closer to 2 lbs of fish, so I doubled the recipe and baked everything on a half cookie sheet, which worked out fine.  The bread crumbs did get soggy from sitting on the sauce, but the flavors were so good that I didn’t care.  Will absolutely be cooking this wonderful recipe again.

This was so delicious! Cooked the recipe as written, which I usually do the first time I try something new. But I don’t think I’d change a thing! Loved the article in the NYT magazine—thanks Sam—especially since we live in Lynbrook, NY!

I made this had all the ingredients and thought it was delicious. It could have a little parmesan but not necessary. Had a little more prep time but was worth it for a quick fish meal. Lightly sprinkled the bread crumbs so that they were not overpowering.
This was a beautiful dish! Saw it in the NYT Magazine and knew I had to make it. Served it with mashed potatoes and it paired well with a Pinot Grigio.

I cooked this per the recipe (except a half batch).  It was wonderful, tasty and paired nicely with steamed vegetables from the farmers' market.  First time I've cooked flounder and will surely do it again.
Made it this week. We loved it! FYI……I pass this when I take my dog to the groomers! https://images.app.goo.gl/hwW8ZoX6E31cqwAc8

I tried this with cod and it was disappointingly bland. I doubled the herbs and more than doubled the garlic. I ended up adding table salt after serving to amp the flavor some more. This one was a rare miss for my family.

Very nice dish, easy, delicious and very presentable.  I used sole filets. Be generous with shallots and mushrooms.  Coarse breadcrumbs instead of fine.  450 degrees, closer to 15 minutes.  Fresh lemon to finish.

Double everything in this recipe, except the pound of white fish. (I like sole.) Then, it's a perfect gratin, having the correct proportions of sauce and topping to the fish.  
And if you prep the ingredients earlier, making your "sauce" in advance, then this can be an "in a minute" dish.  
I appreciate that it's not a traditional rich white sauce - the butter and wine, plus the herbs, hit all the right notes. Serve with a Bibb lettuce salad and a baguette to mop up any sauce left on the plate.

Absolutely delicious!!
I made it for two with the full amount of mushrooms, shallots etc. 
I sautéed the sliced mushrooms in butter, and reserved, before making the shallot/ mushroom, wine mixture. 
Outstanding!!!

Delicious and easy! Tweaked just a few things:  I added a can of mushrooms instead of fresh, worked out fine.  Used yellow onion instead of shallot, added dried chives instead of fresh - and the rest I did as directed.  Accompaniments included: roasted potatoes and peas.
Is Panko a suitable replacement here?
Will rose work? Don’t have any white wine at hand.
try w/ chicken cutlets

Had a wonderful dinner tonight, with fresh fluke filet's from the Belford NJ Seafocd Coop.     Made the Nearly Naked Fluke for the Appetizer, and this Fluke Au Gratin (no cheese) for the main, with a summer salad, and the Crepes Suzette for Desert.... ALL Fantastic :) thank you so much for your wonderful recipe....  I think the paring of the Nearly Naked Fluke as an appetizer, and then this as the main, with the crepes was a 5 Star dining experience at home.   We are blessed with coop here
I made started out with half the sauce because I made the recipe for 2 persons. But had to double it. Loved the recipe though, choose halibut.

An elegant dinner and perfect without cheese. I've made many of Julia Child's gratin recipes, and they're traditionally made with a roux, using butter, flour, whole milk and cheese. This dish is actually much lighter, and I preferred it.
Made this with cod and vermouth and can see how you can morph it into many proteins. It’s delicious but would like to try a reduced butter version.
Don't see why this couldn't be made with drained tofu?
This was so good I ate the leftover fish today for breakfast.  Adding this one to my rotation.  Thanks Sam.
Made this tonight, as written, with cod..absolutely delicious..Thanks, Sam, for sharing..a new simple fish preparation is always welcome by me..
I loved this but Moe did not like the wine flavor. Next time use less.
Made with Pacific cod and oyster mushrooms. Delicious, quick and easy.

This is one of the best recipes I've gotten from the Times.  Easy, elegant, super delicious.  Button mushrooms, which usually are boring, are what make this dish special.  Suggest you try it first without cheese, and then decide whether or not to add it (me: nope).
I found the sliced mushrooms and volume of breadcrumbs overpowered the delicate fish.  I will tone down these two ingredients next time.

Good, fresh ingredients make this shine. Subbed sherry vinegar and a bit of red onion because that’s what I had on hand. Yes this is reminiscent of 1990’s cuisine and I’m fine with it - what’s good stays good.

My oven is a little wimpy but I had trouble getting the fish to cook in the amount of time given. Something about layering everything in another cold pan seems superfluous. That said, it was absolutely delicious and I will repeat it but will use a “country style” pan to oven technique with my biggest sauté pan.

Not sure why he has you mince/cook some of the mushrooms, and slice the rest and put on raw right before going in the oven. The sliced mushrooms on top didn’t cook enough. Also the bread crumbs didn’t turn golden. Also there wasn’t enough buttery liquid at the end. Other than that, you can’t go wrong with these garlic   lemon   wine   oil flavors. And quick cooking time. Would definitely double the ingredients for the sauce so there’s enough.
What non-starch vegetables would pair with this?
Classic and lovely; made with cod. For the doubters: DO use the vinegar, it adds a different dimension.
1 lb fluke, dry vermouth instead of wine, baby bella mushrooms, about 2 T fine dry breadcrumbs. Otherwise followed recipe. Served with brown basmati rice and green beans. Excellent.
Some comments are helpful, even if I may not incorporate them. But can't folks just lose the snarkiness? Really...all this about a little grated cheese?

After many iterations, with tweaks along the way, I’ve arrived at my keeper version.

Mix a few reconstituted wild mushrooms with the diced mushrooms. Save the liquid for grains to accompany the dish. Double the sauce and skip the fussy second step; make it all at once, with no vinegar and a smidge of lemon. As the recipe is written, you won’t have enough sauce for a pan big enough to hold your fish. Use a bit of your mushroom liquid. The crumbs add nothing. Haddock works well, cobia even better
This needed more liquid.
Used 2 portions of cod with a full recipe of sauce and mushrooms.  I had baby leeks so substituted those for the shallots.  Elegant and tasty!
I found it best to sauté the onions and mushrooms separately and add them on top after baking, it kept their char and firmness. I go light on crumbs because the flavors really shine on their own!

I was a bit dubious as I put it in the oven (would the sliced mushrooms cook, would it be dry), but the end result was delicious.  I used more mushrooms than called for, because I just eyeballed the amount and because we love them.  Served it with white rice, which soaked up the sauce wonderfully.

Not sure where I went wrong, but my sliced mushrooms did not cook properly, and the whole dish became a little dry. Had to add a little extra wine to the baking dish. Next time I will make more of the sautéed mushroom mix, and sauté the sliced mushrooms in advance, too
This is a great recipe. I read the comments and doubled the sauce and glad I did.
What do you think about adding some parmesan cheese
It was great as written, but I liked the earlier idea of adding some capers, will do that next time.   I'd also suggest the Nearly Naked Fluke as the appetizer.... was outstanding
Is there anything you can substitute the white wine for?
Fish or chicken stock. Dry sherry.
You can use vermouth.
Is this possible to make this dish on a gas grill in a glass or metal casserole dish? Our oven doesn’t work.
My fishmonger didn’t have fluke but suggested turbot instead. Sure 5x the price.
With bl breast, flour brown 1 side, sauce, broil panko. Used orange pepper instead of mushrooms. Chx 1/2 inch . Sauce improved on usual reduction.
Excellent: made with cod fillet.

If you are of a certain age, this is a fish presentation you would see at a nice restaturant in the mid-late 20th century.  Used 1 lb of striped bass fillets; with skin on the bottom, I used all the sauce on the top.  If you had 1.5 lbs of skinless fillets you would need to double the sauce. I swapped out olive oil for most of the butter, and used panko.  My thicker fillets took about 15-17 minutes.  Not too exciting, but very pleasant tasting.  May make it again.
Made it as written with half the fish and the full amount of all other ingredients. I don't care for fish. My husband said it's drier than he would like it to be.
My husband just brought home some fluke that he caught today. Did not have mushrooms, but it was still very good.  Will make  again.
Double sauce. Use sherry instead of wine.
Would it be ok to line the pan with buttered, folded corners parchment paper or foil to make the clean-up easier?

Wow! Quick, easy, super tasty — what else could you ask for in a week nite dinner? Used cod. The full ‘sauce” recipe worked well for about 1 lb of fish for 2. Upped the garlic a bit. Will definitely make this again. And next I’ll try mixing parm cheese w/ the breadcrumbs to see if it makes a real taste difference.

why do commenters mention cheese?
I don't see it in the ingredients, or the recipe steps. What am I missing?
People are mentioning cheese becasue they see "au gratin" and think "cheese." But "au gratin" doesn't have to involve cheese; it can just involve breadcrumbs, as here.
Too much prep work. This just white fish fillets poached in butter and wine with a few herbs and buttered bread crumbs. Capers would be a good and easy addition.
What exactly is a small shallot minced?  A tablespoon, 2 tablespoons.?  I wish recipes would give amounts other than small, medium or large.
Made w flounder, v good
If you wanted to make this low carb, could you skip the breadcrumbs and just use a grated dry cheese, or would it be better to use a low carb bread crumb substitute like ground nuts?

I wouldn't use cheese myself, but you can just skip the breadcrumbs without substitution. They're not doing much but adding a bit of crunch, and there's a lot else going on. If you really want that crunch, you could toast some slivered almonds and scatter those over the dish after cooking.

This was absolutely delicious! I didn’t have mushrooms or wine vinegar but I used Marsala wine and bit of apple cider vinegar and it was perfect. Definitely saving this recipe for future meals . Thanks for sharing .
5 stars! FYI go extra on the minced mushrooms...
Second time cooking this. Exceptional. A good bit of prep, but all easy. And pleasing, if you like prepping. Can easily see adapting this to other proteins.

Made this with a large piece of cod and it was delicious (and cooked a bit longer). Used extra garlic since didn’t have shallot or chives on hand, and doubled the lemon juice instead of vinegar. Was great with additional sliced mushrooms spread all around. Thanks to all that butter, no need for cheese in this recipe.
More sauce and mushrooms won’t hurt

I did this entirely in one cast-iron pan and one bowl. Made the sauce in double quantity as recommended by others here, put half of it in the bowl, spread out the rest in the pan, put the fish on top. Added the wine to what was in the bowl and proceeded with the recipe from there.
Is this right? You don't cook or saute the sliced mushrooms at all? You put them in raw?

For a uniform crispy bread crumb topping, don't dot the dish with butter. Instead, melt the butter and toss with bread crumbs before adding to the dish. They should be just barely moistened. One tablespoon might even be enough butter for the 1/3 C of bread crumbs. I'd use panko and salted butter, or add a pinch of salt if all you have is unsalted butter. (Don't use evoo for this. Remember, you're cooking at 425F.)

I used flounder for this recipe since I couldn't find fluke and it was half the price of halibut.  Since my boyfriend and I do not drink alcohol, I always use de-alcoholized wine.  I followed one writer's suggestion and used oyster mushrooms instead of the button mushrooms listed in the ingredients.  It was great!

I made this with tilapia and it was terrific. I used the comment here about freezing the butter and then grating it, with worked very well. I also added parmesan to the bread crumbs, which now seems to have been essential.

This is an almost but not quite for me. I knew a sauce like this had to be measured precisely so that flavors balanced, I also made twice the sauce because so many people recommended that and I have to say I think that was the problem. It overpowered the fish which was a lovely $25 worth of halibut.
Made it with fresh tarragon instead of the chives, parsley, and white vinegar. Delicious!

This recipe is one of my stand-bys and I’ve served it to happy guests. In years of loyal use, I’ve streamlined it. I leave out the vinegar and skip the extra tablespoons of wine after the simmer. The mushrooms on top add nothing, and I often don’t bother with the crumbs. With a meaty fish like amberjack, I like to stir a bit of green olive paste into the sauce. It works beautifully but would overwhelm a milder fish.

Definitely increase the amounts for the sauce; I doubled it after reading the comments. I added a lot more mushrooms on top too.  The breadcrumbs didn't brown so I put the dish under the broiler at the end for a few minutes which helped.  Delicious and easy, overall.

I’m not a fan of mushrooms but this was delicious. I used a mix of mushrooms (enoki, button, baby bella). I used fresh Mahi and substituted Panko for the breadcrumbs because that’s what I had. It’s not only easy and pretty fool proof but elegant enough to serve to guests.
I would make more sauce next time. That is what makes the dish so tasty. I served it with rice and needed more sauce for that.
I didn't use parsley or chives.  I added 1 TBS water to wine mushroom mixture and used extra lemon juice. I put under the broiler to brown for one minute.  Very good

More mushroom mixture. Remove mushroom mixture from skillet. Add sliced mushrooms and thinly sliced garlic. Sauté till golden and add remaining wine. Use dill instead of parsley. Combine this recipe with the potatoes from cod and potatoes recipe.

Delish, this one! We used panko on our flounder, but with the mushrooms and shallots, they were good—if not totally toasted because of the mushroom water. But 303 calories per serving means we will be making this again. Stay tuned, but do make it! It’s yummy!

I removed minced mushroom mixture after adding wine and vinegar. Added sliced mushrooms to same skillet and let mushrooms release their liquid. Added a little olive oil and sliced  garlic and sautéed till mushrooms glistened. Then added 2 tbl wine and sautéed till absorbed.

This is delicious! I used halibut because it’s easier to find out here on the Upper Left Coast. Subbed olive oil for butter because we have to limit dairy and used garlic bread crumbs from leftover garlic bread. And I second doubling the sauce. This is now in my kitchen journal because I don’t want to forget about it.
I love this recipe. We live in fluke country and this celebrates this local fish. Five stars.
Really good- made full amount of stuff but way less breadcrumbs on top, and two pieces of fish that were 5-7oz each. Served over wild rice..
I would modify the recipe to say “light cover of breadcrumbs”. 1/3 cup is way too much. Using 1/3 cup credited in a thick,gooey layer.

This recipe worked well with haddock . Thyme works well with mushrooms . As I did not have any white wine at hand so replace it with dry vermouth and only use lemon juice not vinegar. Very nice white fish recipe.
Delicious! I doubled the mushroom and liquid portion and it turned out perfectly.
Loved it to pieces! Next time will triple ingredients. More topping.
This is a great recipe. I made it with sole and I made it with chicken. I must say that I enjoyed it more with sole. It’s best to double or triple the sauce. Excellent!
Can you do this in an InstantPot? I use mine for other short rib dishes.
What do you folks think about doing this with a chuck roast, or maybe pork ribs? Beef short ribs are both hard to find and very expensive in our area.
Why not!

Perhaps try beef back ribs (that's the cut that's leftover when the butcher cuts ribeyes).  They are usually around $4/pound at my Boston-area Whole Foods, so likely even cheaper elsewhere.  Ask your butcher if you don't see them in the meat case.

The picture shows five short ribs. Since the recipe says to buy five pounds, are those ribs a pound each? If so, should I  assume, then, that the ribs I buy will shrink to that size? (They usually come at least twice that long.) Or should I have the butcher cut the ribs to, say, two-inch sizes?

Don't use the photo for accurate representation of the recipe. Food stylists take liberties to make the picture inspirational more than exactly accurate to the recipe amounts.
P.S. I used to work for a well known food magazine and cookbook publisher. ;-)

We cooked this over the weekend. It made the whole house smell fabulous! The bad news: the meat and sauce ended up being way too salty for my family's tastes. I would make this again, but I'd definitely reduce the amount of soy sauce and increase the chicken stock (while keeping the amount of liquid at 6 cups/48 ounces). I'd also taste the braising liquid before adding more soy sauce when making the sauce for serving the ribs.
Can this be done in an instant pot?
Great idea.  I think I’ll try them in the IP.   Reduce the amount of liquid, I would think.

Half cup brown sugar 
Half vermouth half mirin rice wine

I made this in my instant pot - browned the meat in there and then proceeded through step 5. I used high pressure for 50 minutes and then let it vent on it's own. This recipe is a commitment - lots of steps and ingredients. I used white pepper instead of the sichuan pepper and red chili flakes instead of the dried chinese chilis. It came out lip numbingly hot but oh so good. The sauce is delicious. Thanks for expanding my food horizons!!
I made this with a beef neck roast and then pulled the meat to make lettuce wraps. Amazing. I needed some acid though. Something pickled.

It's a braise, so I'd imagine that like any braising you'd do in an InstantPot, this would work. (I don't own one and can't speak from experience, just a supposition)  As for other proteins to use instead of ribs, anything that's good for braising should work (or at least be worth a try). Pork shoulder treated this way and ending up with red cooked pulled pork sounds pretty good to me.
You can use baby back pork ribs, chicken thighs nine in; cut sugar by half ; sake instead of shaoxing and leeks instead of wire onions

We refer to this dish as “the best thing we’ve ever made.” For us, it requires a special trip to the Asian market but it’s well worth it. We have made it with short ribs and modified to make it with a chuck roast as well. We like it over noodles with steamed bok choy and rice vinegar over cucumbers.
@Rebecca—yum! What size roast, how long did you cook it? That sounds better than short ribs to me!

I used an instant pot to do this recipe.  More or less unchanged except for running it in the pot under pressure for 1 1/2 hours (release after 10 minutes when time is up) rather than simmering on the stove top for 3 hours.  Used boneless short ribs.    The beef was fork-pullable tender.    A hit with our family.
this looks really good. Can it be made with Stevia or another sugar substitute? Thats a bunch of sugar in the recipe.
My guess would be that cutting the sugar wouldn't be problematic.  I'm not sure Stevia would give the same flavor profile.
I used the Truvia brown sugar blend…no flavor issues, loved the result
Would you say more about the " a ranch dressing with tom kha paste; " you mention?

I just made this and it was absolutely delicious!!!  I put half the sugar in but everything else I did as directed - stupendous. I love it and highly recommend it. It’s a splurge with the short ribs but
I’m sure it would work with other meats although the pan Juices might be less flavorful
?

I made this recipe in my Instant Pot and here is a pro tip: brown all the short ribs (in batches) at once, and freeze some for later. Only have to clean the kitchen once! Use your stainless steel insert for IP (not non-stick) and brown ribs, which will take at least an hour for all sides, all pieces. I had 6 ribs, took 70 minutes. Then proceed with recipe, drawing off most of the fat as directed. I used full amount of sauce for about 2 pounds of ribs. Delish!

Sublime!Used half the weight of ribs (just cooking for 2) but made the whole quantity of sauce, I figured it would be good leftovers. Didn’t have molasses or doubanjiang (mixed a combo of gochugang, oyster sauce, chile crisp as a sub) but with so many ingredients it was v complex. When making the final sauce I omitted the extra soy and rice wine- I had enough liquid as it was. Delicious. Will make again, maybe adding more chiles   Szechwan peppers. Served w/cuke salad, rice, green beans

Sounds delicious. I look forward to trying it.

I think this is a short rib variation of a Human/Sichuan Chinese dish originally made with pork belly, called hong xiao rou. It was reputed to be Chairman Mao's favorite dish.

This dish is ridiculously delicious!  Super high effort but super high reward.  Unless your grocer has one full aisle dedicated to Asian ingredients, you’ll need to make a trip to an Asian market to procure all the ingredients.  The reward is so complex.  Will make again with Chuck roast and shred the meat before coating with the sauce.  Any suggestions for how to use leftovers?

Here's what I made using the leftover short rib, rice, braising liquid, sauce, and onions (from braising liquid): 

1) Short Rib Tacos: short rib re-heated with water + onion and peppers, served with tortillas and vinegary cabbage salad + guac and salsa
2) Short Rib Fried Rice: last of the leftover short ribs, onions from the braising liq. and a bunch of veggies + rice
3) Braised Chicken: Braised chicken in the leftover braising liquid, used the sauce to glaze and char on broil
In step 6, after you bring the sauce to a boil, it just says to cook for about 15 minutes. Do you cook it at a boil, a fast simmer, or a slow simmer?
I started it on medium-high, then reduced the heat. The cornstarch slurry started thickening the sauce almost immediately.
My favorite Chinese restaurant offers this dish on their "Chinese menu" as Tong Cu ribs, made with pork ribs. We call it "meat candy", and it is very sweet, but delicious.
What is the difference between "Tang Cu" and "Tong Cu"?
This looks amazing - but there's a lot of onion and garlic (to which I am allergic) - does anyone have any idea of substitutions that might work?
Shallots might work instead of onions. Perhaps garlic powder - but I am surely not an expert.

If you’re allergic to onions and garlic, you’re probably also allergic to garlic powder and shallots. Why not just leave them out? I’m sure this would be delicious without them - it’s got plenty of other flavors. Nothing but other alliums will have a similar flavor and any of those could be risky if you have an allergy.

Literal years too late, but hing (asafoetida) bloomed in oil brings some of the savory flavor of onion/garlic without being a problem for allium allergies. Maybe throw in some celery to fill out the sauce instead of the two onions used there.
What can you use instead of rice wine? Love the sound of this, just need to lose the alcohol.
Once the wine or any alcohol is heated, there is no alcohol in the food.  I cook with wine all the time and serve flavorful food to my grandchildren.  It isn't about the alcohol but the flavor.

i haven't made this recipe, but i don't cook with alcohol. i always sub unseasoned rice vinegar for rice wine - although in this quantity i wouldn't do 1:1 i don't think, probably half, and if i needed more liquid i'd use stock. (to C's comment - alcohol actually doesn't cook off entirely, but even if it did, some people cannot buy, consume, or have alcohol around in any capacity for various reasons, and some people just don't like it.)
Apple juice is substituted for wine. White grape juice works well too.

This is the best Korean short rib recipe I’ve ever done. It is a commitment as anyone reading the directions can see. I had no doubanjiang and my Korean grocery store was closed. I substituted 2 T of Sambal Oelek and a quarter cup of sugar in the dipping sauce. I also cut down on the dried chilis in the initial portion of the recipe as my family is not big on heat. There were no leftovers. Great Sunday dinner.

This was fantastic. I did have to substitute sherry for Shaoxing, gochujang for doubanjiang (these items are not available in my small town.) Otherwise made as-is and it was amazing. Served the boned short ribs over rice with extra sauce & all the condiments. Do go overboard on the basil, it really popped.

Had a LOT of the sauce left over (both the finished sauce and the braising liquid). I'm looking forward to repurposing both in the future.

What would be the time and temperature if using a slow cooker?

I’d prefer to leave the stove free to do other things.

I made these yesterday for Sunday lunch.  I didn't make the sauce, simply served the result of the spiced braise with some steamed broccoli.  My normal braises lean French so this was a delicious change in flavors and complemented the beef very well.  I am old to remember when short ribs were cheap family weeknight fare. Alas no more, they have definitely moved up the price scale to be an occasional treat (and I can't remember the last time I had eaten them before yesterday).

We made this for friends. Followed the recipe exactly and it was plenty of food for 6 people or 4 as we were plus 2 lunch leftovers for me :)). Add plenty of stock, sort of a cup or two too many and you can leave in the oven for about an hour longer, we did about 4.5-5 hours and it was fantastic! Add a lot of parsley, Thai basil, cilantro to the salad. Our guests hardly touched the rice...

This was fantastic. Based on other people's comments, I did cut the soy sauce down by half to make sure it wasn't too salty. Other than that, I followed the recipe exactly. It for sure is a full afternoon project, but worth it.

I was lucky enough to snag a reservation to Best Quality Daughter this weekend.  There were 5 of us and so we ordered this dish as well as few others.  We were served one huge beef rib rather than the smaller ones listed in the recipe which was quite impressive as well absolutely delicious.  The meat practically fell off of the bone.  There was enough meat and accompanying lettuce, Thai basil and sliced scallions to take home. Our waitstaff recommended the pickled vegetables which were perfect.
so many ingredients. I ran out of ramekins!

Made it with pot-roast in the slow cooker for the same amount of time (4hrs). Substitutes: Juniper berries for the Sichuan peppercorns. Lemon peel for the orange peel. While the meat rested and I made the sauce. 
I took Eggplant, thinly sliced, lightly cooked, placed the meat (broken up) inside the middle of the slice, and rolled them up, seam side down. Spooned a little of the sauce over the top.

Holy moly, this is THE short rib recipe to end all short rib recipes. The small Asian market in my mid-sized Montana town was out of doubanjiang. I replaced it with a bit of the chunky bits from the bottom of a jar of Fly By Jing’s chili crisp oil. And I skipped the corn starch - after reducing the sauce down by half, it was more than thick enough. Tomato paste has a whole lot of pectin in it. The corn starch really wasn’t necessary. So glad I made 1.5x the recipe. I’ll eat this for days on end.
MUST use low-sodium soy sauce or it will be way too salty as I unfortuantely found out - still flavorful though ,)

These are really delicious, and my guests were wow’d! BUT they are seriously labour intensive. I braised the ribs the night before in the IP. And did the sauce the next day, pouring over the ribs reheated in a warm oven (and rehydrated with a bit of the braising liquid). If you want a truly magnificent dish make this but don’t expect to have a lot of time for prepping other dishes.

Made with 4.5 lb pork shoulder cut into 2” chunks, and cooked in instant pot. Browned meat and did Steps 2 and 3 with instant pot in sauté mode, and then cooked high pressure for 30 min (cook time based on a Serious Eats recipe for pork shoulder). Pork was vey tender, but kept it in chunks rather than shredding. Cooked sauce stove top and drizzled over pork. Quick-pickled the cuc’s for some acidity. Turned out amazing! Lots of leftover sauce, which is great on rice and even on fried chicken!
I can’t figure out why the sauce needs to go back into the Dutch oven. Seems like I can daub in the second pot, no?

Great recipe. Substituted low-sodium soy sauce amount with half full-strength soy sauce and half water - worked great.

Also did 50 minutes in Instant Pot with natural release as suggested by another person, which also worked well.

Alot of the extravagant ingredients aren't necessary to make the dish delicious!
Bean paste and Chinese chilies can be omited. You can just use some extra 5 spice powder instead of star anise(5 spice powder is alot of star anise anyways)
Great recipe 👌

Turned out pretty well, but too salty for my taste. Next time use a little less soy sauce and more stock and wine. Definitely serve with rice and something pickled to cut the salt and richness. 
Also, this was a lot of work and ingredients for not that many ribs, and I ended up with an insane amount of extra sauce. Next time use twice as much meat (8-10 lb.) but the same amount of sauce ingredients. 1 lb. ribs per person is about right, so this way would feed a crowd or leave leftovers.
How does it work making these ahead? Do you reheat the short ribs in the braising liquid then strain the liquid for the sauce?

This was delicious. I made a couple of substitutions, but stayed as close to the recipe as I could. For one, I used ground cinnamon and I don't recommend that. It was still good, but a little too strong on the cinnamon vs. using cinnamon sticks. I also used shallots instead of white onion, which I highly recommend. I ended up straining them from the sauce and saving them to put into fried rice with the leftover short ribs. So good!
I use a graham cracker pie crust. Much easier and tastier.
My friends in Key West always used graham cracker crusts, and always topped the pie with meringue.  I never saw any Conks using whipped cream on their pies.

This is lime pie, NOT key lime pie. Graham cracker crust = key lime pie
Meringue topping = key lime pie

I’ve actually never seen key lime pie served with meringue...always with whipped cream. Tho, I’ve never been to Key West. When I make it, I always use the whites for meringue to top it with. And add a bit of ginger juice, from grated fresh ginger, along with the lime juice. I always get raves from everyone I’ve served it to.
Hence the name, Florida LIME Pie.

This is a Florida Lime Pie, NOT a Key Lime Pie. Ms. Nickerson knew the differences. Different limes and taste. Great recipe. Love the density and flavor of Key Lime Pie, but no one in my house is a fan of graham cracker crust. Sweet and too competitive with the "main event."  It's so amusing to read comments. Every cook has their "take" on any recipe. Fair enough! Having lived in Florida for years, I've tasted dozens of versions of both fillings, crusts, and toppings. Try this.  It's delicious.

It's often tempting to go the easy route and forego a homemade pie crust. But, made-from-scratch often differentiates spectacular dessert creations from mediocre ones. It really is worth the time and effort to create desserts whose taste are worth their calorie count!  
Nothing compares to the flavor of a dessert that is 100% homemade.

For heaven’s sake, just make the darn recipe as written!  It’s delicious. I wasted my time reading about crust preferences and authenticity when I could have been eating another slice of the wonderful pie. I don’t whine when people tell me that cheese whiz goes on Philly steak sandwiches. Just saying

The original concept of mixing egg yolks lime juice and condensed milk was that it required no baking.  The lime juice acts to set the yolks and milk all by itself.  Recipes today specify baking only for food safety reasons.  This kind of pie can be dated to the invention of condensed milk.  In south Florida in the 19th century fresh milk would be almost non existent.

I'm 74 years old. I grew up in Florida and I'll die here as well. The key lime pie I grew up with had a pastry crust and meringue making the pie deliciously tart and refreshing. Graham cracker crust and whipped cream make this pie too sweet. If sweet is what you're after, make banana pudding or sweet tea.

@Linda. I’m with you! I spent 30 years in the Upper Keys with many friends who were conch’s. True Key Line Pie has pastry crust and meringue. It’s hard to find real Key limes these days but if you can find them they are higher in acidity and “cook” the pie!
I find graham cracker crust dominates the lime flavor, so I use shortbread pie crust.

For the crust combine 30 Oreo cookies (none of the white center) 3/4 cup of pecans and 3-4 to 1 tsp. cayenne in a blender.  Blend to a near flour consistency.  Add 4 oz of melted butter slowly until the mixture is pasty.  Press the mixture evenly into the pie dish and sides, pierce with a fork about 12 times and bake at 400 for about 10-12 minutes. 

This combo with the chilled lime and the "heat" from the cayenne makes a wickedly appealing dessert.

I have this book, and it’s fun to read. Her recipe for Tupelo Honey Pecan Pie has a short essay about Florida honeys; her instructions for “opening a fresh coconut” will make anybody who’s ever struggled with that smile.

I remember trying to open a fresh coconut from the store in the 60s!  We used a hammer & a screw driver ... sadly, all of the milk was lost ... but YUM!  The coconut meat tasted divine!  And that texture ... so different from anything I'd ever had before!
I like Key Lime Pie but I really loved this recipe.  It’s lighter, not too sweet.   You could use a Graham cracker crust but I thought the flour crust was perfect.

The best topping for any lime pie--Key lime or otherwise--is sweetened sour cream with some lime zest added. About 1/2 cup sour cream, blended with 2 tablespoons sugar and the zest of one lime. Spread over the top of the pie after 15 minutes and bake for the remaining 5 minutes, then cool.

For those wanting a really good, quick lime pie. Mix the contents of a small can of frozen lime concentrate, with one can of condensed milk and a small container of whipped cream. Put in ready-made graham pie crust. Refrigerate for a few hours, or pop in the freezer for about thirty minutes, or less.  My mother was given this recipe in the 60s, when we lived in Miami. (She used the juice from the key lime tree in our backyard, but the "recipe" called for the frozen concentrate.)
Why "fast" ???

Nellie and Joe's Key Lime Juice is the best.
Use a Graham cracker crust. Floow the recipe on the bottle. 
Easy peasy and delicious.
That key lime pie recipe is the is the best!
Nope. Squeezing your own key limes is the best. Such a difference!
Excellent with walnut crust

S. Florida native here, my mom frequently made Key lime pie from our backyard tree, when in a big rush, she would simply spoon the filling into dishes and top with sweetened, buttered graham cracker crumbs. Usually, she made a pastry crust, considered it the authentic version, moved to Florida in the 1930's. Never topped it with meringue, but occasionally a dollop of whipped cream. I appreciate the writer distinguishing this version from the pie developed by folks who had no refrigeration.

Mine was underbaked (both the crust -- which I had in oven for 35 mins as well as the filled pie, baked for about 25 mins. I should have gone with my gut and baked both until I was convinced they were done.) Still, the flavor was excellent. My go-to pie dough method: I put the fat into freezer until well-chilled and then use the largest holes of a box grater to shred it. I dump it into the dry mix and use my fingers to quickly blend fat and flour before adding ice water. Quick, easy, dependable.

My brother sent me this recipe yesterday and said it left out Step 9: Invite Uncle John over to see if he likes it. He did! Delicious recipe with just the right amount of tang. The consistency was nice. I wish I had cooked the crust a bit longer—it said a little underdone was OK, but maybe next time I won’t bake it on a cookie sheet.

I made this pie as a child in Homestead, FL (just above the Keys), and yes, we used graham cracker crust, but the rest of this recipe is the same.  We beat the egg whites, folded them into the yolks, and served with whipped cream.  We used key limes or persian limes.  That was the key lime pie of the area 69 years ago.
In Olden Thymes, that was called a chiffon pie.
Chiffon pies were usually made with gelatin.
I love this recipe, but when I make my “key lime” pie I do use Whipped cream, so I’m thinking that whether you’re making key lime or lime pie you could either use a meringue or a whipped topping!

This was a very tasty recipe.  To save my arm, I used an electric beater.  I also changed the order of the steps so that I didn't have to wash the beater blades until the end.  I whipped my egg whites first and then my yolks.  We liked it so much, we got the cookbook.  You've got to try the Mango Pie.

I prefer Craig Claiborne's version; 3 TBSP cornstarch 1¼C sugar (plus for meringue), ¼ C lime juice, 1 TBSP line rind, 3 eggs separated (I use 4), (dash salt), 1½ C boiling water. 
 Mix cornstarch, sugar, lime juice and rind, Beat yolks and add. Gradually add boiling water. Heat to boiling, boil gently 4 minutes. Pour into baked shell.  
Make meringue, spread on pie and bake at 424° about 4 minutes.

The first time I had Key Lime Pie was in The Keys in the ‘50’s. It had a traditional pie crust and topped with whipped cream, not graham crackers and meringue. While I like the graham cracker crust I find unsweetened marries better than meringue. As for key limes, most of the bottled juice comes from limes grown in Mexico.
I’m from Miami and we made it with key lines from our tree in the back yard. definitely graham cracker crust and we used cool whip on top lol.
I use ginger crackers for the crust which works well with the particular flavor of Key Lime juice. I also top with meringue which I'm told is the traditional style.

I LOVE the tartness of the Key Lime Pie, but this is an interesting compromise.  Most of the Key Lime recipes call for SO MUCH SUGAR--not for me.  I'm looking to try this recipe using key limes--anybody try that? This recipe is being reported as less sweet, so I may take the plunge and report back.  Also, will try it with tiger nut flour instead.  LOL--hope it doesn't end up in the garbage!

Key limes are much more tart and dense in flavor than regular limes.   This recipe gets the sweetness from the sweetened condensed milk with no added sugar. That's why it's less sweet, but still delicious.  Key limes much more sour and bitter without lots of added sugar.  So, if you give it a try, but don't be surprised if it puckers your mouth.

If you go to Key West you will find that no one uses meringue on a Key Lime pie, always whipped cream, but really, who cares do whatever you like best and not be guided by what some says is the right way to go
Everyone in Key West, these days, is from somewhere else. The conchs moved to Lake Worth Beach!

I grew up in Florida and I agree... it had a regular pie crust and a meringue topping. And yes, it was much less sweet and much more refreshing. 
 My mother also made a “lime pie” just as this one is described with beaten egg whites. It was good too!

This is a recipe for Florida Lime Pie. Why the references to Key Lime Pie.
 I make Key Lime Pie and love it, but that doesn't mean that I won't try the Florida Lime Pie version. It could very well turn out to be a great adventure!

This was wonderful!  I made it without change, and liked its consistency and lack of cloying sweetness.  My husband was reading today's Sunday magazine, which features the recipe, and said "Is this the pie you made earlier today?"  I said yes, and he was very excited - and even more so after cutting into it.  One tiny note:  the crust ingredients don't call for sugar, but I like a teaspoon of sugar for pie crusts.
Make this with Seville Orange juice in place of limes. Verry different, but well worth a try...

I'm curious if coconut milk can be substitued for the sweetened condensed milk? Evaporated or condensed milk has the most lactose of almost any dairy product and for those of us with that issue, pies that rely on it end up being out of reach. I do love lime pie and would love to find a work-around.

I followed the instructions but the filling amount was too much for a 9 inch pie plate/dish. I used a deep dish 9.5 inch pie plate and still had about a cup of filling left (baking that in a bain-marie so it doesn’t go to waste). The pie came out of the oven with a puffy lifted look and it’s holding firm, no sinking, and no room for cream. Will have to dollop that on each slice. Oh the hardship. Yum!

We did not have sweetened condensed milk, but did have a can of coconut milk. This substitution worked great! One caveat: as sweet as coconut milk is, it still needs added sugar for this pie. About 1/4 cup results in a nicely tangy filling.
Agree with the graham cracker crust....cuts the prep time by two thirds.

Dang, that was really good.  I found Key limes at the supermarket and decided to try this.  I was a bit worried there wasn't enough sugar to offset the tartness of the limes, but the sweetened condensed milk is plenty sweet.  The filling is so light and creamy and tart, it's really fantastic.  I used a Pillsbury pie crust and the whole thing was so easy on a Saturday afternoon.  Yum!  I have no opinion on whether this is authentic, but I can confirm it's delicious.

Made this using a 9-in pie pan.  It took 40 minutes for the center of the pie to stop jiggling.  Even with the extra time, the crust was undercooked.  The filing itself was delicious, but the underdone crust really detracted from it. As soon as I get more limes on my tree, I will try this again, using a King Arthur flour recipe for the crust.
BAKE THIS PIE CRUST LONGER THAN 30 MINUTES!  Twice, it’s been underbaked. Try at least 35 minutes next time. Also, be sure to throughly mix even though you are trying to “fold”.

In South Florida, where I spent my teens, I’ve had Key Lime pie in the actual Keys with both merengue and whipped cream. My fervent preference is for whipped cream. For the poster who claimed that whipped cream made it too sweet confused me because merengue is always sweeten but whipped cream needn’t be. Unsweetened whipped cream is a perfect foil for both the custard’s sweet-tart elements.
I make it with a gingersnap crust. Always seems to be a hit. I worked in a restaurant in Florida that was know for its key lime pie--they never had meringue on them, always whipped cream.

There is a difference between "normal," or Persian limes, and smaller, milder key limes. Key limes normally are sold in small, perhaps 1# bags. They have thinner skins and moister flesh. It's like comparing Eureka and Meyer lemons. Both fruits will make a good pie, but you may need to adjust the sugar levels depending on the type of lime used. Key limes make killer margaritas, BTW.
Why not make with key limes? I’ve always made key lime pie and it is fabulous. Anyone know the difference?
How is this different from key lime pie except for the type of lime and the crust?
Key limes and graham cracker are traditional in Florida.  Key limes are not regular limes.

I agree on both!!!

Key limes = key lime pie
Lime juice = lime pie
How does this compare to the Atlantic Beach Pie (which uses saltines for its crust and seems easier to make)?
No egg whites in Atlantic Beach Pie
Isn't Atlantic Beach pie made with lemons?
PS.  Key limes or Mexican limes (those little limes with the thin skins) are not as tart as the larger green skinned Persian limes.  Much better for recipes like this one.

The traditional Key Lime pie does, yes, use a graham cracker crust. And please, please, please don't water down this creation by folding in the egg whites. The 'real' Key Lime pie (I come from S. Fla) uses the egg yolks, condensed milk, 1 1/2 tsp of cornstarch and then, back in 'olden days' it was just shoved into the 'fridge.' Later years, in a concession to worries about bacteria, it would be baked in a 400 degree oven for 5 minutes.

This recipe is not calling itself a Key Lime Pie, but rather a Florida  Lime Pie.  So of course it is a different recipe from the one you're describing.  And I agree a graham cracker crust sounds better and easier than messing with rolling and pie weights.
we love Manhattan Key Lime Juice and freeze the pie once it cools Amazing texture
Can this be made with lemons in place of limes?
Yes. If you want a lemon pie.

why is the pie filling orange in the photo? should be a pale green or white-beige color, not orange. Just wondering.
also, I prefer key lime pie with a pastry crust not graham as people commented. Traditional key lime pie does not have a meringue topping.

This sounds delicious, for all the reasons she created this. I must be the only person in America that hates graham cracker crusts.  Thank you Jane Nickerson for making a Florida Lime Pie I will love, with real pie crust and whipped cream.  Oh and lime.  Yum

You are definitely not the only person who doesn't like graham cracker crusts. Too sweet and competitive with the fillings. Never used in my house, and if anyone orders pie in a restaurant and it has graham cracker crust, we all eat the filling and leave the crust behind.

I got a variation of this recipe on the east side crosstown bus going to sign up for my unemployment (which did not last long).  I still have it in my recipe folders (an addition!), but during a family lime pie bake-off, my version of The Barefoot Contessa's recipe won the day.  Both are divine.
Your article on Jane Nickerson reminded me of a cookbook I had by Marjorie Keenen Rawlings, a deep Floridian, who had wonderful recipes.

Question: This recipe looks delicious to me & I intend to bake it as written.
(Not a fan of regular key lime pie due to the tartness and the often jelly-like consistency.)

Can anyone who has baked this recipe describe the texture of the completed main pie filling? (Not the whipped cream topping.)
Is it more like a mousse feel or a meringue feel? Or something else?

Thanks!

FYI, well-made "regular key lime pie" is neither tart nor jelly-like and, of course, never green; always yellow. Suggest you Google "Manny's Key Lime Pie."  If the recipe you find has either lime zest or graham cracker crust, you've not found the correct recipe because Manny's has neither.  Properly made, the meringue will be about twice as high as the pie filling. And there is nothing cloying about it.
Key limes = key lime pie!!! Obviously. It has nothing to do with the pie base. These are NOT the same as standard 'limes'.
My pie has a huge crack down the middle! What did I do wrong! Thanks for any advice

Hi Elissa-
It sounds like you may have cooked it a bit too long.  Since it's basically a custard, overcooking can cause cracks.  I bet it tasted great, still!

Thank you so much and in fact I thought the same! It was most likely ready as stated in the recipe stated and I left it only an extra five minutes but it was five minutes too long. I should have taken it out when the skewer showed a bit of filling.
I used graham cracker crust also.  It went well with the lime.  So easy to make and so good!  Not too sweet.
This is a variation of a Key Lime pie. It is perfectly acceptable, but I like the taste of a graham cracker crust. In the end, I prefer good old fashioned Key Lime Pie!
This is a delicious pie and can't wail to make for my community group.  Use any pie crust as it's the filling that makes the pie.

The best key lime pie (yes, I know this recipe is NOT for key lime pie) I ever had was at Manny and Isa's restaurant in Islamorada, FL.  Sadly, it's gone now but the recipe for Manny's Key Lime Pie lives on in Linda Gassenheimer's 1991 cookbook "Keys Cuisine." The following quote appears in the headnote for this pie: "Joseph Sassine, chef at the Green Turtle Inn [in Islamorada], says that the original [key lime] pie had a short crust pastry and a thick [towering] layer of meringue on the top."
The recipe made more than enough filling for one pie. I happened to have a Pre made graham cracker crust on hand, so the extra filling made another pie!

I loved the back story about Jane Nickerson and Craig Claibone. This is a wonderful dessert! But I also I loved the back story of Jane Nickerson and Craig Claibone. Anne Mendelson's 1990 article not mentioned here.
This, as written, was great. Not cloyingly sweet like klp. Thanks !
Yum.  Big hit at our table.  We needed 8 limes to get enough juice.  Overall quite easy and delicious.
The crust is tough.  The filling is divine, much better than the traditional key lime pie filling.  I will make this pie again, but use a different crust.
Any substitute for pie weights? In my whole life I’ve never needed them—which shows how much I bake so would rather not buy them if I can avoid it.
Dried beans work great. You can save them in a plastic bag and use them over and over.

I just don't bother with pie weights when blind baking ... but beans do work. If the crust is quite thin, as this should be, no need to fuss with weights.  Just poke with a fork and throw in the oven for 15 minutes or so at 350 degrees F.  It should be dry on top with a little moisture underneath and puffing slightly.  You want it almost nearly done, because you will bake it slightly more once the filling is in. Thin crust is the trick.  If you add a T of sugar to the crust mixture, even better.

Such a light & airy pie. Deceptively rich (I tried to eat 2 pieces, but could finish only 1.5.). I was short on time, so I made a graham cracker crust, but will definitely make a pastry crust next time, so nothing competes with the sharp, fresh lime. A bit of slightly sweetened whipped cream is key, to balance the tartness. I think this will be my go-to vs. key lime pie!
This was so delicious.  An absolute taste explosion.  Thank you for printing this recipe and telling me a little bit about Jane Nickerson.

This was delicious and pretty easy to make. Tastes better than Key Lime pie in my opinion and definitely easier to make. Sure, you still have to separate eggs and beat the egg whites, but it’s way less fussy than a meringue.

I don’t know what happens, but even though I folded in the egg whites to the lime, yolk, condensed milk mixture, they separated. I got a cooked egg white part at the top and a yolk lime unset section underneath. 
Maybe I cooked it too long? I cooked it for 30 minutes because it looked like pure liquid at 20 minutes. Maybe the crust wasn’t cold? 
Although I do separately wish I’d saved myself a few hours by doing a graham cracker crust.
Do not make again, crust underdone, filling bitter.

Great flavor in the pie, but my crust was underdone. Next time try 40 minutes, until it is complete cooked before adding filling. Remember to add 2 tsp sugar to crust—that worked well. 1 cup of cream was too much for topping—try a half cup next time.
My pie did not hold it's shape despite being chilled.  What did I do wrong?

I have made this to twice. Both times to rave reviews. Though they are a pain to squeeze I used Key Limes(well worth it). I also used a store bought crust. If you do as well, I would cook it like you are making a no bake pie (I didn’t the second time, but should have). This really good in a regular crust (I plan on a from scratch next time). As for those crying for no Meringue topping, it’s inside the pie. & it makes this pie amazing!
Very yummy, not too sweet. Made it with a graham cracker crust.

Someone said this was Florida lime pie not Key lime pie because it is not made with Graham cracker crust, and because it has whipped topping instead of meringue.  No, it is not Key lime pie because it is not made with Key limes, the much smaller, richer tasting native fruit, compared to the Persian limes grown in Florida, California, and Latin America.  Key limes are still grown in the Keys, but mostly in the U.S. now they come from Mexico and are rarely available in most of the country.

We were excited to try this Florida lime pie since key limes can be such a pain to find and juice. Would have been nice to have a go-to alternative but this wasn’t it. Filling is too sour/bitter for us and the crust was really bland.

I discovered this today after a character on Days of Our Lives said, "No, I love FLORIDA lime pie. It doesn't have that nasty graham cracker crust. Who told you I like key lime pie?" My whole life is a lie.

I made this with a graham cracker crust, and it was absolutely delicious. The filling is very light, and didn't cut well for me, but still yummy. I added 1 tablespoon and a teaspoon and a half of sugar to the egg yolk mixture, since it tasted a little too sour for my liking, and it came out perfectly!
Ed made thanksgiving 2021 and everyone loved; subbed graham cracker crust and was good
Made the recipe as written and really loved it. The filling is so light and pillowy!

Craig Claiborne, who star eclipsed hers....for systemic reasons we still wrestle with today?

That is such a tease! Please tell us more!

Just an additional thought, The Keys are very small in area. There’s no way that they could fulfill the demand for all those pies on restaurant menus, bakeries and home cooks. Most of the juice we buy is from Mexico. It’s kind of like all that Moroccan argon oil couldn’t possibly be coming from Morocco.  I’d venture that most of it is synthetic.
I ordered Jane Nickerson's Florida Cookbook! Can't wait to try this recipe and all her other recipes!

I love @Robert Basta's idea of adding cayenne and pecans to an Oreo crust.  Gotta try that.

"Her successor in New York was Craig Claiborne, whose star eclipsed hers for, among other things, systemic reasons we wrestle with still."  

Indeed!

Also, check out Maggie Ruggiero's food styling in this pie photo -- for once she managed to do something that wasn't entirely boring!
