Love how some readers - Tara! - declare that this is "NOT" key lime pie. The title of the recipe plainly states that it is a 'Florida' lime pie. The difference is in the type of lime, not the crust.

Yes, people , this is NOT a key lime pie , so why are you all comparing it .  Its a delicious spring pie , i took the easy way out with a graham cracker crust , next time i will make the crust in the recipe because it really has a great light flavor, and comes together really fast .

This is close to the pie I've  made for years, except I leave out the egg whites. And for those who don't want to labor on the crust as written, BUT don't like graham either, try a ginger snap cookie crust. The peppery bite in the cookie crust is a nice contrast with the  sweet-tart filling and the creamy topping. I prefer non-key limes, as this recipe uses, finding them more tart than key limes. Also less work to juice them. My two cents ..
This was delicious, however my filling did not “firm up”. Did anyone else have this issue?

Using other’s suggestions that the filling wasn’t enough to fill up the pie I used a smaller pie tin 8” - huge mistake! I had so much filling leftover and couldn’t make another pie crust in time - made a graham crust and a second smaller pie (8”). If you make your whites and yolk mixture properly you will have a beautiful amount to fill up your pie tin. Wish I hadn’t read the comments - usually they are very helpful - not this time.

This recipe is excellent! If you have the luxury of buying actual Key Limes to use for the juice in this, do so. It’s worth the trouble to juice the little devils. Trust me. But-and I love being able to say this since I’m usually the one who can’t find some unique NYT Food ingredient-key limes have limited national availability and are available in the south only for a short period of time. Crust is just the vessel. Graham or butter are both good.
Used graham cracker crust. Fantastic recipe!

For my pie, I made this recipe's filling only. I put it in a gingersnap crust (home made) and I topped with the meringue recipe from the NYT's Extra-Lemony Meringue Pie recipe. It was perfection. The filling was divine.

I made this as directed with two changes:  1/2 can sweetened condensed milk instead of the whole, and I added a Meyer lemon juice with zest.  Came out excellent.
I was tempted to add a couple T cocoa powder to the crust mixture -- chocolate and lime sounded good to me.  Whaddya think?
This was good - because of the whipped egg whites the filling looked a lot more than an omelet than I was comfortable with lol. Also I think the crust needs to be pre-baked for less time

I loved loved loved the filling! It was perfect! With regular limes :)

The only thing is I wish I had read the notes about the crust. It was undercooked at the bottom, and too crunchy on the edges.

Will definitely make again but definitely will use another pie crust recipe
I will likely make this as is, but if I find key lime juice, can I substitute the exact same volume?

My first experience with true Key Lime pie in Key West was at a local seafood restaurant that was quite old but with great food.  The key lime pie they served was with a pastry crust and filling made same as a lemon meringue pie NO SWEETENED CONDENSED MILK.  It was delicious, tart and sweet but not too much either way.  That is true key lime pie, a version of lemon meringue pie made with Florida Key Limes instead of lemons.
Bah on graham cracker crust! The versions I've eaten in the Keys have regular crust and meringue, not the lazy graham crackers and dairy topping.

title: Pork Chops With Onion Gravy Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
I agree with Juliet Jones that we don't all have chicken or beef stock sitting around ready to use in small quantities. Opening a can, using half of it, and throwing the rest of it away 4-5 days later seems like a waste. 
Bouillon cubes are great. Even better, I think, is the jarred "Better Than Bouillon" (BTB); it has a very long refrigerator life, now comes in many flavors (chicken, beef, ham) including Low Sodium, and can be used to produce any amount of broth, small or large. I swear by it.

I make a similar dish but add apple slices to the onions near the end (not too soon or they turn to mush) and do the whole thing on the top of the stove in a covered pan rather than in the oven. You can also add a splash of white wine or apple cider just near the end to give it a bit of acid brightness. I finish it off with a few twists of the pepper grinder.
I do exactly as NHW suggests. Better Than Bouillon is an essential in my refrigerator when you don't have broth and need less than a cube or can full.

Bouillon cubes don't ever seem to get much of a mention here in the Times recipes, but I use them a lot when chicken stock or chicken broth is called for. (After all, who has "half an inch" of chicken stock sitting around waiting to be used?) Just dissolve one of those small cubes in a Pyrex jug of boiling water.

Unless the pan is a complete mess or burned (which it shouldn't be), I would dump excess oil, leave some, and definitively not wipe out the pan. That's called fond, FCS - put the onions in and as they cook and release their juices, the plan deglazes and you capture the best part of the flavor of the dish.

Freeze leftover off-the-shelf broth in ice cube trays and keep in freezer for months and months. Pop a handful or two into the pan and there is no waste.   Loved this with the added apple slices NHW recommends. I used a splash of Calvados at the end because I can't help myself.

Jullet asks "Who has half-an inch of chicken broth sitting around?"  I keep an open box of broth in the fridge.  I always date it, so that I know it's good, but generally use it all for adding to dishes just like this.  Low sodium of course.  Buillon is loaded with salt.

I too was often plagued by recipes that called for 1/4 cup or 1/2 cup of chicken stock.  
Eventually, I realized that I could store in different sized containers.  Now I also freeze some in ice cube trays;  when frozen, I empty them into plastic freezer bags.  I use as many as needed for sauce, gravies, etc.

To those that are concerned about the saved flour mixture, I might be reading it wrong, but I think what he's taking about is saving the leftover flour mixture to use later in the recipe, when you sprinkle some on the onions.

I love this- thanks NYT!  My Mom would use this method and add carrots, potatoes and celery.  Everything on top of the stove, covered for 40-45 minutes . Mash the potato with your fork on the plate.   Add mushrooms for "fancy."
The leftover flour is not a microbial hazard as it is mixed with the broth brought to a boil and cooked for 45 minutes. No pathogens survive that.
What's the point, Sam, of making a "big, flavorful crust" on the chops when they are later immersed in liquid and braised, turning the crust soft and mushy?
I have the same question. The only answer I can think of is that a big crust will stand up better to being braised? I don't know, but that's a good point.

I save my vegetable trimmings, peels, etc., esp onion ends and the outside layers that get peeled away, and all the chicken bones. I put 'em in the freezer until I have a large ziploc bagful. Costco rotisserie chicken carcasses end up in there, a lot.

I make the broth, skim the top and divvy it up into plastic containers, about 1 1/2 cups each, and freeze them.  If you don't need that much, put it in a pan on low to defrost some and pull the ice lump out when it's enough. Return to the freezer!
I caramelize fennel with the onions and add apples towards the end. But then we're addicted to fennel in this house.
I would not recommend saving leftover dredging flour after coming in contact with raw pork chops.  Saved flour could harbor bacteria from raw meat.
You only save it to use in the gravy in this recipe.

Followed the recipe except for wiping out the pan. My chops were lean so I had to add a bit of butter to sauté the onions. I used thyme, garlic powder, a little cayenne, and S&P. I added a little half and half with the broth as I like a cream gravy. Did everything in my cast iron Dutch oven. Baked for about 45 minutes. Perfection.

To deal with recipes that call for just a small amount of broth, I freeze broth in ice cube trays and use as many cubes as I need. It’s a low bar, but canned broth is usually better than my home made. And when I have leftover broth from a can or carton, that’s what does into the ice cube trays for next time. (When I do like my homemade broth, I, of course, also freeze that in trays.) if I need a lot of broth, I use a lot of cubes.

My thin boneless pork chops were essentially cooked before they browned well on the second side.  So… I Did save the brown bits, deglazed with a little white wine.  Long slow cooked the onions, added broth and simmered until nearly dry, added more butter & flour and then more broth a cooked in oven.  Last 10 minutes nestled chops back in to warm.  No loss of brown crust!  Yummy moist chops with Onion jam to die for.
Rehydrated a handful of porcini mushrooms, chopped them up, and added them to the onion mixture along with the strained soaking liquid and the chicken broth. So easy and so delicious!
Use this technique for turkey cutlets, cube steak, or chicken. Slice more onions than you think you need.

This was one of the most fun dishes I've ever made! I keep thinking about it. It's true, this recipe without exact ingredients is easy to memorize and I've told my friends about it. And, it was delicious! I agree that Better than Bouillon is wonderful to have on hand. The New York Times has just come out with a cookbook with "recipes without recipes" which I think I will  purchase.

I haven't made this dish yet, but will soon. I do wonder why, after browning the chops, you would want to wipe the pan clean and then add oil to cook the onions?  Seems to me the frond in the pan after the frying would add greatly to the onion gravy.

When I do make this dish, I will make it that way and see.
I keep a decent bottle of vermouth at hand for such situations. Great substitute for when recipe calls for a small portion of broth. Better than Broth is also excellent, keep it in the fridge always.
This is a great recipe. My mom used to make something similar when I was a kid. A few things I add is a splash of Worcestershire sauce and a splash of apple cider or cider syrup.

This looks fantastic, but I have not made it yet--I have one question.  What is a pork chop like after it's been pan seared and then cooked in the oven for 45+ minutes?  My experience with pork chops is that they don't take too well to over cooking.

this recipe would be great as a choucroute; just substitute sourkraut for most of the onions, perhaps grate a carrot into the mix, bury a bay leaf, and set in a slow oven for a few hours, add more stock as needed but the result should be just moist not soupy.  adding pork sausage(smoked) and a slab of smoked but not too sweet bacon to cook along with the sourkraut on chops.
OMG this sounds amazing. Thanks!

I would use the whole can of broth. Half an inch doesn't seem as though it would make enough gravy. If I did have leftover broth I'd use it as the liquid to make some rice, either with the chops or a side for something else in a day or two.
But, for a small amount I'll echo those who recommended Better Than Bouillon. It's just the best. I have five jars in the fridge right now. Come to think of it, I'd probably add some to this gravy as canned broth can be mild and I really like salt.

Love the whole 'no recipe' concept and use it a lot in my kitchen. But a safety note - flour or any other ingredient that has been in contact with raw pork (or chicken) should NOT be saved for another use.
It's not saved for another use, it's added to the onions later in the recipe to thicken the gravy. It's cooked so it's perfectly safe.

Mine is almost done cooking and it feels delicious--can't wait to get it on the plate.  

Anyone add mustard?

Made this as “recipe” suggested.  Recommend omitting the flour added to onions and perhaps substituting wine for broth/water for the braising.  It was a real winner!  I served with buttermilk biscuits, not mashed potatoes.

A very good recipe.  I agree w/Kathryn--45+ minutes in the oven is too much.  I left mine in for 30 minutes and found that to be about 5 minutes too long.  If left according to the recipe, the pork would be shoe leather!

I made this last week and it was delicious.
Thank you, Sam.
Gravy...ah, best five letter word!
Bake 1 hour at 225. TJ’s French cut pork chops, 1 1/2 inches thick. Can add mushrooms too.
Excellent.  I left too much oil in the bottom so it was a little greasy, next time i'll wipe out more and mostly let the butter do its thing.  Otherwise, delicious, a keeper.

This was delicious! I did one half yellow onion, one half red onion and 2 shallots. Magic combo. Also, I went for it and tried using Old Bay with additional smoked paprika. I wish I made twice as much, it was that good.
This was divine!

This was so good. Seasoned pork chops in advance with garlic salt. Used applewood smoked salt and fines herbes in the flour.  Added a bunch of chopped garlic to the onions. Our pork chops were double cut rib chops and very thick, but the 45 minute cooking time was a bit too long.  Would cut back next time and possibly use thinner pork steaks which might take to braising a bit better. Otherwise a huge hit - froze the extra gravy to serve on something else in the future - chicken perhaps?
Chicken broth freezes quite well.  If I need just part of a can, I just freeze the rest in a small container (or two)  - ready to pull out and use the next time.

I freeze home made chicken stock in ice cube trays then transfer to freezer bag for later use. Perfect for deglazing pans, adding richness to rice or polenta OR providing that elusive 1/2 inch of chicken stock.
I keep chicken stock, in small amounts, in my freezer and cans of Swanson's in my cupboard at all times.
I use frozen bone broth cubes when the recipe calls for a small amount of broth.

I always have chicken stock in the fridge. Use it anytime moisture is needed in sauces, sautéing, a sheet pan 35 minutes into a 45 minute creation to create liquid and add creme fresh or butter or mayo to . I love to pan fry. I get boneless pork loin from ALDI, Cut in half nd sear for 2 minutes on each side. Set aside and create the sauce for it.

If we open a can of chicken or beef broth and don't use it all, we freeze the rest in an ice cube tray.  Then pop them in a plastic bag and put them in the freezer.  Then you can take out as many as you need.  This is especially nice if you make homemade stock.  I find BTB too salty and industrial tasting.

I made this tonight, with my own variations with spices and herbs. I loved the flavor but felt that the pork chops were over cooked. Next time I try this approach, I would braise the chops at a much lower temperature and only for about 30 minutes. I braised them tonight for 20 minutes at 350 and then let them sit at “hold and warm” for another 10. 

I also did not throw out the oil. I added a little bit of butter.

What you’re all forgetting is the quality of the pork chops. Supermarket species are dreadful; butcher chops are good if the butcher gets his pork from Canada. But the best—and most expensive are heritage pork. I get heritage breed pork from two local farms. Their flavor is incredible, seriously good and are extremely tender. Once you have it it’s hard to return to lesser grades
Sounds like dry pork chops to me. How about baking at 225F for an hour. I roast pork shoulder at 225F and it comes out very moist.

To all you folks whining about how you don't have stock on hand, I have one thing to say: buy a whole chicken, cover it with water, boil an hour, cool and fill some ice cube trays.  I promise you, if you have stock in the freezer, nothing bad will ever happen again. I promise!  Oh and make Chinese chicken salad out of the leftover meat.

I have tried doing that and my stock just tasted blah.  I even tried a recipe where you roasted leftover turkey bones and then added the carcass and was so disappointed in the flavor.  Is there a trick that I don't know about?  Seems I never get a rich flavor.
Delicious, Made stir fry veges rather than potatoes.
For small portions of broth, freeze the broth, from whatever source, homemade or store-bought, in ice cube trays, decant into plastic bags, and use as much or as little as you need for your recipes.
Delicious - will definitely make again!

This is a great, simple dish. I pretty much followed the instructions as written except I didn't use flour on the chops and just dredged them in the spice mix and I only used a little in the onion to make the gravy. I had left over onion gravy but it was great on lots of things so it got used up pretty quickly

The best way to cook pork chops ever!  I have struggled to find a way to cook them that won't dry them out - to no avail.  Thanks to Sam Sifton for giving me my new go to recipe.  Besides emerging from the oven moist and flavorful, they were bathed in what tasted like the best onion soup!  I made them exactly as written, except for a splash of white wine in the pan in lieu of stock, and a little less time in the oven, even though my chops were beasts.  Served with collards and croquettes.  Yum!

I made this last night, using 2 thick boneless organic chops, the flour and spice mixture plus chicken broth. The chops were definitely moist after the oven time and the dish looked just like the photos. However, I used 2 huge sweet onions with a few yellow ones. The taste was a bit strange - was that due to the sweet onions? Any thoughts welcome.

I made this with boneless western ribs (thick strips from the shoulder, in case that's a regional name) as they stand up better to longer cooking. More flavor than loin chops, IMHO. Browned over medium low heat b/c I used paprika in the flour and it will burn and get bitter on high. No need to clean pan. Deglaze, add onions/mushrooms. When soft add broth, nestle in the meat and finish covered on the range top on low.
I agree with those who say bullion is loaded with sodium. Use the small cartons of broth ,freeze left over in ice cube trays then transfer to baggies. Easy,peasy!
loved this no recipe recipe, onion gravy came out great. spoon the sauce over the pork chops repetitively when taking it out of the oven. crowd favorite!

Made as per recipe except I used home-made beef broth (as that's what I had in freezer) and do believe it yields a richer onion gravy as for any good "bangers and mash" recipe. Also agree with not wiping out the pan you've seared the chops in as long as you haven't burned them -- that adds to the flavour. And give the onions enough time to caramelize a bit, another big flavour booster.
I just love the way Sam writes. He makes me want to give this a whirl.

I agree with Better than Bouillion as my go to when making broth but I also use Better than Gourmet when making something special.  They have every type of au jus bases and broth bases and if you're watching your sodium this is great for that.
I freeze some of my broth in ice cube trays, always have homemade stock available
I would never wipe the pan free of those crusty bits in the pan, from the pork chops.  Sam, Sam, I'm surprised at you!

This recipe is easy and delicious. I have concentrated chicken broth in a container in the fridge. I just mix it with hot water.  I buy it at a restaurant wholesale store. I want to try this recipe with mushrooms.

I used a pork tenderloin & it turned out great. I got a little carried away with the amount of onions, but it was terrific. And actually, because there are just two of us at home now, I used half a pork tenderloin. I made the NYT recipe for apple and onion stuffed pork tenderloin for Christmas dinner, froze the other half of the tenderloin, and used it for this recipe for New Year's Day. On the subject of stock...I make my own and freeze it in small batches so I can use what I need when needed.
This is not a no-recipe recipe - my dish turned out terrible. Maybe I’m just not a good enough cook to pull this off!
Braise the pork chops in beer to cover, leave them on the stove top for about 45 minutes, and keep half an eye on them to make sure the sauce thickens a lot but does not cook away

Re: stock vs. bouillon vs. waste.  Freeze stock in ice cube trays (that are used only for this purpose). Once frozen, remove the cube from the tray and seal then in air tight container/bag. These will keep in the freezer for months, using as many or few cubes as you need.

I made this tonight for a group of 18. Used paprika, garlic, thyme, red pepper and s&p. I did season the chops with additional s/p because it didn’t seem like enough in the flour. 8-9 onions, beef broth for the liquid. I baked it at 350 about 50 minutes, then held it at 200 another hour or so until dinner time (I cook for a rehab center).  It turned out very well, definitely worth making again.
I added collards greens after sautéing the onions. After they wilted I added flour, stock, etc. ‘twas excellent.
Tossed some carrots and celery with the onions…. Served with baked sweet potatoes with cranberries. Crazy good. Used old bay and a bit of smoked paprika.

Holy cow this is delicious. The gravy is so simple and perfectly balanced. Thinking to forego my usual turkey gravy on Thanksgiving for this instead. I’m embarrassed that I’ve never thought of  adding lemon zest to mashed potatoes - I may never make it without lemon again.

This was just wonderful!! It made heaps of sweet umami onion gravy with the chop and the mashed potatoes. A confession: I used only a small amount of olive oil in lieu of the butter, and it still had wonderful taste and texture. A keeper.

Hey, y’all! You don’t even need broth as frying pork chops until done makes a great gravy with what’s left in the cast iron skillet. Throw on a side of mashed taters and cooked cabbage and you’re done! I’m having some tonight while listening to some John Prine.
Delicious! I’ve made it twice now but cook pork less time. I guess my oven is hot

I have made this dish so many times!!! In my opinion it is 5 star.. I’ve used different spices mixed with the four and it’s delicious every time. Tonight I’m adding fennel to the onion. I make it for myself with one thick pork chop and put it in my le crueset!!! Delish!!!

Agree with NHW about adding a little acid at the end. I originally did not, then, after tasting, added a splash of apple cider vinegar, and the flavor totally popped. Otherwise, the dredging, searing at a high heat, then baking at 350 for 45 minutes produced an amazingly tender pork chop.
Red’s Original Seasoning

Made as written in a cast iron skillet.   2 brown and 1 red onion was more than enough for 2 large pork chops.   Served over hot soft croutons from a leftover sourdough loaf.  The onions were perfect.  They kept the meat moist and melted into the bread.  Perfect for a busy winter dinner

I agree with others (Nancy & EMCD), Better Than Bouillon is always in my frig, in this I would use chicken or garlic and rarely use broths as you can add it with or without the amount of water recommended as it can just be a base. I also agree with HS that wiping out the pan is a waste, I want the flavoring of the fond in my gravy and onions. A little white wine before you add the broth/water and scrap up an brown bits than cooking the onions didn't.
swanson now sells 8 oz. containers of chicken broth "quick cups" in a package of 4 that are pretty handy.

I used pork tenderloin instead of chops because that’s what I had. I cut the tenderloin into serving pieces and treated the pieces as suggested for the chops. The results were delicious, something I will definitely do again.  I made the butter biscuits, too, so it felt very southern.  Yummy!

Ignore the negative posts here…it’s what miserable people do.  This recipe is exactly as advertised - pork chops, potatoes and onions and it is fabulous.  We loved it.  Make it as specified and then adjust from there.  You will make it again.

Used
Pork tenderloin instead of chops because I had that in my freezer. Cooked in the oven and the pork is so tender and delicious. Used bullion powder and it is really tasty. In hind sight I would remove the pork chops from pan and just add the butter and onion to cook.
For me, this is a take on southern/soul smothered pork chops. A tasty, seasoned roux is all that's needed. My relatives never used stock. Season to your taste and it will be delicious.
Outstanding!
Nancy & Juliet Jones: We keep 1-liter resealable boxes of chicken and beef broth on hand all the time. We use it most every day.  We cook every day. We've never had any go bad.

I have made this recipe twice so far and both times the dish was a huge success!  I buy thick boneless pork chops for several recipes. For this recipe, I slice them horizontally to 1/2 inch thickness which is a perfect serving size (3-4 oz).
I like to use a mix of sweet and yellow onions and definitely prefer stock (my own) or boxed which we use quite regularly so no bouillon cubes needed. We love the rich sauce this makes.
Tonight I served this with smashed potatoes and broccoli.
Pork base broth
This was great.  Will make this recipe again.
How is it that browning and the 45 minutes in the oven does not result in tough, overcooked chops?

Was great. I used two, butcher-provided butterflied boneless chops (before butterflying were about 1-1/2 inches thick).  I cut the butterflied pieces apart and seared at medium to high heat in a scant amount of vegetable oil in a cast iron skillet. Got a GREAT crust.  

Based on the comments here and Sam’s direction to use lots of onions, I sliced up two yellow onions, two sweet onions and two shallots.  Since I used only a little oil, there wasn’t anything to wipe out after the sear, so I added about 4 TBSP butter to the pan and cooked the onion mixture as directed.  Sprinkled in the remaining 1/3 cup of seasoned dredging flour and cooked for 10 mins, then added a cup of chicken stock.  The onions cooked until soft in the soupy (but not thin) sauce.  

I transferred the onions to a Dutch oven, nestled the four chops into them (they were essentially covered), and cooked with the lid on for 30 mins.  When taking the, out of the oven, the sauce was bubbling. The chops were moist and the onion sauce divine.  I added about 2 tsps of apple cider vinegar and mixed it in well before serving.   Will definitely make again!

I added lots of spice to the flour mix, garlic powder, onion powder, pepper, chili powder and japanese chili mix.  I also used 2 cloves of garlic after the onions were caramelized.  After the pork chops cooked i added about 1/8 of a cup of half and half to the gravy and served with mashed potatoes.
1/8/25: aka Smothered Pork chops in Sifton’s book. I used 3 TJ boneless pork chops and one large yellow onion, 45 minutes in oven at 350*.
I thought the chops were dry.  After 45 minutes in the oven the internal temp was way over 160 degrees.  The onion gravy was good though.  If I make it again I'll cook for much less time.

Made this gravy for a cooked pork shoulder from the freezer. Greg added a couple of Sweet Heat peppers from the garden to the onions, which really helped tone down the sweetness of the lightly caramelized onions. Finished in a low oven with the pork. Excellent. 8/25/25

I combined this recipe with Caramelized Apples and Onions (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/6177-caramelized-apples-and-onions) and put everything in a covered roasting pan along with some peeled, cubed turnips before baking it for the 45 minutes or so. Absolutely delicious.
After roasting I always drizzle with balsamic vinegar!  Yummy!
I found balsamic ginger vinegar and added that. Zowie!
For me, the Balsamic vinegar (Or Asian rice vinegar) is almost mandatory.  I suppose that I could eat them w/o the vinegar, but why?

Love roasted brussels sprouts, as well as roasted every vegetable I can think of (with the exception of cucumbers).

One important caveat... the vegetables must not be touching each other, be sure to use a large enough baking pan that they can fit comfortably.
I always cut the Brussels sprouts in half unless they are tiny.  This gives a result resembling the illustration with the recipe, even though it is not mentioned.

When you cut them in half, more of the leaves come off. What do you do with them? It seems wasteful to throw them away, but if you roast them with the halves of the sprouts, they get burnt.

Or is there a trick to cutting them that eliminates this problem?

If I end up with more loose leaves that I want to toss, I add them to the roasting sheet for about the last 5-7 minutes of TOTAL roasting time and stir them a little every 60-90 seconds or so.  This does require frequent, intense supervision, but the results are worth the effort.  The only Brussels Sprout preparation that I've never cared for is the major component of a raw salad.
I add diced apples and curry powder for a superb result.
At what point do you add apples and curry powder?  In order to the curry powder to get blended well, I assume you mix it with the brussel sprouts to the pan when sauteing.
I was looking for a different way to roast brussels sprouts. Adding diced apples and curry powder is just that something. Thanks!

I microwaved (in oven safe corningware) first, so that sprouts were cooked through (about 4 minutes--I used the 2 baked potato setting) and then added the oil, kosher salt and regular pepper. (I didn't have hot pepper.) Then I browned by roasting about 8 minutes in 435 degree oven.

Recipe was delicious. And this is a way to modify it if you need it to be ready sooner than in 30-45 minutes.
fascinated so many 'helpful' - guess more than I imagined join ovens and microwaves...
Peanut oil is far superior to EVOO (or bacon fat) for this application.  Much higher smoking point, and the subtle nuttiness is a great foil for the flavor of the sprouts.
Or avocado oil

Actually peanut oil has a lower smoking point than EVOO - although I'm sure there are websites that extol the virtues of any oil that's not saturated. My favorite go-to oils for cooking are avocado and coconut, which I'm sure will leave most NY Times readers in a state of shock.
I have found putting the pan in the oven as it comes up to temperature makes it nice and hot and provides a great sear when adding the Brussels Sprouts.
Try pomegranate sauce drizzled five minutes prior to taking the Brussel Sprouts out of the oven...
Assume you mean pomegranate molasses? ...which I adore and which I just drizzled on my sprouts ---> delicious!
About the best recipe EVER for brussels sprouts.  I personally add some minced shallots and a little minced garlic, too, but that's just me gilding the, er, sprouts and not really essential.
I use lemon infused olive oil and lemon juice (just a squeeze) in this recipe. It is wonderful.

I've always loved brussels sprouts but this passion is not shared by my family. Roasting them is terrific and easy and everyone likes it. I think it takes less than 30-40 minutes, especially with smaller brussels sprouts which I prefer - they are less cabbagy.
So good. Crunchy outsides, ultra-tender insides. I use the fat from two cooked strips of bacon, which are chopped and cooled and used as bacon bits on the finished sprouts.
Cutting in half speeds roasting and helps in the caramelizing process. Skip the bacon fat. Olive oil and a touch of kosher salt. 25 minutes at 400 degrees is sufficient.
Drizzle with balsamic vinegar after roasting. Adds nice flavour.
What's not to love about this?  As a variation, toss the cooked sprouts with toasted hazelnuts just prior to serving.

Mr. Osinsski: I guess I'm a veggie purist, but limited additions to Brussels Sprouts are usually OK.  When roasting halves or quarters I generally stick with S&P plus a LITTLE butter or EVOO and a splash of Balsamic or light rice vinegar.  Ahem...  A holiday favorite that I eat year-round.  B.S. is a Most Favored veggie on my table and yes, a few chopped & toasted Hazel nuts is a brilliant idea.  Thank you!  -CG

We live in  "The almond capital of world" California's Central Valley
where walnut trees abound too...
Toasted nuts are  part of our bounty and cuisine....chop up some extra nuts and store in freezer container/bag for ready use...they will not go rancid ...do not buy already chopped nuts for same reason.
I roast them in duck fat and after roasting I toss them in a mix of sweet chili dipping sauce and balsamic. Oh yeah
Good, but fyi - Bittman's NYT version halves the sprouts and oven time.
I always cut them in half and use the cast iron skillet stove top .. When seared to a nice crisp I Toss in balsamic glaze.. and then garnish with  Feta crumbles!!! AH-MAZING!
I squeezed lemon over the sprouts once they were out of the oven and then tossed with some grated parmesan. It definitely added another dimension to the dish.

Knowing that my gas oven cooks a little hot, I lowered the temp to 375 and decreased the cook time to 25 min. Gave them a good shake every five minutes. After 20 min, I was concerned they looked overdone. Pulled them out and while the char didn’t bother me that much, they were unpleasantly soft. 

I can’t imagine roasting these at 400 for 30 min.
Maybe 350° or less time? Ended up with blackened brussels sprouts.
@Diane K. Martin same here.
Instead of EVOO, which has a fairly low smoke point, I use avocado oil for this type of roasting.  It has a very high smoke point and a good taste.  This avoids a smoky kitchen and messy oven.

This was a good, basic recipe.  Bacon fat gave the sprouts a subtle smoky flavor, which was enhanced by the IKEA oak smoked salt I used with them.  They were all nicely browned, just as in the photo.  I just put the loose leaves on the sheet with the whole or halved sprouts; I happen to like when these leaves become extra crispy.  Next time I'd like to try this with similarly sized baby potatoes added.
I love those crispy leaves, too!

Made brussel sprouts last night using the exact same method. When they were hot out of the oven the brussels were tossed with a 3 to 1 dressing of miso to maple syrup. Added just a touch of vinegar and voila.
easy peasy recipe. Can improvise much with this recipe
Dribbling with lime juice is another great tweak. And pan frying over medium high heat rather than firing the oven.  All good!
I also halve them before roasting and in addition to the oil I toss them with pecorino romano cheese which adds so much flavor as a crispy coating!
LOVED it! this will be the way to prepare brussels sprouts from now on. period.
Two people mentioned that the recipe didn’t say to cut them in half but it does say that in step 2. Read!!

Love these. Followed instructions but added balsamic vinegar and maple syrup to cooked sprouts.
A perfect combination. Try it.

This is now my ONLY recipe for Brussels sprouts--with the possible exception of an old family fave that calls for sautéing 'em with bacon, diced apples, and walnuts (I have 2 mos. to make up my mind on that little issue)!
And speaking of little, the smaller the better--so look for the 1/2 kilo green net bags of baby Brussels, imported I THINK from Belgium (or the Netherlands, which is close enough).

Just love this recipe.  I added a small amount of sugar to get a bit of sweetness during the roasting.  Then I added ras el hanout to give some Moroccan flavors.  Topped it off with shaved fennel for crunch.  Bottom line: a GREAT PERFECT base recipe from Mr Sifton that we all can enjoy with our own improvising! Enjoy fellow cooks!
I'm 42 years old and this is the first time I've ever enjoyed eating brussel sprouts. I added a bit of red pepper to mine. Now I'm hooked!

Sam: roasting is always favored method, but LIGHT steaming, served w/ S&P and a splash of good Balsamic or light rice vinegar is also wonderful. Easting them since the mid-60s, I had No Idea there were too many recipes to save, but now know where they are! Ms. Roman's primer and lists are great.
Another favorite, not mentioned and labor intensive is to trim, separate individual leaves and steam Very Briefly as above. Roasting leaves is also great, but a High Risk method. Great Sect!!

Cut sprouts in half.

Add diced apples and curry powder for a superb result.
I add fried onions and green chillies (both cut in long strands), and garlic to my roasted Brussel sprouts, and some dried cranberries - this makes it colorful and gives it a some pizazz!
I have done this basic. Bacon drippings is the best flavor. I sometimes sprinkle Parmesan cheese on them
This was dry. Cook some before roasting?  We added garlic.

Awesomely good, even without extra seasonings.  We did cut the sprouts in half and reduce the cooking time, ended with wonderful caramelization.  Even the supposedly veggie hating kid was stealing from the cooking pan.

I cut the Brussels sprouts in half because the ones I purchased were fairly large, turning over after 15 minutes in the oven. Used Spike's all-purpose seasoning rather than salt and the result was delicious.

You forgot to mention cutting the brussels sprouts in half prior to drizzling. Sure, the photo depicts them halved, but NYT's recipes should articulate each step in the preparation process and should not assume that the reader will make the leap of faith based on what can be inferred from the photo.
Very good.  Be sure to use enough sprouts to account for oven shrinkage.  30 sprouts was NOT enough for 4 people.

I used the 2 tbl of bacon fat because we had it, split the brussels sprouts as one comment suggested and cooked  in cast iron skillet in small countertop oven hours before dinner - then reheated later in same skillet as steaks came off the grill. The household eaters want them again next week!
Half brussel sprouts unless tiny.
I cooked one batch for 30 minutes and they were burnt to a crisp. Ovens vary. I suggest 17 minutes as a time to check for doneness.
I like to start in an iron skillet on the stove top, then put the skillet into the over to roast for the remaining time- can get that softness without overcooking them and losing that pretty green.

I too cut the Brussels sprouts in half before tossing them with olive oil and salt and pepper. Put them face down on parchment on a baking sheet spaced apart;turned after 10 minutes; cooked another 10-12 minutes. They were wonderful.
These came out so great they got eaten before the Thanksgiving feast and I had to make more. I'm trying tonight with some Parmesan once cooked.
Have also roasted butternut squash squares at the same time. Some other recipe alerted me to the fact that they both take the same time to cook!
This recipe was great! Like others I did not quite need the 40 minutes. I should’ve made two sheets and doubled the recipe because they were an amazing leftover from the Thanksgiving, 2022 feast!
Perfect!  I made the other NYT recipe where you pan fry the brussel sprouts to crisp them up but these are easier and taste no different!
I found that 30 minutes at 400 degrees turned my sprouts into mush.  I will try again with a shorter roasting time.

A nice weekday recipe. But for Thanksgiving Day, perhaps a little gilding on the lily would go amiss.
Learned this many years ago while waitressing during college.
1. Shred large bag of brussel sprouts - about a pound. 
2. Roast over medium heat 1/3 c of diced pancetta. After fat is rendered, add sprouts. 
3. When sprouts are deeply caramelized, add 1 tablespoon of good quality Scotch. Cook until liquid is absorbed, a few minutes.
4. Finish with a few tablespoons of heavy cream. Heat and serve
Bacon fat makes the difference as does a drizzle of balsamic. I like to add red grapes (halved) and toss before serving. Works well at room temp.

Perfect recipe for Brussel Sprouts.  I wouldn't change a thing but I like to add the outer leaves(oiled and salted) 10 minutes before the sprouts are ready to come out of oven.  The leaves get warm and a little crispy and are a nice addition.

It is a few days after Thanksgiving and I made this recipe for dinner tonight. Sam Sifton's recipe is something I'm thankful for. I used to only get Brussels sprouts when they were on the menu at a restaurant. Now I can prepare them at home for my family whenever I want. This recipe is easy and delicious.  I used to wait around until I had a strip of bacon or two, now I know that they are delicious with just olive oil and salt and pepper. It is too bad that recipe authors don't receive royalties

excellent
Delish!
Cut large sprouts in half and they burned - either leave whole or reduce time in oven

I cook halved brussel sprouts with their round side down on parchment paper at 425, until bottom round side is nicely browned/blackened, about 10 minutes. Then, I steam them for another 5 minutes or so, to rehydrate/tenderize their core, which is too chewy for me if you don't steam.
cutting them, yes, but rest too labor intensive - see above
add om some maple syrup or light brown sugar

I gave this a try yesterday and had a few recipe adjustments I’ll try next time. Fantastic sprouts - added carrots at end to roast for Easter 2020! Used olive oil but next time will try bacon grease
I toss Brussels sprouts with garlic, carrots, and walnuts - salt and pepper and roast high heat 30 minutes...delicious every time!!

I don't understand the appeal of burnt brussels sprouts. Cruciferous vegetables are bitter to begin with. Roasting them "until crisp", i.e., burning them , only makes them more unpalatable. When steamed, boiled, or microwaved brussels sprouts become sweeter, softer, more digestible, and better able to incorporate flavors from additional ingredients. This fad will pass. People still want food that tastes good.

I love this recipe from Sam Sifton. Another one of my all time favorite NYT recipes is Corrine Trang's White Bean and Avocado Salad with Garlic Oil. One of the key elements of her recipe is an easy to make garlic oil. The genius of her recipe is that it yields both an infused oil and crispy garlic chips. I recommend making the garlic oil using her method and then tossing the Sprouts in the infused oil instead of plain EVOO before roasting. Right before serving, garnish with the garlic chips.
Cook for 20 instead of 30 min
30 minutes was a bit long in my oven. Will check more frequently and probably stop around 25 min next time. They were still edible, especially with a little balsamic vinegar.
I microwave the brussels for 2 mins then cut in half. They turn out so tender and crisp

Followed instructions per recipe with a few mods: subbed out oil for bacon fat, and finished with local raw honey instead of balsamic. Served alongside sustainably sourced wild-caught Alaskan Sockeye Salmon with a miso-sake marinade, and short grain brown rice. Winner dinner.

Terrific recipe. Do cut all but the smallest sprouts in half; really large ones should be quartered. Also, you can vary the seasonings — I substituted ground cumin for the black pepper (no red pepper here) and it was excellent.

I make something similar. I don't use bacon fat but olive oil (garlic infused if I have it). I add a heaping tsp of minced garlic and a healthy dose of onion powder and marinate in a plastic bag for a couple of hours then bake as above. And I don't use the red pepper flakes. Easy and perfect every time.
Wow it didn’t take mine more than 10 minutes for the sprouts to be tender and browned. 30 minute s is WAY too much.

much easier just to roast and I get very satisfactory results.  I don't like frying and this was no exception.

My sprouts, even cut in half, needed the full 30 minutes. Beautiful result! I garnished with a drizzle of reduced Basalmic.
Little effort for great result.

I soaked my Brussels in salt water for about:30 minutes before I roasted them at 425 for 25 minutes. Next time I’ll cut the roast time down to 20 minutes. I did not cook them in the pan, I only added them as instructed at the end of the gnocchi cooking.

This is a really good recipe.
I tossed my brussels sprouts with olive oil and finely chopped fresh garlic. Added fried fresh sage leaves part way through roasting. Loved the idea to sprinkle balsamic vinegar before serving. Yum!
Perfect prep and roasting instructions. We ate them all up in about 5 minutes so they didn't even make it to the table. Will double the recipe next time.
Nice, very mild. Consider garlic, balsamic vinegar, other.
I like to glaze them with maple syrup. Together with anything bacon bits or bacon fat, makes them taste better than any candy.

I've been making this for years. No need to shake the pan. Place the brussels sprouts halves face down on a sheet of foil (I like the non-stick type) & roast for 20 to 25 minutes at 400 degrees; remove them from the oven when the tops have become quite brown; the bottoms of the halves will be nicely browned. BTW, use plenty of olive oil and let the halves soak it up for a half an hour or more, stirring them often. And consider the red pepper flakes to be entirely optional (I never use them).
I cheat and steam them first, then pop them under a broiler.

These were absolutely delicious. I used only heated reserved bacon fat, poured it over the halved sprouts in a bowl, and seasoned with garlic pepper and salt. Baked in a convection oven at 375 degrees and it went pretty quick. Done in about 15-20 minutes. I put all of the sprout halves flat side down for the first 10 minutes and that seemed to steam them inside so they were nice and tender throughout when done, and the convection air crisped up some of the outer leaves very nicely.
I often drizzle hot honey or a spicy maple syrup on my sprouts - takes the bitterness out.  Nutmeg is also a good addition. Balsamic glaze when served is my 3rd go-to.
At 30 minutes, a little too charred.  For my oven, lower the heat to 375F or start checking as early as 20 minutes.

I have cooked this dish many times and it’s always a hit. On occasion I sprinkle the brussel sprouts with pistachio nuts for a little extra crunch. Also for additional pop I dust the sprouts with Parmesan cheese after removing the tray from the oven.
Wonder if I can roast just before leaving the house and keep warm and transport in slow cooker?
Nice, simple recipe. Great results!
Delicious and super easy. I have always eaten brussels sprouts out of a sense of duty, but these actually taste great, none left on the plate!

I enjoy this enough that I use it as my standard formula for roasting sprouts, which I do weekly or so. I find my oven is strong enough that only 22ish minutes of roasting at 400 are required or advisable. I've made this recipe several times and for me, it takes less time,  more like 20 - 25 minutes. Be watchful of your brussel sprouts, as size matters, and remove them at your desired state.

This time I inadvertently bought a bag of very finely sliced Brussels sprouts (instead of halved). Followed recipe but needed only 12 minutes at 400. Delish!

Despite its simplicity, this is one of the worst recipes ever published by the NYT Food Section (and I am a huge fan).  At 25 minutes my sprouts were charred almost beyond recognition.  Luckily I had bought extra.  I did a second pass on the recipe at 15 minutes (half of the low end of the recommended cooking time) and they were on the verge of overdone.  And lest readers think my oven is off, I am an avid sourdough baker and get it calibrated frequently. So roast your sprouts accordingly…..

stats.Total Time: 10 minutes, plus at least 12 hours' brining

comments.commentBody: Listen, all these turkey brine recipes are nonsense.  The only thing that matters is the brine -- salt water and sugar.  All the added tidbits are USESLESS!!!  99.99% of tasters cannot notice the 3 rosemary sprigs, the bay leaves and other tiny bits of herbs.  I brined my fresh turkey breast for 22 hours and smoked it on my Green Egg at 250 degrees till internal temp was 162.  By far the most moist and flavorful turkey I have ever had.
Adding orange peel makes it taste even better!

Add a cup of Jack Daniels to this brine for a rich depth of flavor.  There is no alcohol flavor in the turkey; it just gives a richer, moister flavor from the brining process, also rehydrating my frozen turkey.

Used this recipie this  year. Did  not realize the the effect this recipe would have on the drippings for gravy. The salt and herbs made my drippings unusable. Far too salty and the herbs destroyed the traditional gravy flavor. The turkey meat, howver,  was very tender, but not worth the trade off.

@William Nelson. I have made this recipes a few times now. The first year I also found it too salty. I realized by the fallowing year that I had not rinsed the turkey well enough after bringing. So I really really rinsed it and dried it well and it was perfect. Very juicy and I got a crispy skin. The drippings added to the gravy were fine. I only add thyme and lemon to the brine. I’m not sure if it does anything. I also add a compound butter under and over the skin, and stuff it full of lemon and veggies. It just makes me feel better

I agree with the fussy-ness comment.  The key ingredients are salt and sugar.  Note that the ratio of salt to water is approximately a scant 1/4 cup salt to 1 quart water.  If you have a bigger bird and need more brine to cover you can increase the brine using the above ratio and a bit more sugar.  If you have any of the other ingredients laying around and want to add them well,.....what the heck, throw them in, I've tried everything from orange to juniper berry and can't tell the difference.

Absolutely perfect.  I put the bird and this brine in an oven bag on my porch for the night.  Brought it in and used the Orange and Sage recipe. One of the best Thanksgiving turkeys I’ve had. I found a smaller bird...only 9 pounds.

Used 2 cups of Morton's Kosher Salt for overnight brine, 18 pound farm fresh turkey, and was a tad too salty. Next time I would go down to 1 1/4 cup. Substituted regular sugar for brown and added fresh ginger cubes + peppercorns. I will use this recipe again.
Add in orange peel.  Brine should be simmered (not to boil) for 10 minutes.  Cooled to room temp before turkey is added.
I ran out of kosher salt and used sea salt to finish - we sure puckered up on that one!  As one commentor said - watch out if is a lot saltier than kosher salt - I'd cut the amount in half!
Does it matter if I use sea salt instead of Kosher salt?

Yes, it does. Kosher salt has a different crystalline structure and is much less salty than sea salt. You will want to use much less sea salt in the recipe. You can likely google it, but I found that 3/4 t. sea salt is equivalent to 1 t kosher salt.
Substitute apple cider for the sugar. Add Orange peel.

First time brining, added equivalent salt by weight, 1/5 recipe as I was making a breast roll.  I left it in too long, 3 days and was like a ham.  
Follow directions for 24 hours or halve the salt.  It was as salty as sliced ham.  
I don’t think I’ll do the herbs next time as it resembled, with its saltiness, Jimmy Dean Breakfast sausage.
Wally from Fairhope, Yes. Except: all that bay leaf and thyme etc. makes the house smell wonderful ;)
I will try to rotisserie the Turkey -low heat -it's small-will let you know.
citrus and  apple cider  go along  with salt and brown sugar  recipe
Might be a silly question but do I rinse the brine off with water and pat dry before roasting?

Yes, typically you are instructed to wash it off and pat it dry. In my experience, by the time I have mine trussed, stuffed, and positioned on the rack, the moisture has mostly evaporated anyway. If a few peppercorns, etc., are left on the skin, all the better.

I agree that many herbs do not "show up" in the final flavor of the roasted bird. But, if you simmer the ingredients for a good 20 minutes, then cool the solution before brining, the flavors will infuse the skin of the turkey. Also, if I use juniper berries, then I can taste their piney flavor throughout the turkey. It's mild, but definitely present.
I recommend adding a teaspoon of allspice and about 6 cloves of crushed garlic to this brine.

Been making Canlis Salad from original recipe from WAY back in late 70's.  This is a little different and a lot better!  Omitting bacon still makes a great salad - use some olive oil to toast crutons. Noting some don't want to use coddled egg yolk (but it is safe to do), try Lidia Bastianich's trick of using a hard boiled yolk in blender with other dressing ingredients.  You can get by with @ 1.5 tsp dry oregano instead of fresh; whisk into dressing.  Fresh mint is essential.
@dimmerswitch, would you be able to send the original recipe? Much appreciated!! Thanks!

Amazing salad.
That said, it is time consuming. Home cooks don't have kitchen help prepping, slicing and dicing. There is no way said salad is ready in 10 minutes. Try as I might I barely got it on the table in 45, and I know my way around pots and pans.

As a Seattleite my family has made this salad for years. At the end of the day, while it seems straightforward, you really cannot omit anything from the recipe - it is very finely balanced. If you take out the bacon, the dressing isn't salty enough. If you take out the fresh mint and oregano, it doesn't have much tang. If you take out the croutons, it doesn't have much heft. Agree with others - rub the bowl with garlic.
One thing they do at Canlis, which isn't mentioned here: before adding any ingredients, slice a garlic clove in half and gently rub the cut side along the inside of the salad bowl.

This is the perfect summer salad for dinner for a non- vegetarian crowd. The lemon and mint just make this sing with flavor. A brilliant dressing. One of my favorite salad recipes ever.
Make this right away!

The directions here state 'whisk vigorously to emulsify' but I'm reading an Eater article from 2013 about the Canlis salad where Jason Franey, the executive chef, says "The dressing is not an emulsion, but rather it is 'broken'". This would explain why the directions are to add the coddled egg to the oil and lemon juice instead of slowly adding the oil as one normally does when emulsifying a dressing. 

In short, don't worry if your dressing doesn't emulsify: it's not supposed to!
A delicious “10 minute” salad after you’ve cleaned and chopped the vegetables, prepared the bacon and croutons, and made the dressing.
Delicious, but 10 minutes is a joke--more like 45 for me!

I live in Seattle but I can't afford to eat at Canlis, so intead I've made this salad many times since it first appeared in the NYTimes magazine. To turn it into a main dish, I grill a chicken breast and lay slices on top of the salad.
This is a time-consuming but incredibly wonderful salad. I think it's the mint, which makes all the difference. And who can turn down bacon AND croutons. I don't add oregano unless it's summer.
This is an excellent salad, and almost as delicious even if you leave out the bacon (say, for vegetarian dinner guests).
Garlic bowl rub

This salad is delicious. The fresh oregano is key and wonderful.
However, it does take longer than 10 minutes to make.
That’s 10 minutes of COOK time. Prep time could be 45 minutes.
One thing they do at Canlis, which isn't mentioned here: before adding any ingredients, slice a garlic clove in half and gently rub the cut side along the inside of the salad bowl.
Sometimes I have to make a recipe simply out of curiosity.  Mint with bacon? Really?  Well, I am happy to say REALLY.  This is delicious!

Made this for Mother’s Day as per the recipe. It was so delicious that everyone had second helpings of salad while we waited for the chicken to rest. It seems like a lot of mint, but seriously, don’t skimp the mint! All the flavors work so well in tandem that to change the incredients in any amount will throw everything off balance. When I made the dressing and tasted it prior to dressing the salad, it seemed not very flavorful. Trust the recipe and it all makes sense.

I’m not an American with a morbid fear of dying if eating a non- refrigerated egg ...even using an egg that HAS been kept in the frig because of the ( unecessary) FDA mandate to wash ‘em commercially before being sold [which ironically robs the egg of its natural protection against deterioration]..my serious question to my fellow NYT cooks is this : from the view of taste/ texture, what’s the point of the UNEXPLAINED coddling ( sic) the egg for just 1 minute ..it’s basically still raw is it not?
Great salad! Will make again. Seems like a lot of dressing though, I was able to dress two heads of Romain perfectly with just half of the dressing.

Once, when we served this to company, we passed the dressing around in a gravy boat so guests could choose how much to use. One guest, on tasting the dressing, poured most of it on his salad and asked us to bring out "the rest of it." Everyone else had to get by with just a little, but he rejoiced in having too much. On other occasions we've seen guests finish off the last drops of dressing in their bowl with a spoon. Canlis quantity, it seems is a de gustibus matter.

Really liked the look of this recipe when I saw it and wanted to have it straight away!  But no bread in the house so we diced a baking potato and roasted in a little oil as a substitute to croutons.  It worked really well.
Is it spearmint or peppermint or some other mint?
I've got both, and prefer spearmint in savory dishes like this.  Peppermint is very assertive, works better in desserts.

Have also been making this salad for years...& printing out the recipe every time you mention Canlis.  My tattered card from the restaurant has gone missing (again).  Hopefully it will turn up (again) the next time I decide to serve whatever meal's recipes it last accompanied.  The spotted & stained Canlis card invariably shows up as a marker in one of too many cookbooks.  I've tried variations over the years, but seem to always go back to the original.  Thanks for the 2021 update.

I was nervous about the coddled egg but made it exactly as directed and everything turned out great. I made this salad for 12 and it took me 2 hours. Delicious. I didn’t have enough mint and substituted basil. I think more mint would have been better.

I made this salad to take to a friend's dinner party.  Very labor intensive, but very good. So many lovely flavors and textures.  I put all the prepared ingredients in separate containers so nothing would get droopy or mushy before dinner and I could assemble just before serving, being very mindful not to forget a single little thing.  So I forgot a big thing:  the lettuce.  This was the best panzanella salad I've ever had.
Ouch! Honestly, I could just eat the croutons, so yours would be delicious too!

The bacon doesn't look chopped, but I suspect that is the work of a food stylist.

I use an easier way to incorporate an egg into dressings. Whisk the olive oil, lemon juice and raw egg together in a small glass bowl. Place the bowl into a microwave oven and heat for 10 seconds. Remove the bowl from the microwave and whisk the contents together. Place the bowl back into the microwave and repeat this process at 5 second intervals. Within 45 seconds you'll have a smooth creamy base.
Good technique for 'pasteurizing' eggs.  Dressing a bit bland, needs more zing and flavor, perhaps an addition of your favorite hot sauce.

I am always in the look out for a good, easy, yet complex salad for dinner.  At first leery of the short time the egg cooked, i plunged in.  I agree with Mecross, a little hot sauce can be a good addition.
Replace the Romano with feta. Yum.
An egg cooked for only 60 seconds is a health hazard.
You could use pasteurized eggs.
use pasteurized eggs. safeeggs.com has a list of grocers selling the eggs.

When first published this was called CANLIS' Special Salad originated by CANLIS CHARCOAL BROILER RESTAURANT in Honolulu. And, yes, 'rubbing the salad bowl with cut garlic after adding olive oil and salt is in that description !
This is a hit every time I serve it.  It is our favorite.  The mint and the croutons salted in the bacon drippings makes it delectable.  The dressing is the perfect compliment to every ingredient.
It was a delicious salad. I have eaten it an Canlis, and I must say, mine turned out pretty close to the original.
I made this today for dinner for 13, and every man and woman raved about it.  I too added a little Dijon mustard.  Will make this again.

use some olive oil to toast crutons. Noting some don't want to use coddled egg yolk (but it is safe to do), try Lidia Bastianich's trick of using a hard boiled yolk in blender with other dressing ingredients. You can get by with @ 1.5 tsp dry oregano instead of fresh; whisk into dressing. Fresh mint is essential.

My first Canlis Salad was almost 40 years ago!  This is the 1st time I've made it and it won't be my last.  I followed a few of the suggestions in others comments like using 1.5 tsp dried oregano if you don't have fresh.  It's interesting how all of the flavors in this salad meld together  In my opinion use all of the ingredients as they make for a most unusual and flavorful salad!  And don't second guess the dressing.   It works!!  Enjoy!

I bake bacon, a package at a time and freeze it. (+/-15 minutes at 400, checking it and pouring off the grease at about 12 minutes.) I save some of the bacon fat for use in recipes.  Having bacon and bacon fat ready to use would speed up this recipe.
We have always rubbed the bowl with anchovy paste.
Someone said, "Time consuming." Never thought that. In any event, worth it.
I thought I did this perfectly but when I crack the egg into the mixed oil and lemon it looked pretty much like a regular egg. Shouldn’t have looked a bit whiter?

So simple, so good. Made the recipe tonight - this is a perfect summer meal. I added one clove of crushed garlic to the dressing. And I topped the salad with grilled scallops and prawns. Amazing! Definitely going into our regular rotation.

Oh Canlis salad, why didn’t I give you a try sooner?  I’d scanned the recipe a few times, but nothing about it jumped out at me.  Cue day 90-something of quarantine, and, finding almost all the ingredients available in my house (we didn’t have fresh oregano so I added a pinch of dried oregano to the dressing), I gave this vaunted recipe a try.  Believe the hype. Nom nom nom.

When I coddled the egg, it seemed like a lot of the white stuck to the inside of the shell. Was I supposed to scrape that out, like you do when you eat a soft-boiled egg? That would add eggwhite solids to the salad, which I don't see. But otherwise, you don't get much egg. Or am I missing something?

This is a great salad, especially the dressing.  I made it twice, once adding shrimp, the other time salmon. I also add some shredded cabbage, just because I like the crunch and the additional texture.
Agree! Roasted and cubed salmon, in particular, marries well with a Canlis salad.

The recipe calls for cubed fresh Italian bread, yet the salad pictured clearly shows torn baguette slices.  I used a baguette stub I had handy, sliced it, basted with a mixture of the bacon fat and extra virgin olive oil, baked at 350F for 15 minutes to crisp, and then tore the slices before adding to the salad.
This recipe is updated from one that Puget Sounders have used for many years. Thanks for the nod.

If you go to Canlis's website, then follow the link to Canlis Community College to the videos, you can watch the family make this. They actually had a bit of a disagreement with what was printed here. It's also just a fun video to watch.

I substituted smoked salmon for the bacon. Omitted the croutons.  Added 1/2 c edamame.  In dressing I  didn’t have any lemon juice so used rice vinegar.  Used grated parmesan rather than romano cheese as that’s what I had.  The salad was fantastic!!!!   The dressing was wonderful.

This is a delicious salad. If there are ANY doubts regarding technique, original recipe, ingredients, etc., watch the video of the family making it. If you can get through it. Even between the family members, there seems to be differing opinions of amounts and technique.  Because Sam’s “riff” is so darn good, I roll with it and question nothing!
Absolutely delicious, and a meal for two. You can use the extra tomatoes and bread to make a few bruschetta.
Love this salad. I add a clove of garlic to the dressing, and extra bacon.
This is an amazing salad. The mint and oregano totally make it. That said, this only takes 10 minutes if you have an in-home sous chef to do all the time-consuming prep before you start assembling.
Amazing!!
Absolutely delicious. I was out of bacon, so had to take a pass on that & made croutons in olive oil with a little smoked paprika instead. Salad was scrumptious. Going into the summer repertoire.

My favorite salad, and has been for 60 years.  One personal comment to those not familiar with the Canlis restaurant’s original recipe.  The amount of bacon was originally 1 lb., and too much for today’s taste.  But, I disagree the 4 strips in this recipe.  It’s not enough for the original flavor.  My adjustment is a compromise of .5 a lb., or 7-8 strips.

Pecorino 

No oregano 

Pancetta not bacon 

Less lemon juice
A delicious salad in regular rotation.

The dressing was very runny- it didn't emulsify like I would have expected. I may try using my immersion blender next time. The herbs were a great addition. I was worried the mint would overpower but it was just great.
Think the note about more lemon and mint adds the zinger
lovely
Delicious, simple, filling.I've made this salad many times.
Since we are gluten free, I didn't make the croutons, and they were missed! Used fresh parmesan instead of romano, couldn't find fresh oregano. Good, but not a stand out.

Outstanding! Very light and flavorful. I served this with the Cognac and Dijon Stew recipe also found on this site. The citrus and mint flavors were a very refreshing complement next to the savory, rich stew.
Can this dressing be made a day in advance?
delicious, used fresh herbs from my garden and the very last tomatoes
Really great non-fancy, simple yet delicious salad that is a perfect accompaniment to many entrees.  Also great with soup or a sandwich.
Is the coddled egg missing from the ingredient list?
Can you leave the egg out of the dressing?

Cooking outside the US means improvising in some surprising ways for me, but this round was a happy substitution. I couldn't find Romano cheese and ended up using Manchego, of all things, and it was still delicious. This recipe is perfect as written but also lends itself to some tweaking--best of both worlds.
Great salad combination. I have to say- not my favorite dressing. Not impressive. I recommend a Dijon vinaigrette of sorts though I’m sure Mr Sifton is just trying to be true to the original.

We had a Canlis restaurant in Portland 50 years ago atop a Hilton hotel.  We went fairly often and I remember this salad being my father's favorite. At home he would replicate it as the waiter had made it by the table.  He didn't use mint leaves or oregano as I remember but started by sprinkling salt in the wooden bowl and then rubbing a fresh clove of cut garlic all around.  Then the rest of the ingredients listed--egg, olive oil and lemon---and cheese--probably parmesan.  I don't remember baco

I’d not had any success with finding fresh oregano- but took heart reading a fellow cook Dimmerswitch’s  advice dried leaves work well...& they sure did!

Raw egg was perfectly fine in making the dressing - forget the overworked fear of imminent death by insisting coddling in the USA - & I felt absolutely confident tweeking the amounts of leaves/ herbs etc "..mint VERY important to a great outcome though & this must not be ignored...the time needed to achieve the bacon/fried bread bit worthwhil!

I've had the Canlis Salad at Canlis, and this home made was even better. What a really refreshing salad to have for dinner on a summer night. I made it almost exactly as the recipe stated, except I tossed the bread cubes with a little olive oil instead and toasted in oven. I think it could be good without bacon, maybe some salty toasted pepitas? I'm so glad to have this recipe, thanks.
For those concerned with using raw egg - if you have a sous vide you can pasturize the whole egg by heating the water to 140, then put the egg in for 3 1/2 minutes.
This is also good with round Parmesan croutons instead of bread croutons.

Expected to love this because it includes most of my favorite things, but found the mint overwhelming despite being a mint lover. Used all home grown produce and took the recommendation to rub garlic on the bowl. Will try again at the height of summer heirloom tomato season with a little less mint or a different variety.

Took me over an hour to prepare, largely because my croutons were "restaurant style" and needed nearly 40min to harden over low heat. Next time will cut smaller.

This is not the original recipe. The croutons are tossed in melted garlic butter,  salt, pepper, dried oregano then baked (delicious). The bacon is cut 3/8" thick & then into cubes put in a sauté pan with 1/4" water cooked until water evaporates and bacon is browned. Preportions for dressing is one to one, much more lemony. Saveur has recipe.
More mint, more oregano, more mint, more oregano.

My husband and I love this recipe!  It is a frequent addition to meals in our house.  I cook it as printed but truthfully I have not mastered the coddled egg part.  I do not let this deter me.  Canlis on!

My parents and I (as a pre-teen) picked up this recipe at the long-gone Honolulu Canlis. We travelled to Hawaii several times during the 50's and early 60's, where Canlis was always on our menu. Since then, the salad has always been my "house dressing"--and now the go-to dressing of our own kids. I confess that over the years I've descended to using garlic salt but otherwise the recipe stays the same.
Love this salad, but I'm gluten free so I crisp up corn tortillas instead of bread  to use as croutons.  Salad dressing is great for other salads as well.
A delicious, refreshing salad with surprising flavors that really go well together.  Worth the time and work to make it.
Very nice. I did use a  blender to emulsify the dressing. Perfect.
I was not at all impressed.

Delicious. We paired it with grilled chicken for a perfect dinner on a hot night. I ended up using lime juice because my lemons disappeared (summer and kids!) but it was still really really good. Will definitely become a summer staple.
I have had this salad at Canlis a few times over the years and always loved it, so was happy to get the recipe. I actually left out the croutons and the bacon, and it was still delicious!
We thought that the dressing was too tart. There are many other better salads out there. We won't be making this one again.

I followed the directions and took the advice of other reviews about adding more herbs. I doubled the cheese and added parmesan. It was a light dish, that was just ok. It would be great with sausage and some spinach or arugula and more spice.
half the dressing; cook the egg for two minutes

My father got this recipe from the headwaiter of the Honolulu Canlis (long-gone) in the late 1950s. Since then it has been our family's company salad dressing for what is now three generations. We use a whole-egg liquid from our grocery to replace the coddled egg.
Rub cut half of garlic around inside of salad bowl.
LOVE this dressing! And the salad. Plan to have it again tomorrow!

I've wanted to make this salad for quite some time and finally did last night. I was disappointed. The croutons didn't have much flavor other than bacon grease. Seemed like too much work for the results. The mint was a nice touch though and I'll remember than in my next green salad.

Serving portions are HUGE! Made 1/2 recipe and was easily enough for side salad for four. 
Delicious ... even though my egg didn’t really coddle that well. Next time bring egg to room temp ...
I made it last night and my wife said it was the best salad she’d ever had. We used half the lettuce and it was our main dish.
Plan for 45ish minutes from prep to plate unless you have an extra set of helping hands! That being said, this salad was one of the best I’ve made.

This is a wonderful salad! Went perfectly with a strip loin roast, Parmesan crusted potatoes and spinach Madeline (an excellent throwback recipe— please look it up! Make the day before and bake the day of. So good.). Even the 10-year old went back for seconds of this salad. Will make again!

Subbed speck for bacon.  

A lot of cheese- sort of like a carbonara salad with the egg.

Years ago when I lived in Seattle, Canlis was a "special occasion" restaurant for us. I remember this salad and just made it again. Wow, really good, and I'm not usually a salad guy. The bacon fat croutons and the herbs make the dish.

Used one small romaine lettuce. 4 rindless bacon rashers. 

Served with RecipeTinEats poached chicken (poached with lemon). Very good dinner for a hot summer night.

I’ve had this on my list of recipes to try for a long time and finally made it today. Overall, I was underwhelmed. Bacon was the predominant flavor, even though I didn’t use all of it. The dressing was the biggest surprise to me: adding the coddled egg made it so thin—not at all clear what the egg is really doing there. I thought it would make the consistency thicker, like a Cesar-style dressing (which sounded great!) but it didn’t. Meh.
Added salmon
Slice the bread as shown in the picture, not cubed as described in the ingredients
What kind of mint?
@Nola  I associate spearmint with savory dishes. Its the mint most often used in middle eastern cuisine. Id strongly suggest using it here.
For those concerned about the perils of eating coddled eggs, I've been drinking eggnog for eighty years, made with raw eggs, milk and vanilla.
How long does the dressing keep in the fridge?

If using lemon juice from a bottle, go easy. The croutons are divine. If using turkey bacon, add a little olive oil afterwards for the toasting. Hard boiled egg in a blender was great. Couldn’t stomach the coddled egg. Adding an extra hard boiled egg or two to the salad would also be good to make it more of a meal itself.
Really nice along side a grilled skirt steak. But if you are wondering, yes, this salad is a meal unto itself.
Can the dressing and croutons be prepared ahead of time and then assembled right before eating?

I'm on the fence. The dressing ,as written missed the mark for us,, so we added a bit of Dijon & loved it. 
Croutons in the Tablespoon of bacon grease was a decadent treat, but It felt naughty. Our bacon slices were slight,  so I'd love a weight measurement to taste what you taste....for me this salad tasted a little too ' bacony '. 

I picked all produce from our garden, so I was thrilled with the amount of mint and oregano. This was a fun deviation from our classic Joy of Cooking Caesar & any chance to step out to the potager's  garden...I am thrilled! 

Thank you for your art!
Would adding an anchovy give umami?
I must have done something wrong. It needed much more flavor from my perspective.
How far in advance can you make the dressing for the salad? Like could I make it earlier in the day before we are serving it?

This was DELICIOUS, I made it for a dinner party last night and everyone loved it. It did take me almost an hour (I'm not speedy in the kitchen), but it is an easy recipe if you can plan for it. ! Made it exactly as written, the flavors are so good; the fresh mint and oregano take it to the next level.  I want to make it again today.

This is my favorite salad! The mint and oregano are the perfect balance to the bacon infused croutons and bits. I don’t think you can put in too much herbs. This is recipe wheee using the fresh oregano really makes a difference.
Took this to a dinner party last night- it is fantastic and everyone loved. I will make it again. Followed recipe exactly as written.

title: Tofu and Green Beans With Chile Crisp Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: “Best tofu I’ve ever had!” declared hubby, and I agree: it’s perfection!  Instead of fussing with towels and plates and weights to drain the tofu, I took another reader’s tip (from a Japanese cooking show) and microwaved the sliced tofu in a bowl for three minutes on high.  Amazing how much water drained out, leaving the tofu with perfect texture to soak up the delicious marinade.
@SLCatherine. I make several dishes with tofu. You saved me time and love the note on absorbing the marinade.
@SLCatherine this trick is genius, thank you!

@SLCatherine    Yours may be the most useful comment in the entire universe of NYT Cooking comments (and goodness knows there are so many helpful comments)!  Brilliant!  Thank you for sharing this technique.
I've been using jars and jars of Trader Joe's "Chili Onion Crunch" that I now realize is a knockoff of the chile-crisp described here. There's nothing better on eggs, tofu, shrimp, rice....
@Randy is it worth doing it with the Trader Joe's version?
@AlxBeattie the TJs version is a little less spicy than a typical chili crisp, so it’s a good option for more sensitive palates.

@Randy
Costco has a big jar.. Very good
Bon Appetit has a great recipe for chile crisp, you can make it at home yourself without any special equipment and it's not behind a paywall. It takes around 30 minutes to make and lasts for months.

Sifton mentions in the accompanying article (which you should read if you don't know about the chili crisp), that he includes ginger, but it's not listed in the recipe on 4/17/20. I almost left the ginger out, but as I was assembling the marinade, decided to mince about 2 tablespoons and included it. I'm so glad I did. It adds a sweet floral and necessary dimension. Also, be sure to pile the chunky marinade on top of the tofu as it will just burn on the baking sheet. Amazing and simple recipe!

@David. Good tips, thanks.  Recipes are super annoying  when the instructions don’t align with the Ingredienys.  How about SESAME OIL—it’s often listed as an ingredient (here for example), when it seems what is intended is TOASTED sesame oil, not at all  the same thing
@David I noticed ginger it missing too, but then it looks like it was added at the end. Editorial revisions…

As a long-time user of Lao Gan Ma chili crisp, I was curious to try the Trader Joe version.  For anyone who knows the full, rich, umami-laden flavor of LGM, the TJ stuff is far inferior and not worth buying.
@Rich Herold I’ve always wondered! Thx I’ll have to try the real deal
@Rich Herold I assumed this already since LGM is the best ever! I’ve been using it for years.
@Rich Herold. I believe one difference is that the TJ version doesn’t have MSG

Wonderful recipe, but a 450 degree oven is too hot! Twenty minutes at 400 is plenty. You can use firm tofu instead of extra firm. Also, replace the string beans with broccoli tossed with a mix of vegetable and sesame oil.

I’ve taken to making a big batch of just the tofu, and keeping it in the fridge for lunches.  Take a wrap—I favour a protein version— smear it with crunchy peanut butter, top it with some shredded lettuce and half a grated apple, and finish with a couple of the tofu slices, cut into smaller strips.  Absolute perfection— crunchy from the peanut butter, sweet and juicy from the apple, and complex, spicy and savoury from the tofu.
