@Julia, London - I would never have thought of this in a zillion years, but your hack for the wrap with PB, lettuce, and apple is OUTSTANDING! This is now a favorite of mine. Thank you.
@Julia, London, you wonderful human. I just made this and I don’t know how I got to 55 years old without this in my life. Thank you!!

@Julia, London we have this wrap on rotation now…thank you! I have tried a few gluten-free wraps that aren’t great and can make my own tortilla but today didn’t feel like it and just used the lettuce as a wrap and it did not feel like a punishment. Brilliant idea mixing this with the apple and lettuce and crunchy PB…thank you again.

I cooked this and thought it was really good but could use some ginger. Then I read through it again and noticed that he mentions ginger in the introduction but it didn't get into the recipe, Add ginger!

@Renee

They addrd ginger but it’s at the bottom of the ingredients while the rest are in order. I missed it!
@Jamie ginger is missing from ingredients list @ start cooking!

Note:  I am a huge Bittman fan and trust his advice to use what you have on hand.
1.  I used Chile Crunch (Chile crunch.com). 
2.  I used an Indian mash-up of Ginger and Garlic paste - I noticed that many chiefs have moved to pastes.
3. Not having Chinese black vinegar, I used red wine vinegar.
4. I used Blue Agave in place of the honey. (lower glycemic).
5.  Since cilantro tastes like soap to me I used a combo of parsley and basil from my aero garden.
It all turned out really well.  Very tasty.

I love the Fly By Jing chili crisp condiment Sam describes in his condiment review. I was given a jar of it as a sample and it's now a staple in my kitchen. I just had it on my egg & avocado toast this morning!

I made this almost exactly as written, except I didn’t have cilantro. A couple notes:

1. I used tofu that had been previously frozen, which gave it a nice spongy texture to absorb the sauce
2. The green beans needed some flavor - salt or a splash of soy sauce after they come out of the oven would be really helpful.
3. Even though the chili crisp sauce is fairly spicy when raw, the final baked product had almost no bite to it.

This was great. Will probably make another version of it again.
@Anne Marie I really think it helps to marinate for much longer! I’ve never heard of marinating tofu for such a short time in this kind of recipe.

Chili Crisp is amazing! Cooks Illustrated posted a fabulous recipe for chili crisp this Feb - as soon as I finish one jar (max ~3 weeks) I make a new one. It doesn't need MSG, and you can doctor it as you like. https://www.cooksillustrated.com/recipes/12400-chili-crisp-lao-gan-ma It's easy to make and adding it to just about anything (Alison Roman's spicy noodle soup with mushrooms and herbs, for example) takes your meal to a completely different level.
Cooks Illustrated requires one to have a membership in order to view recipe. But thanks

This was good and I'll make it again, but instead of scooping the remaining marinade on before baking, I'll save it and drizzle on top after it bakes. The marinade that ran off the tofu borderline burnt, and the whole dish ended up with a sort of flat, one-dimensional, overly cooked taste. It needed something  acidic/sharp/uncooked. I did put some Szechuan peppercorn on at the end. Especially if you are using Trader Joe's chili crisp, which IMO isn't that flavorful, I would recommend doing this.
@Christy thank you for the tips. Wish I had followed them the first time but I definitely will on the second!
How fiery is fiery - does the condiment burn your mouth?  Now for me then.

Not at all. I've been using Lao Gan Ma chili crisp as my COVID coping food (I'm actually pretty distraught to see it here! It's already hard enough to find family size jars on Amazon!!!) and I consider it milder than all my other hot sauces. I put it on stir fried veggies (esp asparagus), freezer dumplings, and --best of all-- on top of a sunny-side-up egg on sprouted grain toast.
Trader Joe's makes a version called Chili Onion Crunch. It's not too spicy, and I'm a spice wimp.

Great “Chile Crisp” recipe in July, 2018 Bon Appetit (https://www.bonappetit.com/recipe/chile-crisp) to make it all from scratch. Final tofu topping was also great on bread. Great dish that can be adopted to several other foods like shrimp, pork, chicken even pasta.

We really enjoyed this. I had a jar of the Trader Joe’s chili crisp and it was a great change of pace for our Tuesday tofu dinner.  Spicy but definitely not too hot. We had asparagus instead of green beans, and soft tofu, not firm, but otherwise followed the recipe.  A keeper!
Broccoli works as a substitute for the green beans. Cooking time is the same.
Published in today’s print NYT 11/22/20 but still no ginger mentioned. Also a note of what to substitute for “Chinese black vinegar” within the recipe itself would have been helpful.

I served the tofu over a salad the other night and it was really delicious. I tossed the greens in a quick dressing with lots of ginger, lime zest, tamari, white soy sauce, lime juice and sesame oil. In addition to the tofu, the salad included shaved carrots and plenty of avocado. The sweet carrots and creamy avocado balanced out the zippy tofu perfectly. The whole dish was filling but not heavy in addition to being very nutritious. I think I will add this to my rotation!

Delicious! I decided to make this but didn't have the chili-crisp and my store didn't carry it so I mixed Thai red curry sauce, Korean Gochujang and Sriracha sauce together from my pantry (probably 1 1/2-2tbs all together) and also used aged balsamic vinegar instead of black. I doubled the amount of cilantro and scallions.  It had so much flavor and since i have never had this dish before, I don't know what I am missing. So flavorful when heaped on sushi rice. Loved having the blistered beans.

I have decided that I need to get the TJ's chili-crisp after reading all the good reviews but I didn't have any on hand.  I was waffling between using thai red curry and gochujang when I saw your suggestion of both along with sriracha and it turned out great!

This was so amazing.  I ordered the chili crisp and black vinegar online before attempting as I didn't want to substitute.  For the tofu I used "Tofu cutlet" which is pressed and not watery.  I made the whole marinade recipe but half the tofu and green beans (dinner for 2) and served with Sam Sifton's fried rice.  The marinade is amazing and I will be thinking about more ways to use it (I live in Southern CA so I don't have mittens!).

Sifton mentions in the accompanying article (which you should read if you don't know about the chili crisp), that he includes ginger, but it's not listed in the recipe on 4/17/20. I almost left the ginger out, but as I was assembling the marinade, decided to mince about 2 tablespoons and included it. I'm so glad I did. It adds a sweet floral and necessary dimension. Also, be sure to pile the chunky marinade on top of the tofu as it will just burn on the baking sheet. Amazing and simple recipe!

Read the many helpful comments and decided to try.
First, as per a comment, I looked up how to microwave tofu to get the water out. Much better than pressing and draining. I made the marinade as instructed but after marinating the sliced tofu, saved it to put on top after cooking as per comments. Used broccoli instead of green beans and coated them with a little garlic flavored olive oil. Lowered the oven temp to 400 and cooked for 45 minutes. The marinade on top is just perfect. I did overcook the tofu a bit making it a bit firmer and drier but the dish was tasty and I will make it again but maybe put the oven at 425 for 30 minutes.
Broccoli was a great substitute for green beans.

I made brownies and added Spicy Chili Crisp to the batter (I always pour off the extra oil in the jar) and they were delicious, especially when I made a brownie sundae with vanilla ice cream and more Chili Crisp on top!  I AM NOT MAKING THIS UP!  But mostly I just eat it with a spoon...

This has become a staple in our household where we have a dedicated vegetarian and a few flexitarians. We often serve it with steamed snow peas or sautéed bok choy in place of the beans—with basmati or sushi rice as an accompaniment. 

Another recent riff (for the flexitarians) was eliminating the tofu and spooning the marinade onto seared Chilean sea bass filets. We still made the tofu version for our vegetarian, but everyone agreed that their version was insanely delicious.

Chinese black vinegar tastes like A1 sauce to me, and I think it blunted the brightness of the chili crisp. The second time I made this I used rice vinegar and it was much better.  I also doubled the amount of string beans because I really love vegetables and thought that the ratio of tofu to veggies in the recipe was off.  Doubling the veggies gave us four full portions over Thai rice noodles with just enough miso broth to cover, topped with chopped peanuts and scallions.  Delish!

My son, who is usually lukewarm about tofu, could not get enough of it with this marinade. He and my husband argued over who got the last piece of tofu! This dish is joining our monthly dinner-recipe rotation. PS - Added minced fresh ginger as other people recommend, and lacking black vinegar I used rice wine vinegar, and it came out great!

This is excellent. I didn't have Chinese black vinegar, so I used rice vinegar. I also added a tablespoon of minced ginger, since the article about this recipe mentions ginger. The marinade on top of the tofu crisps up really nicely and, of course, as it cooks it gets into the green beans. This is an amazing dish. I plan to order some black vinegar to see if that makes it even more amazing the next time I cook this dish.

we loved this. I was lucky enough to find Blank Slate's vegan Sichuan chili oil which is what I used here. I doubled the tofu and made sure that the extra marinade kept on top of the tofu slices rather than sliding down and getting burnt as some commenters reported. Also kept the oven temp at 425 F, and added a big knob of ginger to the marinade. (oh and used a mixture od balsamic vinegar + rice vinegar for Chinese black vinegar)
Worcestershire sauce is a serviceable sub for the black vinegar.

This is ridiculous. May be the best recipe I’ve made from NYT in a while. I did plan ahead and ordered Fly by Jing chili crisp (omg, amazing) and black vinegar. This will be going into regular rotation. Doubled the recipe and added 2 Tbls fresh grated ginger to sauce. Cooked tofu in a 9x13 Pyrex after dipping both sides in the sauce. Tossed green beans in oil, salt and Argyle Street seasoning from The Spice House and baked on separate sheet pan. Put beans on top rack, tofu on middle rack.

This dish was fabulous. A big thank you to whoever mentioned microwaving the tofu. You taught this Asian a shortcut and I'm very grateful for it! 
I microwaved the sliced tofu for 3 minutes to help dry it out so it could absorb the marinade better and this was without pressing the tofu.
Unfortunately, I didn't have black vinegar on hand so I used balsamic vinegar instead which works decently as a substitute. 
Definitely going to make this again!
What is the best substitute for Chinese black vinegar? I keep rice, apple cider, balsamic and white vinegar on hand. That's really enough variety for my small household.
Happened to have fresh pineapple, which went very nicely with this on the side.

This is now in our regular rotation. I make as written except, instead of pouring on the rest of the marinade and baking, I reserve the extra and use it as a dipping sauce with the final product. I have left out the cilantro once or twice and it wasn't a big loss. Definitely use Chinese black vinegar though--tastes basically nothing like red or balsamic.
The mitten was rather tough but the marinade was superb!

This is an easy weekday dinner to make.  I used rice vinegar since that is what I had, I used frozen string beans that I defrosted and drained, I didn't have scallions, so I used an onion in with the marinade, and left the onions roast on top of the tofu with amazing results. Otherwise this recipe is solid and will be part of our rotation for sure.  5 stars.
Don't forget the two tablespoons of minced ginger that somehow is listed at the end, after the rice, like a garnish.  It goes in the marinade.

We really enjoyed this recipe! I pressed the tofu first to release the water. I mostly followed the instructions listed. I tossed the green beans with extra virgin olive oil (instead of "neutral oil") and a little Kosher salt before putting on my foil-lined baking sheet. I roasted the food at a 400 degree over for 15 minutes, then decided to turn it up to 425. I wanted the green beans to brown a bit more. Next time, I'll try cooking at 425 from the beginning, check it at 15 minutes, then adjust.
There is a great Bon Appetit recipe for “chili crisp”.  So good.

I made this tonight and here is one thing I would change:

The marinade is delicious and fresh tasting. It seems a waste to use it all up when cooking the tofu. I would marinade the tofu and then save some of the marinade to pour over the tofu when it's done. Alternatively, I might use it as a dipping sauce.
Forget the green beans and use broccoli instead. Roasts much better than green beans. Excellent recipe, even my picky eater loved it.
Cooked for 20 minutes at 400° and it came out perfectly. Garnished with additional cilantro, scallions, and some coarsely chopped salted peanuts for an extra crunch. Delicious!

This is so good that I had it again for breakfast.  I’d never had - or even heard of - spicy chili crisp, but I’m in love now!  Sam, you rock!  Several noted that ginger isn’t included in the list of ingredients.  It’s listed at the end, guys, so be sure to read the whole recipe!

Most delicious recipe. My beans were not so fresh but cooked up beautifully. Threw in a head of broccoli I had as well. Microwaved the tofu as others suggested and I think it soaked up the marinade even better while warm. Will make again and again!

This is one of my top fave tofu recipes, and make it often. The sauce is not too spicy, and yet extremely flavorful (and kind of addictive)!  I like to double the amount of green beans, as there is plenty of sauce to go around. Added quartered mushrooms once ~ also yum.
Doubled the recipe – made one sheet pan with tofu and the other with salmon to suit a large-ish family. Everyone loved it! It's going to be put on heavy rotation here.

This is a great dish.  I have made it 2x now, alongside the somewhat similar Sheet-Pan Salmon and Broccoli With Sesame and Ginger for a family with vegetarians and meat eaters.  I used broccoli because green beans are not ready here, but I think you could use any vegetable really-just cook until it gets a char and the tofu will be wonderful.
Subbed Brussels sprouts-was really fantastic
Very delicious; my only change was I cubed the tofu and marinated in a bowl; easier to mix with beans and eat.

I’ve made this recipe dozens of times now. In case it helps someone else save time/hassle: I’ve never bothered to press the tofu to get out extra liquid and it has always turned out great anyway. You can also double the tofu without doubling the marinade.

Substituted balsamic for black vinegar and coconut aminos for soy sauce. Used Brussels sprouts instead of green beans, firm tofu instead of extra firm, and brown rice instead of white. Not because I thought it would be better or that I know what I’m doing, but because it’s what I had. And it was excellent if I do say so myself.
This is so so so delicious but if you cook it for 20 mins at 450 it will burn-- Try 15-20 at 400 instead.
Please edit the ingredients list to position the ginger before the tofu? Twice now I have made the sauce by going down the list and stopping at the tofu, thus forgetting the ginger.

I've made this at least 10 times, varying the veggies (green beans, broccoli, kale rapini, purple sprouting broccoli) and it is always a winner. But hey, NYT folks, please move the ginger up in the ingredient list so I don't forget it at least half the time! I know it's in the written instructions but for repeat offenders who don't read those it would be awesome to have all the marinade ingredients listed together.

Worcestershire sauce is a pretty good sub for Chinese black vinegar, much closer in taste than rice vinegar.  For such a simple recipe this is delicious and very adaptable.  A few days ago I made the recipe as written but doubled the sauce (as others have recommended) which provided leftovers that I used last night to make an entirely new batch with a second block of tofu.  No green beans but tossed the sauce with cauliflower florets and acorn squash slices that needed to be used up - not bad.
I think I would put the green beans in for 5 minutes or so before putting the tofu in just to ensure they are done.

Really good! Discovered at the last minute that the green beans had not weathered their time in the fridge since the last shop very well. Substituted a mix of snow peas and sweet red peppers, but added them to the pan halfway through the tofu cooking, since I expected them to cook faster than green beans. One of the best tofu recipes (of many) we've made.

We use Lao Gan Ma brand chili crips, and we usually make 3x the recipe so we can eat it for the next week. Its one of our favorite go-to meals. We've made it with all sorts of minor adjustments (forgot the ginger, accidentally doubled the sesame oil, forgot this, measured wrong on that) and it comes out perfect every time.
My family likes it with a greater volume of the delicious sauce.

I have made this recipe multiple times now and it's fantastic. I use two 16oz blocks of extra firm tofu, slicing each block into quarters (I like lots of tofu). Double the sauce recipe. Can easily sub green beans for bok choy halved lengthwise, or even broccoli. Bake everything exactly as described. Fantastic over rice seasoned with rice vinegar and kombu. Easy meal prep for the entire week!
Everyone who at this dish found the Chili Crisp so dominant that it detracted from the dish.  I might try this again with 1.5 TB Chil Crisp
This was very tasty and easy to prepare. But a serving for four? Adults? That’s only 170 calories per person. This is more like for two adults

Wondering if anyone else has discovered the best way to insure your tofu is nice and dry and can easily absorb flavor without too much trouble? A game changer- freeze it. Freeze it. Thaw it. Squeeze the water out. Start cooking. Marinade is absorbed and it cooks easily. Don’t say it. Well you know. Cooks and tastes like chicken. Ha!

Only had silken tofu on hand and it was delicious, don't think I will ever switch back to firm or extra firm. Served with brown rice.  I followed all the other instructions pretty much to the letter, only substituted balsamic for the Chinese vinegar and skipped the scallions and cilantro because this was total pantry meal on a Thursday night after a busy week.  Will make again for sure and next time perhaps add some chopped peanuts to give the silken tofu a bit a crunch.

This easy recipe was delicious!  My only edit might be to use low sodium soy sauce and add another teaspoon of honey to balance the flavor a bit. Of course, it may have to do with the brand of chili crisp being used. It’s also excellent with broccoli, as noted in the original recipe.

I thought it very tasty (cooked exactly per the recipe).  Husband found it "too spicy".  This is my first foray into cooking Tofu (even bought a "Tofu Press" -- www.tofuture.com).  Will try a ~bland, meat loaf--type recipe (for tofu) next, to see if that will bring the spouse back to the trough.  Stay tuned.

My new favorite tofu recipe! Excellent flavors. I cut tofu into 4 steaks instead of 8 and m'waved for 90 sec to extract water then marinated 3 hrs in refrigerator. Couldn't find black vinegar so used aged sherry vinegar. Was going to sprinkle cilantro & gr onions on after cooking but forgot - probably better without them. Tossed frozen skinny beans (instead of fresh) lightly in EVOO w/S&P then filled sheet pan to max, no cooking time adj needed. Japanese sticky rice good w/this. Serves only 3.
As a substitute for Chinese black vinegar you could try balsamic vinegar (NOT the really expensive kind).
As someone who’s tried MANY chili crisps and LOVES LGM, also wanted to shout out David Chang’s brand Momofuku Chili Oil (can get at Whole Foods). More expensive than others but one of my favorites!!
@Juliana Yes, I was surprised that no one else mentioned Momofuku since it’s so good and more widely available than others. I loved it in this dish.

I’ve never done this before but I loved this so much that I made it two nights in a row! I’ve made it again two more evenings within the same month. I microwave the tofu for three minutes and give it a quick press, and I use rice vinegar instead of black vinegar and the Trader Joe’s dry chili crisp because that’s what I’ve got on hand. 
It has no right to be this easy and this good, and is giving me the perfect use for all the green beans coming from my garden right now.
Use Agave rather than Honey & this dish is Vegan.
This was really good. The marinade got a little burnt, so next time I’ll reserve some and put on after baking. I subbed agave for honey to make it vegan.

I considered myself a tofu-hater. No more! This is absolutely delicious and I followed the recipe exactly except had no honey so used agave. Someone mentioned that there was no ginger in the recipe but in the 2/28/21 NYT that was corrected. Found the chili crisp (not chile-crisp) at an Asian supermarket.

It's so much easier to use a sprayer with olive or other oil than to drizzle and mix on a pan. I do this with everything that I roast or bake, after lightly spraying the pan first so that nothing sticks. I use real EVOO in a pump sprayer.
I added eggplant to the baking sheet. It was great - the silky texture worked well with the firm tofu. The eggplant was better than the green beans, in my opinion.

Double the sauce - it's good to have on hand for a variety of uses like eggs, tortillas and most everything. Also, you can be flexible with the vegetables. Green beans are a good foundation but I usually also add mushrooms and red bell peppers for some variety and color. Enjoy!
I have made this many times just as written and it is terrific! I have also made it with Brussels sprouts which were not quite as good.

I vehemently disagree that this is remotely too salty. I might add a sprinkle of kosher salt next time, a glug more honey, a bit more vinegar, and toasted chopped cashews. It was so easy to throw together and the green beans were sublime.

Wow, this was delicious - will absolutely make again! Could definitely add more veggies (we added eggplant). Add ginger. Didn't have Chinese black vinegar so used equal parts balsamic and rice wine vinegar. Excellent recipe.
Found the "Crunchy Chili Onion" still available at Trader Joe's. Substituted broccoli and cauliflower for the beans as we prefer that. Excellent recipe!

We loved this. I didn’t read all the comments before making it so: I used the TJ’s Chili Onion crisp-because that’s what I had on hand. I used Balsamic Vinegar for the black vinegar, and added minced ginger as well. I also baked at 400° The green beans were fine, I coated them with avocado oil and added a salt, pepper & garlic blend. 
I’d probably use broccoli next time so it doesn’t break down. I will definitely be making it again.
The ginger should be moved up in the ingredients list if it's meant to go in the marinade and not the rice.
I have lost count on how many NYTs recipes are just “add chili crisp!”  It’s nice you discovered a new condiment but this is a half step from “Ketchup Penne.”

I'm cooking this for the first time tonight.  Is the ginger an after thought?  I'm just wondering why all ingredients used in "Step 1" wouldn't be listed together in "Ingredients", instead of orphaned on page 2.  I find when ginger is in a recipe, it is important.
I missed the ginger and wished I had read your comment before making the recipe.  It was still good but will add next time.

My family loves this recipe. I have made it several times now and is in our rotation. Maybe I am the only one who has this issue but I miss the addition of the ginger just about every time because it is listed dead last after cooked rice. I know it is listed in step 1 of the preparation column but I am not looking there as I make the marinade/topping. I read from the list of ingredients column to get the quantities. It sure would help me if it was listed with the garlic, scallions, and cilantro.
I've tried this recipe, and I recommend trying a different one next time.
The flavors here are great. Agree with others that the green beans ended up a bit overcooked; would consider adding those later or making on a separate sheet pan the next time I make this.
Watch out for the ginger, not sure why it is listed last

Made multiple times. Tasty, healthy and quick. Usually also have ingredients on hand. Quite versatile and have tried with different veggies. Honestly, the green beans are not the best. Get limp and overdone. My most successful combo is halved cherry tomatoes, onion rings, corn and asparagus. Big hit. Guests ask for recipe. I up the ginger and garlic and cilantro and nice with some fresh lemon or even better squeezed lime

Wonderful recipe!  It delicious when we made it, and we also loved it as leftovers.  I made it as written, using Lao Gan Ma chili crisp, with just one change - agave instead of honey.  I also used broccoli instead of green beans because that's what I had!  It was a little spicier than the most spice-sensitive member of my family would have liked, but the rest of us found it just the right kind of spicy that leaves you wanting more.
Lao Gan Ma Spicy Chili Crisp should be a controlled substance- it is truly addictive

Delicious sauce! Panfried individual servings of fresh salmon fillet. Plated the fish. Then added the sauce concoction of chili crisp (Fly By Jing brand) and other ingredients to the same pan. Sizzled this at medium heat for 30 sec.  Poured sauce onto fish. Garnished with chopped cilantro. Served with white rice, and choy and tofu stir-fried with oyster sauce. Tip: here's a reasonable substitute for Chinese black vinegar. Mix 1 part balsamic vinegar, 1 part rice vinegar and 3 parts water.
Made with snap peas and leeks. Excellent combo.

This is phenomenal. Easy and delicious. Didnt have green beans, but did have limp sugar snap peas, soft baby corn and a sad bunch of pak choy. Pressed the tofu then dunked in marinade. Massaged pathetic veg with oil, shoved it to the far edges of the tray, the tofu glistening  in the middle.
Complete silence during eating. Result

Having made this several times, I've come to think the following changes are critical: I cube the tofu for better distribution of marinade, and let it sit for much longer. I do not pour the marinade over until the last few minutes of cooking so that the garlic doesn't burn. And I use broccoli, which soaks up the delicious marinade (I also use only 1 tbsp of chili crisp, but that's me). This recipe is craveable.

450 is way too high for the chili crisp.  It 
Started to burn.  Great flavors! I will make it again at a lower heat.

First time making tofu and this came out amazing! I used Hot Crispy Oil (a staple in our house) for the chili crisp condiment and the dish was delicious. I substituted dumpling sauce for the soy and vinegar. Also, I adjusted the oven time to 18 minutes at 450 since the green beans started blistering early. Instead of rice, we had a cucumber tomato salad on the side that complemented the spice well. This will definitely be a recipe on rotation!

Im noticing a lot of five star reviews on NYT recipes that are easy, but not necessarily fantastic. This is one of them. The ingredients are not cheap - I stock a high quality chile crisp and the recipe uses almost the entire jar. I get that I could make my own next time - but to me this wasn’t good enough for a next time. To my taste, it was a one-note spicy/ginger face slap without nuance or saucy pleasure.
Perfect recipe! I used Lao Gan Ma brand chili crisp, with asparagus as well as the green beans. There was not a grain of rice left on anyone's plate.
This deserves all the stars. Quick, easy and delicious.
Used 1T low-salt soy sauce and added 2T juice from marinated ginger. Skipped the cilantro. YUM!

Made the recipe as written! 5 stars! Beautiful presentation
Fresh beans from the garden, and I saw another commenter post about microwaving the tofu for 3 minutes to remove excess moisture- AWESOME tip! No more pressing tofu!

Nice weeknight dish. I did not have chili crisp so I substituted siracha and a couple dried chilies minced...also no Chinese black vinegar, so I did balsamic, and reduced the amount of honey. The taste was great - I think I'd add the beans after the tofu has cooked about 8 minutes. They were a little over done. Otherwise - A nice recipe that can take adaptations!

This was delicious and pretty quick. I would add another few ounces of beans to go with the tofu. I used Chili crisp from China Live restarant and grocery in San Francisco - this is my favorite, better than Trader Joes, which isn't terrible. The ginger is listed in the wrong place in the ingredient list - another reader couldn't find it - perhaps it was edited in at the last minute. A delicous and simple recipe, loved it!
Cooking tofu has been extremely intimidating for me. Until I found this recipe! Super firm tofu is the move! This was a delicious, quick and easy meal. Perfect leftovers, too!
For quick, throw it together, last minute weekday meal, you can't beat this. Super simple, super flavorful. Definitely has a POW WOW factor. Give it a whirl!

We're meat eaters, but this was every bit as good as everyone says.  Lao Gan Ma chile crisp - I can't eat too hot and my husband loves very spicy, but this worked for both of us!  Used sherry wine vinegar.  Rice and green beans.  Prep & roasting (convection oven 400 degrees for 20 minutes) - a breeze!

Wish they would put the ginger up with the rest of the marinade ingredients, since that’s where you need it. Otherwise, a really good recipe. Tried it with broccoli once and red cabbage another time. Both delicious.
I have made this so many times, and it is always amazing. Hot tip: take it up a notch and add toasted almonds or peanuts on top. Perfection.
After i marinate the tofu, add the vegetable oil to the bowl with remaining marinade and toss the green beans.  Adds a lot of flavor.  Great dish for my vegan friends

Made for first time last night. Subbed broccoli and asparagus for beans. And I did cheat and use Trader Joe's chili crisp in the marinade. 

DELICIOUS!!! So good, we're having it again tonight with haricot verts. Also made simple coconut jasmine rice (1 cup rice, 1 cup water, 1/2 cup full fat coconut milk). Outstanding together!

We like our veg al dente, so cooked the tofu about 15 min, then added broccoli around the slices and bake another 10 min, then added the asparagus for 5 minutes.

This recipe turned out very well. I never go for seconds, but I did with this! I added a bit more honey, and after tasting the marinade, decided to add the juice of 1/2 lime, which lightened it up just slightly, I used the tip from another reader and microwaved the tofu slices for 3 minutes to remove even more water and it worked!! I also marinated the tofu all day. I know myself and I’m the type who feels food is just the carrier for flavor and sauces, so for me, the duration of pre-heat oven time wasn’t going to be enough. Kind of a flavor junkie… I was very happy with the result. I discovered (by accident) that Dragon’s Milk Gold paired very nicely with this dish. So good!!

This is delicious! I made it as written except swapping rice wine vinegar in place of the black vinegar, and minced red onion in place of the scallions. My husband and I loved it, and I would make tofu this way for other uses, as well. I use a Tofu Bud device that I purchased at Amazon for $27 to press the liquid out of tofu; this has be a transformative purchase. I highly recommend it. Also, my Costco carries 4 packs of 18 oz. organic extra firm tofu at an amazing price savings. I freeze them.
This is a good recipe, but Chile crisp is a condiment. I don't see what piling it on does for the final product. It over powers everything else in these quantities!
450 is way too hot. Thankfully I was paying attention and pulled it out earlier. Perused comments and found several others had the same issue. Would make again at a lower temp.

My family liked this dish well enough, but none of us understood why NYT readers have raved about it as they have.
The chile crisp’s heat overpowered the other flavors; that went over better w/some in my house than w/others.
If I were making it again, I’d probably use more scallions, more cilantro/herbs, perhaps more honey and sesame oil - or just less chile crisp. I might also add (toward the end of the bake) some peanuts or cashews for texture and some flavor balance.
So good! Highly recommend this one. It's quick and easy, once you get the steps down, so it has become a go-to on busy nights. Watch the tofu if you drain it very well - it cooks faster.

Delicious! We subbed rice vinegar for black vinegar and sugar for honey. Excellent and easy, and our sheet pan looked the SAME as the recipe (I LOVE when this happens (or maybe even better than the photo (NYTimes coking hire meeee)))
Excellent with halved Brussels sprouts!

True winner, Mr. Sifton! I used cabbage & red onion and julienne jalapeño . Topped  it with Ali Slagle’s cilantro salad (from Sheet pan Sesame Tofu and Red Onions) with some Julienne cucumber to top it. Tossed  the salad in some residual marinade, lemon and sesame seed. Very complementary pairing served over Amanda Hester’s coconut ginger rice. Trifecta of NYT’s brilliant authors plus readers!
The marinade is amazing! I prefer keep the remainder to drizzle on top of the finished product. When I bake the leftover sauce, it burns on the pan which is a mega waste of flavour!

This has become such a staple for us: it has an intense and satisfying chile flavor without actually being spicy-hot. I always double the recipe and put the (drained, pressed) tofu in one sheet pan, the beans in another. Halfway through I add a quart of cherry tomatoes to the tofu pan. It adds another level of flavor without any fuss. I serve it all over coconut rice. Delicious!
I love chili crisp, saute it with rice, scallions and an egg to make the best fried rice.

I added lots of chopped ginger to the marinade. Didn't use the tablespoon of neutral oil for beans, just tossed them with the marinade and that seemed to be enough coating. Will pick up some Chile-crisp for next time!
Great recipe!

We've been addicted to spicy chili crisp for years.  I haven't thought to do a sheet pan style approach!  This is great.  Lots of room for versatility and switching up veggies, proteins.  Spicy chili crisp (I agree with what others have said, it's a milder, rounder heat profile, manageable by many spicy food averse folk) is also really good mixed in with some mayo as a good condiment for, well anything, but especially for oven roasted potatoes, or fries.
Made with asparagus instead of green beans and it worked wonderfully.
I think that’s smarter anyway since the tofu and green beans are the same protein source, though the beans are less concentrated. Better to complement sources, if possible, though not crucial.

I did make this recipe but changed a few things.  I used rice vinegar because I didn't have Chinese black vinegar and used the Trader Joe's "Chili Onion Crunch" instead of chili-crisp.  It was quite delicious and very easy.  Also served with quinoa not rice.

This was a good one and fairly easy. The whole family loved it. Only down side was cleaning the pans as i had to make 2 batches for the 5 of us. Perhaps line pan with aluminum foil to minimize the clean up. Will absolutely add this to the rotation.

I had virtually none of the ingredients except tofu and rice, so:

Marinade - soy sauce, thai fried red chili paste, rice vinegar, garlic, ginger, honey, toasted sesame oil

Vegetables - 1 lb. frozen broccoli & about 3/4 cup frozen edamame

Also whipped up some peanut sauce to drizzle over everything. Delicious.
Don't see ginger listed in the ingredients even though it's mentioned in the description.  How much do you recommend?
@David (comment below) recommends 2 tbsp ginger.
Trader Joe's Chili Onion Crunch is perfect! Nice texture and it's definitely not too hot. I will try the marinade on chicken next time, for the non-vegans in the family.
The chili crisp is the main event here, so don't hesitate to vary the other ingredients. 450 is a bit too hot though; 425 is enough in my opinion.
Great recipe but the green beans were way overcooked.  Next time I’ll lower the temperature.

I made the dish using Chile-crisp condiment from a local Chinese market, firm tofu from Trader Joes, asparagus instead of green beans, and grated fresh ginger. It took 30 minutes in my convection oven at 450. We enjoyed over black rice.
On the plus side, it's a way to cook tofu and vegs w/o having to stir fry. I don't own a wok and find cooking tofu a little tricky.
I probably wouldn't make it the same way again. I will play with it to make it more interesting.

Used 2 tbsp. of chili crisp instead of 3, since we are spicy food wimps. The family felt the dish was borderline too spicy but enjoyed it, so glad I only used 2 tbsp. For some reason the chili crisp isn’t as flavorful once out of the oven (used Trader Joe’s), so may increase some other ingredients next time. Forgot to add the ginger per other comments, which I think would have helped. Cooked the tofu at 450, then added the beans after 10 minutes so they wouldn’t be too overcooked. Worked well.

@JSP -- thank you so much for the spicy feedback! Two of us (youngest and me) cannot handle super-spicy, whereas the other two are "bring it on!" But they can always add after the fact, so thank you for sharing how much you decreased the chili crisp. (I am making this today for our main meal.)

This was delicious and a big thumbs up from my two teenage boys. I didn’t have the vinegar so left that out. I used the Trader Joe’s chili crunch as advised in the comments. I had cherry tomatoes I was afraid would go bad so I cut them up and added in with the beans. I thought it was an amazing add! This was one of my favorite meals recently. Served with brown rice.
This was quite good. In addition to making the tofu for the vegetarian, we also spooned the sauce over a couple of salmon portions for the meat eaters and baked alongside. Everyone was happy.

May I suggest during this time you find a way to share recipes that do not require special ingredients.  Black vinegar and special chile crisp condiment is not something I can have an insta shopper locate nor do I have these stashed away in my pantry.  
Why not offer pantry recipes - using ingredients that are easily available to all of us.

there are numerous pantry recipes on here, check out the pantry recipes category! https://cooking.nytimes.com/68861692-nyt-cooking/13993006-pantry-recipes BTW I found both black vinegar and chile crisp on Amazon.

This contains msg and sulphites.  You should mention that.  MSG particularly causes migraines and even siezures in some people - about 50% of the population has symptoms from eating it. 
Thank you

Made this today and it is delish.  I forgot to add vinegar (I didn't have black vinegar but was going to sub in rice wine vinegar) and it was delish anyway.  A major triumph as my husband does not like tofu with the occasional except of deep fried at a Chinese restaurant and other attempts to cook it at home have been very unsuccessful.
I couldn’t find nice green beans so I substituted Brussels sprouts. I didn’t add ginger, but maybe will next time. Really good.

I made it pretty much exactly, except I added some ginger, and let the tofu marinate about 30 minutes before starting the oven. It is really yummy, I like the combination of flavors. It was way too spicy for me but my spice-nut hubby loved it. Next time I’ll probably cut the chili crisp in half, just a personal preference.

I wanted to tag onto the comments by MTrang and world vegetarian that they might be interested in a little contraption called a “tofuXpress.” I bought one years ago and it’s been a huge asset. No more towels- cloth or otherwise- and much, much faster.

My partner and I ate the whole thing.  Thank heavens I'd cut the tofu into equal portions or there might have been violence...  I didn't have black vinegar, so I used vinagre de jerez and cut the honey down by at least half.

My green beans were past their prime so I used 1 head of baby bok choy (large leaves cut in half) instead.  The leaves crisp pretty quickly in the oven so you have to watch it.  I served with black Forbidden Rice, and it was terrific, even better than with green beans and fried rice, the chewy texture of the rice complemented the crunch of the tofu and bok choy.
This is delicious exactly as written. The marinade is so flavorful I tried it on fresh black cod, prepared the same way, with sublime results.
Marinated the tofu for about an hour and made with asparagus instead of green beans. Really good hot out of the oven and as leftovers.

This was very good but next time I am going to double the sauce, because there was not enough. Also, I added about 1 tablespoon of fresh ginger, and it was delicious. I will also add a bit more fresh garlic next time. A winner and easy and satisfies the craving for chinese food. :)
This is a spectacular use of tofu that's loaded with flavor and pretty easy to prepare. I don't know how good a match it is, but I typically substitute balsamic for the Chinese black vinegar.

I ‘ve made—and shared—this easy, delicious recipe that even friends who don’t normally eat tofu, love. I also encourage adding grated ginger, if possible. Chili crisps and black vinegar elevate freezer dumplings so much. And the tofu makes a great Bahn mi, if you have leftovers.
The Fly by Jing chili crisp he recommends is expensive and sells out often but sooooo delicious. Nice people, too.

Cooking with chili crisp feels like cheating. So easy and delicious. You’re gonna want rice, so don’t forget to get it going. Add minced ginger to the marinade as others have mentioned. If you have time- slice your tofu early and let it dry on a cookie rack. Press with paper towels.
Amazing!  Never heard of chili crisp.  Bought a jar and have my new favorite condiment.

No chile crisp on hand, so did my best with what I had and it turned out great! Marinated 1.5 lbs tofu cut into planks in 3T soy, 1.5T balsamic/rice vinegar combo, 1.5T. sesame oil, 2tsp red pepper flakes, 2tsp sugar. Forgot garlic and ginger in the marinade, so just added it to leftover I poured over the tofu in the sheet pan. Convection roast at 425 degrees for 25 mins - perfect. Had some fried shallots on hand, so sprinkled those on top along with the scallions and cilantro. Then...devoured!

We loved this! Added about 2 tbsp of ginger paste (as others have advised and as Sifton states in his article). Modified slightly to make up for missing ingredients: Did not have black vinegar so swapped in balsamic, and used Lao Gan Ma "fried chili in oil" since we were out of "chili crisp." Still turned out great and looking forward to using the real "chili crisp."
Be careful not to overcook the chile-crisp. The chiles will continue cooking after you take it off the heat because it takes a while for the oil to cool down.

For those having trouble finding the Chile Crisp recipe in Bon Appetite that Leslie mentioned (I did; when I searched for it, it didn't pull it up), you might have better luck if you click on the Pork Wontons with Sesame Sauce. There's a link in there to the Chile Crisp recipe.
When I want Tofu for dinner, this is my go to recipe.  I love it.    I use Fly By Jing Chili Crisp, have not tried the others mentioned here, but it is excellent.
This was simple and delicious. I followed the recipe exactly except for the substitution of rice vinegar for black vinegar. The leftovers were even better the next day heated up for lunch!
I took the advice of David and added ginger, as Sam Soften mentions in the article.  It definitely added an extra dimension of flavor.
Would use broccoli instead. Ok but sauce on tofu burnt. Maybe cook at 400?

After reading the comments, I added ginger (didn’t measure but probably a tablespoon and a half)  and  used the tip to microwave the sliced tofu before marinating. Wow, this is outstanding.  Definitely will use with many proteins, not just tofu.  Delicious!!
Fly by Jing Chile Crisp is the best. I swear by it. Keep it by the stove and you will be surprised how often you use it.
Try it with snap peas instead of green beans—delish!

definitely use the ginger!  fresh ginger is so much better but the powdered kind i had on hand was fine.  rice vinegar was fine, but i wonder what Chinese black vinegar would do to this!  i also chose to use olive oil because that is simply what i had, and the taste was not a noticeable difference.  i served with brussel sprouts too, which was a nice leafy addition since just beans and tofu seems small :)
Rather than adding more oil to the beans, I tossed them in the marinade which has plenty of oil to share with the tofu! Also used Fly by Jing chile crisp which made the whole house smell amazing!
Delicious! I added a few shrimp near the end of cooking.

Love this recipe and Chile Crisps in general, they are so easy to make anything taste great.   There is a new line of Chile Crisps that are handmade in small batches and not only include Szechuan style but applied the same crispy crunchy goodness to Mexican, Indian and Moroccan flavors.  You should check out the new brand oomame.
Delicious, Don't forget ginger- didn't have honey. Used rice vinegar. Saved extra marinade to serve with. sushi rice

As a Sichuan native, I approve of this recipe! For me this is a fresh take (sheet panning) on familiar ingredients. Like many others, I used a Lao Gan Ma chili sauce: chili oil with black bean. But really any flavor of LGM should work. Success on my first try. Highly recommended.
Substitute rice vinegar for Chinese black vinegar; or balsamic but less than 1 1/2 tablespoons.
I happened to have “hot honey” great addition
Loved this recipe. I only tolerate tofu, but this recipe made me a believer in using Chile crisp to transform it into something sublime. It’s a good thing since I have many vegan family members.

I had to go down to Chinatown to find the Chile Crisp Condiment but it was worth it.  This was the best tofu recipe I ever made.  Sometimes I substitute asparagus for the green beans.  It's always delicious.
Doubled the onion and cilantro.  Added a little more soy sauce to increase the liquid.
I blanched the green beans first. Chili crisp is my new favorite thing.

This was fantastic. Didn't have green beans, so I used broccoli. And rather than drizzle I tossed the florets in a bowl with the oil. Better coverage. And don't sweat the black vinegar. Unless you cook a lot of Chinese food you're not going to use it much and you're certainly not going to miss it in this recipe.
The directions mention adding ginger but ginger is not listed in the ingredients list.  How much ginger?
@Jenny did you ever get an answer? any one know how much ginger to add?
This was delicious - only needs 20 minutes in oven

Made this for the first time and although I read and reread all the notes, I STILL forgot to add the ginger! It was still delicious. I saved the leftover sauce and used it marinate and then roast a nice fillet of halibut. “... the BEST halibut ever!” said my husband.
Used asparagus instead of green beans (husband dislikes them) to great success.  Yummy and simple, and a nice break from an animal protein meal.
I was planning to do this so now I'm sold!

Very tasty. Used Fly By Jing chili crisp. I doubled the tofu and put it in a glass baking dish with the marinade and cooked it in that dish. I did not double the marinade. I used a separate sheet pan for the vegetables. I didn’t have green beans so I used sugar snap peas bell peppers and broccoli. I flipped tofu half way through. Broiled the tofu at the end to concentrate the sauce and brown the top. Served with white rice.
In heavy rotation at our house! My carnivore husband is very happy with this dish. Comes together easily and is SO flavorful. Highly recommend.

This recipe, as is, is fabulous.  I have used asparagus, broccolini and sugar snap peas in place of the green beans and actually prefer those to the green beans-cooking time needed to be adjusted but that did not affect the tofu.  I’ve also recently adapted it to the stovetop-its too hot to use the oven.  I use a large skillet and sear both sides of the tofu, remove from the pan, then sear the veggie of choice.  Add the tofu back to the skillet then add the sauce and finish cooking. Easy!

Just made this for dinner. I love chile crisp, and the flavors did not disappoint. I increased the beans to a pound, add a few grinds of sea salt to them for flavor and better cooking. My biggest issue is that, while the tofu could be considered 4 servings, the green beans barely served 2 adults. It needs at least twice as much veg (perhaps add a pound of sugar snaps or brussels sprouts?) to make 4 satisfying servings.
AMAZING. Next time I will press the tofu, but that is simply personal preference. (Looking at earlier comments, I see that the recipe has been amended, and ginger is now in the ingredient list.)

I’m allergic to tofu, so I made this with chicken breasts. I did a longer marinade (about 45 mins) but that was the only change I made. The chicken breasts cooked by the time the beans were done. It was a terrific, easy, weeknight meal.
made with asparagus, also delicious!  20 minutes seemed too long - next time I will check at 15.  Some of the chili crisp seemed burnt but it was still yummy.
Cut the tofu into smaller pieces (like dominoes). Reserved the leftover marinade and used it at the end, served over rice. Also made a small cucumber salad with rice vinegar and sesame oil!

Unable to buy chile crisp I've been using this recipe: https://www.bonappetit.com/recipe/chile-crisp , but it takes SO long. Next time I will try just sauteeing the garlic and shallots--I'll let you know how that goes.

Substituted rice vinegar for the black vinegar, but wish I'd used something stronger instead - maybe sherry vinegar. It needed that bite. Otherwise quite delicious for those of us who are trying to get more plant based foods in our diet.

Super easy Tuesday night dinner. Didn’t have fresh ginger (aside from a moldy nub in the bottom of the produce bin) so added some powdered… it worked. Left chili crisp off the kid section of the sheet pan, and tossed some salt on it- win! Food that adults like (yay chili crisp!), food that kids ate (yay no flavor!).
I buy fresh ginger and cut it into 1" chunks and freeze it. It grates (I actually use a fine shred) beautifully when frozen and tastes just like fresh.

I make space on 2 sides of the pan for a 10 oz. bag of frozen, sliced, shitake mushrooms, and I spoon the marinade over the tofu then slosh the veges around in the runny sauce that falls off the tofu.  I'm using a convection oven so the extra juices from the mushrooms evaporate.  I recommend it highly. 
Also, I found a local chili crisp that is far superior, and spicier, than the Trader Joe's.

Trader Joe's has a chili crisp?? Thanks for that info! Fly By Jing is the favorite in my kitchen. I just finished the jar, so my next jar will be David Chang's chili crisp--can't wait! (Thanks Santa!)
Double sauce

This works great with broccoli too. I just make it all in a 9 by 13 walled pan - mix the sauce in there, then add tofu and flip around a few times while marinating, add the broccoli or beans before baking - and it works great.

I make this all the time, maybe once a week. It's practically a pantry meal at our house. Often I swap out the veggies and use whatever needs to be used-up. On the plus side, it's vegan. On the extra plus side, it can fit into a keto diet. I make this whenever my keto and vegan friends converge. Add a side of rice rice and cauliflower rice and the whole team is happy. Oh, and I use Lao Gan Ma. So tasty and not super spicy.

Really didn’t care for this one; I just thought it was very bland. The chili crisp didn’t hold at high eat and lost most of its flavor leaving behind some unpleasant burnt bits. The tofu also needed to marinate for longer and I thought the dish as a whole needed some more soy sauce or just straight up salt. 

If I were to make it again I’d marinate overnight and strain the solids from the marinade before adding to the pan. Would also add an extra tablespoon of soy sauce.

microwaved the sliced tofu in a bowl for three minutes on high. Amazing how much water drained out, leaving the tofu with perfect texture to soak up the delicious marinade.

Sifton mentions in the accompanying article (which you should read if you don't know about the chili crisp), that he includes ginger, but it's not listed in the recipe on 4/17/20. I almost left the ginger out, but as I was assembling the marinade, decided to mince about 2 tablespoons and included it. I'm so glad I did. It a

I've never heard of Chili Crisp, but I did have a jar of super spicy Chil Black Bean paste, so I used that, and dried shallots. No black vinegar, either, I substituted balsamic. It was still amazing, so... I love the unfussiness if this recipe. I'll make it again... So many times. I may even use all the correct ingredients next time😉
I love this dish.  i generally follow it as written, though i love the marinade so much, i sometimes up the amounts.  I use broccoli, but anything in this marinade is 5 stars.
Also works well with broccoli. Yum!
Made this several times before I used the scallions—and they make a noticeable difference. Don’t skip the scallions!
This is just so delicious and easy. Great use of chili crisp.
Love this sauce. Next time I’ll make extra marinade, pour some over the string beans and save for after the dish cooks (as suggested by others). I’m not a tofu lover but this is a game changer.

I made a double batch for the family of five and they devoured it down to the last tofu crumb, even Veda, who as you know never likes anything. I also have a green-bean hater lurking in the house, so I used broccoli rabe. Used rice vinegar instead of black, and baked at 400F for about 23 minutes or so to avoid smoking out the neighbors. Otherwise cooked it exactly as written. We'll definitely have it again.

This is just so good & so easy to make! If you canget the Chinese black vinegar, it’s worth it. I made it the first couple of times subbing in regular rice vinegar - still very good. I had a bunch of garlic scapes from my CSA this time - so good cut into longish pieces & added in with green beans.

My family limed this recipe.  Thanks!

Homemade “chili crisp”  is easy to make plus allows you to adjust spiciness:
½ cup neutral oil, such as grapeseed, peanut etc
2 tablespoons red-pepper flakes (or more to taste)
2 garlic cloves, peeled and thinly sliced
1 T Szechuan peppercorns, crushed…
~~~
Heat oil to 325 or until a peppercorn tossed into saucepan really sizzles.   Remove from heat, carefully add in other ingredients, cover and let steep for 20 min.  Store in airtight container.

I really like this recipe! I've cooked it twice now, using a different brand of chile crisp each time. The lesson there is that the taste and heat level vary a great deal between brands. I've also learned that I like the dish better when it's cooked for a little less time than Sam suggests - around 16 minutes at 450 kept the beans from burning but carmelized the tofu.

Great recipe. I followed ingredients and prep closely, but used my toaster oven style air fryer. Put the marinated tofu in first, air fried 5 minutes a side. While that was cooking, the green beans were marinating. Then cooked the for about 10 minutes, stirring after 5. Then mixed them together after air frying. Sublime! 
Served with the sweet and sour stir-fried radishes from NYT. A delicious vegan meal.
Best tofu ever, and super easy. The only change that is needed is to salt the green beans before roasting.

Half the chili crisp
Add ginger

Really easy and delicious.  
I always double the amount of sauce and beans (they're good for you). 
I also blanch the green beans and then spread them out on the pan to air dry before tossing with oil.
Very good... You don't need to season the green beans because the marinade will seep from the tofu and flavor the green beans.
Please move the ginger up with the other marinade ingredients, I miss it every time!!

In our house, (maybe also in yours?) a block of tofu serves 2, maybe 3 people. This is delicious. I followed the recipe exactly and it served two. We chose to skip the rice and didn't miss it, maybe because our green beans were super fresh from the farmers market.

I was a little skeptical looking at the recipe. ("How do the beans even tie in?") But WOW. I will be making this again and again. For reference I used Momofuku Black Truffle Chili Crunch, and no cilantro because I was out of it.
I didn’t have black Chinese vinegar…I substituted 3/4 rice vinegar to 1/4 balsamic vinegar.

My whole family adores this recipe. Today I discovered a game changer: switching out the green beans for a mix of eggplant and broccoli! I sliced Japanese eggplant into walnut-size pieces, cut up a bunch of broccoli into thin stalks, and otherwise followed the recipe exactly. It was even more delicious than the original, if that's possible.
Very tasty and easy to prepare. Don’t hesitate to use other vegetables. I had some carrots, broccoli, baby red potatoes on hand in addition to green beans. Made two dinners and lunch for me.

Whenever I'm baking or roasting vegetables on a sheet pan, I spray the pan with canola cooking spray and then spray the food with EVOO in a Misto. It's easier, quicker and less messy than trying to evenly spread oil over food in a pan.

Terrific recipe. Super easy, quite fast, really delicious.   One of those all time great recipes that seems way more impressive than you’d expect from the time and effort. I used Trader Joe’s cranberry orange chili crisp because that’s what I had, and I don’t think anyone would’ve been able to tell—doesn’t scream cranberries for sure. I also subbed in rice vinegar because I’d never heard of black vinegar and certainly didn’t have any. This is definitely going in the rotation.

450 degrees too hot - had to reduce temp to 425. 
Used 2 tbs chili crisp

This was so easy to make and packed big, delicious flavors. The only modification I made was to use my tofudee press to dry out the tofu. Then it absorbed all of that delicious marinade. My husband dislikes “meatless Mondays” but he said he would eat this dish every Monday.

11/23
Made for Darcy with homemade NYT chile crisp.  Had with black rice.
Excellent but use a lot more beans!
This dish is in my rotation and has been for quite a while. Tonight I realized I had no green beans and instead sliced a red cabbage very thin and used that it was delicious!
Terrific and quick

Fabulous recipe that often saves the day! I cut down soy sauce a little and instead of green beans, I use carrot sticks, Swiss Chard stalks (or the like), and a few sliced Shitake mushrooms as the surrounding vegetables for a more pleasing and succulent dish.
This is way more delicious than you think it will be. Double it!
Basically double the amount of green beans asked for in the initial recipe, and increase the sauce proportion by 1.5

Fantastic flavors and such an easy meal! I upped the amount of sauce because I knew I'd want more. I substituted balsamic for black vinegar, used a chili crisp I found at World Market which was gluten free, and used gf soy sauce.  I also threw in some mitake mushrooms on top of the green beans because I had them. Delicious, will definitely make again.
i made this again for probably the 20th time.  Did not have fresh ginger or cilantro, didn't matter - it is just fantastic.  Don't forget the rice!

Inspired by MB's note that this marinade is good on anything, I adapted this recipe for the fish I had handy.  I used two filets of dorade in place of the tofu; left it in the marinade for the first 15 minutes of the green beans' roast, then added the fish to the pan and roasted undisturbed for the last ten minutes.   Not as cheap as tofu, but also very easy and delicious.

I set the oven at 425 and put the pan in for 25 mins.  Chili crisp was completely burnt and the tofu was terrible.  The green beans were delicious.   If I made this again, I'd put just the green beans in and then add the tofu later so it was only in the oven 10-15 mins.  But I won't make this again.
This is absolutely delicious, just as it’s written. Be sure to marinate the tofu for at least 30 min. I usually do it for an hour. So good!

This was spectacular with a few revisions. I'm a long time tofu person. I used 2 Tbs. chili onion crunch and skipped the sesame oil. For the veggies I used the green beans, Japanese eggplant cut in 1/2" rounds (amazing - tasted like little puff pillows), and add 8 large cloves of peeled garlic. Cut the tofu into 12 large cubes, marinated all day. Put in oven at 35 minutes, added the eggplant and garlic  (coated with the marinade) at 25 minutes, then the beans also marinade coated at 15 minutes.
Got my carnivore boyfriend to enjoy this. Delicious. The marinade is insane. I’ve made it several times and recommend doubling it.

Have made the recipe a few times. Yesterday, I did not have chili crisp or black vinegar. Substituted 1 tbls gochuchang and a little olive oil for the chili crisp. Used balsamic vinegar instead of the black vinegar. Also tried broccoli instead of greenbeans. The same cook time worked (10 minutes and stir and another 12 minutes) and it was delicious! Also doubled the amount of tofu without doubling the marinade and it was terrific and I have leftovers : )
Loved it & now addicted to chili crisp!

Delicious all in one pan tofu & veggies! The suggestion that it marinates while the oven heats up to 450 was the perfect amount of time - the flavors were well absorbed for cooking. Served over rice, the juices from the marinade (be sure to pour the remaining liquid on the pan as Sam suggests so you don't lose any), is perfection.

One the best (and easiest) tofu dishes on this site. Followed the recipe to the letter. Used Fly By Jing Sichuan Chili Crisp. Only modification was substituting rice wine vinegar for Chinese black vinegar, which I could not find. Served with leftover basmati rice that had been cooked in coconut water. This was more like 2.5 servings because it was SO good and we kept going back for more!
Love love loved this! Made purple rice with it. Yummy.
Wow...this was sooo easy and so tasty!  Served with rice and pot stickers from Trader Joe's.  Will definitely make again!

I don’t know if the chilli crisp I used (in the U.K.) is different to the ones you have there, but I used less than the 3tbsp in the recipe and it was beyond spicy. I love chilli but it totally defeated me! Still, before I gave up it was delicious and would make again, but adjusting the chilli crisp quite a bit!

mscmfw: I have cooked with chili crisp more than a few times, and recently bought some Don Chilio Habanero CC. The first time I used it I actually thought my head was going to explode. Lesson learned.  For this  recipe I used a scant Tbsp rather than the 3 Tbsp called for and it was perfect for me. Like many cooking ingredients, not all chili crisps are created equal.
Made this for the 2 of us.  I used  Momofuco chilli crisp.  It was great.  A little too hot for me but N loved it. I will double next time except for the chilli

I was not a fan of this, but I did love how easy it was! Sadly, it relies too heavily on the chili crisp. I used momofuku, and although I like a little kick, I agree with another reviewer who said it was just a spicy slap in the face. It’s too bad because it smelled delicious! I would love to make this again with a homemade less spicy chili crisp, but that sort of defeats the purpose of it being easy… alas, this one was a miss for me!

There are many types of chile crisp and often, the Japanese brands are less spicy than other Asian chile crisps. I find this one to be on the milder side of spicy: S&B UMAMI TOPPING Crunchy Garlic with Chili oil. S&B also has several types of chile crisp with different flavor profiles.

This was excellent! Didn't have enough room for the tofu and beans on the same sheet pan, so I put them on separate pans with the beans below. Tofu turned out great, but the beans were overdone.  Next time I'll put them in later and I'll also use a full pound of them. I also followed others' advice to reserve half of the marinade and put it on the tofu after it's done baking: this was excellent advice!

My husband loved it. It was WAY TOO spicy for me, even after I scraped off all the marinade. Not only too hot to eat, but heartburn that lasted into the next day. However, to be fair, I am very sensitive to spices. The flavors were quite interesting. I will try it again with a little less of spice.
Delicious and easy. I microwaved the tofu to drain. I added cherry tomatoes to the sheet pan.
This was enough marinade for about 4 slices of tofu and certainly not enough to  pour over. Double the sauce.

Tofu is very high in calcium and oxalates, which is kryptonite for folks with calcium oxalate kidney stone sensitivities.  I tried this recipe with a batch of firm polenta and had a glorious meal!  Added this dish to our twice weekly vegan-night menu rotation.  Thank you Sam and NYT readers for your suggestions on ginger, alternate vegetables, and a shout out to all of the fans of Lao Gan Ma chili crisp!
This is a standby for me! Honestly think it’s best with broccoli but cabbage is good too, any sturdy green vegetable.
So good! So easy! Trust me, this is a keeper!

Made exactly per recipe, which is highly unusual for me!). Super easy, quick and delicious. Fed 3 adults with no leftovers. Lao Gan Ma (old grandma) brand chili crisp (available at every Asian grocery) is da bomb.

i made this last night and my tofu-hating boyfriend loved it :) it was so easy! you have to use Lao Gan Ma, the best chili crisp! i also saved some of the marinate to put on after it came out of the oven - so good! ginger is definitely necessary. served with jasmine rice and green beans but i'll try broccoli next time :)

BTW, Chile Crisp, or as it is properly known in Chinese and latinized on the label - Lao Gan Ma or Old Step Mother - is not from Sichuan as the author above noted. 
It's from Guizhou.
400 F is hot enough. Pile marinade on top of tofu so it doesn’t burn. Add ginger.

Just hot and boring with little flavor profile.  if you just want to clear your sinuses, go ahead and make this but otherwise don't bother.  I like hot but it has to have actual flavor that is more than just hot.
I agree.  The marinade had so many different elements going in but in the end all that came across was heat.  Won’t make it again.

I must have done something wrong because the chili crisp marinade was extremely spicy.  So much so that we could hardly eat it.  We are used to spicy food so it has to be hot for us to say it was Hot!
The tofu was delicious and spicy, but the vegetables were a bit blah. Next time I would lay down the tofu and then pour the marinade on everything.
@Natalie my thoughts exactly I hope that works
Stir fried the green beans instead of baking them. Kept the tofu in the glass Pyrex and put under broil at end.

If you just Google ‘marinated tofu,’ many recipes will pop up that are better than this. I highly recommend cutting the tofu into small cubes and marinating it for at least 4 hours. Also if you saute them on the stovetop they won’t dry out.
This dish was exceptionally good. I didn't have chili crisp so I used siracha sauce and it was delightful. I also used dehydrated ginger that I put in my garlic press along with the garlic. YUM!

Our drop-in teen dinner guest was unenthusiastic to hear the entree was tofu. But upon demolishing her third helping of this recipe, she declared this was the best meal she ever had in her life. She was so happy! And my tofu-hating kid loved it too. Ten out of ten, will make again. I used chili crisp condiment from a Korean Grocer.

Pretty good for not much effort! Used broccoli florets instead of green beans. I softened four blocks of millet-brown rice ramen noodles, added a little canola oil, and cooked it along with the tofu and broccoli for a crispy noodle type accompaniment.

2 tbs Grated apple (instead of honey)
2 tbs Grated onion (instead of scallion)
1/2 rice wine vinegar and 1/2 balsamic vinegar
Minced basil
Minced cilantro
1 cup tbs Korean miso paste (instead of chili crisp) 
Lime juice
Sesame oil
Ginger 
Garlic
Soy sauce

Perfectly delicious (and relatively healthy) umami bomb! Try using broccolini instead of green beans - it was cooked perfectly following the recipe exactly, and provided a more interesting flavor profile.

I am relatively new to cooking with tofu and this was over the top delicious.  Can't wait to make it again and will probably make a double batch.  didn't bother to press the tofu and it turned out great.  Also used the xtra spicy chili crisp from Fly by Jing as I like it spicy.  thank you for this recipe!

Ive faithfully used NYT cooking for years.  This recipe did not come out well.  Tofu and spiced marinade fine but baking green beans at 450 for 25 minutes makes no sense.  The beans were tough and almost inedible!
Made as directed but just doubled everything for leftovers. Very delicious and easy. I did microwave the tofu for 3 min to get out the excess moisture.

This recipe is now one of our fav tofu recipes. Not a huge fan of green beans so I use frozen broccoli florets instead, no need to thaw, just place them around the pan as directed in the recipe and the broccoli turns out super moist with plenty of crunch. Considering searing the tofu strips on each side after marinating, before going into the oven to add extra texture – anyome ever tried this?
Maybe because I used everything low sodium, it was good, light and tasty, but lacking the seasoning.

So so good! My tweaks: kept some chopped cilantro and scallions aside to sprinkle on top at the end. Tossed the green beans with some of the extra marinade before roasting - that was KEY. The green beans were crazy good. Roasted at 400 for 23 minutes exactly, it was perfect. Thank you Sam, and thank you to the other commenters!
subbed red wine vinegar for the Chinese black vinegar. microwaved the tofu for 3 minutes after slicing. this is delicious.

I enjoy Sam's writing but I have no idea what he's talking about in the last line: "But you could use the mixture on a mitten and have a very nice meal." I could find no alternate definitions for mitten.
I suspect he means that the mixture is so fabulous that you could use it on anything and it would make a good meal.

This has become a favorite!  And my DH doesn't usually care for green beans.  I re-ordered the instructions, making the marinade first and letting tofu it marinate while prepping beans and THEN turning on the oven. If not using extra firm tofu, I microwave the sliced firm for 3 min.   
I tried the Trader Joes Chili-Crisp then tried the Laoganma brand of  Spicy Chile Crisp. The Laoganma is way superior in flavor!  I'm using Balsamic vinegar in the marinade.
One weekly rotation.

I made this as directed, basically, except I used up a bunch of my veg - snap peas, zucchini chunks, mushrooms.  Excellent. Question (which I'll test out next time): any reason not to put any of the sauce on the beans while they're cooking?  Do they do better with just oil and sauced with chile crisp on the plate?
Great idea!  I think it would be even better with finely shredded cabbage instead of lettuce.  hanks! @Julia, London

Too spicy for us! I used 2 spoons of the chili crisp, not 3, but still too hot. I did learn the great tip of microwaving the tofu to get out the excess liquid though! Also the long beans came out tough and dry, despite being picked fresh.
@Dana yes too hot here as well and the jar of crisp was reviewed as mild.

So so good! Don’t forget the ginger in the sauce (it’s at end of ingredient list). I forgot and added it on top of tofu before baking. Served with veggie  brown rice (rice cooked with carrots, zucchini, cauliflower) and quick-pickled cucumber slices. Made with Trader Joes chile onion crisp.

The marinade was excellent! But I would give the green beans a solid 20 minute start in the oven. There’s no need for that tofu to be in there at 450 for 35 minutes, unless you enjoy the burned scallions and ginger pieces in the marinade.
This is a favorite. My grocery sells trimmed great beans in an 18oz bag so I use that. Sub balsamic for the black vinegar.

Absolutely delicious recipe. I used very spicy chili crisp from a popup restaurant in Forestville, CA. (Bazaar Sonoma). I doubled the tofu after pressing it for an hour. Marinated for 20 mins then heaped the marinade on the tofu, keeping some aside. Tossed green beans w olive oil and salt  Roasted everything for about 25 mins at 400*. So so good with leftover marinade on rice.
I made 1/3 less of the sauce and used an olive oil oil spray in the pan. It’s delicious. I think it could have been too greasy otherwise.

I used Momofuku chili crunch and this was a little too spicy for me. Next time i would cut back on that. I ended up pushing most of it off after cooking to get a heat level i could tolerate.

I used super firm tofu (even firmer than extra firm) which i thought worked.

Because of another reviewers suggestion i baked at 400 degrees but that wasn’t hot enough to blister the green beans.
Easily my favorite thing I’ve made from NYT - the amount of effort I put in doesn’t match up with how good it is

This was delicious and came together quickly. I had asparagus rather than green beans so I used them. I also needed to serve more people so I roasted onions and peppers with the asparagus. The ginger elevates the marinade to the next level. Served with coconut rice. What a yummy, versatile, quick dinner option!

I've made this recipe sooo many times. I used to sub black vinegar for a mix of rice vinegar & balsamic. I finally found black vinegar at my grocery store and wow- what a difference! I loved it before but I LOVE it now.
Go to recipe for tofu - delicious!
If you want less spice, the recipe works great with the chili crisp reduced to 1.5-2 tsp!
@Cameron agree. Worked well. Thank you for the suggestion
Renee, it’s quite spicy but not searing like chili oil. It has a fantastic crunch.
Any substitution for chile crisp condiment?
Delicious with brown rice. I’m eating it right now. A winner!
What if you don't have Chile Crisp?  Can you substitute?

I love the chili crisp and I do not like super hot spices. It has a lot of flavor and great texture. It is also very cheap (I bought mine on Amazon after the original NYT article), so, if you do not like it you are out very little. I got three jars for $11.
Cheapest I saw just now on Amazon was about 14 bucks for 26 oz.
What can be used in place of the black vinegar to get a similar flavor?
Cheap balsamic vinegar.  Do NOT use your good stuff as it will be too sweet.  If not that, then Sherry vinegar.
What can I substitute for Chinese black vinegar?
What is Chinese black vinegar?  What is a good substitute for it?
I used aged balsamic vinegar I had and it tasted great in the dish. I don't know black vinegar is or how it should taste in the dish but the other vinegar worked.
It's also sold as Chinkiang vinegar.  You can find it online if it's not available locally.
Substitute for black vinegar?
Balsamic vinegar works fine.
I don't have this condiment, can I use adobo chili paste?  Also, our family has a low spice tolerance, so how much would people suggest using?
Very tasty! I ended up cooking it on the stove as a stir fry and it was well balanced and full of flavor. Added a poached egg on top.

I don't have chili crisp but I do have a packet of hotpot soup starter that is basically oil with szechuan pepper and other spices! I have been meaning to mix up a tahini-soy sauce to replicate the sesame sauce from Shandong in Oakland. Love the idea of roasting the tofu and green beans instead!
What is chilli crisp? I am in Australia. We have lots of asian supermarkets but I am not sure what to look for?
It is sold in Australia by Woolworths as Laoganma Chilli Paste.
Do you need to drain the tofu first for this?
Yes, the ingredient lists calls for drained tofu.

I've had a jar of Lao Gan Ma chili crisp in my pantry for a while, but haven't used it much since my partner has a gluten allergy and I'm unsure of whether it would be okay for my partner to eat it. My reading of the jar's ingredients tells me that the chili crisp condiment is gluten-free, but not sure. Can anyone confirm one way or the other?
The recipe calls for green beans but the photo shows asparagus. Can it be substituted with no change in the recipe?
Those are green beans in the photo. You can do asparagus, but would have the cooking time be less. No one likes overcooked asparagus!

I 'discovered' chili crisp by accident about 5 months ago.  Since that time, I have become addicted and cannot fathom not having a jar in the fridge and jars in the cabinet. I wonder - how did I ever live my life not knowing about this condiment? There is nothing that is not better with this condiment. And I use the original.

Easy, healthy and delicious.  Made with asparagus, a shallot (instead of scallion), and medium firm tofu- pandemic improvising.  Also used the TJ version.  Looking forward to leftover tofu sandwich for lunch. Total keeper.
There are recipes online for homemade chilli crisp!
The description mentions ginger but no ginger is listed in the ingredients.
topping for tofu, with black vinegar, a little sesame oil, a wisp of honey, minced garlic and ginger, along with scallions and cilantro.
Ditto to Randy's comment. Trader Joes Onion Crunch condiment is always nearby for us. Goes great on everything!

Thank you, Martha! I love the LGM chili crisp, but it is so outrageously salty that I find I need to adjust my recipes when I use it to counterbalance the salt content. I love the idea of making my own.

Made this with LMG chili crisp and previously frozen tofu which sponged up the marinade immediately.  Very fast and easy.  Will do it again.  Want to try the ATK and NYT recipes for the chili crisp as the commercial one is not particularly healthy.  Served with some leftover polenta which worked well.
So delicious. Hunkered down, I had no green beans so used purple sprouting broccoli from our farmer's market box. I will make this again and again.
Good for amount of prep time, but not great; maybe try again with actual chile-crisp (rather than stuff from Yuki Yama)
Tasty, but spicier than expected.  (Probably equivalent to 3 out of 5 stars.). We used Lao Gan Ma chili-crisp.
