Has anyone tried Fly by Jing? I am a huge fan of Lao Gan Ma in its various iterations and just stocked up on it at 99 Ranch where it retails for $2.79/jar. The $15/jar for Fly by Jing...well, I need some reassurance it is somewhere near 5x better!

Fly by Jing is not nearly as good as Lao Gan Ma chili crisp. We've been eating the LGM version for years, but bought a jar of Fly by Jing after seeing this article a couple of years ago. 

A huge disappointment, bland by comparison to LGM. That jar of Fly by Jing remains almost untouched even today, but we've gone through several giant jars of LGM chili crisp since then.
Weird combination of very bland (tofu) and not very flavorful spicy. On the other hand,  would definitely toss beans with chili crisp and roast. Good flavor. Wouldn't make tofu again.

The marinade for this dish is out of this world and the dish turned out really delicious.  We ate every last morsel.  It's asparagus season, not green bean season, so I made that change.  I pressed my tofu for a few hours under a plate and a weight, a maneuver that I think always improves its texture.  I still found the tofu a little toothless, so I think I might pop it in the freezer for half an hour before marinating when I try this again.  I'll also try the marinade on other things!
Excellent dish! Just curious...in your narrative you mentioned ginger, but it is not listed in the ingredients nor mentioned in the directions....if ginger should be added, how much?

I had never heard of chili crisp before and I am now a total fan! I used the Bon Appetit recipe with rice wine vinegar. 

This was a real winner.
Forgot to mention that I only used one star anise since Bon appetit reviewers said recipe amount was too strong.

Surprisingly light and beautiful dish,. I served it with jasmine rice and made it using Trader Joe's Chili-Onion Crunch as recommended by others. This TJ product will now become a pantry staple! I'm also used to putting in *a lot* of time patting, waiting, tending, and otherwise wasting many (now-precious) paper towels on drying-out tofu. I love that the tofu in this recipe stood up well to a quick dry and pat down. Will definitely make again and again.
This might work for you? For years I've used a clean, dry cotton kitchen towel to wrap up my tofu and leave it on the drain board. Then into the wash and no waste, no trash.
Tofu Press. Works beautifully, and no more wasted paper towels.
@world vegetarian yes!! I hate when recipes say use paper towels!! Thanks for reminding readers that we don't have to use single use disposable landfill fillers.
Excellent recipe! Made as is, next time will make double the marinade
This was my first time ever using chili crisp in a recipe. This is my new favorite thing in the world!! YUM!
Splurge for the good stuff or make your own. This stuff is addictive and crack-like — also those things slices of tofu were nice and crispy — this whole meal is yum.
TY Randy for the note about Trader Joes! I have been buying Lao Gan Ma Chili Crisp (just got two more jars today) on Amazon but Trader Joes may be easier.
SO GOOD! I loved the Fly By Jin Sichuan  Chili Crisp that Sam recommended.

I made a big batch of chili crisp, based off of the recipe from serious eats. I used Arbil chilis, and then liquidated my dried chili collection for the rest, so good! https://www.seriouseats.com/2018/04/chili-crisp-spicy-salty-crunchy-tingly-and-good-on-everything.html

Made this exactly as written except added a bit of ginger and glad I did. Easy and delicious, well worth making. Satisfies the craving for authentic Chinese food, not the watered down American kind. Wil add more cilantro and, yes, garlic, next time. A winner.

Absolutely delicious!  I also used Trader Joe’s Chili Onion Crunch in place of chile-crisp (Thank you, Randy) and it worked beautifully. So happy to have discovered that wonderfully flavorful product.

Quick easy and tasty. Even the grndsons liked it. Did not have chili crisp so I improvised. (I have seen another recipe on this site using chili crisp from scratch. With shrimp as I recall. A search will find.)

Yum! Comes together quickly! I only had frozen cut green beans on hand, and also substituted Worcestershire sauce for the black vinegar. Used Laoganma brand Chili Crisp. Served with fancy Kokuho Rose rice because we are lucky enough to have a giant bag.

It was good (made with TJ's chile crisp) but not crispy enough, which has been the case for any baked tofu I have made. Next time we will marinate the tofu, coat it in cornstarch, and pop it in the air fryer (as a substitute for stir frying, which would be equally delicious but less health), and use the marinade as sauce.
This was easy and delicious and I’m now addicted to chili crisp. Will definitely be making this again!

The bon appetit chile crisp recipe was great! Just took a while to slice everything paper thin by hand. I burned the shallots and garlic in my first attempt, but it was worth a second try. It's best to freeze the tofu overnight and thaw before using in any recipe like this. It seems to make the texture better. The whole family liked this recipe!

This was really delicious and easy. Made it almost to the letter, except: I didn’t have cilantro, so skipped it, and was low on chili crisp, so I used two tablespoons in the marinade and saved what was left for serving. Will absolutely add this to the rotation. Next time, I might add a little cornstarch to the marinade just to help make the outside of the tofu super crispy, but it was great without it.

I used silken tofu because that was in my fridge and it was fabulous, melted in my mouth like a sauce.  Didn’t have chili crisp, so used Sambal Oelek chili paste, which I love.  Love this recipe!  Over brown rice, my preference.
Great flavor and easy to make. We used S&B chili oil with crunchy garlic. My wife would have like the tofu to be firmer. We had extra firm tofu, but it was pretty silky after cooking.

This was divine and so easy!  I had a jar of Lo Gan Ma chili crisp and used it for this.  Delicious!   I didn't think it was hot at all and actually would make it hotter the next time.  Perfect on all kinds of things, like eggs, rice, etc.  Can't imagine that Trader Joe's would be better.
Wow! I doubled the recipe and also added the sauce to the beans. My teens ate every last bite. We all LOVED it.
I agree with adding ginger.  The Chile crisp recipe from Bon Appetit is to-die-for.  Definitely make this sauce, over buying if you have a bit of time.  It only takes 40 mins.

Well I was curious about the Fly by Jing chile crisp. It costs $15 plus $5.99 shipping which is pretty pricey when compared to the Lo Gan Ma I get for $2-2.50 at the local Chinese market, 99 Ranch. The report: yea it is better, considerably better, more subtle and rich. But worth it at almost over 8 times the price - dunno. Probably not.

I thought a similar thing with Fly by Jing vs. Trader Joe's. FBJ is definitely better. The TJ's is sort of one-dimensional and flat in comparison. But the FBJ costs so much more, plus you have to pay shipping. I will try Lo Gan Ma next. I'd love to see FBJ in stores. I'll pay the premium but the shipping may be a dealbreaker.
Garnish with a healthy squeeze of like before serving. Yum!
Enjoyed this a lot! Subbed Thai basil for cilantro, balsamic for black vinegar; added ginger, extra garlic/scallions; roasted other veggies.
Great recipe we love it and have also become addicted to chili garlic crisp since discovering this recipe.  It’s on my Amazon regular delivery.

Outstanding, and easy to prepare.  The single sheet pan makes prep and clean up simple for a mid night meal.  The Fly by Jing chili crisp turns the tofu into something special (we are so so on tofu but are trying to eat less meat).
Give it go!

For non vegetarians try this with skinless chicken thighs.  The hot Chili Crunch serves as crisp chicken “skin”.  Yummy.  Actually short of putting Chili Crunch in my coffee ... I use this in so so many ways!  Right off the spoon is a-ok too. ;-)

Very tasty!  I wanted to make more veggies so left the tofu in the baking dish it marinated in and used the whole tray for vegetables. I set the oven at 425 after reading the comments about the sauce burning and it turned out very nicely. I will make it again!

Made this for lunch today; cooked it stovetop (sauteed the beans in a separate pan from the tofu, which marinated >20 minutes first) instead of oven baking.  DELISH!!  Can't believe I ignored Tofu all my life! (till now)

We didn't have any cilantro, so sprinkled with extra scallions - still so good. Glad someone recommended Trader Joe's Chili Onion Crunch - that made it much easier.  I can't do super-spicy and this had "just enough" kick to it. Will be in our regular Meatless Monday rotation...

This recipe has become a favorite in our house since it was published and I’m starting to play around with it. Tonight I added a sliced onion to the green beans in roasting and it added even more dimension. The sweet caramelized onions work perfectly with the vinegar and soy marinade.
Chili crisp is my new go to. My carnivores agree this is delish. I took the advice of a few others and grated in some fresh ginger but otherwise this is perfect as is!

Just finished eating this, and it is YUMMY -- I could have devoured the entire pan by myself. Would I regret this? Yes, but probably worth it. I used Trader Joe's Chili Onion Crunch and it was delicious. The only thing I would advise is to press out as much moisture from the tofu as possible -- I would have liked mine (I used super-firm) a little more solid. In the last 15 minutes I also tossed in some broccoli that I needed to use up, and I liked that even more than the green beans.
We loved this. I used broccoli in addition to the green beans, and of course extra garlic.

This was really very good - marinade/sauce is delicious. Used balsamic vinegar for the Chinese black vinegar. Found the chili crunch at TJ; had never seen it before. Used a smaller amount of tofu, and marinated (separately) some large shrimp. Tossed the green beans and lots of sliced ginger with a bit of oil; cooked them and the tofu as directed on a baking tray, but longer, at 400 degrees. Added the shrimp and continued baking until shrimp were just done.

I really liked this recipe.  It is super fast and tasty and the only unusual ingredient was the Chile-crisp which you can get at TJ's.  I looked up the Bon Appetit (mentioned by Leslie) for the Chile-crisp recipe and it looks pretty straight forward and easy.  I'm going to try that so I can make this more often.  My husband wants it inserted into the rotation.

As David said in another review, this recipe left out the ginger which was in the introduction.  I added a small, minced knob.
I wanted to eat this even before it went into the oven.

Soooo since quarantine the cashew tofu has been a staple in my house. I made this the other night and my vegan fiance was blown away! It is honestly so flavorful and EASY!  I went with a union foods chile oil instead of chile crisp and it turned out amazing! I served it with green beans and rice noodles that were served cold and dressed with: soy sauce, garlic, ginger, tahini, coconut sugar, and rice wine vin.  Thanks NYT, ya did it again!!! My subscription is worth every penny.

I put the tofu in (didn't do the green bean part as I had bok choy to use up) for 10 minutes and it scorched at 450F. Did I miss something? The temp did seem awfully high but I trusted it. Now my eyes are watering!

Made this for dinner tonight and it was delightful! My first time making tofu. Per the advice of my vegetarian friend, I pressed out the moisture from the tofu slab with a cast iron skillet onto folded paper towels for about half an hour before slicing. Used the French haricot verts green beans. Otherwise followed the recipe as written. Will definitely be making this again.
Had to order the black vinegar online but all the other ingredients were at my local grocery store. Delicious and easy recipe. I’m sure it be good with any vegetable.
Tofu was flat. Lots of work.

Made 02/12/20. Loved it! Weighted tofu to remove water for about an hour; added chunk of chopped ginger but otherwise followed recipe. 20 min at 425 on convection roast was enough; served over quinoa.
Fly by Jing chili crisp is the best!

Just made this recipe using Trader Joe’s chili crisp. Was super easy and excellent! I did take one of the reviewers advice and put most of the crunch on top after marinating the tofu and baked up just fine. Had tossed the string beans with oil, s&p and used cauliflower rice as the accompaniment. Thank you for these great flavorful easy recipe!
great!

Three stars, because Sam's version is WAY too much work.  Just put the drained tofu and beans on a sheet pan, drizzle a bit of the chile crisp on the tofu, then a bit of soy sauce, and oil over everything, and roast until the beans look a little withered.  Put on some more chile crisp and serve with lots of rice--it will be spicy! You can toss this in the oven, put the rice on to cook, hop in the shower, and emerge to a finished dinner.  No chopping, no mincing.

Delicious and easy. Also added about 1.5" grated ginger to marinade which was excellent addition. Used Lao Gan Ma chili crisp in the marinade but then generous addition of Trader Joes chili onion crunch at serving time. (I love that stuff and can eat it with a spoon!)
Add some salt to the beans

This is a one-sheet pan winner! Perfect for a Tuesday night for two. Keep a close eye on the beans so they don't over-char. We source chili crisp in large jars from the local Asian grocery. Used firm instead of extra firm, still delicious. Resist the urge to doctor the rice - plain rice is just what's needed to balance the flavor bombs of the other components.

This was very good, enjoyed by all. I made the Bon Appetit recipe from the recommended note and it takes just a half hour to peel and slice the shallots and 2 heads of garlic, just FYI. I never got it to brown either. Just wanted to mention that, but this recipe was delicious.

12-2020
Made a fast, satisfying and delicious lunch (no rice).  Definitely make again!
Works great with broccoli too, same technique, and I used noodles instead of rice and tossed them with the extra marinade.  The whole family enjoyed it.

So good! Glad I read the comments, v helpful - subbed balsamic for the vinegar, added ginger (I love using the Dorot frozen portions, that and garlic, from Trader Joe's) as recommended by other users. Used TJ's chili crisp and added 2 Serrano peppers since we like it zippy. Another user said they made it more of a salad, which I liked so I included shredded carrots and half an avocado in my bowl. Easy, quick, filling, plus I love a relatively thrifty cost in ingredients!
I made this recipe with only on mod-I used firm tofu.  This comes together quickly and tastes wonderful.

If I can suggest a correction. Lao Gan Ma chile crisp is from Guizhou province not Sichuan province. Sichuan is well known for many other spicy delights. I traveled to the beautiful Guizhou province a few years ago and tour guides there were very proud of their native daughter Tao Huabi's creation of the Lao Gan Ma chile crisp (and business empire).
Excellent meal. Everyone loved it. I used rice vinegar instead of black vinegar. Next time I will double it for our family of four.
The marinade is really great. Used it on fish. Blistered green beans are never going to be a favorite for me. Give me chile crisp and crunch!
Loved this dish.  Couldn’t find Chinese black vinegar so Google said use rice vinegar.  Definitely will be on repeat here.
I bought Chinese black vinegar from Amazon the first time I made this dish. Then two days ago, I found this vinegar on the bottom shelf at my grocery store in the Asian foods section.
I want to make this tonight but only have chili oil sauce - is there anything I can add to it so I can use this instead of chili crisp?

Reserve marinade and cook down into a sauce (add more soy sauce/honey if needed)
Top with fresh cilantro
20 minutes in the oven
I made this with portobello caps when we were out of tofu and only had frozen green beans. Delicious.

"Chile crisp" originated in Guizhou, not Sichuan, just like Kung Pao chicken, another Guizhou culinary item appropriated by Sichuan. About the latter: http://www.theworldofchinese.com/2010/10/the-story-behind-kung-pao-chicken/
Hit this with a squeeze of fresh like at the end right before serving. This was so delicious I inhaled it.
I loved the marinade and have used it on fish (black cod) and shrimp. Both delicious and it didn't overpower. I didn't love the texture of the roasted green beans so will try cauliflower next time.

AMAZING RECIPE. We serve it with greens dressed in Miso-Ginger Dressing (Martha Rose Shulman). Like others, I used Trader Joe's Chili Onion Crunch, which was fantastic.  We've now tried this recipe with Chinese black vinegar and with red wine vinegar. Whole family likes the red wine vinegar much more and found the black vinegar (which we love for dipping dumplings) overpowering for this dish.
Delicious! Made with broccoli because it’s what we had in the fridge. Family loved it. It’ll be part of our normal rotation.
Reddit says mix of balsamic and rice vinegar possible for black vinegar. Like balsamic, but not as sweet.

Fly By Jing has the BEST Chili Crisp.  Better than Lao Gan Ma (I have both in my refrigerator).  The Traders Joes is really great for adding a kick to western foods because it is deliciously oniony but doesn't have fermented black bean.  But it's not a substitute for Chili Crisp in this recipe or other Asian recipes.

Sam, this is fabulous!
I made one change - sugar snap peas on parchment paper, tofu on top of the peas. Baked for 20 minutes in 425 convection oven. Heaven!
450 degrees too high in my oven.  After 20 minutes, the tofu was dried out and green beans burnt. May try again at a lower temperature as the flavor was good

Liked this a lot-bought more tofu and green beans to make it again this week. Remembered to buy honey this time! Could not find black vinegar-subbed balsamic and Worcestershire. Fed 2 people with only a little left over.

This has become a "go to" weeknight recipe for us.  I do not add the cilantro to the marinade because it just burns in the oven.  Instead, I sprinkle it on at the end.  Be sure to use parchment paper unless you want to scrub a sticky burned pan!  
The tofu will cook in about the same amount of time as another pan of broccoli, cauliflower, or blanched Brussel sprouts tossed with garlic and oil.
Served on top of steamed cauliflower rice, this is a delicious vegan meal.
Next time I would double the amount of green beans and put them in the middle of the baking sheet and the tofu on the edges. Tasty!

I used homemade chili crisp that came from a local Dim Sum restaurant - very good. Forgot to add ginger, that would have been good. Used snap peas instead of green beans and I salted and peppered them along with oil. Next tome - parchment paper!
I have tofu and I have green beans. I have no idea what "chile-crisp" is. Can I just leave it out? (Not going to try to buy it, we are in the middle of a snowstorm here.)
Lots of recipes online: https://www.bonappetit.com/recipe/chile-crisp.
My mother used to say "You can put that sauce on an old shoe and it would taste good!!"
Or apparently, a mitten, as Sam wrote in the headnote. (I don't get it.)

Where is ginger in the ingredients mentiiined in the directions?
PS I have made this recipe and everyone loved it!

-Bon Appetit has a great recipe for chile crisp, you can make it at home yourself without any special equipment and it's not behind a paywall. It takes around 30 minutes to make and lasts for months.
- Instead of fussing with towels and plates and weights to drain the tofu, I took another reader’s tip (from a Japanese cooking show) and microwaved the sliced tofu in a bowl for three minutes on high. Amazing how much water drained out, leaving the tofu with perfect texture to soak up stuff
I added cherry tomatoes to the green beans, as well as onion wedges. Everything roasted beautifully. This is a great and easy dish.

I followed the recipe to a "T" instead of my own instincts. The beans were not cooked through and flavors were not melded. we are now taking them out of the oven pan and cooking them on the stove like we normally would do. I have cooked for many years and at 92 I realize that many new recipes are not worth the time.

I did not add 1/4 C red pepper flakes, I cannot tolerate that level of heat, I added 1 TBSP instead- and altered the type of peppers to personal "heat" tolerance - with those changes,  I must say this is one of the best tofu recipes I have made and eaten (vegan for decades). It was delicious! The blend of spices and herbs along with the thirst like quality of tofu - the perfect vegan protein to drink up all the flavors - was genius! Don't change out the stringbeans, they were the perfect accent!
I do not see where it says 1/4 c red pepper flakes?

I was concerned this would be too spicy hot, but I didn't find it so.  Nose ran, but that was about it.  We both enjoyed this dish.  Might try it with asparagus or broccoli next time.  Had been trying to figure out what to do with the jar of chile crisp...   I mixed it all up way in advance and that might have softened the 'crisp'.

The marinade is to-die-for! I made a large batch of it that I can use for meals all week. I didn’t have Chinese black vinegar on hand, so I mixed equal parts balsamic vinegar and rice vinegar - it worked great.
Unfortunately I see that the Lao gan ma chili crisp has MSG
Delicious!

This was awesome! I had to do a couple of things differently...because...real life. I ended up marinating the tofu for 24 hrs in the fridge. And then my regular oven refused to heat up so I ended up roasting the tofu separately in a counter top oven/toaster oven and blistered the beans in a wok. Amazing. I am not sure I would do things differently! I served with rice vermicelli but that takes 5 mins to be ready!

We loved this. We used Trader Joe's as others have but may have to make or find the real chili crisp. We didn't do a carb so two of us are the whole thing (with about 1# of green beans). I made sure to toss the beans with what marinade was on the sheet pan to get the flavor. Will definitely make this often.

This dish was delicious. It was my first time cooking with chili-crisp. I accidentally used three rounded tablespoons, which was too spicy for my sensitive taste buds. Next time I want to try 2 1/2 tablespoons with an added 1/2 tablespoon of sesame oil to make up the difference. (Most people will be fine with 3 tablespoons of chili-crisp.) I used sliced broccoli florets instead of green beans, and it worked out great.
Such a perfect recipe. Hubby made this as written and it was absolutely delicious. This was our introduction to chili crisp...yum.

I made this for dinner--yummy! I used the Trader Joe's chile-onion relish. Trader Joe's also had "ready to use" haricots verts (12 oz) so all I needed to do was open the bag, swish under water, and put on the sheet pan. Talk about easy! Two of us ate half, now we have two lunches for tomorrow. Yum!
Delicious and super simple. Make this!

We’ve made this three times now. Have to order some more chili crisp. Discovered that the directions talk about ginger but there’s no ginger on the ingredients list. How did hefty portion of chopped up ginger. Yum Yum Yum
This is delicious. And for meat eaters, it works well with a chicken breast or two alongside or in place of the tofu.
Love this with 'Soft' tofu
Yowza!  Total winner.
Absolutely stellar! Can't wait to make this again!

This wasn't as good as I had hoped it would be. The green beans were close to flavorless. I have had better luck with other cooking techniques using the similar ingredients. "Sheet pan" cooking is not good for everything.
green beans need to be blanched before roasting
We really like this recipe. I'm experimenting with following recipes exactly (!) and have been rewarded with flavor balances different than my 50 years of cooking have developed.
I sprinkled Aleppo pepper on the beans and roasted the whole shebang the second time I made this and it was transformed— that and changing the chili crisp I used to Fly by Jin, which was the kicker.

The minimal effort it takes to make this is well-rewarded. Doubled the recipe to have leftovers the next day and it was still great, maybe better, because the marinade had time to soak the tofu and green beans more.
add ginger

Use more than one tsp of harissa, maybe two, with 1 Tbsp Thai curry paste, OR Instead of both use TJs chili crunch. 
A little salt yo soy sauce on green beans
Extra firm tofu so you can turn and toss it. 
Others say put extra marinade on top of tofu, not on pan surface, or add it after cooking. 
Any grain-- farro, I say.
Great recipe, and versatile. Tofu presses are an investment that will make you want to eat more tofu. Added shitakes and broccoli ...great meal. Thank you Sam!
Love the recipe, but what might I sub for the black sesame oil? Don't have it and don't want to buy.
Not black sesame oil, but Chinese black vinegar. Sub half and half balsamic and rice vinegar.
This is awesome.  I added a bit more honey, and for added crunch, topped the whole thing with chopped pecans.  A divine gooey treat.  Yum.
PS.  I did not even try to the the extra moisture out of the tofu.

I didn't have any "neutral" oils like canola or grapeseed,   so I used toasted sesame oil, and the result was more complex and yummy in flavor.   Am I allowed to use the word "umami" if I don't honestly know what it means?

I like my tofu really firm.   So I buy the extra firm, slice it, and then put the slices between paper towels on the counter, a cookie sheet on top of that, and then a heavy pot or a brick on top of that.   Leave for an hour or so, change the paper towels once or twice, and it squeezes the water out.

Works nicely with asaparagus (cut in 1-inch pieces) instead of green beans. I leave the sesame oil out of the marinade and toss the asparagus in it rather than in neutral oil. Can cook at 400 or 425 rather than 450. Served with farro seasoned with soy sauce and sesame oil.
No one had an issue with the marinade burning? Next time I will cook the tofu w/marinade at a lower temp than the veggies.
I've made this twice and it was fine both times. I find 20 minutes is plenty in my oven.
This is easy, delicious, and so fast. Made with gai lan the first time and green beans the second. I think I'll try broccoli next (green beans got a bit tough, in my opinion).

I wish you had told us in step 1 to reserve some of the chile crisp to serve on the side, like you suggest in step 4.  By the time I 'poured the remaining marinade over the tofu' in step 3, I had no more chile crisp left!
Chili Crisp refers to the condiment in the jar. We just take the jar (and some soy sauce) to the table with us. I use all the marinade to pile on top of the tofu.

Thanks to the commenter who suggested the EZ tofu press, I can tighten the screws on a tofu brick to drain and slice in the time it takes to prep everything else and heat the oven. Love easy sheet pan recipes like this.
This dish has made it into our rotation. I probably make it every two weeks. We don't serve it with rice and add more green beans and the two of us eat the whole thing. It is awesome.
Really good. I guess i didn't have the best chili crisp because i had to add white pepper to get a real kick. But otherwise a winner.

This is too easy for so much deliciousness. 

Some commenters have suggested a tofu press. I don't have one, but I usually keep a block of tofu in the freezer. Once thawed, it's surprising how much water you can easily squeeze out, making it absorb marinades like a sponge.

I was a little TOO liberal in my use of the chile-crisp (probably used 4 instead of 3 tbsp, and I used authentic stuff to boot). The flavor was fantastic, but my husband couldn't handle the heat (I found it exhilarating, TBH). Will definitely make again, exercising a little restraint next time.

Easier than take-out and so good- a weekly treat. I usually add more veggies and this makes 2-3 servings for us. I prefer to use firm tofu and not press it for this recipe. Since it bakes in the oven it tends to firm up a little anyway. I don't flip the tofu and find that about 20 minutes (or a little less) is just perfect. I was a little skeptical about the short marination time but it's fine. Broccoli is our favorite veg for this recipe. Have used several types of chili crsip and all work fine

Delicious! Instead of baking, we crisped the tofu in a nonstick skillet with a little bit of oil until golden on each side, then added snap peas (didn't have green beans) and sautéed for about a minute longer. Added sauce and cooked over medium heat until slightly reduced, about five minutes. Didn't have black vinegar, so substituted a mixture of balsamic and rice vinegar. Served with white rice.

I have the Trader Joe's Chili Onion Crunch, which I understand is not as good as authentic chili crisp. When I looked up recipes for chili crisp, I found that many include cardamom and cinnamon. So I added those two spices to the TJ's chili crunch. I'm sure still not as good as the real stuff (which I will buy), but greatly enhanced!

Anyhow, this is a tasty, easy, quick dinner. I subbed broccolini for the green beans.
Chili crisp recipe would be nice.  I’ve seen it on other sites, but would be nice included here.
I purchased Chile crisp from Momofuko online. It was vile. Threw it out.
I highly recommend the Fly By Jing Chili Crisp. So glad they started making an XXL jar.

Really tasty. The chilii crunch (whole foods) was amazing. Would have liked a bit more color on the tofu - may cube next time and roast. Definitely need to  be careful with the marinade as it could burn easily.

Green beans are one of our least favorite foods, but I have made this several times. I use Fly By Jing chili crisp also and the combination of flavors in the sauce (with ginger!) is perfect. I wish I could find more tofu recipes as delicious as this one.

This comes out tasting like legit Chinese food. So good and the taste is so savory, it’s almost meaty. I subbed asparagus for green beans and it was delicious. This marinade would go great on anything, and there’s already plans in the works to put it on fish, as someone else on here suggested. 

The microwave tofu trip works great for removing excess water (3 minutes on high) so the tofu can soak up the marinade. My new go-to instead of pressing and paper towels, and no special equipment needed
This has become a regular in my weekly meals because it is delicious and healthy and satisfies my craving for chinese food. The cilantro is nice but not necessary. Try it, you'll love it.
Easy, delicious, and vegan! Not a lot of recipes do all three, let alone do all three well. This is going to go in my regular rotation.

I did as others suggested and microwaved the tofu for three minutes before pressing and marinating. The result was a bit too chewy for my taste.
Other than that the dish was fabulous!
I finished it with a big squeeze of lime, a drizzle of sesame oil, and a sprinkling of cilantro
Best tofu I’d had in a long time!
I don't care for cilantro so I leave it out.  Rice vinegar worked when I didn't have black vinegar.

Made this tonight using (TJ's Chili Onion Crunch) and it was delicious.  I only had soft tofu in the fridge and, using the Japanese microwave tip from @SLCatherine below, I can't imagine it could be any more craveable but... given comments below, I'll try it with Lao Gan Ma for comparison next and soon.

Excellent recipe, and nowhere near as spicey as I was expecting it to be. I may add some Szechuan peppercorn next time for a bit extra kick. As others say, add the ginger. 450 was also way too hot, my beans were burning after 7 minutes so I dropped the temp to 400.

This is always a favorite for a quick and easy meal. It’s also very adaptable to whatever veggies are on hand - broccoli, bok choy, etc. 
I appreciated the tip to microwave the tofu.

FINALLY ginger has been added to the ingredient list after I sent an email to the Cooking Cares team. Thank you for correcting that omission!
Great recipe, I did not have sugar so used about 1 tablespoon of dark soy sauce, and it was great. I added extra ginger and sesame oil and glad i did. A winner.
Made it with broccoli and drizzled some of the marinade over the broccoli too…..delicious.

used the microwave technique, didn't use beans, so just cooked them in the glass pan I marinated them in. about 20 min. So delicious! Served brown rice with salted butter, a nice counterpoint. No extra chili crisp needed here!

throwing my hat in the ring:
- cubed tofu
- used one pound of frozen French cut green beans
- added some ume vinegar to the green beans for seasoning and tang 
- used the broiler for 5 minutes to “blister” everything up a bit 
- turned out well enough to add to the list of weeknight dinner options! Success!

This did not marinate long enough for the flavor to get into the tofu. I would pour the remaining marinade over after it's cooked next time, as another reviewer suggested. It just tasted like chili crisp (which is delicious, so it was fine), which made me wonder why I did all that work for the marinade!
The ginger was perfect—as was the advice to keep the marinade to the side while the tofu cooked and then drizzle it on afterwards. Would be good with broccoli and with asparagus.
Grate ginger and garlic--easier
MAkes 1 meal plus lunch.

Odd that the ginger is listed at the end of the recipe - I ended up forgetting it because it wasn't listed with the other marinade ingredients. 
Overall a very tasty dish, even without.

Just here to add another +1 for the Fly By Jing brand of chili crisp. It lacks the weird slightly bullion-ish taste that some of us notice in the Lao Gan Ma, and you can really taste the floral note of the szichuan pepper.

This may not be an original idea, (as I haven't read all of the reviews), but I made this last night substituting extra large shrimp for the tofu because I had shrimp, not tofu.  Roasted the beans until they were a couple of minutes from being ready, then mixed in the shrimp, which I had marinated while the beans cooked.  Only a few minutes later all was ready and it was delicious.  What an easy, flavorful meal with the addition of some rice.  Preheat the baking sheet while the oven preheats.

Lovely recipe and I agree about not leaving out the ginger - note that it’s listed well below the other marinade ingredients. Made this to go along with a pho-style broth with fennel and rice noodles and roasted diced kabocha squash along with the beans. It’s a keeper.
The 'firm' tofu did well and made me want to stop using extra firm exclusively. It emphasized the flavor more with a creamier base

I had no chili crisp and tried it with an improvised blend if sunflower seeds and hot and sweet ingredients. It was terrific and a great foil for the soft tofu. I'll get some chili crisp for next time though.
Reduce chili crisp by one third to Two TBL.

Made according to the recipe. I had no idea how much I’d love this dish! So simple and delicious. I roasted a sheet of frozen riced cauliflower instead of rice. Flavorful, spicy, but light. Will be making this again.

3rd time I've made this. Added a little extra of all in marinade. Sliced tofu and put in microwave for 3 min, then poured liquid out of bowl.  Cooked at 400 for 20 min, and it was perfect! Hadn't cooked vegs with it before, and the sugar snap peas were very soft. I will try broccoli, or continue to cook vegs separately. Served on rice with just a little soy sauce on it. I"m addicted to this tofu!
This is excellent and easy. Also works well with slender asparagus in place of green beans.
If you're looking to make just the tofu in an air fryer, I found that 350 F for 15 minutes was perfect.

I made this tonight and absolutely loved it. The ease is a draw, but the flavor! I might have added extra minced garlic and ginger, and with Momofuku Chili Crisp, it has a real kick. I can’t wait to eat the leftovers for lunch tomorrow.
Had no black vinegar, used rice vinegar with a little fish sauce and black bean sauce instead. Was very good that way,

I love this recipe. Could you marinate the tofu for longer in the fridge to give it a make-ahead meal boost, and if so how long is long enough and/or how long is too long? Thanks for any ideas on that front.
Agree w/ Martha.  a longer marination only makes this better.  it is an 11 out of 10.
This is one of my favorites! Whenever I find good fresh green beans I think of this, buy some tofu and then it's dinner.

I have made this many times and find the best results are achieved by cutting the amount of chili cripsp  a bit and increasing the soy sauce, scallions and fresh ginger. Overall, a reliable and easy meal.
Added an orange pepper (in strips) to the beans, worked well and looked pretty. I really like this recipe, but in our family it's more like 2.4-3 servings.
I've made its several times, its excellent

What a great meal concept; so versatile. I made it as written a couple weeks ago and it was wonderful. Then tonight we had it with broccoli, bell pepper, and bok choi in place of the green beans. As one reader suggested I set aside some of the marinade and tossed it with everything at the end. I also added some shredded leftover chicken (warmed) at the end to stretch the meal for an unexpected extra dinner guest.
Super easy and delicious. Added mushrooms, yum.

My first time preparing tofu and thanks to this recipe it won't be my last! I'm so glad I tracked down chili crisp at our local Asian market (the version I found is called 'crunchy garlic'). I found a mild version so my kids might actually enjoy this next time I make it. The leftovers kept well, too.

I've made the recipe as listed several times, I like it!
I'm living in Mexico and chile crisp isn't so easily available, so I used salsa matcha (which is similar to chile crisp, but w/ Mexican flavor profile) and it came out pretty well!
-I still used a reduced amount of soy sauce for umami + liquid in marinade
-Replaced vinegar w/ lime juice (an appropriate vinegar could work, too)
-Replaced sesame oil with a reduced amt of olive/neutral/peanut/corn oil
-Increased amount of cilantro
-No ginger

For those who make their own Chile crisp … don’t break up the cinnamon sticks and anise like I did. If you do, you will end up spending a long frustrating time trying to fish them out of the tangle of shallots out of the soft solids you want to put back into the Chile crisp so you don’t have to eat the hard bits. Same goes for the Szechuan peppers someone suggested adding. Don’t.
What a fantastic dish! I subbed balsamic vinegar for the Chinese black vinegar. Spicy, crunchy and very tasty.
Good, but not memorable. Made according to instructions with Ma Go Lan chili crisp and Thai basil
Oops, edit to my previous review - made according to instructions with *Lao Gan Ma* chili crisp and Thai basil
I double the marinade recipe and toss all my veg in it. It works on most everything. Chard, asparagus, broccoli… dumplings, it’s just good!
Half of the marinade would be enough

This was soo good! I didn't have cilantro so did without. I also forgot to add the ginger until after soaking the tofu. It was still really tasty. I 'll definitely make this again although I'd salt and pepper the green beans which lacked flavor as I didn't add the rest of the marinade during the cooking and used it as a sauce.
My guests loved it. I’ll make it again.
This is so good! I went a bit light on the chili crisp and used broccoli—tossed in a bit of honey & soy—instead of green beans. This is going into our regular rotation.

I made my own chili crisp using the Times Cooking recipe. Even with that minor hassle, this came together very quickly. Results are excellent. I will be making this again asap, a great use for peak summer green beans.

This recipe is terrific and in regular rotation at home. Introduced me to black vinegar. I usually prefer using carrot sticks and slim broccolini spears to green beans. Make my own chili crisp-"ish" by frying shallots with hot pepper just before making dish. Love how it always comes out just like the photo!
Fan favorite

This is absolutely the best! I do not make it with beans but is is a go to for my work lunches! And just use what ever you have after you add in the chili crisp. Sometimes that is all I have and it is perfect. Really, just make this as a no recipe recipe.

I totally misread the recipe and missed the ginger and vinegar when I went to marinade the tofu - added them after the fact and didn't notice a problem. The chili crisp is the star here - I use Fly By Jing. Delicious. Added to the rotation for sure.
This was great. We actually substituted tempeh for the tofu on night as an experiment and it was almost better! Sadly, we had no green beans.

Have made this many times as directed with green beans, then branched out and tried it with zoodles and red bell pepper strips. Now, to use up a butternut squash, cubed it along with red bell pepper and tempeh squares (added them halfway through butternut cook) and it’s wonderful as well. The tempeh texture is a nice contrast with the butternut.

Hubby doesn’t like tofu, so I used salmon instead. Followed the general concept of the recipe: marinated salmon, cooked beans in oven at 450 for 12 min., added salmon, and cooked 12 min. more. Delicious. Also, stick with Lao Gan Ma. Have tried others, but keep coming back to the original.

The flavor is decent but oddly one-dimensional. I used “Mr. Bing” chili crisp which may be an inferior blend. The tofu was too soft- I thought it would crisp up a little in the oven. The beans were great though!
It's delicious but a little spicy. I would use two spoons of chili crisp in future. The recipe now has ginger which adds important flavor.

Good with just two T of chili crisp and some chili oil instead. 
And put the chili crisp over everything not just the tofu.

My only (minor) complaint is that I missed adding the ginger because it’s at the very end of the ingredient list after the “white rice, for serving”. It was still a tasty base, and we roasted extra green beans on a second sheet at the same time. Looking forward to making it again and actually using the ginger, but it’s super tasty, easy and forgiving. I can imagine it’ll be great for a variety of veg/protein/grain combos.
I made this as written. It was delicious, and the house smelled sooo good. Thanks, Sam!
Double to serve family
Best tofu I have ever made or tasted.  Thank you for this recipe!
Delicious and straightforward! Substituted balsamic-rice vinegar mix for the black vinegar. Added a little hoisin to the marinade.
Delicious. Easy. What’s not to love?

Enjoyed it with Hicks.
Will make again.  Added Ginger to the marinade.
Williamstown 
03/07/23

Love this dish   I cannot find black vinegar near me in Delray Beach, FL, so I will have to order it on line, but I used a little Mirin and used firm tofu, that I pressed.  Served with both white and brown rice.  Lovely!

This was too salty for me, I wonder if it is because of the chili crisp brand I used? Oh also I didn’t have the black vinegar and I used some rice vinegar and some balsamic vinegar to sub it. I will give it another try without subbing anything.

I find chile crisp hard to find (even more so now that I'm in Mexico). I've made this with chile crisp, yes, but I've also done some variations:
-Salsa matcha: tweak the other ingredients to be more Mexican. It works well (matcha has a very similar texture to chile crisp), but you really have to watch the heat (esp. if you're not using a mild matcha)
-Sambal oelek: I made it with this last night. It was good, and the spiciness level was nicely balanced. I missed the texture/crunch of chile crisp
found chili crisp in Walmart reasonably priced.  Expensive on line.

This dish did not inspire me.  I uses brussel sprouts which were excellent here.  The tofu  was bla and I let it marinate a lot longer.  I have a lot left over and I am going to use it for breakfast with an egg cracked over a slab.
5 stars! Easy, fast, delicious. Lots of opportunity to sub veggies or add more. Served with spicy Momofuko noodles, added shrooms. Crazy good!
Followed recipe but used firm and didn’t have Chinese black vinegar used sweet soy sauce.
Green beans weren’t quite ready yet so I used the last of the asparagus. Really tummy

We used chili garlic and olive oil and served over farro

Liked it a lot and would make again
Two tablespoons Chile oil
1 tablespoon chili oil instead of chili crisp

This is really tasty. Made as written using Trader Joe's chili onion crisp, and subbed broccoli, pattypan squash, and snow peas for the green beans. A squeeze of lemon on the tofu was a really excellent addition.
Nice spice! Took less time as was using baked tofu and cut green beans bite-sized. Maybe 20  min
This was great and so easy. Served it as a protein side with fish and a big chopped salad. Will definitely make again!
With prep, this takes about an hour
I used rice vinegar in lieu of the black vinegar, added some cabbage I had on hand, and served over jasmine rice. Delicious!!
excellent. Reduced the chili crisp to my taste. Second batch, I doubled the marinade and let the beans and tofu marinate in the baking dish. Served over couscous. Excellent simple recipe.
Topped with fresh scallion, kimchi, sesame oil, and sesame seeds to cut through the richness and add some sour/nutty/fresh notes. Absolutely divine and SO easy.
So delicious, so easy!  My favourite way to eat tofu!  Lao Gan Ma rules!  Subbed broccoli for beans and the nuttiness was a great complement to the marinade.
I had been looking for a way to use a block of Indian paneer cheese and used it instead of tofu. Fabulous!N
A tofu press is the ideal tool for extracting maximin liquid from tofu.  It also works gloriously well on a thawed box of frozen spinach!

This sounds really good and easy so will look forward to trying it. Lao Gan Ma chili crisp is ADDICTIVE! I can eat it on almost anything. Thankfully I work in the Chinese Quarter in Birmingham (U.K.) so it's really easy to get a hold of.

I followed the instructions to the "T".  It all started out promising.  The beans were very good.  The marinated tofu turned out to be quite dry.  The extra chili crisp added at the end helped, but did not really make it much better.  Perhaps this is just personal preference, but I do not care for dry tofu - or sandwiches or pasta, etc. etc.  A little bit of sauce might have helped.
Found this really bland. And the marinade burned on the sheet pan around the green beans.
Is there supposed to be ginger?  It is listed in step one but not in ingredients.
It's at the very bottom of the ingredient list for some reason, instead of with the other sauce ingredients
Started to make but don't see the ginger that's  mentioned in the headnote.
Too salty - cut soy sauce next time, water down sauce. Would be good with mushrooms or peppers

Use sesame oil on the beans
Add extra chives and cilantro

Great dish to share with my Japanese friend who says Tofu in Japan is never like this, but they love it! So good with jasmine rice flavored with whole-seed coriander, coconut oil and one spoonful of bouillon in the rice cooker. Yummy! I used garlic scapes for garlic. And made the chili crisp from the NYT recipe many times over, playing with a mix of paprika and chili flakes, so good on this and my favorite soft boiled eggs. Try it today!

My daughter said this was her  tofu. I might add a little water to the marinade to have more ‘sauce’ with the rice. The garlic burned and I’m not sure how to prevent that. It’s def going into my repertoire.
Best Chili crunch is Momofuku’s Black Truffle.  Amazing and wildly flavorful.
Addictive!
Delicious. Followed the recipe and it turned out beautifully.
Loved this! So easy compared to stir frying. I didn't see the ginger at the end but will include next time.
This is a go-to recipe for me and my friends. Make this twice a month minimum. Easy to throw together and delicious. Cannot recommend highly enough!
I had no cilantro, no scallions, no honey - and no chili crisp.  Substituted 2 tbs harissa w/ preserved lemon, grated ginger, and 1 tbs brown sugar.  It's a different dish, I'm sure - but very good!

We made this exactly as the recipe stated.
The beans burned badly, plus the chili crisp was toasted to the point of being inedible. We were very disappointed - the flavors seemed great. Not sure what happened as I did t see other notes about this problem.
Okay, I don't like chili crisp because I don't like hot peppers.  (Love the sweet ones!)  Could I use hoisin sauce or something else to replace the chili crisp here?
used chicken instead of tofu and basil instead of cilantro.  The whole thing was very good and a great way to perk up supermarket green beans.

Initially, even without pressing the tofu, I thought maybe the tofu would crisp up… so I baked everything for 10 mins longer. The tofu didn’t crisp, but I actually enjoyed it being soft with the rice. And the 10 extra minutes still left the green beans crunchy enough! Definitely will make extra marinade next time as others suggested. Didn’t have Chinese black vinegar so used shaoxing cooking wine and it worked great! Very delish

Made with 2 blocks of tofu and doubled the sauce/marinade - this fed 3 hungry people with not much left.  Used homemade chili crisp, probably a little less than recipe called for.  Had no cilantro but didn't notice its absence.  Quite yummy and easy.
I’ve made this three times in as many weeks. Jasmine rice is the perfect starch. Love it.
I will definitely add this to my list of favorite weeknight meals. So easy and delicious!
This was not a winner for us, the beans were tough, and the chile crisp we had maybe just couldn’t carry this dish. Will not make again.
Really good dish. Tasty, healthy and easy to clean up.
Delicious. Plenty hot for us so we served with a little white rice. Quick and easy recipe.
Had left over chili crunch from another recipe, so decided to give this one a go. Easy to make and delicious, with a great blend of flavours. Will definitely make again!

I could have used a little more black vinegar and a little less chile crisp (at least my super-powered Momofuku one). I think there's quite a bit of variation in the strengths of the ingredients, depending on the brand. Nonetheless, a delicious and quick recipe.

Absolutely delicious!!!
we made it with broccoli (we had a ton of it) but no other deviations. Served with jasmine rice. So easy. So good. I want it again already.

I’ve been making this for a few years now. I like Fly by Jing, but I prefer S&B Unami Topping Crunchy Garlic. It’s really delicious, and at $7/jar, cheaper than Fly by Jing. Also the NYTimes has a recipe for chile crisp - I haven’t yet made it by I was told it’s very good, and easy!

I LOVE THIS!!!
Saved the cilantro and green onion for topping it off. Easy, and absolutely wonderful. Had to push away from the table.

I didn't really like the Trader Joe's Crunchy Chili Onion stuff ( too crunchy) until I used it in this recipe.  I'm sold!  This recipe is a keeper, though I made it with cubed tofu and no green beans as I had none. It's super flavorful and easy.
Ok, so I only made the sauce for a stir fry, but this sauce is great!
Next time your at a solid Chinese restaurant look for Mapo Tofu. It is a standard among us Chinese.
I didn't know tofu was gradable.

If you haven’t tried it, ignore the people hating on Trader Joe’s Chili Onion Crisp condiment. It’s perfect for people who like food medium spicy. There are those of us who do, so please don’t assume everyone else shares your passion for super hot condiments.
this shows asparagus still
Add carrots, add water at end to make sauce

I was afraid to use all chili crisp so did half of that with a delicious scallion oil, and added sweet potatoes to the pan- yum! I wish there was more crispness/chewiness to the tofu. I might freeze and thaw the tofu next time.

for 2 things tofu 
- 1 tbs crunch 
- 4-5 tbs soy sauce 
-2-3 tbs ajonjolí 
-1-2tbs miel 
-2 tbs vinagre
-4 ajos 
-scallions as you see fit 
450 for 25-30 minutes great 
would be good braised (with soy sauce and caldo)
This is brilliant, and also an incredibly simple dish as the chilli crisp does most of the work. We served it with noodles and everyone wanted more!
I had to make one minor amendment, used mint to replace coriander because the latter had gone to seed. Easy, quick, and so tasty. This will be on rotation.

This approach yields rubbery slabs of tofu coupled with greasy,  vaguely salty and oily beans.  Even the chilly crisp was reduced to scorched garlic and oil.   If I bothered trying this again, it would be with soft or silken tofu marinated for a much longer time in a sauce that includes mirin for sweetness.

This was good, but the green beans were not as flavorful as I would like. I am tempted to dump the marinade over them too with the tofu. I think it would make a difference. Does anyone have any experience with that?

Meanwhile, I am going to try some of the suggestions and hear about adding garlic to them or salt.  I’m not a huge fan of green beans, truth be told. Lemon juice does work wonders with them though. Maybe that will help as well.

Thanks, cheers, but overall a good recipe and easy to make with the tofu. That is always appreciated.

I did just that, in the sense hat I tossed my broccoli florets right in the tofu marinade after moving the tofu slices to the baking sheet. Totally made a difference, and I will make way more marinade for next time to have some sauce.

If using Momofuku chili crunch, original version works great. I love heat, and this was plenty. This dish is really balanced, I thought. My bake needed a few minutes less than the suggested time, so follow your nose. Enjoy!

We’ve been making this dish at least once a month for years now. Sometimes we’ll swap out green beans for asparagus, and it works just as well. Super savory, flavorful, and filling while also being very low-effort for a weeknight.

This was great. I accidentally put 2.5 tablespoons of the sesame oil and only 2 tablespoons of chili crisp (simply because I was running low) in the marinade, and added some red pepper flakes. I tossed the green beans in the marinade (and added some white pepper) and then piled the rest on top of the tofu. I bought the tofu that's already drained, cut it and heated both it and marinade in the microwave (separately and then 30 secs together) to help soak up flavor. Used 2 sheet pans.

Fantastic. 

Sprinkled some dukkah on it before serving. Gave it a great added crunch and flavour.

Thanks, as always, NYTimes Cooking. I can no longer live without you!
Too spicy for our tastes.

I just came across this recipe three weeks ago and have already made it 3 times because it's so good. But, I realized as I made it tonight that I accidentally used salsa matcha instead of chile crisp last time and it's equally as good! I have also subbed green beans with asparagus, broccoli and cauliflower plus shredded cabbage and all works well

Proportions are way off. Made according to this recipe,using Trader Joe's chili crunch. the second time i tried it with 7 ounces of tofu and teaspoons of all the seasonings. This worked well for taste but there was so little liquid there wasn't much to cover the tofu and nothing for the green beans--or in my case broccoli since that's what i had. How could i add more liquid but keep the same basic proportions?

I doubled the marinade except the amount of chili crunch (to suit our heat preference), and when I was ready to make the dish, I put the tofu on the sheet pan and coated my broccoli right in the same dish where the tofu was marinating. Next time I will at least triple the marinade, because even doubling it, there was not enough to nicely coat the broccoli florets we had, but it might be enough for green beans or asparagus (fewer nooks and crannies for the marinate to go to). Fantastic!

Delicious! Very flavourful and well balanced. I had some left over "chili crisp crunch" from another NYT recipe - basically a chili crisp crumble made with cashews and panko breading -  which I added for texture and to use up, and holy moly. Just so good.
Such an easy, tasty recipe! I've never used green beans but use whatever other veggies I have on hand and it comes out great every time!

I doubled the marinade recipe, doubled the green beans and did a whole extra pan of green beans with marinade drizzled, which was excellent. And the perfect tofu/bean ratio. Also, 425 did the trick for me. Microwaving tofu trick is genius for making this a fast weeknight dinner.

As other shave said, husband who doesn’t like tofu was eating seconds and leftovers.
The recipe is a favorite.  I add more beans and increase the sauce by about 30%.   Always have leftovers for lunch.   Lao Gan Ma is the best.
Any substitute for the black vinegar?

Black vinegar is a bit syrupy and a bit sweet, so you will want to replicate that. Try maybe Shaoxing wine or mirin with just a touch of honey to add that viscosity? I know mirin and Shaoxing wine are not necessarily what one may have if one does not have the black vinegar, but one of these may be at least available locally. I guess one could also try rice vinegar or white vine vinegar with a touch of honey, but this sub will be more acidic. Black vinegar is not really that acidic.

14 oz. block super firm sprouted tofu, microwaved (not much water came out since this tofu is much denser). Doubled the marinade, except the chili crisp amount (Lao Gan Ma), used low sodium tamari, and marinated the tofu all day in the fridge. Lined a sheet pan with aluminum foil since I know anything with honey will stick and burn into the baking sheet.

Placed tofu on lined baking sheet and tossed 12 oz. of broccoli florets in the remaining marinade. Added to baking sheet and baked in the preheated oven at 425 for 20 minutes. SUBLIME!!! Will at least triple the marinade next time, and the original amount of chili crunch was perfect for us. I loved someone’s idea of serving with ramen noodles! I am SOOOOO making this again! Will try different tofu firmness and/or different vegetables next time, too. If asparagus, will add at 10 minute mark.
This has become one of our go-to tofu preparations. I add some chicken broth to the final dressing of chile crisp to make it stretch further without adding more oil.
4-5 years ago NYT published a recipe for Chili Crisp by Genevieve Ko.  It’s my favorite.

Powerful flavor not for the meek.  I'm not convinced the small amount of Chinese black vinegar (vs. substitutes) made any impact alongside the overwhelming presence of the chili crisp (and I used slightly less chili crisp than specified).  The beans are an excellent complement and definitely need a splash of soy to finish, as one reader suggested.
I love, love, love this recipe. Sometimes I add jumbo shrimp at the last five minutes of broiling. A squirt of lime. So good.

As usual with NYT Cooking recipes, it takes a lot longer than the printed estimate, 30 minutes here.  I am a rather casual cook, and for this recipe I figure on an hour and a half.  (I got the rice cooking well in advance.)  Pretty tasty, though.
Amazing - perfect dish exactly as written!

Don’t put all the sauce on the tofu. If it’s not right on top of the tofu it will char. I made some udon while the tofu was cooking and tossed them with about two tablespoons of sauce before placing the finished beans and tofu on top. You could do the same with rice if that’s your preference. Delish with Momofuku chile crisp.

I had to do a fair amount of substituting and adding items for the sauce. I was thinking about it and wondering if it was worth it. The longer it sits in the fridge, the better it gets. Not sure if I will make it again. I also had to double the sauce. Maybe because I doubled the tofu.
You had me at Chili Crisp.

This sauce would make shoe leather taste good!  I plan to use it on other dishes- intensely flavorful. I used baby bok choy in place of the beans. I will make this again for sure, and maybe add some sweet red pepper for color.
The marinade is absolutely the bomb! Going to use it on a bunch of other dishes as well
A regular for us. I use roasted red chili paste instead of chili crisp. A bit sweeter

This is on heavy rotation in our house! Satisfies my picky kids and my "it's not dinner unless it involves meat" dad. Usually I make it exactly as written, but I love how the marinade still works even with a hit of must-use-up sauces and seasonings. Packs a punch, but not punishing. I'll miss it this summer when I become weirdly feral if anyone dares turn on the oven.

@Christy I agree!  The marinade was delicious, but it overbaked right off and the whole dish was dry and underwhelming.  Maybe will try cooking at a lower temp, too.  I didn't have cilantro or scallion, so I used mint, thai basil and shallot.  I highly recommend holding the marinade off 'til close to the end.

This recipe has become a summer favorite, when green beans are available fresh. I find it is even better when I add to the baked green beans the following: chopped onions and red and green  peppers. It is especially good when served over cooked quinoa.
Why was my marinade dry? I followed the measurements...

Try subbing Lao Gan Ma Chili Oil with Black Bean condiment for the chili crisp. it's so packed with flavor you can use it by itself or just thin it out a bit with some soy sauce before dolloping  and spreading over your tofu slices. the black beans get wonderfully crisp/chewy and are the best part of the meal!!
Would be useful to have a recipe for chilli crisp. The search engine can’t seem to find any.

I've made this twice this week and it's definitely going on my regular rotation for this time of year. what a delicious easy to use up the pounds of beans my garden is producing right now. Temp and time do need adjusting, especially if you're using a convection oven. Lao Gan Ma is my preferred chili crisp, and the version with fermented soybeans adds a nice umami funk to the mix. Makes great leftovers for a cold lunch at work!
I made this tonight but with broccoli because I had no beans. It was a big hit. Delicious, even without cilantro, which I did not have.
I never repeat recipes and I made this two nights in a row, and twice more within the month. It’s simpl

I loved the raw marinade - although next time I will use level measures of chili crisp! - but the tofu didn't absorb as much of it as I would have liked pre-baking, despite the prep and cook taking more like an hour - and the freshness was lost by heating it. My beans were thinner than in the photo and didn't blister but became a bit like crispy rope after 20 mins at 200c fan. I can imagine the marinade would be delicious as a dressing on steamed silken tofu.

This dish is excellent, so tasty. I only have one comment and that is I think it should be cooked at 400 degrees max. My oven is spot on for most recipes but the juices came out charred. Nevertheless, it was delicious. So an absolute keeper. Thank you Sam Sifton for this good one.

This was super easy and fast.  I marinated the tofu longer than suggested only because I prepared it ahead of time and came back to roast it later. I used cabbage instead of beans and it worked out great. Next time I might add a bit of fresh chopped cilantro and green onions to brighten the final dish.

I thought this was really great. I did press a little bit of water out of the tofu with a clean kitchen cloth beforehand, but I probably didn’t even really need to. It absorbed the marinade very well. I will heat up leftovers in the toaster oven. Also thinking next time of adding a soft boiled egg

Delicious. Used bok chow instead of green beans. Also followed the recommendations here to hold back the extra marinade. I cooked the extra marinade for a minute in the microwave (garlic and ginger need heat baby), let it cool and tossed with soba noodles.
Kari Kari brand chili crisp is The Bomb! Not as spicy as some (although still plenty hot), but lots of umami. I will sometimes mix it up and eat a teaspoon-full, all by itself -- it's that good.

Easy and yummy, wish I had seen the comment about lowering the heat. My marinade came out so burnt but once I mixed it with the rice I hardly noticed. The dish came out a little too salty and tangy for my taste so next time I would reduce the chili crisp and vinegar amount and add more honey for more sweetness.
Excellent recipe, but next time I will marinate longer so the tofu really absorbs the flavour.

Made this as written with one glaring exception- I don’t have chili crisp…and it’s cold and I am miles from a store on a stormy night. So I used Hot Chili  
Garlic Crunch from Mar Mar (a Virginia small business). I added one half a fresh cayenne chili and proceeded as written. Yum!

Lovely, simple, and light. We subbed broccolini for green beans, which worked out nicely. We also tossed our vegetables in the spicy marinade before arranging them on the tray and we were glad that we did! We borrowed the tip from another commenter about draining the tofu by microwaving, which did extract some (if not an amazing quantity of) liquid. Ultimately, the tofu's texture was pleasantly soft and chewy. Dressing the final dish with drops of lemon juice brightened up the palate nicely.
Wasn't saucy enough, and I managed to over cook the green beans : (
Pretty tasty!
Without any scallions, I added leeks and they added a lovely sweetness to the sauce. Improvising win!

Both of these have been mentioned separately, but I want to second both:

1) 20 minutes on 400 degrees (I have a convection oven - maybe that matters), and
2) don’t waste that delicious marinade by pouring on top of the tofu - save at least some as a serving sauce on the table.
Pretty bland. The green beans definitely need more treatment, and the marinade also doesn’t feel finished to me unfortunately.
This recipe is great.  My only amendment is I double the green beans. This is in our regular rotation for east weeknight dinners.
Maybe I’m imagining the wrong size of tofu but how can you get 8 slices out of a 14oz package?

So so good!! We have made it several times, with different vegs when we didn't have green beans. I squeeze some liquid from the tofu beforehand. I leave it as a block and put a couple of plates on top. I don't feel the need to spend too much time on this part. Pro Tip: Lao Gan Ma Chili Crisp is the BOMB.
Next time I will only use 1 tbsp of chili crunch as it was too hot for us. Had to eat ice cream to cool off afterwards. :-))))
THIS WAS SO YUM!!!! Umami, spicy (a touch too much thanks to my gf), crunchy, and salty. If your favorite food group is soy sauce give this one a try. Love, Amy
Made as written and it is delicious! The beans were particularly tasty. This will be on repeat.
My husband and I eat this weekly! It is so so good! I have been chopping the tofu into smaller pieces and adding a little of cornstarch to help crisp up the tofu.

@Anne Marie this is the comment I've been looking for -- out of curiosity, would you say the bite is mild enough for a 4 year old? my son loves tofu and I would be thrilled to be able to make this easy meal for our family, but his spice tolerance is about at the mild-med salsa level
Too much chili crisp. Neede 3 tsp not Tbs. Also green beans at 22 mins were like green bean husks. Overall very disappointing.

Really tasty. I only had hot chile crisp, so the tofu had a little bit of heat, but will still perfectly edible. I wasn't sure about the combination of the tofu and green beans but everything worked out perfectly together. Will make again and routinely. Easily five stars.

This is delicious, and not nearly as spicy as one might think. I've done it with green beans and asparagus and both are great. Asparagus cooks faster so cook the tofu for 7-10 minutes before adding asparagus to the sheet pan.

For even more canned fish recipes check out Tin Fish Gourmet by Barbara=Jo McIntosh. Subtitled Great Seafood from Cupboard to Table. With recipes from Anchovies to Tuna, included is a tuna-pasta shell salad with peas, scallions, and diced red pepper in a vinaigrette dressing. 
Recipes in the book are varied and well described.

Made this for my Japanese girlfriend and said that it mostly resembled what her mom makes, my ultimate barometer. She recommended just a pinch of sugar and a few more slivered vegetables (carrots and peppers) to add, which made it a truly excellent salad. Didn’t have any scallions so I quick-pickled some shallots in rice vinegar and they were a great topping. Hope everyone is staying healthy.

I haven't made this yet, but I do cook with wakame. For a couple of bowls of miso soup, I use maybe 1/2 tsp. 1/4 cup of dried wakame, once hydrated,  should give you enough to make this salad for 20 people, not 4.

Excellent! We used this as a base, as I've not been to the market for a week and just rummaged in the veggie bin.  Wound up quickly stir frying some onion and cabbage, then threw in some kale to wilt.  Didn't have the seaweed, sweet miso, udon or seaseme seeds instead just used brown rice ramen (Costco!) and for crunchy topped with chopped sugar snap peas and some cashews.  This was great; it tasted fresh and had some sweet, salty, crunchy and soothing noodles.  Thanks!

Here in France a staple picnic dish or light lunch is a mixture of day-old rice, tinned tuna, olives, cherry tomatoes, chopped bits of hard boiled eggs, scallions, and anything else one might deem appropriate, all bound together with a flavorful vinaigrette.
Nicoise? Adding rice is a new one for me. Sounds interesting.

Lyn,
Seaweed sheet for sushi (called Nori) is a good substitute for wakame, or even those seaweed snack thing work.
What I have isn't a snack item but I know what you're talking about, that would work.

I made this last night as soon as I saw the recipe. Loved it!
I had all ingredients on hand except the Wakame. 
Which I am sure would have made it even better. 

As we are sheltering in place, my kids expected a WWII-K ration type dinner when they saw I was using canned tuna. Instead they had a gourmet surprise. This will be in regular rotation from now on.
Cooked exactly as instructed but swapped in two bags of Top Ramen in place of udon. Sooo delicious. Will definitely make again
Didn’t have udon, so used soba, and used some Korean tuna with red chili, and it turned out great. Quick, easy and tasty lunch.
Sweet miso is also known as shiro miso or white miso. It is the mildest type. If you had a darker, stronger type on hand, you could try using a bit less.
add a little more oil to the dressing if your tuna is packed in water

Made this for lunch today. Turned out great even with some ingredient substitutions. Lightly steamed julienned sugar snap peas for seaweed. Not sure what "sweet miso" is (could use a little help in the recipe text!) but had some miso and used that. Forget the furikake no Asian grocery nearby and no time to mail order. I don't mind experimenting like this, and have a very "flexible" palate, but it would be nice to see tested alternative ingredients for recipes like this.

Alan, so many ways to make this. Any noodle will do. I think the key is noodle, soy sauce, tuna. From there, you can make a dressing from soy sauce and a bit of rice or white vinegar, add a bit of citrus juice, even a dash of sugar or honey. For veggies, you could saute some shaved carrot, shallots, or green onion, spinach, like others said, kale. You can even add a bit of mayo if you like. Sometimes I also boil noodles and then refry them in a bit of oil -canola, peanut, coconut, what have you.
Stupid me didnt get tuna for self isolation but I do have several cans of canned chicken from Costco I was wondering what to do with.  Threw this in instead and it was an amazing and easy dinner.

I keep a fairly well stocked pantry and freezer. So far I have only had to venture to the supermarket for fresh produce (ZERO Costco trips!), however what do you suggest as a substitute for those of us who don’t happen to have Wakame seaweed in our “larder”?
I can probably get dried wakame seaweed, but just in case, what would folks recommend as possible substitute?
Delicious! Julienned a couple of carrots. Could have added some sliced cucumbers. Yum!

We love this recipe- after a couple times with my teen telling me it was yummy but too oily she specifically said “leave out the oil, just use the tuna oil”. Tonight I finally acquiesced, that worked much better for us. I was also super lazy, so did not do the wakame… but def prefer it this way… less soupy, more creamy. Used chickpea miso making the whole thing so smooth with delish umami and less oily (obviously).
I used soba noodles and made 1 1/2 times the dressing. I liked the seaweed much more than I thought I would. We loved this.
Very good. Since no mirin, used white balsamic --great substitute. Added extra veggies and did not use the canola oil. Used crushed seaweed snacks  since that's what we had, unsoaked.
When a recipe calls for sesame oil do they mean toasted or not? I assume toasted in this context but there is also just plain sesame oil.  Anybody know?
@anna In dressings like these:toasted

I have made this many, many times. I'm not a big fan of scallions so I have been substituting cucumbers. Today I subbed Trader Joe's canned calamari for the tuna. For me, I have finally found perfection!
nice.  didn't have all the ingredients.  substituted spinach and  red onion.  skipped the Miso, though I'm sure that would have made it better. used soba noodles as that is what we had!
I have Kombu seaweed in my pantry would that be an ok sub?

No, kombu is really different. I use kombu when I'm cooking beans and it rehydrates to be very thick and leathery. Rehydrated wakame is a lot more delicate. Also wakame is already in flakes, where kombu comes in sheets.

I think you could actually use a little frozen spinach, if you have it.
Is this toasted sesame oil?
Whoa, that’s a lot of wakame! Use 1.5 tablespoons dried at the most. Otherwise it’s a seaweed salad with tuna and noodles.
omg delicious. didn't have miso so used a splash of fish sauce.

Simplified this immensely (as follows) and my husband is still licking his bowl—

Salad Ingredients:
whole wheat spaghetti
sauteed cabbage w/ ginger powder and chili powder
cucumbers 
tuna
topped with toasted sesame seeds

Sauce Ingredients:
4 parts olive oil
1 part soy sauce
1 part hoisin sauce
The recipe also worked with less tuna and also, with less wakame
GREAT dish added raw mushrooms was amazing!

Delicious!  Also sauteed some garlic and baby bok choi, used roasted shredded seaweed, didn't have miso so threw in a dash of Red Boat Fish sauce, topped off with red pepper flakes. (Lots! love thing spicy...)
I added cubed avocado and cucumber to serve cold. Delicious!

I always have a bag of big frozen shrimp in the freezer.  I  sauteed them with some garlic and used that instead of tuna.  I also had a bag of slightly wilted arugula, so used that as the green.  I didn't have wakame (I don't think I've ever had it), so didn't miss it.  My husband loved the dish! (Me, too.)
Added edamame. Family loved this! Should have doubled the recipe. Now a part of the weekly rotation.
That's way too much dried wakame. It expands like crazy. Try 1 tablespoon.
I used a quarter of a cup dried and didn’t think it was too much at all

I had everything on hand but mirin (used the last of it the other day!) so I substituted with cooking sake and a teaspoon of sugar. Also used spaghetti instead. Still turned out great! This gave me a great excuse to use a crab furikake I got from the Tsukiji Fish Market in Tokyo, and I’m sure different flavoured furikakes would vary the flavours a bit. A little shichimi togarashi (Japanese red pepper mix) spices it up too!

Thank you very much for describing your mirin substitute. I don't care for mirin and don't keep it around. I always puzzle over what to use in its place. I will try sake + sugar when I make this recipe.

This was amazing! I usually try not to make any changes to NYT recipes the first time I make them but on the recommendation of another commenter I added some very thinly sliced red peppers. I would definitely recommend!

I have made this several times. It's versatile which is important to reduce trips to the grocery store. I hardly ever have scallions but chives are lovely and I'll try Tevan's tip to quick-pickle shallots or maybe a red onion. It's also good with canned salmon.

Made this as written, but the seaweed I found in the supermarket was Nori.  It didn't hold up well when put in the water.  It was just green glop.  I think it would have done better just being thrown in dry to the pasta and sauce.  The sauce would have moistened it just fine.  That assumes the Wakame version is still unavailable to me.  Otherwise, this is a good use of the ingredients.
Really good summer salad; add slivered red pepper, cucumber, and maybe diced tomato. Tofu would be good. Add salt.

Have made several times. We love it. Serve with a Korean pancake from TJs. Add julienned carrots and red pepper cut on mandolin plus scallions. Dressing very salty with reg soy- sugg lo salt. If using Asian pasta DO NOT salt the water and monitor cooking time carefully as they seem suggest much longer than we like. Used cut up nori snack as my wakame did not work at all. Top with togaraski and hot Japanese pepper but Aleppo or gochugaru would be nice, too. Prefer tuna in oil, well drained.
Don't drain the oil-packed tuna and leave out the Canola. Add broccoli slaw for crunch.

Add edamame 
Use 1 tbsp hot sesame oil, one regular 
OR 
Use regular sesame oil and top with chili crunch

Consider adding a pinch of tingly season salt (Momofuku) for different flavor and avoid all heat.
Good but could use a bit more soy sauce and vinegar.

Some have asked for possible substitutes for wakame and furikake. 
Since wakame gives you umami, I might used dried mushrooms (shiitake or whatever) or those tiny dried fish used to make dashi). Instead of furikake, those little packs of dried nori snacks work great. I use scissors to cut them into ribbons to sprinkle on top. The little dried fish work also, but they're better toasted a bit in a dry pan till they get crunchy.

I had a bit of tuna, dressing and green onions left over. I mixed it all together and added a little mayo then spread it on a slice of homemade sourdough. Yum. I now have two new ways of using canned tuna.
Added Daikon matchsticks and scallions for texture and flavor.  It's a solid and tasty recipe that is really quick.
I used leftover salmon instead of tuna. Quick, easy, and delicious
Really good, really tasty, and quick to assemble. Add some dry chilli flakes for a bit of kick

I've been making this dish regularly and really love it. I'm not a huge fan of scallions though (and rarely have them on hand), so I have been substituting diced cucumber, which I think works very well. I'm tempted to swap out the tuna for shrimp next time too!
Sauce was good enough to go with shredded chicken, firm or soft tofu, ground pork - but for us it didn’t do much for the canned tuna, so next time we’ll try it with one of the other ingredients.
