This is in our rotation, it's so easy and fast. It's better with tuna in oil, but still excellent with tuna in water. It's good with dried noodles, fresh noodles, even spaghetti noodles. I usually make it with extra wakame, about a third of a cup instead of a quarter, and make a little extra sauce because yum.

Made this as directed, but added a tin of sardines with oil as well as tuna as mine were in water. Added a splash of toasted sesame oil to sauce. Delicious and easy. Might add a little chili spice next time (I know, not very Japanese- maybe some fresh ginger instead).
I cook for vegetarians- any suggestions on sub for the fish? Tofu marinated in the sauce? Chopped hard boiled eggs?
This is excellent! Had some tofu needing to be eaten so quickly browned that in soy sauce and used it instead of noodles.   Otherwise followed the recipe.   Great flavour ! Fast !
What rice noodles would be best as a substitute for udon noodles?  Even soba noodles have wheat in them.

Very adaptable... use whatever noodles you have. Just be sure to cook them per their instructions; rice noodles cook a bit differently than wheat-based noodles but should work nicely. There are great Viet salad dishes made with rice noodles. Vaious pastas, including some/most gf pasta noodles, can also sub with decent results. Prob wouldn’t use chickpea noodles but rice/quinoa pastas will work.
Rice noodles would work, but there are 100% buckwheat soba noodles.
Pandemic version: No miso at the store, and the only seaweed I could find was nori. I had soba noodles instead of udon. However, it was reasonably good even with these substitutions.
I served this over rice instead of udon and added a sunny side up egg to each bowl for a savory breakfast.
Very easy and tasty. And uncomplicated ingredients even in these trying times. You can't get tuna, noodles of some sort and soy sauce? Please. No one will be scandalized if you don't add the wakame.
I add cold steamed spinach to this salad. Or serve it on the side as sunomono.
Robin M - some people have these ingredients at home (like myself), making it perfect for a time like this.
Tasty. We made this using soba noodles, homemade furikake, and had wasabi available for those who wanted.  It will make a great summer meal, too.

Happened to have all the ingredients for this in my pantry, so it came together easily. We all loved it, but found the dressing a little oily, especially with a good oil packed tuna on top. So next time I'll cut the oil by half to start.
As much as others complain about substitution suggestions, I’ve always loved them; and I’m loving them even more now. Thank you, flexible, creative  cooks! Keep your wonderful ideas coming(-:

Thank goodness for suggested substitutions. Locked down on an island off Puerto Rico where in the best of times seaweed would be a stretch (at least at the grocery store). But we do have cans of tuna! Thanks for the ideas.

I think that you could get away with half or 1/3 the amount of tuna. I usually cook double the amount to get some leftovers. Needless to say, I will be having tuna fish sandwiches for a month or so. The recipe was also heavy on vinegar. Probably why the previous poster with the japanese girlfriend said to add some sugar. I would half the vinegar.

I made this during the COVID-19 days of limited shopping and social distancing, so needed to make a few substitutions in the main ingredients but I did have tuna and noodles and was able to make the dressing as prescribed. It was very tasty, though I could tell my missing ingredients would have made it even better. I'll definitely make this again.

Alan, I haven't made this, but as I'm reading it I'm planning to use whatever linguine or spaghetti noodles I have on hand and just skipping the wakame that I don't have. I'm sure it will miss some of the desired flavor and texture but hopefully will still be tasty.
I have cooked a lot of Japanese food, but never would’ve thought canned tuna would turn out to be so inspirational and delicious....until now.  Well done, Sam!

So don’t take my word for it. Take my bride’s who is not easy to please once I try to branch out to Japanese cooking. My sole change to the recipe was accidental - I ran out of sesame oil and only had Chile sesame oil which added a bit of heat that made it tasty and unexpected. Will cook this experimenting with other proportions adjusted in the near future.

1) What is rice wine vinegar? Or did you mean simply 'rice vinegar'? 

2) wakame should be soaked in cold water. If you soak it in hot water, most of the taste as well as the minerals are lost in the discarded water.

Rice vinegar is made from rice wine, just as red wine vinegar is made from red wine and apple cider vinegar is made from apple cider.  "Rice wine vinegar" and "rice vinegar" mean the same thing, the latter is just a common shorthand.

Easy, fast and delicious pantry-ingredient recipe, perfect for pandemic quarantine! I substituted angel hair pasta for udon noodles and sweet vermouth for mirin. We’re looking forward to enjoying the chilled leftovers tomorrow.
Would be so good with fresh, seared tuna.

This is an easy and fabulous dish.  My family loved it. The sauce is very easy to make. I used regular spaghetti and substituted finely chopped cucumber for the wakame.  I happened to have all the sauce ingredients but you could probably get by without the miso if you don't have it, substitute white wine vinegar for rice and use sherry instead of the mirin.

This was yummy, but would probably have been even better with some slivered vegetables like carrots and cucumbers. I added a little sugar to the dressing to balance the flavors, and some hot sauce for a little zip.
A month ago canned tuna was maybe 1.99 now at $4.00 a can I will try this with fresh albacore instead!!

I made this as close to the recipe as my pantry allowed, only substituting a packet of Wakame ramen noodles I happened to have and aging spinach from the crisper. I made the ramen soup and otherwise made sauce as instructed. Though soupier, still delicious! It's a keeper and super easy/fast.

Great notes from our community, helpful and fun to read.  This dish lends itself to improvisation, and I agree with adding extra veggies.  I have radishes in the garden right now, so I threw lots of them, plus tops, into the cooking water with the soba noodles I used.  I think anything with 2 T toasted sesame oil in it would taste delicious, but I would strongly suggest ditching the canola oil and just using the oil from the tuna instead.  For water packed tuna, drain and use the canola.

I added some fish sauce as I didn’t have any miso and a little brown sugar to the dressing for sweetness. at the end I healed on chili crisp for added texture and heat as well as a little kewpie. So so so good. I couldn’t believe how simple and tasty this was!

The local Asian market had several types of furikake. I guessed and got one without msg which worked well.  My family does not like much sweet flavor with seafood or meats, so I left out the mirin and it was not missed. The furikake added a pinch of sweetness. Added snow peas and sliced red pepper.  Otherwise, made as directed. It is quick and delicious pantry food.

I was doubtful but had a big bag of wakame so thought, why not? Closest thing I had to mirin was sherry. It was delicious. I'm making it tonight with smoked salmon. Toasting the sesame seeds adds a ton of flavor so don't skip it if that's what you're using.

Prepared as directed except that I subbed in a cut up nori sheet for wakame because that was what was in the cupboard (#shelterinplace).  Ate it hot not cold.  Wonderfully filling and flavorful.  Simple to prepare.  

Highly recommend a little squirt of Kewpie mayonnaise to the tuna before adding other toppings.

I didn’t have wakame so cut up some red pepper and cooked it in oil for a few minutes, added to the tuna and noodles, at the last minute threw in a handful of fresh spinach and a few cashews. Husband liked it very much. Will become a staple for lunch.
Probably can substitute a lot. French version uses day old rice, tuna, olives, cherry tomatoes, chopped hard boiled egg, scallions and vinaigrette.  Add anything else available.

Hunkered down with family in rural West Virginia without wakame, furikake or miso.  No scallions but I might find some ramps on the hills. I brought tahini and other things with me.  My sister has some canned salmon.  I will try to rise to the challenge.  There is a lot of venison in the freezer but some fish would be so nice.

Wish me luck and I hope you all stay healthy!
Used dried dulce seaweed from Nova Scotia (which, it turns out, needs no soaking) and soba noodles; was excellent!

I made this with what I had on hand due to the Coronavirus, so there were a few substitutions. I didn’t have the same seaweed flakes but used the snack flakes, breaking them over the top as a garnish. Used red miso paste (maybe that is sweet miso?). All in all it was good, not great. Would recommend doubling the sauce for more flavor (oh, yeah, I only had spaghetti) and would blanch some additional veggies like carrots & broccoli when I remake it.

We thought this was very good. I did not have sesame seeds so I used peanuts and added grated carrots. I also did not have mirin but I don’t know how much difference that would make. Ordered the wakame on Amazon. I’ll add red pepper flakes when I make it again

Didn't have wakame, but used wild garlic leaves, and added julienned carrot and radishes for crunch and colour - very good. (Used 220g noodles, which was the perfect amount for 3 people - would have been a stretch for 4.)

Very nice. I live in rural AZ (shopping is limited) and in quarantine due to being high risk. I had everything but the seaweed, which I ordered from Amazon! My husband thought it was a ridiculous purchase but after he tasted the salad, he said he was happy I bought it! Now I have plenty to add to soups, etc. Thanks!
Made it tonight and feel it needs a bit more depth through ground ginger, garlic, sriracha.

We loved this dish.  I had never used wakame before (available in the Asian section of our wonderful grocery store.)  I followed the recipe exactly and we thought the wakame amount was perfect (it is the "greens" for the salad; lovely and tasty!  We had scallions and toasted sesame seeds for toppings.  Served it room temp.  We will do it again, and probably next time for company!
Delicious! Didn't have sweet miso, only shiro miso, so added some sugar to balance. And subbed grapeseed oil for canola. Added some sliced green pepper and cucumber, easy and very tasty!
Used leftover penne, can of Genova tuna with its olive oil, lime juice instead of rice vinegar and finely sliced nori instead of wakame. Excellent!

This is a quick, easy and great luncheon dish.  It could easily work for a light supper with a side of sliced cucumbers, tomatoes and daikon using the same dressing.  If you don't have udon noodles, you can use any regular Italian pasta like fettuccine or linguine or even tagliatelle - just make sure it is cooked al dente.  This will also work with canned salmon instead of tuna.   I suppose you could eliminate the wakame, but I love the flavor and texture of it with the noodles and tuna.
I made it with canned chicken, used soba noodles, and served it over a bed of lightly pickled cabbage. The best thing I've made in a long time.

This was an easy and tasty dinner to make. I had some soy noodles I had bought on clearance. Didn't have mirin so substituted sherry and didn't have rice wine vinegar so substituted plum vinegar--still turned out well, however. Used chickpea miso and Bragg's liquid aminos. Hit the spot without being overly heavy and filling.
This was a hella good lunch! Used some linguine and leftover salmon. Amazing Sam!

We liked this a lot.  Left out the miso, as I'm not sure how long it keeps in the fridge, not sure how long mine's been in thee, & covid time is no time for a trip to ER!  

Added arugula, shredded daikon, & shoga & used togarashi in addition to furikake & scallions.

Easy, too.

Pretty sure miso is one of those foods like ketchup, mustard, pickles, olives, etc. that pretty much last forever in the fridge.  Even fresh ginger will last forever if you cut it up and keep it submerged in sake or dry sherry in the fridge.  That storage method also makes a great tasting sake or sherry that may be used in any asian recipe calling for sake, shaoxing or sherry.  I used the same Chinese preserved turnip in spicy stir-fried green beans for about 20 yrs. without problem. Still here.
Do you think coconut aminos will be an okay substitute for soy sauce?

Made this for a quick lunch today with somen noodles and added sliced cucumbers. Also subbed a bit of agave nectar for the mirin. Didn’t have wakame but still very delicious and will make again. An excellent lunch-packs, stores, and eats very nicely at any temperature.
Made this with soba noodles (and forgot the scallions) and this was easy and delicious. Served it room temp.

I happened to have almost all the ingredients at home so was able to follow the recipe pretty closely. Super easy and quick (10-15 minutes including cooking the noodles and soaking the wakame), and really delicious! 

I used white wine vinegar instead of rice wine vinegar (smaller amount), halved the amount of oil and added a teaspoon of sugar instead, as the acidic flavor was pretty dominant. 

I would make it again this way, only maybe with rice wine vinegar.

Perhaps dried wakame seaweeds differ in how much they become when soaked? I love seaweed, so I used 1/4 cups (dried) for 1 serving, and even then, didn't think it was too much. It was still 80% noodles to 20% wakame.

Made this for lunch today.  Cut the recipe in half for 2 only.  Cut way back on the wakame based on one of the notes.  Believe if I hadn't, the wakame would have dominated the tastes. Some of the wakame stayed in the mixing bowl because of the oils.  The dressing was very toothsome.  Used spanish tuna from Ortiz.  Used furikake which we prefer to toasted sesame seeds. Not normally a fan of scallions but it worked well here. A serving bowl doesn't serve much purpose since it's plated in bowls.

I followed the recipe as written. 

We really enjoyed this dish, but thought that it would taste better at room-temperature or warm rather than chilled. 

Will make again.

A solid, easy to make dish. I sautéed an onion, carrots, red bell pepper, and a few white mushrooms first, then added the already cooked noodles to the pan with the sauce. Also, I substituted smoked salmon for the tuna because I had some in the fridge. It turned out delicious! The dressing is particularly good and would work with just veggies and noodles.

This was delicious! We'll definitely make this again. We substituted Korean laver cut into strips and medium rice noodles (bahn pho) and added thinly sliced red bell pepper as suggested in another post. We didn't have any miso so we left it out. The adults added chili-garlic sauce at the table -- which was so good.
My second time making this, I kept the drained tuna oil and used that instead of canola. Also used tamari instead of soy. No noticeable difference, and delicious all the same.
Easy dish to throw together. The dressing is delicious - we tend to double the miso to add to the salty/creaminess.

This dish did the un-imaginable - both of our very finicky daughters (age 5 and 7) love it - so much so that we have had it for lunch every Saturday since I first made it.  Mama loves it, nanny Carly loves it, and Papa Paul D. does too.  Daughter E1 helped take the meat out the can the first time we had it, and noted only the first word on the label: "Chicken..." (of the Sea - shhhhhh!). She has thought it was made with chicken ever since, which is marvelous, because both daughters hate tuna :)
This is an awesome recipe! It’s in the vault for sure. Made it as written and wouldn’t change a thing.

Had to modify to meet my needs, so obviously I'm giving my opinion on what I made, not the actual recipe...

Made with potatoes and nori instead of noodles and wakame. Ate hot one day and cold the next. Delicious!! The dressing was went well with the tuna and nori added something really special to the potatoes. I imagine both statements would be true to when made with noodles.

I will be making this regularly and would highly recommend.

Picked up on suggestion to add more veggies, julienned a few small carrots and sweet red pepper, sliced two radishes with mandolin. Followed directions on wakame pkg. but it was very soft and difficult to disperse. Will try nori next time.  One can Albacore tuna in oil. Added a TJs Korean pancake to make it a meal. Two of us ate it all with enthusiasm! Did add just a bit of good quality toasted sesame oil in the finishing dressing.

You can use any kind of noodle — half a box of leftover penne in our case. No seaweed but subbed some blanched celery. Any veggie with a crunch would work, I think. Served each portion with sliced avocado and a soft boiled egg. Leftovers keep well, too!
Wasn't a fan. Average dish.

Yum!!! Wonderful quick dish to throw together after driving all day. Easy to add herbs or anything else that sounds good. I threw in some Thai mint - wasn’t sure how it would play with the sesame, but it was great! Add some chili oil for some 🔥
Excellent lunch in less than 10 minutes. I had everything but the wakame, and I used 100% buckwheat noodles. I dropped the canola oil, the tuna had enough oil of its own.
This sauce was delicious on cooked chard as well. Great combination.
love this!!  It is a new regular in my house and no need to go to the grocery store if you stock the ingredients

We loved this, but might make with a firmer finer pasta next time, maybe soba. I’d say this much wakame is not too much — this isn’t miso soup but a salad.  And adding slivered other vegs would be good, too.  That dressing is really good!

The key things here are tuna, noodles, wakame, and the dressing. Because it’s a salad, you can really toss in anything you like. This time I added some thin-sliced radishes and chopped Thai chilis for heat. I also add some minced ginger to the dressing, but it’s all about your taste. I love the surprising sweetness that a small amount of miso & mirin adds. And the fact that you can throw it all together in about 20 mins is even better!
Wasn’t great   Doubt I’d make again

Very easy and totally delicious.  I made this with fresh seared tuna steaks covered in the toasted sesame seeds and it just added that extra level!  We ate this cold which was also perfect for summer.  Soba noodles also work perfectly well as a substitute in a pinch.
This was easy and delicious. I had some chopped veggies and edamame in the frig so I added that and was happy with the outcome

I had everything but the wakame. Otherwise made as directed. It was very good. I used good, oil-packed Italian tuna and added a shaved carrot, pickled red onion and cilantro at the end. I will make it again with a pinch of salt.

Made tonight with soba noodles! Added julienned green bell pepper and thinly sliced radishes - I wouldn’t make this again without the addition of the crisp veggies. I think it could use a spice, perhaps the chili oil we have on hand next time. Also, I put the tuna in the sauce shortly ahead of time to take some of the fish I was out of it. Oh! I don’t know what wakame is but chopped some seaweed snack sheets as a garnish on top. They were meh.

I made this exactly as written, wakame and all. I liked it. It’s a good base recipe for some other ingredients. I think steamed salmon or shrimp would be delicious with this. The tuna, even oil packed, was kind of dry for this, for my taste. I will add another teaspoon of miso to the dressing next time I make this recipe. Overall, tasty and adventuresome ingredients.

I made this with slight variations and was lovely! I wish to add that I fled the city and am now a tenderfoot hillbilly but I still like to cook a`la Times recipes~ saying that because I find all the ingredients needed on Amazon and they're delivered right to my door. I wonder about folks who write that they can't get this or that
Has anyone tried this with Tuna steaks instead of canned Tuna?

This recipe is WAY better than you think! What an easy meal, great use of pantry staples (except for the seaweed!).  I made the recipe exactly and no doubt will make it again and again.  Delicious dressing.

Nice dinner recipe. I used Cento canned tuna, which didn’t taste tinny, omitted the canola in the dressing as per the suggestions here and put a healthy sprinkling of furikake on each bowl. I’ll make this again.
Quick, easy and tasty. One recommendation: I could have used more of the sauce.

I make this often (I live in Japan), but learned from my host mother to make it with a strong tsuyu as base, with grated daikon on top (place the jarred tuna on the daikon), a drizzle of sesame oil, and chiffonaded shiso leaves on top. Sometimes I make with scallions instead (if I don't have shiso). Always served cold.

I've never tried with wakame but think it'd be a little too umami paired with the tsuyu and tuna the way I make it. I might try this sauce instead to see how it pairs.
Is wakame the same thing as the dulse flakes I can get in my local co-op?
We liked this! Next time reduce the oil and use more noodles. It was just enough for four of us. One can of tuna would probably have been enough.

Only had spaghetti used it. Doused it with all of the delicious dressing, grated half a carrot and set aside. Sautéed remaining carrot with the mushroom and garlic and ginger. Topped the bowl of spaghetti with sauté, and fresh carrot, and slice an avocado in. Wonderful meal.

Pretty good, and a super easy pantry meal! The dressing is mild but tasty. Added more oil to dressing to make up for tuna packed in water, as suggested in other comments. Did not have wakame or anything similar, but added some mixed greens at the end for texture. Also did not have scallions, so quick-pickled some red onion in white vinegar, sugar, salt, lime juice.
I’ve made this a few times with tuna but swapped the tuna for edamame tonight for a meatless meal.  Worked out really well.
Is the ingredient rice wine or rice vinegar?
Very yummy we added a little grated zucchini and sliced avocado for more veg.
Used 2 packs of Trader Joe’s green tea udon noodles, doubled sauce recipe, and topped with leftover honey fiber glazed chicken thighs. Only used maybe 2/3 of the sauce.

One of my favorite nyt cooking recipes I’ve ever made. This has became a quick weekly go to, and I have adapted it so many times and added extra veggies I had on hand. So easy to always keep these ingredients on hand.
Tasty but it needs, for sure, more veggies. Carrots, cucumbers, celery, red peppers, etc. Some shredded greens, too: basil, for example.
Will make this for the fourth time tonight. I do recommend getting some furikake - mine has dried mushrooms in it, along with sesame seeds and seaweed, and it adds a wonderfully complex taste.

What a yummy, easy dinner! I was lazy and just made the sauce, chopped the green onion and didn't toast the sesame seeds, but put them on raw. I also didn't have any seaweed on hand, but did have fresh pasta. Looking forward to trying this again and making the enhanced version, but really feel this will become a quick go-to backup dish for me.
Pretty tasty for canned fish.  Michael noted it's not as saucy, and more dressing-y. Makes a HUGE portion.  Best to half it.
Delicious and easy meal.  Used rice noodles instead and subbed hot sesame oil for additional spice.  Like others, look forward to adding veggies to future batches.
I also go a little heavy on the mirin.
So good! I didn’t have miso so added 1tsp of peanut butter, it worked… Also took advice from one commenter to add carrot and also cucumber which added some nice crunch.
We added a dollop of chili crisp and a squeeze of lime juice. Delicious.
Delicious. Made it with somen. Great light salad.
Very easy and was delicious even though I omitted the seaweed and the mirin.  I added edamame and it went so well with the dish.  I ate 3 out of the 4 servings, it was so good!

This is sooo good and a great way to use up small amts of condiments and salad greens.  I used somen noodles, and added rice vinegar and a little oil to the water they cooked in.  Served them cold on a bed of arugula.  To the recipe dressing I added capers, a bit of sliced shallot, some leftover peanut sauce, and some sweet chili sauce.  I used TJoe's Skipjack tuna in a pouch (no oil or water added), and topped the whole thing with dried salted edamame and sesame seeds.  Wonderful easy meal!
Really good, my only modification is to use a tablespoon of miso (either salty or sweet), and some fresh thinly sliced veg like peppers etc.
I used Seaweed salad [from Costco] instead of wakame, and made a few other alterations.  Great for hot weather!

Because I had fresh Kale, Tatsoi, and Mizuna growing in the garden, I substituted the greens along with diced fresh red pepper and celery for the Wakame. I briefly sauted them while the udon was cooking. Based on other comments I added a teaspoon of honey to the dressing. After dressing the udon I added the sauted veggies, sesame seeds, and a couple of dashes of furikake.

The dressing is very good. I could see adding more veggies - slivered carrots, bok choy, etc. I think this would be great with fresh tuna, salmon or prawns.  However, as written, neither my husband or I liked it very much.  And I am generally a fan of canned tuna.

I make this all the time. Here's my advice. You can use whatever noodle you want, but udon is the best choice IMO. Toss noodles, wakame and tuna in dressing, plate it, sprinkle on the other toppings then drizzle a little more dressing over the top. I add diced pickled carrots or serrano peppers, and toasted sesame seeds or furikake. Chef's kiss! Warning; You have to nail the balance of wakame & tuna or it's too fishy.

This is unduly complicated in its effort to avoid using the proper ingredients:  the "Lutheran binders", aka undiluted Cream of Mushroom and Cream of Chicken soups.  Also, there is no need to layer the meat and soup -- just mix it all together, add a layer of cheese, top with Tater Tots, and bake at 450 for 30 minutes.  Top with another layer of cheese and stick it back in the oven for a few minutes until the cheese melts.  I made three pans yesterday for a lunch today after church!
Hard to believe someone in Minnesota made this recipe so complicated!

For people who truly enjoy cooking, the extra step of making the cream soup from scratch is a labor of love, and the flavor is more than worth it.

There's nothing wrong with using highly processed convenience foods if that's what you prefer, but you shouldn't criticize a recipe you haven't actually made.

Agreed! I grew up on hot dish, and while my diet is 98% Mediterranean and whole foods now, I still throw one of these together occasionally.

For a weeknight I'd just do it with condensed soup and frozen vegetables (peas, carrots, corn, whatever) and it really won't be much different than recipe. Amy Klobuchar's recipe works well. 

Beyond Meat's burger brick or the crumbles work well here.
Jane, Mary, Debra and Randy got it right. The point isn’t to make it cause it’s fancy, it’s because it’s a hot dish—hot and fills a family fast. And don’t knock it till you’ve met a Minnesota winter.

For those of you that want to scream Sodium, Calories, and Heartattack, skip this recipe. For the rest of you, this is a very good comfort food dish. Two caveats - you can complete the casserole assembly, minus the tator tots and keep in the frig until ready to bake. Add the tots in their frozen state, then bake as directed. And, I cheat on some of the time by using a can of condensed cream of celery soup and 1 cup milk or cream for the homemade cream soup. Cuts the time almost by half

@Mary do you still use the chopped celery for the soup when using the condensed can?
Thank you for the time saving tip

Hello, Minnesconsinite here. I am making this ‘bougie version out of curiosity but cannot tell my grandmother or any of my kinfolk about this roux-travesty. The beauty of tatortot *hot dish* is that it’s flavorful, easy, and inexpensive! (Get outta here with “casserole business”.  food culture and food history is important and nothing grinds my gears more than when foodies take midwestern food that I saw my grandmother’s hands make and don’t respect it enough to use the proper terms.)

I swapped out the ground beef for ground pork.  I subbed the tater tots for silken tofu, removed the carrots, and all milk products.  I then sauteed the pork and tofu into some simmering sichuan peppercorns, douchi and doubanjiang and called it mapo tofu.
Carol J, Duane is being cheeky.
I, for one, am glad to have a recipe that doesn't call for canned cream of something soup. I have yet to find one that is gluten free and tastes decent. I would rather make it from scratch anyway.

@Vicky Bee there is now gluten free cream of chicken and gluten free cream of mushroom both made by Campbells! They are available at wegmans market if you are lucky enough to have a wegmans near you! My son has celiac and we are strictly gluten free at home.

The first time I came across a Tater Tot casserole recipe was in a cookbook put together by the residents of a retirement home in central Illinois, by a woman named Opal.  Spiral bound, what looks like mimeographed pages.  I've made this recipe religiously for around 13 years with add's and sub's along the way.  However, if you are using tater tots you also have to use the "cream of" soups with it.  It is known (as the Mandalorians say).

My wife and I enjoyed this tater tot casserole receipe at dinner tonight.  We both grew up in Iowa in the 70's and ate many a hot dish.  Now we are vegetarians and I substituted vegetarian hamburger.  Making one's own cream sauce was so much better than the cream of mushroom soups our mothers used.  We both agreed that this receipe was tastier than the tater tot casseroles we remember as kiddos!  How often does that happen?  Thanks :)

I enjoyed all the comments. As a transplant to the MN-IA-SD area, I have to speak up for the canned soups. I also love cooking from scratch; but using the cans isn’t just about economy. I lived for six years 15 miles from a town with a grocery, and learned quickly to keep shelf stable items (like cans of condensed soup) on hand, especially in winter. With frozen hamburger and tater tots and a few cans , you and the family will still eat, even if you can’t get to town, or even across the road!
@Erma that's a really excellent point - if you've got the stuff in the freezer and pantry and it's snowstorm, you can still eat well
If you switch every other tot with a half grape tomato, it’s much more delicious.

As a born and raised Midwesterner, reading this recipe was a good laugh.  No need to layer the meat/vegetable mixture and the "cream of" soup...  just mix it all together and throw it in the casserole, top with cheese and tater tots, and you're ready to go.  

Also, in my neck of the woods (northeastern Iowa), the overwhelming style is cream of mushroom soup and green beans.  I don't think I've ever seen it with any other combination, although peas sounds good.  I'll have to try that.

Born and raised MN, I make this but I make a vegetarian version with mushrooms rather than meat. 

I also make a version of tuna casserole with homemade "Lutheran Binders".

My east coast kids don't know the difference, which is bittersweet.

Made this tonight. Halved it. Used skim milk and gf flour, and used onion soup mix for the bouillon. Beyond meat ground ‘beef.’  So delicious we can’t stop laughing. Thanks for this!  Best comfort food!

I have not made this particular hot dish..but all of molly yeh’s recipes are wonderful..i often do her tater tot chicken pot hot dish and i really appreciate that she has created recipes that’s dont use the soup or mix, although it may not be 
“Authentic”, there are those of us who just enjoy doing everything from scratch!
This recipe is AWESOME!  It's become a favorite.  I like not having to depend on canned (high sodium) soups!

This is NOT a casserole, it is a hot dish.  That's a Minnesota thing.  Every family in Minnesota has their own version of this hot dish.  There are no rules and no ruling recipe.  If your family likes green beans and not corn, then use green beans.  This is truly a no recipe, recipe. Everyone personalizes this hot dish.  Our Senior Senator, Amy Klobuchar made her version and entered it in the Senate casserole contest, and won.  You can find her recipe by googling Taconite Tater Tot Hot Dish.
@Mary Myers - I came here for the ‘hot dish‘ clarification. Thank you from a fellow minnesoter!

Tater Tot casserole tip that totally, completely fixes this dish, a “where have you been all my tot lovin’ life?” tip:  pre-bake the tater tots. Get them good and crunchy brown on all sides. Then toss them on top and proceed as directed.  

If you don’t pre-bake the tots, the side that’s touching the casserole never cooks. You get icky, mushy frozen potatoes, not “OMG this is so good and I wonder if I’m going to get in trouble for picking all the good part off the top?” quality.

As a non-Minnesotan with no preconceptions of how this dish should taste, I added 3 cloves of minced garlic to the cream sauce, plus mushrooms (fried separately & added after the sauce was made) & a hit of paprika & thyme. 

For me, 1/2 c flour was more than sufficient to thicken the cream sauce. 3/4 c would have been way too much. I ended up adding some chicken broth to the sauce to thin it (though it was still nice & thick) and ensure it would cover all the beef mixture.
you can complete the casserole assembly, minus the tator tots and keep in the frig until ready to bake. Add the tots in their frozen state, then bake as directed.

Thank you for providing an alternative to canned cream soups.  Love it! I chose to make the cream soup in a sauce pan and brown the hamburger in a separate pan. It eliminates the need to transfer the cream soup mixture into a heat proof bowl.

I would recommend leaving out the butter to saute the onions in before you add the hamburger.  If you are using 85/15 hamburger, you can saute the onions at the same time you are browning the hamburger.

Best trick for making tasty tater tot casserole: pre-bake the tater tots. That way you get crunchy brown flavor throughout. Otherwise you get them nicely browned on top, and mushy, half-baked on the bottom. Ick.

As a NYC transplant to Minnesota, I habitually turned my nose up at hot dish,  However, I did give Tater Tot Hot Dish from a lunch place in the downtown skyways a go on the sly and - its good!  I've been sneak eating it ever since. This is a good version.  So is Senator Amy Klobachar's recipe which you can find on the web.

We are trying to be low meat consumers, so I made one small change: substituted a pound of mushrooms for one pound of the meat.  Otherwise, followed the recipe exactly. It was a fine dinner for a chilly November night.
What a great idea. I will do that next time if there is a next time (which will depend largely on family request level).
Super Yummy. The roux is definitely an extra step but well worth it, in my opinion.
Save yourself some time and use a can of mushroom soup instead of making one from scratch. It's already a pretty junky dish - don't beat yourself up about it!

This is like semi-homeade shepherd's pie. I am sure my grandma had a dish like this when I was a kid. I substituted the creamy soup for Trader Joe's brand turkey gravy and it was DELICIOUS. A winner that the kids and husband could all get behind. We are survivng on comfort food and NYT reporting right now. Thanks for sharing recipies to get us through.

Ho-geeze. Ya know, a “casserole” is the vessel you serve a HOTDISH, in. A hotdish is a hotdish only if you have a mess of stuff from the fridg, usually leftovers, & (typically) gr meat and canned cream soup. But it must have a crunch on top: tatertots, potato chips, Corn Flakes, french-fried onion rings to name a few. I use gr beef, cream of mush., & whatever else you want. Use ‘some milk’ if too dry. Add some tots to the base and stir together. Top w more tots & spray w/oil to crisp! Ho-geeze.

I’ll be one of those annoying people that says they changed the recipe in this way and that. But the idea of using a half cup of butter was a bit much for a weeknight meal.

I cooked the carrot mixture in a tablespoon of olive oil. Added the milk next, brought it to a boil and then added 1/4 cup of flour rather than 3/4.

Cooked the beef in another tablespoon of olive oil, instead of butter.

Delicious! Not the same I know, but still well worth it.

1) Leave the fresh veg out of the gravy & up the frozen veg instead.
2) Use unsweetened soy/rice/almond/oat/whatever milk instead of dairy.
3) Sub Trader Joe's Soyrizo or similar for beef.
It's as convenient as the traditional version because the time saved skipping meat and veg prep more than makes up for the extra time spent making your own gravy instead of using cream soup. 
Result? Delicious, doesn't hurt animals, better for you, and quick.
Serious comfort food! Added thyme and garlic powder to the creamed soup and substituted ground turkey for ground beef - excellent.

Halved the recipe, and prebaked the tater tots (20 minutes at 450) to ensure crispiness. Because nobody wants soggy tater tots. 

Will make again this winter!

I use Edward and sons Not Beef bouillon, Imagine Food No Chicken broth and soymilk in place of milk. Melt vegetarian butter and Gardein Beefless crumbles in place of the animal meat (beef). This makes it extremely lower in fat and calories and tastes amazing! I substitute Chao cheese in the mixture and then top it with a little regular cheddar cheese at the end of cooking. Tastes wonderful, is healthier and I reduced a little suffering in the world.

As a native-born Minnesota, I believe this is appropriately titled "Tater Tot Hot Dish." Minnesotans don't do casseroles. We do hot dishes. I look forward to trying this--by skipping the "cream-of-something" soup traditional to these recipes, I can make this with non-dairy milk, for my dairy-allergic husband.

I followed the directions, up to a point. (I have an issue with authority, says my wife of 51 years, who should know.) Instead of tater tots, I used pre-roasted Yukon gold potato slices. To the meat sauce I added a one-pound yellow heirloom tomato and 1/4 cup of Marsala wine, then cooked it all down. I also used one pound ground bison instead of beef. I think bison is a denser, heartier meat, and one pound is equivalent to the recipe’s two beef. I rejected peas as too much like shepherd’s pie.
You sound like a really good cook. Better than me.
@John Yearwood. Wow, John! You and I came up with the exact same modifications!  Only difference is I add bean shoots and a few chocolate chips.

I have had several versions of this dish at other people’s tables, and it’s good. Very filling. But forget the arguments about homemade vs. canned soup. If I brought a bag of tater tots into the house, my husband would have the vapors. His health nuttiness and food snobbery have become entrenched during the pandemic. I think I will make this on Saturday and call the tots “quenelles.”

I'd like to second the observation that no good canned gluten-free cream soups seem to exist - so instead of seeing this recipe as "high-brow," I see it as something I can actually cook for my boyfriend, and I'm grateful.

Tot Dish is a soul food for this West Coast Minnesotan. I am making one upgrade. Cheese is going in the sauce. From scratch is a good idea because it lowers the sodium. You can’t get it out of the tots so it’s good to get it out of the sauce. I welcome this recipe. I doubt my finicky picky eater husband will deign to even try it because he is a Tot Dish mocker. He took my church lady wedding gift hot dish cookbook to work to publicly mock hot dish and someone stole it. Probably for the recipes.
I made this in the 70’s minus the carrots and used cream of mushroom soup instead of the white sauce.

Love all the notes! My mom cooked with cream soups a lot, but I prefer to start from scratch. I grew up in New England and Shepherd's Pie was our version of this. I love to cook and time spent in the kitchen these days is time well spent. Chopping away is good therapy. I followed the recipe exactly until I mixed the soup and meat together. :) We eat pretty healthy things most days so this was a guilty pleasure. My family loved it. Thanks.
I’ve made this 3 times in past 7 months or so, it’s perfection. We are back on lockdown here in Germany starting on Monday and this perfect comfort food will going back into regular rotation.
My children, now 24 and 20, still ask for this when they are blue. I make a much simplified version though, using a can of cheddar cheese soup instead the cream of mushroom preferred here in Iowa.
How sweet! Goes to show you're never too old for some of Mom's comfort food.

As a southern girl I would have no problem using canned soup in this recipe. But the taste is much better if you take the time to make the soup base from scratch.  You can skip the layering process and just mix it all together. I recommend using a cheese that will melt nicely on top.  And I used canned corn instead of frozen because that’s what I had in my pantry.  It worked just fine. I will definitely make this one again.

Not invested in this as a Minnesotan and do not eat Tater Tots or canned soup, but I've been fascinated since Sen. Klobuchar published her recipe.  We made it for a large family gathering and everyone raved.   The homemade cream soup is worth making, but there is absolutely need to layer the filling.  Mix drained meat into the soup.  My frozen corn and peas came in 9 ounce bags, so I used all of them, plus a can of mushrooms and some of the liquid to thin the soup. Finally, used 32 oz of tots.

Followed the recipe and I thought it was good but not great. The meat filling was a bit bland for my taste. Next time I will increase the veggie content, punch up the flavor with green chiles, and choose a sharper cheese.

You can throw in some Hot n Sweet pickled jalapeno rings from Trader Joe's to liven up the taste. Or sauté a couple of seeded jalapeños that you chop before adding to the "cream of" soup. Be careful of using raw too fatty ground beef making this hotdish. It doesn't take twice to make the mistake of having a greasy hotdish! Better to cook the meat, drain then add to the dish. Corn, carrots and green beans were the veggies Mama used. Cheddar cheese UNDER the Tater Tots, if you please.
This was very good, my family was delighted with it. Making the soup elevated this classic midwestern hot dish to another level.

It was a fun to make (we used morningstar farms meat crumbles), but when I served it my 10 year old took a bite and said "So it's Shepard's pie but with tater tots?"

Yep, that's pretty much what it is.
So much better than the soggy and bland versions I grew up with, from the same part of the country Molly now calls home. A keeper.

I thought this was an excellent take on the old classic.  Of course Sam is not going to use canned "cream of" whatever, this is a cooking site.  My husband grew up on Tater tot Casserole, though his family's recipe is just ground beef, cream of mushroom soup and tater tots...not too appetizing (IMO) and I greatly appreciate swapping the old family recipe for this one.  I used just a pound of ground bison and a package of baby bella mushrooms to cut down on the amount of meat and it was fantastic
I appreciate the healthier alternative of making your own cream soup. In Minnesota we call this TaterTot Hot Dish!

Jane In Alameda has it right, "Lutheran binders", aka undiluted Cream of Mushroom and Cream of Chicken soups. These are tater tots—as potatoes as processed as they come. Campbell Cream of Mushroom and Chicken soups are the way to go. 

If making tater tots (aka potato croquettes) from scratch, then it might sense to make a base from scratch. But no need to pull out all the stops for frozen processed tater tots.
Well said. I can't wait to make it (with Campbell's cream of mushroom soup)!
I halved this recipe and baked in an 8x8 pan, and it came out great! It was the perfect amount for 4 (or 2 with leftovers). I served it with a side of broccoli, but a salad would be even better.

As a proud Minnesotan, I think this recipe is fantastic. I get why people are commenting they it’s more complicated than taking a can of condensed soup and dumping it with frozen veggies and ground beef. I would eat that version of this dish happily! However, there is a labor of love and care that goes into the preparation of the cream of chicken soup that in my opinion is well worth it. 

The only critiques I have for this recipe: please rename this “Hotdish” instead of “Casserole” - it’s just part of the legacy of this dish. Also, Ore-Ida Tater Tots are essential and think worth noting in the recipe.
Can you substitute one of the plant-based "burgers"?
You absolutely can. I've been using Beyond Meat's brick of burger "meat" for anything that calls for ground beef, like this dish, shepherd's pie or chili mac.
did anyone find, like me, that you had to add extra milk to the carrot mixture?  I probably added at least another 1/2 cup of milk!
The "soup" mixture was definitely thicker than I expected.

I made this recipe using frozen hash brown patties. We had a lot in the freezer we needed to use up. 

I actually lined the bottom of the pan with patties, to make a bottom crust, and put it in the 400 degree oven for 15 minutes - without any of the filling on top of it. They crisped up nicely. Then came the filling, mixed all together as someone said, with layer of hash browns on top. Shredded cheese. 

Used peas AND corn. Used 1lb bison meat and 1lb mushrooms. 

Magical.

I expanded this recipe and made the mistake of expanding flour would use less next time to make it more loose. Likely less than called for . Also would throw cheese on last 20 min as they were just dried out on top in an unpleasant way. Some other folks mentioned this but “helpful” comments  crowded with people grumpy about this not being their recipe and I missed it till it was too late! Nevertheless it was a hit at our Super Bowl party.

North Dakota representative here, I do like the homemade roux better than the canned, but I skip a lot of the steps in this recipe to make it simpler (brown the meat and keep adding the mier poix and roux ingredients all in one pot, works fine, also no layering). I always use the mini tots because they get crispier, and always use the cheese.
I made this for vegetarians with Beyond Meat's "ground beef" and it was fantastic!

As an Illinoian I’m very annoyed by the Minnesotans claiming this as only their dish! While my mom made it basically completely differently, it was absolutely my favorite dinner. 

This is delicious too, although I added cheese right to the homemade “soup” to make it closer to what my mom made (she used Campbell’s cheese soup)
Farm bred and corn fed true Midwesterner from the great state of Nebraska.  THIS is heritage.  Its an American version of shepherds pie. ENJOY

Made this last night and it was a huge hit.  In order to make it dairy free due to dietary restrictions I subbed olive oil for the butter and plain soy milk for the milk.  It turned out amazing - never thought that I would feed my family "Tator Tot Surprise" :) 

Thank your for yet another great recipe.
I can’t wait for someone to make a Tex-Mex version of this— it’s one of my favorite guilty pleasures at a little taco joint in Houston

I grew up eating this and the recipe was meat, cream of mushroom soup, and tater tots. I like the additions, but in making it again, I’d do a soup more similar to a homemade cream of mushroom and probably use beef bouillon and mushrooms rather than carrots and chicken bouillon as I went with in attempt # 1.

I’m a little confused about when to add the grated cheese. In the intro, Sam says he loves  the grated at the end. In The  recipe, you sprinkle it on before baking. But if you do that do the tater tots get crispy? I appeal to Minnesotans for the answer—when is the proper time to add the cheese?
Crispy tots are mandatory. Add the cheese at the end and slide back in the oven until it melts.

A poorly labeled package of frozen ground meat lead to some happy variations. Ground lamb with some mid-eastern spicing ( ras el hanout)
Peas and carrots and a bit more garlic... in a hurry so channeled my midwestern mother and used Cream of celery soup. I could hardly stop myself from having thirds and my husband of course did! Thanks for the memories and all the comments!
I too cheated with condensed cream of mushroom soup - it saved almost an hour of prep time on a busy weeknight and it was outstanding.

Complicated?  What did I miss?  Threw everything that needed chopping in the processor--easily made that soup mix--it reminded me of Welsch Rarebit without the beer, tossed it in a casserole, stuck it in the fridge and poured the tots and cheese in just before cooking--loved it that nite and the next night reassembled with the left over tots that got brown and crispy--doesn't require precision or cooking skills, my kind of dish.

Made this last night and it came together well and was delicious.  I am betting leftovers will be really good too.

Thanks Sam

Ha! Ha! Ha! I love everybody’s comments. Some polite. Some snarky. All very useful and fun to read. Just what I need as I and my family.... and community.... enter another week of SIP. I’m fortunate to have good food and internet access to all you cooks who provide entertaining comments. Thank you. Good food in the time of covid. Stay safe everyone. Hoping and praying and sending good vibes to all as we brace for the monster wave to hit us in California.
This puts the comfort in comfort food.  So delicious.

This has become a quarantine favorite. I’ve simplified prep by browning the ground beef and cooking the vegetables together. I then just add the butter & soup base, let it cook for a few minutes, and then start adding the milk while stirring. It means we don’t have layers, but I’m not sure what that would bring to the party that’s worth twice the time. It’s very tasty and everyone loves it.

After looking askance at what I was making, my mid-western born and raised husband loved this. Hot dish.  Casserole.  Yes.  It definitely sounded like much needed comfort food and very unlike anything we normally make.  Definitely hit the spot.  Added garlic to the "soup," some pickled chopped Peppadew peppers to the beef.  Would probably use a bit less flour next time although it all held together really well when scooped out of the, ahem, casserole dish.
This was a huge hit. My 16 yr old son loved it!

Made the casserole according to the recipe and added sliced mushrooms. I used a mixture of corn and peas and with the carrots this provided a lot of great veggie content to the meat mixture. The Better than Bullion Vegetable Base really gives great flavor to the base and dish. Also, I used 80/20 ground beef. I strongly recommend reading the recipe multiple times & setting out ingredients pre measured/processed before proceeding. Having ingredients ready made putting this dish together a breeze!
Tried this before and it was totally disappointing - only NYT recipe that failed epically!  Unappealing visually and tasted like yesterdays french fries rebaked.  Uggh!

This was very good for a comfort meal! I used ground chicken because I had some that needed to be used. Two of us ate the entire pan between dinner and lunch the next day. 
Will definitely keep in the recipe repertoire!

Made as directed. Easy prep but it did take a while to do all the cooking and combining, It was just okay. After one serving we both had no desire to go back for more, the flavor was just kind of one note.

I lb ground beef
Package or two of mushrooms
Green beans instead of peas/corn

Just assembled this "hot dish" (I'm not from Minnesota) for my small family of me, my husband and our adult son. Cut the recipe in half. I made it ahead and will stick it in the oven later today. I'm super excited! I'm not a meat eater generally, but my men are and I know they will be thrilled with this elemental combination of red meat, carbs, fat, and salt. Thank you!

This was surprisingly good and exactly what we needed on a 14F night. The soup mixture came together super easy, and I used Better than Bouillon for an extra rich flavor. Also used Ore Ida extra crispy tots, and sharp white cheddar. It’s just warm comfort food of a classic order. I’m NOT a condensed soup fan, so this was exactly up my ally!

Followed the recipe exactly and it was a very satisfying hot dish.   I appreciate being able to make the cream soup rather than opening cans.   For those who appreciate the Zen or cooking, this is a fun process.

My children and I were served a much simpler version of this recipe 30 years ago by a Mormon friend who called it Mormon Children's Casserole, because the recipe was simple enough for children to prepare. It called for 1.5 pounds of ground beef spread raw into the bottom of a 9x13 casserole dish. Top with one can of Campbell's Cream of Mushroom soup undiluted, and spread evenly over the top of the ground beef. Top with neat rows of Tater Tots and bake in the oven for 45-50 minutes at 375 until t

(continuing from below):...until the meat is cooked all the way through. I have dressed this up by making my own Sherried sour cream of mushroom soup, using g 2 pounds of ground venison with one pkt of Lipton Beefy Onion soup mix added, along with a can of chopped green chiles and a Tablespoon of garlic powder. Pat the raw meat mix into the pan, top with Sherried sour cream of mushroom soup, top with rows of tatertots. Bake per below, top tater tots with shredded cheese of your choice and chives

I'm going to leave out the meat. Instead of corn maybe I'll use peas and carrots or sliced carrots. So, soup on the bottom with layer of tater tots, then cheddar grated on top. I'll try it, see how it holds together.

Delightful! Just the right combo of trashy and "real food." I made it with the best ground beef we could afford, because trashy or no, we don't eat much meat, and when I do, I want it to be worth it.

Adjustments: 

1) My normal casserole dish is too big, so I put it in an oven-proof round straight-sided pan.
2) I avoid milk, so I used unsweetened almond milk (but did use butter).
3) My subbed pan didn't fit all 24 oz. of tater tots in one layer; I was able to fit about 20 oz in there.

Rick made this a it just seemed a little too moist and creamy. He used a deep soufflé type dish so maybe a regular roaster or 9 x12 would be better. Also more surface for making the tater tots crisp. Could use a little spicing up too.
This was great. The only change I made was to use plant milk (I didn't add the optional cheese, and I used veggie stock made from Better Than Bouillon organic veggie base) and everyone loved it.

Made a vegetarian version of this using Earth balance sticks and almond milk for the cream soup portion.  Used 1 brick of beyond meat burger, which is 1 lb, so added some finely chopped mushrooms to the “meat” onion mixture to bulk it up, as it called for 2 lbs meat. As some others noted, I just mixed the whole mess together, instead of trying to layer, and topped with the tater tots.  Not the healthiest, but if you are looking for rich and warm comfort food on a cold winter night, hits the spot

I loved making my own cream soup for this.  The chopping and stirring gave me time to think happy thoughts about family - cooking can be a labor of love, especially when it's a dish that I know will be gobbled up by every member of the family.

I grew up eating this with ground beef, cream of mushroom soup (canned of course) and canned green beans. Criss-crossing the hot dish with ketchup, I'd gladly inhale. Excited to see it making a "comeback".
Ore-Ida Tater Tots are gluten free. Sub GF flour in soup mixture for a GF dish.
Anybody halved the recipe?
I have! Works great. Feeds our family of 4 with leftovers.
First grate gold potatoes and make small, cylindrical latkes. After frying, chill until the tater tots are called for. Takes the hot dish to a whole new level.

Thanks for reminding me of "Tater Top Hot Dish" which frequently graced my late wife's table. She inherited this ultimate comfort dish from her mother who, in turn, inherited it from hers. Norwegians all who knew how to cook for loggers. However, a clarification of terminology is needed: Hot dishes are made for weekdays, casseroles for Sundays or special occasions. In addition to proper naming, there are also differences in presentation and at times fanciness of ingredients.

One of our favorite meals. Even the five-year-old inhales it. I halve it for our family of three - it makes plenty with leftovers. The only change I make is adding a few cloves of minced garlic with the onions, which adds a little more flavor to the soup. I've never layered beef and soup, just mix everything together.

I subbed shredded cooked chicken for the ground beef, tossed it with the creamy sauce (thinned it with chicken broth) and seasoned with cayenne.  Spread everything in a baking  pan, topped with tater tots (the crispy kind) and lots of shredded cheese.  Comfort food right out of my Midwestern childhood.

I made this last night, and my whole family wants to keep this in the rotation.  Making the cream soup base from scratch was worth the extra time. Recipes using canned soups would definitely save time, but I didn't consider this recipe to be difficult. I agree with cutting the flour by half; my soup base was a little too thick. I would also add the cheese before placing the tater tots on top next time so they'll be a little crispier.  Served with ketchup and hot sauce on the side--delicious!
If making ahead of time, should you proceed to step 5 as opposed to step 4? That seems to make more sense to me. I think this was a typo.
Surely to make ahead we should complete throght the end of Step 5?

Most of the comments here are ancient in today’s framework, but I just want to tell the AU questioner that tater tots are awesome over-processed hash browns in small scale, jug-sized tater tots at some Cleveland emporiums are entirely more satisfying, and to anyone wondering about proper hot dish or casserole combos:  if you can throw whatever’s left before a needed grocery trip onto a pan or into a baking dish and your high-schooler moons about it years later, you have hit the ULTIMATE hot dish

This was my first time trying hotdish (I am from Southern California)! I made a half sized version using 1 lb of Impossible meat and really enjoyed it. I was too cautious with the salt; next time I will be more liberal. Used a gluten free flour blend plus a bit of cornstarch and that worked great to thicken the sauce. Also, I recommend putting the cheese underneath the tots. Leftovers for breakfast with a fried egg!
Myself & company found this to be an amazing dish — entirely worth abiding by the ingredients in entirety & making the soup by scratch. A definite repeat dish in the future.

Is this more complicated than a hotdish need be? From the layering standpoint, absolutely. I mixed it all together, dumped in dish, topped with tots. Done. The use of soupbase may also negate the whole avoidance of canned soups too. In fact, 2 tbsp of Better than Bouillon almost made this too salty. I just didn't add salt to the meat mixture. It all balanced out. But the mirepoix makes it taste better overall, imo. I also added green beans. This was delicious. Exactly what I wanted.

This was delicious.  I have made many recipes using the canned cream soup as the binder, and this homemade method definitely gives it a special flavor. I will keep it in mind for days when I am not in a hurry.

5-star comfort food. I followed advice here and simplified prep. To reduce cleanup, I sauteed everything in one pan, one after another and prepping next item as each cooked. As they were cooked, all went into a large bowl. Last step was white sauce and then all were mixed well with salt and pepper. Into casserole, topped with cheese to cover. 15 minutes in oven to melt, then topped with hash brown potatoes (store didnt have tator tots) and back in oven for 20 or so minutes. Fantastic!
Oh my gosh…so good! Not healthy, but really good!
Lefse and tatertot hot dish. It's carb heaven! The AAL Cookbook in the late 80s has five different recipes. Pacific Organic is the best GF cream of chicken or mushroom soup.

This was delicious! Lots of comments about how this is supposed to be made with condensed soup, but I prefer the “from scratch” instructions so I can control fat and salt to suit my specific needs. And let’s be honest, these types of dishes were being made LONG before condensed soup existed. Definitely worth making. Everyone at our table had seconds and I easily could have gone for thirds! Highly recommend for a comforting family dinner.

I made this last night as kind of comfortable food dinner. I used soyrizo instead of ground beef cause I’m vegetarian and this could very easily be made vegan too. It was delicious and it could also be made using chickpeas or other white beans.  Probably going to hear a lot about this version but for my fellow vegetarians out there, this was quite delicious and comfort food all the way. Just don’t ruin the moment by serving it with a salad:)!!

If you are from Butte, MT, you know tater tot casserole.  Its appeal is in its simplicity.  I agree that making your own cream soup is a labor of love, but the original intent was for this to be fast, yummy and I think a little campy. So using a can of cream of mushroom soup is what the original recipe intended and there is no reason to go to the trouble to make your own unless you really want to.

My milk- and gluten-free tater tot hotdish subs beef broth, tomato paste, red wine, Worcester sauce, and thyme, with some cornstarch mixed with the broth and wine as a thickener.  Heat the sauce ingredients with the cooked veg and meat, top with the tater tots, bake, and you have a yummy hotdish version of cottage pie.
Can of Cream of Mushroom and Cream of Celery replace the creamed soup. You won't know the difference.
Taking the time to make the creamed soup mixture from scratch makes for an amazing hot dish! My family of Minnesota natives agreed this was the best we've ever had.

I just finished making this - with peas.

I agree with one comment - 3/4 cup of flour is too much.  Next batch I will add some more liquid broth, add some garlic, and no cheese…I was still very good!

I enjoy many of the NYT recipes.
Just putting in a vote for making the recipe as written. Made this exactly as described and it was GREAT. Not difficult in the least and one of the most popular at our holiday potluck.

This recipe has the most bizarre comments. But I made this mostly as directed and it turned out really delicious! Leftover keep well too. 

Lightened up with olive oil instead of butter and used skim milk. Also saved some time by browning the meat in a pan while the soup was thickening.

This is absolutely delicious!  I used 2 chicken bouillon cubes in that step, and then an entire small bag of frozen peas (because 9 oz is only most of a bag) and the entire 2 lb bag of Tater Tots (they fit in the casserole dish, so why not?).  I opted for cheddar cheese.  Yum. I fed it to family during the recent snow storm and everyone went back for seconds.  I loved making the cream sauce from scratch because it allowed me to adjust the salt to my taste (the bouillon cubes were plenty!)
This is decent, but veers on the bland. The second time I made it I added crimini mushrooms, red bell pepper, and tomato paste and got a much better dish that I will make again.
I lived in Idaho. The potato processing plants are near the potato fields. If you saw the potatoes that are sorted out to become Tater Tots you might not find them appealing.
Uh, that's why they're made into tots!

Planned to make for church potluck except using Impossible "Beef" &  swap cornstarch for flour.  Wife was grossed out by the idea of cream sauce over burger.  Then had lunch with cold mezze and potato cake.  Inspired to make Middle Eastern (instead of Midwest) Tater Tot casserole.  Kibbeh seasoned Impossile with pine nuts, baba ganoush, mummarah, ersatz bechemel of ricotta, feta, greek yogurt, eggs, lemon and instant potato granules.  Added baked tots then baked at 350.  Hit of the potluck.
Never made casseroles so this was a delightful surprise. Made as written and ate two spoonfuls of the creamed soup before it ever became part of the whole dish. Really delicious.
My friends love this dish. I often make it in two 9” casseroles. I agree with skipping the layers.  Delicious comfort food that freezes well. It’s in the oven right now.

As someone who eats healthy, regularly makes laborious recipes from this site that have over 25 ingredients, I would like to suggest you stick with the unhealthy original version. You won’t be sorry. 1lb ground beef, onion powder, frozen peas and carrots, canned cream of chicken soup and tater tots. Easy and my kids are famous for it because their friends all ask for it when they come over.
Can this recipe be halved easily? I'm curious to try this, but I cook just for myself.

This would be incredibly easy to cut in half...just use one carrot, one stalk of celery, one pound meat, six tablespoons flour, etc. and bake it in a two quart dish. Note that I was able to get nine servings out of the regular recipe, so you will still have plenty of leftovers!
Or split into two smaller size casserole dishes & freeze one for later? I cook for one also & am happy when I remember there is a homemade dish I can pull from the freezer!

I had long written off comfort food like this due to some food intolerances in our home and the inability to use condensed store bought soups. I used Silk Unsweetened almond milk and King Arthur 1:1 gluten free flour for the soup base (subbed in per recipe measurements) and it turned out wonderfully. This will now become the base for other soups and dishes when we really want some cozy comfort food.
delicious!  agree on all the comments about no layering necessary.  so much tastier and healthier this way vs canned soup. family loved and I will make again.

Made this to store in the freezer as a “ready to bake” meal. It seemed to check all of the boxes of comfort food and held up well in the freezer for 2-3 weeks until they were ready to bake it. It was a hit and I would make it again. No additions or substitutions.

The issue for some is the unnecessary use of soy in Campbell soups. There are similar 
casseroles using cornflakes and hash browns. They too often use Campbell cream soup.
Has anybody tried this with veggie tots?
this actually looks like a form of shepherds pie. If you are watching your sodium then this recipe would not be for  you.
What’s next? Jello molds?
Full on Betty Crocker Hot Dish realness. Delicious, and it should probably only be served once a year, lest your heart might explode.
We made as written and enjoyed it. Could be spiced up, lightened up, complicated or simplified any number of ways. Who could argue with tater tots and ketchup?
Hi, What are the dimensions of a 4 quart casserole dish please? I  have a 9 x 13 pan I would like to use instead of buying a new 4 quart casserole pan. Would a 9 x13 work? Thanks very much.

Pam - I always make this in a 9 x 13 pan or casserole dish.  It will work fine.  As others have said, I suggest simplifying it by simply not making it at all and looking up Klobuchar's basic recipe instead.

I subbed lean ground turkey for the hamburger. It came out really well. 
Upon tasting I realized this would be a great recipe   for post Thanksgiving turkey leftovers. Shredded dark and light meat.

I hate to say this, but I made this casserole from scratch just the way it was written and it just was ok.  Not even as good as the old Cream of Mushroom Soup casserole I used to make when I was first married.  Therefore, I only give it a 2** as I would not make it again.  Sorry!  Most of your recipes are very good.
A can of Trader Joe's chili plus Tater Tots and grated cheese. In times like these ...

I love Molly Yeh (saw her make this dish on her show), I worship Sam Sifton, and adore Tater Tots...and I followed the recipe to the ounce. But I am disappointed. The soup was more like a mousse and I think it would be better to mix the meat and soup together in the pan. Also, some thyme and other herbs would have tuned it up, but then it’s not Minnesotan. Still worship you, Sam.
I was so excited to cook this. It was at the start of our shelter in place. It took way too long and, in the opinion of my family, was not really very good.
Has anyone ever made this with chili?
Kinda-mixed cheese with leftover chili, more cheese on top, add potatoes, bake till bubbly, more cheese and back in the oven to melt.

Making the cream soup from scratch is pretty easy and tastes great. Watch the sodium if you are adding bouillon. 

I can’t say that this dish wow-ed me. I think it would taste better with chicken than ground beef (kind of like a chicken pot pie with tater tots instead of a crust). Great comfort food though.

So grateful for this recipe when I have no creamed canned soups in my pantry and can't run out to the store. I am housing three voracious college students during this quarantine and this is a godsend to the burnt out cooks in the household. I subbed ground moose for the beef because that's what we have in our freezer.

Thanks for the helpful comments. I followed Jane's instructions and really like the results. I'd like to spice it up a bit so may use some cayenne and or jalapeno next time. The recipe calls for a 4 qt casserole pan but it fit just fine in my 3 qt. Is that others experience? Is it different if you make the soup vs used cans of soup?

Very good with a few modifications. Omitted carrots, peas, and corn. Added mushrooms. Reduced flour slightly. Used 1 tsp. veg base and 1 tsp. beef base (might depend on brand, but I think 2 T. might have been an error. I used better than bouillon.) Mixed the meat into the soup mixture. No cheese.

Made this as Covid comfort food. Used Beyond Meat-but I think I’d feel the same way about cow-and felt it was too much. Next time I’ll try 1 lb ground, and then divide the remaining pound between more veggies and more tators. Otherwise tasty!
I substituted beef Chorizo for the ground beef in this recipe, along with sweet potato tots because I knew the color orange would really pop for Instagram. Would recommend!

I made this using leftover BBQ pulled pork instead of beef; skipped the cheese and subbed cauliflower tots (that's what was in the freezer and wanted to use them up) for the regular tater tots. it was a great casserole to use up leftover meat. I liked it a lot, but the cauliflower tots didn't really do anything for me and if I make it again I would definitely use real tater tots.
In Eastern Washington we used mushroom soup and it was called “Real Good Meat”.
If using canned condensed soup, do you use it undiluted?
We mix in sour cream.

I found this to be quite bland and not very tasty at all. This was agreed on by my husband and two 17 year olds. I tried to spice it up while making, but nothing worked. Too much flour and milk turned into just a pasty goo. I am glad so many others liked it though.
Do you even need the flour? I'm leaving it out.
Oh my lord this is good. My husband felt it was too rich, which I cannot argue with. Wondering if there’s some kind of acid to add to cut through?
For the make ahead option, do you really mean to the end of step 4 or should it be step 5?

I made this and enjoyed making the "soup' from scratch but I am afraid that I and  a mistake with the "2  tablespoons chicken, vegetable or beef broth base or bouillon" .I interpreted this to mean a tablespoon of the undissolved beef base ie " Better Than Boulion". Was that correct?? It gave a strong taste to the sauce.Not unpleasant but a bit too strong??

This came out great! I used half the beef and added more veg, but I accidentally grabbed a bag of "super sweet golden corn" made the entire dish so sweet, heat helped to balance a bit. Next time will grab normal frozen corn and I know it will come out perfect.

I used Beyond Meat to create a vegetarian version of this and it worked perfectly. I would avoid going to the trouble of layering the meat and soup mixtures. It's a lot of effort for zero effect. Just stir the drained meat mixture or Beyond Meat into the soup and then top with the tater tots.

LOVED! Read about hot dish many times, but never worked up the nerve to try it. I couldn’t quite imagine what it would taste like. The BF likened this to Shepard’s Pie, which is spot-on. Did not include corn, but certainly will next time, I think it would add a sweetness and balance. Didn’t find making the soup difficult or time consuming.
Great, stick to your ribs meal. Used ground turkey instead of beef this time. With beef bouillon powder, it was plenty meaty enough. Will continue to make it regularly and put my own spin on it.

To save time, I made the veggies/sauce and browned the meat at the same time. It's worth washing the extra pan. The sauce was rich and worth the time; plus, there are no artificial ingredients in it, which is best. 

Very tasty.
I’m a New Englander. I had never eaten or even heard of this mess. I thought my meat-and-potatoes husband would like it. But sadly no. Personally, I’d rather eat meatloaf, peas, and tots.

I needed to save my carrot and celery for another recipe so I skipped it in the “soup” part and it was basically cream of onion. I used a bag of mixed vegetables that had carrots in them anyway. I also used green pepper in the beef instead of more onion. I thought this was really good but my husband said it was too heavy and my son was not a fan because I don’t make casseroles much. I’m glad I tried it though.

Made this exactly to the recipe.  It tasted good, but the soup was too thick for it to disperse and give the dish a moist feeling.  Next time I try it I will try a soup with a thinner consistency. All of that being said, everyone had 2 helpings :)

Made twice. First time my beef base was very salty so when added to my seasoning of other elements during cooking it was too salty Your beef base could be different so I highly reccomed you wait until the soup mixture is made before you pre/over season it. I changed a couple other things to the second attempt. I decided to use 1 lb of beef and 1 lb of  impossible beef (fake ground beef) it was totally unnoticed by all. Added 2 tbs tomato paste and mushrooms to the meat mix to up the umami.

I made this after a day of shoveling the 8 feet of snow we got in a big storm. Made the soups the way the recipe suggested, used half the meat because that's what I had on hand. Tasted great for 4 weary shovelers on a cold and wintery night.
I just want to thank all of the Minnesotans on this thread for the education about hot dish, though I will not be using a canned cream soup when I make this recipe.
What are tater tots, please?!

Any Minnesota Lutheran will tell you a casserole is bakeware in which you cook a hotdish. 
Personally I choose to avoid prepared soups. It allows for gluten free, dairy free, or reduced sodium choices.

Gussied up and quasi healthy.  Home Ec major, grad 1975 from ND made it for the dorm cafeteria. A major hit.  everything out of a can:  cream soup, drained and rinsed green beans, browned ground beef, salt and pepper (only spices allowed or available), tater tots.  all mixed together. no cheese.  I wouldn't make it now but most ingredients were pretty much all that was available.  still think it's a very satisfying dish.

Minnesota Norwegian Lutheran here, although I never resided in Lake Woebegon, but a town very much like it. 
If you have time try the recipe above to see if you like it.  Not sure what layering brings to the table, but whatever.
Yes, the AUTHENTIC version is very much like Taconite Tater Tot HotDish depending on where you live, because there are the Campbell's Cream (soup) shortcuts to save time.  It fed everyone and gave them calories for those -20F days.

I learned this dish in Richfield, MN at Mama's knee. She made it the old fashioned way with "cream of" soup and real beef. Im changing the way I eat and am going to try Tater Tot Hotdish with plant based beef AND cream of soup! My comment is regardless of where your soup comes from whyever would you squirt ketchup over this loving dish? Try it with a bit of mustard, it is so much better!
What would be a good substitute for the flour? I would like to make this for a GF guest. Thanks.
Try either potato starch or sweet rice flour.
Won’t add the corn next time I make. Added a sweetness to the dish that wasn’t necessarily welcome.

I made this but subbed pork for the ground beef - I did not think making your own “cream of” soup was too much work, I am more than happy to do an extra step to avoid the junk in that canned stuff. You can totally brown the meat in a separate pan while making the soup to cut down on time if you can multitask. Apparently I can’t bc I only added half the milk! But still it turned out great! My husband and son loved it.

Don't worry about layering the beef and soup mixtures, just dump and mix.  Next time, I'll cut back on the bouillon, but otherwise, this was a solid dish everyone in the family liked. 

*I recommend sprinkling fresh, diced jalapeños on the adult side of the casserole dish.

Total hit!  I made this tonight.  My mom is an 83 yo Minnesotian and relates totally to the ”hot dish”.  My daughters 13 and 16 loved it but wished the tot to main dish ratio was more to the tots and thought maybe next time wecould stand the tots on end.  Or bake them all as they come in a 2# package and add as needed.
Loved this, the only change I made was to use a 1/2 cup of flour as recommended by another review which made the sauce consistency perfect.
Thank you!

I think one must have grown up, or spent considerable time, in the Midwest to really enjoy this dish. And that’s true whether it’s this recipe or standard canned soup. I tried this, but it only rated a, “Meh.” I even added a splash of worchershire sauce and sautéed mushrooms.

If I have hamburger and tater tops on hand, I’ll opt for meatloaf with a side of tots. About the same amount of work.
Halved this recipe, no celery so I used celery seed and celery salt with extra carrots and some green onions. Ran out of tater tots and subbed half onion rings. Worked perfectly.
I used cornstarch instead of flour.  Used half the amount

We loved the meat version, but my younger son is getting interested in vegetarianism, so I decided to try making this with beans instead of beef. I substituted three cans of beans for the beef (just used what we had in the cabinet - black, kidney, and some kind of red bean). It was faster and turned out great.
I made it last night. The recipe works. Taste great.

The cream soup thickened nicely, with 3T butter instead of 6 and 1T butter and a little evoo when browning the beef. Instead of attempting the layers, I mixed the meat into the cream soup and spread into a 9x13 dish, topped with cheddar and nearly forgot the frozen peas which I then stirred through.
