One addition was to sprinkle with grated parm before topping with extra crispy tots. Had a request for the recipe and leftovers so I’ll definitely make this again.

I made a delicious tatertot casserole (first time I ever ate it!) last Thanksgiving, and trying to recreate it for this fall, remembered it came from this recipe! I used ground bison and added lima beans and probably more garlic and black pepper, but otherwise it was exactly this recipe and it all disappeared before the day was over with only two of us to eat it (pandemic). I say casserole because I am not Minnesotan, my boyfriend says hotdish because he is. It works fine.
Imagine quibbling over the difference between a casserole and a hot dish—and I say this as someone whose entire family on both sides is from Minnesota.

My Iowegian Lutheran in-laws would disagree with the addition of veggies in any form other than chopped onions sautéed with the beef. And, depending on the family member, onions might have to be omitted as well. American cheese, not cheddar!

Native Northwestern Iowan here, we always use French-cut green beans, but the addition of corn I will try when I make it next week. I add cheese to the top and then the tots, something my mom never did, but it makes it oh so much better!
So yummy!  Subbed lentils for the ground beef….delicious.
With the exception of mixing the sauce and sautéed burger/onion and dumping everything in the dish together, I followed recipe exactly. Delicious and very filling.
I browned off the Tater Tots before started the rest of the recipe.  I like the crunch of the cooked Tots.
If you want shepard’s pie just make it. It isn’t too difficult to boiled potatoes and mash them.

I once used only a couple of TB of butter and olive oil for the rest, and 2% milk. The "soup" thickens a lot so the difference is unnoticeable. Also, fresh peas worked perfectly. Tonight I'll try with ground turkey, chicken stock and frozen breakfast potatoes - my store rarely has tater tots. Also, the cheddar tends to melt down into the dish; if you add freshly grated parmesan, it will remain on the surface and form a tasty crust.
Used 2 percent milk and an extra 6 ounces of ground beef. Turned out great. Didn’t miss the whole milk. The cream soup was more flavorful than canned cream soup - worth the time imo.

I always appreciate recipes that don’t use the canned soup, as I can adapt them to be gluten free. (Also, why would the NYtimes bother to publish a recipe that is already on the internet in a thousand places?) So you lucky people that can eat whatever you want can stop whining. Some people with dietary restrictions need recipes like this. I used the Trader Joe’s AP GF flour and it worked well. I will probably use the cream soup part of this recipe as a sub in other condensed soup classics.
Looseknit your note made me lol. I tried to explain to my husband why I enjoy this site so much and when I read it out loud to him he got it!
I loved this recipe. It was easy, fun, and delicious! Made just as written. Would make it again and again!

This is the most 1950s recipe I have read in the Times. It reeks of a period when women thought of themselves as modern cooks and took advantage of all the modern conveniences. Chopping your own carrots, celery and onions is an affectation. A modern cook would use a frozen bag of mixed vegetables. I have the boxes of 3x5 cards of  recipes from granny's 4 sisters. this recipe could be slipped in unnoticeably with the jello molds, but there are some gems as well.
What is the best substitution for the milk, for those with a dairy allergy? Something like a soy or oatmilk, or a chicken broth instead?

I make this but we add a teaspoon of Berber to to cream soup it adds just the right flavor and we can reduce the salt that way. This cream soup I now use for most my other casseroles it is a perfect base and tastes better than any can soup.

I am always amazed at the inappropriate level of huffiness exhibited by commentators. If you want to use a more traditional recipe and use canned soup do so.  But that doesn’t negate the value of a different approach. 

I will say, though, that there is a simpler way to avoid canned soup. The Americas Test Kitchen version has you sauté ground beef, mushrooms and onions, then you sprinkle flour and add milk and cook till thickened.

I made this for dinner on a weeknight. The kids loved it. There was a lot of the meat/soup mixture; I ended up with a 9 x 12 and an 8x8 which was OK because I spiced the smaller one up a little. I would probably use more tator tots next time or make less of the meat/soup portion.  It now occurs to me that this is Shepard's Pie with a twist.

This is good, uncomplicated comfort food.  LOVE the tater tots on top (because really, who doesn't like tater tots?!).  I substitute  cup canned chicken broth (plus a pinch of thyme) for the milk because I do not typically use broth base or bouillon. Will definitely make it again.

-didn't have bouillon, used a couple dashes of Worcestershire sauce
-replaced flour with a heaping 1/4 c of cornstarch, made a slurry with some of the milk

I used this as a jump off to make my own based on what I had - great framework recipe! I used cubed chicken, mushrooms, carrots, onions, kale & corn. The gravy made from the Maillard reaction browned bits is critical - it added a delicious flavor depth. Tots were so fun as a topping (and easier than pie crust/biscuits) & the cheese was melty tasty!
Way better than the casserole slop from my youth that I was expecting.  Surprisingly easy, too.
I loved the comment section here and cannot believe not a single person mentioned the potential liberal use of Velveeta in this recipe!!!
This was a fun and yummy weeknight dinner! I was surprised at how easy it was to make. Comfort food! To me, the ketchup and hot sauce were a necessity.

Northern Minnesota here. At the first slight snow of the season yesterday, I made this dish. But the soup was far too dry/gluey, though the flavor was fabulous. Possibly the problem arose because I tried to cut it down, not needing to feed an entire Lutheran congregation. 

This morning, I tried a corrective, and it worked very well: I cut a slab of the cold hotdish, laid it in a pool of cream of mushroom soup, heated the sucker a couple of minutes in the microwave, and Bob's your uncle!

I rarely comment, but this recipe was a dud at our house. I was hoping for indulgent comfort food, but we found it bland with unappealing texture and taste. Maybe less milk would have improved the soupy base?
Also used a leaner grade of ground beef than recommended.

First time making or eating tater tot casserole, and it was pretty good overall. The sauce was much too thick and dry, though. I was surprised at the amount of flour called for. If I make it again, I’ll use much less flour, and maybe even the dreaded canned mushroom soup. Amy’s makes a delicious mushroom bisque that I think would work well here and provide more flavor. Or I’ll go rogue and mix the beef with salsa and cheese instead of bechamel.

This was delicious! Grew up in ND. Ate this a lot, but always made with canned "Cream Of …" soup. This is worth a try when you got some extra time. I made it exactly as written until I started to put the tots on top and realized I forgot got to add the frozen peas. I picked off the few tots that i had already placed, dumped the peas on top and mixed 'em in. Still turned out great. Not sure there is an advantage to the layering step.

My first time making Tater Tot casserole/hotdish. I made it with gluten-free cup-for-cup flour (celiac disease family member) and added sautéed mushroom bits to the cream sauce. Otherwise, followed the recipe. Overall, the dish is m'eh. Kinda salty and bland. Definitely not a family favorite.

Sorry to be negative after all the glowing reviews. But, unless you grew up in the Midwest and have fond memories of “hot dishes”…give this one a miss.  It’s heavy and bland (even with copious amount of black pepper and shredded cheese added and, ultimately, hot sauce),   It’s also more trouble to make than any number other, much better dishes, e.g.,  a roasted a chicken with vegetables roasted with it, and some rice pilaf on the side.  Even my teenaged son refused to eat it after a few bites.
Made exactly as written (except cut in half) and it was terrific. Not quick to make, but otherwise very easy. Will most definitely make this again (and again).
My wife is a huge tater tot casserole fan and she declared this to be TOO GOOD. I made it with Impossible ground beef and it was delightful.

Made this and it was pretty tasty, but would have been better without my modifications. I subbed half the meat for white beans (because I didn’t want to go to the store!!) and missed the part where the bouillon was added. I didn’t add the cheese either, just because we never made it that way in Winona, MN. But my family loved it!

Tasty and satisfying on a frigid day. The cream soup comes together quickly and tastes a lot better than the canned version. Cook until it has a thick consistency like condensed soup. I took others’ advice and stirred together the beef mixture and soup rather than layering. I also put the cheese on before the tater tots. I jazzed up the beef a bit with some garlic and paprika.
I thought this was just ok.  Would not make it again.
I feel like I tasted and seasoned this many times, but still wished I'd added more salt and pepper. So season liberally, I guess.

This was a big hit with my family, including my parents who are picky eaters.  I did add a bit of extra cheese as I layered the meat and soup.  I love that the recipe includes instructions for making your own creamy soup instead of using canned soup.  It's an extra step, but still very easy.  It also gives you room to adapt the recipe a bit.  Next time I think I'll add mushrooms to the soup.  The recipe is very forgiving - I did not have real butter.  I used a mix of margarine and olive oil.

This wasn’t bad at all. But as a native Minnesotan, this is no where near as good as it can be. Skip the work of making your own soup and just buy a couple cans of cream of mushroom/celery. It’ll save you 10 minutes and a whole lot of mess.
It was very easy to halve this recipe for dinner for 2 - and it yielded plenty of leftovers.  Delicious!
Cream of celery soup   frozen peas and carrots would work well. Consider the tip to cook tater tot first for 25 then together 20 min. Use low fat milk. Very rich.
Make extra tater tota

I prefer the Cook's Country / America's Test Kitchen Tater Tot Hotdish recipe version.  It calls for 1 pound of sliced mushrooms cooked together with onion and ground beef.  It also adds garlic and thyme to create a very flavorful base.  The liquid is half milk and half chicken broth.  Grated Parmesan cheese is added to help bind the base mixture and create a cheesy flavor.  Highly recommended!

I have so enjoyed reading all this controvery!  Being from the East Coast I had never heard of this dish....but after reading all the notes, I can't wait to make it.  I am usually in favor of making my own, fresh sauce as per the NYT recipe...but, hey, I'm going to do  the midwest version with good ol' condensed soup!  Some of the old timey convenience recipes are delicious.
One more thing.I read these recipes/notes daily and am so annoyed by bashing reviews when the cook has changed the ingredi
@Betsey, Bittman has you covered! https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1014464-tater-tots Always amused by the insanity of this comment section!
So good! We doubled up and did about 9 oz each frozen roasted corn and peas but other than that were faithful to the recipe. With a green salad it made a perfect dinner for a damp winter night!
It’s fine. It’s not flavourful. Shepherd’s pie is better. I ended up covering mine in HP sauce, and can see why ketchup is recommended.
Expected to hate this. But it was pretty good and the kids like it.

As a Midwesterner, specifically one hailing from Ms. Yeh's region, I can attest to the presence of Tater Tot Casserole (or hot dish) on our dinner tables. I particularly liked Ms. Yeh's inclusion of making your own creamy soup mixture as opposed to opening a can of cream of mushroom soup. I was able to adjust the flavors to my liking. It is right, though! It does come to a pretty un-spreadable mess. I would suggest editing the recipe to a point where everything else is ready first.

I make this every few weeks for the family. It makes for a great dinner and lunch for tomorrow combo. Making the “soup” is very definitely worth the time and effort over prepackaged soup. I did want to add that just before adding the 2nd portion of whole milk I turn the heat down to just above medium. This allows me to control the thickening process more precisely and gives me a more “spreadable” product. Other than that small change I make it as is, and it comes out delicious every time.
My family loves a good Tater Tot Hot Dish with lotsa shredded pepper jack cheese on top.
Very easy recipe that could be sped up by cooking the meat and soup at the same time.  This recipe is an easy take that ups the flavor game of a pretty simple casserole.
Made with chicken.  Added fresh sage and dried thyme.

I’ve made this recipe many times because I love it.  But you can save time and reduce the amount of butter by sautéing the entire onion with the other vegetables in the ‘soup’ steps. Then just brown the meat without adding the extra fat, which isn’t needed.  Also forget layering it.  Such a fussy and unnecessary step!  Just mix the browned ground beef into the soup and spread it all out in the casserole dish.

Re: my  favorite comment. Mary Myers tells us that this is not a casserole,  it is a hot dish. She does so emphatically, and I quote - “This is NOT a casserole, it is a hot dish. That’s a Minnesota thing.”
Yet then she writes that Minnesota’s Senior Senator, Amy Klobuchar, entered her version in a Senate casserole contest. It won. 
So… if it’s NOT a casserole, how could it have won a casserole contest?
It  can’t be both.. Hot dish… casserole…. which is it?
Must be a Minnesota thing.

I'm 96% certain I stuck to the portions in the recipe and there was no way this would fit into a 4 qt pyrex. I got a 5 quart because that was all Target had, and the ingredients totally filled the 5 qt pyrex.

Also, I added cumin, cayenne, paprika, and pepperoncini, next time I would also add jalapenos, cherry tomatoes. I used ground chicken instead of beef.

Lastly, maybe it came out good but would have benefitted from another 6 minutes in the oven.
Tried this knowing it wasn’t really my thing, but thought it might be something different for the fam…we just didn’t enjoy it very much.

I make this a lot but today was in a hurry and streamlined: fried the meat in a dutch oven threw in specified veg in stages, added mushrooms peas and spinach last, in that order, salting as needed; sprinkled the whole mess with about 4 tbsp flour (was making approx 1/3 recipe for my small family) stirred till it disappeared;added milk and water and about 1 tsp btb till a nice sauce came into being;  placed the tater tots on top and  all one pan and i like the effect of meat dripping in the gravy

I cooked a Trader Joes mirapoix in 6 tbs of butter and made the rest of the soup as directed. Make sure to season this and the beef well.Left it in a Dutch oven. Browned the beef in a little bit of olive oil in a separate pan, then just added it to the soup in the Dutch Oven, leaving out the extra juices. Mixed this all together with the frozen peas and did the tots/cheese as directed. It was tasty with hot sauce and/or ketchup.

FWIW, my first experience with TTC was in the dining room of my sorority at Purdue University in the 80's. So many of us disliked this dish, we concocted a plan to remove the recipe from the cook's recipe binder. Results were a sound scolding from our house mother,  and the realization that our cook didn't need the "recipe" to construct this dish! Lol...

This is a great recipe, thank you! Here are some modifications I made to create a version that’s a bit healthier since I’m watching out for saturated fat: I made this without any butter, I just used 1 tablespoon of olive oil when cooking the carrots, etc and 1 tablespoon when cooking the meat. (You could also just use broth instead of oil.) I used lean ground turkey (or chicken) instead of ground beef. I used 1.5 cups 1 percent milk and 1.5 cups almond milk (no sugar) instead of 3 cups whole milk. I used whole wheat flour instead of all-purpose. No cheese either. I added chopped fresh spinach to the meat mixture to sneak in another vegetable. Everything turned out great! And I don’t feel so guilty now.

My 16 year old son likes it and went for seconds.  His fussy twin brother looked it and tried a mouse size portion before declaring it was not for him.  It tasted great to me.  My only regret is that I forgot to include the corn so we had that as a side.  I’m looking forward to leftovers.
Going with a can since I’ve never made this recipe and don’t have much time! Keeping all proportions the same- how many cans of condensed cream soup do you recommend I use? Thanks!
Very easy and delicious! I used a can of cream of mushroom soup and a can of cream of chicken soup and added enough milk for 3 cups. Mixed everything together and tops with tots and cheese

Honestly this didn't work for me. I have no idea why. Is the milk layers supposed to melt into the meat layer? It didn't. And the layers just tasted bland. I have no why my favorite food groups did not come together.
@KK i also found the flavours pretty muted - I actually spiked the meat filling with a few more flavours and it ended up tasty enough - but overall not worth the effort, I think

I made this today and it was really good - I made sure to use sufficient seasoning as I read the comments about blandness but both my husband and my mother-in-law commented on how much they enjoyed it and that doesn't happen very often. I like the fact that the cream soup portion was from scratch as we have gluten and lactose issues here as well as heart attack and stroke survivors so we try to avoid to many processed foods.

For a little more flavor I added a packet of onion soup mix to the ground beef. I also have never made a "cream of chicken" from scratch, but it was delicious and worth the extra time if you are not in a hurry. This recipe is a hit and wonderful warm family meal in the winter.
I tend to be meticulous with prep so it took me an hour to get it into the oven.  My wife says it's a keeper.
One kitchen chemistry trick is to mix a little corn starch with the sour cream before adding to prevent it from curdling.
@Mike - thanks!
@Mike 1/2 tablespoon to 1 cup?

My mother made this 50 years ago.  She used flank steak, slightly frozen, sliced across the grain.  It was an economical cut of meat then. First she put on the water for noodles and they cooked whilst she made the beef. She sauteed onions and mushrooms and removed them from the pan. She browned the beef slices and removed them.  Then she deglazed and reduced the white wine and added the creme fraiche.  Seasoning was done along the way.  Dinner was ready in about 20 minutes. Magical!

Just in case folks don't have access to creme fraiche, Daisy brand sour cream does not curdle and has that cultured tang. I think this is because it is only made with cream a cultures, and not stabilizers? But I don't actually know why. It just works. 
I have no affiliation with the brand - I've just been frustrated by a lot of sour cream!
I use only Daisy for all of these reasons.  Good body and flavor to boot!
@Jessica yep, this absolutely worked. Thanks!

You can make an acceptable substitute for creme fraiche if you can't find it. Add a tablespoon of plain yogurt to a cup of heavy cream and leave it on the counter for a few hours. I use a jar covered with a cloth or paper towel.  Once it reaches the consistency you desire you can store in the fridge for a week or so.
My favorite recipe uses nutmeg in the sauce!
I always add a glug of dry sherry instead of wine then some thyme or nutmeg and plenty of fresh ground black pepper.
My mom also always used fresh dill, something else that made the meal “fancy”, and it’s pretty hard to beat.
Why do I have to put the beef strips onto a rimmed baking sheet?
To let the excess flour drift off the meat.  On a flat surface, it would get gummy without any air circulating around it.
@Chatulim9 a rimmed baking sheet is a flat surface, unless it has a rack placed in it

You can increase the amount of gravy by adding 1-2 tsp of oil & using the left over flour/paprika mixture to make a roux. Use either low salt chicken or beef broth & more wine then add the cream. As soon as it’s the consistency you want add the meat & mushrooms. Be sure 2 adjust the seasonings. A dash or 2 of hot sauce will brighten it up as will a dash of really good sherry vinegar (from Shiraz).

Must confess to preferring James Beard's recipe in his "Great American Cookery" cookbook.  Elegant in its simplicity.  Shallots, Worcester, Tabasco, sour cream, tarragon and best best cut of beef your budget allows.  Look it up.  Cooking to rave reviews since the 70's.
@Craig Burdick My thoughts exactly. Tried this one because it's fun to try new things, and it was OK, but ... You just can't top James Beard's Stroganoff.
@Craig Burdick If anyone sees my comment and has a link to the James Beard recipe mentioned, please post the link.  Can’t find the mentioned recipe online, so far, and would love to try it.  Thanks!
@Crea May. I feel like I am being completely disrespectful to Sam Sifton (who is a God as far as I am concerned) but I would be interested in perusing the James Beard recipe as well.
My mother also made this dish, and I have made it for my family as well. IMHO, the parsley is more than merely a garnish. It is MANDATORY. It makes the dish.

I’ve been using the stroganoff recipe from the New Basics cookbook for years and it is a family favorite. That one calls for creme fraiche which as always worked beautifully.  Also, I usually use leftover cuts from a great steak or roast and just lightly sauté them.  I also offer caraway seeds as a topping for the noodles which my mom always did when I was a kid.  Yum!  I’ll try this recipe next time to compare.

It wouldn’t surprise me if everyone’s mom introduced this dish, and for my mom and us kids beef stroganoff was fun first and always because this was her first experience using any alcohol in cooking, which we thought wasn’t just weird but pretty darn fancy. To this day I get a smile on my face when I deglaze with brandy before adding the creme fraiche.

My mum taught me to make this when I was about 12 or 13. And indeed, it was pretty fancy and so flavoursome to deglaze with wine or brandy. I return to stroganoff every winter and enjoy its simplicity and deliciousness.
I use Greek yogurt for the dairy.
By all means, use generously buttered noodles, sprinkled with just a quarter cup of parsley for color and freshness, as the perfect blank canvas for practically any stew or braise.

If you like this, and I love it, the Craig Claiborne recipe from 1963 on Cooking is for my money the gold standard. His uses tomato paste, not dijon, and half a cup of flour in which you dredge the beef before sauteeing. No wine, but beef stock. The flour effectively creates a roux as you cook the beef. I like the paprika and dijon in this one though. Next, I may try to make the two recipes side by side. I once had stroganoff over shoestring fries, and it was awesome.

@Steve: I’ve also had it as the filling for pasties in Auburn, CA. I was VERY hesitant to try it as I had my heart set on the traditional beef/potato filling but they didn’t serve the traditional. I was pleasantly surprised and loved the stroganoff filling—really terrific!
Sherry makes a food substitute for the white wine-

Stepped off the beaten path of this recipe and pulled one off the internet that used NY strip steak, lemon zest and butter in the noodles, and Brandy among other things. Knocked it out of the park. If you want to turn this dish from something good to great this is the recipe:https://www.epicurious.com/recipes/food/views/steak-stroganoff
@BM I can’t access the link since I am not an Epicurious subscriber. Meh

I made this tonight and it was wonderful! For the beef, I used choice angus tip roast (St. Helena Beef) that the butcher cut into strips. I used cremini mushrooms instead of the button variety and deglazed the pan with port.  The result was perfectly, tender beef in a delicious sauce. I served the beef on pappardelle pasta with fresh green beans on the side. Shut up! It was GOOD We loved it. If you love comfort food, this is for you.

While waiting to check out at the grocery yesterday I saw this recipe online, bought ingredients, and made last night. Followed as directed, except could not find “hot” paprika at the market so used smoked paprika instead. This is delicious! A fairly quick make; lots of rich flavor from the spice, mustard, and Worcestershire. Perfect for a wintry Sunday dinner. Going into regular rotation.
Can you substitute tri tip for the sirloin roast?
Tri tip is part of the bottom of the sirloin, and people recommend it for stroganoff.
Sure, no problem.
I made this exactly as described. Used top sirloin strips, smoked Spanish style paprika, and creme fraiche from Vermont Creamery, and it was superb. Perfect.

Alain Allegretti, late of Le Cirque, Atelier and his own restaurant, La Promenade des Anglais, recomnends adding a touch of prepared horseradish to the creme fraiche (or in this case, sour cream).
It really makes a difference.

Using a (rimmed) baking sheet is helpful prep method for keeping pieces of food separate from each other prior to and following cooking. A baking sheet is a nice, flat wide tool that makes it easy to manage food when cooking/frying in batches especially if laying it out to keep dry or to lay on paper towels to absorb oils/grease.

Delicious!  The hot paprika and mustard elevate the dish!  We used tenderloin and I will definitely make again.  Not like the bland other stroganoff dishes on the internet.  As per another suggestion, I added half of the heavy cream, then the meat at the end.  After the meat was incorporated, I substituted the other half of the cream for sour cream.  Rave reviews all around!!

While I liked the preparation of meat in this recipe (don’t bother with a rimmed baking sheet, ps), the final meal wasn’t good. Way too much butter, and the heavy cream was too heavy. What really ruined it for me was the addition of Worcestershire and mustard. Gave it a terrible flavor. I’m no stroganoff connoisseur, but I will not be making this recipe again. I’ve always loved Chef John’s Classic Stroganoff recipe from All Recipes.
GG - Yes, you can use tri tip which is just the end of the sirloin, a small, triangular cut from the sirloin. It is also known as a triangle steak, bottom sirloin steak, or Santa Maria steak.

Made this last night and my husband, who is not a beef lover, raved about it.  I used a filet mignon and creme fraiche and it was perfect.  Used all the other ingredients as listed. Yes, you do not need a rimmed baking sheet but it helps to spread out the seasoned meat to keep the seasoning intact.  After you sear the meat, you can put it on a plate.  The only change I suggest is that you wait to boil the water until after you have seared the meat.

I made the recipe as written except I substituted dry vermouth for white wine and added some extra vermouth. Delicious but takes a long time to prepare the meat. Better for a weekend than a weeknight.

This will become my new go-to beef stroganoff recipe, I'd been making Jonathan Waxman's version for years but lost the recipe.  This one is similar but I like the addition of the Worcestershire sauce and dijon mustard.  I also doubled the mushrooms, because you just can't have enough in this recipe.   Served with German egg noodles. Delicious!
With thanks to Jessica… She is absolutely right. Daisy Brand sour cream goes in smoothly and deliciously. No separating! A delicious recipe.

My dad was a pastor in a small town. We frequently received a cut of deer meat when hunters harvested a deer. Usually it was a tough piece of meat, not choice, but a nice thank you to pastor. 

Mom used these cuts for stroganoff. Long slow simmer. Stroganoff is a great use for cheaper and tougher meats, as well as wild game. Enjoy.
This dish was a crowd pleaser. Very rich and full of flavor. I used smoked paprika instead of hot and it still turned out great.

This is a great, easy recipe.  To reduce the number of bowls used, I floured the steak in a bowl, seared the steak as directed and returned to floured bowl. Using a cutting board, cut up the mushrooms and scallion. 

I doubled the sauce because I had a little extra meat and I'm glad that I did. Overall it was a quick, easy, tasty meal. Next time, since my kids don't love steak too much, I think I will try chicken thighs.

I made this dish with leftover prime rib scraps and cap meat, chopped. Gave prime rib scraps a quick browning , dusted with flour, sweet paprika and a dash of cayenne ( I didn't have hot paprika)  Love the idea about using thinly sliced tri tip here- I think that would be great. Cognac is a great alcohol to deglaze with. I used both white wine, and Cognac.  Tips I realized: dry mustard would've been better than Dijon. Parsley is required. Do not skimp on browning the mushrooms!12 minutes!
Rookie question. Why are people talking about sour cream when the recipe calls for heavy cream? Thxc

@Clint Woods
Because sour cream is traditionally used.

@Clint Woods it is also traditionally used in place of the heavy cream or creme fraiche. In fact, it seems strange it wasn’t also listed as a dairy option considering the tang I usually associate with stroganoff. I see some people claim sour cream tends to curdles, but I’ve never had that happen when stirred in finishing the dish. Perhaps it depends on whether the sour cream is of good quality without the stabilizer or thickener additives.
Loved this recipe, however the Dijon mustard was the overwhelming flavor. If I were to make again I would reduce the mustard by 2/3 or remove entirely.

Made this in '73!!! And now...Definitely needs adjustments:
1) double the mushrooms; why not?; more moisture & stretched ingredients.
2) reduce sauté time for beef to achieve rare= 2-3 minutes or less per side
3) substitute Greek yoghurt for sour cream; yum & healthier: texture perfect!
5) If using a large sauté pan, needs at least 1C+ white wine to deglaze
6) had no noodles in pantry; substituted pappardelle; excellent!
Voila
My husband and I have had this twice. It is definitely a keeper. Better than our other stroganoff recipes.
Wow! This was delicious!  Used "Baja steak" and Daisy sour cream (because I didn't read the recipe before shopping).  We both liked our moms' stroganoff in the 1960's, but this was sooo much better!
I made this with leftover prime rib.  Winner Winner. used good Sherry instead of wine(usually use Brandy but had none) and some of the prime rib ju.  Buttered parsleyed egg noodles are a must.

I did not like this recipe at all!  It wasn't bad necessarily, but definitely not good.  It was as if the ingredients were all arguing with one another--and I followed it to the letter.  I notice most comments mention varying the recipe.
Next time, I will use the one in New Basics.  So disappointed!

Disappointingly bland. For the meat, I subbed 8 oz. ribeye (a cut we love) + 4 oz. shredded carrots for color and crunch. For the heavy cream, I subbed a half-cup each of whole milk Greek yogurt and half-and-half (no curdling problems). All other ingredients were identical. No zip, no zest, certainly not the "silken" effect touted in the intro. Still in search of a yummy stroganoff I'll try the Epicurious recipe recommended by another commenter, though it means buying some brandy.

Spoon sauce into the cream before adding to temper it and prevent curdling. I used 1/4 cup heavy cream, 1/2 cup sour cream. Also 1 1/2 cups soup stock for liquid apart from cream and wine. Agree, parsley is mandatory. This was the first time I've had stroganoff and I emerged a changed man.

Delicious! After deglazing the pan, I added 1 1/2 c beef broth, which gave the sauce more volume as well as lightening it up a little (got the idea from the Craig Claiborne NYT recipe).  I tempered the sour cream with a few tablespoons of the sauce and a splash of whipping cream before stirring it back into the pan (off the heat), and had no curdling issues. And yes, the parsley is a must!

This is a tasty rendition of Beef Stroganoff. I always used sour cream in my Beef Stroganoff, but I now realize that I prefer using heavy cream, as indicated in this recipe. When I make beef stroganoff it is usually because I need to use up some left over cooked beef. This time it was left over prime rib - excellent.
Crème Fraîche is super easy to make:  Stir together one cup heavy cream, 1/4 cup buttermilk, and 1 Tbsp lemon juice.  Cover and let sit at room temperature for 6-8 hours before refrigerating.

A couple of things changed to accommodate a 97 year old's needs: 1) Used 94% lean ground beef divided into small clumps, and 2) went drastically low salt. 
The meat, once mostly browned, didn't need draining. At that point added the paprika and a tablespoon of flour. 
The lack of salt compromised an otherwise delicious dish. I would recommend adding about 1 teaspoon minced/grated garlic if preparing a low sodium version.
Yes, I always add garlic and make a low sodium version. Those who want more salt can add it.

I make this frequently, always using tenderloin.  This is just as I recall a friend of mine in Budapest making it, when I first fell in love with the dish.  That was all the way back in the mid 90s when I lived in Eastern Europe briefly, but I always remember.  At the time I lived in Poland and when my friend visited I asked him to make it, I had enjoyed so much at his home.  We foraged for mushrooms (it was the season and a big thing in Poland) and made with the wild mushrooms.  I was thrilled to find this recipe after so many years.  I must have made this a dozen times, and it’s always a hit.

Made this tonight and afterwards, thought, unfortunately, there is often a glitch in NY Times recipes, albeit, I turn to NY Times for new recipes to try.  First, there is no reason to transfer the meat to a rimmed baking sheet.   Ext, the instructions for cooking noodles is too early in the process and should say, cook according to directions on package, because if anyone boiled egg noodles for 10 minutes they would be mush.  
All in all this was a good recipe but not the best.
This is my go to recipe. Tonight I only had sour cream so I diluted with oat milk.  The result was scrumptious
My formerly wealthy ‘56 refugees got me to put in some horseradish.  Can’t do without, now.
Mine didn’t turn out anything like the picture but still tasty. Would also echo the doubling of the sauce
Not enough sauce. The addition of broth or more wine would be helpful. It was too dry to properly coat the meat and noodles.

We used about 1/3 cup of sour cream whisked into 2/3 cup heavy cream, added a little tang with minimal risk of breaking. Also used a Syrah left over from the previous night instead of white wine. Was a great dinner!
I am allergic to dairy, so I used dairy-free sour cream.  It did not curdle, and it was absolutely delicious.

So the mustard for me made this way too sour, but I did use Dijon and I used a heaping tablespoons. Once I added the mushrooms back in thank God it saved the dish. It also reminded me that mushrooms are amazing and need to be highlighted in winter more.

I cooked this, 4 out of 5 stars. My 11yo picky eater liked it. I have always cooked stroganoff with stew meat, which requires longer cooking before putting the cream in. I seared the meat, cooked the onions garlic and mushrooms, added the salt pepper & paprika directly to the mix rather than in the dredge, and then added some beef stock. I let it cook for more than an hour before adding cream and the noodles. I put corn starch directly into the cream as another reply stated and it worked great.

I have made this a couple of times with pork loin, which is very reasonable compared to beef sirloin or tenderloin. It’s delicious, and less fussy to cook because there’s less risk of overcooking the pork. I sliced the pork in advance and seasoned with salt, pepper, and paprika (smoked instead of hot, as that’s what I had) and skipped the flour.  A quick sear, then proceeded pretty much as written.

Great variation: Sub venison for steak, use Spanish hot smoked paprika, sliced button and porcini mushrooms and a red wine, like Bonanza. We got some poorly butchered venison chops and this variant was born… best thing ever
Would plain Greek yogurt work as a substitute for the cream?
Like a charm.

I love beef stroganoff, often use a blade roast for a crock pot version on a week day.  Concocted a ground beef stroganoff when we first got married to make a budget meal, a mushroom stroganoff and another squash version for vegetarian family members.  Have served it on rice, gnocchi, even on mashed potatoes.
Would love to know how to make a mushroom version. Somehow substitute portobello for the beef?
I've made this with lean ground beef - works well. And dill is an absolute necessity! We like it over rice.

Rich, delicious. I made it with sirloin because I didn't feel like spending $30/lb at my local market. It was fantastic.  For my non-meat eater (who doesn't mind if food is cooked in animal fat) I added more mushrooms and I removed some of the mushroom sauce before adding the meat.  Served with green beans. Nothing more required. Excellent winter dinner without a lot of effort! (oh and I made it with creme fraiche)

Made this tonight for the first time. Superb recipe. I agree the fresh parsley is key. As it happens, we had some volunteer parsley in the garden left over from last year. We used heavy cream, and will try half and half next time.

Wow! What a great dish! 86 year old father-in-law and wife loved it. Used 1 tsp of Hungarian paprika for my heat intolerant family and it was just right. Served with Franey’s steamed broccoli. What a special Sunday meal. Looks like I’ll have to buy your book! May we get a signed copy?

I think the McCall’s cookbook recipe circa 1969 for “Perfect Beef Stroganoff” is better than the Sam Sifton recipe.  I don’t mean to be a foodie snob, but topping stroganoff with anything except fresh dill strikes me as prosaic.  And using any cut of beef less tender than filet seems stubbornly proletarian.  Also, meat extract paste, catsup, and bouillon in the McCall’s recipe strike me as clever shortcuts.  Serving the sauté atop a melange of wild and white rices a la McCall’s?  Da!
I used fine almond flour, ghee and zucchini noodles for a still amazing, slightly healthier version.

Add dill, 
Add cornstarch to sour crm to prevent curdling?
This is lacking in flavor. It’s just like frying steak and pouring heavy cream over it. Not a good recipe in my opinion.
I followed this recipe and it was full of flavor. Did you follow this recipe?
@catherine exactly. No umami at all. Used heavy cream. So boring.

Stupid question: Why would I transfer the beef from the bowl with the flour to a baking sheet only to remove the meat to a skillet and then place the seared meat back on the same baking sheet? Could I just remove the meat from the bowl to the skillet then place the meat on a baking sheet?

Perhaps to let it dry off a little before searing it?  Makes for better browning.  Important to keep pieces separated from each other, as well, and there's enough space on the sheet pan, both before and after browning to do that

Cooked triple the amount of creminis first so they got nicely browned, instead of steaming in a salty pan. Added shallots and seasoning after mushrooms were deeply browned. Cooked the dredged beef next and removed. Made a roux using surplus dredge, and extra sauce using dry vermouth, heavy cream, sour cream and dijon, over med-low heat. Cooked the noodles just before serving, and didn't butter them so they'd soak up the sauce instead. Definitely lots of parsley. Fantastic.

When you brown the meat, there will be a big dark swath of blackened flour on the bottom of your skillet. Don’t be alarmed. When your wine and cream goes in, this fond will come up from the bottom and makes tasty rich sauce.  Just delicious.  My kids could not stop eating this.

I cooked this for my family (and in-laws) and everyone loved it. I cooked as directed but had to increase the quantity and did so with ease by simply adding more of all of the ingredients and by adding the flour mixture to some chicken broth to sort of make a roux. I used 2 lbs of meat, 1.75 lbs mushrooms, 3/4 cup wine, etc. Turned out so rich with so much depth of flavor. Some of the NYT’s recipes have been tough to double but this one was very forgiving. Everyone had seconds, even the kids.
definitely a keeper.  would cut the beef into smaller chunks next time, otherwise a great meal, and prep time was actually accurate (i find i often have to double the nyt estimates)

Used tenderloin not because I was “feeling fancy” but because—in this trip down nostalgia lane—my mom did. Also, if semifrozen, it can be thin-sliced without pounding, and cooks faster (both good reasons, in my book). Instead of wine, used Cognac, and skipped the Worcestershire. And BTW, let’s recall that even plain ol’ unsmoked, “sweet” paprika once seemed pretty “exotic”!
Re curdling issues: Daisy and/or heavy cream’ll work, but yogurt’ll yield an ugly, “uncorrectable” sauce.
Delicious! Whole family enjoyed this, but next time, use smoked or sweet paprika rather than hot.

We were all out of meat and other protein during this shelter-in time, but had a large harvest of beautiful fresh mushrooms from our csa  box.  Added 1/2 cup beef stock with the wine, otherwise followed the recipe.  Served over buttered basmati rice.  A delicious dinner.
I used Labneh because that’s what we had (#quaratinecooking) and tbh this tastes exactly like a Big Mac. I’m not mad about it?
Did anyone find it a little too mustardy?  Next time I’d reduce it by half a teaspoon or measure more carefully!

I made this tonight using one big piece (about 1.15 lbs) of American wagyu ribeye. It was on sale at Eataly for 50% off, which made it cheaper than the tenderloin, so I thought I'd try it. It was absolutely ridiculous. The most delicious experiment ever. I used heavy cream as well (instead of sour cream or creme fraiche).
Make it solely with tenderloin. Wonderful and great recipe. I love the coarsely cracked pepper, for it adds great flavor. Far superior to the NYT times cookbook recipe.
I used whole milk Greek yogurt in place of the creme fraiche/sour cream/cream.  It works very well.
A little fish sauce is my secret ingredient. Instant Unami. Highly recommend.

My recipe (delicious!) changes
2lbs New York strip trimmed of fat and cut i to strips
Cremini mushrooms 9 oz
1/2 Lemon zest and Juice
Heavy cream 1/2 cup or so
1 can beef broth
1yellow onion
4 garlic cloves
2 tsp hot paprika 
Generous fresh pepper grind
Parsley finely chopped
1 1/2 teaspoons dry mustard
Reduced with cognac 1/2 cup or so
The better the meat, the better.   Blend mustard and wors sauce with sour cream and add at end after heat is off.
A little too much mustard for my taste. Ended up needing more salt and Worcestershire to get the right balance but tasty!!
The mustard was...not good.
Mine came out tasting overwhelmingly of mustard. Turned me off the dish, which is sad because sirloin is not cheap. To be quite honest, I prefer the hamburger helper version.

In an unusual move, made exactly as directed! Used beef loin and creme fraiche, definitely not my mother’s stroganoff. It was exquisite.   And easy.  Perfect comfort food.  When we can once again entertain, this will be on the menu.

Adding both dill and caraway seeds, as some say they do,  is very Russian. My go-to recipe was from James Beard and was always excellent. This one fancies it up a bit by adding mushrooms, some paprika and mustard. I learned to work with sour cream since we didn't have access to creme fraiche, I never used heavy cream.  It was a dinner party dish in my house and was always enjoyed.

Unlike many other commenters, I have never made beef stroganoff before and nor has it been cooked for me by family (a Czech friend once cooked it for me but I don’t think he was a great chef). So, I followed the recipe exactly, and found the outcome to be delicious. My husband agreed, and we rarely share food preferences. I look forward to cooking again, maybe tweaking with some of the adjustments found in the comments to find the perfect dish.

I remember this recipe from years ago. It was the one recipe my Mom could cook well.  She also used nutmeg.  I decided to use zoodles instead of the buttery noodles, but it came out perfectly. Lightly heat the zucchini noodles in olive oil, butter, and a pinch of salt and pepper serve over the scrumptious stroganoff.  You really can't go wrong with this recipe.  I have made it with turkey burger meatballs, chicken, and sirloin steak.  All wonderful!  You can even use cauliflower rice. Light carb
Many years ago, a friend gave me a great tip on beef stroganoff, and will work with any recipe for it -- grated orange peel in the sauce. A stunning and wonderful difference.

I have made the original Craig Claiborne NY Times recipe since 1970s - it is crazy delicious and simple with fewer steps and ingredients. It is important to stop the meat at medium rare - if you do it will be so tender and flavorful.

Made this with a leftover piece of very rare sirloin.  Added a sprinkle of flour to the sauce to thicken slightly.  Used a combination of a little heavy cream and Daisy light sour cream because Stroganoff needs the sour cream tang.  It was delicious and will be made again.

Made with lactose-free whipping cream (for those who are lactose intolerant) and hot smoked paprika.  Was very good, best beef stroganoff I have made, but next time I think I will use regular paprika.  The smoky flavor was distracting.
Add nutmeg, pepper, dill and mustard at end

This is a excellent modernization of the awesome old standard!  Like several others wrote, I grew up eating a version with nutmeg....so I made this recipe as written plus 1/4 t. nutmeg in the dredge.  It was fantastic!  The meat alone is a worthy meal for carnivores.

Used my sirloin cap from butcherbox - next time, would cube this, just due to the cut. This was tasty and rich, albeit maybe a little too salty (which is shocking for me to say!). I used a bit of cayenne in the flour dredge, which gave everything a nice spice, and maybe would scale back to .5 teaspoon of salt until I tasted later on. This is a repeat!
Worked as written. Except there is no way you're going to get medium rare meat at the end of all this.

This was terrific: I haven't had beef stroganoff since I was a young girl, and my father would have loved this. It really came together very easily. I would love to make again. Did not drown it in 1-1/2 teaspoons of salt, however.

I chopped up some fresh rosemary and when I braised the beef i added it in then.  I also chopped up some dill and added that to the noodles when they were done.  Also, use plenty of butter on the noodles as well.  Delicious!

My mother also made this about 50 years ago. We loved it — egg noodles and sour cream. Yum!!!
It wasn’t until I was a grown-up that I learned it was supposed to include wine!

Ok I’ve compared this recipe to all the other stroganoffs I’ve made. Hands down this is our favorite.. I don’t think I  used paprika in stroganoff before .. I did use sour cream ,portobello mushrooms, and a nice tri tip. I followed the measurements ,  because I like to try the chefs  before I alter anything. Bravissimo !!

I added a little beef broth right before I added the cream. Used a quart and then half a pint. 

I think in the future, I will either half the venison or double the other ingredients.

Made this with half and half instead of heavy cream or cream fraiche (or sour cream as mentioned in the comments).  Definitely needed more than a cup of half and half though and after, I kept adding a bit of water to keep the consistency of the sauce right.  I also added a tablespoon of store-bought demi-glace and that took it to another level.  Fantastic recipe overall, highly recommend.

Served this to our ladies group last night as I could cook ahead of time. It was a big success and my husband is thrilled we have leftovers. I was more familiar with Joy of Cooking recipe which calls for nutmeg, so I grated a bit and mixed in before serving. Delicious.

For a vegetarian-friendly option (that isn't mushrooms), change out the beef for seitan. Coat and season it and throw it in your Dutch oven with a bunch of butter to brown it, and follow the rest of the recipe as directed. The seitan provides that nice meaty component without the meat.
Ok, my mother also made this dish. It wasn’t till I was an adult that I learned that it was supposed to be made with wine, but we loved it. Wide egg noodles and sour cream—how could you go wrong?!
This comes out really dry for me, not much sauce. Suggestions?
This is a fantastic meal.  Suggestion; double the wine and double the mushrooms to make the delicious sauce go further.
Here's another vote for using dry sherry in place of the white wine.  The stuff I use rhymes with dry sack.
Use cremini mushrooms for a richer mushroom flavor!
I made this exactly as written, and my husband and I both loved it.  I used filet tails and crème fraiche.  I will double the mushrooms and sauce next time.  Rich and delicious!
Made this yesterday. Proved to be popular with the teens (they forsook their iPhones during dinner). I would go easy on the salt though.

I found that when searing the meat, I needed far more than 1TBLS of oil and I ended up needing to do so in 3-4 batches instead of 2 as the recipe states. Also - if you prefer a more saucy stroganoff like I do, I would 1.5x the sauce ingredients. The flavor of this dish is spot on but I found I needed to tweak quantities slightly to get it just right.

Just lovely and easy! Make this all the time in the winter! Beef is expensive right now so I usually double the mushrooms and use a pound of beef, rather than skimping on quality. Still plenty filling and absolutely delicious. 

I have also never seen the need to place the beef on a rimmed baking sheet? I skip that step every time just to avoid another dirty dish, and it still tastes wonderful but maybe someone else can shed light on the purpose of this step.
So where do you place the meat if not on a rimmed baking sheet? You have to put it somewhere! Rimmed is the recommendation because that will prevent the juices from running onto your counter.

I’m not an experienced cook, but I had trouble with the flour cooking on the pan and browning thickly. So I decided to deglaze the pan with the wine between batches (I did three). Then after deglazing I reserve the soupy gravy. I add it back at the end.
Used 1 teaspoon sweet paprika, 1/2 teaspoon hot

I agree with KatherineB, great with leftovers. I sliced a very tough steak thin and pounded with a meat tenderizer. Dusted with smoked paprika/salt/pepper/flour mixture and added to the sauce at the end. Tossed until warm. Very satisfied with this recipe.

Clear, easy recipe yielded a rich, comforting and oh so tasty meal. Some specifics:  used whipping cream for the sauce and beef tenderloin.  My only change for the next time, and there will be a next time, is to double the button mushrooms.  Thanks

I love this recipe as is but to make it week night friendly, I pop 1 lb of frozen meatballs in the oven then proceed with step 5 onward to make the sauce and noodles. It’s done in 30 minutes and my kids love it.
maddies fav- creme frach huge difference, make with more mushrooms if can
If you’re a student, on trying save a penny, I used stewing beef and it worked great. Just need to be more judicious with your tenderizing.
Prepared per recipe with thick sirloin and heavy cream option. Simple and delicious. Highly recommended.

use white wine to glaze pan after beef AND after mushrooms
-
add 1/2 cup white onion, 1/4 cup shallot
-
little over a teaspoon of grey poupon
1 1/2 teaspoons worstershire

I was glad I spent the extra money on Creme Fraiche 
used NY Strip steak cut and cooked as in the recipe, it seemed to work well
Double mushroom and slightly less meat seemed to work
Family enjoyed it even after watching the cream and butter go in
Made this with leftover Christmas rare tenderloin and it was incredible!
Prepared exactly by the recipe for my New Year's Eve dinner. Outstanding!
I made this with ground venison and it was so good!

Absolutely delicious! I used Angus top sirloin steak, cut thickly & it was perfectly cooked to medium rare. I used a heaped tbsp of regular paprika & after frying off the beef sprinkled a generous measure of smoked paprika on the beef. I used a whole small 187ml bottle of wine & cooked it down and was generous with the Worcestershire sauce & mustard. This is definitely different in flavor profile to stroganoffs I've cooked before but I really liked it and so did my very picky 15 year old.

Used already cooked leftover prime rib. 
Onion instead of shallots

Made with leftover rare beef tenderloin. Perfect. Two small adjustments: 1/2 cup of wine instead of 1/4 cup and chives instead of parsley. The breading of the beef adds a tonne of flavour; do not skip.
Less mustard. More acid,
Absolutely delicious. A favorite winter recipe.
This is a great recipe. Made it many times especially with leftover steak. I use cognac and add chopped fresh dill, otherwise as written.

Didn't have any Dijon mustard or shallots. So skipped the mustard and used a yellow onion which carmalized beautifully. Great hardy meal. Perfect for after a cold day of searching for a Christmas tree!

I have made this recipe twice.  Once exactly as written using the heavy cream.  It was very good.  The second time I hunted down the Crème Fraîche (not always easy to find where I live) and substituted brandy for the white wine.  OMG!  Spectacular!  Truly a world of difference in flavor and complexity.

So so so good. And easy and fast! I also upped the cream just a bit and doubled the mushrooms because we are mushroom lovers. And I also used a few tablespoons minced onion instead of shallots since I didn't have any on hand. Love an easy dinner that yields good leftovers.

I took cues both from Sam Sifton's recipe and Craig Claiborne's. I used 15% Śmietana, tomato paste, and bone broth. The sauce had a rosy tinge and tasted like silk. Nothing curdled. I bought a beef tenderloin from Metro and cleaned it myself (thanks YouTube). I learned a new skill and saved about 1/3 by my reckoning over what I would have paid the butcher shop. The Stroganoff won hearts and minds even if I dared to serve it over Pappardelle in Italy to Italians!

Delicious rendition of the old classic. Double the sauce and maybe the mushrooms too. Cook meat half the time or less to get medium-rare. Reduced salt to 1 tsp. Used just 1 tsp. of hot paprika because of husband's aversion to heat, but may sneak in a tad more next time. Heavy cream is perfect and makes a silky sauce. My sauce was golden-brown, darker than the photo, and therefore more appetizing!
Delicious!! I added a bit of tomato paste on the mushrooms/onion step and also 1 tsp soy sauce with the mustard/Worcestershire sauce. A real winner
Remember a little nutmeg

This was great. I used leftover steak and added fresh thyme when cooking the mushrooms and shallots. Also sprinkled those with garlic powder. Used sweet vermouth as I was out of white wine and the flavor depth was amazing. Finally, substituted the cream/creme fraiche with 1/2 cup Greek yogurt thinned with some milk and it worked well.
Hard to believe this gets 4 stars. The flouring of the beef was unnecessary and thickened the sauce much too much.
Definitely needs more sauce. But tasty
We did this with just a variety of mushrooms rather than adding beef- the mushrooms hold flavor!

This recipe was perfect. I realized after i added the beef tenderloin back that there was too much beef so added an extra cup of heavy cream & an extra tsp of both mustard & Worcestershire   a tad of s&p & it worked out perfectly. I’ve never had such rave reviews w/ stroganoff before. Never made it w/o sour cream before but the white wine   extra Worcestershire & mustard made up for the tang wonderfully. Didn’t use hot paprika because of spicy sensitive grandma but i bet it would have been tasty
Fantastic recipe... better than the one I grew up with.

Sirloin sandwich steaks were on sale so I used those. Didn’t have hot paprika so I used smoked with a dash of chili powder. Cut the mustard back a bit. Deglazed w chicken stock instead of white wine bc it’s what I had. Turned out great!

Perfect, I made it with beef tenderloin in bigger chunks and then pulled them apart to get rid of the blocky vibe I accidentally ended up with, but still rare, and used creme fraiche and made the egg noodles from scratch...big hit!

As mostly vegetarians, we made this with Beyond Beef’s “Beyond Steak” strips.  They breaded well using 00 flour and tasted very good after frying. We also used sour cream instead of regular cream and used beef broth to supplement the wine. Unfortunately, the pasta we made from scratch soaked up what we thought was enough gravy, making the dish dry.  Next time we will use store-bought pasta and add more cream and/or broth at the end.

THIS is the stroganoff recipe I’ve been looking for!I used a boneless rib eye steak and used the toothed side of a pounder. Sour cream instead of crème fraiche. I’d love to try the nutmeg next time. Terrific flavor!
Horseradish is a MUST

Making tomorrow for bridge girls' dinner party. That picture is really wrong, looks like chicken. The beef and mushrooms need to be well-browned, the fond is the thing. The meat can still be rare (tenderloin) or mid-rare (hanger) but needs a sear.

The ratio of beef to mushrooms is reversed. Half a pound of the thinned sliced sirloin to a pound and a half of sliced crimini mushrooms is perfect. Slice, salt and pepper the beef well in advance. Brown the heck out of the sliced mushrooms in a hot, dry pan until all the juices are given up have evaporated. Quickly brown the beef in a touch of oil in a large, hot nonstick pan. Simmer the beef, uncovered, in two cans of Campbell’s beef consommé (I know! I know! Bear with me—comfort food for the family, not a Michelin star) for 20 minutes. Add the sautéed mushrooms and simmer for another 10 minutes. Slowly stir in most of a 16 oz. tub of full-fat sour cream. Then serve over buttered noodles boiled al dente (Manischewitz brand are pricey but totally worth it).

Beef, mushrooms, sour cream, noodles.

That’s it. No mustard, onions, herbs, or garlic.

You’re welcome.

Nope. Just the basic flavors. Simple. And heavenly.

You won’t be able to stop eating it—serve with crusty (garlic?) bread to y and you’ll be lucky to have leftovers—in the unlikely event you do, store the sauce separately from the noodles).
Follow the recipe!  You will be a hero at the dinner table.  I used beef tenderloin and Bella mushrooms.  This recipe is definitely a keeper  for another glorious meal.

Nearly fainted when I saw the price of of tenderloin yesterday, and even sirloin, so I opted for flank steak as per previous comments, though even that….Used 1/2 c crème fraîche with a glug of milk, because we don’t need that much cream, and a little more wine and a little less butter, because ditto.  And it was lovely.

A chef trick is to "temper" the hvy or sour cream before adding it to the pot.
This is done by placing the dairy in a bowl or measuring cup then ladling some of the sauce into the bowl which raises the temp of the dairy. the "tempered" dairy is then whisked into the sauce without fear of curdling.
 z
It may be sacrilege to some, but left over rib roast works well along with any leftover pan drippings or gravy
Traditional "Russian" herb would be Dill, not Thyme.

I liked this but would cut down on the mustard next time. I used sour cream and the flavors concentrated quicker than I expected, making the mustard stand out more than I intended. Really good base recipe to play with though! Also recommend having a bit of beef broth on hand to loosen up the sauce if needed.

Overall underwhelming. Meat, although seared off as instructed, ends up with a floury gooey surface at the end.  Since there's heavy cream there's really no need for the flour--at least for thickening. Flavor is monolithic.  Will add celery cubes and green peas for interest.
Agreed 100% especially if using tenderloin. if seared properly no need for it!
The recipe description mentions sour cream, but it is not listed as an ingredient. Is it used in place of the heavy cream, or is it an additional item?

Why not pound the whole piece of beef, then cut cross-grain into thin strips? Why the 1/2 in. cut before pounding? What am I missing?
Should we be worried about putting the cooked meat back on the baking sheet it was on when raw--or just wash it in between?
The meat goes back into the pan a second time for further cooking, so I would not expect a problem.
what can be subbed in for the wor sauce and mustard? don't like either. maybe brown sugar? soy sauce?
I feel this recipe had too much pepper, good flavor so I'll make it again, but cut the pepper in 1/2.
Delicious! Just needs more mushrooms for my family.
Made this with beef tenderloin and it was totally worth it. The only thing I’d add to the recipe is to salt and pepper the meat before the dredge. Definitely needed.
Any thoughts on what to sub for mushrooms as the moisture?  Family member has allergy to them.

I lightly salted the pasta water; did the dredge as directed; and ignored the directions to salt the mushrooms and shallots. The end result is very good but definitely edging toward the too salty side.

Sour cream instead of heavy cream (because that's what was still good in the fridge).
The sour cream didn’t curdle, but the Dijon ruined the dish for us.  Stroganoff shouldn’t have zing.  I’ll make it again without Dijon and report back.  Disappointed tonight.
I use Coleman's powdered mustard which is delicious and a better choice over the prepared version as it doesn't have vinegar or wine which would definitely overwhelm the meat.
I serve this with the meat on top of the noodles alongside roasted winter veg, it's a great piece of comfort food
Substituted hot paprika with smoked paprika. Woah... completely changed the character of the dish.
The mushrooms cook down so much I doubled to 1 lb. i ended up added much more salt and worschestshire otherwise it’s a little bland for my taste

I won't cook or eat any dish containing sour cream or cream sauces in geneeral. Does that mean I should not bother making Beef Stroganoff? It sounds so good otherwise. Is there anything I can substitute? TIA.

Then don't. The creamy texture of the sauce in this dish is the main feature, so Stroganoff is not your thing.  

You could make a brown sauce instead, using red wine or brandy to deglaze the pan, then adding a little flour and beef broth for the gravy element. It would be good, it just wouldn't be Stroganoff.

I make this dish often when I have leftover steak. Flank steak works well. My sauce has never curdled, I mix the sour cream, dijon and a bit of chicken stock in a separate container. Then add this mixture to the meat and onions. I don't use mushrooms as they are too strong a flavor for the dish.

Not sure where this recipe went awry but I barely had any liquid in the pan when it was time to add the cream. And I mean barely--I ended up with beef in sour cream. I've been making stroganoff for decades. Never had such a weird result!

I understand your observation.   I made at 3 PM, but could not serve until after 7.  I added a little pasta water at that time to get some "juice".  It did not take away from the creaminess, though.

Note: I used an inexpensive cut of sirloin, and it was REALLY good if prepared as directed.
This was really tasty.  Splurged and used beef tenderloin.  Very tender.  Used crème fraiche and it was perfect.  Yum!

Made this tonight....followed recipe exactly....it is delicious....only one complaint....not enough sauce for the noodles.  Any suggestions how to make more sauce after the creme fraiche has been added?  I pre-made it planning to serve tomorrow night but need more sauce.  I saw note below about how to make more sauce before cream is added - should have followed that advice! Thanks!
Add some of the pasta water to make a more gravy like consistency.
Directions were easy to follow, everything smelled wonderful while cooking and tasted great.  Will keep this one for future use.
Made tonight and although the ingredients are basic, this was a fantastic dish.  Taste, texture, visual.  I will serve this to guests.

Don’t eat red meat so used sliced boneless skinless breasts (against grain)instead and used good quality fino sherry to deglaze. Turned out super. Recommend going with creme fraiche, it’s lovely, kind of melts.

I made this as directed, but swapped out the beef for Quorn cubes (adding a little shoyu and to thebpan while cooking them). It needed a little extra cream and came out a bit spicy for my kids' taste but was otherwise delicious. Next time I'll try using a different veggie protein and see what happens (my wife's a vegetarian).

Loved this. Full of flavor, easy to make. I used tenderloin ( because I was indeed feeling fancy), dry vermouth instead of wine, regular paprika instead of hot, and half and half instead of creme fraiche. I like mine a little more saucy, so I increased all of the liquids during cooking and even added a little pasta water at the end.
This is superb.  I used beef sirloin tips. I didn't have hot paprika so I used a smoky Spanish paprika with great results.

I don't know why one has to put the floured raw beef on a rimmed baking sheet (instead of a piece of waxed paper or a plate).  And I certainly wouldn't return the same now cooked meat to a surface on which it had been placed in a raw state.  ??
Fabulous recipe. I used filet and light cream. Thinned the sauce with a bit of water. Outstanding.  5 stars for me !!

**veggie version** (yes I know this is a 'different recipe', if you feel you must add that comment pls do) - I used 'beyond meat' (fake ground beef), fried it with the paprika, S&P, then at the end added the flour and cooked for 2 mins - did not bake it. 

Did everything else for the mushroom/shallot sauce exactly as printed. I did add 2 cups of water as it was a bit too thick...

I used basmati rice instead of egg noodles.
It was supremely delicious & we named it 'Beyond Stroganoff'
Delicious, worth the suspension of disbelief to try.  The mustard works very well here.  Even better heated up next day, let the noodles get semi-crisp.

Double sauce. 
Pound meat thinner.

I just cooked this following the directions exactly and I enjoyed it so much, I am tossing my old, heavy, sour cream & marsala stroganoff recipe that I've been using for more than 25 years.  This new one is a keeper! The white wine and heavy cream is a fine  combo that made for a silky sauce, no thickeners needed, and the Hungarian hot paprika added a nice kick. Excellent!

Too much cream, not enough seasoning, and not enough cooking time to tenderize the meat. I added about twice as much mustard and worcestershire sauce as well as more salt. It would also benefit from more onions or shallots, and more mushrooms.
Grew up on this dish & love it. I recently started using teres major. It’s a much more affordable substitute for the tenderloin.

This was sooooo good. I used a sirloin steak for the meat, and the Creme Fraiche. The meat came out so tender and succulent. Full of delicious flavor. Way better than any other beef stroganoff I've ever had!

I made the following adjustments - mostly caramelized an onion before adding the mushrooms, used red wine instead of white, homemade mustard, didn't bother measuring the Worcestershire sauce or mustard and added a few herbs as the wine cooked down - my husband loved it!!

Anchovy paste instead of mushrooms

1/2 cup of white wine

I made this as per directions and it was fabulous. For me, the Hungarian hot paprika is a delightful discovery and makes the dish anything but reminiscent of a 1950’s casserole

Made this last night. Skipped the pounding part of the steak strips and just cut thin strips from a semi frozen couple of tenderloin tips. Tossed the steak strips in a plastic bag with the flour. 

Followed the rest of the recipe. It was delicious but next time I’ll probably leave out the hot paprika. Liked this recipe better than I one I usually use.

I can’t wait to have this again! Used beef tenderloin. I haven’t used hot Hungarian paprika before, so I used a half teaspoon of it; teaspoon of regular. I made double the sauce. Just a bit of prep time and it came together beautifully.
oh to dine upon this at the Russian Tea Room. At least in my memories it was edible. In reality, it was far from.

This was good, with exactly the right amount of heat. An accurate representation of the dish with bold, savory flavor. A solid weekday standby.
My mom made this for company in the 60's.  It was a big hit.
Absolutely brilliant.  Used 3 entrecote steaks; a tin of mushrooms left from our Atlantic crossing and cream.  Also added half a bunch of fresh spinach at the end.  Fabulous.
I made this.. actually a melange of two NYT recipes two nights ago. No creme frache or sour cream but full fat yogurt worked well
Insanely good.  My advice: don't buy tenderloin.  Ask your butcher for a good part that is more economical.  It will be just as good.
I made a vegetarian version of this with shiitake and button mushrooms. Used sour cream rather than crème fraîche, veg stock instead and dry sherry instead of white wine. Insanely good.

I suggest you start with a smaller amount of hot paprika in the flour mixture and adjust upward to taste before deglazing.  I like spicy, but the full amount was just a little too hot for me, particularly while sautéing the meat.  This recipe (doubled) is in Sam’s new cookbook and 1Tbsp really wafts upward.

I made this last night, with a few changes out of necessity. No Worcestershire sauce, a combo of 3:1 sweet to smoky FRESH paprika, one shallot and 1/4 yellow onion, a combo of heavy cream and plain yogurt. Served over egg noodles with a sprinkle of chopped fresh parsley. Definitely a keeper!
We used sour cream and didn't add any meat.  Yum!
leftover leg of lamb replaced the beef, everything pretty much followed. hot and smokey paprika really made this delicious
This is a dish my family always made growing up. My mother and grandmother taught me to use sour cream at the end not heavy whipping cream. Adds more flavor.

for shallots sub yellow onion more finely chopped.

As someone one who’s raised beef for many years, sure you can substitute tri-tip ( or many other cuts). Just be aware of the unique characteristics of what you propose to use, and adjust prep and cooking accordingly.

My experience: 3 to 5 minutes at med high will give you charred, well done beef. Oops. Reduce heat and time. Same for mushroom/shallot time: easily overcooked. Cut salt and pepper to 1/2 tsp (one third less). Heavy cream subbed for usual sour cream: YES!

This is one of my all time favorite recipes. I rate my recipes with this question: "if I were to order this at a restaurant would I order it again?" Not only would I order it again, it would be the only thing I would order at this restaurant; I would go back just for this!!

Because the sauce is the best part, we doubled the sauce but kept the meat portion at 1.5 pounds of beef tenderloin.

We ate a lot and it warmed us up on a gloomy day.

I used tri-tip steak,  1 small box each of cremini and shitakke mushrooms. Dressed the noodles with butter and lemon zest. Used sherry to deglaze and added 3/4 cup of beef broth after that for additional gravy. Went with Creme Fraiche but only used 1/2 cupAbsolutely delicious. .

Definitely try with sour cream instead of heavy cream-needs that tang.  Otherwise good, but explore different beef cuts and make pieces bite sized.

After reading many notes I decided to make as Sam recommended. Would modify if displeased the next time. No worries! This is perfect just as written. It was an instant winner and enjoyed by grandparents and grandchildren alike! Thank you Sam!

Made several times with filet, twice with creme fraiche & once with heavy cream...both excellent!

1: Slices of dill gherkins. 
2. Cooked slice Beets.
One of my favourite restaurants in Hamburg serves the beet version, and most of Germany will use gherkins.
Any way to make this without the cream/sour cream?
Mom made Craig Claiborne's version, and so have I. I think I'll try this one.
Foe a veggie option...use different types of mushrooms or just double up. Use Cashew or other nut yogurt in place of the dairy...works great!
Allrecipes has Chef John’s easy Creme Fraiche recipe.  Cream and buttermilk are the ingredients.

This one always pops up and makes me crave it.  This is a GREAT recipe.  Almost too rich if you use tenderloin...Next time I will grab the roast.  Next time I want to try the Daisy sourcreme as suggested.  Creme Fraiche was expensive.  I like that it has white wine and saves a trip to the liquor store!
Don’t like mushrooms. Any suggestions for a substitution? Thanks

I love beef stroganoff, but we have a lactose intolerant member of the family so I’ve never made it as an adult.  Any recommendations for a substitution for the cream?  I can use plant based “butter” for the noodles.

Add 1.5 tsp Worcestershire sauce a
And 1.5 tsp Dijon 
1.5 tsp paprika
I always used fresh dill and taragon.
I make stroganoff as a way to use up leftover steak. Slice up the leftovers, make the mushroom sauce add the meat and sour cream at the end and serve over buttered noodles.
Leftover steak is an oxymoron.
The dish I always asked my mother to make for my birthday dinner - 50 or so years ago.  I am happy to be reminded.  And, my birthday is coming up.

Oh dear!  I love Sam, and I have fond memories of Stroganoff. I had everything the recipe called for and I was so excited when it popped up in my e-mail.  Bummer.  I made it exactly per instructions, with nice steak, and it was just tasteless.  And all those calories, too!  I think the whipping cream was a mistake - creme fraiche or the traditional sour cream would have been better.
Did anyone else's mom use ketchup in the sauce? I remember an orange-colored sauce (1970s).

Took in so many of the recommendations from the notes, but basically this recipe is very good. Used left over rump roast, added when beef is added near the end. Yes low fat Greek yogurt and some sour cream to replace creme freche. Was fabulous as was the addition of sherry vs. wine, nutmeg, fresh thyme, and good fresh pepper. Great for the use of leftover beef.

Cut the meat small for MIL
New York steak
3/4 cup heavy cream and 1/4 c Daisy sour cream 
Extra shallots and mushrooms

Until now, Craig Claiborne's recipe for Stroganoff in the original NYT Cookbook was my one and only, but Sam's version must be added to my game.  As written, using tri-tip and Pimenton de la Vera. No creme fraiche at the market, so used Daisy sour cream per commenter.  It didn't curdle, but gave a thicker than desired sauce.  Thinned the leftovers with a bit of BTB vegetable broth, yummy. Even better than Mother's!
I add sliced cornichons - they add a lovely crunch and nice contrast.

Well, just out of solidarity with all those other cooks who read but don’t really read a recipe until they get going...reader, I bought stewing beef. I know! Rookie mistake. But still worked beautifully by adding stock and slow cooking the beef after the deglaze, Worcestershire etc. Then frying up the mushrooms separately and adding cream to meat at the end.
Just made this for supper tonight, using venison backstrap instead of beef. It was the best stroganoff I've ever had, hands down. Thanks Sam!

Substituted 2 zucchinis for the mushrooms.  Sliced, then let them dry a little between paper towels while prepping everything else, and it worked out great.  Also ran out of paprika, so added a little cayenne and hot chili powder for a nice extra kick.
Dill and tarragon

Or, sauté beef in an instapot. Save noodles & sour cream & subst. an 8 oz can mushr. soup for flour & + 1/2 tsp curry pdr, for colour, put ingredients, including raw mushrooms & shallots in & use slow cooker mode. Go nordic skiing.  Back from the piste, boil the noodles. Garnish with sour cream (if you must add it the cornstarch suggestion is a good hint) & chopped parsley,  I prefer mustard seeds in the same prop as prep. mustard. Healthier (the skiing part) & faster to prep and cleanup.

OMG -- this is outstanding!  Comes together easily, smells wonderful and oh -- tastes great!  Substituted beef broth for the wine & used your reader's tip of corn starch to prevent curdling, whisked the sour cream with 1 tbsp whipping cream. Up here in the True North, we have a new product in the sour cream department that boasts 18% butter fat! Very rich, but so worth it for a special dinner. Memories of Russian Tea Room!! We're hooked-and thank you so much for this tasty treat.  MORE, PLEASE!

Love making a version of this low carb. Instead of pasta noodles, I slice cabbage VERY thin. Sauté the cabbage in a little butter — healthy cabbage noodles with the stroganoff over top.  Delicious alternative.
A wonderful recipe. My neighbor gave me fresh venison, so for tonight’s dinner I swapped out venison for beef. Beef or venison, either way this is a keeper. A hearty winter dinner.

Liked what Mike said- add cornstarch to the sour cream- even though there is no sour cream called for in this recipe. I have been making beef stroganoff for over 50 years (yes, I am that old)- I learned it from my Mom(a Russian Jew) she used a stew meat (cheapest meat)and sour cream to make the sauce and no wine ( we never had wine in the house- my parents were not drinkers). Although I don't follow a recipe- I make it basically the same as this recipe with above mentioned changes!

This made for a delicious and comforting dinner after shoveling 10 inches of fresh snow today. I used Smoked Paprika but followed the rest of the recipe as written. Prep was not too difficult, definitely will make this dish again.

I made this last night following the recipe except for substituting sour cream for the heavy cream.  The flavors are delicious.....the Dijon and paprika really add a nice touch.  Next time I will double the mushrooms because they do shrink down.  My husband requested more of that delicious sauce so I will also add more liquid.....maybe some beef stock and a little more sour cream.
I recently used hanger steak for stroganoff.  Very tender and flavorful.

Excellent! Loved the flavor rendered out of the carmelized and mushrooms. I deglazed with red wine instead. The steak was tender and it came together quickly. Might add more Vermont Creamery creme fraiche  and mushrooms next time. Be sure to salt throughout.
