I made this for dinner last night following the recipe exactly with the small change of deglazing the pan with white wine right before adding the shallots. Maybe the mushrooms didn't have enough moisture. This dish is a delight and will be regular favorite in our household.
I like to add sweet vermouth in place of the white wine, adds an herbal note to the sauce, which I find delicious.
Followed the recipe and next time I will substitute olive oil for some of the butter.
Absolutely delicious! I will double next time, between me and two hungry males there were no leftovers.
Completely perfect as is. Wow. Wow wow wow.
Went a little easier on the mustard. Because my mom's meat slices were about 2"  in length, I just had to do it that way. Great recipe!
Outstanding!

I only had a roast, which I slow cooked.  Since the beef was slow cooking, I floured the mushrooms and shallot mix and cooked per instructions.  I then shredded the meat and used the juices instead of wine to deglaze.  I poured the cream mix in per instructions and added the mushroom/beef mix.  I did add a bit more of the beef juices to the mix to thin it out a little.  It was fantastic.

This is very good particularly if you use a good cut of meat. It was a bit peppery which we found fine, but some who don't like "spicy food" might say this is too spicy. If you want to cut something, I would cut some of the black pepper, not the paprika. FYI - while most NYT recipes that claim to be enough for 4 or more like enough for 3, this one really is two meals for two people.

I mostly followed the recipe.  Had fresh grass fed tenderloin which really helped.  Had creme fraiche which worked well.  Substituted cayenne for spicy paprika, and half a sweet onion for the shallots - and put the onion in the pan to start cooking before the mushrooms.  It turned out with a spicy kick that probably would have been too much for guests but my husband and I - and even our 12 year olds foodie - thought it was one of the best things I’ve ever cooked.  Yum!

It's safe to say that 4/6-7 recipes I make each week are from the Times. Most earn a 4-5 star rating. This is the worst recipe I've ever tried and, in general, I've come to conclude that I cannot rely on Sam Sifton recipes.  Cooking mushrooms for 12-15 minutes??? Obviously, I didn't do that or they would have been completely dried out but the entire recipe begged for sour cream -- or something more.  I threw away $30 of beef tenderloin because this recipe was not edible.
The recipe calls for crème fraîche; why would you say it needs sour cream?  It’s no more tangy.

This was a great recipe. But I wish that the recipes would have more specific ingredients. Such as the mushrooms I could’ve been told that they were white mushrooms or that I could use bell mushrooms that would’ve been helpful and maybe the type of pans I need it.

So yummy!! Made with regular paprika as I was afraid hot or smoked paprika would overpower the beef.  I used regular heavy cream as suggested in the recipe and it worked really well. Also, I highly suggest using beef top sirloin. It was so tender and perfect for this dish!
Delicious. Used sirloin and heavy cream, followed recipe as is.

Adapted to make it dairy-free: Use 14oz of dairy-free sour cream, about .5 cup of oat milk, make a slurry of flour to thicken if necessary.
Adapted to make it vegan: Just lots of mushrooms and onions, plus the dairy-free substitutions above :)
Used hot paprika as added i tsp Bubbies horseradish along with mustard and Worcestershire.  Could add a little bit more salt to flour for dredging.  Parsley adds a lot to final presentation/flavor

Hey! I’ve made mannnny versions of this recipe over the years of family cooking. From hard times (mushroom gravy mix, a bit of onion and a dollop of sour cream) to better and best times. I really like this recipe although I always have to add mustard to recipes’on the sly’ because my husband thinks it will ruin it. 

I need to ask tho, why am I putting the beef on a baking sheet? I searched and even asked for a second opinion! Just asking.

This was a big win. Develops a luscious sauce. Subbed soy sauce for worcestershire and baby bella for buttons. Also used a little extra mustard. Didn't have hot paprika so used a little cayenne along with smoked paprika. 

Had beef tenderloin tips from Farm Foods box that I wasn't sure what I wanted to do with & this was perfect.

I doubled the butter while preparing the mushrooms and shallots and also doubled the wine during deglazing. A little more complicated than I prefer on a typical weeknight after work, but totally worth it. Entire family loved it. This is definitely going in our regular rotation.

Absolutely fabulous. I did use half heavy cream and half 1/2 and 1/2 and eliminated the butter from the noodles. Seemed like overkill. Cutting the meat slices a bit larger also helped to not overcook it.

I let the mushrooms cool for a minute or two and then tempered the creme fraiche with some of them. I added the mixture back to a cooled pan and brought the heat slowly up. The cream didn’t separate. 

I used 24 oz of mushrooms because I love them. :)

Made this using sour cream, which did curdle but is more traditional; didn’t have hot paprika, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. The sirloin worked fine. It probably didn’t need to be pounded. The result was delicious, but it lacked sauce. Next time I will double the liquid and keep some broth on hand for last-minute adjustments. And will add sour cream at the very end and just heat through.

I think next time it can be streamlined. No need for the cookie sheet.

Substituted plain Greek yogurt for the cream and was very generous with the mustard and Worcestershire sauce.  Used flank steak.  Everyone loved it! 
 Will definitely make again but would increase the cream (or yogurt) quantity.

I’ve made this as written before and it’s delicious. I was craving it, but with a toddler and a baby in the house, I decided to simplify.

I popped all beef frozen meatballs in the oven that needed 30 minutes to cook. I prepped the sauce ingredients and followed the recipe starting with step 5. 

The toddler got some buttered noodles and plain meatballs dipped in ketchup. My husband and I got the beef stroganoff that I was craving in just over 30 minutes.

I used a quart and half a pint, drained. Also added some beef broth right before I added the cream. 

Next time, I will either half the venison or double everything else.
Anyone have ideas for making this gluten free? Almond or rice flour and xanthum gum perhaps? Or just the flour?
Used part paprika and part smoked paprika with a dash of cayenne as I didn't have hot paprika. Delicious...added a little water at the end to have a little more sauce.
Slice the mushrooms and onions up really thin
This recipe is really good! One of my husbands favorites. We once tried it with venison instead of beef and it made it even better!

This was fantastic and exactly what I was looking for in recreating the one dish my mom mastered in her life (although hers was 1980s powdered packet mix-based).
I used thin-sliced NY (shabu shabu), quickly seared in cast iron to get some crispy edges, homemade papardelle, sherry for the wine, and more sour cream in line with my memory of this dish.  Thinned my extra creamy sauce with some Kettle&Fire beef bone broth, which made it even more magical.    Homemade sourdough, steamed broccoli=BLISS
I tried with NutPods for cream, apple cider for wine, fennel for shallots, vegan butter, tri tip. Nice German harvest twist on an old favorite.
My grandmother added diced dill pickles at the end -- I recently started doing this, and it adds a wonderful crunch and dilly acidity to the dish.

Sirloin, creme fraiche, egg pappardelle, 1/2 teaspoon of cayenne and 1 teaspoon of reg paprika - deeeelicious! And my husband, who was hesitant - negative association growing up - went in for seconds. Will definitely make again - cozy comfort food.

I don’t cook beef often, but love this dish. The difficulty in it for me was always that my pan-seared beef turned out tough, but I recently discovered the secret to tender meat: a 15 minute soak in baking soda brine. Much cheaper than buying tenderloin!
In much of the former Soviet Union, the stroganoff is served over French fries  rather than noodles.  I highly recommend it!

My grandmother learned this from a Hungarian family and they add caraway seeds. I add a little lemon rind and I substitute Greek yogurt for the sour cream to give it extra tang. My grandmother would make bread dumplings, but we use noodles or even rice.

Hands down, best stroganoff recipe I ever made!  Didn’t have any beef in the house, so I used skinless chicken thighs and it was wonderful. No problem with the sour cream separating. Will make this often!

Used this recipe to make stroganoff for the first time last night. Was worried as the flour mixture got a little thin towards the end of my beef tenderloin. Regardless, I was told by guest it was the best beef stroganoff they'd had. Very pleased with the simplicity of the dish - will revisit!
Artery hardening should be more pleasurable than this.  The meat was tasty, but the sauce was boring and heavy.
Great dish, cooked as described. Will do again.
shiitakes, heavy cream, dill

I remember when I used to make the old Bon Appetit recipe using Liptons Onion Soup and Campbells Cream of Mushroom Soup mixed in with sliced top round.  After 45 minutes you’d stir in a carton of sour cream.  This was back in the early 80’s.  Looking forward to trying this one!  All you really need to do is greet your guests with their favorite cocktail at the door and who cares what you put in the stroganoff!
Made with left-over tenderloin ends and lots of mushrooms, garnished with the fresh dill I had on hand. Will definitely make again and again.

A kitchen „standard“ in our household. But what perplexes me: where are the gherkins? In Germany EVERY Boeuf Stroganoff recipe has gherkins (thinly sliced and finely cut) in it. Is this only a German thing?

Used four tablespoons of yellow onion, out of shallotts. Used Daisy sour cream, as out of crepe fresh or heavy cream.  Otherwise, I followedpmade a Hamburg stroganoff eons ago, and it is similar to Craig Clairbo4n recipe on the site, Hamburg meat, with stock or bullion, And sour cream.  It is really wonderful.  

Sam Sifton recipe with very minor changes was disappointing.  Very dry, low beef flavor and bori.  Without the beef bullion, etc., it was not gravy like, but flav
You can't blame the recipe for tasteless beef. Talk to your butcher.

Heresy I know, but in keeping with Mr. Clayburn’s recommendation to use the best quality beef your budget allows…my mother made this with ground beef during the graduate school poverty years in the 50’s. Loved it then…love it now!

I made this recipe vegetarian by substituting seitan for beef and using vegan Worcestershire sauce. I supplemented an 8 oz. package of seitan (chunks, though strips would be ideal) with slices of one large portobello and a handful of shiitakes. The dish turned out beautifully, boasting all the meaty, umami goodness of the beef stroganoff I remember so fondly from my omnivorous childhood. I also used smoked paprika, which seemed to deepen all the flavors.

This was definitely delicious!  I used two pounds of impossible meat  and 16 ounces of noodles- incredible!  I didn’t have hot paprika but did have smoked paprika and it was sooooo good!  Next time I think I’ll add bit more paprika or splurge and get the hot paprika. My wife said it was a unique taste but loved it too.

Very disappointing. I don't blame the recipe per se; but the quality of beef, cream, and some other items is painful to try to substitute.  The beef was not very 'beefy', the cream (Alaska brand) was too thick by far, and even a bit of wine and pasta water couldn't give the sauce much creaminess.
Love this recipe.   And yes, we loved it when my mother served it in the fifties. Our granddaughters love it now.   My mother added dill at the end which i still do along with parsley!
Great for leftover steak too - just skipped the steps that involves cooking the steak and added Smoked Paprika to onions and mushrooms. Delicious!
Blasphemy, perhaps, but I gotta tell you, this is amazing with ground beef. If you're pinching pennies, using GB lets you splurge on the cream and other good things.
I accidentally used smoked paprika and the result was very tasty
This version of beef stroganoff turned out to be rather boring.  It's edible, but nothing beyond that.

Update

I use 1 lb of meat for the recipe and 2 lb to double everything else. Disregard previous note on 2lb recipe.
Perfect ax described

This is a great recipe. The sauce is wonderful. The amounts, however, seem a bit much. Instead of 1.5 lb. of beef, we used 8 oz. of bison medallions. That was plenty for four. Another substitution: no hot paprika on hand so added 1/8 tsp. of cayenne. We opted for creme fraiche which mixed in beautifully with no curdling or other issues. Delightful. p.s. Harpswell is around the corner ;-).

I thought this was very good. Would have been better if I’d added some broth to thin out the sauce. I used 1/4 cup heavy cream and 3/4 daisy sour cream. It was too thick and dry. Taste was great though . Have enough for leftovers so will make some adjustments for tomorrow. Used top sirloin which the butcher cut into strips for me and saved a ton of work.

I have been craving Beef Stroganoff for a while and decided to try this recipe. It was a lot of work and it created a tremendous amount of bowls, plates, pots and pans. The end result was disappointing and my small kitchen is a mess even though I washed dishes as I cooked. I won't make this again. Followed the recipe exactly. Not happy after considering the cost of beef which will probably go out to the trashcan. My easy going husband who usually loves my cooking was kind but sad with the result
I just discovered this recipe and I made it as written except I used some dry sherry in place of the wine which I didn't have.  It came out great-definitely going to make this again!
It was easy to follow, easy to cook, my family loved it. I will repeat it.
This was very good but I prefer not adding the mustard.
would chuck work for this?
Made 4/22/23 with Emily with filet mignon and it was SO GOOD. Egg noodles a must. Used creme fraiche.
I have made beef stroganoff before and ground beef is way wayyyyy better.
Hosting a 70s party next weekend with 5 couples. Is there any way to make this dish in advance so I’m not prepping it while people are here?

I like this recipe; it's different than my usual beef stock/tomato paste/worcestershire/sour cream. Milder, creamier.  I used Sherry instead of wine because I prefer the taste; heavy cream, and added a spoonful of Greek yogurt at the end for the tang, some fresh thyme bc I had it.  Everyone including tweens ate it up.
Used Mexican crema, has the cultured taste of sour cream and doesn’t split.
I was feeling fancy and used beef tenderloin. This dish is excellent.
We’ve made this dish a few times. This last time, we had only pork on hand and this dish is very good with either beef or pork.

My husband and I really enjoyed this and stroganoff is one of his favorite meals. The heat level is higher than other recipes I’ve tried but perfect for our tastes. It’s a bit on the salty side; I’ll reduce it to 1 tsp. the next time I make it.

This is a really good and fairly simple dish.  I don't think paprika added much in the way of flavor. I added minced garlic and fresh thyme to the mushroom sautée just prior to reducing it with the wine. I could use a bit more Worchestire and Dijon as well.  I think next time will use sour creme instead of the heavy cream for a little more punch.  Overall, good staple and should make the dinner rotation!

Not enough dredge, too much meat, overall a lovely flavor overall, but this took two people to execute in a 90-minute timeframe. So back to Beard and Bittman, I go. Only switch I made was using Daisy sour cream instead of crême fraich. Sue me! Still, dry and if even flavorful, no way can you use high heat and expect medium rare if you have a prosumer stove. Lots to adjust with this recipe, methinks.

I had to substitute yogurt as I could not find any crème fraîche in Greece but it worked great! One to one and full fat Greek yogurt. I would maybe have added English mustard instead of dijon to make it a bit more tangy.

I used tri-tip for this and it came out tender and flavorful. I forgot to get sour cream, but I had a little nonfat Greek yogurt. I was afraid of having it curdle so I added 1 cup of beef broth to finish cooking and turned off the heat, then stirred in the yogurt. I will definitely make this again and follow the recipe, but the alterations worked out and this absolutely satisfies a craving on a chilly, rainy night! With steamed broccoli it brought me right back to my childhood
Delicious!  Only change was to use a mix of wild mushrooms. Exquisite! Everything else was as written!

Where’s the brandy? 3 tablespoons added once beef nearly  cooked( for serving 4) is a must ! I use tbsp paprika?! 
I’m going to try crème fraîche ( instead of natural yoghurt which is what I normally use) next time.
Kind of bland. Need more pepper
When I make this I always use way more paprika. Does anyone else only use 1.5? Teaspoons.   I use at least a tablespoon.

I did not love this recipe. The sauce tasted okay. I made it all as directed. I prefer less mustard and would add some brandy to deglaze next time. I used sirloin which wasn’t as good as filet. I’d also use pappadelle instead of egg noodle.

I have a piece of skirt steak.  I'm going to try velveting it before I make this dish.
A very clever chef in a small cafe would make hamburger stroganoff by clumping ground beef.

Made this with the boneless sirloin I had on hand. Not great. After following the recipe the steak was too tough ( and it was a good cut of meat) and I had to add broth and simmer this for an hour. Of course that affected the sauce, so I had to doctor it with sour cream. All in all, I’ll go back to my old recipe.

I used ribeye for the meat, heavy whipping cream and a reduced amount of cayenne instead of paprika. I didn’t have Worcestershire either so I used some balsamic, Kitchen Bouquet and cooking sherry instead. It came out perfectly!  Next time I’m going to put the flour mixture in a freezer bag and coat the meat by shaking it in the bag.  I used about a pound of meat and would have liked more sauce. I cooked all of the meat at once in a large cast iron pan and then just piled it on a plate while the mushrooms cooked.  Nothing stuck together. Will definitely make again.
Also I wouldn't add flour to my beef making it again. It made my meat gummy and didn't really make a gravy like I expected.
This dish was very greasy- too much butter. I don’t think I’ll use this recipe again.

I prefer my mother's recipe. I use less fatty round steak, cut as described here, dredge it and saute it until it is appetizingly brown, follow the steps here, but skip mustard and hot paprika. We don't use wine or Worcestershire, but those would be good. I serve it on noodles with sliced beets and a salad on the side. I've never tried heavy cream or creme fraiche, but that sounds good. We use sour cream and/or Greek yogurt, taking care not to let it curdle.

I know everyone will think I am Nuts ,but in my Long life ,I don't remember ever having Beef Stroganoff ... Ever.  Sure would like to try it - if even only once.
I did save this recipe - looks like a good one.  Who knows ,maybe someday ?

Such amazing flavor! Instead of the sirloin/tenderloin, I subbed Mark Bittman’s drop meatballs made with American Wagyu ground beef, and used baby Portobello mushrooms instead of button. My whole family enjoyed this, including my 2 picky kids.
Made this with white wine vinegar instead & it was wonderful
The Joy of Cooking recipe is very similar to Sam's. Calls for sour cream. I like the tang.
I used the suggestions in the comments re: daisy sour cream, etc. and it's a 5 for me.
half the time cooking the meat.  Double the cream sauce.
Excellent. 10/10 would make again I made just as recipe reads. We used sirloin with picaña.

I got my recipe from the galloping gourmet cook book from the 70's.   Easy and my family loves it.  I mix in sour cream at the very end so it does not get funny.  I made extra meat n mushrooms and froze so it is ready to go the next time they ask.  Great for a working mom.

Jayne, I remember Graham Kerr, "The Galloping Gormet" from the 1970's.  I used to watch his shows with great admiration when I was a boy growing up in the UK.  (Look after your copy, it might be very valuable one day!)
John - New York
This recipe ends up being very acidic. I made a half portion and am considering adding more sour cream or crème fraiche and a bit of sugar to balance the acid
Ketchup instead of Worcestershire sauce gives it a deeper flavor and texture. Also, fully endorse using Daisy Sour Cream instead of crème fraiche.

I cooked a rather large teres major cut and used thinly sliced steak. No noodles but used farfalle instead and used dried thyme. I’ll try it again with the original ingredients but the porcini powder that’s calling my name might be useful in the bext time.
I used leftover cooked prime rib and sour cream but found it very bland. Adding oyster sauce helped a lot. I think it needs beef juices of some kind to be tastier.

I know that using very tender beef and cooking it fast is the authentic way to make stroganoff, but after making this version I realize that I prefer it with the beef braised low and slow.  For some reason this recipe didn’t have a lot of flavor.
Loved this recipe although it was a bit dry - try adding sherry (2 T) to mixture to loosen it up.  GREAT spice flavor!

Got rave reviews from all of us, including someone who raved that it was a big thing at dinner parties in the ‘70s and this was exactly as she remembered (complimentary.)

It all took longer than suggested in the recipe, but I’m no professional. Great directions, excellent results.

Next time I’ll use a blend of smoked and hot paprika. The full hot paprika was overpowering. I’ll also reduce the salt slightly. I also cooked the meat in 4 batches … maybe mine was cut too thin but I didn’t want to crowd the pan so I did little by little and flipped each piece one by one to ensure even searing.
Also I will use a combo of crème fraiche and heavy cream next time to loosen the sauce up a little
Easy and quite tasty.

SwissLauren, I use Heavy Cream alone and it's utterly fabulous.  (My wife is largely Vegetarian but she adores this dish)
John - New York
This is the real deal. Rare meat and crème fraiche. Yum!
Excellent meal. Made it exactly like the recipe.
Very comforting! (and iron rich!) Does anyone have any tips on freezing? I don’t think I will be able to finish this in a timely manner

Less than four tablespoons total of dredge for 1.5lbs of high surface area meat was very much not enough.  I would recommend increasing it by a factor of four or more.  You might find the amount of seasoning similarly sparse.
So delicious! I doubled the recipe and served to a small crowd. I also substituted half a cup of cream for sour cream, and threw in some extra mushrooms. Everyone loved it.
If the beef is fatty, don't use as much flour.
Subbed yellow onions for the shallots and used whipping cream. Absolutely delicious

Meh.  It's heavy.  Needs double the mushrooms and double the people to eat it, plus a light salad with lots of acidity, and sorbet for dessert.  Won't make it again.
FWIW, the heavy cream didn't curdle.
Our family loved this recipe.  Yes, I tinkered a bit. I added 1LB of mushrooms, some fresh nutmeg, 1 tsp cumin, and a splash of tabasco.
The most boring stroganoff ever. Needs some beef broth or something. I would never make this again.

title: Chinese Roast Pork on Garlic Bread Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: The rumors that Herbie's was "first" are just not true. Singer's in Liberty (my family's restaurant) served this sandwich long before. For the garlic bread, never butter. We always had minced garlic in oil ready to go. Spread on the "club" bread, under the broiler for maybe a minute. Use bigger pieces of pork, we always used char siu butt, sliced thin by machine, then dipped in master sauce. Sauteed onions. Duck sauce and mustard were always on the table (or in packets, which are gross now).

@jeff goldner
 Hi Jeff- my family had Eddies Antique Jewelers across the street! we loved the mix of Jewish deli and Chinese foods. Alma the waitress was always there(in fact we visited her at the local nursing home where my grandmother ended up!)And the Boston cream Pie!- the standard for all others my whole life! thank you for having such a wonderful restaurant. Singers & Katz's bakery fed us very well all the years I grew up in South Fallsburg while my mother worked at her parents shop.
I love this story.  We need to revitalize the Catskills.
Katz's in Liberty? I was a counselor at Camp Wawayanda for a few years, in the Forstmann State Something Something, and we'd go into town on day's off and get their jelly doughnuts. Huh.
One thing I always do when using marinades is that I cook the marinade on the stove until I have cooked out any bacteria from the raw pork.  Then, I use it to baste.  Be safe folks!

Delicious recipe! I agree with Adele's comment. I make a master batch of roasted garlic butter almost every week. Here's how:
Roast 2 bulbs garlic (I know you know how). Let cool. Squeeze them out in a small bowl and mash them with whatever masher you have.
Add 1 stick soft butter (1/2 cup, 4oz.) and mix it up.
Salt and pepper to taste.
Store in a tightly sealed container in your fridge.
Then use as is, or add Italian seasoning, or any other flavors you want to add in any quantity you need.

I found that using raw garlic can leave a lingering and unpleasant after taste. If you use roasted garlic, you still get the taste of garlic but also a better consistency for spreading it over the bread.

without doubt, the sandwich that evokes every Brooklyn kid's childhood.  Rather than the apricot jam (which, yes, is delicious) hit your local Chinese Restaurant and ask for a  half pint of their fresh duck sauce.  H E A V E N.

This Sifton story and recipe are wonderful.  When I saw, "Catskills" I was confused   The comments from Jewish writers made me laugh.  I have seen Jews eat pork at Chinese restaurants.  When I said something, they would put a finger to their lips and shush me.

The Jews are perhaps the most successful, literate, erudite tribe of people.  If one of them has a pork sandwich once in a while in modern times, it's not a big transgression.   I think it a nice slice of Americana.
Most people don't believe me when I tell them about this sandwich. It was on every diner menu on Long Island, which includes Brooklyn and Queens as well as Nassau and Suffolk. It was my favorite!
Rather than the apricot jam and vinegar mixture I used bottled Thai sweet chili sauce and it gave a wonderful kick to the sandwich.

I thought Roast Pork on Garlic bread was invented by Pop-Ins in South Fallsburg, where I grew up! It was always on the menu there throughout the 50/60's.
 Thanks for the memories!

Why would I break the pork down into 1in x 4in strips? Doesn't it seem like i'm making the post cook slicing more difficult? Why not into bigger, sturdier pieces? Is it to create more surface area for the marinade and caramelization?
Yes. It's to increase the surface area

This recipe's vague temperature guide to "turn your oven all the way up" probably did not contemplate my situation (Wolf oven, 550 degrees).  It's a good thing I don't mind carbon with my meat.  Ditto with the "toasted" roll.  You'll want to monitor things more carefully than I did.

This was a fun sandwich to prepare and eat after reading all the posted recollections.  I used half the five spice as I’m not a big fan.  I left meat in marinate overnight.  Made garlic butter by baking garlic first as recommended. Thankful I lined my baking sheet with foil.  I toasted ciabatta rolls which made great base.  Next time I will definitely try simply using country style ribs. Would be easier to have the meat already cut up.

Me and my wife made this for a sunday night meal. 
One of the best sandwiches I've ever had! The sweetness, tartness, butter, and other favors creates an amazing flavor that I'll never forget. Five stars all the way.

Made this twice and it’s delicious. Can even freeze cooked pork. Second time used country style ribs which is more convenient than cutting up a roast. 450-480 on temp and less time. No one has mentioned this but 1 jar of fermented tofu gives plenty of flavor.

Thanks for the tip about using country-style ribs.

Pretty sure Sam means 2 cubes of jarred red fermented bean curd, not 2 jars of cubes. My jar of "chili oil beancurd" weighs over 8 oz.--adding the entire jar would alter the flavor of the roasted pork drastically (and not in a pleasant way).  Maybe your jar is smaller?
Didn't make the sandwich, but did make the roast pork for an Asian dish I was making, and it was perfect! Will definitely use this again.
Would Saucy Susan duck sauce work?  In the 50s my mother used it on everything.  Does it still exist?  I make a pork tenderloin with a gussie up char sui glaze. Delish
It does indeed still exist, but I have to tell you, I bought a bottle in a fit of nostalgia and....and there are some things that should stay in the past.
Has anyone used pork tenderloin for this?

I used a pork tenderloin (approx 1#) since we wanted the sandwich and that's all I had on hand - I sliced it thinner than 1" - I set my oven to 500 and watched it. I flipped once, basted it, had some crunchy on the tips (which we like).  It turned out very good, albeit probably not as 'juicy' as a pork butt would be. We would do it again, it was very good, but I'm going to try the butt next just for a comparison.
Do you have to use oyster sauce? I am allergic to shellfish
"If you mix soy sauce and hoisin sauce at a 1:1 ratio, the result will be close enough to oyster sauce to get you by."  per Bon Appetit magazine
Wendy & Flutist: Loin of pork is very lean and is used for quick stir fry recipes. Pork shoulder is fattier and good for roasting and braising.

The calorie count in the nutritional information sounded delightfully low for something so decadent sounding. But, then I noticed that it was not per sandwich, but—inexplicably—for one of fourteen servings.

I set my oven to 500° and it was way too hot. Good thing I checked the meat early and turned it down to 475°.  Otherwise, I would’ve had charcoal.   I sprayed the rack with cooking spray, and that made clean up a little easier, although I still had to scrub.  
Next time I will cook at 475° and baste every 10 minutes. All that being said, it was still tasty.  There are just two of us and I bought boneless country ribs, which have about the same amount of fat as a pork butt.  Next time I will make the cuts a little thicker than I did.
Great recipe! Thanks Sam! We started at 550, then monitored meat carefully as it started to get burnt ends. Once we flipped the meat we lowered the oven temperature.
475°-500° is more reasonable, easier to manage and a lot less likely to yield briquettes. “As high as it goes” (550°) effectively carbonized parts of it for me. Other than that, this was excellent.
Even if you don't make the sandwich, make the pork!  I used pork tenderloin (because that's what I had) and adjusted cooking time accordingly.  Really lovely!
Gosh, this was so good with such a little effort. Absolutely an authentic flavor. Must make it once a month!

I found the cut pieces very small and cooked well faster than the recipe.  The first time I made this, I followed the instructions and severely burned the pork.  The second time, I used a Bluetooth thermometer, cooked to the temp (about half the time) and the meal was great

The oven all the way up at 550 is way too hot. They began to burn even before I flipped them at 20 minutes. I turned it down to 500 after that and still could only keep them in the oven for another 10 minutes before they became almost inedible because of too much char. Might be better to try cooking at like 475.

See Robert Farrar Capon's Chinese Roast Pork, a NYT Cooking recipe (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/10099-chinese-roast-pork-char-siu?action=click&module=Global%20Search%20Recipe%20Card&pgType=search&rank=2). His instructions for roasting have you start at 450 for 20 min, then lower to 350 for another 10 min. I haven't cooked Capon's version, but when I made Sifton's a while ago, I kept the oven temp at 450 for 40 min total, and the result was delicious.

I was sadly underwhelmed by this sandwich. Having Chinese and Italian heritage, I was so excited to try this and combine 2 things I love! I think the marinade on the pork doesn’t really shine here, so if you have a splash leftover from basting, heat it through to kill any bacteria and slather that on your finished sandwich in lieu of the mustard, or in addition to it! I’ve never had one of these sandwiches in the wild, but I’d love to try it to get an idea of what it should really be.
Made exactly as written - absent optional bean curd - and so delicious.  Would not change a thing.  Keeper!
I'm confused. Only half the pork is used in the recipe, the other half is just kept for another use? If so, why not just call for a pound and use it all?
Because why not?  I imagine both are fine, but he's making the point that this is a very versatile meat to be used in different meals.  You do you.
Char Sui with just soy sauce is the way to go!
Had my first roast pork on garlic bread at Buddy's in South Fallsburg in 1961,  then later at Martin's in Sheepshead Bay. I have been making it ever since.
I meant Buddy’s in Loch Sheldrake.
I first had a rpogb at Buddy's in Loch Sheldrake in 1961, then at Martin's in Sheepshead Bay. I have been making it ever since.
Can you substitute pork tenderloin?
Sounds delicious.  Can you do a straight substitution of veal for pork or are there any other mods to the recipe? Thnx.
The article says you can substitute veal. Is preparation and cooking the same as indicated for the pork? Veal shoulder?
I don't see where it says that? It seems like a bad idea.
long before Singers, there was Molotovmanns in Brighton Beach.
The Chinese roast pork and homemade apricot sauce sound yummy, but I'm truly not understanding the choice to pair it with buttered garlic bread.  I'd definitely go with rice or scallion pancakes...
Oh my gosh this sounds wonderful! I have the meat marinating now. It will hang out until tomorrow. I made my own 5 spice powder. Had none. I can’t wait to taste it! Thank you for sharing Sam!

Having trouble tracking down the red fermented bean curd cubes. Anyone have a brand that they prefer as well as a location to buy it (or perhaps a worthy substitute)? Is the whole item optional or just the liquid from the jar???

I live in northern Virginia and found Pearl River Bridge brand fermented red bean curd at a local Korean grocery store. It was around $3 for a 10-ounce jar. I mashed two cubes (which are about one half-inch square each) with a fork and drizzled some sauce from the jar into the marinade. The pork is marinating and on the menu for Wednesday. Can't wait! The recipe, which I am following exactly, appeals to me on so many levels.

As someone who was dragged to the Borsht Belt as a kid in the 60s, I cannot fathom how pork anything could have been a Jewish classic. Bacon, yes.  Any substitutes available on the pork, by the way? Still not a fan.

I ate the version of this that Parm (NYC) offers tonight. I'd been intrigued by it, but also pretty put-off in the past. On the strength of this recipe I ordered some tonight. How bad could it be?, I figured. I got my answer -- unimaginably bad: sticky, with a weirdly burned undertone/then-overtone. I'm sure the recipe here is better, but after tasting this gloppy mess today, I'm in no rush to try.

We had a house on Sackett Lake for almost thirty years and went to Singers regularly. Love the mix of Chinese food and deli. Add a Katz’s black and white or vanilla cupcake and you’re all set. We also had a mobile truck in the area that cooked Chinese food along with bakery delivery trucks. Stumers chocolate covered donuts were great. 
 
I do think that Lefty’s on Route 42 had perfected the roast pork sandwich on garlic bread. They were like candy. I could go for one now.

It would be good to clarify temp - my highest is 550F and I feel like the pork was overdone. Still tasty, but maybe not as succulent as it could be. Does it need to be that hot or would 450 be fine? Also I put some water in the pan to keep drips from burning, which creates some steam in the oven. Good or bad?
A delicious combo but easy on the Chinese mustard as it can overpower
Set oven to 450. Cook the marinade on the stove until I have cooked out any bacteria from the raw pork. For the bread use roasted garlic

A note for those who cannot tolerate honey (IBS, IBD sufferers) and want a low FODMAP alternative: Lyle's Golden Syrup has very similar sweetness to honey and worked very well for me in this recipe. "Wok Me" brand makes gluten free hoisin sauce and oyster sauce that are also also low FODMAP (obviously substitute tamari for soy sauce for celiacs). I used this char siu along with shrimp for fried rice, since one member of my family has celiac/IBS. Very delicious!

My pork was overcooked.  My oven runs pretty hot, well over 550, at high settings.  If I try to make this again I will use a lower temp as others have suggested.  

I was able to salvage the dry tough meat by slicing it VERY thin and heating it in a pan with a little extra hoisin, soy sauce, and sherry. That softened it and gave it back a bit of moisture.  Not bad in the end.  Liked the mix of sweet, salty, and spicy.
Has anybody tried this with tenderloin? That's all I have right now.
I did.  Adjust the cooking time.  I did 375 for about 45 minutes for a 2.5 lb tenderloin in 2 pieces after browning in a cast iron pan. It was lovely!
3rd time I made with pork tenderloin. Not quite  quite as moist which is expected but in the sandwich or fried rice, still very delicious.

I had the same notion and made a special trip to UWS to try the sandwich at Parm. It wasn’t a version of this sandwich. It was aversion. Not even close. Also not even edible. It was disgusting. Al, make the sandwich yourself. Or seek out one of the dwindling diners that still make this succulent sandwich. As of c. 2015, the Apollo Restaurant and Diner on Merrick Road in East Meadow, LI made a good one. Get the packets of duck sauce, cole slaw and pickle spear for the full experience.

So...I did not make this as described, I’m sure that anyone who has fond memories of this sandwich from their past (or present) would be appalled at what I did.  But for anyone else, the sauce made for a lovely Asian flavored pulled pork in the crockpot. It’s a nice change from southwestern or barbecue flavors. (I landed on it because I searched NYT cooking for recipes using fermented bean curd, yum).
What did y’all use if you don’t have bean curd? Thanks!!

Fabulous sandwiches, but instructions to "turn your oven all the way up" are problematic, I fear.
I turned mine down from 550 to 500 and still had way too much char on the pork with 20 min/side. I'd try 450-475 next time.
Also, don't skimp on the Duck sauce and Chinese Mustard! A little Kewpie mayo goes really well as well. My wife was dubious about the marinade with the fermented bean curd, but is a convert now! :-)

One more thing: don't use the junk that comes in the plastic duck sauce packets. There is literally nothing real, just high-fructose corn syrup and food coloring, with more plastic waste which might taste as good as the sauce. I go with Gold's, which is easy to find in grocery stores in the "kosher" section. Huge jar which lasts a long time, real ingredients.

I have a routine that takes this to a new level.  Rather than cook at high heat, I sear the marinated pieces in a dutch oven on all sides at about 8 pm.  I then put the cover on the dutch oven, turn the oven down to 200, and let it cook all night. I'm up at 6, take it out and let it cool, and stick it in the fridge.  At 5 pm, I then slice it.  The cooking-then-cooling process makes the otherwise fall apart meat slice much easier and keeps it in slices, and improves the flavor.

title: Pork Chops in Cherry-Pepper Sauce Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: I make this with the whole pepper.  I pierce them before adding.  I also use a half a cup of chicken or other stock.  Leaving the seeds in makes it hotter, and keeping the peppers whole effectively makes them a spicy side dish.  I have also made this with by adding a whole onion sliced very thin and sauteed with the peppers until slightly caramelized.  Sometimes I will add a head of roasted garlic to the stock, spin it around in the Cuisinart and then add it for a richer umami flavor
Prepare the pickled cherry peppers with garlic, butter, a bit of pepper juice and a bit of chicken stock or white wine, and toss in a pound of cleaned, shelled shrimp. Delicious over pasta.

Delicious dish!!! Sam misspoke when he called this agrodolce. Agrodolce means sweet and sour and usually implies vinegar and sugar reduced to a syrupy consistency. No sugar in this dish. Also, this dish is akin to Scarpariello sauce, which is I recommend you try.

I’ve made this twice: once with a jar of sliced hot cherry peppers, which was painfully hot, and once with a jar of whole mild cherry peppers. The second time’s the charm: the dish didn’t feel under spiced at all!
I use a few hot cherry peppers and several more sweet cherry peppers; I can't imagine using a whole jar of the hot ones!   eeeekkk

Delicious.  I did prep the OTB chops differently because mine were over an inch thick.  I generously salted each chop w/Kosher salt and pepper 8 hrs before cooking and placed on rack in fridge.  Removed 2 hrs before cooking to come to room temp.  Blotted and seared in oil per recipe.  Finished in 385 degree oven to 130 degrees.  Remove and tent with foil for 10 mins.  Served on warmed plate with this wonderful sauce on top.  Tender, flavorful and juicy!  Will definitely make again.
I couldn’t find hot cherry peppers whole, so I made this with sweet ones. They still have a little heat, and we thought it was great, if tamer than I envisioned.

Made this tonight with boneless skinless chicken thighs and it was delicious. I used less of the brine and wine. It has a nice umami tanginess and was great spooned over roasted potatoes. Timid folks might find it challenging. Start with small dollops for them.
Good one.

Delicious. I couldn’t find hot cherry peppers so I used the sweet variety. I’m glad I did, I don’t like food too hot. Still had a nice kick. I used too much salt on the chops before searing, I’ll cut back. The reduced sauce melded beautifully with the drippings and fond left in the pan.

This sauce is a great way to jazz up plain, baked white fish, such as cod. I usually don't have wine so use vegetable broth. Just hold the salt if using broth, the broth/brine combo is very salty. I don't even salt the fish. I usually have to take brine from some other pickled pepper or pickled okra to make one cup.  And I only find mild cherry peppers. Still turns out great.
@Bobbi I will try this with halibut. Thanks for the tip!
I grew up eating this fish but instead of wine, Mom used my Dad’s home made red wine vinegar.  Absolutely delicious.

This is the sauce on our favorite Fried Calamari (or Steamed Mussels) at an excellent Chicago-style pizza&pasta place in Marco Island FL. Thanks for sharing! The sauce is also good on an Italian Sub, or sauteed chicken, or just about anything.

echoing the sentiment of another poster, there's absolutely no "dolce" to be found in this recipe. it's 100% agro. as in "burn your mouth off your face" hot and spicy. a good amount of tang from the vinegar in the reserved pepper brine. but zero sweetness. is there perhaps an ingredient missing from the list? sugar? honey? balsamic vinegar? idk. my hubs loves. but he is heat-seeker. i like balance. this makes my tongue cry.
Truly and absolutely awful! Those peppers remain painfully hot no matter what you do to them. I can't believe Sam put this out there.
@Serge Kappler I’ve made this a dozen times as written and it’s a favorite in our house. So we’re delighted he put it out there. Wish people would recognize the wide difference in tastes.
Prepare the pickled cherry peppers with garlic, butter, a bit of pepper juice and a bit of chicken stock or white wine, and toss in a pound of cleaned, shelled shrimp. Delicious over pasta.

Not a faint of heart eater or cook, but recipe as written is over the top in heat category.  First cooked as written, but Pestene hot cherry peppers seared our nostrils while cooking and when I couldn't salvage, tossed and started over.  Cleaned and thoroughly rinsed new batch.  Added sugar, chicken/veggie stock to white wine and more sugar at end when adding butter.  Local pork chop served on seared polenta but would never make again close to as written.  Can jarred peppers vary that much?

Okay, folks: this is absolutely fantastic, and absolutely idiot-proof easy.  I used *sliced* cherry peppers and saved even more time.  Served with linguine aglio e olio, with a heavy shower of grated parmesan, and steamed broccoli.  Perfection on a plate.  Just fantastic, and restaurant-quality in no time at all.
Pretty over the top salty, vinegary, spicy. Don’t know what I did wrong other than too much salt for the “aggressively” seasoned crust.

Use 1/2 cup brine and 1/2 chicken broth

Cut peppers in 1/2 inch pieces
How spicy is this?  I have timid folks in my family.
It's going to depend on the peppers that you buy and whether you leave or remove seeds and internal membrane. You probably are going to have buy a jar or two and test them.

Taking a page from Dan G's book when he comments that this dish is delicious and akin to Scarparello which he recommends everyone try, I agree if you enjoy this dish you will likely enjoy that one. And a recipe published on this site is one of the very best for Scarpariello I have ever had. See link here. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/11990-raos-chicken-scarpariello-shoemakers-chicken
Used hot cherry peppers -- so good, fSO hot.  I'll try mild next time. Delicious over bucatini with lots of parsley.

This recipe was for those who are used to fire in the mouth food. The taste was interesting but the heat was too much, and I am a hot pasta sauce fan. I spread some cheese on the pasta which reduced the heat but there should be a way making it less hot to begin with- reduce the amount of cherry peppers/add some whipping cream to the sauce.
Using a cup of brine makes this way too salty for me. I used 1/2 cup and it was almost too salty. And I usually like a lot of salt! Otherwise, it is a great recipe.
Mmmmm. Use sliced peppers. Fantastic over chicken thighs sauteed in olive oil and butter.

The brine to wine ratio seemed high as the dish was very salty.  Tried cutting it with chicken stock but still was too salty.  Will try again with some adjustments as we like the idea and flavors of the recipe in general.
Be careful cutting and seeding the cherry peppers - maybe use gloves. My hands were on fire the rest of the evening.
Too spicy! Almost killed us.

Great sauce recipe: omitted the pork chop, but added a large chopped zucchini & a small chopped bell pepper to the pan, when I added the jarred pepper. Served over linguine with grated parmesan. Definitely a repeater. Also, used black garlic, instead of fresh.

I made this with a jar of sweet cherry peppers, I can't imagine how spicy it would have been with the hot variety. Otherwise, I followed the recipe, and I agree it was too salty. I'll make it again, but won't salt the pork "aggressively" next time, and I'll try adding more vegetables to neutralize the salt a bit. Incidentally, the brine in my jar was almost exactly a cup, so that worked out nicely. I'll also use boneless chops, as this short cook time doesn't get them "fall-off the bone" tender.
Bell peppers work well with it, also adding sweet and hot cherry peppers.

Great flavor - though we thought that the hot cherry peppers were WAY TOO HOT!!  Clearly should have read the reviews before grocery shopping.  Would definitely make again with mild peppers, and wouldn't change anything else about the recipe.  We served over pasta, with broccoli on the side.
FANTASTIC - my husband adores this and it is so easy!
I recommend mixing mild and a little bit hot. We love spicy but the Cento brand hot cherry peppers would have been inedible for us.
My peppers seemed leathery!  What did I do wrong?

I used sweet cherry peppers instead of hot, and a handful of sliced, jarred peporcini peppers. Perfect, just enough kick without being too hot. I also didn't have a neutral oil handy so I used a mild olive oil. I don't generally like pork chops so rarely make them, but this recipe is a keeper! Served with sautéed broccolini, fresh bread, and pasta. Next time I'll skip the pasta, we it was delicious with the sauce, but too much food.

I have made this dish a couple times now. Once with whole peppers and once with sliced...a brand Whole Foods carries. We are very fond of this dish but much prefer half sweet and half hot. I take the liberty of mashing the peppers down a bit for more of a sauce, pour over the chops and back in the oven to cook thru as our chops were very thick, bone in. Very good.

I made this dish with hot cherry peppers. I ate one half of a pepper. My mouth is very irritated. I probably won't make it again or if I do I would use maybe 4 peppers. On the upside, my sinus have not been this clear in a while!

The first time out with a new dish I usually stick pretty close to the suggested recipe, I made all of the sauce for just two chops and we ate it all anyway.  I made it in my favorite carbon steel pan, forgetting how acidic the brine is so I cooked off some of my hard earned seasoning but it will come back.  Also substituted the sweet for the hot, knowing my audiences tolerance for heat.  This was on point and I will definitely add this to the rotation, I feel like Tony Soprano eating this….
We like half sweet and half hot cherry peppers, same as on our philly cheese steaks.

OMG! 

I had bought a pork chop for dinner and was trying to decide what to do with it when this recipe showed up in my inbox.

I went to the grocery store to buy the pickled sweet cherry peppers. Everything else I had.

This was soooo good! I might add more peppers next time. The sauce was so flavorful....very interesting flavors that made my mouth smile. 

I could only eat half of my pork chop...I'm hoping it warms up just as good the second time.

This is company worthy.

I really wanted to love this recipe, but it was too hot, even with my attempt to dilute the sauce (skipped brine, added 3 cups chicken stock, then fished out the peppers). Was really sad to throw it out! I may try again with mild cherry peppers if I can find them.

yes, they are called sweet cherry peppers, though it has no sugar; it is just to show they are not hot cherry peppers.  I use a mix of both and none of the brine; just chicken stock...works beautifully

Same happened to me and paid the price the next day as well.  I'm real suspicious of the jarred hot cherry peppers we used.  If I work up the courage to try again, will use the sweet/mild and possibly ad a few Calabrian peppers in.
Insanely hot. And we love spicy food, but this pushed the outer limits. We made it with shrimp and it was fantastic. Just rip roaring.

I just finished eating this dish - love, love, love!  Since there is only one of me, I made the whole sauce recipe and only two chops.  I can hardly wait for tomorrow night! I will just gently warm the chop, and since that’s all I have to do, I’ll make a bit of buttered and parked spaghetti to accompany.  I have 2 more chops in the freezer so I can make this again, probably next week!
Fantastic and spicier than expected! Woohoo!
cut the pickled peppers by half and added roasted red and diced apricot.... seemed to be great... also used only a tiny fraction of brine and the salt was still pushing the limit

Made this twice. First time as is, and it was awful. Do not use the whole jar of hot cherry peppers. Too spicy - even for spice lovers- and too salty. Used 1/2 cup of cherry peppers and 1/2 cup brine. Added chicken broth, two punches of sugar and butter to balance the sauce. Added flour also the thicken. Turned out much better than the first time.

I think I failed to reduce the sauce enough before adding the butter, which meant it was quite soupy. Served atop some browned chicken thigh meat and pasta, with Parmesan for grating. Used half hot cherry pepper slices and half sweet pepper slices. We all really liked it. It left our mouths very tingly, and the dog was not interested in licking our plates, but the heat level was bearable.

Having a hard time finding pickled hot Cherry peppers on the west coast. I see others have subbed. Anyone tried Mezetta sweet Cherry peppers? They’re a mix of green & red. 
Thanks for your input!

I think they’ll be fine.

You might add a bit of your favorite hot red pepper or a dash of hot sauce to get the heat to your taste.

Made as directed using sweet pickled cherry peppers. All the tang none of the heat. I’ll definitely make this again. And oI’ll start slipping in a bit of heat until we hit our limit. To me the tang was the big win!

I have been looking at the recipe for this , wanting to try it.  All I had was roasted red peppers packed in oil, so that is what I used. 

It was delicious. A touch of sugar and salt finished it. Wonderful with fried pork chops. Currently one of my husbands favorite.

Dinner Party Worthy! Made it tonight and it was fantastic. I doubled it and used 2 jars of pepadew peppers and their brine. They were the perfect amount of heat and sweet for our family. I think anything hotter would hurt the kids. I will add that the plates looked almost identical to the stylist’s.

I made this recipe for my family.  They loved it! Only difference is that I made the pork chops sous vide (1 hour or longer at 145 degrees) instead of cooking them in a pan in Step 2.  For Step 3, I added the sauce, along with the liquid from the sous vide bags, to the pan I used to "finish" the pork chops, and otherwise followed the instructions.

Delicious. The only tweak I made was to add a teaspoon of raw sugar to the sauce along with the salt and pepper. I thought the flavors were well balanced. Very tasty. Happy to use up the large jar of cherry peppers I bought for some reason.

Due to comments about too much heat, I rinsed my cherry peppers in water before adding them, and instead of using the brine from the jar, I used chicken stock. I also added about a tablespoon of honey. The heat was just right.

Made this with a jar of Mezzetta Family Co Sliced Hot Cherry Peppers, and for good measure the jar has another “HOT” on the label.  And yes, the sauce was HOT, as in spicy, and way to hot/spicy to be enjoyed at all.  1 star.
I live in Southern California and I can’t find cherry peppers anywhere. Is there a substitute or a source for these you can recommend?
Way too hot.
HOLY HOT!  My hubby loved this, he loves the spicy.  I ate about 2 of the peppers and called it quits! (I just ate the pork chop).  Definitely delicious!

Used fresh hatch peppers which I roasted over the gas stove, then bagged in paper to finish cooking. Did not peal them but removed the seeds as they were medium hot. Used pickle juice for the liquid. My guests LOVED this meal!

I thought the level of heat was just right – but unless we measure Scoville units, there's no way to tell whether your "too hot" is the same as my "just right." Heat may vary with brand, or even with pepper-growing conditions. But I digress. It was a little on the tart side. It was also kind of overpowering for a flavorful grass-fed chop, but then again, I can't recommend factory-farmed pork either. I wonder how the sauce would be on tofu. Serve with polenta to soak up juice.

I used a jar of Mt Olive Sweet and Hot Salad Peppers,  and it was wonderful.   I totally got the agrodolce-I reduced on high heat for 12 minute and it became sweet (I may have over reduced it),   Served with pasta with butter and Parmesan also.   Loved it with juices from pork seeping in!
Very tastie recipe.
Made as directed. It was boring. Cherry peppers right out of the jar, served with pork chops would have been easier and more interesting.

A keeper.  Couldn't find a jar of pickled cherry peppers (I know, right?) so used peppadews instead and threw in Outerbridge's sherry pepper sauce (the world's greatest condiment) to fill out the cup of brine.  Served on egg noodles and roasted some broccoli.  This is now added to my regular rotation!

I prepared this tonight, following the directions as closely as I could.  See below.  First, no need to aggressively season with salt and pepper.   While I was brining the bone-pork chop, I went to the store looking for hot cherry peppers.   Whole Foods had no cherry peppers at all.   My neighborhood market only had sweet peppers.   So I used hot giardiniera instead, as if was hot cherry peppers.   The end result was simply OK, not a very interesting sauce and over salted.    I will not repeat.

1/8 cup of brine
Add sliced onions
Go light on salt, adjust to taste at table
I don’t know whether I did something wrong, but my sauce tasted like pure vinegar.  We couldn’t eat it.  I followed the recipe, though a cup of brine did seem like a lot.
just made this to recipe, except used thyme instead of parsley, it is definitely going to join the rotation, this sauce is delicious. i could see using this with other protein as well.
Wow. I'm game for a spicy dish and this was quite tasty but the heat left us panting for more white wine.
Not a timid eater at all but this was a lot of peppers and brine. And I second the person who said it's not agrodolce -- it really needs a sweet element.

Double the wine and cherry pepper liquid but not the cherry peppers
Serve with angle hair pasta and salad
This was quite spicy. Even my husband who eats raw jalapenos found it spicy. Good, but be warned.
I made this once, and was disappointed.  The hot cherry peppers had a tough skin...leathery.  Did I do something wrong?

I’m a little puzzled - the recipe says cherry peppers in brine, but the only jar i’ve seen ( from Sun of Italy) has cherry peppers in oil.  Who makes the ones in brine?  That said, i rinsed the ones in oil a bit, and used them.  Very good.
The ones from Mezzetta are in brine -- trying this tonight with bluefish.

Mezzetta, Sclafani, Cosmo's, Cento  are just a few (all in brine). I'm in CT, and there is a huge Italian contingent here, so all the stores carry some of these brands....check Amazon, if your stores don't have them.
Great recipe and easy. Why not cook pork chops at the same time as sauce? Much easier and efficient.
Delicious!  I cooked pork chops and cherry peppers at the same time (not together).  Speeds things up.  Took much longer to reduce sauce than the recipe says.
A great dish, but adding vinegar is a must!  I also put some roasted peppers in too
What's a good non-alcoholic substitute for the white wine in this dish?
Made the dish as directed,easy to make, and very tasty.

My store didn't have the hot peppers, so I used mild; the dish was still delicious. I halved the recipe to serve two people. I served it with buttered spaghetti topped with grated parmesan and broccolini. Mu husband loved it! Definitely wants me to prepare this one again. I'm hoping to find the hot peppers and maybe mix with the milder; I like the heat. Definitely will make again!

So good! I embellished this by marinating the chops in the pepper brine for six hours. After cooking and plating the chops, I added some white wine and olive oil to the pan and lightly sautéed chopped frisee. I added the frisee and some buttered angel hair to the plate. The flavors blended well.  The chops were very tender and flavorful. A great dish: thanks!

every Italian restaurant in New Haven has a version of this dish, and often add fried potatoes to it.  It's delicious, and I make a simpler version, first browning the chops, remove them to a plate, then chopping sweet and hot cherry peppers; put the chops back in and pour some of the pepper juice over them...cover a minute or so for the chops to heat back up.  Simple and delicious...you do not need the whole jar of peppers.  Love it my way!

I used the pickled hot cherry peppers as well as the brine, I made sure to cut them in half, and remove the stems and all of the seeds.  We all found the sauce to be way too spicy and hot, I'd even used 4x the amount of chicken stock (in lieu of wine which I didn't have) and cooked it down thinking it would dilute the spiciness, but we still found it too hot.  I may try the recipe again as it had a good taste, but  I'll use mild peppers instead and I'd only use 1/2 of what's recommended.
Added a tsp of Chopped Calabrese Chile for more heat and a touch of vinegar for a little more bite. Great basic recipe to tweak to ones liking!

Scalfani hot cherry peppers are quite an experience - I only used half and the rest a fresh red pepper, but the sauce had quite a burn. Next time I will try sweet cherry peppers, or maybe brands vary. Basically, a great sauce for pork chops but adjusted for what you can tolerate as to heat.

I followed the recipe exactly and it was way too salty.  Maybe the brine my peppers came in were different than what others are using?  I didn't add any salt to the recipe at any point.  Had to throw it out.  : (

I love this recipe! I used a pork tenderloin instead of chops. Whole cherry pepper are usually found in the isle where pickles are displayed. The mild ones are still hot, just not as hot as Hot Cherry Peppers. Onions, garlic adds to dish! I serve it with broccolini and a simple green salad...Wahla!!
Didn't have cherry peppers but did have pickled banana peppers.  Flavors were terrific!

I made this using roasted red peppers which I first caramelized a little with onions.  We cannot eat hot anything but liked the recipe.  It turned out a real agrodolce since the peppers are a little sweet.

I do this with "peppadew" or similar sweet/hot peppers - mama lil's if you can get them, and boneless, skinless chicken thighs. I sautee the chicken, set aside, do the sauce in the same pan (a bit less brine, no salt, no added butter) and add the chicken back to simmer in the sauce. Very tasty, and a nice quick weeknight meal.

I used Juanita peppers to have a sweet/spicy thing, and to cut down on the heat- also added 1/2 cup of chicken stock to the sauce. It came out okay- there was a strange metallic taste I thought, but it could just be me because my fiance didn't notice anything weird about it.

Made this last night, it was delicious! I would make sure to wash out my pan thoroughly after the sauce since the remnants started to burn in the pan as I was browning and finishing the chops. I also salted in advance and left on a rack to brine for a few hours. Served with creamy polenta and loved the meal.
Halved recipe - used boneless chops I had cut from a pork loin roast. used 1/2 cup hot cherry pepper and 1/2 cup banana peppers with 1/4 juice from both and 1/4 cup wine. This was delicious.
Pork medium-rare? I haven't cooked pork for ages but the last I heard was that it needs to be cooked well-done. Has that guidance changed? I'd love to know!
Yes, all that nastiness was bred out of pigs decades ago so no need to cook to well done or you will end up with dry, sad pork.

I could not find “pickled” hot cherry peppers 
So I used jarred cherry peppers that are found in grocery stores.  This was beyond hot and made it inedible
Using hot cherry peppers and the brining liquid is way too spicy. We couldn't remedy this sauce. Perhaps would be good with something milder.
Used a jar of Mama Lil's peppers, turned out great! I added some additional wine when deglazing the pan also.

I wish I had read the comments before making this dish.  I searched high and low for Scalfani hot cherry peppers and made the sauce ahead of time.  One taste and realized it would be too hot for me.  I split the sauce in two and cut one half with chicken broth, reduced to almost a syrup.  Cooked the pork chops as per the recipe and it was so delicious.  Can't wait to use the second half of the sauce!

A new "gotta try" recipe for me but wish to ask members is this is super hot using the hot cherry peppers? That would ruin it for my family. Maybe mild peppers are the way to go but we like a little kick in our food, just not napalm levels..... Thank you!

This is a sublime dish and the only way to go is with the B&G Hot Cherry Peppers.  I was having a hard time finding them locally so I bought a case from the manufacturer. Best purchase in many a moon. I also recommend using a beautiful Berkshire Tommy Hawk Chop--preferably about 1 1/2 inches thick.  Serve it on a bed of linguine and call it a job well done.

I made this the other day. I would double the cherry peppers next time. And not use sweet ones as they did not have enough pop. I added an onion, too, just to have more veggies on board. I also did not brown the pork chops to begin without. Big mistake. I was being lazy.
11-21-21. Salt the chops, but go lighter on the pepper.
True comment about the agrodolce mistake.  Fix it with a teaspoon of turbinado or to taste!

this is absolutely agrodolce...no sugar necessary, whatsoever.  I make this with a mix of hot and sweet (which the jar calls the vinegar cherry peppers that are not hot), no sugar involved.  it's delicious, to toss in some fried potatoes and onions as well, to make it a meal.
I'm curious about the pin holes in your oven floor that you mentioned. I have the same affliction.  Where do they come from!  Sorry to divert from your recipe...#:-/

Thank you for this lovely recipe. 
Used Cento Hoagie Spread (pureed cherry peppers) and scallops sliced from a pork loin, as they were what I had. Brine from pepperoncino jar, a good glug of dry vermouth. Delicious sauce, a real keeper. Thinking of making it again to serve on breakfast eggs this morning!

This is reminiscent of a chicken w/ sausage, onions & peppers recipe from CI I make all the time.  After reading the notes, I added an onion to the sauté mix & doubled the garlic.  Next time I'll skip the brine & sub chicken stock (my house is still redolent 3 days later!) and add a fresh red, orange or yellow pepper sliced thin.  A delicious way to have pork chops which I rarely make.

I agree with NM: Pastene cherry peppers don't work here. The sauce cooked as written was very spicy but also much too sour. Since I only had two chops I had enough sauce for another meal. I added two sautéed shallots, some honey, a lot of parsley and some shrimp and tossed it all with spaghetti. Even having tossed 1lb of pasta in the pan, I recoiled from the harsh vapors when I went to wash it! What was supposed to be agrodolce was all agro. You must taste the brine and adjust the ingredients.
I used half hot and half sweet peppers and used brine from the sweet. Still a little spicy but the kids could eat it.  Half and half seemed a good mix.
Use sweet red cherry peppers, otherwise too spicy.
I've been using Peppadew Peppers instead of Hot Cherry Peppers. I tried it with Hot Cherry Peppers last night and was disappointed. I love this dish, but the Peppadews make it for me.
Use combination of hot and mild peppers- mainly mild!!!
I thought the peppers were leathery, tough.  I did buy a good brand.  Did I do something wrong?
Used chicken thighs instead of pork - excellent!

I made this with the “sweet & hot” cherry peppers, which was all I could find, and put it on chicken breasts. I couldn’t find our white robe so I used chicken stick.  Yum! For me, it could have been a bit spicier, but that was the peppers.  I will make it again and try it on fish.

Cut the garlic to be as you don’t want as possible to prevent smaller bits from burning when sautéing.

Go modest on the peppers and keep our fuel, possibly pumping up the wine, salt and add plain vinegar.

I was that the butter goes in at the very end.

I wish someone at NYT would weigh in on the jarred peppers. So many readers have commented. We love spicy but the hot cherry peppers made the dish inedible. I can’t believe it wasn’t a mistake to specify hot peppers. Sweet peppers still have a nice spicy kick. So, which is it?
The recipe says hot cherry peppers. How is that not clear? If you prefer sweet peppers, by all means use sweet peppers.

Glad I read the notes!  My pantry yielded a jar of sliced hot red peppers, and as I started to sauté them, I realized just HOW hot they were!  I added a sweet (red) bell pepper to try to dilute the hot.  I sauteed the pork chop with the pepper mix, and when I flipped it, I added a huge dice yellow zucchini and left-over rice, and some chicken broth.  Result?  Delish!  Hot and just hot enough ;)

I really love Sam's writing and enjoy many of his recipes.. but a pork chop served with pasta? Really? It doesn't all have to be healthfood, but could we please try dialing down the with the diabetes-fastrack/money wastefulness combo?

Oh, Kate! Don't feel obligated to serve this with pasta... or with anything. And what is less expensive than pasta that would be suitable here? 

But... what's wrong with a pork chop served with pasta?

Delicious, but do heed the advice of many and combine equal parts wine, brine and chicken broth to dial down the heat from the peppers (it was still mighty hot) and be sure to add the drippings from the chops after they've rested.  Use a cast iron skillet and instant read thermometer to char the outsides of the chops and achieve perfect doneness at 145 degrees.
I am having a hard time sourcing whole hot red peepers pickled in brine here in Columbus, or online.  Does Sam have a source?

Thus sounds delicious.  I do wanted to make it.  Unfortunately, I discovered that I’m not going to find jars of hot cherry peppers in DC or it’s suburbs/ I’ve done a search and no one has them in store.  I could order them from Amazon for $13 but that would make this dish cost well over $20 a person.
I've had this dish at Dominick's in the Bronx and they do it with vinegar peppers. Can you find those?
Delicious as written!  We served it over polenta with a bit of grated part
This is basically a Scarpariello sauce.  Works with chicken (add sausages) and pork cops.  Fantastic dish, made it a million times

This was spicy and delicious. I brined the pork chops first and added a little extra wine and butter to the sauce. It’s the kind of spicy you can’t stop eating. I paired it with farro with mushrooms and peas which helped to tame the heat of the sauce. My husband said it was one of the best meals I have ever made! Yum.
I took Barbs advice and cooked shrimp in the sauce. Really good! Will get the hotter peppers next time but had a good flavor with the mild ones.  Served it over jasmine brown rice.
This was good. We made it with linguini and clam sauce. Would make again.

This reminds me of how my Italian American mom made baked chicken. She would season the chicken with salt,pepper, garlic powder, and parsley flakes. Add hot cherry peppers and some of brine from the jar, a splash of wine and a good handful of Romano cheese sprinkled on top. It's still a family staple in our house.

Made this last night with a jar of mild peppadew peppers.  I started by following the recipe closely but when I tasted the sauce before cooking the pork chops, I added 2 teaspoons dark brown sugar and completely forgot the butter and parsley.  Served this with zucchini/corn fritters and it was heaven on a fork.  I have not been this pleased with a new dish in a long time.

Added 1/2 cup chicken stock to the 1/2 cup of brine I got out of the jar of peppers. Added garlic and shallots. 1-2t honey helped round out the flavor. 

Next time I’ll do this with shrimp as a pasta dish. It’s not a thick enough sauce for a piece of meat, but the flavor is amazing.

WAY too hot.  Difficult to eat, that's how hot it was.  Too much liquid in sauce. Never gets syrupy as described. Try just a Tbsp or 2 of brine (it's hot too).  Cooking time for chops was way too long.  I followed precisely and had well-done.  Chops were even MORE than 1" thick. Still overdone.  This recipe needs serious tweaking.  Try using sweet peppers and adjust heat to taste with cayenne or crushed red pepper flakes or tabasco.  Peppers' heat can vary widely. Go mild and adjust to taste.

Could only find sweet mild peppers in my area. Followed recipe exactly but found the sauce very sour.  Added 2 tablespoons of honey and 2 tablespoons of chili crisp to balance out the sourness.  Delish!

Quick, easy, pretty good, few ingredients. The  "salt liberally" instruction makes a nice crust on the chops, but don't also salt the pepper concoction. The brine/wine makes a dense, syrupy glaze and holds well until adding butter and pork juices (as recipe states). Next time, will maybe another clove of garlic, a little vinegar.  I just topped & quartered the cherry peppers in the pan (mild, what I had), left the seeds and will do that again. Sweet corn-on-cob was a good side dish.

After everyone at the table…. Started to sweat. I asked myself, why me?
Way to spicy for no apparent reason. I love spicy ? Tobasco  is a favorite cousin. 
After dinner and everyone had cooled down I reread the recipe…. Nope didn’t make any mistakes.
No thanks
Prepared as written. Tasty but wasn't able to achieve anything close to syrupy. Just a briny, thin sauce.

Used fresh hatch peppers - seared them whole on my gas stove then bagged them in paper to finish cooking. I did not peak them, but I did remove the seeds as they were labeled “medium hot” and I wasn’t sure how much heat my guests would like. Used pickle juice for the liquid. My guests loved this recipe!!
Used whole peppadew peppers, since my grocery store didn't have hot cherry peppers. Turned out very well with the addition of a few chile flakes.
I did 1/3 of the peppers and added tomatoes to the sauce to get similar color. It was still pretty hot so added chicken stock (as recommended by others) and honey. Helped mellow it out a little
Used freshly grilled hatch peppers and substituted dill pickle juice.  My company loved this!  Hatch pepper season is over…..going to try this again with grilled fresh shisido peppers next time!
This is a go to dish when we are hungry and done with our pescatarian diet. I add capers. They work. Serve it with pasta cooked dente. Sauce on the pasta. I add some chicken broth to make more sauce.

This is not something I eat but I made it for my husband who orders Chicken Scarparollla whenever it's on the menu. I love him so I made this. He loved it. A winner. It's not for everyone but if you like it, this is a fantastic rendition.
Made this with venison & was a massive hit with our family.
It was delicious one of the best things I ever made!

title: Veal Chops in Cherry-Pepper Sauce Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: What would be a good substitute for the hot cherry peppers? a different type of hot pepper?
pepperoncini

I have read that Professional Chefs sear veal chops very quickly then transfer them to an oven preheated to the desired internal temperature of the customer's order to finish cooking.  This method would presumably hold the meat at the desired internal temperature & not allow it to continue to climb higher making for overdone veal chops while the additional chops in your batch are being seared.  Unfortunately cooking veal at 5 to 6 minutes a side after searing would make my two dogs smile widely.

And PS, it helps to coat each side of the chop or halibut steak with a thin coat of mayonnaise before searing. You won't taste the mayonnaise but it will brown more evenly and at at a lower temperature. Amazing Ribs and Serious Eats both explain science reason this works.

This had not been my experience at all.  These are 1" chops. They need to be cooked longer. If you are concerned, adjust your heat after searing and pay attention.  You will have delicious chops that will never make it to the dogs' bowl.

I make this often with pork chops, and halibut. Pork and veal chops, and halibut, benefit from an hour or two in a 5% brine for some science reason.  For chops /halibut less than 1" thick a high temperature sear in a preheated cast iron pan works - maybe 3 minutes a side. For thick chops and halibut I find reverse sear is easier to get right than sear and finish in oven (Google "reverse sear Amazing Ribs or Serious Eats.")

I grew up eating this dish in upstate New York but with pork chops. The pickled cherry peppers are delicious and you can cut them smaller to cut down on the heat. (We eat these in Utica Greens all the time, a scrumptious dish that can scarcely call a green.) I do brown the chops in my cast iron skillet then transfer them to a sheet pan to finish in the oven while I make the sauce. I love the idea of trying this with Ahi or Halibut! Thank you for the suggestions!

This was superb, very flavorful, and easy to make. My “a piacere” was that I used pork chops. I could only find sweet pickled cherry peppers so I added a pinch of hot chili flakes. I was out of white wine. I had a grossly sweet rose so I used that, just to use it up. Didn’t hurt it at all! Followed all the other directions, served with the spaghetti with butter and parm, and green beans. I believe the brine helps to tenderize the chop. So very delicious, this goes into regular rotation!

@Sandra - I couldn't find cherry peppers and used a banana pepper mix, it came out excellent. Peppadew would also work. Just be mindful that cherry peppers are naturally more fruity, and if you use a more hot/acidic pepper mix, you may want to add something sweet for balance (I used honey)

In my opinion, veal chops are much better when cooked for a very long time. So after searing them add some water, white wine or broth and let them cook for half hour, then proceed with the sauce as suggested in the recipe. Or let them cook in the sauce if you can keep it from burning
The cherry peppers I bought weren’t spicy but your mileage may vary. I used fried tofu instead of meat and was pleased with the results.
Beware - the jared spicy cherry peppers can be too spicy and we like spicy very much. Made exactly as written and taste too spicy with no depth of flavor.

Unless you like pickled peppers I would avoid this one.  I made it according to directions except I could only find sweet pickled peppers.  The sourness of the sauce was disconcerting and didn’t I my opinion go well with the rest of the meal.

My Aunt, 1st generation Italian-American, used to make a variation of this dish, with pork chops and hot cherry vinegar-peppers. No wine. Only olive oil. Instead of serving with spaghetti, she always made it with mashed potatoes on the side, which she made first without any butter, then just before serving, she'd put the mashed potatoes in the skillet so they got a brown crust from the sauce. Fantastic!
