It seems there are many for whom the hot pickled cherry peppers are not working.  Try "sweet pickled cherry peppers"....same jar, same brand, same peppers.  They are by no means sweet.  In this case I think sweet simply means mild, not hot.  Many thanks to the reader who suggested a light mayonnaise coating to brown the chops.  I always use this for grilled sandwiches, but never considered it for anything else.  It's real!

Sam, I’ve wanted to make this for some time - but in the time of Covid…
I didn’t have cherry peppers, so I subbed pepperoncini. I added a tsp of honey and 1/4 cup of homemade chicken stock. I still added the white wine and brine. Pan seared the veal chops, put it all together and it was oh so good!

Thank you, S.S., once again for another tasty treat! Great to find this recipe for the cherry pepper sauce here this morning. Only recently I bought a jar of them (mildly hot ones and sweet,  not extra hot.) They have a wonderful taste, and now  I can’t wait to use them to make this sauce.
Not a comment but a question.  Is this dish very spicy-hot?  Would love to try it but I am wondering if the "heat" is too much.
why don't you try the peppers and see before you make it...

Cherry peppers vary widely in the heat department. Some are not much hotter than an Anaheim, others can be as hot or hotter than a spicy Jalapeno so there's no real way to say if it will be too hot for you. I'm a hot pepper aficionado so I consider cherry peppers in general to be on the mild side of the heat index so I would say just taste a bit of the peppers you plan on using before making the sauce and then decide.
this is an old family favorite that used to be a regular weekly dinner.  Mom usually made it with chicken but pork chops were a Saturday night treat.  We also would add sliced potatoes to the pan.
What is a decent substitute for the pickled hot cherry peppers.  Hard to find, even in Austin TX!

Made this tonight using pork chops and pepperoncini as that is what I had.  I seared the chops for about 3 minutes per side and they were done nicely. The sauce was delicious!  My husband who does not like pepperoncini or  very spicy food loved it.  There is a mild sense of heat but nowhere near as hot as eating the pickled pepper directly from the jar.
Are all brands of the cherry peppers similar, or would some be preferred?
They apparently vary. I used Pastene brand and the dish came out horribly sour. I concluded that you have to taste the brine and adjust the recipe accordingly.

Made this tonight w/ bone-in pork chops. Terrific. Made the sauce as directed. The cooking time, at least for my taste, is excessive. I pan-fried the chops as I would a good steak (cast iron skillet, high heat, grape seed oil) and had an outstanding result, after the chops rested, with no more than 6 minutes cooking time. Chops were about an inch thick and at room temperature.
You just halve the peppers? No further cutting?

Sear the chops until just brown then place in 250 degree oven while finishing the dish. I used pork chops and browned new potatoes and mushrooms adding when browned to the chops in oven. Reheat the sauce, pour over everything, garnish and serve. Perfect!
Per photo, peppers are halved lengthwise. Made this with ahi steaks instead of chops, accompanied by NY Times Spaghetti al Limone. Excellent.

We couldn't find hot cherry peppers so we used the sweet peppers = which had just enough piquancy to satisfy us.  We ised veal chops; I can't see using pork chops. Be careful when you add the wine and pepper liquid to the pan - it splattered all over the place. This recipe exceeded our expectations - it will be in our regular rotation.
Would it be ok to do this pork tenderloin?
I couldn't find hot cherry peppers--used sweet and added a sprinkling of flatiron pepper co, dark and smoky blend.  No white wine either, used tomato bouillon.  Turned out fine.

The  pickled hot cherry peppers were so hot 
they rendered the sauce inedible for us.  Perhaps a different kind of cherry peppers would be a little more palatable.

My husband said this is the best thing I’ve ever cooked.  I have to agree.  Used my home grown and pickled cherry bomb peppers and served it with pasta as pictured.  I only made 2 veal chops but kept the sauce recipe for four.  The more sauce the better.  This is heaven!
This was fantastic, although I will say that cooking the veal chops at 5 mins per side made them medium-well. They would have been better with 3-4 mins per side.
Delicious! Just a bit too hot for my taste. I used sliced hot cherry peppers - next time I think I will use a mixture of hot cherry peppers and sweet ones. This is a keeper!

I guess I broke all the rules here.  Tried chicken breasts, seared until meat thermometer read 160, then chucked into toaster oven set to 300 while sauce was finalized.  Used mixture of hot and sweet cherry peppers, the latter of which are sweet in name only, then only 1/3 cup of pepper juice.  Final sin was pouring in a few ounces of Trader Joe tomato sauce as a coda.  End result is so terrible we have had this every other Friday night since the recipe was published.

i have made this with pork chops and my home-canned cherry peppers (moderately hot.)  would love to try it with veal chops but they are nearly impossible to find (tried all three of my usual grocery stores - if I drive about 40 minutes each way to the other side of town, maybe they could be found.  maybe not. )  it is a delicious dish with the pork, lightly seared.

This is my husbands go to  at least 2 times a week... he uses pork chops, chicken, steak.... delicious!!! Since we moved to AZ from NJ it was rough trying to find our fav cherry pepers by B & G... no where to be found in Tucson.  We found Mazetti sliced cherry pepers and they work!!!Everything here is chili peppers!!!
Delicious!!!

title: Pork Chops in Lemon-Caper Sauce Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: Searing aggressively for about  1-1/2 to 2 minutes per side, and finishing cooking in the sauce until an internal temperature of 143-145°F is reached is definitely the way to go. Don't go by time; use temperature to determine when cooked through. The interior should be pink and juicy, not grey. Use an accurate, instant-read thermometer for consistent results every time. Clocks are for telling time. Thermometers are for telling when food is cooked properly.
In response to Vanessa:  2 teaspoons create a sauce.  2 tablespoons (in conjunction with 1 1/2  cups of stock and 1 cup of white wine would create more of a gravy.
@mjan agree unless reduced substantially which intensifies the flavor. Rule of thumb from a French chef friend is if it coats the back of a spoon, it’s a sauce.

Sounds good, but the thing is: Pork chops sold today are almost fat free, You can cook them any way you want, and you'd still have tasteless meat (and if well done, then they're really hideous, tasteless cardboard).

Switch to Pork shoulder (aka pork butt), cut it into 1 inch slices / steaks and cook / grill them with some oil, salt and pepper (or cook them according to this recipe but only 2 minutes per side). You'll never touch pork chops again.

PS: Costco has them for about $2/lb
@Paulo trader Joe’s has beautiful, thick cut bone in pork chops with plenty of fat
Wondering if it should be 2 Tablespoons of flour. The sauce is really thin when the directions are followed.
It’s more of a buerre blanc than a thick gravy

I think that is as it should be.  As noted by another poster, with the 2 tsps  you have a lovely sauce...2 tbs would be more of a gravy.  I guess in the end it depends on which you prefer.  You might try it once as written and make alterations afterwards to make it your own.
Two tablespoons would make the sauce a paste, so no.

I just saw this but basically cooked the exact same thing Tonite for dinner except with pounded chicken breasts, and it was phenomenal.  Only difference was I used dill instead of parsley, added a few slices of lemon into the reducing sauce, and did not use capers or zest.  I actually thought I should have added capers as we were eating.  Did not even think of doing this with pork though.  Can't wait to try it.
Why drain the fat? That seems unlikely that anyone in real life would drain the fat...especially just to add a different fat back in.

There is a danger with seasoning the chops with dried Thyme before frying them - the Thyme is likely to burn and end up adding bitter grains of charcoal to the resulting dish, ruining its taste. Instead, add the dried Thyme to the sauce with the wine and stock. I adjusted the dish slightly - I added medium-dry cider (alcoholic, as always in the UK) instead of wine and added a chopped leaf of fresh Sage instead of dried Thyme to the sauce. I also added the jus from the chops to the sauce. Great!
@Mari Nope. Do it correctly and the Thyme plays its role perfectly.
Thyme? Sage would be better. And why would you throw away all that flavour and caramelised fond in the cooking fat? Dust the chops in flour before you cook. Simpler and better.
My kids don't like white wine in sauces. Besides, I didn't have any available because I drank it all. So I substituted 1T of apple cider vinegar. The zestiness of the sauce was a hit!

This dish was super delicious.  My husband raved. I incorporated a lot of tips from other commenters: I dusted the chops with flour before cooking. I fried on high heat for 2 min and 15 seconds per side; I saved the thyme to put in with wine and chicken broth; I did not drain the fat (there wasn't much and it's full of flavor). I saved out some parsley & capers to sprinkle on when serving. Instead of hot sauce, I sprinkled with chili pepper flakes when serving for even more color. Beautiful!

Served this tonight with mashed Japanese (white) sweet potatoes. Used 3 Tbsp. Flour but made no other changes. Parsley instead of the usual dill is genius. This is a recipe I will not tinker with.

Cooked the thick chops five minutes on a side so they didn’t toughen up. Might even do four minutes next time.

This is the first caper-lemon sauce I have ever really loved and with the sweet potatoes it was simply incredible!

Just made this - a lovely recipe with a great sauce. Here's a genius addition: chard stems. I had a bunch of them leftover after harvesting my chard and using only the leaves. I cut the stems into 3-4 inch lengths, and simmered them after adding the liquids in Step 2. I covered the pan and let them cook until tender - about 8-10 minutes, then I removed the cover and reduced the sauce. Everything else stayed the same. I served the pork chops on a bed of chard stems with the sauce covering it all.

I recently read an article about the magic of mayonnaise (eggs and oil really) and how you should lightly coat the meat before cooking to help it brown. I also read a tip to sear both sides but then to flip every minute or so to help brown. They came out nicely browned a juicy.
Yes I’ve been doing this to nearly everything since I read that article, should’ve thought to do it here.
Always a good idea to score the outer fat ring on the chops all the way through the fat to the loin.  This prevents them from curling in the pan.  Awesome dish!
I make a similar dish all the time.  Whisk a little Wondra into the sauce to thicken. Stoneground Dijon mustard is also a tasty addition.
How about posting the celery recipe?

Wonderful recipe. Cooked it as instructed and delicious. Chops need to be from a good butcher, not thin. Take your time to boil down the sauce with the liquid after gently whisking in the flour. I served with a rice and collards recipe — while the rice is at a low simmer put chopped collards on top in the same saucepan and when the rice is done so are the collards — and this was great. I grew up in NC but now live in Northern CA and this dinner made me happy. Thank you for this!
Braised celery: https://www.foodnetwork.com/recipes/alton-brown/braised-celery-recipe-1939479
Thanks!
I haven't made this yet, but on the first try I'm not going to drain the fat from the skillet.

You’re draining the fat in the skillet because you are making a butter sauce.  Leaving the used olive oil-pork fat combo will give the sauce a dark, “dirty” flavor rather than the clean, bright taste intended with this recipe.
I don't understand how bone-in pork chops are cooked through in 10 minutes.

Jane, If you sear the pork chops first, mid- high heat. 4-5 minutes on both sides w/olive oil as noted in the recipe.  They should finish cooking through in the sauce.  Perhaps a few minutes extra if they're very thick cut chops.  Gone are the days of overcooked dried out pork.  Trichinosis isn't much of a work today. Try to purchase good quality Berkshire pork.
Agree with what MDR stated, if you cook them any longer they will be like shoe leather
Jane also make sure the chops are not straight out of the fridge. Let them sit for 30-60 minutes on the counter. Season them and let them sit for a bit before cooking.

Lovely sauce; even better the next day. That being said, I agree with others that two tsp flour is not enough, nor is three. Next time: two tbsp. Also, an eight ounce bone-in pork chop is ca. 1/2 inch thick. The searing time is too long and risks drying out the chop. I reduced the time by half; it was still on the edge of too dry. Instead of medium high heat, I'd suggest high heat for about two minutes on each side.

I will definitely make this recipe again. It was delicious. I changed nothing except that I accidentally put in 2 tablespoons flour instead of 2 teaspoons (advice, when you need reading glasses, use them). The recipe turned out perfect for me so I’ll probably always use 2 tablespoons.

One of the most flavorful sauces Ive made with pork chops. Delicious! I used close to 2 TBSP of flour for thickening & cooked it slowly & longer with the pork chops in sauce to make them more tender & flavorful. Added chicken broth as needed.  Used half the lemon juice & half of the capers in the simmering sauce. Saved the lemon zest, parsley & remaining half of the capers & added during the last few minutes of cooking to preserve their delicate flavors. Had to shoo my husband out of the kitchen
Thank you for Nutritional Information.
“Season aggressively” has got to be my favorite recipe instruction of all time.
Interesting to know some of the history mentioned here but as a German born, I have to note that my mom made it often with mashed potatoes and red cabbage. Wonderful to see this again.

I made this tonight with two large-ish chops, but added other ingredients as written. Regrettably I had no parsley, and I used all chicken stock in lieu of stock and wine. Although there are similarities to piccata, the recipe yielded a lovely, robust brown sauce, and the addition of thyme was delightful. I cooked the chops as prescribed, and after warming in the sauce, they were slightly pink. Highly recommend!

Pork tenderloin sliced into medallions
Vermouth instead of wine
More broth
Dollop crème Fraiche & mustard
Lots of thyme
Brown medallions, follow recipe &
Finish cooking meat in sauce

Excellent!!  Will definitely do again but agree with the other notes below: dust the chop with the s&p and flour, sear max 4 min.  Did not drain the fat, instead used 1/2 the butter listed and browned the shallot and garlic.  I added 1 tbsp of flour so it wasn't gravy, but not as thin if you only used 2 tsp.  Deglazed the pan with wine, stock and thyme.  After it almost reduced, added the chops back to the pan to finish cooking.  Finished as written but didn't add the extra butter to sauce.  yum

This recipe is also similar to Romeo Salta's delicious recipe"Breaded Pork Chops with Caper Sauce" from his 1962 cookbook 'The Pleasures of Italian Cooking', which is one of my favorites. I'll try this version the next time I cook pork chops.
For a gluten-free daughter, used cornstarch, but delicious sauce.

sauce thickened with flour is not done in France indeed not necessary as sauce will thicken automatically during the reduction of the added wine. Keep all flavours clean - no  flour interference in a great sauce.
Actually, it is done in France. This a roux, a fancy name for flour mixed with fat, which is the foundation for four out of the five French mother sauces.

Sauce was excellent.  Changes-based on others’ & my suggestions....  1: added thyme in with wine & stock 2: sprinkled flour on both sides of the chops 3: I brine pork chops, to keep them moist, by salting both sides w/ kosher salt & refrigerating 1-2 hours. No add’l salt @ time recipe says  4: Scored fat  5: used Pinot Gris for sauce since my choice to have w/ dinner. 6: used meat thermometer to 140.  7: left fat & EVOO in pan.

Would use this sauce with chicken & fish.  Had with sweet potato.

I used more broth, white wine, lemon juice and flour to make a lot of extra sauce and served the chops and sauce over rice. I definitely recommend using a cast iron pan to develop an excellent crust on the chops and then deglaze the pan with the sauce before adding the chops back in to finish cooking.
I used boneless, skinless chicken thighs instead of pork chops and they were DELICIOUS!  Otherwise followed the recipe.  Served over orzo.

I made this tonight and it was excellent.
I served it with brussels sprouts and sweet potatoes. Very nice and will make again"

Clocks are for telling time 
Thermometers are for telling when food is cooked properly 
Searing aggressively for 1.5 -2 mins on each side 
Cook in sauce until reach Internal temp of 143

the only changes I made to recipe is adding the lemon juice just before putting chops back in sauce. lemon juice looses its brightness when cooked. and the chops only need to be warmed in the sauce. after they've been pan friend and rested - all they need is a warm up. garlic capers and lemon juice! the perfect trifecta! (although maybe next time I add some anchovies)

Delicious! I halved the recipe for 2, and used less chicken stock and wine after reading the comments about how long it took to reduce. It was perfect. I was concerned the chops would not be cooked in this small time but they were, and remained moist and tender after spending time in the sauce. Served it with mashed potatoes and sliced mushrooms cooked in the sauce.
Add 1/2 cup heavy cream at the end.

I don’t make porch chops often but this has become my go to preparation.  As others note, any leftover sauce brightens up fish, chicken or other veggies like broccoli, cauliflower, or asparagus.  I cut the last butter addition by  1 T and don’t miss it.
YUM! I had a leek that needed to be used and no shallot, so subbed the shallot for leek. I skipped the flour. We were licking the plate. This sauce is dangerously good.

This was a breakthrough dish for me to prepare.  I'm relatively new to NYT Cooking, and still make mistakes, especially with herbs and spices.  But this pork chop dish was a game-changer!  I substituted apple juice for the white wine, but was anxious that it might not work. But it did. My wife is still talking about how tasty this meal was!  Thanks NYT!

Glad to see this delicious recipe pop back up! The only change I make is to finish the chops in a slow oven (maybe 275 degrees) for about 1/2 hour, instead of on the stovetop. After Step 2, I put the browned chops into the liquid, then finish covered in the oven. When the chops are done, reduce and finish the sauce per the recipe. Sooo tender and flavorful!
I make this with pork tenderloin which I sear on all sides and let rest, then cut into medallions and finish cooking in the sauce. Delicious served over polenta.
I like cooking seared pork chops in the sauce for a long time over low heat.  The meat "falls off the bone."

Loved this recipe! I am often frustrated by recipes because they are either too complicated or boring. The sauce was fantastic! I followed the timing of the recipe and everything was perfect. I did not have dried thyme or fresh parsley, so I used Herbs de Provence and fresh thyme instead. Amazing. Served it with sautéed spinach. Yum!
Don’t be afraid to really reduce the sauce!

The winner in this one is the sauce, lemony delish.  No doubt would also work for chicken breasts.  Cooked it pretty much straight up as directed.  Served with creamy mashed potatoes and steamed endive and bok choy.  Smothered everything and it's brother in that sauce.  Definitely a repeater.

I prefer thicker, bone-in cuts than the ones mentioned here, so start like I'm cooking a steak (cold-pan fat render, followed by lightly searing each side) then I gently braise them IN the sauce with some extra liquid. I remove the chops, country ribs, or whatever cut and then reduce the sauce &thicken with a little butter. Delicious with a bright salad; the extra chunky bits of the sauce (capers, shallots) should be thrown into the salad.

MAKE THIS!! RIGHT NOW. This is the best meal I have ever cooked. Even with mid chops (the best I could get where I live), the sauce will make you feel like you're in a Michelin star restaurant. I licked my whole plate clean. It's vibrant, bright, acidic enough to punch you in the face, yet with deglazing the pan and the white wine it has enough depth to not let the brighter flavors burn you out. And it was so easy!! Go try it;don't let the elitist pork comments scare you. It will turn out great.
We didn't have dry white wine, so I subbed in dry sherry with a splash of white wine vinegar and it was one of the best dishes I've ever cooked at home.
I ignored the words "drain the fat" but otherwise followed the recipe exactly and it is so good it is immediately going into the weeknight rotation
Made it, taste great.
I used vermouth rather than white wine

Just made it tonight (3/2026). Overall Delish. Input: Too much liquid. Next time will almost halve the stock/wine. Used overall 3T butter—plenty. Used more garlic, shallot and capers than called for.  Used commentary advice to dust chops with flour, S&P. Served with rice—FYI: sauce is very thin (not a criticism). Rice, or some absorbent base is optimal.

Liked the dish, but I’m not sure about NYT cooking times. Since I’ve been making these recipes, I’ve been better at prepping, but it still takes a long time to pull everything together. agree with any of the other commenters, a little bit less cooking time for the chops.
Easy recipe with  their Unami flavor of capers and lemon-delicious
"Pork Chops in Lemon-Caper Sauce" is a derivative of an old European recipe.  It is merely pork chops piccata style.  Call it what it is.
Don't mess with the recipe. It's stunning as is.
Would be helpful to beginner cooks for recipes (where appropriate) to include suggestions  on what to serve    as a side dish to complete the meal.

For any main dish featuring a sauce or gravy, I would think something on the side to soak up the sauce/gravy would be appropriate -- rice, polenta, grits, couscous, quinoa, dressing, lentils, mashed potatoes, pasta, biscuits, cornbread, popovers, or just good regular wheat bread.
Lovely sauce. The chops were think and so they took longer to cook. Each ingredient was distinctive and at the same time melted into the others. Will make this again, it would also be good with fish!

I made this last night and only had 2 pork chops and unfortunately, they were not bone-in.  The dish was still delicious however, I would cut back on the liquid by half (wine & stock) and increase the flour slightly.  The sauce was very thin and I think it would have been tastier to have it be slightly thicker.  Also, browning only and then finishing in the sauce as someone else suggested, is the way I would do it next time.
Needs some more Zip. I would add red pepper flakes as a sub for the tabasco.

We love this dish! Have made it three times and my girlfriend has coined the sauce “magic sauce”. I didn’t have any white wine, so subbed apple cider vinegar and love the tanginess of it. Will make this again and again, and with chicken too.

definitely less time searing to avoid over cooking the chops -- 3 min per side more likely to get to 140-145 degrees. And I used more wondra flour to thicken the sauce. End result was great. Pork chop picatta!
I don't understand how bone-in pork chops can be cooked through in 10 minutes.
I recently made this same recipe using chicken of the woods mushrooms for my vegetarian friend.  Everyone loved it, even the carnivores!

Sam! Great versatile recipe. I used chicken thighs and doubled the recipe. Also I used BIG caper berries that really added a briny, bright flavor to the sauce. Served the dish with potato gnocchi and broccolini. And drizzled the delicious sauce over EVERYTHING. Thanks for the recipe.
I would love to know what style of pork chop works best for this dish.
They look like pork rib chops in the picture accompanying the recipe.
More shallots, no garlic

This is an Italian picatta sauce (generally used with veal or chicken) with capers added. The French repertoire includes a sauce grenobloise, which is a lemon caper butter sauce generally used with fish and shellfish.
Pork chops tough And chewy. Brimming should fix that.
Is there a substitute for the wine?

My pork chops were an inch thick and certainly weren't done in five minutes per side-I should have left them in the sauce while it was reducing as I would usually do. I also used two tablespoons of flour. As a South Louisiana resident, making pork chops "etouffee" or "smothered" style is pretty normal for me, but the capers and lemon juice were a piquant touch and very good!

I haven't made this yet, although I certainly will. It seems to me to be essentially a pork piccata, and since veal & chicken piccatta are among my family's favorite meals, this will be a lovely variation.

Just cooked this for dinner tonight, turned out perfect.  Followed most of the directions; but used dried thyme and dried parsley (didn't have fresh stuff on hand).  Sprinkled the thyme on the chops with the salt and pepper. 

Used 2 teaspoons of flour, and watched the reduction until it was the right consistency.  After I returned the chops to the pan the sauce thickened nicely into a very nice roux.  The sauce was so good you could eat it separately with a spoon.
Cooked this with boneless chicken thighs instead of the chops  and served over red skin mashed potato.  Steamed some brussel sprouts as well--fantastic.

Just made this tonight. Home run. Served with broccolini (with lemon, dijon, garlic, balsamic and olive oil dressing) and quinoa and chick pea salad. Followed recipe exactly, and it all came out perfectly. My chops were a bit fatter than 8oz., so I seared for 2 min/side longer, and they were as moist as could be.

Delicious.  Used hard cider instead of white wine.  Didn't have Parsley, used cilantro.  It was very very very good!  I doubled the recipe so we had lots of sauce. Planning to use some of the leftover sauce on pasta!

Delicious!  The sauce was a perfect texture. If you thought it was too thin, you probably didn't reduce it enough.  I didn't add the lemon juice as the sauce was tart enough without it.  I recommend tasting before adding it.
This dish was so good I went out and bought the cookbook referenced, "Jubilee"!

So... I had really fat chops. Brined them (all good chops start with brining). Dried them off, seasoned and seared. Stuck them in a 400 oven while I made the sauce. Timing worked well. When they were done, so was the sauce. 5 stars!
How do you brine the chops?

Just made this tonight. Used 2 pork chops and halved the recipe. It was fabulous. The sauce was delicious and a great consistency. One chop was thicker than the other so I pulled the thinner one off first. Some of the timing may need adjusting based on chop thickness and stove temp but very easy to determine. I will make this for company as the sauce tastes complex and sophisticated.

A roux is typically made with equal parts of fat (butter in the case of this recipe) and flour. The recipe calls for two tables spoons of butter so to make a roux you would use two tablespoons of flour. 
That made for a thick velvety sauce similar in appearance to the sauce pictured in the skillet. I think the cup of wine and cup and a half of stock was a little excessive. Perhaps a cup of each would suffice. Left to boil down and thicken for about ten minutes, though -  it was delicious.

Basically, this is pork piccata with hot sauce added (Crystal if you can get it!).  And my wife is now requesting it as part of our dinner repertoire.  So thank you for bringing a little more happiness in the house!
Cooked as instructed. Superb.
I substituted bone-in chicken thighs. Browned them in a cast iron skillet, roasted in 375 oven for about 30 minutes and then finished per recipe.  Really good.

Just made this tonight, and I agree with others that the sauce just is too thin if you follow directions as is.  You shouldn't need to boil half your precious ingredients away in order to thicken it, only to end up with less of the delicious sauce.  I will add a bit more flour the next time or omit the extra half cup of chicken stock.  I served mine over a bed of angel hair pasta and it was indeed a lovely dish.

I found that when a recipe calls for additional cooking of pork in the sauce, the pork comes out very tough. This recipe is no exception. From now on I will pan sear the pork, put it aside and then make the sauce. When you pour the sauce over the pork and let it sit for a while off heat, you get the same taste results but the pork remains tender.

I used boneless chops each an inch thick.  One required some additional time in the broiler to finish cooking.  I also used about three times more thyme than called for but it did not seem remotely overpowering.  I enjoyed this recipe and intend to make it again.  I am interested in what wines you would recommend with it, especially reds.
We followed the directions but added 1/2 TSP of Dijon in lieu of hot sauce.
2 teaspoons create a sauce. 2 tablespoons (in conjunction with 1 1/2 cups of stock and 1 cup of white wine would create more of a gravy.

True as some have noted, there are other recipes similar to this, but this one is wonderful just as written--all the cook has to do is keep from over cooking the chops.  Only thing I did different from the recipe is that I brined the chops (1/4 cup Diamond Crystal Kosher salt in 1 quart water for 1 hour).  It helps ensure juicy chops.
Outstanding!  Didn't change a thing, and probably never will.
Realized mid way I was out of flour so used half and half instead and let reduce to make a cream sauce. It was delicious.

Our butcher sold us 1 1/2 inch thick well marbled chops and warned us not to overcook them.  We told him not to worry we were going to be using a meat thermometer.   Seemed to make him happy.  We followed the recipe exactly and it was a great dinner.  Haven't had pork chops this good in forever.   Everything stood on its own, lemon zest, capers, and the not optional imho, Frank's Hot Sauce; yet it all fit together.  I am so buying Ms. Tipton-Martin's cookbook!
Made this according to directions, including about six shakes of Tabasco. Easy, quick, and very good without modification. Sauce is good accompaniment to veggies.

Followed the instructions exactly except that I used one tablespoon of gluten free flour. The sauce was beautiful and delicious. The chops were perfectly cooked in my favorite cast iron skillet. The problem is that we were not wild about the super-lemony sauce on the pork chops. To us it just wasn’t a good combination. The sauce seemed much more suited to fish. In fact. I recreated the sauce with some pan seared grouper and it was delicious.
My wife is on the Low fod map diet, so. No garlic or shallot. Instead did a chile serrano pepper diced. Only had oregano. Did add dash of hot sauce. Delicious.

Whoa...that’s a flavorful dish. Accidentally added two tablespoons of flour rather than teaspoons, ended up creating a gravy but was still insanely delicious, the lemon and hot sauce really make it. Also used lamb chops rather than pork. Had a giant bag of salad greens about to turn so I sautéed them on the side and topped the chops with them, and served with some roasted squash. A  decadent fall meal.
i added 1 t of grainy mustard to the sauce for a little extra zing.

I made this in a cast-iron skillet. I used far less fat. I sprayed the pan with olive oil spray and seared the chops. I finished cooking the chops in a 325-degree convection oven. I sauteed the shallot and garlic in 1/8 tsp oil. I deglazed the pan with the chicken stock to which I had whisked a little cornstarch, then I added about half the wine called for along with the drained capers, lemon zest, and lemon juice.
Made this last night and it was a home run! The sauce was perfect; I followed the recipe exactly, didn’t change a thing.
Brilliant as is!  No changes.  Served with a purple cauliflower mash to rave reviews!

I made this last night. The sauce was very piquant and I worried it would be too sharp for my husband and I. Nope - it kinda mellowed out when added to the pork - and was very delish. But I had forgotten to squeeze the lemon after zesting and I'm glad I forgot. Also, I used thin cut pork chops and I think the sauce really helped the meat tenderize a little. Can't stand thick cut chops anymore - too tough.

A really good recipe.  Followed as written and honestly don't see where any changes would enhance this.  Someone mentioned about the initial cook time of the pork chops.  Mine were a little over an inch thick and the 5 minutes per side plus keep them warm while putting together the sauce plus rewarming the chops in the sauce resulted in a nice tender, thoroughly cooked pork chop. While trichinosis is not seen much it should be noted that with reduced meat inspectors it could become problematic.

Capers are going to become a pantry staple for me. I have made this a half-dozen times now. It is so good! I keep forgetting the parsley, but I remember to grab capers and lemons when I buy pork chops. It’s very easy to make, and absolutely yummy. If you haven’t tried it yet, do so.
I would drink this sauce if I could. Actually I can and maybe I did a little.
very runny; use more flour.
Reminds me of a very similar Pierre Franey recipe from his classic "Cuisine Rapide." There, you use pork loin medallions. But this is better with the bone-in chop.

the pork chops benefited from sitting in a very warm oven to finish cooking through while i made the sauce. I needed to add more flour and simmer the sauce longer to get more texture to it. the flavors were lovely together.

2 tsp of flour is plenty!!! Make sure you follow instructions of adding it to butter before the liquid goes in. I also suspect that those that said it was too thin needed to let it reduce longer. You really have to let it rip on high heat to reduce. My only change would be I think it was too much parsley I would cut that in half otherwise it was spot on!

Just made for dinner guests. Delicious. Used GF flour, 2tsp, worked perfectly. Took a while for sauce to thicken. Wasn’t sure how it would compare to regular flour.

Not sure why cook the chops first and set aside. Next time I’ll make the sauce while the chops are browning in another skillet. 

Except for the flour sub (dietary necessity here) used exact measurements. Wouldn’t change a thing.

I followed the recipe but was disappointed in the toughness of the chops. They were center cut bone in. I think the time given for frying them in the olive oil is too long. Can someone tell me what the secret is to get tender pork chops?
The secret to get tender and juicy pork chops is Sous vide. I gave up pork chops several years ago, but once I got my Anova, I have made them several times, and they are always perfect.

I agree with the previous poster that additional flour is needed to make the sauce less like soup and more like a sauce. However, thick or thin, this sauce is delicious! Made it this evening for my wife and she wants me to use the leftover sauce with chicken tomorrow. Will do!

Use the thermometer to cook the pork - i used very think ones, so time is not relevant.  Added sage instead of thyme (just happened to have it, but I think it goes better with the dish).  Added a little stone ground mustard, and finished with cream.  Served over cheese grits and a side of sauteed mushrooms.  This is a keeper.

I’ve just made this. It’s delicious. I added one bay leaf to the pot liquor. And used 2 birds eye chilis instead of hot sauce - for some heat. It worked really well especially with a Cederberg Cabernet Sauvignon (origin West Coast South Africa) as accompaniment.
Recipe is great but recently substituted swordfish for the pork chops and everyone raved about it.

Good clear-the-fridge recipe that can be easily modified with what you have on hand. Used rosé wine instead of white; sage instead of dried thyme; minced white onion instead of shallot. Flour was gluten-free all purpose. Result was lick-the-plate good: we'll be recreating this sauce for different proteins to come.

Simply delicious. With 2 pork chops I halved ingredients and it worked perfectly.  Only change I would suggest is cooking more like 3 minutes per side. Elegant and so tasty. Each individual ingredient made an appearance in my mouth. Served with sweet potatoes which was a good choice for the sauce and steamed broccoli. A weekday meal worthy of a weekend dinner party.
I used fresh lime juice for a more robust, intense citrus flavor. Incredibly delicious!

I actually loved this recipe but cook time wasn’t long enough. I would do an extra minute or two in the pan (in all fairness we got fairly large chops from Whole Foods that were definitely bigger than 8 oz a piece). But all in all delicious. Served with broccolini and it was a great meal
Great recipe!  Also works with chicken.  I use boneless breasts

I'm with Vanessa. I thought the sauce was a little too thin. I may add a small about of cornstarch with my final butter addition next time around. The flavor was great. Also, I made the sauce separately and grilled some pork tenderloins and added them to the sauce at the end.

I agree it needed a bit more flour (I used about 2 tbsps) for the sauce. It was a delicious recipe. Definitely need to keep an eye on the pork so it doesn't overcook. I erred on less cooking time by about a minute because the warmup at the end gives it a bit more cook-time. I didn't have chicken stock and used vegetable stock, instead.

Excellent dish. 
My guests asked for the recipe.  I substituted thick boneless pork chops as that is what I had on hand. I browned on top of stove as directed and then finished in a 400 degree oven for about 10 minutes or less. I will definitely make this again but will try with bone in. I also made thicker sauce.

I substituted chicken breasts and parts, bone in, and the cooking time was way off. The sauce was good and followed the 2 TBS rec for flour. If using bone in chicken, plan on at least 20 min of pan cooking in step 1 or maybe not using bone in parts at all. Pounded boneless breasts and thighs would have been better than just parts

Pretty good! I used 3 pork chops and a full recipe for sauce (the more to dip with sourdough bread bits, my dear!)

Everything is fine, note that dried parsley works too (1 tsp-ish). 

The recipe doesn't say, but you should SLOWLY pour the wine and stock onto the roux, a little at a time. 

Cholula works for the hot sauce. Few dashes.

SERVE IMMEDIATELY so sauce doesn't break. Bony appetite!
Goofed and used 2 TBSPs flour instead of 2 teasps. but it was still delicious.  The capers with the lemon really elevate this to a restaurant-worthy meal.
My daughter made these last evening, as directed in the recipe. It was delicious and not at all difficult to prepare. Next time I will do the honors of preparing this dish!

This seems like a good recipe to ask about a problem I often have with recipes. Which is to say, what can I substitute for white/red wine ? Since I don't drink alcohol, I rarely have wine around. Well I never have it  around :)  I hate buying a bottle just to throw out most of it but a lot of recipes call for 1/2 cup . I would love to get recommendations as to what I can substitute. Thanks in advance : )

Merrick Dean, To answer your question, some grocery stores sell "cooking wine" which I don't really recommend. Instead, go to a liquor store and just buy a half bottle. This will be less that $10. I don't know the exact volume equivalent, but if you used half of the half bottle for your recipe, you could freeze the rest for another time. I think you will find that a little white wine is invaluable for deglazing a pan after cooking any white meat or even fish.
Hi, I don’t drink red wine and agree with your issue with the waste. I started buying small four-packs of 187 mil. to keep in the pantry. Woodbridge is not terrible!

Merrick Dean:  Try buying a bottle of dry vermouth, which keeps a long time.  They come in smaller or larger sizes.  I always have dry vermouth (don't mistakenly buy sweet!) because I like martinis, and I don't want to open a good bottle of wine just for a couple of tablespoons.
I'm pretty new to following recipes so closely, so I have a question. Why does it have to be unsalted butter? Most of these recipes seem to specify. What's the difference in flavor?

This would be a good sous vide recipe to make juicy, tender pork chops. Just cook the chops in the sous vide, remove and pan sear the chops for a couple of minutes and make the sauce as written. I will try this the next time I make sous vide pork chops.
We enjoyed this yummy dish with pork chops but think the sauce would be equally as good with chicken breasts. Thanks for sharing this dish.
Use 1" chops with bone.  Cook to 135 degrees in sauce. Remove if sauce is still too thin.  Reduce sauce, return chops and heat through.  Lots of sauce.  Serve with noodles.

Oh my, delicious decadent and make sure you have a loaf of nice crusty bread to dip with!Our three high schoolers devoured this with no complaint and asked that this is on the regular dinner rotation.Used thick-cut bone in center cut pork chops (4 chops ~12oz each but more than enough for a hungry family of 5).This meant a longer time in the pan searing/browning, but chops turned out tasty and not dry at all.Upped the wine/stock slightly because my family likes to dunk.Yum!
How would this work with chicken, either breasts or tenders? About the same except for cooking time adjustments?

This is basically a piccata recipe and can be riffed with pork, chicken or veal - the difference being not breading the protein.  Capers, for the one who asked, last for quite a while in the fridge given the brine. If they smell fine, they are probably okay. This was a delightful variation from what I usually do with chicken cutlets. My humans loved it.
Delicious! Serve with potatoes or quinoa or something to soak up the sauce
I thought the lemon caper sauce was good and my pork chops were good but didn't like the combination.  I was disappointed.  I'd use the lemon caper sauce on chicken and not pork.
I only needed 2 pork chops but made the full amount of sauce and I was glad to have extra because it's so delicious.
I suggest getting all ingredients prepped before you do anything. It makes this an easy dish and so delicious.
Made this recipe and followed instructions exactly as written and WOW! They were a hit and so simple to do. Going into a non-existent rotation that may begin with this recipe. So so good!
Add thyme and sage to wine  to avoid burning it
Good recipe to riff on. I made it without flour and less liquid for a lighter version. Sage, parsley, and leeks were on hand.

Made this for the first time tonight with two pork chops.  Kept the rest of the recipe the same and it was quite good.  Good enough that I shall make it again.  having extra sauce worked out well as it went on the leftover Farro I was serving with it.
The next time though, I will cut back on the capers as they seemed to overpower all the tasts.

I agree with adding thyme or other herbs to the sauce rather than at the beginning. If you use hot sauce consider reducing the lemon juice by half to pull back on the acidity. For those insisting pork is flavorless: I serve "Asian family style" by cutting the "chops" (I use thick pork loin from Costco) into half-inch slices then warming in the sauce. This allows me to cook a bit on the rare side to start while dialing in the doneness at the end. Always turns out great!
This is ridiculously delicious! I served mine with rice and roasted green beans. Bold flavors! Too many lemon recipes do not have enough souce or lemon flavor but this one does NOT disappoint
would add more flour next time, just sayin

I made this with large bone-in chicken thighs. It was so good! I seared the chicken, poured off the fat, made the sauce and then right after it thickened some and I’d added the parsley, lemon juice and capers I added the seared chicken, turned the heat down to a simmer, put on a loose lid and cooked the chicken until it was done.
I added the 2 T of butter at the end to the sauce but I may skip that next time. It seemed to make the sauce separate a bit and I could do without all that richness.

Most delicious meal! Served with risotto in which I steamed fresh 1” slices of asparagus. 
Best ever meal. Can’t wait to have company again!!!!
Cut the salt by 50%, and it's divine.
For such a southern inspired dish, I think you're crazy if you don't add a bit of paprika to the seasoning. It sets it off just right!
2 teaspoons create a sauce. 2 tablespoons (in conjunction with 1 1/2 cups of stock and 1 cup of white wine would create more of a gravy.

Delicious!!! Used very thick chop from the coop (3/4 lb) since it was just for 2, half the recipe of ingredients, covered it and baked it in the oven at 325 for 1 1/2 hours. Added the juice, capers and rind. Soooo yummmy. Good dinner party food. The sauce was perfect thickness when it came out of the oven and the meat was wonderfully tender.

I made this dish last week and it was good, but not good enough or easy enough to go into the regular rotation.  The amount of dried thyme called for was not enough to make its presence known among so many other strong flavors, that should be doubled.  The sauce sat a little uncomfortably between a gravy and a reduction without the best qualities of either.  I’d  give it 3.5 stars.

Pork recipes should specify the cut of chop. It matters. A center cut is almost always dry and tough. Rib chop: less dry. Shoulder chop should deliver a moist and juicy result. But regardless, brining in 1 qt. water plus 1/4 C. DIAMOND Kosher salt will mean a juicy, tasty result. Just be careful not to aggressively salt the chop after brining.

used dry champagne, since I had some; onion for shallot, since I didn't have shallots. less lemon (skipped the zest). More garlic. Will do again.  Sear then braise is how my mother cooked pork chops, though I think they were always plain at our house. She'd have loved this.

This is REALLY REALLY good.
My suggestion is to make more sauce than is allotted for in this recipe: this recipe makes enough sauce to cover the meat.  But in my family, we are all fighting over the sauce at the end for dipping our bread or fries or rice in...  So I've started doubling the sauce quantity to appease the natives.
Excellent
This recipe is fantastic just as is. Use a cast iron pan and serve along side the gnocchi and lemon Brussels sprouts (also NYT recipe). Adding to our regular rotation!

Unless I did something wrong, this is definitely a pork piccata. I made it as recipe indicated. I will probably make it again, but will be sure to not skimp on the seasoning elements, as in…I’m going to over do it a little to be sure there’s enough flavor. Definitely needed more shallots, perhaps mine was a little too small. Will do two small or a medium-large. Will do maybe 1 1/2 tsp minced garlic. Must be more generous with the S&p and I think I’ll try fresh thyme next time.
As per notes, score fat to prevent curling. Add stone ground mustard or cider vinegar

This is probably my favorite pork chop recipe. I always add a mix of dill and parsley to the sauce—the capers practically cry out for dill. I serve this with roasted Brussels sprouts and crusty bread. Delicious!

My husband is a pork chop fanatic and I have a hard time finding a way to cook them that does not end up dry!  But this recipe did that and so much more!!! The sauce was wonderfully rich, savory and tart.  A perfect, rich balance.
3 Tsp flour,  not 3 Tlb.

Great help from others because I did not want to overcook the pork. Seared the chops (salted, peppered, but no thyme) in dry cast iron skillet for 1.5 minutes per side. Covered them and put aside. Warmed them with the sauce; they remained moist with a hint of pink. Otherwise followed the recipe, but will submit that the hot sauce is a must.
I love this recipe! It is so tasty. I use 1 1/2 inch pork chops that I get from my butcher. A great side is either multi grain rice or grits. Love it!
Delicious as written with swordfish

I do a version of this where i don't cook the chops all the way through until the sauce is finished (i usually add spinach and grape tomatoes) when i add the chops back and let them finish in the sauce.
Why bother with chicken stock, go all wine.
Good basics guide and yes don’t overlook the chops, I love capers but be careful with the lemon juice! Thank you again NYTs fast good healthy food on a busy night in DC!

Made this exactly as written (minus the hot sauce) but substituted boneless pork chops for bone-in.  The dish was excellent, easy and quick.  The first 2 steps can be done ahead of time and then finished off in a matter of minutes.
Chops didn’t Brown up- should have used higher heat, maybe heat up my steel pan longer. Definitely more flour as way too thin for ‘gravy.

I made this recipe as stated using 1.5-inch bone-in center-cut chops and searing the chops in a cast iron pan on the grill. I removed the chops and made the pan sauce, then added the chops back to the pan, and removed the pan from the grill. I served the chops with polenta and sauteed asparagus. The family loved it and wanted the recipe to go into the keeper file.
Couldn’t find capers at the store so I subbed in some anchovies instead. Tastes AMAZING! An easy recipe that tastes gourmet!
Delicious! And my friend who doesn’t eat pork said she uses this recipe for chicken.

This is one of the best things I have ever cooked. I cooked the pork chops on a cast-iron skillet and didn’t have the need to add the additional 2 tablespoons of butter at the end. I use a cheap Kim Crawford Sauvignon Blanc and homemade chicken stock, and it turned out simply amazing.
Honestly a really lovely recipe and everything turned out perfectly, though I did add a little cream in substitute for the half cup of chicken broth and the sauce turned out so rich and lemony!!
Make sure to use about 1” pork chops, no thicker than that and don’t skimp on the reducing of the broth/wine. Okay to use boneless, just not too thick.

Delicious! My husband asked me to please save this recipe and make it again! I used pork tenderloin, cut in thick medallions. Used the rest of the can of chicken broth plus water to make the accompanying white rice. Spooned the sauce over the pork and rice. Served with Mustard Roasted Carrots from an online Charleston Magazine recipe. So good—every bite!
Delicious citrusy sauce is very nice with the pork chops over rice. Great recipe.
A lot of helpful comments below.
Delicious! Husband & I snarfed! Made as directed but without the hot sauce. May add a little more flour next time to thicken the sauce a bit more. A keeper!

Pork piccata - who knew? The flavors are awesome! Now I always use fairly thick center-cut chops: sear them 3 min per side in an oven-proof skillet and then roast at 375 for about 15 min. While they rest, use the skillet for the sauce.

I made this sauce last night to use with a sous vide pork tenderloin.  It was amazing.  I did make some changes on the fly.  I had a lemon that had been previously zested so I sliced it very thinly and used that instead of lemon juice and zest. I added more garlic and shallot.  I thickened it more as I wanted to use it more like a gravy.  I added the thyme in when I put in the wine and stock.  I will definitely keep this handy!!

Flour pork instead of adding it to pan
Add thyme to sauce not directly on chops as it will burn
You can use dill instead of parsley

Really enjoyed this recipe and the suggestion to throw in some rainbow chard with stems when you add the liquid - it nicely rounded out the meal. Definitely recommend temping the meat before you pull it off the first time - would be nice to have it close to 130/135 before resting since you'll have to reheat it again to get it to 140/145.

I prepared it exactly following the recipe except added the dried thyme to the sauce with the wine and stock (as someone else suggested so the thyme wouldn’t burn when searing). I served it with white rice and a green salad. It was easy and delicious.

I made this delicious recipe with kinda thick boneless chops, like an inch and a half. Came out perfect with added time in the sauce at the end. With thin chops you'd really need to watch out that they don't overcook.

I added a minced aji dulce pepper to the shallot and garlic for some spicy zing.

I had some savory waffles I'd made with cooked rice, chili crisp, sesame oil, and a beaten egg. This turned out to be a great way to sop up the extra sauce.

I made this tonight, and it was delicious. For two of us, I used 2 thick pork chops instead of 4. I made the sauce as specified but just used half the wine and all the chicken broth. There was plenty of sauce. I agree, a little more flour would have helped to thicken the sauce. I didn't have a lot of time, so I decided to put the chops in the sauce to simmer and let it reduce. This is a good recipe for pork chops, and I'll make it again.

One of my biggest challenges in life is following a recipe exactly. This dish was still delicious without the wine, flour and chicken stock. Maybe next time I’ll add those ingredients. I also made (NYT) pan roasted cauliflower (one night, and subbed broccoli another) and sliced tube polenta fried in a Tbs of ghee. Extra lemon and fresh parsley on the meat and the veggies. Everyone raved.

Nice dish but a bit too tangy for me. I would have used less capers ~ 1 tbsp and only 1/2 tsp lemon rhind and skipped the lemon juice. Like other reviewers I used more flour- about 2 tbsp so it made a thicker gravy. I also added 1/4 tsp ground thyme after the white wine reduced. Separately I sauteed mushrooms in a little olive oil and added them at the end prior to reintroducing the pork. Served it with baked potatoes and asparagus. My husband sopped up the gravy with crusty Italian bread!
Flavour was on point, but I would use additional flour next time as the sauce was quite thin. Will definitely make again.
Made this per the recipe but used tomahawk pork chops for the presentation factor and served with roasted fingerling potatoes and homemade applesauce. It was delicious!
I added some sliced baby portobellos to the pan with the capers. Highly recommend!
Heat the pan. Once hot add the oil thenn food. The food won't stick.

The sauce was outstanding. Great recipe and fairly easy to make. 

I didn't have thyme but instead used Mediteranean seasoning (Greek Freek) and turned out well. 

The pork was juicy and the searing really sealed in the juices.
I used thick boneless cuts, and served them on top of mashed red and yellow potatoes with the sauce drizzled over the top. My husband loved it.
"4 bone-in pork chops". no hint what type. There are lots of different types of pork chops: loin, center-cut, Boston butt, etc. They all cook differently.
Bone in Loin or Center cut worked for me and I like the thicker cuts & buy the, on sale or offered as a lead inexpensive item at the more expensive grocery store.
Pork? Pink in the middle??? Never !!!! Pork and fish always well done.
Absolutely delicious.
Beyond delicious. Cut the prep time by preparing the sauce ingredients prior to cooking the pork chops to be on the ready as many recipes fudge on the actual prep time it takes for a non-chef.
Dredge the chops in flour instead of spreading it in the pan. Pan sauce should be done in stages (shallots, wine, stock) instead of pouring into the pan all at once.

@Paulo 
Right you are! Tho the OG review is now 6 years old, the logic and price variable (way cheaper this way) remain the same. I do this all the time. Less $ and you can cut to the thickness you want!

Searing on both sides and then resting and then adding back to the sauce gave me well done pork chops. I tried this recipe again, hard seared on one side, rested while I prepared the sauce, then added the chops back in briefly and it was much better.
Would not change a thing. Wow.
How thick should the chops be? Would 8oz chops be done through at 5 min. per side?

Has anyone substituted for the flour? I find not all options are equally goo.

Thanks
Love everything about this but I 86ed the capers, don't care for them.
Can you use red wine in place of white wine?

Red wine isn’t really right for the clean, fresh, bright flavor profile of this dish.  You can use red wine to make any number of other sauces, if that’s what you prefer, but the butter-lemon-caper flavoring calls for something lighter and crisper.
It will stain the pork and the flavor won't be as subtle. If that doesn't bother you, go ahead.
This looks phenomenal, but I'm not sure what to serve alongside it. Potatoes doesn't sound quite right.  Maybe hot, buttered rice? Any suggestions would be welcome.

I added ribbons of kale to the sauce after the liquid really gets going. Meat and veg in one pan!  Super delicious.   I wondered about potatoes as a side but did not need them.  Maybe a slice of crusty bread at the end to catch the last of the sauce. 

Preserved lemon zest with the parsley, etc added complexity that fresh zest did not.  Mmm.

Rice, cooked as usual (sauté rice in oil, add same quantity of liquid as rice plus salt, bring to a boil, etc.) except: use stock for the liquid and, for each 3/4 cup rice, sauté 1 tsp whole fennel seed in the oil until fragrant first, add rice and sauté as usual, add 1 tsp turmeric and continue to sauté a minute or so more, add liquid and proceed as usual.  Variation, especially to accompany fish: double the fennel and omit the turmeric.
Baked sweet potatoes? Mashed Irish potatoes? Dirty rice? They’d all be good.

Everything but the capers I have cooked however southern fried would have salt and peppered the chops first, then SBA Ken in a paper bag with flower prior to frying chips. All else pretty much the same
A teaspoon of Dijon mustard is a nice touch. You have to whisk it in hard though. It wants to stay separate.
Oh my god, please give us the recipe for braised celery you mention in the article.

I agree with Vanessa, I wondered if there should have been more flour so the dish would be a bit more like a gravy and less like Pork Chops piccata. I would go up with the flour and down a bit with the lemon.
For a gluten free version leave out the flour and wait to thicken the sauce with corn starch until just before serving. This is actually more consistent with the Picatta roots of this dish.

My bone-in pork chops were just a bit over 8oz. each. 5 minutes per side worked perfectly! Nice, pink interiors at the end of cooking. Very tasty and moist. No need to overcook pork these days. Made the recipe exactly as printed...perfect! I am definitely adding this easy recipe to my rotation. Thanks, Sam Sifton!!!
Kevin: see Julia Moskin’s Chicken Francese on this website for a good take on that recipe

Here in Yucatan we get fantastic pork chops, but it is impossible to find shallots or lemons; what we can find is green onions and limes (which are almost costless, by the way).  Do those sound close enough or will the ruin the whole thing?

I would use onions (red or white) instead of shallots. Scallions are not the same at all. As to lemons vs limes, I'm more stumped. Lime juice would taste totally different. Maybe you should try vinegar instead.

This is sublime. I used a pork tenderloin because that’s what I had on hand. I cut it into 2 inch coins and lightly pounded to about 3/4 inch thick. I think next time I might use more shallots, but everything else was just right.  IMHO the hot sauce isn’t optional — I added a few healthy shakes of Tabasco and it was just right.  Can’t wait to try it with bone-in chops.
I’ve been kinda off pork unless I know where it came from, but this is delicious. Gained five pounds overnight!
How thick is this pork chop before cooking? Thanks
As with so many nyt recipes, go with about half of the liquid here.
Excellent. Two farmers market/local/heritage breed pork chops, recipe pretty much as given. Dinner tomorrow will be leftover sauce with remains of a rotisserie chicken cubed in, over pasta.
Ha ha, we too felt the sauce was too delicious to waste so the next day I bought some Chilean sea bass, pre-seared it, sous vide to 137 for 30 min and put the sauce on it.  Delicious.
Fresh thyme can really overpower a dish. It’s the one herb of which I usually prefer the dry version.

I achieved the same flavor as jhernandez reported. We still ate it, and everybody said it was delicious, but that’s because it was a dinner party. I’ve never posted a negative review, but I had a lot of trouble. Chops not browning in the predicted time, sauce not reducing in time, then overall not great tasting. I think the recipe or the dish itself should be adjusted.

Amy you could definitely leave the fat but it will change the flour to fat ratio for the sauce.  Most likely leaving some fat free to float in your sauce or a very thin sauce. You could change your flour and liquid amounts but it helps to know how much fat you are starting with.
Delicious!  My husband made this last night, most certainly a keeper.

I cooked mine in a cast iron skillet and had 1 1/4 inch T-bone chops. To cook these to a golden color and proper internal temperature made the sauce too dark. Still they were delicious. I do prefer this same sauce with boneless skinless chicken thighs. Or definitely use thinner chops than I did.
Why chops with bone -- more flavor?

Not a red meat eater, so made it with boneless chicken thighs. Quite tasty.  I might up the shallots next time. And there’s so much sauce I would definitely suggest making rice or polenta to go with it.
Pork is not considered to be a red meat.
There is a lot of sauce.  We heated it up the next night and added a few tablespoons of chicken stock which made ample sauce for the second time around.
Every time I make pork chops they come out tough. Would you recommend brining the chops in advance to avoid this?

Dry brining (heavily salting meat before hand like Judy Rodgers of Zuni Cafe recommended in her cookbook) would work.  What I find works best is sous vide then sear at the end.  Never overcooked or dry.

I’m not sure what’s uniquely ‘African-American’ about this method of cooking pork chops - this here Jewish white boy has been doing this, with variations on the theme, for the past 30 years or more, without prompting from an ‘ethnic’ cookbook. I do not add minced garlic, but will soften a crushed clove or two in warm oil and remove before searing the chops; I may make less ‘sauce’ and bind it with butter rather than flour; I may use minced fresh sage rather than thyme when in season.
This is a fantastic recipe.  I made it last night and the recipe is perfection.  No need to change a thing.  We had it over a bed of pasta and there was plenty of sauce to flavor it.

I've been making something similar to this for years, but I also melt a few anchovies in the pan sauce, or lay a few over the top of the meat (or both, if I'm feeling it) and melt some cheese over the top. Provolone is good, so is muenster, jarlsberg, etc.

It's all about the lemon. I punted (substituted) for stuff I didn't have (dried thyme, parsley, shallot) and it was still yummy. Similar in technique to a Caribbean recipe using tomatoes and soy sauce, but a wholly different taste.

Loved this as did my family. In particular, the sauce was lovely. I may try this same recipe with another cut of meet as well, at the prodding of my family. It seems like it could go well with chicken or possibly beef, not to suggest that it isn’t anything short of perfection with a good pork chop.
My 13 year old loved this! It was really easy to make, and didn't require a lot of expensive ingredients. Great weeknight dinner.
Excellent

used less wine, more chicken broth.  Takes longer than advertised.

don't add pork chop juice back into the sauce, as it thins it out at the last minute.
Recipe was easy to follow and produced what it was intended.  It was fine, but non of us liked the strong lemony taste as much as I hoped we would.

Delicious sauce, would be good on most meats (except beef) and many fish.  Pork chops (in my case c. 1/2-3/4" thick - c. 8 oz.) were a little touch, despite my adhering to the recipe.  Three to four minutes per side might be better. Also, sauce was a little thin.  Next time I'll try closer to the 2T of flour.

Had the same issues with slightly tough chops and thin sauce. However, I think 2T flour would be over the top. Added a teaspoon of instant flour late in the reduction and the sauce turned out wonderfully. Will go with 1T flour next time which should create the sauce faster as well. . . a great deal of the meat juices drained out while the sauce was making. Will start with a very hot skillet and do just 4 minutes a side next time.

For gluten-free cooks: Brown rice flour works great! I would advise than anyone with a heavy palate double the final spice mixture, and possibly go a little heavier on the garlic. Plenty of Tabasco gives it  a lovely, lingering kick.
We halved the butter and added more capers. Easy and absolutely delicious!

Brined the chops for 2 hours, rinsed and patted dry before cooking. 2tsp flour wasn’t enough to emulsify the sauce. It needed more flour (I used a cornstarch slurry because it was late in the reduction). Really enjoyed this recipe (not just because hubby helped!).
Definitely a keeper recipe.

Followed basically to the letter, except the following:

-I got bone-in lamb chops instead of pork
-I used sherry instead of wine
-I halved the recipe since I only got 1 pound of lamb
-Used fresh thyme instead of dried 

Results were OUTSTANDING. Will definitely be making this at my next dinner party.

My husband and I made this with only two pork chops, but kept the same amount of ingredients for the sauce, and it was fantastic!  We will make this again, but next time, likely won't use the lemon juice - just the zest.  The sauce was just a bit too sour for us.  But even considering that - this was a delicious Sunday night dinner paired with roasted Brussels sprouts and potatoes.

This came out really well. My chops were thick, so after the browning part, I put the skillet in a 400 degree oven for 10 minutes before doing the sauce. They probably were a little more done than they had to be but I didn't have to worry about them being underdone. Still very moist and flavorful. Served with steamed broccoli and baked potatoes. Would definitely make again.

Terrific recipe as is, and the pork chops were perfectly cooked in the indicated 10 minute time.  Personally, we will halve the lemon juice in the future - just a tad too tart.

Thanks to the reader who provided the link to the braised celery recipe which was very good alongside the chops.  Two keeper recipes!
Cooked as per recipe but there seemed to be something missing - did not seem like it needed the optional hot sauce so did not use - maybe that would have perked up sauce

We made tonite, and I kept tasting sauce as it cooked down.  Added salt and pepper and about 10 drops of Cholula hot sauce.  Started with just a few drops, and then added to taste.  Definitely don’t skip the hot sauce.
Wonderful dish! Depth of flavors make it worth the effort!

Cooked as directed, was delicious!  Served with roasted sweet potatoes, the caper lemon sauce worked really well with that.  
Do not, please, do not - leave the oil in the pan.  The sauce is similar to a buerre blanc, use more butter if you need to, but to not drain the cooking oil will make the sauce to oily.  It should be silky and smooth.

I usually alter most recipes a tad, but this one didn't need a thing. I followed the recipe to a tee and it was fabulous! Get everything ready as in "mise en place" and it will come together beautifully. Even a good company dinner.
leftover sauce was pretty good with leftover rotisserie chicken breast chopped, stirred in, then over pasta, with Parmesan.

This came out really well and actually took me approximately the amount of time the recipe said it would. Sometimes I find the flavor of capers too strong, but this was delicious.  

I thought the optional hot sauce seemed an odd addition, but I added some Frank's and it blended in with the rest of the flavors in the dish.

We had the chops with baked potatoes and steamed broccoli.

I had 2 chops & after reading reviews about the sauce being too thin, I adjusted the proportions: 1/4c wine:3/4 broth, but left the 2t flour as written. As a result, I didn’t need to refuse as much which meant the chops stayed warm. I added 1t of Dijon as I love the combination mustard, lemon, & capers with pork. Great recipe and very easy.

The sauce was absolutely delicious.  Only problem was getting it to reduce in a reasonable length of time - 7 to 10 minutes didn't do it.  Once the sauce was reduced and the pork shops "nestled," it took several more minutes for them to warm up.  Although they were very thick, had been cooked minimally and kept warm, they would have been much nicer had they been moister.

This was excellent. Alot of prep of the ingredients but once done it was a snap to put together.  I used thinner chops so I had to adjust cooking time but the finished chops and sauce is a keeper.  I used fresh thyme so I add it near the end with the parsley.

270/5000
I simply replaced the chicken broth with some water and added a small pinch of sugar to the sauce (I've learned that where there's acid, there must be some sugar to top it off). It tasted wonderful. Greetings from the Black Forest.
This was simple , quick and nice  will make again
Inadvertently left the capers out and this was great. Otherwise, made as per the recipe.

Altogether delicious.  I sauteed them for 4 minutes on each side medium-high heat and after I put them back into the sauce, they still needed to cook for a bit!  I just added more stock and kept stirring.
Delicious chops! I would use less liquid in the sauce. Though extremely tasty, there was too much sauce. I did find a use for it on leftover brown rice and green beans.

I used 3 tsp of flour, for the slightest hint of more thickness (I'm used to making more of a gravy), and I'm happy with the viscosity!  I served it with turmeric-tinged jasmine rice, and peas and carrots sautéed in butter.  My kitchen smells amazing!  Thank you for the recipe.

Not enough lemon flavor for me. I agree with other comments in regard to thickness of the sauce. I added 2 Tbl. of flour and it was still a bit thin. Don’t skimp on the “aggressive” salt, pepper and thyme. This recipe needs it, because of the large amount of fluid in the sauce. Try red pepper flakes in place of hot sauce, which really adds some zip. Also increase the amount of lemon juice for more tart, piccata-like flavor. Overall this was a good recipe, a nice change from the usual.

Made this last night but cut recipe in half for empty-nest serving size. It was delicious. Next time I will be sure to make mashed potatoes with it as the sauce is so good it needs a vehicle for eating every drop. Did not use lemon zest as I had a zested lemon I had to use up - didn't use hot sauce either but will try a dash next time for extra Creole flavor. Also did not add extra butter to finish - still "glorious."

This is the first time I have successfully  cooked pork chops in a pan.  I cheated on the cooking time by cutting in to look after five minutes on a side and that was about right.  However in my pan on my electric stove, I had to cook at medium high to get the meat really browned.  Starting with good pork and rather thick chops kept things tender.  Next time, I might brown in a mix of olive oil and butter.  I have a recipe for Greek-style pork tenderloin medallions with a very similar sauce.
I made this as is, but sous vide the pork chop for 3 hours at 135 and then gave it a quick sear. Sauce was really good.  Reduced nicely.  This recipe is a keeper.
I brined it for 8 hours before proceeding with the recipe.  I used a thick supermarket pork chop and turned out moist, juicy and flavorful.
i made it just like the recipe with pure de papas con un touch de ciboulette, really good!!!

I followed the instructions except for substituting 2 pounds of thick mahi mahi filets. 

Twas a big hit next to quinoa with sun dried tomatoes.
What is a recommended accompaniment...?

These were the best chops I have ever eaten. We grew up on chops. Mom was not a cook. They were dry and tasteless. I hated them. Now I don't! Although there is a lot of ingredient they were quite simple to make and we will now have them eat least once a month. I made a recipe card for them. Always at the ready!!!
This looks yummy but I don’t eat red meat. Can I substitute chicken, and what kind would be best? Bone-in breasts?
I used boneless skinless chicken thighs, cut into pieces for speed. Delicious; I also used anchovy paste, yum.

Be very careful when adding the garlic to the oil, it cooks very quickly and you don’t want to burn it. Agree that thicker chops will require longer cooking in the sauce. Trichinosis may not be much to worry about now but red pork is not to my liking. Recipe has great taste.

I followed the recipe precisely with top end pork chops from the local butcher. This recipe turned out an awful tasting, bland, terrible set of chops. Don’t waste good meat on this one - it’s terrible!
I always bring pork chops, pork loins, and chicken breasts first. That way, you don’t have to worry about them being dry.
Delicious and easy especially on a week night. Bone in pork chops definitely have more flavor for my family.
Used boneless chops.  Since there was no much sauce, I served it with quinoa, sliced mushrooms and spinach.  Delicious
I make this often but also add a bit of anchovy paste.  Delicious!

What keeps the chops from drying out in the cooking process described in the recipe?  Just "well browned and cooked through" on each side has been, in my experience, the way to render pork chops dry before eating, regardless of later saucing.  I have turned to a quick browning, both sides, then simmering in a liquid braise to achieve tenderness and juiciness in today's (overly, IMO) lean pork (chops and some other cuts).  Is there a suggestion for me out there?
Made it as written.  Great recipe.  The sauce is fantastic.
Remember to use hot sauce next time

This sauce as written also goes great with pork tenderloin, a cut that is harder to turn to leather. Brown on three sides, cover on low for 10-20 minutes to 150 degrees, let it rise off heat to 155 (or your preferred temperature points.) Transfer, cover, proceed with sauce. Meat thermometer a must. This sauce is ambrosial.

Oh, this was good!  My picky family lapped it up.  Only tweak was mounting the sauce at the end with another 3 - 4 Tbsp butter rolled in flour (lazy Buerre manie), since we like the sauce more velvety and a bit thicker.  Used instant read thermometer to 145 degrees and the chops were perfectly tender.  It’s a keeper - great balance, full flavor, etc.
Seemed like it was missing something, so added a splash of vinegar at the end. Only had Chinkiang on hand but if did the trick! Lots of tasty sauce to pour over side dishes.
Delish. Fine with boneless chops. Good with roasted white sweet potatoes and spinach
pretty darned good

This was absolutely delicious and so easy to make. I used 2 Tablespoons of flour. The sauce came out the consistency of gravy. Next time, I will use only 1 Tablespoon of lemon juice and put in a tad more wine instead. I will also use a hotter hot sauce, as all I had this time was Chihula hot sauce.
yum.  Sauce would be good on a schnitzel

Delicious. But why drain the fat? That's silly. There's not much there and it's tasty. Just leave it be. I cooked the pork on high for a shorter amount of time than suggested, so the chops weren't entirely cooked. That way they finish cooking in the sauce at the end. Also I took Anne's suggestion. I had leek greens leftover so I sliced them and added them after adding the stock and wine. I simmered that for a few minutes with cover on, before taking the cover off to reduce the sauce. So yummy.

3 tbsp of flour, by mistake, made a lovely sauce! Really delicious. I will balance the umami against the lemon with a little caramelized white miso next time - personal preference only. The husband loved it as per the recipe.
Cooked the recipe exactly. My family did enjoy it. I would double the capers next time.

Whoa...that’s a flavorful dish. Accidentally added two tablespoons of flour rather than teaspoons, ended up creating a gravy but was still insanely delicious, the lemon and hot sauce really make it. Also used lamb chops rather than pork. Had a giant bag of salad greens about to turn so I sautéed them on the side and topped the chops with them, and served with some roasted squash. A  decadent fall meal.
Halve the sauce ingredients for two pork chops. I think ½ c of wine and chicken stock would suffice.
I made 1/2 the recipe but added 1/3 cup wine. I also deglazed the very hot pan with the chicken stock so that the wine would not completely evaporate when added to the pan.
Could use a little less lemon and rind for my taste.
I’d like to use chicken instead how long would I cook it?

I made this exactly according to the recipe, except I cut everything in half for two servings. The chops were perfectly cooked, and the sauce was the right consistency with the amount of flour in the recipe after cooking for ten minutes.  Delicious.

Outstanding, simple, and reliable recipe. I did not remove the pork fat before adding butter and flour. Wondra is a great idea. Will use next time. I did not add Tabasco to the sauce in deference to my spice-averse partner. I will add it at the table.

Agree with some of the others that there was an excess of (delicious) sauce, even after letting it reduce quite a bit. Also felt funny to pour out the pork fat and then replace it with butter, so I skipped that step.  I'd start with half as much wine and stock next time. I served it over rice and still couldn't use up all the sauce. But tasty enough!

This is a huge hit with me and my partner! The only thing I wasn’t sure of was if I should add the pork chop juices from the plate back into the sauce when I return the chops to the pan. I did, both times I’ve made them, rather than waste the wonderful juices. I served it with baby Yukon gold potatoes I smashed on the plate. The first time, I cooked this on a weekend and made cornbread in my cast iron frying pan on top of the stove. This makes a restaurant-quality meal. Thanks!!!
