Wow.  Cut everything in half, as we are two.  I did not have excess sauce, unlike other commenters below.  Used Bermuda sherry pepper sauce and a nice Rkatsiteli in the sauce.  Served with roasted broccoli and boiled potatoes.  Will make again.  (And again, and again...)

So delicious.  We cut back on the amount of liquid used, and we only had two pork chops.  So we used 3/4 cup white wine and chicken stock.  And, we didn't use the hot sauce.  
Will make again.
sauce is absolutely delicious.  my chops needed a bit more time so i put them in the oven.  would make this sauce again...
1  1/2 cups chicken stock and 1/2 cup wine  Reduce butter in last step to 1 tbsp.

Great recipe. Needs more flour (4 tsp instead of 2) and chops need to go into sauce for about 10 minutes to tenderize. Also, I added more butter to enrich the sauce. Takes a little longer than 35 mins. More like 55, depending on how adept you are chopping and mincing. Would be nice accompanied by noodles or rice.
I made this recipe with thin bone-in pork chops and it was delicious.  Definitely a keeper.

Savory dish with lots of umami flavors and rich texture. I think I added too much lemon juice so it took longer than directed to make the reduction. Make sure the bone-in pork chops are cooked all the way through. If you find they're a little rare you can put them in a covered dish in over while the sauce is cooking.
Tasty, but next time I will definitely use the always tender pork tenderloin. I would drain the fat as the sauce seemed greasy

I would've liked a thicker pan sauce, so next time I might use a bit more flour to create a stronger roux. That being said, the flavors were delicious. I already love the combo of lemon and capers and didn't even think to pair them with pork, too!
Delicious dish! I made it with boneless pork chops and reduced the amount of cooking time to avoid drying them out. Otherwise I followed the recipe as written.
Thank you for a delicious recipe.  My chops were a bit thick, so after searing I kept them covered in a 250 degree oven while I continued with the sauce.  My family loved this.

Made this with overnight brine from Smothered Pork Chop recipe, to tenderise the meat. Turned out okay but to me, insanely salty --although the pork is very tender. Also agree with writers about the flour - much more is needed, swirled slowly into the sauce and cooked. I added save and rosemary, otherwise sauce is dull. This is the 5th recipe from Sam that...well, wasn't worth the effort.
Did you salt the pork chops as the recipe suggested after you brined them? That might be why they turned out so salty.

This was so easy and delicious! My boyfriend and I didn't have thyme so we chucked some fresh sage in toward the end. We thought it was easy to improvise which is nice for cooking on the weeknight. The sauce was so good that I nearly poured the extra in a glass to drink it. I haven't made many white wine reductions, but this recipe really opened up the idea for me. Thanks Sam!

I served this dish over polenta with spinach. It was going to become a regular on my Sunday night dinner list, but it's pretty quick and easy so I have even made it during the week. My 12-year-old daughter helps by reading a book out loud to me while I cook. She also is very much involved in eating every last bite!
wow - what a disappointment! so many ingredients & steps...so little to show for the effort,
These were fantastic!  We raised our own pork for the first time this year and I wanted a recipe to showcase the pork chops.  This was perfect!

Didn’t realize I was nearly out of wine, so I had to substitute 1/2 cup of mirin — the closest thing I had — and it a turned out a little too sweet for my taste. My overall opinion was that it was fine, but I wasn’t excited about it. I think it deserves another chance, so next time I’ll check the wine bottle before I start cooking, and maybe try one or two of the suggestions I read above — sage v. dill, and Japanese sweet potatoes instead of the Japanese wine! ;)
Delicious as is! Served with cauliflower puree. Sauce was amazing. Quick & easy, will make again!
Double the sauce ingredients. Otherwise delicious. Made with farro and roasted broccoli.
Use chicken breasts, whole but sliced in half lengthwise. Coat with mayo, then sear until lightly browned. Add a few dollops of grainy mustard to sauce. Serve w mashed potatoes.
Use chicken breasts, whole, but cut in half lengthwise. Coat w mayo before searing then brown. Add grainy mustard to the sauce. Mashed potatoes.

I made this tonight.  I used 2x the flour.   The gravy was excellent.   The meat, not so much.  I had a rib pork chop from Whole Foods and brined it for two hours.   Perhaps a better chop would have sealed the deal.   Of course, this would work with chicken, too.

I've made a version of this with with pork tenderloin, chicken breasts, and sole. It's a standard piccata sauce which is supposed to be on the thin side and translucent. It's a reduction, not gravy which happens when you add additional flour as many readers are want to do. The recipe(s) I have used don't call for flour at all unless it's a dusting on the meat/fowl/fish before browning.
What a delicious recipe! We went with the two tablespoons of flour and cooked the sauce way down and had a wonderful gravy. Really nice!
Can the white have a substitution for those allergic to alcohol?
Just use more chicken broth.

I love this recipe! the lemon caper sauce is exceptional. It reminds me of a mustard sauce in color but very different and tasty.   I only cooked 2 pork chops but made the sauce as directed - i put the rest in the freezer.  Trying to think what I could add that to.  I may use it on some asparagus tomorrow.
Tasted Great.  I cooked about 4 minutes per side and used 3 teaspoons ( 1 tablespoon) of flour.  Next time I'd back off a little on the lemon juice.
Would this recipe work with fish? If so, what kind would be best?

Just seasoned chops with salt and pepper to sear (6 mins) and added thyme with liquids later.  Added a couple of tablespoons of flour for thicker sauce.  Needs to finish in sauce at end to bring up to 145.
Fabulous. Used pounded chicken breasts per another reviewer’s suggestion. Halved the recipe as I was cooking for 2. Used a tbsp of flour. Will definitely make again.

I added more shallots. Three shallots, which I sauteed until golden brown. I also added three cloves of crushed garlic, which I also browned. I served the dish over fettuccine noodles with fresh green beans. To make sauce a bit thicker I added some corn starch.

The result was a hit.

This was a big hit at my house. I amped up the butter, garlic, shallots, and lemon zest a bit and generally seasoned heavily, but otherwise followed the recipe pretty closely.  Biggest departure was use of almost 3 tablespoons of flour, which made a nice gravy.  I also used about 2 or 3 teaspoons of crushed red pepper instead of hot sauce. Will definitely make again.

I've made Sam's smothered pork chops recipe several times, and my wife and I both like it.  This recipe is so much quicker, and we love the bright flavors in the sauce.  I served it with brown rice and some braised kale.  This will be my new go-to recipe for pork chops.
This was really good - halfed all
I added fresh ginger along with garlic. And being of Indian origin had to add fresh chopped green chilies instead of hot sauce.
failed to use low sodium stock- it was too salty. still good, but beware
This is fabulous!!!! Just fabulous!!
Could use chicken instead of pork.  For a great lemon caper sauce, just start at Step 2.

I sometimes despair of the seemingly unnecessary fussiness of certain NYT recipes. That's on me; a sorta lazy foodie. Then comes along a recipe like this simple one that was equally popular with my picky 5-year-old grandson & caper-averse paramour (the capers dissolve nicely into the sauce). So I will be more diligent in picking through the daunting recipes & risking disappointment (sausages & grapes, a big fail) for gems like this. Thank you!
Disappointing.  Followed the recipe almost exactly and found the good-quality pork borderline-tough.  The sauce was good, but I would not return to this recipe.

Delicious sauce, wonderful recipe! Love the lemon and capers. Heeding the comments of other posters, I added about 2-1/2 TBS of flour and had a roux on standby. Otherwise followed the recipe as written. I used (white) vermouth for the white wine. My pork chops were thick, at least 1 1/2 inches, so seared them slightly longer than 5 minutes per side.
This recipe is a great building block for an even better dish. Replace butter with bacon and add mushrooms with the spinach. Also, I used red onion instead of shallots.

Great taste - dry chops. My attempt to
Find a recipe for pork chops that are moist. Give up?
@Amy Drain the fat and add butter because it has so much more flavor than olive oil.  Or do what you want.  You are the one eating it.
Sauce was delicious. Would be good with chicken.

Absolutely scrumptious.  I was also hesitant with the amount of liquid, but if you are patient and let it reduce, it thickens and is marvelous.  We had two chops each weighing about 14 ounces and the sauce was just enough if we wanted to put some on the rice or other side dish.  don't know how this works with 4 chops, even at 8 oz each.  a real winner!!
Wonderful recipe! Thank you NYT and Sam Sifton!
Used more flour to make the sauce thicker, maybe 3 tbsp. Don’t get the unsalted butter specification, since you need to use salt to season the pork chops.

I made this with fish, starting straight in on the sauce. I had fresh thyme, which I put in with the alliums. Otherwise followed the recipe, maybe cooking the fish a bit longer than it would take to reheat the chops.

Didn't have the white wine, so I used 1/2 cup of vodka plus more broth and it was fantastic! Subbed scallions for parsley and also added harissa. Used an inch thick of neck instead of chops. Used flour as directed. Wonderful.
Italian parsley or curly?
This recipe is delicious! I made as specified but added 1 extra teaspoon of flour.
The last ingredient listed is “hot sauce.”  What sort are you suggesting? I like the idea of adding a little zip, but don’t want to overpower the other flavors.
What type gluten-free flour would work best as a substitute for all-purpose? Any other change needed if I made this substitution?
Used more flour to make more of a gravy instead of a sauce, and this recipe was perfection. Don’t skip the hot sauce!
This is really good, definitely not difficult and very Tasty!

Interestingly enough, the recipe originally published in Jubilee calls for the chops to be seared stovetop and then finish cooking in a preheated 400 degree oven for 5-7 minutes until the chops register 145 degrees. I did that and then spooned the pan sauce over the chops after letting them rest for a few minutes.
Excellent.  I did add twice as much flour.  I like a thicker sauce.  used a non stick skillet.  Will try cast iron for better browning next time.  Definitely a keeper.  Well liked by all.
This would be hard to mess up. I didn’t drain all the oil after cooking the pork chops, and found I didn’t need the final 2T of butter. I also used more capers.

Made this for my mother's 96th birthday with boneless pork chops and it was fabulous.  We are now slicing most of our meats for serving and find we eat less that way. Will definitely make again and will try with chicken per a comment I read.

The dish was wonderful. I made a full recipe for two people (2 chops), so I had leftover delectable sauce to be re-purposed. I added a large rounded teaspoon of Beaufor whole grain seeded mustard, more fresh zest of a lemon, a tin of wild caught boneless & skinless sardines (Season brand) and a couple of turns of ground black pepper. It was divine with baguette. Yum!
I can't wait to make this again! It was so tasty.
Could I use almond or coconut flour for this recipe?

What a fantastic dish! Made this with two 2-inch thick bone in chops so it took longer to cook but kept an eye on the meat thermometer to avoid overcooking. Will confess that I thought I had capers but actually did not so I had to make do without - just added an extra splash of white wine vinegar as a sub, but I will make again with bc I love the lemon capers combo.

I made this the other night, and while I bought "thin pork chops," they were not that thin. When I make thin pork chops they are tender. These were not. So the next time I make this, I am going to marinate the thicker pork chops in a brine to tenderize them. That would mean that I need to be careful when I add the chicken stock and salt. Does anyone else have tips to make the pork chop tender?  Great recipe!!!! It was great with hot sauce. I am going to buy this cookbook.

This was very good.  I cooked the pork using a sour vide stick and it was the most moist, tender and flavorful pork chops I’ve cooked.  I used shallots & garlic with a tblspoon of olive oil. Cooked for 1:15 at 140.  Seared all 4 sides to 145.  I then cooked the sauce. It was good but not fabulous.  I think I would reduce further than 1/2.

I appreciate a new pork chop recipe.  It will go, occasionally, into the rotation.  Mostly I was pleased at how good it was cooking with sous vide stick.
I made this creamy by changing a half cup of the chicken broth to a half cup of heavy whipping cream. If you end up thinking the sauce is too thick by doing this, add a slight 1/4 cup of white wine.

I've cooked lots of your recipes with very tasty results, but I was very disappointed in this one that produced very little taste lots of time, work, and chopping. Pork is one meat that seems to me is always dependent on some kind of sugar based sauce. The best part of spare ribs, one of my favorite dish, is the barbecue sauce.  So I'm deleting this from my saved recipes. In the future I'm sticking with your veal, lamb, beef, chicken, fish, seafood & your killer eggplant, squash, tomato recipes

Overall this was really good, but I’d skip the hot sauce next time and instead add dijon. Served this with a side of roasted veg and put the chops on top of sautéed cabbage, kale and leeks. Loved having the greens underneath to soak up the sauce.
This was very good. I brined the chops overnight for extra flavor.
Made as described, however used fresh thyme leaves - delicious. Used a few drops of sriracha. Sauce is thin, but excellent accompaniment with rice, noodles, or oven roasted or mashed potatoes.

Very tasty, but I didn't pour out the oil/fat after searing the chops, that seemed like a waste, especially with home-raised pastured pigs.  The chops were just a tad overcooked, next time I'll use higher heat and less time to sear the chops.
This was SPECTACULAR. After supper there were completely empty plates and no leftovers. I followed the recipe exactly, and will do so again!
Very good
Did 4-5 tsp of flour

My 7 & 9 y.o. boys inhaled this meal. They loved the sauce over noodles and the pork chops. We used much thinner pork chops and the meal still came out deliciously. I might increase the sauce portion next time, but that's because my kids love gravy/sauce over noodles. This is a keeper in our house.

Made this ... it was ok. Not sure the chops need 5 mins a side. Maybe I hear was too high but I find pork can dry out quick.... so be sure not to dry ur pork out. If I was to do again I would just brown the pork at the beginning and then let it CONTINUE to cook while it was on the plate and then finish off in the sauce when it says to ‘warm’ 
Wife loved it but I was so so on it.

Dish did not meet my expectations.  Very partial to lemon dishes, thyme is a
favorite and indulge in capers as often as possible.  I'll stick with Clairborne's 
recipe for Herbed Pork Chops (NYT Cook Book, 1961).
On my arbitrary scale of 1 - 10 (10 high), I give this a 6.

Delicious and easy.  I cut the fat by half -- 1 T of olive oil, total of scant 2 T of butter.  Used a large shallot and chopped instead of mincing. Served with roasted red peppers and roasted white sweet potatoes.
4 pork chops- not browned on medium heat at 5 min per side
Fantastic!!!
This was a tasty dish and I would make again.  However, was really just a pork chop piccata.  I used 2 TBSP flour and was happy with the consistency.
This is phenomenal! I was missing a shallot and subbed 1/2 julienned onion; also added a splash of sherry to the sauce while adding the wine. Will be making again!

This has become a staple for us and is very flexible. Two nights ago I roasted a pork tenderloin and used the roasting pan to make the sauce on the stovetop.  Worked beautifully.  Last night I reheated the leftover sauce on the stovetop and poured it over chicken thighs from the night before. Yum.

This is going down as the Best Quarantine Dinner so far in my home!   I followed the directions exactly and it came out AMAZING!!!!  I served it with creamy garlic potatoes and green beans.  The sauce was a great addition to the potatoes.   This is a wonderful comfort dish that I will most definitely make again.
This has become one of our most frequent meals. At least once a month whenever tonkatsu pork is on sale.
Seriously one of the best sauces I have ever made. I nearly licked the plate. Will put leftover sauce on pasta for lunch tomorrow. Filled the recipe exactly except used 4 teaspoons flour.
Husband loved this! Wasn't difficult to do, either! :)

Some have mentioned that pork chops today are lean and void of fat and flavor. I agree except to say that that describes center cut pork chops, which are to be avoided. Chops labeled 'rib loin' are excellent and can be identified by the fatty ribbons and marbling in the meat. And pork butt or picnic shoulders will do also, especially if you can find some boneless and  pre-sliced.
even my daughter liked it and she usually won't get near a caper.

Made this recipe because Instacart delivered some pork chops that I didn't ask for and because it seemed simple enough! I ended up making a ton of substitutions (because COVID has me trying to reduce my grocery runs) but this still turned out pretty good! 
- Use red wine instead of white, which I probably wouldn't recommend because it changed the flavour profile pretty significantly
- Used homemade mushroom broth instead of chicken and tossed the mushrooms into the sauce

Made this last night. I forgot to get capers but honestly, I didnt miss it. I saw a note from another chef about the flour. I added 2 tablespoons and added more wine, stock and lemon juice. I had a lot of gravy which is fine. It paired well with the mushroom and onio risotto that I served with the chops. My family raved about this. Bone in chops are def the way to go. Thank you for this amazing recipe.

This is terrific and make sure you have a good baguette to sop up the sauce. Speaking of which, ditch the flour. The first time I did it, the sauce was too dense. A real chef never uses flour to thicken a sauce but I deferred to the illustrious Sam Sifton. Wrong. Use your whisk, use the heat for the reduction, and emulsify it yourself, no flour. My second effort was sublime. So if you mess up the first time, try it again. And burn the pork at high heat for 3 mins a side, not 5. Superb.
I was out of white wine so I used 1/4 cup of vodka instead. Came out perfect.
I've made this with slices of turkey breast and it's delicious.

We have been trying so hard to eat out of the garden and out of the pantry and freezer...

No porkchops - but I had some fresh lemons, a bottle of capers, and lots of fresh parsley that over wintered. I had a package of boneless skinless chicken thighs - and lots of fresh thyme - also overwintered.

No wine - but used Vodka (recommended by another creative!) and some rice wine vinegar.

Had a huge bag of Costco potatoes - made mashed potatoes served this over the potatoes and yum to the yumth!
The sauce was the best part. I made sure to have a nice loaf of crusty bread to use to soak it up.

Makes WAY too much sauce. Clearly, I wasn't thinking when I made this, because the liquid is enough for a stew to serve 6. Also, I cooked pork in oven at 500 on lowest rack, 5 minutes per side. Preheat a cast iron skillet and oil only the meat, to prevent smoking. I served sauce on side with polenta and roasted veggies. Other comments correct--if you want this to be more of a gravy, add 2 tablespoons fo flour.

I will use extra sauce for a chicken casserole I make with artichokes and chevre.

This is delicious! Pretty easy to do as well. The store had only blade steaks or loin chops. I chose the blade because I find loin to be boring but it would work fine here. I also added about a cup of chopped mushrooms at the same time as the shallot and garlic. Served over mashed potatoes with green beans. It will be in the regular rotation.
Super pork chop recipe, the lemon juice and zest and capers really make it distinctive. I used more thickener and liked the fact that it was more like gravy.

This was good -- I actually sliced a pork tenderloin into pieces about an inch thick instead of the chops as I have the tenderloin on hand.  I love the sauce -- I love capers and the lemon juice and zest gives it such a great fresh flavor.  I sprinkled some hot sauce on mine instead of adding it to the sauce since my husband doesn't like it.  I will make it again!

Kate
To earlier notes, used scant 2 TBS flour vs 2 tsp.  Nice smooth sauce.
Try doing this with chicken breasts.
Do you have a recipe for making this in an Instant Pot?
Made tonight and it was a hit! Halved the recipe but followed suggestions to go tbsp vs tsp on the flour. Worked great. Such a delicious combination of flavors!

I made this for dinner last night and it was wonderful.  I love the wine sauce and capers.  I added a little more lemon juice because the lemon was a large one.  This recipe was phenomenal as written so I saw no reason to make substitutions except as I said I threw the extra lemon juice into it.

Sauce is basic but good - but yes as a previous poster said, let  the meat finish cooking in the sauce! Pork chops are so easy to turn into shoe leather....Just barely brown it on each side and you'll be fine. I did a trial recipe tonight and cooking for company tomorrow....
This was really tasty! I followed recipe - but when I first cooked chips I cooked them under so I could finish cooking in sauce. A huge hit for the whole family.

I’ve been wanting to make this ever since the recipe was first published. The stars aligned and I finally had all the ingredients to give it a go. I thought it was incredibly fussy over all, which I don’t normally mind, but the taste just didn’t deliver. Too much chicken broth maybe, or the strong flavors of the wine, lemon juice, and capers just did not sit well on my pallet with the assertive chicken flavor poured over pork. Everyone else pretty much raves so it’s probably just me.

This is a 10/10 recipe for sure, the gravy was "out of this world" delicious!  Made only the following modifications: white wine was substituted with diluted apple cider vinegar (I added 4 tsp of water to every 2 tsp of vinegar), and used 2 tablespoons of strong white flour. I skipped the lemon juice (added the zest) and hot sauce.

This is really good, but a few notes.  I had thick pork chops so even to get them medium required more cooking time in the sauce-- like 10 minutes.
I agree the 2 tsp of flour was not enough to make the thick sauce I wanted.  I made a slurry of olive oil and flour-- equal parts- and stirred it into the reducing sauce.  It became nice and velvety.

Switch to Pork shoulder (aka pork butt), cut it into 1 inch slices / steaks and cook / grill them with some oil, salt and pepper (or cook them according to this recipe but only 2 minutes per side). You'll never touch pork chops again.
Why on earth would you discard the fat from cooking the pork chops and replace it with butter!? No self-respecting Southern cook would ever do that! You want that porky flavor in your gravy.
Can this be done with chicken breasts? If so, any adjustments?
Very, very good.
i agree with a bit more flour. The sauce needs to be slightly thicker.

This recipe is wonderful. I had only 2 bone-in pork chops (3/4" thick), but made the full sauce recipe exactly as described (no hot sauce). The instructions resulted in moist, perfectly cooked chops. There was a generous amount of sauce, so next time I will halve the recipe if making it just for the two of us.

We did a quicker sear on medium high heat, removed, then finished the bone-in tomahawk chops in the simmering sauce, using a meat thermometer to get to 140. We also added Swiss chard stems as per another cooks comment and liked that. I should’ve removed the stems after the simmer and before finishing the sauce. Otherwise followed recipe and they were delicious. Sautéed Swiss chard leaves with garlic, shallot and lemon zest and farro made a fabulous meal we will certainly make again!

Agree with all the positive comments; this was a hit here last night. 2 chops for 2 people, but left sauce quantities as written (Hot sauce is rarely optional here; half tsp was just right). Sauce risks being too thin and really needs reducing. Added a dab of corn starch slurry to thicken it up some at the end and had enough to go over new red potatoes.  I thought it was a bit too lemony, but She said it was spot on. I'll defer to her! This just got short-listed here as a do again and often

This is my second note.  I prepared this with a pork chop that wasn’t great, though that was no my intent.   Great sauce/bad chop/three stars.   

I prepared this again today.   I had an excellent pork chop.  Five stars.
June 3, 2020 - Delicious. Makes a large amount of sauce.
I combined this wonderful recipe with another that suggests rubbing the chops with a combination of the salt, pepper and fresh thyme. Then sautéing the chops...it adds something.

Based on what other cooks shared, I upped the flour to 2 TABLESPOONS.  I would decrease the chicken broth to only 1 cup the next time I make it. 
Other than that, good and tasty recipe.

Delicious!  I made this and the pork chops turned out perfectly.  The sauce was delicious.  I served it with rosemary french fries and a green salad.  If making again, I would eliminate the olive oil at the beginning, as it can burn if you're not careful.

The sauce was really great.  But the pork chop was tough.  I have never had good luck with pork chops - these were rib chops, so I think I will try this with pork tenderloin and also boneless, skinless chick thighs...the sauce would definitely work for both.  And BTW, I did use the 2 teas of flour and reduced and it was much better than gravy.
I thought this was very good and it can hold for a while if needed.  Sauce is thin so I would use 3 t flour

This recipe is delicious. My only note is I would not necessarily classify the recipe as "easy" since there are more than three ingredients, preparation from start to finish is closer to 45-50 minutes, not including cleaning up (at least if you are also cooking dinner for kids under 7), and it helps to have some experience cooking with a skillet. Overall, totally worth the effort. Thank you, Mr. Sifton.
Very good - I followed a poster’s suggestion and added kale and it was outstanding. Agree that a bit more than 2tsp flour needed.

Wonderful sauce that would also be delicious with other proteins or over pasta. I had to make a couple of minor substitutes due to what I had in the pantry. I used onion instead of a shallot, and fresh rosemary instead of parsley. I took the advice of another reviewer and sliced half the lemon to soak in the sauce. Delicious!

I served with rice and poured the sauce over all to finish. Did not use the hot sauce, but if you do I recommend keeping to something simple and vinegary. My one stray from the path: I did not empty out the grease from the pan before adding more butter and the aromatics. Fatty and delicious!
Brined chops for 72 hours.  Browned both sides, 3 minutes, then finished cooking at 200 in oven, to 135 degrees.  Halved protein, full condiments in sauce.  Topped with cilantro.  Delicious.

Cooked as written. But my 12" (and burner) pan is way too hot for garlic and shallots.  They burned in 15 seconds.  Next time I will add aromatics (garlic, shallots} AND flour with butter at the same time and make the roux so garlic and shallot does not burn. Delicious! My hot burner is too hot for this dish. Will have to turn it down when I make it again. Sauce is thinner than a gravy (to be expected since it's sauce) and absolutely delicious!! Will make again.

This dish was absolutely as good as it sounded... and relatively simple to make. So glad I made it for my daughter when she came to visit. We both loved it! Highly recommended. I want to get Toni Tipton-Martin's Jubilee cookbook- very excited about all the great recipes and the due credit to the chefs within it.

The sauce is delicious, although I did adjust it to have more of a gravy consistency by using 2 Tbl. flour, but I left out the extra butter at the end. Next time I'll try this with pork tenderloin sliced into medallions as I find it very hard to cook a full pork chop properly. Mine are always dry. This would be nice served with mashed potatoes or even noodles to take full advantage of the sauce.
In the interest of heart health I eliminated the butter at the end. Not needed and still delicious.
wish there was a vegetarian option for using this sauce... my mom made a dish with lamb (or mutton) and a caper lemon sauce that was delicious.

So good and so easy. Like some said, I made a revision on the thyme leaves... using fresh, and adding to the sauce before I added the chops. A big hit with brown sugar sweet potatoes and a nice Pinot Grigio.
Had to add much more flour to get a sauce.  Perhaps 2 teaspoons should be 2 tablespoons?  Added capers earlier with good result.  Delicious.
Deelish. I made only two pork chops but the sauce for four, and no sauce was wasted.
Made it with 1/2 in slices of pork tenderloin, sauteed quickly.  It was delicious.
Anything you can sub for capers?

I made this two nights ago and it was delicious - nice restaurant worthy.  I followed the directions and ingredients completely, though I added more lemon zest and lemon than it called for (zested a whole big lemon and used all of it's juice).  I liked it lemony.  I think one of the suggestions of adding a little bit more flour may work to make the sauce thicker - but for us it was perfect.  This would definitely work w/chicken too.

Delicious.
It takes longer to lay out the ingredients then to cook the dish!
Substituted apple cider vinegar (with the mother), equal amount, for the dry white wine.
I had to add a tablespoon of flour in 1/4 c water to thicken the sauce. Otherwise it was easy and delicious. I also did not salt the chops.
Katie S.   To control the salt they call for unsalted butter. If you have salted butter adjust the amount of added salt
Cook this according to the directions.  Do not mess with the recipe, it is a fantastic dish and the chops came out more tender than you could believe possible.
This dish was so delicious. My only mod was two bone in pork chops instead of 4 which meant extra gravy to go with the side of crushed sour cream potatoes we made. Amazing one pan dish!!
I was disappointed with dish it needed double the lemon and flour half the broth and it was just okay not what I expected won't bother to make this again
The recipe is delicious, I reduced the amount of wine and used bone broth. I served the chops with a side of angel hair pasta.

I am with Vanessa that more flour would make this more like gravy, which would take it over the top. You just can't even believe how good this sauce is, although I will use less lemon next time- either omit juice or zest...not sure which.

have to pause here for a moment, in this climate of sensitivity to cultural appropriation and giving props to where they belong, I'm having a hard time seeing this as even close to a Southern American soul food based smothered pork chops with a twist.  Remove the Thyme leaves and take the bones off the chops and you really have Italian or ItalianAmerican PorK made Picatta style.  Does the optional "hot sauce" try to get it where it's being said it comes from?
Loved this recipe. Next time I think I will have mushrooms on the side, or with!
I added a bit of extra flour and butter until I got the consistency I wanted, good veal stock and dry white wine gave it a bit more depth.
After I finish cooking as directed, I put the covered skillet in the oven for 30 or so minutes just to make the chops a little more tender.  And I definitely used the hot sauce.
This misses the most important step. Refrigerate all meat, liberally salted (and seasoned) at least 24 hours before cooking.
What kind of hot sauce do you recommend for this dish?
This was a very tasty sauce.  Will try it with chicken cutlets next.

The pork chop directions cooked them perfectly. However, I also had very thin sauce, like broth. I tried adding a little more flour, but it wasn't successful. Next time I'll use less wine and chicken stock so maybe it'll cook down better and the taste won't be as diluted.

This came out pretty badly. Tough, shoe leather-like pork chop (my fault, didn't tenderize first...) swimming in too strongly lemon flavored wine. Maybe that's the recipe's fault, or my lack of experience? It was like sucking lemons while chewing on my shoe. Yuck.

I am finally learning to read these notes from people who have tried the recipes, because Sam gives not great instructions for more beginner / intermediate cooks. Some of us need directions to boil water!

Your recipes are so amazing...keeps me interested and entertained.  Don’t know how I would have made it without you...pre-Covid we ate out Five nights a week.  Thank you for teaching an old soul new tricks.  Love cooking again!

I pretty much followed directions;I had 2 bone in loin chops about 3/4 " thick and stuck to the measurements. Well, I made more of a gravy then a sauce, I guess. But I have to say there's nothing left except the excess gravy, if that's a problem. Used fresh thyme. Pretty delicious.

This is a great pork dish. I used thick chops so the chops finished cooking in the sauce. Since the thyme could burn on the chops I added some fresh from the garden sprigs to the sauce, and it worked wonderfully.
This is a go-to dish.

10/3/20. Awesome!!  Karen said it’s a keeper. We each had 2nds. 1.5x the recipe. 6 thick, bone-in chops. No parsley, but otherwise, pretty much exact to recipe. Sauce was fantastic, capers and lemon worked well together. Served with mashed potatoes.
Made this on Sunday. I didn’t have dry white wine and the shallot, but it worked for me. Yukon gold mashed potatoes, sautéed collard greens and steamed carrots made for a very enjoyable meal.

Made exactly as written and it was fantastic.  My personal rule is to first make, ad written, no alterations.  Ok to try variations after first try.  No need to try variations.  I doubt we could improve on this.  Served with brown rice.  The sauce was delectable.
Best pork chops recipe ever. I made it exactly as written but used plant based butter (I avoid dairy). Used pork loin chops. So amazing. I'll make this over and over again.

Delicious!  Used one pound of pork tenderloin sliced into 1/2 inch medallions. Added 1/4 tsp aleppo pepper to seasonings.  Cooked 2-3 minutes on each side. For sauce- halved the butter, flour, stock, wine and capers.  Used shallot, garlic, lemon parts and parsely as written. Served with rosemary lemon orzo.
I made this tonight.  It was absolutely fabulous.  The flavors are subtle, complex, and delicious.  Highly recommended.
Excellent
I cooked this carefully to the recipe and served it over Florence Fabricant's Warm Bread Salad.  Delicious in summer, but I expect it will be even better mid-winter.
This was easy and wonderful, truly gourmet. I did not use hot sauce and I am glad i did not.
Brine the Pork Chops in Salt Sugar Water for 30 mins prior. Makes huge difference
Will GF flour work in place of AP flour?
Yes, it will.  I often use GF to replace AP.
We had pork tenderloin on hand so I sliced it about as thick as chops would be. Cooked faster -about 2 minutes per side- but otherwise the same. This was a hit.
How long do capers keep in fridge?
I haven’t ever commented on any of the many recipes I have cooked from NYT’s cooking... that being said, this is a delicious recipe and I could eat the sauce with a spoon. YUM

I made exactly as instructed.  This was a hit for my family (an 11 and a 13 year old included). Served with Israeli couscous. I had a higher sauce yield than I needed, which is better than not enough!

In response to Vanessa: 2 teaspoons create a sauce. 2 tablespoons (in conjunction with 1 1/2 cups of stock and 1 cup of white wine would create more of a gravy.
315 This is helpful

Vanessa1 year ago
Wondering if it should be 2 Tablespoons of flour. The sauce is really thin when the directions are followed.

What a delicious sauce this is! We were spooning it over the sweet potatoes & peas! Halved recipe for two, took advice to add thyme with garlic & shallot instead of when searing chops. Definitely a winner.
I'd make the sauce a roux next time, it was too thin. I also concur with another commentor that 10 minutes isn't enough cooking time for the chops.

I make this recipe a lot, and using only two chops, as it's just me and the missus.  It works just fine.  I really like the sauce, so I extend it with some extra stock and a touch more flour, and when I don't have white wine, I use some white wine vinegar and more stock to equal the liquid.  This recipe has yet to fail.
This was delicious. I made the mistake of not putting flour in and cooking it, but added it in time to thicken the sauce. I would cook this again.
Lovely flavors, quick and easy. We didn’t have shallots or parsley. Seemed fine without. Only had boneless chops, also worked fine. The bit of hot sauce was a nice touch. Thanks NYT!

It might be wise to add the lemon juice carefully and adjust the taste to your own likings - and that of your family. It quickly gives a quite sour note which not everybody might like. Otherwise it is an easy and interesting recipe.

I made this and it was divine. I was unable to find bone in pork chops and didn't want to go to a bunch of store (hello, Covid) so I used boneless pork chops instead, and was extra careful not to overcook them. We had them with Pioneer Woman's smashed potatoes and steamed green beans. Perfecto. We don't really cook pork much in our home, mostly because I don't think I do it justice, but I think this recipe is a keeper!
Can I brine these before cooking?
Was still grilling weather last week so the chops were grilled and the sauce made from scratch in a sautée pan. Excellent. Leftover sauce was also great on grilled fish the next day.

This took an hour including prep work. Next time, I'll prepare the lemon, chop the parsley and the shallots hours before cooking. Using 2 tablespoons of flour gave the sauce more body. Be careful with the lemon juice. Add it slowly as this dish can quickly become too acidic. I was pleased with the results.

With thick chops they will take longer to cook through. I'd recommend that you brown them at least 7-8 min. per side for 3/4" chops. 
I made these last night and I have to say. . . they were spectacular !  My husband isn't fond of capers so I decreased the amount to 1 Tbsp. and increased the lemon zest to 2 tsp. and they were soooooo good. The gravy is wonderful and the seasoning is perfect.

Added dried thyme with capers per reader’s note; dry sherry for wht wine cuz that’s only wht  wine on hand.  Chops were very thick and needed more time.  Put rarer part with sauce in pan in 350 oven for another 10 min.  Absolutely delicious.  Chops moist; sauce flavorful.  Big yum...thank you Sam and those who left notes.

Made this for the second time tonight. Served with rice and brocolli. I used 2 tablespoons of flour this time for a thicker sauce. I did not drain the fat off. Lovely meal. Great leftovers for my sweetie to take for lunch.

Reduce to 1/2-1 tablespoon of lemon juice. Increase to 4 teaspoons.

Great dish and family loved it!!
this is excellent.  instead of hot sauce i used gojujang which gave it a lovely hint of heat.  2 t flour was just perfect.
Omît the lemon and capers

This was fantastic. Cooked for a friend who is lactose intolerant, used Ghee instead of butter was pefect. Doubled the garlic, used a large shallot, used double the flour as it was too thin with only what the recipr called for. We licked the pan! Will definitely make this again. Easy and a crowd pleaser....

This is nice - cooking time was perfect for my size chops. Important to reduce liquids enough 7-10 or maybe longer. Adding butter at end smooths it out and adds gloss. If you don't add butter it will be more of an au jus. Add more flour for a gravy, continuing to stir over heat to thicken and cook off flour. As an alternative, try Veal Chops Beau Séjour on this website. It's easier and I prefer it. You can sub pork for veal. Also try with sage as  suggested in Cooking Notes.
The sauce is better without the flour, a little runnier.

I am learning to cook pork chops in the oven, making them much more tender, essentially browning over med. high heat then cooking in a hot oven until 165 degrees just reached.  Want to experiment that process with this recipe.

I have made this dish twice.  First time I substituted marsala for the white wine. Most whites don't add much flavor.  I served this with roasted Yukon gold potatoes. It was good, but still a little bland. Last night I doubled the garlic and shallot and served the fish with brown rice to soak up the sauce.  Big improvement!
Since I have trouble finding tender pork chops— they seem to come tough and fibrous these days— I will try this recipe with pork medallions, cut from a pork tenderloin, instead of chops.
Too much sauce left over.  I should have made mashed potatoes instead of potatoes au gratin.
I do not use alcohol in my cooking. What should I substitute for the white whine?
I made this with chicken thighs and it was even more delicious than with pork chops!  I double all of the ingredients except for the meat in order to have plenty of that incredible sauce.
Make for dinner tonight, just amazing, thank you so much.
I would add more lemon and reduce flour. Made with thick, double cut rib chops so placed in oven to finish after searing in skillet.
What would be a good side dish to pair with this? My brain says maybe brussel sprout leaves fried up or like a polenta?

I used a cup of chicken broth with 2 healthy tablespoons of white wine vinegar-out of wine.  Was a delicious substitute, white wine brings a brightness to the dish that I am certain is even better.  Used chix thighs.  Used the sauce to top baked potatoes-yum
Drain the pork fat after cooking the chops and then add butter to the pan? Pork-flavored fat sacrificed? NO WAY!
This was delish. I had a pork lion that i cut into 6 pieces instead of bone in, and used coconut oil and tapioca starch instead of flour to keep on the paleo  side of things. Will make this again!
Used pork loin instead of chops, 2 tbsp of flour instead of 2 tsp. Delicious!!
Delicious. I will definitely make it again.

I made this (almost) as directed last night, and it was great!  I didn't drain the fat, and instead I halved the butter.  I also followed the suggestions and added the thyme AFTER I seared the chops. As the sauce simmered, I added a bit of Wondra to thicken it.  I served it over spaghetti.  Quick, simple, based on ingredients I always have on hand: it's definitely in the rotation.  Next time, I'm adding some sun-dried tomatoes.
Excellent!  I had boneless port chops so used them.  Also, since we don't use alcohol, I doubled the chicken stock and added some apple cider vinegar to it.   A real keeper and easy to make.
Make less sauce or reduce longer especially when cutting the recipe in half.

Isn’t this essentially pork piccata with hot sauce optionally added? I cooked it and enjoyed it with some wilted spinach in chili oil and rice. But as I was preparing it I thought—sauté off some veal or chicken instead of pork chops and you’ve got piccata.

Cooked today for lunch - definitely a keeper!  The only change I made was to use fresh lemon thyme from the garden, so I tripled the amount of thyme - used some sprigs for color on top of the cooked pork.  Delicious!  Thank you Sam for a very easy and tasty recipe.
Amazing!!

While I find this an amazingly flavorful sauce, i only use about 1/2 the butter and a little olive oil in the sauce.  A little less over the top rich and sti;ll amazing.  Also, the sause freezes well, so when I do make it I double the recipe and frezze some in small containers.  Also great on chicken and fish!
Fabulous recipe!  The only change I made was to use 1 cup of stock instead of 1 1/2.
Sauce would also be good on Chicken and white Fish like Cod or Flounder.

We cooked the chops until they reach to 160 which I think we should’ve done. It made them quite tough although that is the temperature. I think we should’ve stopped a little bit before then. It’s a lot of work this recipe and a lot of mess and it didn’t turn out that great
It's a lot of work for a weeknight dinner, but so very delicious!
Make full amount of sauce for 2 pork chops.

Other than mistaking the 2 tsp for 2 Tbl of flour, this was still quite good.  Didn't realize my mistake until the morning after, re-reading the directions - a nice reason to make it again, the right way.  When we return to entertaining, I would consider serving this dish without hesitation.
The sauce did not thicken up as I had hoped. I believe it needed more flour.
what have people served with this?
Absolutely delicious!
What can I sub for the wine?
More chicken stock.
I crave this recipe - it’s currently a highly anticipated part of the weekly rotation.

The pork chops were tougher than I would like. Brining might help.
Sauce really needs to be reduced significantly for flavor. The drippings from the chops are essential.

I chose to brine the chops in kosher salt, water, a few peppercorns, a clove of garlic and a bay leaf for about 4 hours before cooking them. So I did not salt and pepper the chops before seering them. Brining them did work to tenderize them. I used too much salt in the brine which made the chops too salted when served. I'll still go the brine route, but cut back significantly on the amount of salt in the brine.

The ingredients in the recipe were awesome.
To the person that recommended adding Gochujang for hot sauce, THANK YOU. I keep this on hand for my other favorite recipe, Fire Chicken. It was perfect.

Too lemony for my taste (and I like lemon). I’d probably try half as much if I make it again. Other than that, a good recipe. I had thick chops and they cooked nicely. I added a bit of cornstarch during simmering/reduction because I could tell the sauce wasn’t thickening - maybe 3 big pinches - which helped. Good but just too lemony.

Actually took closer to an hour. Chops were about 3/4 inch thick but weighed 8 oz each. Perhaps slightly over cooked because they were thin. I prefer my pork pink. These were not but certainly not tough and dried out. Maybe sear on a hotter flame at the beginning. Gravy thickened nicely. Because we were only cooking for two used half as much stock and wine but the same amount of butter and flour which may be the explanation. 1/23/2021

I really liked this easier method of a “smothered” pork chop. I was intrigued by the flavors of capers and lemon, but in practice, I didn’t love it. It tastes ok, but I tasted the gravy before added the lemon and capers and much preferred that flavor. 

My rec? Warm the capers and lemon juice together on their own and add it on top when plating, you’ll be able to control the flavor a bit more and those who’d prefer the gravy without can have it that way.
Don’t let the pork sit that long! Cook the sauce independent of the pork. Otherwise pork gets way overdone. Cook sauce first, then pork.
Delicious, easy, impressive, flavorful, fabulous!  Will definitely make again!  Made as is except for the parsley, which I didn't have on hand.
Amazing recipe!  Don't change anything!
SO GOOD. Loved the flavors. The wine and the capers really make the flavors

This was a big hit.  But I do agree that it is better with two tablespoons of flour.   The sauce is great but if it's gravy-like it adheres better to the chops and any other accompaniment.  We had small yellow potatoes which soaked up the sauce.  It's a fairly simple preparation.  Will definitely make this again.
The sauce had good flavor.
I did not love this dish and didn't think lemon and caper goes well with pork, seems more suited to fish and chicken.

Made this tonight, with boneless pork chops, about 1.5" thick. Read the comments and made the following changes: Drained most of the fat after cooking the pork. I wanted a slightly thicker sauce, so I did use 1.5 tbsp of flour to make it a little "gravier", but it would be fine if you follow the recipe for a thinner sauce. I subbed fresh rosemary for dried thyme because I just love rosemary, but I agree with the other comment of adding the herbs when you put in the garlic & shallots.
Made these tonight—delicious! Followed the recipe exactly, except cooked them about 7 minutes each side. They were perfect!

made 2 pork chops and adjusted ingredients
by half, but since I used 1 TBS for butter with shallots etc, used 1 TBS of flour.  We don't care for zest so used a little extra lemon juice..
It was tender and excellent.
I made this with pork loins and it still turned out excellently!

I also thought 2 tsp was not enough.  However, by cooking the sauce a little longer it did thicken nicely.  I did the sauce ahead of time and had it ready to add to the pork.  Yes, the sauce is delicious and was a great compliment to roasted white sweet potatoes and broccoli.
Is there a non-alcoholic substitution for the wine?

I may have been slow to find and make this recipe but count me for singing its praises.  Delicious.  I may have added too much flour (more towards 2Tbs.) but it resulted in a delightfully silky sauce.  yum.  I plan to make this again with chicken as the bone-in pork chops didn't have that much meat on them!!
I make this regularly. One of our favorites. Leftover sauce can be used on vegies, chicken, salmon.  Yummy!
Flour sauces are opaque and tend to be gummy--I've used arrowroot dissolved in a bit of cold water or even wine very successfully--recipie also adapts easily to fish filets as well--
Has anyone tried this with preserved lemons?

This sounds so much like a recipe my mother used to make with veal chops, when veal chops were cheap, that came off the label of a can of Del Monte peas. The peas were also included in the finished dish. I tried for years and years to find that recipe, Del Monte denied any knowledge of it, all my aunts, now sadly gone, made it but no one wrote it down. I can’t wait to try it! We loved it even as kids!
This was really flavorful, but the sauce was too runny.  Perhaps I needed to reduce it farther, but I believe 1 tbsp of flour would have been better.  Definitely will make this again.
Followed the recipe.  Brined the chops.  They came out tough as shoe leather.  Is it the meat or is it me?  Ideas?
Excellent. Next time I will double the aromatics (garlic, shallots) and the sauce. I also added a bit of fennel because I love it, it added a new dimension. A can't miss recipe.
I used thick-cut boneless chops and cooked them 6 minutes per side. It came out deliciously.
Good enough to repeat

Soooo tasty! Loved this recipe, the sauce reminded me of chicken piccata but elevated. Substituted the flour for corn starch (family member with gluten allergy) and added an extra teaspoon to thicken it up. Also did not remove the pork fat after cooking the chops in the pan. I'm from the south and love my meat juice.
Made this exactly as written and it was delicious! Restaurant quality. Family loved it.
Made as per recipe. This is so good! Served with asparagus. This is going on the regular rotation for sure!

I followed the recipe precisely, as I usually do the first time I try one out.  The idea is good, but for my taste it came out too salty and too sour.  But we liked it enough to try it again.  So next time I'll salt much less "aggressively" to begin with and add more later if necessary.  I would also eliminate the wine, or reduce the quantity because, in combination with the lemon juice and zest it makes the sauce too acid and sour.
Didn’t add parsley or hot sauce, was great but don’t overlook chops at beginning. Stick to recipe. Use cast iron.
Family loved this.  Delicious sauce.

This was delicious.  Just cooked two pork chops but used full amount of sauce.  Will use left overs for chicken tomorrow.  Per other reviewers:  Lightly dusted  (one inch thick) chops with flour and seared on high heat for 2 1/2 minutes per side (light pink and moist), didn't drain the fat (very little), added fresh thyme to wine and stock. Used 2 T flour for sauce - bit lemony but fine with us.  Also put in chili pepper flakes. It's a keeper.

Made this for the second time, and it was just as good as the first. I followed the directions nearly exactly, adding additional wine, stock, flour, and butter to increase the amount of sauce. Delicious!
Good sauce, but not a great way to cook bone in chips.  I have better success roasting or grilling. Those approaches lead to better texture, more fat cooks away.
This was easy, but takes a bit of attention. Also, the important part about removing the grease from the pan is that it cools down the pan which is important.
Solid recipe for a week night. This is one of my go to recipes.

Used boneless chops because that's what I had. Otherwise, followed the recipe as written. This is a really, really delicious dish. The sauce was on the thin side, but I don't think it would have been enhanced by more flour, as some speculated. It was perfectly balanced. I served it with the Potato Roast from the Smitten Kitchen website, and asparagus from my husband's garden.  Everyone loved it. Not a speedy recipe - more suited to a weekend, I think. Loved it.
Did not have dried or fresh thyme on hand so used Herbes de Provence with great results. Very tasty dish and goes well with mashed potatoes to soak up the sauce.
Really delicious. I used only 2 tablespoons of butter, I wanted to make the dish a bit lighter, I’m sure that it would be wonderful either way.
Bet this would be just as good with a veal chop.

The sauce is “diveen” but store pork chops? Yecch! Simmered-in-the-sauce thin scallopini actually do better. You can slam ‘em on high for a very short time to brown. Tender & definitely cooked enough once simmered. I’ve a bit of an issue with the gent who says “cook to 143-145°F.” The trichinosis safe temp is 141°F. Home kitchens (even with the most expensive instant-read thermometer) are just too imprecise - meat thickness/bone, pan size, burner BTUs, etc. - to chance it. Fresh thyme elevates.
Next time use more flour to thicken sauce. Maybe use hot sauce

omitted lemon zest, less lemon juice maybe; more garlic. lo salt broth would have been a good idea, but didn't have it. No parsley, didn't have any.  Dry white wine--all I had was a dry champagne with no other purpose; works just fine.  Definitely will do again.
We are lucky in having a source of pure-bred Hampshire hogs' pork through our local farmers' market.  They are wonderful with very simple preparation; they stood up to this highly-flavored one.
Where do I find nutritional information on this recipe?

I like to use dried tarragon in lieu of thyme. For cooking the chops, try searing on really high heat one side in a preheated, scorching hot cast iron pan, then flipping them and finishing in a 400-degree oven. The cast iron helps the chops brown to a beautiful dark gold and the oven is less messy than stovetop.
Brine the pork chops!!!

This was phenomenal! My sauce was still seemed too thin- I added 1 tsp cornstarch to thicken and the consistency was perfect for us. Served with mushroom rice pilaf and the cauliflower gratin side.  I will definitely make this again.

a keeper!  made for one chop, reduced amounts but used a good amount of onion and garlic, no wine, watery chicken stock to make it go a little more, bit of flour, no zest just lemon juice.  forgiving and easy recipe and very tasty sauce (with pig raised nearby, that always helps).

This was perfect. I had a pair of delicious butcher pork chops, nearly a pint of frozen pork bone broth in the freezer, lemons from the tree, and capers in the fridge. No shallot so i used a bit more garlic. There was plenty of delicious sauce, I did use 2 T of four rather than 2 tsp.

Really tasty. Made only two pork chops instead of four but kept the written measurements for the sauce. It made a LOT of sauce. I cut out some of the butter (about 2 TB) and the sauce was still really yummy. Had to sub a little white wine vinegar and water because I didn’t have enough wine. Still turned out great.

Made with two pork chops instead of 4, but kept the sauce measurements as written. Lots of extra sauce leftover which I might use with some chicken or something later on. I used kosher salt because I don't have sea salt right now and it was a tad too salty, would reduce to 3/4 teaspoon in the future if I need to do this again. Had to sub a little white wine vinegar and water because I only had about half the needed wine. Still good! Made with cauliflower mash and broccoli. super yummy.
As written, the pork chops would be overcooked. In the browning step, I did not cook them until done. They finished cooking in the sauce.

Used 3 regular sized bone in pork chops. Cooked 3 min each side at the beginning and it was still too much. Would probably do 2 1/2 each side and then simmer them in the sauce at the end. 

Also, sauce was really thin - needed to thicken it with about 2tbs corn starch in a slurry. 

Served with green beans and Costco instant Jasmine rice.
My chops were small and this made way too much sauce. I should have adjusted. I didn't have parsley; didn't miss it.
Maybe a minute less
Sauce isn't thin, and there isn't too much of it, if you follow directions and use common sense.  This is a wonderful recipe, and is going into my regular rotation.
Really delicious. I'd use this for other Piccata sauce recipes. I did cook the sauce down for quite a while so it was quite a bit thickened and luxurious.
Meh just deleted recipe. Too little bang for the effort albeit limited. Barely pedestrian.
Turned out great. I prefer it saucy as is, but I understand why others like it reduced. Grits made a great side to take in the sauce.

I used this sauce on grill pan chicken breast really brightened up the meal. In fact enjoying the remaining sauce at this writing on left over baked cod and couscous. Will def try on pork chops as directed in recipe!
Coked this tonight and it was a hit, took about 15 minutes med/high to reduce where I wanted it, next time I'll probably just go with a 14 oz can of low sodium stock instead of opening two cans.
We both liked this dish, served with mash potatoes. Bread would have been good in lieu of potato
Winner
This was a delicious recipe.
Amazing!!

Made this for the first time last night. As always, I make it to the recipe the first time and modify later if needed. It was a hit and quickly declared a “make again meal” by the family. Served with roasted baby red potatoes and salad. Looking forward to trying with chicken. 

Note, the pork chops we had were quite thick, so cooking time was increased for them to come to 145°.

This was fabulous! Followed the recipe except used zest of Buddha's Hand since I had that at home and it has become the theme of today and tomorrow's menu. I cooked the chops to 140 degrees in the pan. That was a bit too much. Next time I need to stop them at 135 or so and let resting do its job.
Delicious. followed the recipe as is. will make again; only next time I will double the sauce!

Cooking time of chops varies. I had two thick dry aged pork chops - seared until brown on both sides and then finished on a sheet pan in the oven at 400 until 130. No more. But if they’d been thinner I wouldn’t have bothered with the oven. Love all that sauce. A little bit of flour and reduction worked fine.
Superb
Tasted like something we'd get in one of our favorite restaurants. The sauce was simple and made it so good.

Instead of the usual fried pork chops with onions I saw this recipe, My husband really liked them this way.  I only fixed 2 chops, so I had sauce left over and used it the next night with chicken. I could see this sauce with salmon also.

This was delicious.  I did a dry salt bring of the pork chops the day before so they would be nice and seasoned.  They turned out juicy and perfect.  I coupled this with roasted potatoes with garlic and lemon and then made some pan roasted carrots with rosemary, honey, and butter.  So good.  Will make this again.
Excellent. Used half the amount of butter
This is really good.  I simmered the pork chops in the sauce while it was cooking, to make sure they were really done.  Also, I used a small onion instead of the shallot.  I served it over rice.

I agree with other comments that the sauce was extremely liquidy. I followed the directions to a T and it came out like soup. I also didn’t find the flavors that sophisticated or compelling. Major pass for me.
Made with swordfish and 1/2 cup less broth-delicious and still plenty of broth.
Excellent. Cook chops a couple minutes less than indicated. Use a little more flour for a slightly thickened sauce.

Sauce was great but '5 minutes on each side' was way too long. My chops, which were pretty thick and slightly more than 8 oz. were overcooked. I agree with comments encouraging 1-2 minutes on each side in the beginning, then cook longer in the sauce while taking temp with a thermometer.
Used boneless pork chop and cut each into two. Added cut up grape tomatoes because I had them. Came out great

Made this recipe as written, but used thick boneless chops (cooked to about 135°), because that's what I had. It was amazingly good. Served it with a vegetable tian (baked zuchhini, tomato and potato w/onions), and crusty bread. We couldn't stop ourselves from sopping up the sauce with the bread, so there was not as much left over as I'd thought there'd be.
I decided not to season "aggressively" and went about it nice, easy and calm. Even complimented the chops! All came out awesome anyway and no one got hurt.
Followed almost exactly (took more time to reduce sauce) but loved!  So flavorful, colorful and delicious!

We made this but didn’t put in the wine. W/O chicken stock,I drained broth off of a large tin of canned chicken for making the sauce. My husband used the thermometer and the pork was just perfectly cooked. I used teaspoons of flour, not tablespoons which kept the sauce light. We didn’t have all the herbs the recipes called for, but I had fresh sage and basil. When seasoning the pork before cooking, we “painted” the pork with a little olive oil, and used Rufus Teague Meat Rub. Delish!
Delicious! Forgot the thyme and it was still perfect. As always, thickened with gfjules gluten-free flour; thickened beautifully.

Can be made ahead of time. Sear chops first then make the sauce, adding more chopped shallot and reducing about a half. When ready to serve put the chops in, turn heat to medium low and braise until the chops are warmed through. The chops were roughly more than half a pound each and one and a half inches thick. I added a pinch of cayenne and paprika, also putting in the thyme as I was frying the shallots.

Excellent! Bye bye dry, boring pork chops! A beautiful American melange: The sauce that makes this dish so successful is French in its conception but Creole, Cajun and African American when it comes to the ingredients. Since I'm on a diet right now that sadly rules out alcohol for the first month, I used four tablespoons of white wine vinegar instead and topped it up with some extra chicken stock to make it a cup, and it worked just fine. Can't wait to try it with a nice white wine or a pinot.
These pork chops were really delicious. Very tender and the sauce was divine !  Also easy! Who could ask for more?

Then I pounded the boneless pork chops, seared them at a high temp on both sides and poured the sauce over, getting a good sizzle. Really nice.  Next time, I will pound them to scallopini thickness.

4 to 5 stars for me. Not a meal that is special but it does taste really good with straightforward and easy preparation. Straightforward and easy is good for an extra star. I did not read the notes beforehand but followed the directions and it came out perfect.

Of late, I have been brining pork chops or fish whenever they are to be grilled or sautéed. There are no worries about dryness at all. Two TB salt (plus brown sugar and other aromatics) dissolved in cool water, add chops and refrigerate for a couple of hours or overnight, pat dry and sauté. Fish only gets brined for 30 minutes. You can use dry white Vermouth instead of having white wine open (I am not a white wine fan) and use cornstarch to make it gluten free.

Made this with finely grated lemon zest first mixed with 1 cup sugar to transfer the essential oils to it - and 3/4 cup (6 oz) soda. Absolutely delicious! Didn't miss the other 1.25 cups of sugar at all. Never be afraid to try less sugar, since most modern recipes have too much to start.

Using just unmelted butter to prep the bundt pan may not work for the cake to come out neatly (and I have the same pan that the cake in the picture was baked in).  Coating the inside of the pan as a bond-breaker makes a big difference.  I use Baker's Joy and then add a light coating of almond flour, which works very well.   There are other methods and a fuller explanation on the King Arthur Flour website which you can find by Googling "How to prevent bundt cakes from sticking".

Jackson, Miss. is where I got my recipe too, but it wasn't the Symphony League...it was the yellow Bell's Best Cookbook...published by Bell Telephone Company Pioneers (bought when & where you paid your bill!) and source of some of all the well-traded recipes for suppers. They were de rigueur wedding gifts! 7-up pound cake was the first I learned to bake and I can still recite the recipe, even from a dead sleep. Thanks for the memory, I think I'll pull out the Kitchenaid and cream some butter!
Just want to say that Sprite is not a comparable substitute for 7up in this recipe.

I used this recipe but I don't use oil of any kind just unsalted butter.  I have noticed the absent of salt in the cake.  My next one I will use salted butter.  Three cup of sugar does make very sweet and I thought about 2 1/2 cups of sugar.  Slice thinly as possible.
For those asking about reduced sugar---Mark Bittman once wrote that you should not reduce the sugar in a recipe by more than a third. I follow this as general rule and I have not had any problems.

That rule should not apply to recipes with too much sugar to begin with - as more modern recipes have due to a growing sugar addiction (yep, a real thing). Like a recipe for apple pie with 2 cups of sugar instead of a more healthy, tastier 1/2 cup.

I always cut sugar by 50% to *start*in any recipe - and can often lower the amount further in subsequent tries. Moistness - if that's an issue - can usually be added back via an exta egg yolk or fat. Big plus - the other flavors shine through!

Because I live at 7000', I have to reduce the sugar in cakes (decreased air pressure results in evaporation / concentration of sugar and affects a cake's structure) and I have never thought the end result wasn't sweet enough. 

Most American baked goods are too sweet for my taste. This recipe has way more sugar than it needs.

To Randy:
Adjustment for 7000+ feet:

Reduce baking powder: for each teaspoon, decrease 1/4 teaspoon.
Reduce sugar: for each cup, decrease 1 to 3 tablespoons.
Increase liquid: for each cup, add 3 to 4 tablespoons.
Increase oven temperature by 25 degrees F.

Very easy to make; came out moist, light, and delicious. A winner! I subbed:
*Ginger beer for the 7Up (it's what I had in the house);
*Salted butter for the unsalted; and
*530g sugar for the 600g specified.
Also, due to bitter experience, I greased AND floured the Bundt pan liberally before even thinking about adding any batter to it. Buttering alone is a fool's errand with a Bundt.
I used 225 grams of melted salted butter and mix it with 225 grams sugar, then mix in 225 grams of flour and only 4 eggs added. One cup of 7UP and it is awesome!

Most recipes for bundt cake will work if split into two loaf pans. For this, I'd use loaf pans that are 8X4X2.5, or 8.5X4.5X2.5. Fill no more than 2/3 full, and if there is batter left over and you don't want to toss it, you can make a muffin or two. Here is a good resources for cake pan conversions: https://thebakingpan.com/baking-pan-sizes/. You may need less baking time for loaf pans.
I made this using raspberry lemon San pellegrino and it was wonderful.

a 9x13 pan or 2 9 inch round pans.  watch your baking time

wondering if it would work with 2cups and a bit of lemon zest...?
Maybe do as the variation the writer suggests: club soda and lemon juice in place of the 7-Up to reduce the sugar.

I made this using grated lemon zest from one lemon and the vanilla extract, no almond extract. And I used 2 cups of sugar, not 3. Came out delicious! Finished in 58 minutes. Definitely watch it after 50 minutes because ovens vary.

My mother (a lovely southern lady) made this 7Up cake every Thanksgiving. She also put a glaze over it using sugar and lemon juice. She left us in 2016, so now I make her 7Up cake for the holiday. I also make her delicious fruitcake for Christmas every year!

I made this today, but because I was committed to making an easy snacking cake for the start to the week, I had to make a couple of substitutions- I used cake flour instead of AP flour, I used ginger ale in place of 7 Up, and lemon zested sugar in place of lemon extract.  The only reason for the substitutions was that I did not have the called for ingredients, but I did my best to use what I did have on hand.  I made the cake in my vintage 10 inch bundt pan, & it was lovely.

I would like to request (again) that the authors of recipes in the Times remember that not everyone has a stand mixer. I grew up mixing cakes by counting strokes. I have a hand mixer but it doesn't do heavy dough. A small NYC kitchen does not have room for a stand mixer unless perhaps the cook is a full time one or a professional baker. Since you now include measurements in milliliters and grams, how about strokes? Old fashioned, I know, but I can't be the only one.

For Barbara, you can use 2 loaf pans instead of the bundt pan.  I just read this is one of the other NY Times [I think] recipes for a bundt cake. I would play around with the timing as they might bake faster or slower. If you look online there are several sites that give you baking pan equivalent sizes.

For those concerned about adding sugary drinks, this is from the article referenced at the bottom of the recipe description. "You could just as easily make the dessert with Sprite, with an Italian blood-orange concoction, with un soda au pamplemousse.... omit the soft drink entirely and replace it with plain soda water and a spritz of lemon or lime."

This sounds terrific, particulary that fact that someone's Grandmother in the South created it -- I wish you would also include her original recipe to honor her and so we can do a test comparison!  Great grandma recipes are few and far between and should not be tinkerred with the the ages!
I used Pear Cardamom French soda (World Market) and added cardamom, delish!

Made this with Orange Crush instead of 7up, and substituted orange oil for the lemon oil. Oh me oh my! Add a scoop of vanilla ice cream and I’m in creamsicle heaven. 

When making with 7up, I use 1/2 tsp lemon oil and 1/2 tsp lime oil. Maintains the 7up taste without being over lemony.

I have been making this recipe since 1990. I do not use oil. My recipe  is slightly different.   Mine is 3 C sugar
3 Sticks softened butter (salted)
5 eggs
3/4 Cup 7-up
1 Tsp Vanilla
1/2 Tsp pure Lemon Extract
1/2 Tsp pure almond extract
Sometimes I add a little more lemon and almond.
I think this is one of the best cakes   If there is left over, I wrap in wax paper and then heavy duty alum foil. Put into refrigerator and lock it up.
3 C flour?

I made the 7-Up cake for my Culinary Arts class project back when I was in high school (1999-2003). I never made it again until 2 years ago and it has become one of my main dishes!  I have made it for holidays and for coffee hours at the local Y.    I have been trying to get back into Culinary Arts and Baking and Pastry so I use this dish as an example of my work!  The recipe I use only calls for 2 TBSP. lemon extract.  I was thrilled to see the article in the New York Times magazine!

Made this yesterday with two changes.
I used Gus's lemon soda and only 2 cups of sugar.
Perfect
I should have read all the comments before I started.

I used Sprite and grape seed for the oil, reduced the sugar by about 1/2 a cup, only because I ran out. Left out the almond extract because I didn’t have it and subbed a teaspoon of lemon juice and the zest of one lemon because I didn’t have lemon extract either.
Mine was done in about 60 minutes. Beautifully browned and left the Bundt after a long cooling period with just butter.
It was delicious!! I was nervous because of the reviews, but this cake is fantastic.
My subs: 2 cups of sugar, 3 sticks of salted butter, and pineapple soda: delicious
My friend used to make this with champagne.  Chef’s kiss!

I started making this cake and, as we all do, I tasted the ingredients as I mixed the batter. I tasted 7UP for the first time in years. What a shock! That stuff is NASTY!!! A quick look at the listed ingredients showed they have started adding Stevia and dropped the amount of sugar. And, no, I didn't have Diet 7UP, if that even exists. I bought real garden-variety 7UP. The finished batter tasted flat and chemical. I am afraid I wasted some good ingredients on this mess!
Sam said I could use sparkling wine, so this was made with Prosecco, and butter, no oil. Delicious.

I have used this technique since I got my first bundt pan at a thrift store and it works every time (well, not one time but that wasn't the pans fault!) I use melted coconut oil and apply it with a pastry brush, helps with all those little nooks and crannys, then a liberal sprinkling of turbinado sugar.  Not only doesn't the case stick it has a glossy, crunchy shell.  Divine!  My son got me a fancy new bundt cake at Williams Sonoma for Christmas.  It's lovely!

I followed the advice of some other commenters and made the following adjustments:  reduced sugar to 2 cups, used a lemon-blackberry club soda instead of the 7up and added the zest of a lemon. It's remarkably light and delicious - certainly doesn't need any more sugar for an adult palate (though my kids will probably prefer it with ice cream).

it got stuck in the pan :(  [next time I will let it cool for longer] but goshdarn it's delicious!!! totally gonna add it to my permanent collection. i want to try it next with Dr Pepper and cherry extract.

Lighter than the usual pound cake.
And it was not cloyingly sweet when made with extra dry sparkling wine.

This cake was delicious. Next time I would put lemon zest in the batter and do only 2 cups of sugar. The texture was delicious very light and airy. It needs more zing something exiting so that is why I rate it a 4/5.

From a subscriber: Made this with finely grated lemon zest first mixed with 1 cup sugar to transfer the essential oils to it - and 3/4 cup (6 oz) soda. Absolutely delicious! Didn't miss the other 1.25 cups of sugar at all. Never be afraid to try less sugar, since most modern recipes have too much to start.  Next time though, I'll add a bit more salt and sub 1/4 cup almond flour for same amount of AP - for extra richness and moisture.

Do yourself a favor and don’t make this cake and most especially if you don’t drink soda pop. I’m a baker and found this cake to be less than in every way. The taste is too sweet with a funky aftertaste from the soda pop. It is a waste of good butter and eggs. The 1950s can stay in the past along with this albatross.

Sprite works fine as a substitution. Also, definitely start checking your cake after about 50 to 55 minutes. I can’t imagine baking this for one hour and 15 minutes. I took mine out at 60 minutes and wished I had done so a bit sooner.

This is a great cake.  I made it decades ago and thought to try it again today.  Other than the amount of sugar which I reduced to 2 cups, no almond extract (I didn’t have any) and no icing, I followed the instructions as noted.   I would say the next time I make this I can do with just 1.5 cups sugar.   I found 2 cups sweeter than my palette prefers. I zested a large lemon and it was so fragrant while cooking in the oven.

My whole family loved this cake. I used 1.5 c almond flour, 1.5 c GF AP flour. Also used just 1.5 cups of sugar and added about 1/2 tsp salt. It was plenty sweet. I didn't have lemon extract, but will add it next time if I get it, or just try lemon zest. The cake was dark brown after 1 hour, 15 minutes, so next time I might look at it after 1 hour. Next time I'll also flour the pan with almond flour because part of the bottom stuck to the pan.
“Old school” and delicious. Used ginger beer in place of 7up. Worked beautifully. Better the next day, decadent with fresh berries and whipped cream. Thank you Sam!

Agree with many others that 3-cups of sugar is too much. I couldn't taste 7up, butter, or lemon-lime flavor or the cake. All I could taste was sugar. And it made it not taste like the light, delicious, tangy version my mom used to make when I was a child. I'm going to try making it again with half the sugar and will try adding some lemon and lime juice.

To all the people who automatically assume that a stated amount of sugar in a recipe is TOO MUCH, please understand that everything in the recipe is for a specific chemical reaction. Cutting the amount or subbing almond flour isn't the same recipe. Did it brown correctly after you eliminated the sugar? Probably not.
Can 3 cups of sugar possibly be correct? Seems like an awful lot. Especially with the soda. Sam, is that a typo?

I have 2 bundt pans -- one is copper and has at least a 6-cup capacity with about 1 inch of space remaining at the top; its shape looks more like the cake in the photo. The other is Nordic Ware and has at least a 10-cup capacity with about 1-1/2 inches of space remaining at the top; it has slightly straighter sides. Both bake well -- the copper one with butter prep and the Nordic with or without prep. Which one do you recommend for this recipe?
The one in the picture is a 12-cup pan.  Use the bigger one.

I have made a very similar recipe that came from an aunt around 1975-80.  Hers is called a 7 Up Cake.  It calls for cake flour instead if all-purpose and just 3/4 cup 7 Up.  Also 3 tablespoons lemon extract but no other flavorings.  It uses  1 1/2 salted butter plus 1 tablespoon Crisco.

I made it again earlier this summer--excellent results.
Whoops, a mistake on the lemon extract.  It should be 1 tablespoon, not 3.
