It says 1 teaspoon.  Where you getting Tablespoons from?
I've used loaf pans to substitute for a Bundt pan when I wanted a different look.

I came across this recipe when a friend clipped the recipe from a newspaper in Gettysburg, PA showcasing comedienne jean Robertson's 7-up Pound Cake.  Google it for the original recipe.  The measurement for shortening is missing but it's a 1/2 cup.  I finish it with a confectioner's sugar glaze, about 1 1/2 cups with a few tablespoons of 7 up or milk..  It's a delicious cake. I also make a fresh strawberry pie using 7-up or Sprite.  See the www.thegoldendish.com for recipe.

LOL, this is a classic Southern pound cake--pound of butter, pound of sugar, pound of flour. But you really do need the Crisco instead of the vegetable oil. And if you don't want to use 7-Up, use a cup of milk. Glaze it with a simple syrup flavored with the three extracts; pour half on when the cake comes out of the oven and the rest when the cake is out of the pan.
There are only ~22 grams of sugar in a cup of soda. So if you're aiming for 3 cups sugar to 3 cups flour, you would only want to reduce the sugar by a tablespoon or two, not down to two cups.
I cut the sugar to two cups and it was not quite sweet enough.  Maybe 2-1/4 or 2 -1/2.  Very good with ice cream or berry compote.

I've been making this cake for years. The only difference is I use 3 sticks of butter, no oil or almond extract & Softasilk cake flower instead of all purpose flour. The recipe came from a co-worker who's family is from the South and I never alter the ingredients. I always get rave reviews!!

3 cups of sugar sounded excessive to me too, so I used 2. MISTAKE.
Just a tad bland and dry. It is still fine with coffee.  Sweeten it up with "white butter" aka whipping cream.

This was a waste of time and good butter.  The cake stuck to the pan in spite of spray and butter.  It was too sweet, even though I used 2 rather than 3 cups of sugar.  The combination of lemon and almond extracts gave the cake a chemical rather than fresh taste.  The finished product looked nothing like the photo-shopped picture.
Couldn’t resist trying this.  Used a sugar based lemon lime soda from Whole Foods.  Did reduce sugar to 2 cups..did not miss the extra cup!  Loved it!

Used a small 7.5 oz can of 7Up,   0.5 oz lemon juice.   Only cooked for 55 minutes.    Made a glaze with powdered sugar and limoncello.   I rarely make something new for a get together ( I don’t like to experiment on my guests) but this was a hit and I will definitely make it again.  Next time I will also add some lemon zest to the batter and/or the glaze.

I finally learned how to prep the Bundt pan. I use a brush and melted butter and make sure to coat every crevice. Then I add flour, cover pan with plastic wrap, and shake, then dump out the extra flour. I haven’t had a problem since.

To Juliet Jones...This is basically a pound cake recipe-so the 3 cups of sugar isn't out of line and the soda has a little over 3T of sugar in 12oz-so the soda isn't really going to make the cake any sweeter than it would have been without it. The soda actually replaces any leavening agents (baking powder or baking soda) that would have been in any other pound cake recipe.

I agree there should be a note to flour the pan after buttering it. Mine wouldn't come out of the pan in one piece, so I served it torn into pieces and drizzled with a tart lemon & berry sauce to cut the sweetness.  Good but not sure I'll make it again.
Two cups sugar, and cooked for about 60 minutes in the oven because the top wasn't brown enough.  Very tasty and moist.
Very easy. I baked for just over an hour (65 minutes) and the cake was done. Any more and it would have been dry. I used a darker bundt which may have been the contributing factor.

No notes - just a wonderful memory. I used to make that recipe when I was in my young 20s. The recipe came with my NordicWare Bundt pan, along with the enormous Bundt pan shaped meatloaf. Staple of my hospitality back in the day
Try it with Cheerwine.
Made this cake to kick off week 3 of the quarantine. Super easy to make, filling and delicious. Freezing in pieces to hopefully last the spring.
For a 5 minute belly laugh about this cake google comedian Jeanne Robertson on Don’t Send a Man to the Grocery Store.
I’d like to bake this cake, but will definitely reduce the sugar to two cups or one and a half cups. I also plan to use seltzer and add salt as other bakers suggested.

I didn't love this cake.  Flavor was nice. It was the texture that got me.  I thought the carbonation would result in a light cake, but this was dense.  But not dense and rewarding like a pound cake.  It just wasn't a winner for me.

Didn’t have lemon extract, so added the zest of two lemons and one lime to the sugar before baking, per suggestions. 525g of sugar, which made for a still very sweet cake. Served with a dollop of plain yogurt, which helped to balance the sweetness. If I made it again, I’d probably reduce to 500g, or slightly under. Buttered and lightly floured the Bundt pan. Baked for 1 hour and though the cake tester came out clean, probably could have gone another couple of minutes.

This was a wonderful cake and was a hit during a Saturday afternoon poolside gathering. 

Maybe it’s because I’m southern and this cake was a fixture of childhood (I’m 31) but I thought it was a great and easy cake to make when you need something quick. 

I’ve learned that when NYT cooking commenters say things are “too sweet” that know not of what they speak. This cake is sweet—it’s cake!!! It was wonderful. Would have been even better with some whipped cream. Easy to make and will make again.

Here, here!!  Agree 100%!  You can never have too much butter or too much sugar.  Check out Sally's Pound Cake recipe online, 3 sticks of butter, 6 eggs, a full block of cream cheese and 2.5 cups of sugar along with a bit of sour cream!  It actually fits in a bundt pan. I add a cup of coconut and a teaspoon of coconut extract and it is soooo good!

This cake had a lovely texture but an awful taste, a chemical taste like something artificial. I assume it was the 7up.  I was sorry to have wasted five lovely eggs making this cake.  If you want to try it you might try a better quality soda like limonata maybe.

My girlfriend's late mother gave me this recipe about 20 years ago. Using sodas in cakes goes back a long time. No oil in her recipe, just butter. I haven't made it in years but I'm thinking about making it with ginger ale since the comments are saying they have changed the formula for 7Up, which is too bad. Good ginger ale would work fine. Does it have a lot of sugar? Yes, the cake has a lot of sugar but it's a cake! It's not salad.

Nice cake and fun to try this nutty sounding recipe. I’m looking forward to experimenting with different fizzy beverages and love the idea of trying champagne! 
The recipe makes an enormous batch; and could easily be cut down. I didn’t want to make one enormous cake, so I ended up splitting it into three small pans. It yielded two 7” cakes and one small Bundt cake.
Can this be made without a Bundt pan?

There are many ways to make this cake. My family's version uses all butter, originally just lemon extract, but my mom would add a bit of almond extract on occasion, and cake flour instead of AP flour. The most important thing is to cream the butter and sugar for at least 20 minutes until it is light and fluffy. Do not skip this step as it makes the cake very tender.

A great tip about using a Bundt pan that I learned only about 5 years ago: butter (with a brush), dust completely with flour, and then place the greased-and-floured Bundt pan into the fridge for at least 30 minutes before filling.  Releasing my cakes from my Bundt pan has been a picture-perfect dream ever since.  I dare to try my deeply-creviced pan (as in Sam's photo) next.  I wish us all luck.
I'd forgotten about cake with 7Up - my mom used to make a strawberry 7Up cake that was a favorite as a kid. I'm going to have to ask for it now that I was reminded of it. Thanks for this!
Almond extract? It masked the citrus flavor. Nice texture but couldn’t taste the soda. Won’t make again.

Author says that it could also be made with sparkling water, but what would that do to the amount of sugar called for? I'm assuming that he means regular 7-UP, not a diet version, so using club soda would eliminate quite a lot of sugar. Anyone tried this with a non-sugar version of the soda?

The soda gives an aeration and lift to the dough that makes for a unique bite.  Club soda would do the same.  You can add some lemon/lime zest to accommodate the missed lemon-lime taste, or use lemon-lime seltzer.

To the question about a Bundt pan - my grandma always made hers in a spring form pan, a donut-shaped one with a cone/hole in the middle. I still use one and it works great.

A question on the recipe - would it need and adaptation for high altitude baking? I've tried the typical changes on my grandma's recipe - more liquid, less sugar, higher oven temp, baking a bit longer - but it is dull and tasteless. Suggestions appreciated!
Try this helpful guide: http://dish.allrecipes.com/high-altitude-cake-baking/
article says plain soda water and lemon juice could substitute for 7Up.  Assume no sugar needed for this substitution?  Sugar called for in the recipe does seem excessive, as others have noted.

I have had a similar recipe for years, It makes a very easy pound cake like cake. Uses 3 sticks of butter, no oil, 3/4 cup of 7UP and 2 tsp of lemon extract. Bakes for 1 1/2 hours. I've also made it with ginger ale. And I don't recall it being over sweet.
Barbara, have you got an angel food cake pan?  Works like a bundt, just make sure if the bottom comes out that you grease really well between the edge so it doesn't leak.
P.S. (about bundt pans) I forgot to mention that both pans are about 10 inches wide at the opening.

From one Texan to another: I'd say use the bigger one. This recipe makes a full-size Bundt cake, which sounds to me like the 10-cup pan. I made it last night and my old standard Bundt was full to the brim.

Article says plain soda w lemon juice can be substituted for the 7up,  The amount of lemon juice can probably be figured out, but since the 7up is sweet, shouldn't the lemon/soda combo be sweet too?  On the other hand, sweet 7up PLUS 3 cups of sugar seems excessive, as others have noted.  So:how best to substitute a soda/lemon combination for the 7up.  And how much can the sugar be reduced and still maintain the structure of the cake?  Wouldn't it be great if the NYT folks answered key questions

Hilarious! I spent 31 years in Texas so I got this DOWN. Will make half a dozen of these and freeze them. May I recommend the original recipe, from Jeanne Robertson on YouTube (definitely the original smart Southern Woman): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-YFRUSTiFUs (if you can figure it out from her grocery list. If not, use this one)

Jackson, Miss. is where I got my recipe too, but it not the Symphony League...it was the yellow Bell's Best Cookbook...published by Bell Telephone Company Pioneers (when bought where you paid your bill!) and source of some of all the well-traded recipes for suppers. They were de rigueur wedding gifts! 7-up pound cake was the first I learned to bake and I can still recite the recipe, even from a dead sleep. Thanks for the memory, I think I'll pull out the Kitchenaid and cream some butter!

I haven’t made yet .. I substitute unsweetened apple sauce for oil /butter/ eggs .. in this case recipe asks for all 3 .. I will sub apple sce in place of canola oil & perhaps a few of the eggs .. keep the rest for integrity of recipe.  Will cut down on sugar .. anyone else tried this ?
Query:  What temperature should the oven be set on?
The very first instruction states that oven should be preheated to 350.
Anyone who wants to try cooking with sodas should try using Cheerwine (if you can find it). There's a cookbook on the subject available from Amazon.
You answered your own point.  Cheerwine is very limited in availability other than the South.
Thanks for the tip!! I'm in the Chicago area and Cheerwine is widely available at Jewel-Osco and Mejier... It's not "very limited in availability other than the South."
Has anyone tried this recipe using Swerve (or equivalent) sweetener and any type of carb-reduced flour?
Some recipes don't lend themselves to substitutions. I'm guessing this might be one of them. Worth a try, though, and please report back with results.

This may surprise you same but it's a very old recipe.  Though perhaps not exact, the use of coke and 7up has been used in poundcake for a very long time.  Used also in baking quick breads and think about it - beer bread.

With the exception of the almond extract, this is the same recipe that I learned almost 50 years from the best cook in our neighborhood. She was a cook for the Chicago Public Schools and also baked the best butter and peanut butter cookies. I make this cake for my husband's birthday during the Christmas season. It is his favorite.
Mark Bittman is anti-sugar. Always use the least amount of added sugar as possible
Can you make it in something other than a bunt pan? I usually make my pound cake in a tube cake pan.
I can't wait to make this cake. The question I have is can I use DIET soda??
Total disaster.   Stuck to the Bundt pan and the cake had no flavor.  Couldn’t even salvage it to make bread pudding.

I make this a lot. Though I use three sticks of butter instead of oil.  Also 6 ounces of 7 up or Sprite. You can also make an orange version with fanta. 

A stand mixer is helpful since you want to cream the butter and sugar for 20 mins. It's worth it for a truly fluffy pound cake. Add some lemon zest for a bit more kick.

The bottom half inch of the slices are the best!

Also refrigerates and freezes well for future use (slices for school lunch dessert).
I made this cake with ginger beer, lime and lime zest instead of lemon, and added a lime glaze. It was lovely and went over well at the office. But it cooked up very fast - about 65 mins (vs. 75)

Way too sweet, even with only 2 cups sugar. Frankly it was not as moist as expected with the soda. I was a little disappointed with this recipe. Will not do it again. Since I have a big cake sitting there now, I will serve it with raspberry puree and whipped cream for dessert or  maybe with a lemon glaze for a change.

Top an already "way too sweet" cake with a sugary lemon glaze?   Why not cut it up in pieces, freeze it in thirds and pull some out occasionally and make a trifle with a not too sweet mousse and fresh fruit?
It is too sweet (my kids agreed with me).  I will reduce the amount of sugar in my next trial.
Can you make this in one loaf pan rather than the Bundt pan?

The texture and lightness of this cake are amazing! Unfortunately, the combination of 3 different extracts plus 7-up gave it an odd, artificial flavor (I used Nielsen-Massey extracts). Next time I’d use plain seltzer water instead of 7-up and just one extract flavor.
Read the article,  read the article .    It says sparkling water can be substituted for the 7-up.

I made this according to the recipe except using 2 cups of sugar as opposed to three and cooked for about 55 minutes. Unfortunately this was not a success and we ended up throwing most of it away. It tasted a bit like chalk.
Made the cake per recipe. It not do much exploded but erupted in the oven. Should the 7up go flat before adding to batter? Where did I go wrong?
I tried it -- It tastes good, but is too dense.
Has anyone tried this with Mountain Dew?

tried making this last night - way too much sugar. Will definitely cut it next time. And sadly, it did not come out of the pan-I used a spray on a bundt pan I have used many times before. Not sure what caused it to stick .
Try greasing and then flour the bundt pan. It should work for you then.

Great recipe, we used a tad less sugar than recommended and it was still very sweet.  The first time we used a Bundt pan and the second time a muffin pan for cupcakes. I also prepared an orange butter cream frosting that added an extra special touch.
You had me at sparkling wine! I have an older bottle that needs to be used and a week of corrections a head of me. I think my colleagues will be equally enthusiastic.
Made this with San Pellegrino Blood Orange. I don’t really care for the taste of 7-up. I admit it might not have worked because of that but I found it came out very dry.
Use ginger beer?

Did not like this recipe. As I read through the comments it seemed as though I should have gone with my instincts and made the alterations I thought it needed. Too much sugar, no salt, just not a good recipe.
I used only 2 1/2 cup sugar, added lemon zest to sugar prior to mixing with  the butter and put a lemon icing on the top.  Delicious, light and lemony and pretty too!

I used a tube pan, ginger ale, and only 2 cups of sugar. And lemon zest rather than extract. Subbed applesauce for the oil. Don’t know what it would taste like made exactly by the recipe but it was pretty good.

I made this for a dinner party and it was a huge hit!  I also reduced the sugar by a third, skipped the oil and followed the recommendation of lemon zest instead of extract.  I made a drizzle frosting with lemon juice from those bald lemons and confectioners sugar.  People seemed to love that drizzle.  I did one crazy thing as well, adding some almond meal to substitute for a small amount of flour.  I don’t think I’d do that again.  Two couples split the leftovers, loved them!
Came out very dry and powdery for me. I didn't use a bundt pan: instead used a bread tin.
Used ginger ale  and it was fine

This is one of those junk recipes that give southern cooking a bad reputation. Using 3 extracts? Lemon extract is always a bad idea, on its own or with anything else. Use fresh lemon juice and peel if you want lemon flavor. It’s especially entertaining to read comments from those who haven’t read the recipe or who advocate making a different cake.
This sounded interesting (especially the use of sparkling wine) but reading the the amazing amount of fat and sugar in the recipe, sealed the deal-  thanks, I’ll pass.....

I plan on making today, but will use pure vanilla extract - imitation vanilla does not work very well in my experience.  I also plan on using lemon zest instead of extract, and will try to grind up fresh almonds in my Vitamix.  I usually avoid extracts if I can.  

I do not drink soda - so I will use sparkling water.  I am saving this so I can find it and report if my substitutions work.
Made this halving the sugar and it’s still borderline sickly sweet. I cannot imagine making it with the full amount. The texture is very good even with half the sugar. Fluffy.
So so sooooo sweet. A kid would love it, I thought it was borderline gross.
I made this with sparkling water and it was still too sweet for me. I'll cut the sugar to 2.5 cups next time. The kids loved it, though!
Reduced to two cups sugar
Decrease cooking time to 55 minutes
Champagne!
My Mom made a version of this but since it was the 60's and convenience was the key, she used a yellow cake mix and used 7up instead of whatever liquid was required.

Ok, super serious comment here.  As many note below this cake is too sweet.  But that is not really the problem.  The problem is that the cake is UNBALANCED.  I love sweet, but after making the cake I glanced at the ingredients and there is no source of salt!!  This is a no no.  No salt, no baking powder or baking soda.  While the cake does not need this for leavening it really should have at least a pinch of salt that will bring out the citrus etc........... go look at cake recipes.  Salt.
@Mirella Huber 7 Up contains salt, and authentic pound cakes that I have made many times do not contain baking powder or baking soda.

Made it - totally unimpressed. Lots of ingredients and prep for very little payoff. Cooking time was way too long as well. I took it out after 1h05m and it was already burnt when I removed it from the pan - and yes, I use an oven thermometer!
Was your pan too shallow? That would effect the bake.

Not gonna make it again. I reduced the sugar to 520g and still it was too sweet. The texture was too soft and it broke during taking out from the pan. I expected denser and richer texture. Moreover I didn't taste the 7up. All I could taste was sugar and butter.

This might be one of the easiest cakes I've ever made, and it is a lovely pound cake.  But caution--I used a rum glaze, and it is very sweet.  Cut down on the sugar in the cake as recommended in the notes, and use a tart glaze--with lemon zest/lime zest.  I used Baker's Joy to coat the pan about 1 nano second before pouring in the batter, and the cake popped right out.

I was extremely disappointed in this cake, which I made for my book club. I followed the recipe exactly, except for using 2 tsp. of lemon zest instead of extract and cutting sugar to 2 cups. Also I cooked it for only 55 minutes. The golden-brown cake fell out of the pan easily, but the lovely appearance was deceiving. We agreed that it tasted OK but not special. The 7Up fizz completely disappeared. And the almond extract was undetectable. Moreover, it was a bit dry. I won't make it again.
@Patricia Fels You made a different cake. The changes you made were so major, and they were mistakes on your part, which is the reason the cake lacked flavor.
Could this be made with Greek yogurt instead of mayo?

Certainly, good idea. Several herb-dressing recipes substitute yoghurt for all or part of the mayo. Even if you're concerned about fat content, you'll probably need to use some full-fat yoghurt rather than entirely fat-free (dressings need some fat for flavor). A clove or two of garlic (mashed up with a pinch of salt, Greek/Middle-eastern style) stirred into the yoghurt definitely helps: garlic is the not-so-secret ingredient in tzatziki.

To all the cooks who want to substitute or eliminate the anchovies: DO NOT make this dip. The anchovies in this recipe don’t taste fishy. They are the secret Unami bomb that makes this dip so incredibly tangy and delicious.

This is the most delicious dip and definitely a keeper.  Not being a mayo fan, I used a 1/4 cup of mayo and subbed a large ripe avocado instead.  It's a healthier alternative and the results are very tasty, umami goodness.
The avocado is such a good idea. I'm going to follow your lead and make my dip with avocado, too. Thanks.

For heaven's sake, people, just use good mayonnaise. It's a dip- if there is no fat, there will be no taste. It's a party, or you wouldn't be making hors d'oeuvres. Make it taste good and don't eat too much!
Not an anchovy fan, have you ever made this with something else?  Vegan or meat option perhaps?

You could try miso for that shot of umami. I'd suggest about 2-3 TBS. I'll give it a go since my husband, although an omnivore, is not a fan of anchovies. You'd probably have to add about 2 tsp of olive oil to balance it out. Also, I'll use Vegenaise instead of mayo, that will make it vegan. Stir that one in by hand, it holds up better.
Well, the base of the dip is basically anchovie... but maybe cured olives would be nice?

Awesome dip. Made it exactly as written. Turned out great.  It's definitely on my favorites rotation dip recipes. What a recipe. Thanks. 
Understand why someone would try and bribe to get this recipe.

I have had a similar recipe to this that a dear friend gave me 35 yrs. ago.  I make it with a brick of cream cheese, 1 C. Mayonnaise, Anchovy paste, a clove of garlic & green onions. Fabulous. And nobody would ever guess that there are Anchovies in it.
Is this in addition to all of the other ingredients in the recipe? Adding cream cheese sounds divine!
Measurements for anchovy paste and green onions, please! Sounds yummy.
For those who love the anchovy umami effect but are fresh out of anchovies: fish sauce is mostly anchovy, and works in a pinch.
How much fish sauce would you guess?

In dips and dressings, the anchovy is usually not identifiable as such. Even the most staunch fish-hater would not be able to taste it. If you don't tell them it's there, they won't notice anything other than the delicious umami flavor.

Make it with mayonnaise and just eat three tablespoons of it. Then decide.  Mayo and yog don't have same characteristics. 

and to "Not an anchovy fan" - make this anyway and taste it before you say you don't like it.   Most anchovy recipes taste completely DELICIOUS in a seemingly undefinable way.... I never tell anyone there's anchovy in my vitello tonnato recipe - I don't have time to because they eat it so fast, just after saying they don't like anchovies.

Fond memories of The Store in East Hampton early 70s. I used to work in East Hampton in the summers as a nanny and cooked for the family. But I also lived on Long Island so my parents would go there occasionally when they were in the Hamptons. I remember Bert Greene's Ziti pasta salad. Was soooo good.

@Donna P I make that pasta salad every year as part of my Thanksgiving/Christmas menu! I even specially purchase G Washington beef bouillon via mail order to ensure I have the "secret ingredient" on hand at all times!

For Stephanie - if you want a substitute for anchovies I once substituted miso paste for a couple of vegetarian friends, and it was really good!  (Personally I adore anchovies, but I like to keep friends happy, too!)

Don’t be afraid of adding anchovies, even when you don’t like them. In a sauce like this they enhance the flavor and won’t turn it in to “anchovy” sauce. I’d use 1/3 mayo and 2/3 whole milk yogurt. Can’t wait to try it...today.

Even better than yogurt is the dip made ground raw cashews. You need a really good blender, Vitamix is the best. Grind 1/2 cup of cashews into a sandlike consistency. Add all the other ingredients and maybe 3/4 cup of water. Blend a little  and let sit 15 minutes. Then blend until smooth. Add water if it’s too thick.

Definitely some garlic, pounded with salt to a paste to dissolve into the background! Maybe twice the capers, a little basil... and home-made mayo with olive oil to take it up a step if there's enough time. Then devil some eggs with a little of it to go with the retro-tray of veggies. Delicious.

Answer re:  Yogurt sub for Mayo
I have subbed Greek yogurt to Mayo 50% - 50% w/ several recipes/dressings & it generally works well. You may have to adjust to your taste.
Enjoy!

I was freaked out at how much anchovy this called for, but NYT never lets me down, so I went for it. This vibrant dip was a huge hit. No one knew it was anchovy until I told them (and then it clicked). I tossed in the remainder of some plain yogurt I had that I didn't want to go to waste, and I think next time I'll do half and half with yogurt/mayo. I like mayonnaise, but it's a strong flavor here. I made it the day before and it held up great - didn't break, stayed a nice light green color.
Because I didn't have shallot or chives on hand I used one medium sized scallion and substituted vegenaise for mayo.  I also used flat leaf parsley and upped it to a scant cup.  Delicious!

In this sort of context, anchovies are like fish sauce -- they disappear as an identifiable ingredient but add impressive depth of flavor. While I would never mislead someone about what was in something I served, I think you might try the recipe as written and see if anchovy-haters will agree that it's good.

I made this the other day and it's wonderful. The anchovies (I used best quality Cantabrian ones from Spain) and the anchovy oil make this dip sing. I made my own quick mayo from scratch but next time I'll try good store bought for comparison. If anyone wants to see how I presented the dish check out my insta link: https://www.instagram.com/p/B14ei6hHQIN/?utm_source=ig_web_button_native_share
This sounds amazing, I love all the ingredients. Can't wait to hit my local farmers market now!
1 container 5% Chobani Greek yogurt + 1 small avocado subbed for mayo. Delicious!

I'm allergic to seafood, including anchovies, to which I'm especially sensitive. Can you recommend a substitute, or do those of us with such allergies just have to skip this one? (Also - note to other cooks, please warn guests if you use anchovies, anchovy paste, or even Worcestershire sauce to flavor foods where this is not expected. I see the ingredient creeping into more and more recipes as a flavor enhancer, but many are allergic to seafood, including anchovies.) Thanks!

Thank you for that note about warning guests! I *constantly* see recipes in the NYT where the writer--or commenters--exhort us to include anchovies and not tell the "squeamish." Fish/seafood is a super common allergen, and I hate always having to ask about it!

If you're looking for a little balm for the soul, Bert Greene's cookbooks are just that. His intros to each recipe are kind and funny vignettes that capture the best of people and the food they create. He was a lovely man.
Do not use a regular size Vitamix jar because there is not enough substance to “Almost liquefy”. I had to switch to a mini food chopper in order to get the anchovy mix to the correct consistency.
I have been making this dressing since the book was published and I would opt for scallions over chives every single time!

Very similar to the Florentine Dip, which inspired me to try this dip with a greek yogurt (1 cup) and sour cream (1/2 cup) base. It was delicious! Definitely more my style than a mayonnaise dip. 

Did not use food processor (didn't feel like washing it) - just hand mixed and it was fine. I was afraid the 2 tablespoons of red wine vinegar would loosen up the dip too much but it was fine and tasted better with the 2nd tablespoon.

I use the Vegan Anchovy Substitute from America's Test Kitchen Plant Based Cookbook.  

Use 1 tsp. to replace either 1 anchovy fillet or 1/2 tsp. anchovy paste.

Blend:
1/4 cup white miso
1 tsp. nori powder

If you can't find nori powder you can grind 1 sheet of crumbled toasted nori in a spice grinder to fine powder. Do not use snack-size sheets.
I put a small piece of Saran wrap over the top of my electric spice grinder before grinding to keep nori from flying out of the grinding container.

Made with 0% greek yogurt (what we had) and added olive oil to compensate. In addition to the parsley, added in the languishing extra herbs - handful or so of cilantro and dill. Very tasty if not quite what's described here. Definitely improves the longer it's chilled - better after 24hrs (plus a squeezed lemon wedge to perk it up).

When I'm making this for just me I use overripe avocado, lowfat yogurt, and just a dollop of Duke's mayo instead of all mayo. Equally delicious imo and much lower guilt. I also like to add a serrano or 2 to feed my spice addiction. Extra green! I thin it a bit to use as salad dressing sometimes, because why not? 

Just FYI, I disagree that you can't taste the anchovy when made exactly as instructed. Not a problem for me at all, but for anchovy haters I wouldn't use more than half the tin.
2/3 guests i had literally spat out and one threw up at the intense anchovy taste. Whoever said you can't taste it has to be lying. It might be the worst recipe I made in teh NYT.

Very good. I forgot to rinse the capers and I regretted it as it was a bit salty - and I love salty. Next time I will rinse the capers for sure.  Still, really good.  Enjoyed with a platter of veggies and crackers.

I hadn’t made this in a while and forgot how crazy good it is—simply the perfect veggie dip and a delicious departure from tired old ranch. If you’ve got your tinsel in a tangle over the anchovies, make something else. The anchovies are the linchpin of the whole thing.
We subbed Caprino (goat cheese) and sour cream for mayo. We added a shot of Colatura di Alici (an Italian version of Thai fish sauce) and a sprinkle of Tajin. This is a major hit at aperitivo hour.
I added a clove of garlic and loved the result.
Delicious!  Such nice layers of flavor.  Used a cup of sour cream and half a cup of mayo.

This dip needs more than 1 hour in the fridge to cool down and mellow out. I made it for a party and it spent the minimum 1 hour in the fridge before serving. The flavor was fairly strong and it was not a favorite dip for most of the guests. I had only put out ~1/2 cup but even that was barely touched. I left what wasn't used in the fridge until the next day and tried again with some carrots - totally different and much more enjoyable flavor profile!

I have had a similar recipe to this that a dear friend gave me 35 yrs. ago. I make it with a brick of cream cheese, 1 C. Mayonnaise, Anchovy paste, a clove of garlic & green onions. Fabulous. And nobody would ever guess that there are Anchovies in it.
You could try miso for that shot of umami. I'd suggest about 2-3 TBS. I'll give it a go since my husband, although an omnivore, is not a fan of anchovies. You'd probably have to add about 2 tsp of olive oil to balance it out.
Oh my goodness, this is Soooooo good.
This is delicious and well worth the effort when you are having folks over for happy hour.

Made as directed but subbing Greek yoghurt for half the Mayo.  Wow!  Best dip I have ever had.  Can’t wait to use as a salad dressing, too.  I think I may try next time with 2/3rds yoghurt and 1/3rd Mayo to lighten it up a bit…. Really good and so easy
Not good.  Don’t make this dip.  We threw most of it out after our guests avoided it at a cocktail party.  And we followed the recipe exactly except used red onion instead of chives.

I firmly believe that the use of higher quality anchovies is key here.  There is a huge difference in taste.  One of the best brands is "Anchovies de L'Escala in olive oil, from Spain.  They’re hand-packed in jars, with excellent, meaty, filets,  not at all fishy tasting.   Overall, excellent recipe.
I used the whole anchovy instead of the fillet. I think that was a mistake. Couldn’t find the 2 oz can at 2 different stores. It’s still good but intensely flavored. Live and learn

Even though I used more than the stated amount of parsley, this dip tasted so strongly of anchovy that I had to throw it away. I have nothing against anchovies and use them in other dishes. Perhaps there is simply too much variation between anchovy brands for the results to be consistent.
Made exactly as instructed and it turned out delicious. Would also make a great dip for chilled seafood or steamed artichoke.

I made this with a mix of Forager Cashewgurt (yeah, a vegan yogurt...) and mayo. It turned out well - I used to eat Greek yogurt and I do know the tang of it is very unappealing in a dip for some people. This vegan stuff has no strange or unappealing aftertaste and couldn’t be detected when mixed in.
I don’t see any yogurt listed in the ingredients or the recipe. So I’m confused.
I should add I used a small amount of tamari in place of the anchovies - it did the trick for me, saltwise.

Some people were making this with a mix of mayo and yogurt, or wanted to make this with yogurt instead. I wanted to try a mix of both that was vegan. The flavors are basically the same in all cases, and still delicious.

The first time I made this I stuck with the recipe and although I like mayonnaise there was just too much of it to suit my taste. The second time I used 1 c mayonnaise and 1/2 c full fat goat yogurt and it’s perfect.

Everyone who says you can’t taste the anchovies … I made this over the weekend, and the taste of them was overwhelming to the point that none of my guests would even touch the stuff. A waste of time and money.
Two best ingredients...mayo and anchovies what’s not to love?

Made with scallions, garlic, fancy anchovies not-in-can, half siggis whole fat and half lite 365 mayo. Blanched (do this!) thin orange and purple carrots and parsnips from farmers. Brown cherry tomatoes and scallions. At first did not like it, but changed our minds as we kept eating it. Next time maybe avocado instead of siggis? And more kinds of vegs, like broccoli and sno/snap peas. It makes A LOT of dip.  We had to stop eating and there's at least 3/4 of it left.
Kinda feel like the mayo overpowers this here. Anyone else?

I like cutting the mayonnaise (I use homemade) with Greek yogurt--1/3--1.2 cup
or however much tastes good on the day I make it.
I didn’t like this. It was super heavy since it’s basically all mayo. The anchovies added a nice flavor but I couldn’t eat much of it and neither could anyone I served it to at a dinner party.

Made this as directed.  I did find it very fishy and with a heavy mayo taste. I ended up adding a ripe avocado as recommended to cut the mayo flavor. If I make it again,I will reduce mayo, keep the avocado,  and maybe half the amount of anchovy and omit its oil.

In contrast to others, I really REALLY tasted the anchovies and it was overwhelming. I love the idea of this dip and it came together beautifully but the anchovy flavor was not to my taste. I added lemon in an attempt to make this more palatable for me but sadly, it didn’t help. I did make it with an avocado and mayo so for anyone else wondering if this is a decent sub, it is. Creamy, thick but taste was not for me.

Very OK to make with yogurt, or combo of mayo/yogurt.

I have had great success making not just dips but sauces for fish and meat using unhomogenized yogurt, which must be stirred briefly before using and produces a more liquid-y mix which is great for sauces.
why are the capers rinsed?

I used lactose-free yogurt to sub for half the mayo, upped the parsley by a few sprigs, and made one critical mistake — I started off in my blender, not my wee food processor. Use the food processor! At least for blending the herbs and anchovy, etc. 

It’s also perfectly delicious without black pepper.
Made it yesterday for an appy board. It was a hit! So delicious!

What a delightful surprise to read Sam Sifton's article about Bert Greene's  "The Store Cookbook" today. The added bonus is all the ingredients are in my frig already!  The book  has its honored place among many in my cookbook library, well worn from years of use at home and in a catering business from long ago.    I have torn out the article from today's magazine to tuck away inside the cookbook's front cover for posterity.    Some days, looking back is really fun.
Made mine with half Greek yoghurt and more basil than chives since that is what I had. All the anchovy-haters loved it. Am going to try it with deviled eggs, great suggestion!
easy substitution for anchovies is Worcestershire sauce

The first time I made this dip I had less than the called-for 2 oz. can of anchovies on hand so I used about half that amount and the dip was a hit. I made it again yesterday with the full amount and found it too salty and the anchovy flavor a bit overpowering- and I love anchovies. Next time I’ll go back to using only 4 anchovies.

A trip down culinary memory lane—obviously, those small glass jars of Spanish and/or Italian anchovies weren’t available in the 1970s (much easier to deal with than the flat cans).
Also, the idea of rinsing the capers (I always do) is to remove any excess brininess (or salt, if using salt-packed ones).

I used 1/2 Greek yogurt and 1/2 mayo (because I didn’t have enough mayo on hand). I also increased the vinegar by a tablespoon and also an extra 1/4 cup of parsley.  My main note, however, is that this is delicious straight out of the blender but it tastes even better after sitting in the fridge for a few hours.
Wonderful dip. I made just as directed using chives rather than shallots. Everything fit in my 3-cup Mini-Prep food processor; so clean up was a snap.  Planning to make another batch soon!
I was wondering if the recipe should be chives or scallions, rather than shallots? It seems like scallions esp the dark green parts would be a closer substitution for the chives?
Don’t fear the anchovies. You won’t know that they are even there except for that amazing punch of some salty deliciousness. Jump all in on this one! You will be glad you did!

I made this dip and even though I don’t love anchovies and really don’t like capers, it was phenomenal!  I was too conservative with the parsley, which was a mistake (added some later).  It is a magical recipe that proves that the sum is great than all its parts.

Question:  Can anyone tell me how long I can keep this in the refrigerator???
I made this and used it as a sauce for crab cakes. Yum!
it's delightful! Going to thin the leftovers and use on a kale salad tonight. No one could identify the anchovies, just a bright and intriguing flavor.
Helpful hint:  made exactly as written, but put 1/2 cup of mayo into the blender/processor before grinding.  Made it easier to "nearly liquify".  Mine did not look as "green" as the photo.
Made almost as written, using shallots and subbing 1/2 c whole milk yogurt for an equal part mayo. Good.
Yummm. Do not leave the anchovies out. Umami bomb. Would love to find a way to make this slightly less mayo heavy (purely a taste preference) but loved it overall.

You can easily swap half the mayo for plain yogurt.  Gives it a bit of a sharper flavor which matches very well with the umami of the anchovies.  Use either Greek or plain yogurt, preferably full fat.

I make a very similar dip with silken tofu instead of the mayo, not because we're vegan (anchovies are delicious) but because we don't like the texture of mayo and because dip + veg (+ crackers if you're inclined) then equals a healthy, reasonably protein-balanced meal.

Delicious! I had trouble getting thins liquified in step 1...but once I added 1/2 mayo and 1/2 greek yogurt, it all blended nicely. Refrigeration is an important step as it made the consistency just right. Served with homemade herb pita chips.

Delicious! Quick and easy to put together with whatever green herbs you have. I had some parsley (not enough), a lot of basil, chives, and some tarragon in the garden. I included a chopped clove of garlic and doubled the anchovies. I started by making the mayonnaise, scraped it out of the food processor but didn't rinse it out, put in the rest of the ingredients, and added the mayo back in. I'll try it with the recommended amount of parsley next time -- even if I have to buy it.

Mayonnaise repulses me. Is avocado fat enough to be a sub? 
Avocado and yogurt?
Yogurt and sour cream would be a better option I think.
Well you repulse Mayonaise, so there.
Try it.....sounds like a really tasty twist!
Seeing as mayo is involved, jow long does this last in the fridge?

Made as written. I'm a mayo gal, but found the soybean oil to have too much of a presence. I used Hellman's, which I think was the mistake. Next time I will try with a bougie organic mayo, homemade or avocado oil mayo, or cut with a whole avocado and adjust the ratio of herbs.
This is just Green Goddess Dressing - one of the most popular salad dressings of the 1960s and early 70s, before being dethroned by Ranch.  It's ok.

There are similarities, but not the same. No overpowering anise flavor from tarragon here, nor lemon juice or sour cream. Agree to disagree, but this dip is one of the main reasons I keep my NYT cooking  subscription each year. Even if I do prefer low fat yogurt as the base over mayo.
Yum, made as written. We have been eating a little with fresh veggies at dinner, in place of a salad. Love the anchovy undertone.
I added the zest of one lemon to this - gave it a little extra pop, in my opinion!
Where's the watercress mentioned in the intro?
Absolutely perfect with no modifications. I even used parsley that was on life support and it still turned out great.
I thought the 2oz anchovies was wayyy too much. Added greek yogurt and amped up the herbs and vinegar to mellow it out.. was still too overpowering.
I added garlic and jalapeño , a cup of mayo and half of sour cream

We used Caprino (goat cheese) and sour cream in lieu of Mayo. We added a shot of Colatura di Alici (the Italian equivalent of Thai fish sauce), a bit of Tajin, and roasted garlic. This was a smash hit for aperitivo hour.

Made for a big family gathering and received only thumbs down (I promise, no toxic family dynamics reflected in their reviews). I prepared exactly as written and used fresh parsley and chives from my garden. The typical reaction:

A smile - “Oh, this looks amazing!” Grab a carrot or pepper, dip in, take a bite - still smiling. Smile disappears and nose wrinkles. “Why does this taste like fish?”

So for us, more than umami.

I have made my own mayo for 3 years now. It’s a 20 minute kitchen event about once every three weeks. Use quality oil and vinegars, lemon juice and Dijon. Use a blender or a food processor—they both work. You will never buy the shelf stuff again and you will also never again worry about substitutions for that tasteless gross store bought stuff.

For the vegans in the chat: I did use miso as suggested, and some extra capers, along with vegan mayo.  I have no idea if it tastes as intended, but what I did make is delicious!  Vegan Worcestershire might be a good substitute for the anchovies too.  Really good starting point for the dietarily restricted, and I’m sure straight-up delicious for everyone else!
@Leigh came looking for this! Thanks for posting.
Easy and delicious! I served it with a vegetable platter and Ruffles potato chips. Game group loved it.

For me, this is not a dip for vegetables.  Too greasy/mayonaise-y. Needs lightening and lemon juice.  I mixed some 3:1 whole milk yogurt:sauce and it got edible as a dip.  I would not make this again.  We'll use the rest as a coating/sauce for baked salmon.
I have Bert's wonderful cookbook. His bluefish salad is one of my favorites.
B. Walter, how much anchovy paste. I'm new to it.
Going to try with garlic-infused olive oil instead of anchovy as we have a fish allergy in the family.
And I will try subbing low-fat yogurt for some (half?) of the mayo next time
Green goddess minus the tarragon.
I made this yesterday (as written) and it was delicious but there's a lot leftover.  How long will this keep in the fridge?

I must have done something wrong.
Followed recipe exactly, or so I thought.
But it wasn’t green, it was brown.
And the 2oz of anchovy were overwhelming.
Down the disposal-
Had just enough time & on hand items to make Greek Goddess dip
Yum! I added juiceof one lemon- it was too salty AND too thick for my NutriBullet to blend. Came out KILLER

I used basil mixed with the parsley and it was amazing.  The anchovies add a deeper flavor, but I bet nobody would guess they were in there.  It's great on veggies and also chips.  Definitely gonna make it again.
This was the only item barely consumed during my party. I won't be making this again. It wasn't good enough to deserve a place in my recipe box. Just ok.

I made this dip for a party last night and it was a hit. However, I definitely disagree with the reviewers who say you cannot even taste the anchovies. I could absolutely taste them but luckily my friends and I are a fan so it worked out.
This is a spin on Green Goddess dressing, always delectable. Try the anchovies.

This dip was fabulous with fresh veggies, baked pita triangles and even sturdy potato chips. I used 3 parts mayo and 2 parts greek yogurt. Sour cream and mayo would also work. Used 2 tbsp each of chives and shallots to amp up the flavor. Very quick prep time. It would make a great sandwich or burger condiment too.
Try making this with home made mayonnaise!  It's really good.
Made as directed.  Added the whole 2 punch tin of anchovies and the oil - Did taste fishy but that was okay with some.  Not to my taste.
Didn’t love it.  It didn’t really have much flavor although I made it exactly as directed.  Will try something else next time.
to Sydney: personally, I almost always find the suggested additions, subtractions, modifications to be at minimum interesting and informative, and generally implement them.

I used about half the anchovies suggested and still found it much too fishy for my taste. The dip tastes strongly of anchovy, as opposed to a caesar dressing where they blend in. I would suggest starting with just a few and tasting as you go. I used homemade mayonnaise for this and subbed roughly half for greek yogurt. The dip was otherwise excellent. I think you may find it more watery with half greek yogurt instead of mayonnaise only, which is more gelatinous.

Excellent dip/accompaniment for vegetables. The anchovies add a punch of flavor that can’t be skipped. I know some people are “anti anchovy” buy they are critical to this recipe. I thought the dip was better after sitting for twenty four hours. Also, I used the primal kitchen mayo (with avocado oil) because I realized I was out of Duke’s...halfway through making this dip. I do not recommend deviating from standard mayo because the lack of any sugar in the mayo was problematic.
This was extremely fishy for me, I used a high quality fish. Any ideas?
Use less fish
To make this slightly lighter, I used equal parts mayonnaise and natural yoghurt. It was delicious served with grilled sea bass, crushed roasted new potatoes, and green vegetables.

I made it just as the recipe is and it was fantastic. Super tasty. It doesn't taste "fishy" at all but more savory and salty like a Cesar dressing. I would like to sub Greek yogurt just to make it a healthy weekday snack option. I can imagine using this as a topping to salmon or on as a spread on a breakfast sandwich. Yum!

Made as directed, it's delicious. All taste are subjective, but for me it wasn't too fishy. A little goes a long way. Does great after overnight (might even be better), though it loses a bit of its bite. I'll be putting this one the list! Hellmans mayo for me. Delicious on anything, chips, crudité, toast, and I can't wait to try it on fish. If you're a fan of homemade Ceasar this hits many of the same notes with the anchovy bit more forward and the red wine vinegar playing the part of the lemon.
You might want to try it with home-made mayonnaise next time -- takes another 5 minutes, and you were going to use the food processor anyway.

We call this Beard dip in our household after the famous James whose dip was almost identical (sans anchovies and vinegar).  Our favorite for decades and is delicious on every single thing you can think to put it on.
Yum! How long will these stay good in the fridge?
What about half mayo and half veginase?  Has anyone tried that combination ?
I do that all the time and it works great!  Cuts the fat/calories but I still get that real mayo taste.

I didn't have anchovies on hand the first time I made it, so subbed with anchovy paste & it was a big hit. Have also made it with the anchovies, but didn't go over as well. Maybe it has to do with the brand/type or anchovy? Any suggestions for good quality anchovies?
WendyK is right. I use "Scalia", maybe the same brand? Sounds similar. Available online as well as in stores like The Meadow in Portland OR, and at the same price.

Meh.  Followed the recipe exactly and it was just ho-hum.  One of my guests commented it tasted like Caesar salad dressing, although there was no garlic in the recipe.  Probably will look for a more enticing dip next time.
This looks like a great save if the stores run out of fresh tarragon and I can't make my favorite Green Goddess Dip.  Thank you.

I doubt I'll make this any time soon, but I'm writing to thank Sam Sifton for the reminder that I own one of Bert Greene's cookbooks. It's The Grains Cookbook, and I'm looking through it for the first time in a long time. It's full of great recipes and a good read besides.

My mom has a copy of Bert Greens The Store Cookbook,
the pasta salad is fantastic....

Made this exactly as written noting that the anchovy fans said don’t dis the umami main ingredient. “It won’t taste fishy” they said. Well, the fat intensified the anchovies.  And had to throw it out. Maybe an inch or two of anchovy paste or I am thinking about using some brewers yeast flakes because I am drawn to the creamy herbyness of the rest of this dip.
This was an excellent dip when fresh herbs are abundant!
This was so tasty! I added more of the green onions to the mix.
How long will this dip keep?
Could one make this ahead? If so, how many days ahead?

I echo others' preferences for a more yogurt/sour cream base to dips. I love mayo (especially Kewpie!) but my 1 cup greek yogurt and 1/2 cup Kewpie was still slightly too mayo-forward for me. I don't think I'll have any trouble gobbling this up tonight, but I think with this particular ratio it will be even better as a dip for french fries or as a sauce for a burger.

Delicious. Do not omit anchovies! Not fishy but give it depth. At end the color wasn’t as green as above so I added a handful of spinach. Really delicious. Could be tasty on baked salmon. Or in a baked potato.

This is delicious. I made as directed, used white anchovies because that’s what I had. All the comments about this not being anchovy dominant are spot on. It’s all about the flavor. This will be part of my repertoire.
I made this recently and it was good but definitely not green like the picture. I even used chives; not shallot!  Am I the only one?
This dip has become my husband's specialty! Everyone wants the recipe!
We love this dip.  Guests love it too.  You might want to ask any guests if they have an allergy to shell fish.  Normally this does not include anchovies, but I have heard otherwise.
This is a flavor bomb!  Perhaps my anchovies were saltier than the authors, I added a wee dab of honey to balance.
Can this dip be prepared a day in advance?
Delicious! My finicky nieces love it!
where is the watercress garnish in the recipe?

Caution – label this as contains fish – It just doesn’t look like it would contain fish and it may not even smell that way since parsley tends to counter fishy odors.  For the safety and well-being of people with fish and seafood allergies The label is critical. Most would not
expect a green dip to be vegetable based and not ask before tasting.  A large attached label that cannot be missed – Could save a trip to the emergency room or even save a life.

I made this yesterday for a family barbecue, using 5 oz while milk Greek yogurt and 8 oz mayo. The dip is delicious!  Of note:  it was a bit runny yesterday but today it has thickened up to an excellent dipping consistency with no loss of color so I recommend making it a day in advance.   It makes a large amount (about cups plus).

Whoever said this recipe is not fishy is crazy. I also made it with Greek yogurt, and avocado, and cream cheese which should've even cut down on the fishiness. It tasted like a green fish dip. But not in a good way. Waste of ingredients
I would try this with just a little mayo, substituting Greek yogurt and maybe some cottage cheese.
The best herby dip/dressing I have ever tasted. Made exactly as written.
Sadly made this for the first time on election night in 2016.  It was delicious, but I can never eat it again.

@lorna
Good one! I can never eat it because all the fiery heat gives me horrid heartburn.
@lorna Try is again with less gochugara spice. I used about 2 teaspoons. It had a little heat but not crazy hot. Such a good, fast dish.
Here's the link to the video by the way since Sam "forgot" to do it: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=T9uI1-6Ac6A
Oops! Thank you, Maangchi! 😆

everyone should watch this! sam did link it in the article but i agree it should be in the headnotes here as well. it actually addresses most of the questions in comments so far, as well as being a great visual aid. (reduce the pepper flakes if you want it less spicy, use shrimp or whatever you like instead of chicken, the cheese is optional, use rice syrup, etc.)
@Maangchi-stan thanks for sharing the video. So informative and really charming.

This was easy and great. The gochujang was even available at my regular grocery, but I could not find the Korean pepper flakes even at the Asian groceries near me so I used a tablespoon of red pepper flakes and it worked great.  I know its kinda lame to leave a review if you've changed the recipe a bit, but this was so good over rice that I thought others should give it a go even if they can't find the gochugaru. Oh, and I didn't have ginger so I used ginger ale instead. Haha. Just kidding!  :0)
@ed ground red pepper should be a staple in everyone’s cabinet. This is easy to find in any Asian market & can be ordered online as well.

@ed: I know I’m many years late but I came across this recipe recently. I also had a hard time looking for the red chili flakes and realized in my grocery stores they had it labeled as the red kimchi powder. Hopefully that helps someone who was having the same problem.
@ed Use Amazon for hard to find ingredients.

This. Was. amazing. I don't leave comments very often, but this was thoroughly worth tracking down the red pepper ingredients. Smoke and spice, but not a lot of heat and really delightful. Served over both rice and blanched broccoli.
When I ate this in Korea last year there were corn kernels mixed in with the chicken and sauce.  When I made it at home I added half a bag of frozen corn, and it tasted just like it did in Korea.

We made this as written 2 days ago. I put in less of the pepper flakes than in the recipe. Overall we really liked the dish though I found the sauce a bit sweet. 

I just remade it using 1/3 pack tofu (what I had left in the fridge) and a cup or so of cut up cauliflower florets instead of the chicken. I reduced the sugar to one tablespoon. I thought the tofu and cauliflower worked very well in the dish.

Loved this recipe. I only had the paste and not the flakes pepper so I substituted 1/2 tablespoon red pepper flakes. Turned out perfectly spicey...good heat but not painful. Paired with coconut rice and a cucumber salad. Will make again for sure.
Substituted gnocchi for the rice cakes. Wonderful.

Well, Mr. Sifton made such a compelling case to make this, even in the midst of a nasty summer bronchitis cold, I pushed through.
Subs: Chicken tenders, straight Badia crushed redpepper, sambal oelek and chopped cucumber and Vidalia onion for the scallions.
Wow...any semblence of congestion quickly disappeared, the dish was stunning with heat and the sweetness and cheese did balance it a little.
The bonus was today cold for breakfast where everything tempered a bit...couldn't get enough. Thanks

I got the pepper products at Amazon and was excited to try this. First of all the dish is gorgeous...the brick red sauce with white slightly browned mozzarella in a well seasoned cast iron pan makes a very pretty, appetizing picture. I found this dish to be oddly tasteless. Sure it is hot. The heat is tempered with the sweetness of brown sugar and the creamy fattiness of the cheese. But sweet heat is not enough. This dish needs a savory, salty, umami element that I think is sadly absent.

Crushed red pepper flakes are NOT a substitute for gochugaru flakes. We made this dastardly mistake. The dish looked delicious and smelled amazing when it came out of the broiler. We dug in. The tears started. Then came coughing, releasing the demon fire. A mad dash was made for the sweet sweet release of dairy. It did not help. Crying, we tried to power through. Each bite only made the pain worse. There is an inferno in my stomach. I can smell color.

@Lexy I have to assume you don’t eat red pepper flakes a lot, or just weren’t thinking! No way could I imagine putting that much in this recipe! But it’s good to know the Korean version is less spicy!

I took a reviewer's advice and made this with vegetables and no cheese. It was fabulous, quick, easy and only one pan to clean. The sauce was delicious; spicy enough and a little sweet. The chicken was juicy and tender. I used bok choy and diagonally sliced carrots, however I expect a wide variety of veggies would work. I'm not opposed to the cheese version, but glad that we tried it without.

Have made this dish twice, as written, and we can’t get enough. It has every variation of sweet and hot and crunchy (thanks, rice cakes!) that one craves from guilty take-out. Here in Anchorage I live two blocks from a fresh rice cake bakery and am now addicted. The colors, flavors and mouth feel are crazy delicious. We read the cheese vs no cheese debate, and for us decided the mozzarella was a “go” after trying both ways. If undecided, just add to half :)
Carrie I'm in Anchorage as well and desperately want to try this recipe- I must know to which bakery you refer!

I wonder if people finding this too spicy are using 1/4c crushed red pepper flake as a sub for the gochugaru? I...would not do that. I also got regular gochujang paste instead of the extra spicy variety. We're wimps when it comes to spicy heat and this dish, made as stated, was not overly spicy even for us. Definitely spicy enough to feel it, but extremely flavorful...smoky and fruity. 

Typically I roll my eyes at "you don't need the cheese" panic but you could certainly go w/o in this case.
I’ve found that gochujang has different heat levels.  You need to look at the package, because it usually specifies the “hotness.”

My husband made this once, I made it for our second go-round. This really is a super easy recipe to make. For me it was harder to find the chili flakes (and mind you I live right on the edge of an area with a huge Korean population). Get them from Amazon, much easier.  We used half the sugar the first time, I left it out completely the 2nd time and we prefer it that way.  We love the fruity flavor of the chili and really enjoy this dish.
I add add a package of ramen noodles to this instead of the Korean rice cakes. Break it up into smaller pieces and they cook just enough.  A favorite in my house.
Substitute thinly sliced Yukon gold potatoes for rice cakes!

The red pepper flakes come in a 1-lb bag at H Mart and it's called "coarse" red pepper powder. It is flaky. The word "gochugaru" doesn't appear on the bag, at least not in English! I thought a pound was too much but it keeps in the freezer and we are already on our second bag due to the popularity of this dish in my home.
I really didn’t think it was possible for cheese to ruin a dish until I made this. This recipe is nonsense.

This was a really lovely dish.  We paired it with crispy kimchee rice waffles.  So yummy. 

One warning: at our local Korean grocery, the kind store clerk directed me to the 'mildest' gochugaru; having been unpleasantly surprised by dishes being too spicy to eat, I tasted the  gochugaru before adding it to the dish.  Thank goodness I did - 1/4 cup of the stuff would have rendered the dish inedible.  

1.5 teaspoons made for a wonderful, moderately spicy dishl.  Moral: taste your pepper first!

Made tonight and loved it. I think 4 oz. rice cakes must be wrong though. We have tteokdokki from the local Korean market and 4 oz. would be only 6 or so of them. It takes 10-14 oz. to reach the ratio of rice cakes to meat that we’re familiar with at our favorite Korean restaurants.

I admit, I was skeptical about the cheese. But I was wrong. This is one of my all-time favorite recipes. It has everything: sweetness, saltiness, heat, fat, and ginger. Love, love, love it. I’ve served it over rice, used it as a pizza topper and added it (without mozzarella) to mac and cheese. If you can’t find the ingredients locally, order from Amazon.

Loved how this turned out. Like another reviewer I substituted red pepper flakes. I also added some spinach before broiling it for a little extra veg. It was awesome. Highly recommend adding a bunch of chopped spinach about a minute before broiling and stirring it around with the chicken. Gives great color, makes it a little healthier, and absolutely delicious!
I’ve made this many times and shared it with my daughter who’s done so as well. I think this is made for cauliflower in addition to or maybe as a substitute chicken.  Maybe half and half.

Contrary to several commenters, I found this recipe too hot to enjoy even though I normally like spicy heat, and I suspect it has to do with differences in potency from brand-to-brand of gochugaru and gochujang. The cooling effect of dairy is welcome, but instead of mozzarella (weird stringy texture, zero flavor) I suggest to skip the broiling step and use plain yogurt instead.

Stellar!! Things I would change after first try: I used chix breast as hubs will not eat dark meat. Worked ok - do remember to salt, pepper, and a little acid on chix before mixing with paste. Cut chix in large pieces. Scant 1/4 c water. Reduce sugar to 2.5 T and try rice syrup or mix of brown sugar and molasses. Loved it!!
I adapted this recipe to be a sheet pan oven recipe without cheese and then also roasted cauliflower with it.  Delicious.  400 F oven.  30 minutes covered with foil; 15 to 20 without.
Can't get my head around Korean flavor profile and mozzarella cheese.  Hoping someone else will try this.
I did. It’s just weird.
As a Korean, I don't understand the appeal of adding mozzarella to Korean dishes. It is definitely weird.

I don't understand the comments criticizing Sam for not including the video link in the introduction/header note. It IS there. And I understand even less the number of thumbs up for that comment. Did you people not read the introduction?

I make this monthly - it’s my son’s favorite meal. I skip the sugar & red pepper flakes upping the Gochujang and ginger. Smoked soy sauce really elevates it. I marinate then roast the thighs on a sheet pan, putting the cheese on top for the final few minutes and serve over white rice. I double the marinade for a bigger batch (2lbs) and once I’ve moved the marinated thighs to the sheet plan, I add 2 more lbs of raw thighs to the marinade and freeze for next time.
High deliciousness to effort ratio.

We make this almost monthly. Notes: 
•We double the whole recipe for 4 people. 
•Gochujang comes in different heat levels. We like the mild. 
•We add a little salt.
•Cauliflower works well for a vegetarian version.
•Any mild-ish cheese works well. I like provolone for this. American, which I usually dislike, is surprisingly luscious here. 
•We almost never include the rice sticks, but their chewy texture is fun.
•We usually broil at the end for max cheese-meltiness, but watch for scorching.

A huge hit. Everyone ate way too much. My Italian-American husband called it Korean chicken parm (a compliment). After tasting gochugaru I got from Amazon, I halved the amount, and it was medium spicy. I cut the sugar by 1 tablespoon. Maangchi used 2 pounds of chicken—I used 1.5. Also like Maangchi I cooked the chicken on med high for 8 minutes, added the rice cakes, and cooked on low for another 10. Served with rice and quick-pickle cucumber.

I found this too sweet and greasy as written, but I love to grate an asian pear into the marinade in step 1 and then incrementally add sugar to my taste before adding chicken. Agree with comments about adding vegetables -- red bell peppers were nice. Reduce the cheese to just enough to sprinkle to cover. Tried it with feta instead of mozzarella -- it works!

I know, “cheese” is in the recipe title. But I’ll delete it if I make this again. It serves no purpose. It doesn’t “cut the heat.”  It’s just a stringy, chewy, tasteless mess. 

Otherwise, I liked the flavors. And it was a quick-fixer. 

But hold the cheese.

I made this pretty much as written. The only thing I didn't use was rice cakes. Instead I served it over white rice.

I expected this to be very spicy hot, but it was actually quite mild. If I were to make it again I might kick up the heat some by using cayenne. 

Other than that,  it was positively delicious.

Because my husband has issues with too spicy food (GERD), I halved the gochujang and left out the pepper flakes. It was the right amount of spice for him and still spicy enough for my daughter. We all loved it. Definitely will make again, and will always use the rice cakes.

FYI, gochujang is a little spicier then the comment below makes it seem. From: https://happyspicyhour.com/how-spicy-is-gochujang/ Gochujang clocks in at between 5 and 10K SHU. Jalapeños range from 2 to 8K SHU. So it's in the same range but probably slightly spicier than a jalapeño.
My husband ate the whole thing but looked like he was in severe pain while doing so. Worth the burn!

@Natalie 
My husband also suffers/enjoys - barrels through with a pained red face and lots of coughing  haha, but wants fire chicken over and over again haha

This was delicious! I'm a complete wus when it comes to spice, so I was a bit worried about all the red pepper. While it made my nose run, it wasn't too spicy at all. Like the other reviewer said, "good heat but not painful". Served it over rice, as I couldn't find Korean rice cakes. I'll be making this regularly.

When I see the word "fire" in a NYT recipe, I typically up the fire since it's my thought that these dishes tend to be more "fiery" for those who really don't tolerate hot or spicy dishes very well (I've heard both words used to describe "fiery".  On the left coast we usually say "hot" when we mean fiery). It wouldn't be very good for the food section to have its base tossing out food because they couldn't eat it.
In this case, though, NYT didn't name the dish. Sifton got the name  from the Korean-American chef-blogger Maangchi, and "Buldak" means "Fire Chicken."
Beyond delicious. The spice level and complexity in the sauce is fantastic, but still uses some pantry items. We used gnocchi instead of rice cakes and it was great. 10/10 will make it again.

This recipe might be my all-time favourite from those I've tried on NYT cooking. FYI we've found that using previously frozen chicken seems to release more water (resulting in a more liquidy sauce) so we sometimes reduce the amount of added water in those cases.

I have Maangchi’s actual cookbook ... and the wrtten directions (order, steps, logic) in the original book are, counterintuitively, rendered better here in NYT Cooking than in the cookbook itself! I made it once from the cookbook and something was unclear/out of order. So good job, NYT!

It’s difficult to find gochukaru at the asian markets like Lotte, H Mart and others because the label is in Korean. It’s a reddish ground powder form not flakes, and the label wording will have the following word in Korean appearing like: 고추가루. A quarter cup is a crazy amount and terrified me when I read the recipe so I halved it and that was plenty. This was delish with rice cakes, and I used grated cheddar as I didn’t have low moisture mozzarella on hand. A definite keeper recipe. Thanks Maangchi and Sam! xo

I wish I hadn't waited so long to try this amazing recipe! I ordered the gochugaru and gochujang from Amazon but forgot about the rice cakes. Based on a previous review, I substituted gnocchi for the rice cakes and it was delicious. My husband is not fond of overly spicy food so I was a bit worried. He absolutely loved it and said he can't wait for me to make it again. I also doubled the recipe so we'd have left overs.
6 cloves garlic and 2” piece ginger

I never thought I'd say these words, but I actually don't think it needed the cheese.  As other commenters also noted it would be great with the addition of cauliflower too.  Used a 1/2 teaspoon of red pepper flakes, couldn't find gochugaru and was happy with the spice levels. I'll definitely put this in the rotation with cauliflower included.

I cooked this (again) tonight out of the Maangchi cookbook, but I prefer it with Sam's tweaks (scallions add some color and texture, I also added black pepper).
It's really good! But doubling down on the name "fire chicken" with references to a "fiery sauce" is misleading. I know spiciness is subjective, but another comment notes the low Scoville units here. This *is* somewhat spicy, but it's fairly mildly so, like a low-key slow burn.

This was excellent! I couldn't get the tteokbokki so subbed gnocchi instead. That's the only thing I changed. Just the perfect amount of heat and such amazing flavor. I served it with quick pickled cukes and onions.

Made it as written and, WOW, was it spicy! We like hot food but 1/4 cup of gochugaru is a lot. The other flavors were great, but when I make it again I will definitely cut back on the spice (to maybe 1/8 cup gochugaru). Otherwise, fantastic comfort food.
utterly splendid. The Korean rice cakes add a nice crunch and are great with the spicy sauce.

I've been making Maangchi's cheese buldak for years - one of my favorite fun recipes! Tonight I wasn't feeling like cooking or eating rice. I shredded some green cabbage, added some sliced almonds, and dressed it with a simple mix of seasoned rice vinegar, sesame oil, and black pepper (a quick slaw of sorts) - and served the buldak on top/beside of that. The fresh crunchy cabbage was absolutely delicious with the rich cheesy buldak - try it!
Maangchi forever!!!

So yeah. I didn’t add the gochujaru. I made the gochujang 3tbsp. IM A BABY with heat. This was my compromise. Still a 10/10 dish (or 5/5 for NYT). Heat babies don’t let “fire chicken” discourage you!!!
this recipes effort to impress ratio is very good! we doubled the amount of rice cakes which was maybe excessive. the perfect amount of spice
What’s a good substitute for gochugaru?

This recipe is a fantastic base! If you look at other recipes you’ll see that buldak has many variations with honey instead of sugar, with additional peppers blended for a kick, and caramelized sugar instead of plain. You can real make it your own with this dish. Other great additions include pears, apples, and grapefruit.
I wonder if you could do this with eggplant? A vegetarian version?
Firm tofu would probably be a good substitute for the chicken. I’ve cooked that with gochugaru in the past
While not on point, I must say that Maangchi's recipes for Kimchi are incredible!

If you can't find rice cakes, try these (per Sam): gamja-ongsimi (potato birds’ eggs).

Grate a potato, then wrap the grated flesh in cheesecloth and rinse it in a bowl, reserving the cloudy water. Let that water sit until the potato starch falls to the bottom, then pour off the water and combine the starch with the rinsed, grated potato and a little salt. Make dumplings the size of, yes, little birds’ eggs out of the potato-starch mixture and then simmer them tight in water and use at will

I have not made this yet, but it sounds interesting. I found a new use for gochugaru recently. A certain squirrel has been making us batty trying to chew its way into the top of a bird feeder until I smeared about a half teaspoon of gochugaru on the area where she keeps at it. In about 10 seconds she was over that bird feeder. Peace reins again with no incessant chomp, chomp, chomp of a squirrel gnawing on plastic. No squirrel or birds were harmed; all are still romping on the deck.

This was good! I followed it exactly and the spice level was perfect. We served it over broccoli and it paired well. I don’t think I’ll use the cheese next time, I didn’t find it really needed it. I’d love to experiment with using the chicken in lettuce cups!
I love Maanchi's recipes!!  Can't wait to try this one.

I airdropped this recipe to my husband yesterday and he made it today. It was spicier than we expected but still Delilah. The dumplings a nice addition, although we both thought they could have benefitted from more salt.

Excellent, omitted 1/4c pepper flakes and spice level was just right.  Used gochujang (paste) from local Asian market (came in a red plastic tub with Korean label).    Added 1.5 bell peppers chopped, braised with cover on, then with cover off until sauce reduced.   Added ~1/2c rice to chicken before topping/broiling mozzarella.    Served w/ brown rice and steamed broccoli on side.
Was skeptical of adding cheese after tasting cooked chicken, but worked well with the heat.  Would work either way.
Thanks HK for the tips.  I omitted the cheese for health reasons and added bok ahoy to the braise.  Excellent.

I reduced the sugar by 1/2, skipped the chili flakes entirely (since "pain" is not what I'm looking for in food), and replaced the rice cakes with croutons. Can't find the cakes, & the birds eggs sound great, but are too much work in this heat.  Home-made croutons were an easy,  home-economical solution (great texture in sauce). No more fusiony than the mozz, right? We LOVED it, but next time, I will skip the mozz (as per my own idea, and HK's impassioned suggestion - gomabseubnida, HK).

Quite tasty. Made it without the chili flakes because I have spice sensitive family members. Without the flakes (with just the Gochujang) it actually turned out pretty mild. The cheese is fun, but the chicken would be just as good without it. Served it with Smashed Cukes over Coconut rice. A delicious combination.

This dish was outstanding! Served with coconut rice, cucumbers pickled with rice wine vinegar and ginger, and side of kimchi. That said, the mozzarella was completely superfluous, adding nothing worthwhile either in terms of flavor or texture, and the gochugaru peppers aren’t spicy enough to warrant something to temper the spice.
Followed the recipe exactly—so easy and so good!

All the right spice and flavor levels good Korean chicken should have! My only issue is needing to add some liquid to the mixture, as the gochujang paste makes it almost impossible to adequately mix everything together and to mix in the chicken properly. I added a bit of water, could add oil as well.
Watching the heat is important, both for the spice mixture while cooking and for the cheese under the broiler. 

It's a winner!

I was afraid that this dish would be too spicy for my husband, but he loved it. The mozzarella helps to cool it as well. There was a comment about keeping Korean Chili in the fridge. No dried or herbs spices should ever go into the fridge or the freezer. They have essential oils which will be chemically changed in the fridge and will adversely effect the flavor. Have you ever put a croissant into the fridge and tried to revive it? Not so much. Trust me, I'm a spice merchant.
Delicious and super easy! Bought rice cakes online and was glad I did. They made a difference! Not too spicy at all and could have used a bit less sugar!
I was thinking "rice cakes" but was pleasantly surprised they weren't regular rice cakes :)
I think this would work well with firm tofu or portabello mushrooms (or any mushrooms) for vegetarians.

I’ve found a new favorite sauce. Will use it as a base for a variety of ingredients — I’m thinking I’ll cauliflower next. 

My husband volunteered, without knowing about all of the similar comments here, that it didn’t need the cheese.

To quote Lennon and McCartney, "Hey Jude": You may've misunderstood the step you queried. When it says to remove the chicken from the heat, it doesn't mean remove the chicken from the pan! Simply take the whole pan off the heat, add the mozz', and run under the broiler. It works as written.

Very good, quick, and extremely easy. (Would have been easier if I hadn’t had to skin and bone the thighs.) I didn’t use cheese, because it didn’t seem right. Korean chili is not all that hot anyway; I’m a little more tender about this than my husband, so I put sour cream in the table for me. Worked fine.

Delicious. I will omit the mozzarella next time, I don't think it's necessary. I didn't have the gochugaru flakes, just the paste, so I put in a bit of regular red pepper flakes (about a teaspoon). Had just the right amount of heat and great flavor. Served with coconut rice and a cucumber salad...heavenly!
