We loved this dish. I love spice and my husband doesn't really. We both found this dish to be a good heat level. I used a little more than a pound of chicken, perhaps a pound and a half, so maybe that cut the heat down a bit. We did use the cheese not knowing any better (based on previous comments that's not a well-liked ingredient) and honestly - it's unnecessary. The chicken in sauce is all you need. Next time I'll try adding veggies and maybe a rice.
This was easy and delicious. Not as spicy-hot as you would think given the amount of red pepper flakes involved. I was skeptical about the cheese in this dish, but it works.

Easy, quick and delicious! Followed the recipe closely (used breasts instead of thighs) and added a little soy sauce before serving. Found both the gochugaru and the gochujang on Amazon.  Served it over basmati rice. Had one serving left and am looking forward to eating it for breakfast!  Will definitely make again!

I went to a big Asian supermarket and asked for help finding the ingredients. You have your choice of mild or hot gochujang paste; I chose mild. The gochugaru (Korean red-pepper flakes) comes in coarse or fine; the person  helping me suggested coarse. Don't skip the brown sugar. I added more than four ounces of Korean rice cakes (about 8 ounces) that were tubular in shape and fun to eat. The cheese is controversial among purists but I stuck to the recipe. This was a delicious dish.
Waaay less chili flakes and oil
I used Way more. Go figure!

This is amazing! Just made it for the first time and it will be on the regular rotation. I followed the recipe but ended up adding much more water to make sure the chicken cooked thoroughly and the rice cakes softened. I did this by watching the cast iron skillet and stirring to prevent sticking. Then when liquid level was low I added water. After tasting it I also added about 1 tsp of salt to balance the sweetness. Other than that, no changes. SO GOOD!!

Made this last night and the sauce is great even without the flakes. (My big box grocer had gochujang but not the red-pepper flakes.)

The chicken does NOT need cheese. I love cheese but felt like it distracted from the sauce and I was scraping it off while eating; I agree with the writer who said it would have been better with veggies instead of cheese.
I liked this, but didn't love it, mainly due to the rice cakes, which I went to pains to obtain. Also a bit too sweet and not savory enough for me. I think I'll try it again sans galettes de riz.
Great and easy! The cheese didn't add much flavor, in my opinion, so it's an easy item to skip if you're looking for lower-cal or dairy-free.

Great simple meal! But really spicy hot-lives up to its Fire Chicken name!  I cut the gochugjang flakes and paste in half when I made it the second time and still had a lot of ‘fire’! Definitely in our meal rotation! Makes enough for 2 with leftovers or for 4 without...and is more fiery the next day!

Made this 2 times now; it really easy. I found all the ingredients in the Asian grocery in little Stevens Point, WI. The rice cakes look like string cheese, but are mild and chewy. They add a great texture to this dish. Don't be afraid of the heat; the mozaarella tempers it down nicely.

My husband and I like spicy food and generally have no trouble except, perhaps, with “Indian hot.” We’ve joked that my husband would eat cardboard if it had enough hot sauce on it. THIS was at the border of what we could tolerate. It hit all parts of our mouths. Next time, I’ll probably cut the gochugaru by half and then work up from there. For our leftovers, I am going to add more rice cakes and perhaps some vegetables and serve it with a dairy side dish. It looked like the photo, though!

Made exactly as written.  It wasn't painfully spicy, but next time, and there will definitely be a next time, I'll use a little less gochugaru.  It was both quick and easy to put together, and thoroughly delicious.
Could you use gnocchi instead of rice cakes and pan sear them
Would we leave the chicken to marinate for, say, about one to two hours?

For those who have not used gocjhujang before, be prepared.  At my Korean market, I found a nice lady who directed me to the the version that might be appropriate for old white guys.  I love it.  But two tablespoons would kill me.  One or two teaspoons makes more sense.

Having said that, if you are the kind of person that takes a couple of large jalepenos, chops them (seeds and veins included), and proceed with the recipe – go for it!  My rule is:  a teaspoon of gochujang is half a jalapeno.
Korean rice cakes are wonderfully chewy. I get them on Amazon, since we do not have a local Asian foods store.

Good recipe.  I usually make a similar dish called dak galbi.  That usually has more vegetables in it and I usually mix the sauce in a blender with an apple or Korean pear.   I made this “bul Dak” according to the recipe, but used sesame oil instead of a “neutral oil.”  It still tasted good.   I’d make it without the cheese to cut down on the grease, or adding rice when the pan is nearly empty to make fried rice with the left over sauce, and then add cheese.

Great recipe! We are not huge fans of very spicy, but used the full amounts of "heat" and it was not overwhelming. Followed Maangchi's original measures for 2 lbs of chicken, but subbed a pound of small cauliflower florets for 1 lb of the chicken thighs. This gave up a lot of liquid, so next time will leave out the water in the recipe. Also, have started to sub provolone for mozzarella in recipes using melted mozza. More flavour. Used Maangchi's recipe for Japchae last night. Also a winner!
OMG... my husband who can not cook with one pot made this for dinner - one pot.  Fabulous!  Served with living lettuce in lieu of rice.  We ate all of it.

Made this as written and loved it - super quick and flavorful. For me, the mozzarella didn’t lend much in terms of flavor, but it did offer an appealing chew. Served with white rice and a cucumber salad.

Loved this dish so much we made it twice in one week when we discovered it. If following the recipe exactly, it was warm but not overly spicy. The second time we made it, I skipped the cheese and it was MUCH spicier. Still excellent, but apparently the cheese makes a big difference.

I thought this was pretty good. Didn’t knock my socks off like some of the recipes I’ve found on here though. I took the advice of some other reviewers and used a tablespoon of red pepper flakes as I couldn’t find the Korean variety. The addition of mozzarella cheese on top was a bit strange but it worked.  I served it with white rice. It’s a good recipe to have on hand if you need something really quick. The cheese melted under the broiler but didn’t brown so I just use a kitchen torch.

Just made this after reading all the reviews. A huge hit with the family. So easy to make if you have the ingredients at home. I too used regular red chili flakes. I used a teaspoon but my family said I could have used more (they have a high spice tolerance level). In any event, it is definitely a keeper.

So I went back and forth between here and the original recipe and decided to go with the original which uses larger quantities of the same ingredients except honey instead of brown sugar and 1 lb of mozzarella. Definitely spicy so next time I will dial back the red pepper as well as the red pepper paste to what Mr. Sifton has listed in his version. Did use sliced rice cakes, but honestly next time I won't. Not impressed with texture.  Definitely worth a repeat to get this right.

Sam says to cook the thighs for 8 to 10 minutes, not nearly enough time for thighs. The original Maanngchi recipe says to cover and cook for an additional 10 minutes until thoroughly cooked.  Then the cheese gets added and put into the oven to melt.

This is amazing! I did use the rice cakes so didn’t serve it over rice, just ate it as is. Next time I’ll double the recipe, have friends over, and break out all my favorite Trader Joe’s Asian frozen appetizers to make it a party.
Double the sauce and add a lot of broccoli and then serve over rice.  So good.
Made exactly as described. Probably one of the best NYT recipes I’ve ever made.

We enjoyed this.  I don’t think you need the cheese.
My husband wasn’t a fan of the rice cakes- a texture issue.
I would leave the cakes out if you can’t reheat the leftovers.
Outrageously good! Also topped with a little cilantro. I made mac 'n' cheese for the kids, served that on the side of this, topped with that gochujang pan sauce, and that was also divine!
Absolutely delicious dish, and very easy.  Makes great leftovers too.  Probably doesn't need a full 8 oz. of cheese, but boy is it tasty.  Well worth tracking down the Korean flakes and paste.

We love spicy food so this really hit the spot - easy, quick and Hot! I haven't used the rice cakes but plan to seek them out and try it with them. An added benefit was we discovered Maangchi's Emergency Kimchi to serve with it and now we use regularly with other dishes. https://www.maangchi.com/recipe/yangbaechu-kimchi
Maangchi's version uses *2 pounds* meat and i think that's better for 4.

DAMN this is good! Used 2 Tbsp red pepper flakes, skipped the sugar         (gochuchang is already quite sweet) and upped the garlic by 2 cloves. Added a few pinches salt as well. The cheese and the rice cakes really make the dish! The chicken was delicious; I used drums instead of boneless thighs and did a covered oven braise, thinning the marinade w/ enough h2o to simmer the rice cakes in (instead of pan frying). Skin+bones=extra umami. Sooooo yum. Will be making this regularly.

I used my Mom’s stash of Korean red chili pepper flakes that she leaves at our place for when she visits and this turned out so good, but way too spicy for me. My husband loved it exactly as made. I will definitely make it again, probably for a large gathering (perfect for potluck), but will either use a more generic version of red chili pepper flakes or just use less.

Yummy! I wasn't sure about the cheese, so I sliced some thin slices, maybe 3-4 oz. so good! Gochugaru is worth looking for. In the Asian markets usually you find it in large portions, but it's not that expensive. I use it in lots of other things. It is a course red pepper that is very flavorful but not very hot. that's why you use 1/4 cup. Not sure about 4 servings tho. This is better than pizza!

This was a nice, quick meal -- and had a nice heat that builds up. But I found the flavor lacking a little. I was hoping it would taste a little more like tteokbokki sauce.

Next time, I think I'll try using stock instead of water, use a little less sugar, adding a little fish sauce, and maybe cooking the aromatics before.

This is good.  Hot, spicy, with mozzarella melted all over the place.  The rice cakes where hard to find, and I should have sliced them more thinly and toasted them in the oil a little longer.  Also I think I will reserve them next time, and sprinkle them on when served - so they stay crunchy.

I only had regular red pepper flakes and didn’t know the difference so I used a large pinch, which was fine.  The dish had a mild kick and was delicious in every way, except that I turned my back and managed to over cook the sauce, (prior to broiling) not the chicken.  The sauce burned quickly into a black sheen on my cast iron skillet.  It tasted fine, especially with the addition of cheese and scallions but I will have to pay more attention next time.
Love this recipe.  No better food to eat with cheap, icy cold beer.  I didn’t have rice cakes so I used boiled fingerling potatoes that smashed slightly and crisped up in the pan.  Delicious.
No rice cakes but everything else used as directed.  So, so. Not a keeper in my mind.
Wow.this was a HIT. “10 out of 10” from my daughter. Didn’t do the rice cakes, and I was judicious with the chili flakes. Used fresh mozzarella (because it was what I had) and we loved it.

Made this tonight for my family.  Three of us loved it. My husband couldn’t get enough, and my son’s girlfriend asked for seconds which she never does. One of my sons and I didn’t care for it- we both thought it had a fishy taste and neither of us care for seafood.  I can’t for the life of me figure out where the fishy taste was coming from.  I used the exact ingredients - no substitutions. It will still go in the permanent recipe file for the part of the family who loved it .

Great!  A keeper recipe.  Spicy but not too hot.  I have a Korean grocery nearby and was able to purchasee the rice cakes.  If you can get them, they are a nice addition, but eating this with regular rice would also be fine.
Delicious and simple.  The rice cakes make it far more interesting.  Don’t omit!
I make 2 versions of this for dinner; ove with chicken and the other with cauliflower for my vegetarian children.  Everyone loves it!

Amy Notes:  Loved this recipe. I only had the paste and not the flakes pepper so I substituted 1/2 tablespoon red pepper flakes. Turned out perfectly spicey...good heat but not painful. Paired with coconut rice and a cucumber salad. Will make again for sure.
Delicious 10 recipe, july 2020. Used two tbspoons sambal oelek for spice flakes. great dish.

This was very spicy for me, but in the kind of way that I wanted more.  I left out the rice cakes and served with broccoli and steamed rice.  I also added a bit more water to make the sauce a little thinner. I think next time (and there will be several 'next times') I'd like to try building some veg into the dish, maybe a green beans, shaved onions, and roasted cherry tomatoes? I'd guess this would be great with tofu as well.
Great meal. Made it for family supper, including two who don’t like heat. I served it with some plain Icelandic yoghurt and basmati rice, and no leftovers. It is a winner.
Fabulous. Made as written. We love this dish.

Whoo boy!  I needed a fire extinguisher after making it using the full measure of the red pepper flakes ... and I love and tolerate spicy food!!  Didn't have the milder Korean pepper flakes, so used Badia. That's when the fire started.    Will absolutely make again, but substitute 1 T. pepper flakes for 1/4 cup.  What a great easy recipe!

Made this with Aleppo pepper instead of the gochugaru (I couldn’t find it). Regardless - this was DELICIOUS! So tasty and full of flavor. The spiciness definitely mellows as you cook (plus the sugar). Very good and easy.

Made this as written (maybe a few extra rice cakes)  and it was absolutely fantastic. You can find the Korean pepper flakes on Amazon- they came in just a day. 

I served it with cucumbers and some stir fried greens. I can’t wait until we can have people over for dinner again because it’s at the top of my dinner party recipe list.
I use about 6oz. of pre-shredded low-moisture mozzarella on the top. Quick and easy.
One of the best dishes I have ever made using this website.
I make this just about weekly. If you want to increase the heat, add some red pepper flakes AND the instructed measure of gochujang and gochugaru.
Less sugar more red pepper

Absolutely fantastic, made it exactly as recipe is written. At the limit of what I can tolerate for heat, and the rice cakes were so much fun, so chewy. I looked up some recipes for stir fry recipes with rice cakes, to use up the rest of the package, but no.  I’m going to make this every few days for the foreseeable future.

Excellent. I followed the recipe exactly, except I didn’t use rice cakes. I would say “fiery” is accurate. I like spicy but everyone has a different level of tolerance. And I suspect the Korean peppers vary. I was surprised how spicy it was considering some of the comments here. But it was just right for me.

Really enjoyed this! Made it with some rice and soy cooked green beans. Delicious and different. I used chicken breasts like the original recipe, since that's what we had. Really tasty and just the right amount of spice!

Excellent. We used sriracha because we ran out of gochujang paste. Spicy but delicious and easy. Served with lots of scallions and cilantro. Would have a sour cream/yogurt/creme fraiche sauce on the wide next time.

I looked forward to trying this for months, despite cooking for a heat-averse diner. Followed the recipe closely (using breast meat because that’s what I had and it’s what Maangchi uses). It was just ok; it wasn’t too spicy for either of us but the flavors seemed dulled by the sugar. Browning the meat and a hit of acid might have helped but it’s still a relatively bland flavor profile, beyond the heat. Won’t make it again. 

However, I’m thrilled to have added gochugaru to my spice repertoire!
Very tasty.  Used 1 tbs. sugar (Palm) and half amount of gochugaru flakes.
Tried this. Mystifies me as to why anyone would put cheese with this flavor profile. Not a good match up.
Add veggies,

Total winner and so simple! Made this as written except that I mixed the chicken with the seasonings a few hours ahead of time and skipped the rice cakes. Both of our kids, one of whom likes spicy heat and one of whom prefers pap, asked for seconds. Also, I put the mozz (the pre-shredded stuff) on only half because the spicy kid dislikes cheese. Tasted totally fine without it, but I thought the mozzarella made the dish much richer and more delicious.
Just made this for the second time for my family of 4. Everything got gobbled up the first time so I did 1.5x the recipe and still, every morsel was consumed. Highly recommend.
Delicious as originally written, but for best ever Korean chicken steak: leave out rice cakes, substitute provolone, eat in soft hoagie roll.
This recipe really is amazing,  I didn’t have the chili flakes so added red pepper flakes and a touch of sake.  1.5 lbs of chicken thighs and extra rice cakes (yum).  Highly recommend, total keeper!
Was watery. Try breasts or less water.
Don’t substitute the first two ingredients since they may not be easy to find. Order them on Amazon. It makes all the difference having the right ingredients.
Just a bit of trivia.  In the late 60's "Fire Chicken" was the nickname given to Pontiac Firebirds; mostly by Camaro owners.

Less sugar.
More rice cakes.
Definitely more rice cakes!
Delicious and fast - added 3/4 c water which gave a nice sauce for the cauliflower I served it with.
Absolutely a family favorite! i have toned it down a little with mild gochujang, but amazingly tasty either way. Rice cakes  are a must if you can find them-
This recipe is delicious, although I did not find Maangchi's recipe to be spicy at all.  However, I used standard crushed red peppers instead of gochugaru and harissa instead of gochujang.
Wow this was delicious. I want to put cheese on more spicy juicy dishes. And it’s not hot, which I’m not a fan of.

Made this last night. Was a bit skeptical about adding mozzarella — didn’t seem very Korean to me. Followed recipe as written. It was great!! As others have said, cheese fat tempers the heat. Had all the ingredients, including Korean rice cakes. Fantastic. I have only used the rice cakes in kimchi-jigae. The crisp rice cakes were a nice addition to the dish. I will add more next time.
I substituted chickpeas and eggplant for the chicken, and everyone in my house loved it. I made the sauce as written, and everyone loved it.
Next time I will add steamed broccoli to the chicken mixture before broiling

Excellent!  Loved the ease of it and the incredible flavor.  I had to substitute Chile de Arbol (used 2 rehydrated ones) for the gochugaru and it worked like a charm.  Took a page from a couple of other reviewers and put atop seemed broccoli, and instead of rice, chow mien noodles.  A keeper!

Really good and worth the time tracking down the ingredients.  For the rice cakes you'll probably need and Asian grocery store (check the refrigerated foods). I'm gluten free and had to special order an gluten free gochujang, but it does exist.  This is a savory rather than hot fire and made me think of a really good backyard barbecue.

Bought Korean red pepper flakes for the first time a couple years ago and never looked back. A spice rack essential-- like Korean paprika. Buy the big container. They make everything delicious and are worth seeking out special.

I made this with boneless chicken breasts as Maangchi did but otherwise followed recipe exactly as written. It was fantastic. Although spicy it wasn’t painful and the spicy sauce was delicious. Husband and I could not stop eating this. With an ice cold beer it was perfect.
I was not expecting this to taste as good as it does. It tastes even more scrumptious hours later heated up!! OMG!! So easy too!!

First time, I thought, how about broilng fresh mozzarella on top? Outcome: a plastic mini-tarp! Won't try that again but we love this recipe, always playing with different kinds of chili flakes and a bit of paprika to balance the heat.
I followed the recipe exactly - it is a show stopper
I just love Maangchi's recipes and videos. She singlehandedly kept me sane during the first 12 months of the pandemic.

This is now a fave in our household. I cut down on the gochujang because it is sooo hot even for a chili lover like me. I burned holes in the inside of my lower lip.  The rice cakes are a must. You get crispy, spicy and gooey mozzarella in each bite. I’ve found cakes in Asian grocery stores, and I’ve also made them with mochiko sweet rice flour. Maangchi has a recipe for them on her website.

Wow, you succeeded with that recipe for rice cakes? I tried but did not succeed. But I had to grind my own rice into flour because I couldn't at that moment find sweet rice flour. I'll check the recipe and see if there are any tips on it to help for next time. Otherwise, I can buy rice cakes now. It was during the pandemic and I was not shopping much.
We’ve made this recipe three times and love it. We prepare as written and then instead of rice, we started serving on tostado shells. Nice crunch and a fun mash up!
Use scant tablespoon red pepper flakes.
I make this more than anything else on NYT cooking. I usually skip the cheese but otherwise make the recipe as-is. Quick and absolutely perfect.
Made with tofu and added some kimchi when serving. Really good and easy.

1. I recommend making this without the cheese.

2. It's not as spicy as it looks.

3. This recipe barely fed three of us, and we had side dishes of rice and vegetables.

OMG so so SO good!  To be fair, I made Maangchi’s original recipe (which calls for rice syrup instead of brown sugar, and twice as much gochugaru - but I pulled back to a scant 1/3 cup because my husband can’t handle a lot of spice).  And I only had 8 oz of cheese (she calls for a full pound) just because I wasn’t paying attention.  But holy buckets—-really minimal work, and SO delicious and so much more than the sum of its parts.  Seek out gochugary - much fruitier than other red pepper flakes.

Absolutely delicious!! And so fast and easy. Without the mozzarella, it would be a delicious and clean-feeling meal. With the mozzarella, it is delicious comfort food.
Changes I made:
1) Served with lime wedges.
2) We didn’t have gochugaru, so I looked up substitutions and used 3 tablespoons of smoked paprika and 1 tablespoon of chili powder, instead. My spouse ended up adding red pepper chili flakes, but it was pleasantly-spicy for me.

Delicious!! I used mild gochujang and (since I live by an H-Mart) followed Maangchi’s original cheese buldak recipe, using rice syrup and Korean soy sauce. It was amazing—spicy, but really complex with a sweetness behind it and lots of interesting flavors. I thought the cheese was a strange idea, but it was incredible. I will definitely be making this again and, if you don’t like anything too spicy, I encourage you to use mild gochujang!

I goofed up. Not all pepper flake is created equal. Ran out of Korean pepper flake so I used Thai chili flake instead. Instinctively I knew this was a mistake but thought maybe it’d mellow with the melted mozzarella. 

It did not. This was a plate of pure picante. 

I intend to give this recipe another shot because everything about this looks tasty, if only I’d follow the directions.

We made this when it was first published. We love spicy food.  However, to delicately put it, my entire household had to be glued to the house for the after effects for the next 24 hours.  

Subsequently we have modified the recipe so we can function in n the real world after eating it.

Didn’t have the Korean chili flakes and adding 1/4c of regular chili flakes seemed insane, so I replaced with 2tsp sambal oelek. It was great. Would recommend 1tsp for people who don’t want a lot of spice and 3  for those who do. Will definitely make again.
Delicious. Used chicken breast and it was amazing.

This is fantastic as written or close to as written as I used really large cloves of garlic.    I didn't find it all that "fire" and I think people with even mild ability to like heat would love this.   For me...  I could push the heat factor up a notch or two.   We sometimes make this as a one pot meal and veggies like broccoli or peas.  With the veggies it is an easy weeknight complete dinner.  Go to your local Korean market for red pepper flakes which have a smokey quality that makes the dish

I haven’t made this yet but this is just a note for those unable to find gochugaru flakes. They can be found at Kalustyan’s in Manhattan. I ordered online and about a week later I received a one pound bag of it and it’s lasted me almost a year already even though I use it extensively. Worth every penny.

I made this without Korean rice cakes, rather served it over brown rice.  When it came time to add the cheese I was reluctant to do so because it was absolutely delicious at that point and didn't want to ruin it.  But I prepared the dish as written and the cheese was like a surprise guest at a party!  Quick and easy for a mid-week supper!

I have had the luxury of being introduced to the Korean version of this at a small restaurant up in the mountains of Gangwon do and this recipe is legit! The Ganwondo restaurant added cabbage and corn so I tossed those in as well as some left over baked, Korean sweet potato. Served with rice, red leaf lettuce, and perilla leaves to wrap the buldak and eat as ssam. To the person who said gochujang is not spicy: you have obviously not tried the O Foods brand which delivers an intense back heat!

Maybe I got the extra spicy Korean pepper flakes, it was covered in Korean letters so I don't know. But, my God, this was super spicy. Like, up there with the spiciest hot wings I've ever had. 

Also, don't fail to slice those rice cakes or else they won't cook through. Don't worry about getting flavored ones since you'll just throw the flavor pouch away.

I agree with another commentor that this is a bit on the blander side, I think it needs a bit salt/msg to take it over the top, maybe some fish sauce for additional umami. This was still a very solid base but I can imagine some additions taking it to the next next level.

DO NOT SUBSTITUTE RED PEPPER FLAKES AT 1/4 CUP. 1/2-1 TSPN would be perfect to get similar levels of heat as 1/4 cup of gochugaru.

This dish is a good one, but some of the ingredient proportions seemed off to me. I cut the gochugaru pepper flakes to 1 1/2 tablespoons, which hit the right amount of heat for me. Sam Sifton tripled the ginger from the original recipe and it is too much (Korean food is lighter on ginger than some other Asian cuisines). I'd keep to it to 1 teaspoon. I thought it was a bit too sweet (though my wife liked it)--I'd probably cut the sugar to 1 1/2 to 2 tablespoons next time.
Don’t be afraid of the cheese—it might seem unusual in this context but it really balances the spice. Served with sushi rice.

Just FYI—the common  dried crushed red pepper flakes in your spice drawer are significantly hotter than the Korean pepper flakes in the recipe. 

The Korean flakes are what is used in Kimchi.
Korean lite. Make this only for peeps who are uncomfortable with authentic Korean food but comfortable with food from the U.S. Midwest. Bland and too sweet.

A definite quick, delicious meal to save. Chose nutty flavored brown rice instead of the rice cakes. Nice compliment to the other flavors. Added a generous splash of dry white wine at the “add water” stage. Served with cucumber salad as someone else suggested. An instant favorite.

I made this without gochugaru and with a little less than the called for amount of gochujang because I wanted my four-year-old to eat it. It turned out delicious, and my daughter ate most of her bowl before starting to say that it was a little spicy for her. Success!
good but next time i would skip the mozzarella…

Easy to make this w no oil added and vegan and awesomely delicious! Just use dry pan lightly  browned cubes of well drained firm tofu instead of chicken. Rice cakes will brown in a dry pan too.Use a cashew mozzarella sauce on top (I like Chloe coscarellis recipe) and voila! I serve w steamed Chinese or regular broccoli.

Easy to make and a big hit. Husband said he could have eaten it all. Added corn as one reviewer suggested, and used provolone cheese. Served with white rice and a cucumber salad, which were perfect with it.Bought the Korean spices on Amazon. Can’t wait to make for my adult children, who love a little spice.

Made this, came out great! Only recommendation is that the given recipe is a little lacking in salt, before broiling the cheese but after cooking the chicken make sure to adjust the sauce to your taste with salt!
Double the sauce so you have enough for the rice and skip the cheese all together.

Good stuff! I made 1.5 times the recipe, because that was how much chicken I had. That produced more than enough for the two of us, but not quite enough for a second dinner. So I added a can of black beans to the leftovers, which was great, maybe even better than the original. Served it on hamburger buns, like a sloppy Joe. Try it!
I add 1 chopped onion, 2 cups chopped cabbage and 1 to 1 and a half cups chicken broth with a tbl of corn starch mixed-in, to create a sauce.

TOTAL comfort food crowd pleaser for heat lovers! 
1. Rice cakes are yummy: be careful not to overdo it: the dish becomes too chewy. And, yes, painstakingly turning over each cake makes for "toasted consistency." 
2. Cut down the red pepper flakes to 2 Tbsp. because, while we like heat...ohhh - so many tears and we couldn't fully enjoy this amazing, amazing meal.
Cook this. You will not be disappointed.

It’s like a magic trick. You grate a potato, then wrap the grated flesh in cheesecloth and rinse it in a bowl, reserving the cloudy water. Let that water sit until the potato starch falls to the bottom, then pour off the water and combine the starch with the rinsed, grated potato and a little salt. Make dumplings the size of, yes, little birds’ eggs out of the potato-starch mixture and then simmer them tight in water and use at will. It is a kind of alchemy: a potato that now tastes like rice.
Don’t get this obsession with “fiery”. My philosophy is food shouldn’t hurt.  So the about as far as one can get from best imho.

some of us enjoy spicy food and aren't hurt by it. it's no obsession, tho there are those that are just in it for the pain -trying to outdo one another on the scoville scale - but that's neither here nor there. this is not the first time sam has used the word "fiery" as a misplaced buzzword, including describing dishes containing only mustard or black pepper. to wit, gochujang ranks at only 1000 scoville units, one step above a bell pepper (and you can buy it even more mild). a jalapeno is 3500.

While “fiery” might be a buzzword that the original poster enjoys, it’s hardly a buzzword when Fire Chicken is the literal translation of the name for the featured Korean dish (Buldak). While Gochujang and Gochujaru are relatively mild compared to other peppers, some would still find the dish spicy if they are not used to adding chile paste to their food. Buldak is delicious, and Maangchi’s video is enjoyable to watch. The Fire Ramen Challenge also features Buldak flavored noodles.

@stephanie that’s good to know because I’m one of those whose esophagus simply cannot tolerate spicy heat in food. Seriously, am I the only person on earth who dislikes the hotter, the fiery, the better? Doesn’t anyone else suffer from heartburn or am I the only person that does?!
Can you use regular red pepper flakes instead of the Korean one which may well be hard to find.
Korean are far milder; would cut it way down if you're substituting regular crushed red pepper flakes.
Could you add diced tomatoes or other veggies to this dish? Maybe mushrooms?
Are there any possible substitutions for the Korean red pepper flakes (would regular red pepper flakes work?) or for the Korean red pepper paste?

Korean red pepper flakes are milder than most others, so maybe just use less of whatever you have.  Adjust to your own taste.

I can't think of a good sub for gochugaru.  Maybe a different Asian red chili paste, and then play with the spices to get a flavor you like.  It won't be just like this, but if you like it, then it's good!

you can get both on amazon or ebay, and the paste can be found in a lot of grocery stores nowadays. (i have a few brands to choose from at market basket - a new england chain - in the asian aisle, for instance. :)) i  would def not sub 1:1 red pepper flakes unless you want it to be ridiculously spicy.
Aleppo pepper is a pretty good substitute for gochujaru. Both are fairly mild and a little smoky. A blend of ancho and chipotle could also work
My family doesnt like spicy but I do. Do I have to use the top 2 ingredients?
Yes those two ingredients are the heart of this dish. This dish is spicy, make chicken parm and don’t bread the chicken and you get sort of this dish if you want.

This is delicious. I made it with slightly less gochugaru, as I was afraid it would be too spicy, but might include a little more next time. Also, I added cooked sushi rice to the chicken mixture before putting it all in the pan. Made for a nice "casserole" type of dish. But in future, will have rice on the side.
I wonder, could (cooked) potato gnocchi be substituted for the rice cakes in step 2, or is it better to just skip it?
I left out the ginger b/c I forgot to buy some and it was still delicious.

Outstanding. Had to use Gochujang "sauce" instead of paste so did not need to add all the sauce ingredients together. Just marinated chicken in 3 tablespoons and followed rest of recipe. Used jasmine rice as that is what I had. I added 1 cup of one minute blanched haricot verts cut into 2 inch pieces after chicken was almost done.  It was a huge success.

Haven't made this yet but will soon--with the "special pleasure"--after all these years of fresh mozzarella--of actually being able to add a piece of old-fashioned rubbery mozz to my shopping list--Polly-O, here I come!
Can honey or dark/lite corn syrup be a sub for rice syrup?
How can I get rice cakes? Or where I should say

Followed directions exactly, with the exception of using rice syrup instead of sugar.  Very, very tasty but a bit too hot.  Next time I’ll cut the gochugaru amount in half.  I need to watch the video again too since I’m not sure if I cooked the rice cakes properly.  Excellent recipe.

Made tonight following Emily Kim's video with 2 lbs chicken breast, but used 1/2 of the gochugaru and gochujang amt she uses, didn't want as much heat.  She suggests doing just that if you don't want it as spicy.  Local asian market had everything except rice syrup so I used the lt brown sugar Sam calls for, but kept that at the doubled amt for 2 lbs of chicken.  Loved the Korean rice cakes, gave it a great chewy texture.  LOVED THIS, hubby had 2nds!

We couldn't find the Korean red pepper flakes at our local Asian market, but they told me I could just use the basic red pepper flakes we had on hand.  I don't know if there is a difference, but this dish came out as the HOTTEST & SPICIEST dish I've ever cooked.  I mean, we're accustomed to the killer spices of the heavy-handed crawfish boiler, but we've never experienced anything like this!  Two hours later and we're still guzzling ice water!  Was it the pepper flakes that made the difference?
This dish is supposed to be SUPER spicy!
Wow.  Just wow.  Made exactly as written for my husband and I.  Loved everything about it!  Comfort food of the best kind.

This was outstanding, perfect, and EASY. Anyone who likes that Jim Harrison Caribbean stew recipe but doesn't have the time should make this. I used rice syrup per the original because I couldn't deny the power of the word "shiny," but let me know if the brown sugar delivers the same gloss. Anyone fearing the spice shouldn't worry. It's very manageable. GREAT!

A variation of this dish "chicken kalbi" can be achieved by adding a bit of sliced onions, carrots and cubed sweet potatoes and skipping the cheese (or keep it, whatever). After serving the chicken, add rice to the leftover sauce in the pan for spicy fried rice with caramelized leftover bits. Surprisingly delicious.
Where can I get the red pepper paste and flakes
Amazon... albeit expensive...better your local Asian, Chinese-American grocery store.
If you're near a major metropolitan area, find an HMart.
My local (NJ) Wegmans had them.

This was delicious! I doubled the recipe and used chicken breast. Didn't have any brown sugar so I used white sugar with a little bit of blackstrap molasses, and used ghee instead of oil. 

I was unnecessarily skeptical of the cheese and we just used just a thin slice or two melted on to our portions since I missed the "oven-safe skillet" part of the instructions, but we thought that the cheese worked very, very, deliciously well with the chicken.

Adding kimchi to serve made it amazing.

Wow, delish! Just made it exactly as written, except used pure maple syrup instead of sugar. I was afraid it might be too spicy, but it was perfect! A very flavorful and pleasant heat. I think the rice cakes added interesting texture. You can find them in the frozen case in Asian markets. Mild white kimchi makes a great side. Can’t wait to make this again.

Awesome! Added sliced onion and zucchini and left out the cheese. Sauce is nice. Would be great served with rice or a cooling veggie, like quick fridge pickles I most enjoyed the pieces that got slighted browned from the bottom of the pan.
A winner!! Couldn’t find the flakes so added a little extra gochujang. Not too spicy for hubby! Did I mention a winner!
I so wanted to like this. But it was so hot, even with mozzerella, that we couldn't taste it. We'll try it one more time, with half the gochujang.

Made exactly as listed except swapped out the neutral oil for toasted sesame oil. Served with some coconut rice and kimchi. So many kinds of good. The vinegar bite of the kimchi lent itself well to the spice and the fat/sweetness of the coconut rice balanced out the heat. Dreaming about leftovers now at 8am.

This was fantastic! The mozzarella may not be traditional but is absolutely essential to the texture of the dish; it reminded me of the eggy bits in fried rice. Highly recommend using the rice cakes as well, especially when you crisp them up a bit. I added a bit of lemon juice before the mozzarella for some extra acidity. A little bit of quick-pickled cucumber would be an excellent addition as some have suggested.

I love Korean food and I love gochujang -- but this ended up tasting too much like chicken cooked in ketchup for me. I know, I know: gochujang is peppers. But the look was not as bright or as thick as the photos and the taste was -- meh?

So you remove the chicken from the heat and then add mozzarella to the pan and melt? Why not keep the chicken in the pan and top with mozzarella and then broil. Makes no sense,
The recipe video link in the notes is easier to follow and makes sense.
Stephanie, i imagine that the 1/4 cup of Korean red pepper flakes kicks this recipe "up a notch," in terms of "fiery."
I would omit the rice cakes and serve w rice.

We loved this! I found the red pepper flakes at Wegmans and, for the folks who say they skipped them, I recommend using at least some. The taste is really unique. I used 1/8 cup instead of 1/4 and it was hot but not insanely so. If you watch the original video cited in the article, the chef uses 1/2 cup of flakes for a pound of chicken - ouch!

A crowd pleaser for sure  Cooked per recipe apart from the rice cakes. Served it over short grain rice instead.  Topped with cheese only over 1/2 the dish to see if cheese was needed and crowd was split. Some liked it but I didn’t really feel like it needed it. There is so much flavor in the sauce that the cheese gets lost. .Save yourself the extra calories.

This was fantastic!  I made it on relatively short notice and was unable to get gochugaru.  I used a mixture of equal parts crushed red pepper, pimention, Hungarian hot paprika, and paprika.  I was able to find the gochujang at a local grocery store.  It was thermonuclear hot, but delicious nonetheless.  Presumably my improvised spice blend increased the spiciness.  I will definitely make it again; had the last bit mixed into scome scrambled eggs this morning.  Or a burrito like Momofuku Ssam!
This was super easy and delicious.  Will definitely make the again.

We loved this!  Made it by the recipe except without the rice cakes, and it was delish and very Hot - which totally worked for us. (Based on the comments, I wonder if some brands of gochugaru have different heat levels.)  Made Maangchi's Emergency Kimchi to go with and it was a great combination.  Can't wait to have it again -

I have to agree. We used with goschugaru but substituted a mix of Aleppo Pepper, Paprika and chipotle paste for the red pepper paste (as recommended by internet)and it was not too hot. Spicy yes but not too much. Since online sources said that the red pepper paste is smoky but not that hot on the Scoville (less than red pepper flakes)  I've got to think it is differing gochugaru.
A good recipe but way too spicy, even for someone who prefers some spice. Next time I will reduce the pepper flakes (purchased on Amazon) to 1/8 cup!

This was so amazing! Luckily my husband did not use the 1/2 C of red pepper flakes and just 2 Tbsp, bc it was spicy enough for me. The Korean rice cakes were a nice surprise! Leftovers wee even better!!! Amazing dish! Definitely a repeat in this house!!

Yes, this is a delicious dish, which I doctored because I didn't have every ingredient...for instance, I used cheddar cheese and didn't grill it. After simmering for 8 -10 with chicken cooked through, I arranged the chicken with all the sauce into a low baking dish, spread grated cheddar and meunster slices on top and baked the dish at 375 degrees for another 10 minutes longer. The result was fabulous and my family loved it. Spices were great even though not the Korean brand.
Can I use the pre-shredded whole milk mozzarella I already have, leftover from pizza making this weekend?

Cooked the whole dish on the charcoal grill.  First grilled the marinated chicken. Tossed that into a cast iron skillet with remaining sauce and cooked indirectly for a while. Tossed on the cheese to finish.

This was awesome. Loved the sweet and hot flavors. Served over some rice. Honestly,  I could do without the cheese.
Added splashes of rice wine vinegar, sesame oil, and fish sauce. Really doesn't need cheese.

Incredible flavours. I used a fresh turkey breast. Get the rice cakes if you can—adds the starch needed for the dish, and an interesting texture. Served with a cucumber watermelon salad as a perfect counterpoint to the spicy sauce. I’m fortunate to have a huge Asian grocer in town, so was able to find all the ingredients... well worth the search.

Made this with bone-in-skin-on thighs and it was good though it took longer. This amount of marinade was plenty for 4 to 5 thighs (1.8 to 2 pounds).  I might saute these thighs initially in the pan to get the skin crispy, then add the marinade and braise it with skin side up.  Cook them on the stovetop until the thermometer says done, then under the broiler with the cheese.

This is one of the best NYT recipes I've ever cooked. Don't be afraid of the spices! I couldn't find rice cakes, so I followed Sam's advice (from a What to Cook email) and served them with gamja-ongsimi. My family was just as blown away by them as they were by the chicken. The words "magic" and "alchemy" were used. It was an incredible meal.

When I saw this recipe, I hoped that it would be like my favorite dish from a Korean cafe in Ann Arbor that has been on my mind since I moved from there 20 years ago.  It's pretty right-on, and I will definitely make it again. I used garlic and chili puree from the Indian market to save time.   I also added sliced onions before cooking the chicken, and skipped the cheese and rice cakes.  Next time, as another wrote, I'll add some cauliflower, and try with tofu.

So spicy, but so good! It was quick and easy to make. Really wasn't too spicy, very flavor full. I can't emphasize enough how easy this was though. I would highly recommend it for a weeknight meal. I served it with some carrots that took 8 minutes in the air fryer with some salt and pepper.

This was great! A perfect combination of sweet and spicy with very little heat. I used Mother In Law's gochugaru (1/4 cup) and gochujang from Whole Foods. After watching Maangchi's video, I used 6 large cloves of garlic, about a 2" piece of ginger and minced them together before putting them in the paste. Also used 2 lbs of chicken breast & a lb of mozzarella. She also showed how to cook the rice cakes and add them to the dish. Served it with an Asian salad mix. Totally awesome meal.
Back off the gochugaro by 50%, up the ginger. Used barata cheese. Splendid

We are very happy that we tried this recipe - it took our understanding of Korean food to another level.   It was hot but also sweet, smoky and very tasty.  We did add the cheese and loved it.  One could definitely eat it without the cheese if one wanted.   I also cooked up the rice cakes as directed and we found them to be a delightful offset.    I am SO glad I went out and got the correct ingredients.   We will definitely have it again!  I highly recommend it!
Double the recipe! My family of 3 devoured this, everyone wanted more, and we're sad that there were no leftovers.  We could eat this for days.  AMAZING!
Cheese topping may not be “authentic,” but it was really good, melted over the spicy braised chicken. Also, the optional sliced rice cakes? It’s worth hunting it down. It’s the best part!

This was so delicious! Tried it as a dare to myself b/c couldn’t imagine the cheese would work. It so did! Used one home grown Thai pepper instead of the flakes and had to sub dried ginger for fresh. Two of us polished off the entire dish, happily. Can’t wait to make again with the rice cakes and Korean pepper.

Excellent!  I halved the recipe for two and omitted the pepper flakes.  I also used grated mozzarella because it was in my fridge.  DH thought the rice cake ovalettes were too chewy, so I’ll try boiling them for a few minutes next time before adding them.  I did notice that the ovalettes that ended up golden brown and crunchy were much easier to chew than the ones that stayed pale.  A definite keeper.

This was fantastic. Full disclosure: I didn't cook it, my neighbour found the recipe and did it. But it was lovely. Not knowing much about Korean food, the addition of the cheese didn't trouble me. I will definitely be making again for myself!
My husband LOVES this recipe.  I personally think the cheese is a terrible addition.  The next time we prepare this I will leave the cheese off and add veggies.

I have not made this yet and was prepared to make it without the rice cakes if I couldn't find them, but I found some at Whole Foods (in N. VA): Jayone Crunchy Rice Roll. I bought a package with brown and white rice. https://www.jayone.com/WEB/?mid=snack&product_srl=20401 Planning to make this is a few days but I thought I'd mention the cylindrical rice cakes I was able to find in case people had trouble finding them.

We loved this, and the crunch from the rice cakes was a nice textural contrast. While I think I used the right thing, they're so light that 4 oz. would be an enormous amount. I had a package of 3.5 oz. and only used slices from 2 - 3 of the cylinders. (If not the right thing, they worked as intended.)

I used chicken breasts as called for in the original recipe and somewhat scant measures for the pepper paste and flakes. Otherwise, this is pretty straightforward and I made it as written.
Good but way to spicy. Cut the flakes by 1/2
Too spicy

Tempting to add cilantro to this, but it really doesn't need it!  Got the spices and the rice cakes from Amazon, absolutely worth using the funny little rice cakes, as they add that little something different.
Wonderful, easy, hot-sweet, beloved by my crew!  This will be a simple, quick meal to look forward to on a regular basis.
I used shredded mozzarella, and it worked just fine. Also, the heat is not nearly as fearsome as may be thought; really just lots of full flavor.

Follow the Maanchi video and you’ll be a happy camper: 1/2 cup gochugaru, and about twice the gojuchang and garlic. Skipped the rice cakes. My guests didn’t complain at all about the heat they went for seconds. 

All I had was quicken quarters, so I separated the thighs and drumsticks while removing the skin. Worked brilliantly. Skipped the 2T oil because I figured the dark meat was fatty enough and I was right. Served with rice and Korean sesame coleslaw. Amazing meal, can’t wait to make again!

I made this recipe after finding the ingredients in my Korean grocery store. I have made it twice now, and it is surprisingly not as spicy as it seems it would be (which is a good thing to me). I serve it with the pre-made stir fry noodles from the grocery store. After trying it first per the instructions, I now skip the cheese - the dish is naturally oily/fatty, the cheese on top makes it eat very rich.
Super easy to make and very favorful!  My wife doesn't like 'hot' food but this dish is favorful-hot and she loved it!
I love spicy food but this was painful. Admittedly, I used a new brand of gochugang that may have been the culprit. Can't wait to try it again to actually taste it. Be careful.

I had twice the amount of chicken so I doubled the recipe. Couldn’t find gochugaru where I live, so I replaced with 1 tablespoon of red pepper. It came out fantastic. I will be making this again very soon
Made as written and it was tasty! If you use a substitute for gochugaru, be careful what you use. I made it with a combo of cayenne pepper and ancho chile and it packed heat.

Korean rice cakes are not the same as the puffed rice cakes you find at the supermarket! Totally trolley different—they are chewy and dense, made from rice flour (‘ore like mochi but not sweet). Also, supermarket mozzarella melts quite differently from fresh mozzarella or burrata, which generally shouldn’t be cooked.
I've been cooking the NYT brussels sprouts with gnocchi recipe lately (delicious, by the way) and wonder if gnocchi could sub for the rice cakes.  Is the texture similar?
Any suggestions for replacing the chicken with a another meat or a non meat?
I could not find the Korean Spices where I live. I used Hot Sauce, and tasted terrific!

This was really good! Had to substitute harissa and red pepper flakes (adjusting the amounts slightly) because it was what was on hand, but the end result was a really quick—perfectly spicy dinner that I wouldn’t have thought of making.

The sliced rice cakes are a wonderful, chewy textural contrast — Do not skip them.  While I am not a vegetarian, I could imagine making this with extra firm, baked tofu, along with the rice cakes.  And...”Fiery” is exactly the right word.  My mouth was still burning even after the dishes were done. But we loved it and can’t wait to have it again.  This afternoon, I ate the small dish of leftovers cold, straight out of the fridge- just as delicious.

had korean all my life - cooking from mom, aunts, korean events, and restaraunts and never has there been chese.  

do away with the fusion

Absolutely delicious. I slightly cut down on the ginger (I am married to a ginger hater) and added a splash of rice vinegar for a little acid before piling on the cheese. Rice cakes were a wonderful addition. Can’t wait till I can find an excuse to make this again!

A great trick for getting golden brown cheese in any dish that calls for Mozzarella, is to substitute 1/3 of the Mozzarella with Edam.  Edam is a mild cheese that won't appreciably alter the flavour, but it browns much better than Mozz and yields a nicer appearance to the finished product.

This was a fun dish to cook, and quite tasty.  I think the rice cake bits were a bit gummy/chewy, not to our liking, but others might like that, they were definitely interesting.  It also did benefit from the addition of some rice wine vinegar, and a bit more salt or soy sauce.  We cut the cheese about in half, and that seemed perfect.  And, it is a gorgeous dish to serve to people.
What can I use instead of chicken? My other half doesn't eat any meat, but would love the cheesy spiciness of the sauce. The strength of the spice might kill off any fish, so.... any ideas?
firm tofu or tubular rice cakes, which are a bit pasta-like?
Mushrooms work great. I used mushrooms and Bok choy to add some veggies to the dish and they worked great.

Well I am gobsmacked - my husband loved the dish but said he could do without the cheese!!!!  Never saw that coming.  Delicious dish but needs a bit more liquid to serve over rice.  I also added some crushed red pepper to add a bright note.  Will definitely do again.

For english audiences:
450g chicken
110g rice cakes (the ones I buy are fatter than these coins so I cooked them for the whole time with the chicken)
200g pack of that pizza mozzerella you dont find everywhere now.
I used a casserole instead of skillet, ending up adding quite a bit more water as I was scraping all the delicious gunk off the bottom during cooking. Then I put it under the grill on high with a baking sheet under the casserole, which took longer than they said.

Made a considerably modified version of this, with sambal oelek paste instead of the gochujang and ground cayenne pepper instead of the gochugaru. Tried to compensate for the increased spice level by increasing the sugar. The result was spicier than the recipe as written (I believe), but had a richer stickier sauce with more intense garlic notes. A quick and easy meal that has impressive depths of flavour!
This was delicious and very easy to make! I added lots more garlic.
Yikes this is hot!  Good, easy, delicious, but far too spicy for my family.  Will definitely make again but will cut the pepper flakes in half.
This was delicious but spicy. Cut red pepper flakes to 3/4 tsp. Really good with basmati and wild rice. Sautéed Baby Bok Choy would be great with it.

I made this and its fantastic - the leftovers are even better! Put on top of sticky coconut rice for an even better meal.  Couldn't find the Korean red pepper flakes so I used a the ones in my pantry and added a a little extra brown sugar and a tough of maple syrup to the mixture.  Oh and the Korean rice cakes are completely worth it - find them and  watch Maangchi's video for tips.

Excellent, tasty dish!.  Made it just per the recipe.  Only issue was that at a local K-Mart, a Korean mega store, I could not find Korean red pepper flakes.  They had ground red pepper and "course grind" red pepper.  I got the course grind which looked closer to red pepper flakes (assuming flakes were something like the normal red pepper flakes).  I used the exact amounts called for in the recipe and it came out great.  Just a mild heat; not really a very spicy dish.
I had kimchi on the side to have a bite here and there with the dish. The vinegar flavor of kimchi helps balance the sweet spice of the dish.
This was a complete home run!  I cut the Korean pepper flakes in half and it was spicy enough ... I used chicken breast too and will again.  Served it over cauliflower rice - super easy!

I ordered gochugaru from an oriental webshop and got a bag of coarse red powder instead of flakes (enough to last the remainder of my life, by the way). Can I substitute flakes with powder, and if so, in the same quantity?
That powder is gochugaru, the Korean red chili flakes.
Absolutely fabulous!  How in the world do mozzarella cheese and Korean spices work so perfectly together? Such a great dish.  Thank you!

This was great, but next time I'll do things more like how Maangchi did in her video. I also think that using rice syrup (like Maangchi) instead of brown sugar makes a thicker sauce, which I would have liked. I think I'll just add a cornstarch slurry after the chicken finished cooking through and just before putting it under the broiler with the cheese. I used Chinese-style chili flakes (hotter) and an extra spicy gochujang so I was worried it would be too spicy, but it came out pretty mild.
I love this recipe!  I find myself getting cravings for it.  BUT 1/4 cup of red pepper flakes??? Are they HIGH or what?  Or maybe Korean red pepper flakes are light-weights?
This was excellent, made exactly as written. Yes, the mozzarella seems weird. Yes, it totally works! Not too spicy, great balance of sweet, smoky, spicy, salty.

I made this with chicken, Brussels sprouts, and palm noodles. It was delicious. I used gochujang paste that I made at home (being Canadian, the maple syrup was a great touch!): https://minimalistbaker.com/easy-gochujang-sauce-korean-chili-paste/
Is there any reason NOT to let the chicken marinate overnight in the sauce?  Thanks.

I improvised with the Korean peppers, but otherwise stuck to the recipe, including the rice cakes, which were interestingly chewy, but also gut bombs. Solid carb.  Husband thought that the rice cakes and mozeralla added very little, but liked the meat. I am inclined to agree with him on this one. 

Now what to do with the rest of those rice cakes?

This needs more water than is called for. I added .25c to the base mixture, the paste was too thick otherwise to coat the chicken. Added another .25 when it went in the pan and a 3rd and 4th 4th during cooking so it wouldn't dry out.

It takes longer than 8-10 min to get the chicken cooked through.

Added some chopped onion, peppers, and blistered cherry tomatoes to get some veg in it.

- Really suggest the rice cakes for texture. Wait for the pan to be HOT.
- Non-stick if you don't have cast iron/carbon steel.
- Sub 2 Tbsp honey for sugar if you prefer the thighs more tender than meaty.
- I prefer mild gochujang (2/5).
- Gochugaru is pretty much mandatory. Coarse or fine works.
- Breasts work too. Add less water; they cook faster.

@Stephanie
While “fiery” might be a buzzword that the original poster enjoys, it’s hardly a buzzword when Fire Chicken is the literal translation of the name for the featured Korean dish (Buldak).  While Gochujang and Gochujaru are relatively mild compared to other peppers, some would still find the dish spicy if they are not used to adding chile paste to their food. Buldak is delicious, and Maangchi’s video is enjoyable to watch. The Fire Ramen Challenge also features Buldak flavored noodles.
Has anyone made this with a seafood substitution?  We are pescatarian, but love this flavor profile.  Perhaps Thai eggplant, even?
I always make it with tofu and it's delicious.

I've made this twice.  Very easy to execute.  Was a little daunted by using so much gochugaru (I had never used it before) but truth is they're not that spicy (taste them first and see).  It's hot.  Head sweating nose running hot.  But kinda more paprika hot.  And it's DELICIOUS!  

First time was a challenge; I don't mind heat but this needed more balance.  Second time, another Tbs of sugar.  Perfect.  (Also, I used more chicken cut in larger pieces.)  This is a keeper.

We were skeptical at first - spicy chicken, rice and...mozzarella?!? We've made this three times and it has become a favorite when we want something spicy & comforting. Boneless, skinless chicken thighs are now essential for our freezer. We are currently on our 2nd bottle of gochujang. . .
In the video she uses 1/2 cup gochugaru which would explain why she calls it spicy/fiery. (And it is.) This recipe calls for 1/4 cup. She also uses a pound of cheese.
That's also for double the chicken, she does twice as much as this recipe.
My family can’t handle all the spice, but we found that cutting the amount of red pepper flakes made it much less spicy.

I don't have either of the "g" products.  Any suggestions for substitutions so I don't have to drive around or hit up Amazon? Obviously (and per comment below), I can use red pepper flakes.  The paste is trickier. Would Trader Joe's "Chili Onion Crunch" work or chili garlic sauce"?  I'd love to make this tonight.
Do you have an Asian market anywhere nearby?  They would surely carry it as it's a very common condiment in Korea.
They definitely carry the gochugang at Whole Foods or even Safeway by me has it.
Great recipe!

Loved the video! Made the recipe verbatim. Fiery all right, but one-dimensional heat. Plan on using the same method, but varying the ingredients/pairing. E.g., harissa paste with Aleppo flakes. Cheers!
Hi ya’ll out there! Has anyone tried doubling this recipe? And if so, would you recommend it? Happy New Year!
I doubled it last night and it turned out amazing :)

I followed the recipe but used less gochugaru, as what I purchased in a Korean grocery was a red powder (with salt in it) instead of flakes, but from other's comments, not realize this is probably what was meant.  Next time will use the full 1/4 cup.  I was dubious about the cheese but used it anyway.  We liked it very much and will make it again.  The rice cakes were purchased already sliced and frozen.  They had an interesting texture.

When I think of rice cakes, I think of the dry snacky things you put peanut butter on for an afternoon snack.  So I’m puzzled by Step 2 where you sauté the rice cakes “until they are a little crisp.”  Seems like the rice cakes start out wet & fry up.  What am I missing, please?

Different kind of rice cakes - definitely not the snack food. They are often in slices or cylinder shapes. You can get them at Korean markets. Really fun to eat, great squishy texture! https://www.maangchi.com/recipe/garaetteok
Do you think this dish would be good with tofu? I need a meat substitute.
I'm making it with eggplant (and possibly chickpeas) tonight.
Yes, and maybe some diced mushrooms
Used 1/2 the pepper flakes.  Try 1/4 next time.  It was wonderful.
I think the sauce has too much sugar, I’d use only 1 tablespoon. I’m a cheese lover but it’s unnecessary here. The sauce is soooo good. The gojuchang I got from Whole Foods worked for me

I had a few mishaps during this, but it still was very tasty. I forgot to chop up the chicken, so we just had full thighs, but that was fine (although I definitely see the appeal of having pieces over a bed of rice that mix in with the rice cakes). Also, medium high was just a bit too hot on my stove, so I had to add water a couple of times to loosen up the sauce so it wouldn't burn, even after turning it down. It was a bit diluted but still tasty! Excited to give it another try.
Now in regular recipe rotation with chicken or cubed tofu. Very tasty with either (didn't use mozza with tofu version).
Use 2x rice cakes and leave in for whole cooking time. Grate garlic and ginger.
I made it as written, including the rice cakes.  Fabulous.  The heat was spot on for a family dinner.

Delicious! Instead of rice cakes, I sautéed about a pound total of sliced green bell peppers and onion and added to the mix.  I also used closer to a pound and a half of chicken.  There was still plenty of sauce to coat the chicken and veggies as well as spoon all over the rice.
Delicious! Perfect weeknight dinner or hangover food
Believe it or not, I was able to make a "credible" version of this using plant-based chicken. Very nice.
This is delicious. Will definitely make again. Used half the cheese called for and still good.

So easy! Starving with chicken wings in the fridge and no idea what to cook - this came together in a snap.  Easy to toss some veggies in the sauce too- broccoli, or green beans, and have a full healthy dinner.
Wow! Easy and delicious! I only used an eighth cup of the Korean pepper flakes and could have used more -it was spicy but could have been more so. Great recipe. My husband loved it.
We all loved it - even mildly picky teenagers.  I don't think the mozzarella adds anything though.

I added a tablespoon of oyster sauce and mirin. Some tofu because I didn't have enough chicken. Provolone instead of mozzarella. Was good and the family liked it. But I won't use honey instead as someone suggested, because it had too much flavor for my taste. Regular sugar next time.
awesomely thanks man though and I'm really hoping I was

This dish is so delicious, and so easy! Been a pandemic favorite in our house. Order the gochugaru online if you have to, red pepper flakes aren't the same.  Used less heat as a personal preference, and shredded mozz that was on hand. Do yourself a favor and make this.
I didn't have gochugaru so used a combination of aleppo flakes and chipotle powder. Just right. Love this.

Sub regular chili or bbq sauce or a blend of same, place over crusty potato cubes or Duchesse potatoes, and melt a cheddar-mozzarella blend on top.  Serve with creamy slaw or a green salad with an orange flavour to it, and cornbread.
This is the best ever. Can't get enough of it!
I love this recipe and make it again and again. It's better the closer I adhere to Maangchi's instructions. I don't have any additions except that advice: make it as the recipe says.
I had whole chicken thighs and didn’t want to cut them into pieces, so I combined all the seasonings in a baking dish, mixed it with the chicken, and baked it. Came out great. Juicy.
So.Good. Thank you for this wonderful recipe. It was easy to make and very delicious 😋
This was super easy and delicious!  Served with rice, sauteed bok choy and spicy cucumbers. Definitely a keeper!  Personal preference…..omitted the cheese.
Has anyone tried this with cubed tofu instead of the chicken?  Maybe brown tofu cubes dusted in cornstarch in place of the chicken pieces?  Or leave soft?
Perfect cooked as written. Added to our regular rotation.

i subbed gochugaru with a mix of chile powder, cayenne, and red pepper flakes and it was HOT, which i loved but did not totally expect. things mellowed out and were really delicious after day 2. probably could have done with less cheese.

We really enjoyed this.  Reduced sugar to 1 TBS, increased garlic, and skipped the 
Mozzarella.  Couldn’t find Korean rice cakes, so substituted ramen.  Added vegetable broth to have enough liquid for the ramen to cook.
Also only 1 1/2 Tbs brown sugar, definitely enough. Other ingredients as per recipe, x/c for the powder. Was good, Tom had seconds.

The tragedy of this recipe is that, to fully appreciate its true genius, you need to be so hungover you wouldn’t feel like cooking, or so drunk cooking would be dangerous. That said, even moderately sober, I enjoyed it quite a bit. 

I added about 4 cups of blanched cauliflower along with chicken, and a cup of frozen corn with the rice cakes. You better believe I finished it with plenty of broiled mottza. Plan to try cubed tofu in place of the chicken next time.

I have made this dish several times and added my own variations. I cook it in a Dutch oven and this last time added potatoes and ripe plantain. It was delicious. It's one of those dishes where you can use up whatever you have lingering in the kitchen.
How is this any different than Maangchi’s recipe?

I have no idea if it is authentic - East Asian dishes typically don't include cheese - but it is nonetheless a very good dish to have made. Delicious over rice.  Don't skip the rice cakes, they soak up the sauce and are delicious.
I agree with another reviewer that this lacked some depth of flavor by the end… but I found a splash of oyster sauce helped with that! Ended up delicious with a subtle heat

Great, easy recipe I've now made many times. I prepare this in a covered, initially cold, cast-iron skillet on medium heat for 8-9 minutes and don't stir at all during that time. Remove from heat, cover with shredded mozzarella and pop into broiler.

Drop the mic! So darn good. Perfect balance of spicy/sweet and the cheese adds a gooey richness that melds with the flavors in a wonderfully unexpected way. Had it over pan fried leftover rice with a couple of glasses of Verdejo and my belly is so happy!
Agree with Christy P. On adding corn kernels to the cheese, this is actually one of the banchan (Korean side dish) that they serve at one of our favorite Korean bbq restaurants.

Wowser! I just made this AND had a 2nd helping. I used Tofu instead of Chicken and Cauliflower patties instead of rice cakes. No mozzarella so I used a mix of cheddar & jack. This was sooo good, fast AND easy. How's that for making do? Gerry in S. Oregon
Very tasty. The rice cakes didn't cook enough so I'll put them in earlier next time.

Really we agreed that it was a 4.5, but there's no way to rank that  way. It was really yummy and delicious and just the right amount of spice for the spice spectrum of my family. Not  sophisticated or complex, but truly delicious. And pretty easy, too.

Omitted the pepper flakes, which leaves the deep dark milder heat of the gochujang, and more palatable to a wider range of tasted. Delicious.  I add some oil to make a little more sauce and we serve it over rice.
I love this recipe. Tonight I went to the fridge and realised I’d run out of gochujang. I substituted it with Sambal instead and loved it too.
Great flavor.  Skip the cheese.  I made it with and without and I don't see why anyone would use it in this recipe.
Because we like cheese???
I have made this many times. I always add chopped onions. Don’t use rice cakes.  They’re not really readily available here.

Instead of rice cakes I add 2-4 cups of fresh or frozen corn kernels before covering with cheese and putting it in the oven. Delicious and more nutritious. Served over couscous it is a staple in my household. Loved by all.

This is one of my go-to recipes. It’s so easy.

I’ll often triple the recipe, but make it without the rice cakes or cheese—then freeze extras in individual portions. 

Pull out frozen portions to pair with rice (another thing I love to freeze individually), & a veg like roasted kale or a quick pickled cucumber salad—no one will be mad to eat this!
I cooked this as written this evening. It was good, but a little bland flavor-wise.
Made this with tofu, added shredded carrots, chunks of bell pepper…fabulous!!
Make sure to stir every minute or so, as the gochugaru easily burns at high heat
Excellent as written. Add finely slivered onion as recommended. Pour the water into the bowl from the chicken to include the rest of the sauce. Make in cast iron skillet.
Two tablespoons of brown sugar, not three. A bit extra soy sauce. About a teaspoon of crushed red pepper flakes. Measure oil. I used 4 string cheeses. Ate with broccoli and rice.

Very delicious! I had slightly less than a lb of chicken, but it was extra saucy as a result! Served with rice and lemon green beans, and reduced the brown sugar to 2 tablespoons. My husband and I liked it, will make again!

I made this for the first time today. I had every ingredient and didn’t sub. I love spicy food, and added additional Korean red pepper. Five minutes before it was ready I added fresh green beans to the pan. So good with the chicken, sauce, and rice. Thanks again, Sam, for a delicious, really easy meal.
Made as is and is delicious! Lacking in salt - will add salt both ay marinade and cooking stages next time.

Followed the recipe except we were out of mozzarella so used fontina. Ate without rice because rice cakes. Awesome flavors you can't eat fast enough. Will definitely make again and again (maybe use less chili powder, it's pretty hot).

Spicy enough to sweat although used three TB of gochujang instead of two.  I added dashes of Mountain Sauce, Shaoxing wine and fish sauce and a little extra soy since I had 2 lbs of ground turkey I needed to use.  Loved the rice cake texture, it’s the first time I’ve used the ones I bought at the local Asian food market.  I also added peas and carrots to make it a one-pot meal.  All these changes were superficial.  I used the cheese (some burrata I also wanted to consume).  Less spice next time

Reading the marinade ingredients I'm thinking chicken wings in the oven - not sure how to calibrate...but I'll study it.  Sounds yummy, but how could fiery arinated chicken and then melted cheese not by delish?! Thanks. Ch
This looks great!  But…Sugar.  Can’t eat it a.  Any work arounds?

For those in search: Whole Foods and Thrive Market sell a very good/reliable gochujang and gochugaru called Mother In Law's. I believe both are found in the asian section of the store! They even have several flavors and spice levels to choose from.
What if you don’t have a broiler- ‘not a thing’ where I’m from!
In the UK it's the grill, if that helps.

Cheese melts well in a cast iron skillet or nonstick pan.

Keep the pan moving to help adjust the heat to get exactly the texture you want.

I love this recipe and would suggest you buy the rice cakes
I get mine on line as I live in a rural area
They are so chewy and good !
Also I like to substitute dark corn syrup for the brown sugar? Gives it a lovely taste and makes it glossy

Used chicken tenders and gnocchi, added broccoli. Used chopped leeks in place of scallions and frizzled them prior to crisping the gnocchi. I couldn't find the gochugaru, but used Aleppo chili flakes at 2 T and the heat was medium and just right. Delicious and will make again!
Made as written and I loved it. Quick and easy with lots of flavor. Good heat. Teenager approved. It’s going in the rotation.

My husband and I really liked this! So good and so EASY! No Korean rice cakes so just served on Uncle Ben’s coconut Ready Rice. No gochugaru so used 1/2 tsp of crushed red pepper. A nice kick without being too hot (but “hot” is subjective”). Otherwise … everything exactly as the recipe called for. I’ll keep skinless, boneless chicken thighs in my freezer, and I then I can make this anytime I need a quick meal. Will be making this over and over.
Made this dish tonight and added a little more of the red pepper paste - great heat and very flavorful. Served with Jasmine rice and steamed vegetables.

I bought the red pepper flakes as recommended, and OMG was this dish hot! Our eyes sweat!  We eat very spicy Asian food by our friends standards, and my husbands heat meter is higher than mine and he could barely eat it.  The flavor was good. I think with much fewer flakes this would be a very good dish.
perfect dish!
I didn’t have the rice cakes and just served with rice.  So good!!  Definitely will make regularly.  Very easy to make too.
Subbed in firm tofu, honey. Baby spinach at the end until barely wilted. Super fast and delicious over rice.

1tbs hot gochujang and 1/4 c Aleppo pepper. White pepper for the black. 5 large garlic cloves and a bag of frozen corn thawed. Used one pound of rice cakes. Chicken thighs. Shredded Chihuahua cheese on top. One whole bunch of scallions on top at end. Everyone loved this. Watching the video was very helpful.
Made this and it was delicious. Did make some changes…first, sautéed an onion, sliced mushrooms, and a sweet red pepper. We don’t like chicken thighs, so sliced up chicken breast tenders.  Loved it.

A coworker shared Maangchi's original video with me about 6 years ago and I make her recipe regularly.  It is *NOT* spicy hot, but it is super delicious!  I haven't found a source for the Korean pepper ingredients so I improvise.  I have never made it with the cheese as it would take away from the spice flavour that is there.  Highly recommend this recipe.

We like spicy food.  I loved this, but it was too hot for my husband.  I used 2 T crushed red pepper instead of the 1/4 c of gochugaru.  Next time I'll leave it out altogether and just put some on the table to add if I want it.
Outstanding! We made this and served it in a bowl format with rice and steamed broccoli. I made more sauce than the recipe called for to give us more sauce for the bowls. So delicious!
This is a dish that needs to be eaten hot.  The Cheese gets firm fast when it starts to cool.

Not my favorite right out of the pan. I appreciated the peppery heat, which was not overwhelming, but the whole thing felt a bit unbalanced (heavy on top notes, light on umami). One wonderful surprise, though: this dish is delicious cold! Because I was not wowed initially, I put most of the chicken in the fridge, though I was not feeling looking forward to the leftovers. I took a bite as a snack the next day, and then I took another and another and another. It was much better cold! Go figure.

Made this the other night and it was much easier to make than I thought it would be. Heat was perfect and didn't overpower the flavor. There were no leftovers. This will go into the regular menu rotation.

This was delicious! Next time I will halve the amount of pepper flakes and gochujang as it was too spicy for my little ones. I added about 1 cup water more to dilute it and ended up liking it with a thinner sauce. Added 1/2 bag of frozen corn and 8oz of rice cakes.
Made with what was in the frig. Used beef and American Cheese. Don’t knock it until you tried it. Was good

I made this as written, but without the rice cakes.  Served over rice, and it was delicious but definitely at the limit of our spice tolerance.  My family requested that I make it again but halve the pepper flakes and paste.  Even with the sweaty brows and numb lips, there were no leftovers.

Made as written without rice cakes and I’m obsessed!! Yes it’s hot spicy but not intolerably so, and of course it would depend on the gochugaru and gochujang spice levels and your spice preference. The only problem is this is not 4 servings, more like 2.5. Looked up Maangchi’s recipe and hers has 2 lbs of chicken as 4 servings which makes more sense, so I may double this next time. Leftover the next day was good as well.

Very good! I threw a little ginger beer in the sauce to help coat the chicken. Simmered until the mixture had a tar-like texture; makes for a good sweet heat. You shouldn’t add vegetables imho they don’t belong in this dish.
The cheese didn't add anything, and was really gross as leftovers. The underlying recipe is tasty and easy. I'll make it again, sans cheese.

I was able to find gochujang in my grocery store, but I wasn't prepared to pay $6 to use 2 tablespoons of it. So I subbed about 2 tbs of red curry paste with a generous squirt of Sriracha. I also used 1/2 tsp. of red chili flakes. This gave it the desired kick! Left out the rice cakes, and didn't feel like I missed them when served in a bed of rice. Not quite made to the letter, but still quite tasty and easy.

Very tasty, and easy.  Followed recipe exactly, except served it with ramen noodles rather than rice cakes, and omitted pepper flakes as the gochujang I found was labelled "hot." (It was only mildly hot).  The cheese is a nice addition.  Will definitely make again.
