Made the s for the second time and used half the pepper flakes and half the pepper paste.  This made just the right kick for us.  We also felt the cheese didn't add anything, so this time I instead served the chicken over rice with a drizzle of a yogurt sauce I made the other day.  My husband claims this is now his favorite meal.

This is a terrific dish, but we definitely prefer to skip the cheese and rice cakes, which weigh it down and don’t add much, if anything, to the flavor. We also use a little less oil. The gochugaru can be a bit spicy for some, but that’s easily adjustable. (I do understand that spiciness is one of the key points of this dish.)
Fabulous, nice and spicy, bit more than that, delicious dish.

Everyone gave it 5 stars, only change was adding half tsp Momofuku chili crunch for extra heat.  The rice cakes I found at a Korean rice cake store were fresh and less than a half inch diameter. I sliced them up thinking it could not work well... they had the look/texture of mini-marshmallows.  They took longer than expected to brown, but came out perfect, crispy/chewy, definitely worth it.  We were all skeptical of the cheese topping but again, all agreed it was perfect - browned nicely.

This is an excellent recipe. Very easy to make, came together really quick and it’s absolutely delicious. I wanted to make it a littler spicier and added a bit of cayenne powder. Perfect for a weeknight dinner. I served it with rice and blanched broccoli like another note suggested.
My wife doesn't like spicey but we love this.  The spice is very subtle and fades quickly. It has become our "goto". Quick and easy and just different enough to shake up the week.
There are a lot of 5 star reviews here.  I'm not sure why.  This tastes ok - nothing great.  If I'd ordered this in a restaurant I wouldn't send it back but I wouldn't order it again either.

Wanted to cut down for a 10 inch pan, so used 6 eggs, 105 grams flour, a little over 1/2 milk, and a scant 1/2 tsp salt and pepper. It turned out SO good; the Parmesan forms a crispy crust in the oven that was delightful. There are only two of us on this lovely Sunday morning and we are now groaning from trying to finish the whole thing in a sitting!

Delicious! Now a staple in our house. I don’t have gochugaru on hand, so I just leave that out, and it’s still plenty spicy for us. My main note is that if you’re using chicken that’s injected with water: omit the 1/4 cup water added to the pan even if it seems too thick. The chicken I have available releases so much water that I have to spend extra time cooking with the lid off to get the sauce back down to the right thickness. Sometimes we add cauliflower for a “one pot meal”.
Excellent and easy. Followed recipe except cooked in cast iron skillet on grill w/ wood chips.
Prepared just as directed and it was delicious!  The two of us nearly scarfed the whole thing down. Will perhaps double, next time.
This is a family favorite! It isn't spicy though... Even my least heat-tolerant child loves this.

Tasty and fiery!!
Listened to the comments
- didn't have Korean chili flakes so I did a tablespoon of red pepper flakes. Might have been too much since my Gochujang was pretty spicy
- I added some frozen corn at the end and let it cook another 5 minutes (def add some kind of vegetable for color and flavor)
- skipped the cheese and broil (though I may try it with leftovers later)
- an overnight marinade became 2 nights & it was fine

I made this dish as directed with Assi red pepper flakes and Gochujang from Trader Joe’s which is not that sweet. I didn’t do the rice cakes and I used one and a half pounds of chicken thighs. 
It came out quite spicy maybe because of the pepper flakes that I used. It had that smoky hotness that I expect from Korean food.
It was so delicious. I definitely ate two servings. It’s ridiculous to think you’re gonna get four servings out of 1 pound of chicken.

used red pepper flakes and siracha as replacements for gochugaru and gochujang. I also removed chicken from sauce and pan fried the bottoms of thighs for 3 minutes, while I thickened sauce with corn starch and msg. Amazing dish.  Used half of the pepper flakes, added a little chili crunch, 2 tbspns of tomato paste, and a little paprika.   Really flavorful dish.
I will make this over and over. We make a lot of it and eat it every day for a week. It's perfection. So yummy. One of our favs.

I can’t take too spicy so I used half the gochugaru powder and used Ssamjang which is milder than Gochugang. This resulted in a nice, mild kick. Skipped the cheese. To the spring onions I added some chopped cilantro. Served over rice. Delicious.
I didn't love the rice cakes but the chicken and sauce was delish.  Used Aleppo pepper flakes which I think were perfect.
Delicious. I would use more than 1 lb of chicken next time. There is a lot of sauce and 1 lb isn't a lot!
It's OK. That's all I have to say.
OMG- delicious dinner ready in the time it takes to make rice? Yes, please! I subbed regular red pepper flakes and this recipe deserves 5 stars!

This was delicious. However, within hours it created huge “GI issues” for everyone in my family. And we are a “spicy-leaning” family. I thought I was in the clear but had to return home when I was less than 1/2 mile away from home because Augghhh! Be warned!
Loved this. Cut the gochugaru to one heaping tablespoon as my wife not highly tolerant of heat but was still nicely spicy. Served with rice and a Korean cucumber salad.

Made this without rice cakes but did serve over rice. I used 1 tsp of red pepper flakes in place of Korean chilis but also my gochugang sauce was pretty spicy. It was pretty easy and quick to make and also good.

Love this so much. Had to substitute premade Korean Buldak Hot Sauce in a pinch once. Common shelf item in even big box stores. This might be the most addictive condiment EVER. Now everything i make can be “Protein” Buldak. Like my ex-wife once asked, “does everything heave to be hot???”

I make this so often (twice a month).  It is great comfort food since it's hot and sweet and rich with cheese.  it takes no time at all (cutting up the chicken takes the most time).  I can't recommend it enough (i do add extra ginger).  I love it with bitter greens and Forbidden rice (it's black and very flavorful).
Added another tablespoon of Gochujang Jang instead of the pepper flakes. Added two cut up bell peppers. Came out fabulous with just the right amount of heat and sweetness.
The real name for this dish is Chicken Buldak. No idea what the dish title is in this recipe. Sounds made up.
@Manny Pedi As I understand it, “buldak” refers to a Korean spicy chicken dish, so the title is correct in that it denotes the atypical addition of cheese.

I used Thai red-pepper paste and skipped the rice cakes. It was an easy recipe and turned out very well though I will halve the chili paste in the future. I don't think the mozzarella adds much to this dish. It was fine served over rice with a side salad.

Delicious and I just realized that I completely forgot about the mozzarella!  Could not find Korean red pepper flakes so I used about a 1/2 tap of regular red pepper flakes.  Also couldn’t find rice cakes so I used gnocchi.  Otherwise I made it as written except I forgot the cheese.  Served over rice with steamed broccoli.  I’m curious to know what the mozzarella adds to the dish so I will definitely be making again. Appreciated the actual measurements on the garlic and ginger.

Did not have gochugaru so instead used a mixture of sweet paprika (half of total needed), regular red pepper flakes, and chipotle powder (a quarter each of total needed).   Otherwise prepared as written.  Great recipe.

Used red pepper flakes, since I had them, vice buying the Korean pepper flakes. The dish was excellent and I’ll definitely make it again.  I’ll skip the cheese topping in the future, though. Hubby and I agreed it was just gilding the lily.

The marinade is really more of a paste. I added the water and a cup of veg stock to the bowl, mixed it then added to the pan. Def needed to be wetted up. Also roasted a head of cauliflower and added it to the pan after the chicken was cooked. The flavor was bursting all over 🥰
Holy moly. This very well may be the best thing I have ever eaten. Soooo worth seeking out the Korean ingredients. Very very easy for weeknights.  Yummy squared.
Superb!! Love the heat.  I didn't have mozzarella so used a soft local creamy goat's  cheese instead.  Delicious.

Lordhelpme this rocked my world. I'm waiting for Amazon to deliver the Korean peppers so used 1/2tsp. regular red pepper flakes. I added some leftover cauliflower thanks to notes here. Next time, I'll add cooked kimchi, more chicken (better the next day) and whatever veg or mushrooms i have on hand. What wouldn't taste wonderful in this sauce?

Much too spicy for us with the red pepper flakes 
Can’t taste anything else!

I made this as directed other than cutting it in half & leaving the chicken in the paste for 90 minutes. It was not spicy, just a nice flavor, and detected no sweetness but feel like the sugar is vital for balance. This is the second time I’ve made this - a quick & delicious meal. Served with rice & roasted broccoli. It’s definitely a keeper.

We didn't love this.  Substituting soy curls for chicken and GF gnocchi for rice cakes, for us, this smacked us as just pure heat.  You'd get a brief sense of the flavor mix that was seemingly desired for a fraction of a second, and it was just all heat from there.  My guess was the gochugaru is quite a bit high, but not sure....  And the cheese dominated the texturing.  There's a great recipe in here somewhere, but this didn't do it for us.

Made this tonight and it was exceptional. Substituted red pepper flakes (used 1 tbsp).  It’s the first time using rice cakes.  These were delicious and definitely added to the dish. Will make this again and try a vegetarian version.

Instant keeper with an incredible sauce. Sauce so good, in fact that I'm saving what's left--along with the rice--to make chicken fried rice with the leftover chicken from this weekend's spatchcocked bird. I don't have access to the Korean chili powder and substituted red pepper flakes. I had plenty of turkey stock, so I subbed it for the water. I decided that I preferred white pepper to black for this recipe and I added a splash of toasted sesame oil just because.
Made this for myself and adult son. We liked it but didn't love it. The cheese was too mild, the chicken too sweet. I loved watching the Maangchi version being made - she is so much fun.

honestly, so delightful and delicious!  Did not have rice cakes, but no matter.  We will put this in the "best" category and make again, with some smashed cucumbers and sautéed spinach on the side.  YUM

Really delicious and simple recipe. A bit too sweet, I will definitely cut back on the brown sugar next time. Also, I probably only used about 4 oz of cheese and I thought it was plenty, in fact I think I would like it without cheese just as much. I watched the video and now I look forward to trying this with shrimp!
Made as written and it was good overall, but would use chicken breasts instead next time!

This is a unicorn, i.e. hugely rewarding for the amount of work required. Sub cauliflower, omit rice cakes and cheese, and its also inexpensive. Trifecta! Thanks Emily and Sam! I got an 8oz. cylindrical container with the words "Organic Red Pepper Powder (Gochugaru)" on the label. It's Surasang brand from HMart. The product tastes like red bell pepper for a moment, then earthy, smoky, and mildly hot. I’ll use the pepper as a hot sub for Aleppo, or flaky sub for smoked paprika.
Easy and delicious. Added some beet greens and stems because I had them on hand. Would make again and riff on it as my asian pantry ingredients allow.
Added a lil doenjang, some leftover veggies from earlier cuz they were on their way out; subbed kecap manis for brown sugar. Hits all of the sweet, salty cravings, well rounded and umami. Perfect.
This was insane. So so delicious.

I LOVE LOVE LOVE this dish!
But Spice Coward mods: 
Mostly OFoods Gochujang sauce (with a heaping T of 'real' gochujang paste)
1T Gochugaro not 3, God help me
Added red/yell bell peppers
Tried with breasts but thighs so much better.
Reheats in skillet pretty darn well and fast.
The little rice cakes in the sauce were tasty! I could skip the chicken and triple the rice cakes for Korean deokbokki at home.
How can I make more spicy and oily? Not spicy and oily enough when I made this recipe, but still good tasting.

I just realized this recipe was written nine years ago ago, likely during the early wave of Korean food trends. As a Korean who loves spicy food and cozy, gooey comfort dishes, I found this one overly spicy in an unbalanced way.Honestly, I wouldn’t recommend making it. The recipe could really use an update to reflect the authentic Buldak( Firely Chicken), a dish known for its unforgettable harmony of fiery heat and subtle sweetness. This version feels too Americanized and misses the authenticity.

This is one of the best NYT recipes I've made. Made as written, although I added more water and cooked the thighs a bit longer because they were bone in. SO good and easy. Not too spicy. I found gochugaru (the Korean red-pepper flakes) at a Korean market. I'd say it's worth seeking out because it has a unique sweet+smoky flavor. I found frozen rice cakes at the same market. They added good texture to the dish. Try this one. It will be in regular rotation at our house.
Delicious
This was absolutely delicious and my whole family asked to put it into dinner rotation!

We kept the ginger and garlic out of the marinade and briefly heated it in the pan before adding the marinated chicken. This seemed to improve the flavor. Also, we added chopped red chard to the dish at the end. Very good. We served it without rice in a bowl along with corn on the cob.
delicious but we'll cut the cheese by 50% next time, way too much cheese

Easy delicious recipe. Must include the rice cakes and fry until crunchy. Once mixed with chicken and sauce, the rice cakes have a nice chewy, crunchy texture. I've never had this served to me with cheese in Korea and omitted it. The one time I don't agree with the fabulous Maangchi.
Added about 3/4 cup of chicken stock at the end and simmered the chicken. My kids ate every drop of the sauce with rice!
Just made this for dinner. So easy, so tasty.

This was delicious.!! So yummy. I cut back on the pepper flakes bc I was serving people who can’t take a lot of spice. I’ve made twice and still haven’t made full force. With about 3/4 of the pepper flakes than the recipe suggests, it gives great heat and lots of flavor. Neither time did I do the rice cakes (couldn’t find) but served over rice. Everyone raved, different recipients :) Reheats perfectly too.

While I was surprised by the amount of heat from Korean spices, mostly gochugaru, I enjoyed the taste as it was not sharp.  Next time I will halve gochugaru amount.  I also cooked the chicken five extra minutes with extra water as mine dried out (used cast iron skillet) and added corn per someone's recommendation.
This dish was really delicious. I only used about 2 teaspoons of the gochugara spice. I followed the recipe exactly after that. So quick. Soooo good. Many thanks.
Can I mix in the chicken several hours before cooking it? Serving tonight and I want no mistakes…

You can make your own SCOBY.  Prepare one-half the basic tea and sugar recipe.  Add a bottle or two of high quality store-bought kombucha and ferment as directed in the basic recipe.  You will start to see the SCOBY form on the top.  Add more tea and sugar in the correct proportion as the SCOBY develops.  Once the SCOBY is large enough to remove from the top of the brew, use it to make the first batch of Kombucha.

I believe you omitted the need to remove/strain the tea leaves before adding the sweet tea to the SCOBY jar. The sweet tea should be completely free of any organic material during the first stage of fermentation. (For years I have used a mixture of 2/3 green tea to 1/3 black, removing the black tea bag after the first five minutes of steeping, allowing the green to remain until the mixture cools. This creates a less bitter, clearer tea.)
Hi there! Thanks for your point. We have tweaked the recipe to make it crystal-clear when the tea bags should be removed, before straining the tea in Step 3.

Please don't confuse this kombucha, though it has a Japanese name, with real kombucha as I did.  In Japan, kombu or kobu is an edible kelp which is boiled and flavored with salt and sometimes sour pickled plum, and enjoyed as tea or soup stock.  According to Japanese Wikipedia, the completely different probiotic drink became popular in Russia and Europe, and at some point, an English speaker confused it with kombu, and hence, the name stuck.  I learned something today!  Thanks for this article.
If you have everything else but the Scoby mat, you can do without it, it just takes a little longer to ferment. Your kombucha will grow a new Scoby after about a week.
What is a scoby mat? Is that the tough skin that is atop the softer, darker part of the scoby? Or is the mat the whole scoby, skin and darker part together?
The Scoby mat is the entire mat.

You also forgot to mention a critical step in the secondary fermentation: you must burp the bottles daily or risk exploding glass. It's a real thing. Some batches are super bubbly, some aren't; but you should be burping the bottle daily. On the last day of secondary fermentation, i'll burp the bottles in the morning, and the stick in the fridge later that night.
I have never burped the bottles daily. I do not over fill the bottles, though. I leave space at the top. I do hear a bit of a pop when I open a bottle to drink it, but I do not have explosions.

While it's true that you don't want Kombucha to be exposed to direct sunlight, allowing the Kombucha to ferment in a cool place actually increases the likelihood of mold development, and it takes more time. Fermenting at temperatures between 75 and 85 degrees Fahrenheit will produce a better tasting Kombucha and reduce likelihood of mold development. I use a heating mat around the glass jar to achieve these temps. The people at Kombucha Kamp have a great book and recipes.

For the second fermentation, I recommend placing the jars inside an empty ice chest/cooler.   The pressure can cause the bottles to explode, especially if you're reusing them, as most people do.  Easier to clean a cooler than the walls/ceiling and contents of your closet or wherever you've put them for those few days!  Hasn't happened to me, but has to a few friends!

I made my own SCOBY in a quart mason jar with half sweet tea as above and a bottle of plain GTs kombucha- try to find plain, not flavored, as you flavor just your second ferment (2F)  The clear jelly layer that will form on top is your SCOBY.  Your first "brew", you will swear is "bad".  Brown stringy stuff (yeast byproducts) and a vinegary smell.  All normal and you're doing great!  Lots of blogs online for more info... I've been making it for years and love it!  Good luck!!!

The longer you brew, the more probiotic it becomes.  I love this hobby because you can forget your booch for weeks and it just gets better!  Add 10-20 percent fresh juice (not just ginger) in the second ferment to get natural carbonation.  Just be careful not to let it explode:  pineapple is the fastest carbonator  and can get wild in just a week if the weather is warm. Do NOT strain until serving. Experiment with different teas (anything EXCEPT Earl Grey) and juices. Keep brew away from bread!

Try it using oolong tea. Very delicious. I use 2 liters water, Tabs tea, bit less than 1/2 cup sugar. Favourite flavour is putting a raspberry in each bottle for the second fermentation, perfect and it all goes pink.

I have made Kombucha for a number of years. You can make your own scoby. It takes about two weeks. Set everything up as noted here, add your starter (the store bought bottle of flavourless kombucha) and let it sit for about two weeks in a dark corner with a constant temperature. Your scoby will form. I've done this twice, once out of necessity, once just to remind myself how easy it is.

Suggest the following:
1.  organic, loose tea--or at least, organic tea in a bag. ( Amazon)
2.  Green tea (with some black) results in fizzier, healthier drink
3.  Brew for 5 mins--this reduces some of the caffeine & avoids risk of
bitterness
3.  Temp. of 'cooled' tea, before adding 'Mother' needs to be 65-85 degrees or we risk killing the mother
4.  To each bottle, add 1/4-l/2 tsp. Vit. C powder (Amazon)-, some organic, dried hibiscus---for health &  color
5. Suggest used Kombucha bottles
You can use a commercial kombucha to start your scoby, but make sure it is raw and not pasteurized. You will usually see sediment in the bottom of the bottle. That is the start of a scoby.
Look at beet kvass also for fermented beverage ideas
I personally don't use juice. You can use any combination of herbs and raw or frozen fruit for your second fermentation and it's much more of an adventure.

Super addition to morning green drinks - even adding some of the SCOBY if you choose. I've been making Kombucha for years; sharing SCOBY's with our local Buy Nothing Facebook group here in Talent Oregon. A great probiotic for a clean and vital gut.

I offer a caveat about taking on the over-enthusiasm for kombucha. My mom had a weird reaction from kombucha she and her husband made. It manifested in an inability to form complex language, almost like a mini-stroke. Luckily she recovered when she embarked on a (mostly) sugar-free diet, so I think it was related to a yeast growth within her body, exacerbated by additional yeast and sugar in the kombucha.

I also have had a weird reaction to homemade kombucha. Twice my vision has become “shaky”, for lack of a better word, and I had to pull over to let whatever was happening “pass”. I can drink kombucha sold in stores with no trouble. Both times it was my aunt’s kombucha but different batches. Any thoughts?
Like Carrie, I briefly lost my vision after drinking homemade kombucha. It was probably only 30-40 seconds, but I will never drink kombucha again.

I have always heard that a Scoby could not come in contact with metal because it may kill it.  Because of this fact, I question cutting a Scoby with a  scissors to separate them. I always take off all jewelry before doing my Kombucha.

Metal is only a problem for long-term contact, and then you might be concerned about the acid in the kombucha reacting with reactive metals. SCOBYs are wonderfully resilient and really don't require much fussing.

Have made kombucha many times but damaged/ruined my Scoby last year and have not taken the time to ask others for some of theirs. So very good to know that you can make your own Scoby from store bought kombucha.  Have been wondering about this for some time.  Thanks.
Makes good booch. But why all the straining? Ive been doing it without.

You can make a scoby by buying a bottle of kombucha, pouring a cup into an open mouthed jar, adding a teaspoon of sugar, and waiting a few days or a week. It'll form a nice scoby and you're all set to make more kombucha!

Tip: simple syrup with at 1:1 raw cane sugar water ratio first helps a lot. Do not economize on the type of sugar! Make a cup/Picardy glass of tea. Let tea and syrup cool.strain tea.  Sterilize Mason jar or vase with wide open top. Add sugar syrup until sterized jar is 1/8 full. Weigh scoby, double the weight is the max amount of kombucha you can add. Add kombucha liquid to Sterilized Mason jar. Add scoby. Add water, do not fill to top leave at least 3 cm air.  Cover with clean kitchen towel.
Kombucha liquid can make great syrup! Just make a reduction! It can be used in salad dressing or on pancakes.  I was inspired by the noma book on fermentation.  Much recommended

jun is a cousin of kombucha in that instead of black tea, substitute green tea and instead of sugar use honey. i go organic with both. it ferments more quickly and has a lighter taste. jun scobys are available online. it was love at first sip for me. the minimal prep time is so worth it compared with buying bottles that end up needing recycling.  yummy and so good for tummy!

What is the purpose of the thumb depression, and do you put that side down or up to start?
Thumb imprint down. The protein contracts as it cooks. No dimple will form the classic fat belly hamburger. The dimple will result in a hockey puck shape. Both taste the same.
Burgers puff up on the grill. If you start with the center pushed down slightly, it’ll wind up reasonably flat.
Good recipe, but ...... if you make the beef patties as directed but slightly smaller, about 6 oz each max you’ll be happier.

The problem with this dish is the main ingredient, "curry powder". In India, 20+ variants go under that name. Some South Indian variants use loads of coriander+cumin roasted until dark brown and almost bitter (plus mildly bitter ingredients like ground fenugreek seed, an acquired taste) and need to be combined with a sour base like tomato or tamarind. Parsi variants are fragrant and low on cayenne. 
Knowing what ingredients the "curry powder" contains would make the recipe usable.

The “curry” powder of the Caribbean is bright yellow with turmeric and not very spicy.  My favorite Jamaican brand includes only Tumeric, coriander, foenugreek, salt, cumin, allspice, pepper, and garlic. It’s pretty representative of the style.

Thanks for your note. I actually think the curry powder here is a feature not a bug. It's a Caribbean curry powder we're using, as Karolyn Schalk notes below (or above?), what I call murky and yellow, heavy on the turmeric. It's sometimes labeled "Jamaican" curry powder in stores, other times as "British-style."

If this were a classically Indian dish, you'd be right. But Carribean cooking is touched by Chinese, Indian, African & British flavors, so maybe the curry powder used is not as critical. I haven't yet made this dish, but I do find that Carribean cooking is very "forgiving" about substitutions and tweaking! Here in the NYC area, many stores carry something called Jamaican Curry powder, so I'm off to get some! I'll save my Patak's curry paste for another day.
Does 2 shallots = 2 bulbs or 2 cloves?  Of which size?  Since shallots come in a wide range of sizes, it would be better to have a measurement for this in tsp or tbsp.
Shallots are small - so it means two whole shallots.

In a recipe like this the exact amount of shallots really doesn't matter -- it's to your taste: If you like shallots, add more; if you're unsure, add less; if you don't like shallots, omit them. That being said, when a recipe calls for two shallots, it usually means two whole bulbs, not two cloves.

Unlike Phil I have seen some giant-sized shallots (and lamented the disappearance of the small ones I grew up on; I turn cartwheels when I find them still small) and, I regret, the flavor is not the same as the smaller ones. I think William is right... it's really to your taste but, to my mind, one is aiming for a subtler flavor from them so look for as small as you can (unless you really like their flavor).

This is my ultimate fav brand of curry to use for West Indian dishes.  Curry, Oxtail etc.   Kala Brand Madras Curry Powder from Trinidad.  You can find this on Amazon or in Caribbean markets, where we have lots here in Toronto.

Wow! I happened to have some leftover rice in the fridge, so I made this right away. What are curry leaves? Curry is a mixture of spices, not a specific plant. Or does the term refer to the leaves of the Thai lime tree, which are fragrant and used in Thai cooking?

From Wikipedia:

The curry tree also known as sweet neem or kadi patta or curry vepila, is a tropical to sub-tropical tree in the family Rutaceae, which is native to India. Its leaves are used in many dishes in Indian subcontinent.

Curry leaves are from the curry leaf plant. Very aromatic, different to lime. Leaves can be bought at Asian / Indian groceries (or here in Australia in the supermarket). Fresh is best, but you can get dried. They freeze well.

Curry leaves come from a tropical bush common in India and are used in a variety of southern Indian dishes, not just those called ‘curries’. They are not related to curry powder. The flavor is distinct and not easily substituted. They can be found in a good quality Indian grocery store. I buy them in bulk and freeze the ones I can’t use.

Curry powder is not a quantifiable thing.  It is a blend of whatever the blender feels like and has laying around when it is made. Even if you were to quantify ratios I suspect the base ingredients vary so much in strength, age and pungency as to make any Indian recipe little more than a stab in the dark.
I giggle when I read instructions saying toss your spices after four months. How old they were when you purchased them is anyone’s guess.

Teach us to fish, don’t give us a fish.

There’s a fine recipe for Jamaican curry powder (not too hot) at www.africanbites.com) which should (1) match up with the general trend of the recipe, (2) is also excellent for a curry goat or kedgeree, and (3) means, if you make it yourself, that you know exactly what’s in it and how old it is. No need to toss it after 4 months, it’ll be used up well before then!
If you get spices from Penzey's, they are fresh and they are dated. I don't work for Penzey's or know anyone who does. Just a big fan.

A curry leaf is the leaf of a curry leaf plant, not to be confused with a curry plant, a rather attractive plant with grayish leaves and a distinct curry aroma. As far as I've been able to tell, the curry plant has no particular culinary  value. On the other hand, the curry leaf plant is used in a variety of Indian dishes. It imparts a distinct flavor, but it is not an identifiable "curry" flavor.

To replicate both the herbal and citrus notes in curry leaves, use the zest of a single lime to replace 8 curry leaves. Also use the same amount of basil that your recipe indicates for curry leaves.

Chicken bone in thighs:
8 tsp light soy (or regular)
2 tbl lime or lemon juice
2 cloves garlic minced
4 tsp grated fresh ginger
2 tsp ground coriander
1 tsp sugar
1/2 tsp cayenne pepper
4 bone in skinless chicken thighs

Mix 1st 7 ingredients & add to thighs.  Marinate at least 3 hrs.  Grill thighs

Are the fried curry leaves used as a garnish (as it appears in the photo) or are they crumbled and stirred through the seasoned rice? I gather they are edible, and not, like bay leaves, used to add flavor but never eaten. Is that right?
Yes, curry leaves are deliciously edible, especially when fried (preferred way of eating)! No need to crumble them after frying. Just toss the whole leaves through the finished dish.

Curry leaves are a push in Montana. I used the lime zest and basil and cut the basil leaves length-wise to better distribute throughout. This is a fantastic flavor curry. I served with seared pork chops.

Would Kaffir Lime leaves be OK?

Although kaffir lime leaves are also an awesome ingredient from that part of the world, they are very different from the leaves of the curry (kari) plant (which is unrelated to curry powder, for those who are unaware ... although clearly many commenters here know their curry powder!).

imho (as taste is of course subjective), yes and no. i've cooked with both curry leaves and lime leaves and curry leaves have a smell that is kind of funky, honestly to me it has notes of fresh greenery and burnt electronics. it's hard to describe, but they really are lovely when fried and mixed into dishes. lime leaves are delicious in their own right too, so i think if you used them here, you'd probably still have a good tasting dish but i wouldn't consider it a substitute.
Kaffir lime leaves are not the same as curry leaves. It's a different flavor profile.

Can anyone recommend a good oyster sauce that does not have MSG in it?

All oyster sauce is laced with added MSG, though shellfish are naturally savory: read Harold McGee's "On Food and Cooking" for why this is so. While the harms of MSG are illusory (it sickens you only if you believe it will), I agree that excessive MSG makes a dish taste one-dimensional. 

To substitute, I'd use a more complex umami source: e.g.,make the rice with meat or shrimp stock (the latter made from shrimp heads/shells) instead of water. (Paul Prudhomme said "Water's only for washing!")

There are vegetarian equivalents to oyster sauce that likely don't have it, and you can make it yourself. They are usually based on shiitake mushroom and some other umami stuff, like miso. I found a good sounding one googling (omnivore's cookbook).

Check Panda green label, it has no MSG.  In my opinion, Lee Kum Kee Premium is better, but it does contain MSG.  When checking the ingredients, look for a sauce that actually contains oyster extract, and the higher on the list the better.

Several variants of this are in the Indian cooking - both north and south. Curry originated from India and was absorbed and changed across Britain and Jamaica mainly. Turmeric, cumin, coriander, curry leaves, and chili powder are native across many Indian recipes. Prakash points out some of these variants. As food is globalizing even more now, and secrets are being revealed, i see a lot more variants emerging among all of us.

This is tasty, but note that it's not 1 lb dried noodles - the recipe says fresh or frozen, but those are hard to find where I am, so just a note for those of us working from dry. I used 1 lb dried noodles and wound up with enough noodles to feed an army.

Hi Meg,  When using fresh pasta in place of dried pasta, the amount used will need to be adjusted because when cooked, dried pasta will produce close to 60% more than the fresh pasta.  So you will need 1lb 10oz of fresh pasta to be roughly equivalent to 1lb of dried pasta. Generally a portion of uncooked pasta is 4oz (1cup uncooked makes 2 cups cooked) for a main course and 2.5oz for a side (5/8 cup uncooked gives 1 1/4 cups cooked).
Agreed... 5 or 6 ounces of noodles is more than enough for two.

Really enjoyed this. I make a similar sauce all the time, but for some reason the proportions in this one "clicked." I admittedly used tahini.

I doubled the sauce, used 8 oz of dried whole wheat spaghetti, and threw in baked tofu, steamed broccoli, and halved cherry tomatoes. Making again tonight with turkey cutlets, red bell pepper, and spinach.
We made this. It was delicious. We added a bunch of sauteed mixed veggies--snow peas, carrots, broccoli, greens, etc.

- 1.5X the sauce
- add carrots, red peppers, scallions, peapods, bean sprouts, etc.
- add chicken or shrimp add cilantro
- you can prepare noodles and added vegetables ahead of time
- add a minimal amount of sauce to noodles and chill
- just before serving, add more sauce to taste.
I used almond butter instead of peanut butter, and it was delicious.
Radishes! Radishes have become popular and make a lovely, crunchy contrast thinly sliced and added on top of the plated dish.

This has become a staple in our household.  So easy to make, and so delicious.  Real comfort food.

What I love most about the recipe is how forgiving it is.  If you're short on one ingredient, or don't feel like measuring everything exactly, it still always turns out well.

Love this recipe because it's so versatile and so easily doable at the last moment with ingredients at hand. Am diabetic so I go low on or omit the sugar altogether.. In a pinch I've used balsamic (a cheaper variety) which gives sweet and tang all at once. I prefer crunchy peanut butter myself and when garlic chilly paste is not around just crush a few cloves of garlic with dried red chilies. I also add chopped scallions and cilantro at the end. Minced pork or lamb to make a one-dish meal.
For those who start with dry noodle, double the sauce. You'll thank me later.

Just made this tonight. It was delish! I also doubled the sauce, and also could not find sesame paste at my local grocery store so I used extra peanut butter as well- 1 tbsp. I used Barilla High Protein noodles. Extras I added in were 4 sliced scallion stalks (green and white parts), and one cup of salad-in-a-bag chopped cabbage, julienned carrots, broccoli.

When you make this, and YOU WILL MAKE THIS dish. make sure you double the recipe because people absolutely love it! My suggestion is to make it as the recipe says and then start adding/changing/adjusting the recipe to your taste....just make it!
Excellent summer recipe!  I added finely chopped red peppers, red cabbage, and carrots for some veggie crunch.

Easy and very tasty. For me, adding broccoli is a must, because it tastes great with the sauce, and it's a nice counter to an otherwise heavy dish. Tofu or other vegetables work, too (I might try it with egg). I always cut the sugar in 1/2, and usually use a little less sesame oil and peanut butter, too. The Chinese sesame paste definitely tastes better than tahini in this dish, if you can find it.

I'm using shirataki noodles to cut down on calories and carbs, and I came across recipe this for ideas. My adapted, very simple version: One tablespoon each of sesame oil, rice vinegar, soy sauce and peanut butter; slash of Sriracha; one green onion, chopped. Whisk to incorporate the peanut butter. Add 4 oz. shirataki noodles. Amazing.

I always use sesame paste diluted with sesame oil then thin out with a little water. Then add all the listed ingredients. No need to use peanut butter. The sauce will stay emulsified. If more protein is needed add shredded cold chicken with the noodles.
So good — added green onions and cilantro and used ramen noodles

Made with these modifications and it was a dynamite one-dish meal:
- Linguini 
- Tahini (roasted) (could not find Chinese sesame paste)
- Natural crunchy peanut butter
- Doubled sauce recipe for 1lb pasta
- Additional optional toppings: raw red pepper, cilantro, lime wedges, baked tofu cubes, chicken, steamed broccoli
- Mung sprouts & sesame seeds would have been dreamy additions

Not that this needs another rave review, but this recipe is simple and excellent. I didn’t even have the proper Chinese ingredients and used regular old tahini and some Japanese rice vinegar (plus I subbed honey for sugar), and they were stellar. I’d eat these every day!!

I used an 8oz packet of rice noodles.  Added extra peanut butter because I had no tahini or sesame paste at home.  Served in a big bowl and topped with julienned orange and yellow bell peppers, cucumber and shredded chicken to make it more of a complete meal.  Hubbs LOVED it, kids ate without complaint.  A big win.
What kind of noodles?

This has become a staple in our household and it makes everyone (toddlers, kids, grown-ups) happy. We've found that if you do 1.5x the recipe, that's the right amount of sauce for 1 pound of thin spaghetti, which is the kind of pasta we always have in the pantry. The kids seem to like it with a little less rice vinegar and just a splash of chili-garlic sauce (grown-ups can add more sambal/sriracha/hot sauce to taste).
I added more Sesame oil and soy sauce, eliminated the sesame paste and added more peanut butter. I used the ginger and garlic in a tube and it came out great.

I went to a Chinese grocery and picked up the ingredients.  Fortunately a few people there knew some english!  Out of curiousity what is the difference if any between rice vinegar and rice wine vinegar - just brewed and and seasoned?  It's a bit confusing.  Also, I didn't find anything just labeled egg noodles. They had "wonton noodles" and when I read the ingredients and saw they had eggs and others did not, I bought those noodles.
This was delicious! I dont think I'd change anything. Could add some green veggies or a little chicken to make it a great take to work lunch.will look for a chewier noodle.
This recipe is also known as Wuhan Dry Noodles.  Often eaten for breakfast in that part of China.  Delicious with cilantro and some of those Chinese spicy pickled condiments.
We have probably prepared these, exactly as directed, 10 or more times.  Our only problem is the debate about which is better, served hot, or the next day for lunch, cold.

The recipe calls for "sesame oil", (which is clear and light), but in the video it looks dark and thick - like toasted sesame oil, (which has a much stronger sesame flavor). I'm betting it is the latter.

This was amazing. I was wary of including peanut butter for it tasting too much like peanut butter but omg it’s perfect. I used 2 TBS toasted sesame seeds instead of the paste. 3 cloves of garlic. I put everything in my vitamix and blended. I poured it over zoodles and added cilantro, scallions, and sesame seeds. It was one of the best recipes I’ve ever made. Used 1 TBS of honey instead of the sugar. Used Tamari instead of soy sauce. This is so amazing.

I only used peanut butter and sesame oil because that's what my dad told me to do. He said peanut butter has more flavor and sesame oil is stronger than sesame paste and i also cut down half of the garlic and ginger because i am not a huge fan of either but it is still really good. I also didn't add peanut but instead toast sesame. Everyone loved it at the party. My dad said chinese recipe is very flexible follow the general guideline then tweak however you want.
Leave out sugar

Don’t let the sesame paste stop you from making this quick and easy meal! It’s also very forgiving. I used a bit more pnut butter in place of sesame paste and forgot the rice vinegar but still turned out great. Cooked chopped bok choy w noodles and added a sliced bell pepper and scallions also. Everything came together, including chopping and prepping, under 30 mins.

Don't sauce the noodles until serving. Just dress them with some sesame oil.
This is a great recipe. I double the sauce recipe for 1# dried spaghetti if I cant get fresh Chinese noodles. I also sub in extra peanut butter when I'm out of Chinese sesame sauce.
Peanut butter and soy vay teriyaki make an easy alternative.

I've made various versions of this for years. I like making the sauce a day ahead of time to let the flavors meld, then adding to noodles shortly before serving.  I've put the sauce on shredded cabbage (even the bagged stuff meant for Cole slaw). I like cucumber and scallion garnish, though I've also made it with chicken and asparagus. Sometimes I'll make the sauce and use it as a dip for raw veggies.  I like to make it in a food processor, because it becomes light and fluffy, and easy to mix in

I have made this recipe once so far and it was delicious! I am definitely going to make it again. Although I think there should be more sauce, it was good anyhow. Instead of cucumber, I used peas. I am a kid and this was fun to make.
I've made a version of this for years. But how silly to use creamy peanut butter and then add chopped peanuts! CRUNCHY peanut butter, people!

Completely disagree. More aesthetically pleasing when the peanuts are used as a garnish. Also, the contrasting rough texture and concentrated salt tang of the peanuts is muted when coated in peanut butter. It takes all of 15 seconds to coarsely chop those peanuts, people! PS: If you want to ensure that everyone gets a bit of the garnishes in their serving, simply serve additional chopped peanuts and julienned cucumber on the side.
When I make my egg noodles, I put a big dollop of sesame paste into the dough.

The first time I made this dish I couldn't find sesame seed paste and substituted with tahini. Don't do this. I made the dish a second time and used sesame paste. It was far better.

This is a good tutorial for chopping up the cucumber.
https://youtu.be/6Ol8guFt7g0

I have made this dish with a variety of noodles (Thai, Chinese, Japanese), and found that a good quality spaghetti noodle seems to result in the best end product. They have a little more tooth and are much easier to find (I went to two Chinese supermarkets and still couldn't find fresh egg noodles).
Also, it's better you get the Chinese-style chili-garlic sauce, not the Thai-style.

These are now a staple of mine. I often serve them with Chinese broccoli in oyster sauce (dim sum style).

I used dried spaghetti (without egg) here and it was perfect. This substitution makes the dish vegan as well. It's a great make-ahead dish - divide the oiled noodles into four containers, add the sauce on top (without stirring), and then the garnishes on top of that. Before eating, allow it to come to room temperature, then stir well and enjoy.

Thanks you Sam Sifton!! This recipe is so good. Just made it, and writing this after eating way more than I intended to. Some notes: I used more low sodium Soy Sauce, so if you do the same you'll need to add much more than what the recipe calls for, perhaps twice as much. I also used several veggies: mushrooms, zucchini, bell pepper, and fresh bakchoi. All of them I sauteed and cooled before adding. I also used a sambal chili paste, instead of garlic chili and grated in a little extra Ginger.

The classic recipe for cold noodles with sesame sauce is by Craig Claiborne and Virginia Lee in The Chinese Cookbook. You can use either peanut butter or toasted sesame paste. It comes out thick and delicious, clinging to the pasta. Claiborne and Lee top it with a chicken breast.

I haven't found the recipe on-line, but the book is still available on Amazon. It is my family's favorite sauce over Chinese noodles or spaghetti. Topped with some thinly sliced scallion, it can't be beat!

I found their INGREDIENTS: Craig Claiborne and Virginia Lee's The Chinese Cookbook for 2 to 4 servings, from 4 ounces of fine egg noodles, calls for:
1/4 cup sesame paste
3 T brewed tea or water
2 T hot oil (Optional, but if you want spicy, it's got to be there)
3 T light soy sauce (that is, the regular kind)
3 T red wine vinegar
2 tsp sugar
salt
1/4 tsp MSG (optional) [this recipe dates from 1972!]
1/4 cup peanut oil
2 T chopped garlic
1 T sesame oil

I made the Claiborne/Lee recipe many years ago(~30) from the recipe in the times for a large crowd and it was a smash success! Some thought it was the best sesame noodles they'd ever had.This one sounds good and easy as well. Can't wait to try it.

This was honestly incredible.  I have made a version of this myself for years but just eyeballing the ingredients.  But this recipe just makes the perfect balance of all the flavors to create the best tasting sesame noodles I’ve ever had in my life.  Even my mom, a Taiwan native and noodle aficionado, couldn’t stop eating it!  I followed the recipe faithfully, but only had tahini one hand so added another tablespoon of sesame oil to the sauce.
I wish they would put what kind of noodle. I get overwhelmed when I go to my Asian grocery store and the fresh noodle section. There are SO many types, sizes and variations! Help!
@Maggi me too! Which noodles should I use?!

Sichuan peppercorns are available from Oaktown Spice Shop in Oakland, CA. You can order them online, too:

http://oaktownspiceshop.com/collections/peppercorns/products/szechuan-pe...
I would add Scallions to this as a garnish. Kinda crucial ingredient if imho. The sauce came out a bit runny as well. I would definitely add more peanut butter
Is there a substitute for the peanut butter? My daughters have peanut/tree nut allergies,  and I have always missed this dish.....
Wow butter it's a soy butter it's amazing
Sunflower butter. It worked great in this recipe for us.

IndoChineseLover, I would say a nice vegan sub would be Japanese soba noodles made of buckwheat, water and salt. It gives the same chew and adds a nice nutty quality.

This recipe makes me really happy as it answers my sesame noodle search!

I followed the directions exactly and it was okay. I am going to try adding shredded purple cabbage, scallions, julienned carrots, and chicken to it. Maybe I was expecting something a little more complex even though this is a recipe for "Take-out Noddles"!

Yummy. Like a couple of other reviewers, I had an issue with the noodles becoming really dry after sitting for about an hour before being served. The sauce was nice and silky when I first tossed it in with the noodles, but an hour later, there was no "sauce" to speak of and the noodles became fairly dense. But great flavor. I added the Sichuan peppercorns and I think they enhanced the flavor a lot.
Rinsing the noodles really well helps keep them from getting sticky.
I sometimes use tamarind paste instead of sesame paste. YUM!

Was a bit dry, and I thought actually tasted better warm.
I used the Chinese noodles in dry form. Next time, I'd use less porous noodles.
I had considered serving warm, making them tonight,so I might go with it, thanks

I've made this numerous times, usually with soba noodles since I always have those around. This time I used fresh Chinese egg noodles and they were great. Nice and chewy. I always slice up whatever fresh vegetables I have, red pepper, scallions, cucumber, jalapeños, carrots; extra garlic and ginger is a must. If you don't have peanuts sprinkle with toasted sesame seeds. I was out of Chinese sesame paste so used tahini instead, along with twice the peanut butter called for. It came out great.
Had tahini and sriracha on hand for substitution. Also, added a little steamed then chilled broccoli to stretch as a one course main dish.  Really delicious!  Will definitely make again.
Hint: Sesame paste = tahini
Tahini is not the same thing as Chinese sesame paste
you can just add one tablespoon of sesame oil to tahini to make it a little more similar to sesame paste. even better if you toast the oil before adding it!
Dress small amount and chill.  Then add rest when serve.  Double sauce recipe for 1 lb pasta.  For a potluck consider 1.5 to 2 lbs.   with 9 people, 1 lb wasn’t enough.

I followed the tip of using Tahini because it's what I had on hand.  Next time I make it I'll double the sauce as others suggested.  Very flavorful and very adaptable to your own tastes.  This will make a great easy summer dinner.

I've made this dish multiple times... last night was the first time with Chinese rice vinegar AND Chinese sesame/peanut paste (a thing I bought by mistake at the Buford Highway Farmers Market last week).  It was revelatory with authentic ingredients.  Highly recommend seeking a source for them.  Wow!

Hands-down, my favorite NYT cooking recipe of all time. Over nearly 10 years I have made this countless times, with endless combinations of noodles, proteins on top and vegetables/garnishes. It never disappoints. This is my go-to meal to bring to friends who just had a baby or are recovering from surgery. It even facilitated a verrrry picky 3 year old boy eating vegetables! Now that I am busy with my own baby, I make a x16 batch of the sauce and freeze it in smaller portions, which makes for a quick and delicious meal after boiling some noodles. The sauce is also very good tossed with cabbage to make a slaw or drizzled on salmon and rice. All in all, a real winner!
I made this, just with the tahini and without the peanut butter. Delicious! Also added some chopped scallion and some leftover cooked veg and chicken.

I added shredded carrots, snow peas (from the garden), salmon. I used almond butter in lieu of peanut butter. It totally disappeared in one sitting and I'll make it again. So easy and I had most of the ingredients in the pantry. I like the idea of adding mango - maybe next time. And maybe a little more heat - we like it spicy.
I used korean sesame/garlic paste and extra chili garlic paste. Also mixed in a large handful of chopped cilantro because we all love it. Excellent!

I'm clearly in the minority here because I didn't care for this at all, despite using the exact ingredients and following the recipe as written. I found it gloppy with dulled flavors and needed to do lots of repairing to make it appealing. I think there are other far better recipes for this classic dish, or maybe I should try again using these ideas from other cooks.

For the person looking for Asian ingredients above Chinatown, the M2M market on Broadway near Columbia University is very good.

Made this for the 1st time tonight while watching Serena win the 3rd round in the Open which accounts for my adding 2 T chili paste instead of 2 tsp!  I also added toasted ground Szechuan peppercorns but only 1 tsp and they definitely provided another dimension to the taste riot in my mouth.  Turned out very good...but now I want ice cream!
did not have access to either pastes so I used tomato paste, 1/4 tsp. chili powder, 1/8 tsp. cayenne and more garlic; next time I would cut back on cayenne, pinch?, and garlic
used Western Family Wide Egg Noodles; probably the only poster who doesn't shop at boutique stores
Not really.  Many of the recipes have hard to find ingredients and can be substituted or in some cases omitted.

These are the ones I use in different recipes...can be in Asian food section at Wal-Mart, Safeway, Albertson's, etc.
Cheap too!

http://www.amazon.com/dp/B00BQXBDTM/ref=asc_df_B00BQXBDTM3995337?smid=A2...
What's a boutique store?

Any pasta works fine. Even GF. I substitute sunbutter for a nut free version and coconut aminos for a soy free end result. Still amazing! And, I use tahini in place of Chinese sesame paste. Very forgiving recipe. Have fun and enjoy!
Sprinkle sesame seeds on top - to give an authentic texture.

Great pantry recipe, very adaptable. I left out one ingredient, the sesame paste, as a trip to the store for one item wasn't happening. Also didn't have the sambal, so I used Gochujang since I have a tub of the stuff, worked out great. Threw broccoli into the pasta pot (had soba noodles on hand) for a minute at the end and topped with some green onions from the garden, white and green parts. Delicious. I think the best part is that I have everything on hand since I cook Asian food regularly.
Forgot to mention that I used 9.5 oz package of Udon - just the right amount of noodles!
If you’re a stickler for proportions, the best hack I’ve found for the Chinese sesame paste is to mix 1 part tahini combined with 1/4 part toasted sesame oil. The sauce is addictive!

I've made this many, many times.  Make the effort to find real Chinese sesame paste. It makes all the difference. The recipe does not make enough sauce for 1lb of noodles.  I up the soy sauce, sesame oil, and rice vinegar by 1 tablespoon and the sesame paste and pb by 1/2 tablespoon. I don't add sugar and eyeball the fresh ginger and garlic.  I use the Simply Asian LoMein noodles which are easily found in supermarkets and W*mart.  I also add shredded left over chicken.

I just ate so much of this my stomach hurts, which I think merits a 5 star-rating. That being said, in a caricature of NYT Cooking notes, I added 1/2 head sauteed cabbage, a bunch of green onions, and 8 ounces mushrooms.

We got home after a long day out on Easter Sunday and realized that no one had the time, energy or appetite for the elaborate meal I'd planned to make. Enter these noodles to save the day! I added a cup of frozen shelled edamame and about a pound of fresh broccoli florets to the pasta during the last three minutes of cooking for some extra protein, vitamins and fiber. Used bog-standard spaghetti and regular old tahini. This made five delicious portions for us! Will absolutely make again.

The sauce is important, and looks wonderful. But I would argue that the kind of noodles used are equally important, as almost the entire texture of the dish.  So to be left with no notes, no guidance, no description other than “one pound noodles, frozen or fresh,” is particularly unhelpful, at least for those of us not super experienced with Asian cooking. I know hundreds of people in the comments give their two cents on what noodles they used, but regular old dry spaghetti can’t be right. Help?
Used tahini w/extra sesame oil & chili oil instead of chili-garlic paste, plus a little extra garlic. Loved it. Tasted just like the takeout noodles I remember.

The sugar is overkill.  Especially as most peanut butter and rice vinegar used is sweetened.  This dish works best with the addition of finely cut veg, scallions, chillis,cukes, slivered red peppers, and tons of cilantro.

Used 12 oz of dried egg linguine. After draining pasta, added a little sauce, sesame oil, and water, tossed and allowed to cool.
Doubled the sauce. Use tahini which worked fine. Just added a little extra PB and sesame oil. Used about 2/3 of the sugar.
To the noodles, added: julienne carrots and cucumbers, sliced napa cabbage, scallions, mung bean sprouts, fresh snap peas, a chopped up pan-fried large chicken breast. Forgot cilantro.
Very tasty. Will make again. Made a ton with all my additions.

I have made this loads of times and love it. I usually double the sauce as I just love sauce. I can never be bothered to add the cucumber or peanuts but sometimes add spring onions if I have some. So nice! Leftovers a few days old are still good, sitting around doesn't make it suffer at all.

A refreshing switch-up from Mark Bittman's sesame noodles, which I've made countless times. Very easy to make, and I tossed with roasted broccoli from the farmstand. Haven't made these noodles in a while and pleased to return to them. Being the wimp that I am, however, I'll reduce the chili paste to one teaspoon next time; wouldn't leave it out completely, however, as it adds a refreshing kick, even for a Midwestern wimp raised on German meat and potatoes.
Can one use either chili garlic paste or chili garlic sauce in this recipe?  Also, can someone recommend an easy-to-find brand of paste?
I made this last night for dinner and used chili garlic sauce.  Result was fantastic!

I made this dish twice before giving it 5 stars. I cut back on the soy sauce and added a big move peanut butter and tahini. A good squeeze of fresh mandarin orange made it perfect for us.
Served it with sautéed shrimp, coconut oil, ginger, garlic, and scallions, and Mark Bitman's watermelon and tomato salad with feta, mint, a slash of sherry vinegar and EVOO. A superb meal. Big smiles all around.

other Julienne veggie options include carrots, jicama, waterchestnuts, sweet red bell pepper; chiffonade of napa cabbage, scallions (sliced or chopped), cilanto for garnish
protein options include shredded chicken, duck, pork butt (asian style)
Topping it with lots and lots of chopped scallions makes it even better---like 200 per cent!  (Although it's terrific to begin with, absolutely addictive.

If using jarred, minced ginger, cut the amount in half. It comes through very strong. 

I saved some time by putting the peanuts in a plastic bag and crushing them with a meat mallet, as opposed to chopping.m
I live in the boonies and can’t find Chinese egg noodles.  What’s the best substitute or can someone suggest a recipe to make my own? Thanks.
The humble ramen noodles can be a good alternative.
Spaghetti is another possible substitute. Some teppanyaki (hibachi grill) restaurants use spaghetti for their noodles dishes.
I enjoy using soba noodles, easily found in most grocery stores.
Excellent. Doubled the sauce; made sesame paste; used less PB (try 2/3). Topped with oven baked tofu/grilled chicken, shredded carrots, green onions, mung sprouts, cucumber.
I added a few peeled mandarin ornge sections, to cut the fatty taste from the sesame tahini and peanut butter.
Are the noodles rice or wheat?
Is the sesame oil in the recipe toasted, or not?  I sure do wish recipes would be clear on this!
I think that would be to your taste, but the recipe says regular 'ole sesame oil.
Always toasted.
Toasted sesame oil has a lot more flavor, as well as toasted sesame seeds.

Have made this several times & it's always good as is and with substitutions for missing ingredients. This time, used gluten free (GF) ramen noodles, Tamari GF soy sauce, white balsamic vinegar p/o chinese rice vinegar, sesame tahini p/o chinese sesame paste, phuong tom dam for chili paste and omitted sugar. Still delicious!Have used rice noodles in past. Quick satisfying meal.
 I also like the times recipe from the mid 80's that uses black Chinese tea.

DeCecco linguine -- I Chill the under-sauced noodles until you are ready to serve, and then mix in as much extra sauce as you need. Adding toasted and crushed Sichuan peppercorns is totally legitimate, and at least one tbsp sesame oil as well to the noodle sauce. To make sesame paste go http://www.tastehongkong.com/recipes/homemade-sesame-paste-from-scratch/. Also can add scallions, cucumber, shredded chicken

I added several handfuls of spiralized zucchini, which I squeezed as much water as possible out of and blanched for about 90 seconds.  Not a standard or authentic hack, admittedly, but not a detriment either. I liked how the zucchini noodles added a flavorful veggie note to the pasta and Chinese sauce.
I have been making this for years, since my kids were small. I often make extra sauce and keep it in the frig for another meal or as a dip for fruit or vegetables.

Here's my one pet peeve with the New York Times cooking section:  Will you please specify "toasted  sesame oil" when you call for sesame oil, because there is plain, (or untoasted) sesame oil also. 
Thank you.

Subbed Sunflower seed butter (a sweet creamy one) for the peanut butter to accomodate an allergy and jacked up the veggies a bit with addition of thinly sliced red peppers and a shower of cilantro leaves.  Easy to double and great to make ahead for a crowd.  Tasty.

I guess I grew up eating this style of noodles that were just a little sweeter, so I add a glug of maple syrup and it comes out just right. I use tahini because that’s generally what I have on hand. And this past time, I was even out of that — so I subbed almond butter, and it actually tastes just fine. Also, I have no idea why it took me this long to figure out that as long as I had the micro-plane out for my frozen ginger, I could just go ahead and grate the garlic, too. So much faster. Cheers!
Where is the recipe for the noodles?  The video shows them making homemade noodles.
I subbed in the tahini. I liked the spiciness of this dish, but would probably dial it back in the future for family members who don't like a lot of heat.
Delicious, easy, and flexible.  I didn't have all the right ingredients, just flexed it so it would be about the same amount of dressing.  I added some scallions.  4 teenage boys loved it too.

This is a very forgiving recipe!  It looked so good I had to make it for dinner last night.  Of course I had no sesame paste or the right noodles but I made it with soba noodles and tossed in about 1/4 cup of toasted sesame seeds.  It was delicious.  I look forward to finding the sesame paste and actually following the recipe as written but really, it's good.  Just make it with whatever you have on hand.

Substituted Joyva Sesame Tahini (puree of roasted sesame seeds) for the sesame paste and it worked very well. We also tossed in some leftover diced boneless chicken thighs, chopped into small pieces. Instead of topping with chopped peanuts, we topped with some chopped scallion. We served it with the Chinese Smashed Cucumber Salad with Sesame Oil and Garlic. This was a very good summer Sunday night supper.

Couldn't find Chinese sesame paste with Asian foods at Whole Foods, but discovered several styles of tahini with the nut butters. Several tahini brands, like your Joyva, are made with toasted/roasted sesame seeds rather than raw. I agree--it works out fine.
These are so good. I used tahini and they came out terrific!

Delicious but had a small problem. The only sesame paste at my Asian market was black sesame. Never having bought Asian sesame paste, I wasn't certain I was buying the correct item. The taste was fine but the appearance was off putting.
I don't have anything close to sesame paste; I used tomato paste
Add some steamed broccoli too!
Great recipe. I bought the Chinese sesame paste and compared it to Mid-east style tahini.  Not enough difference to justify the purchase and refrigerator clutter of having both kinds.

I use fresh Korean noodles called kal guksu because they are available locally. They only take 3-5 min. to cook. Can't find Chinese sesame paste so using tahini to good effect. I also am a chili head so I double up the chili paste and sometimes add fire oil to the finished dish. Recipe is very easy to halve. The noodles come 5 skeins to a kilo so each one is about 7 oz. - just right for 2. This recipe is much simpler and tastier than the 30 y/o one I've been using.
Love the sound of this.  What to do when your child has a peanut allergy? So many kids do now.  Substitutions that would work?
Just increase the sesame paste ... similar consistency.. It's all about taste. You could use almond butter if your child isn't allergic to it.
I found that using a batch and a half of the sauce with and additional 2 TBS of peanut butter worked well. As others have mentioned, scallions as a garnish go well with this delicious dish.

Way, way too sweet, and too bland to boot.  We cut the sugar in half and will cut it to a bare teaspoon next time.  We doubled the chile/garlic paste and added more at the table.  Easy as written, but it just needs lots of tinkering.  I'm going to keep looking.
Easy and delicious. added a bit more vinegar, ginger and chili paste because favor the spicy/sour aspect. Topped w/fresh cabbage and scallions. Better than take - out

So I used cashew butter instead of peanut butter. Tahini instead of Chinese sesame paste. Tamara instead of soy sauce. Whole wheat linguine. Sprinkled black sesame seeds, topped with Persian cucumber, ramps, scallions and nasturtiums! Absolutely delicious!!

This is a wonderful summer dish as it is delicious cold. I like to add chopped scallions, slivered carrots, blanched snow peas and red pepper strips to make it more of a vegetarian meal. I make extra sauce to accommodate the all the veggies.
As stated by previous reviewers, you can substitute spaghetti for the Chinese noodles

I make this all the time and it's delicious! I like to use 2 full cucumbers (the small Japanese type), add some blanched broccoli and snap peas (fully cooled), and some bite size pieces of cold chicken to make it a whole meal. Scallions and sesame seeds are also nice on top. I've used many types of noodles- soba, thin udon, ramen, somen- and they've all worked with this sauce!
Foolproof, tastes great, just like in New York/Long Island restaurants. I put in sriracha instead of the chili paste, about 1-1.5 tb worth to keep the spice down.
For a different twist on this recipe, try putting the sauce on fusilli lunghi, and add fried tofu cubes with broccoli and carrots. Not tradition, but really tasty!
Used Brown rice ramen and tahini in place of sesame paste. The results were still great.

I couldn't find sesame paste at my local grocery store, so I used tahini instead. This recipe still turned out phenomenal! I threw it together in ~10 minutes and served it alongside Trader Joe's orange chicken for a simple, yet satisfying substitute for takeout. I will be making these noodles often - I was heavyhanded with the chili paste and found that the sweetness of the peanut butter could compensate for a lot of spice.

I have been making this every day since I splurged on the ingredients.  Never really measure, sometimes vary ingredients, always delicious.  I use much less soy sauce than called for and usually skip the sugar.  Still wonderful.

If I had to live off of one thing forever, it might be this! SO good! I doubled the sauce to have extra, but otherwise followed the recipe. Served broccoli on the side with flax and hemp oil, and a little salad. The adults and kids all loved it!

I prefer these noodles with a mixture of regular, light Chinese soy sauce and the sweeter, dark soy sauce.  You can leave out the sugar if you use the dark soy sauce.  Because I grow Sichuan "Facing Heaven" chile peppers every summer, it's also good if you replace the chile-garlic paste with maybe 10 de-seeded and chopped fresh chiles (or even dry ones).

Really loved this recipe. I had to make some substitutions as I couldn't find Chinese sesame paste or chili-garlic paste. I used tahini and an extra 1/2 tablespoon of peanut butter, and chili oil and a little extra garlic.

After reading about this dish in the Cooking newsletter, I wanted it soo bad. But here I am social distancing. No fresh noodles, no Chinese sesame paste, no chili-garlic paste. Sigh. I dug out a pack of soba noodles from the back of the cupboard, splashed on extra sesame oil, quadrupled the peanut butter, threw in some Sriracha and topped it all with some peanuts I toasted in a pan, some left-over chicken, some stray bits of cucumber and a few cilantro leaves. Delish.

Loved this and will make it often as its one of our favorite takeouts. I used tahini as I almost always have it and sesame paste was not available. I used crunchy peanut butter, ground ginger, instead of fresh, chili pepper sauce instead of paste and rice noodles - 1 lb dried. I topped it with chopped green onions. I made it in two batches, saving half the sauce overnight in the fridge. The second batch was much tastier so next time I will make the sauce the day before and let it sit.

Nice creamy flavor. Our family was craving a little extra something, so I added fish sauce and a squeeze of lime juice for a vaguely Thai-ish twist—it kicked up the flavor a lot, and gave us a great meal for a fraction of the price of actual takeout.

I think the real super power to this recipe is the sauce, which can easily be used with a lot of other things. My girlfriend recently has been just pouring it on top of some tofu and it's honestly quite good. I'm sure you could try lots of other things with this sauce.

My own preferences, add more garlic and reduce the ginger to about 1/3. Also, I just think it tastes better hot.

It’s worth noting that thinning the dressing with a tablespoon or two of plain water will help it coat the noodles evenly and keep them loose and separate as they chill. Also, agree with the other cooks that you should dress the noodles with a small amount of dressing, chill, then dress again before serving.

I was introduced to Hwa Yuan while in high school and lived on these noodles all the way through grad school. Their recipe was (and unfortunately still is) a mystery. I was SO excited to see this recipe, but it is just more of the typical cold noodles available at most Chinese take out joints. Hwa Yuan's was deeply flavorful and spicy, sauced but not pasty and topped with scallion and some other crispy bits. I've tried doctoring with any ingredients I can think of, but no luck. Oh well.

Sorry, but I disagree. I remember in the mid-70s eating at Hwa Yuan and their noodles were a revelation. I think this recipe is almost identical. Perhaps you might tinker with ingredient amounts or add more high quality chili oil. I love it as written.

I made this as written, with tahini and chili-garlic paste. It was incredible. I made half a recipe of sauce with one package of fresh udon, and ended up with quite a bit of extra sauce at the bottom of the bowl, which I just ate with a spoon :).
I accidentally omitted the sugar and it was great.  I pan seared firm tofu and edamame beans and tossed it in and was excellent and added a bit more protein while keeping it vegan.
Personally I would make this by gently frying fresh udon noodles until lightly browned and definitely add some red pepper flakes.
I fell like it could have been better.

I made spaghetti with a very garlicky tehina sauce. Fresh lemon from garden. Salt. Tehina. Tap water. Half a bulb of garlic. All mixed together. Kept adding water and a bit more salt to taste. Easy and delicious.

The sauce is absolutely phenomenal, and very versatile for those days when the lunch plan is "what ingredients can I find shoved in the back of my pantry?". I didn't have sesame paste on hand, so I subbed in an extra TBSP of peanut butter to thicken the sauce. I also used sweet potato noodles because again, pantry raid. Next time, I'll try out a slightly more substantive noodle, and may add a sprinkling of green onions on top or sesame seeds for an extra garnish.

Fuchsia Dunlop, pretty much the world authority on Sichuan cooking, uses Sichuan peppercorns in both of her published versions.  I can't recall a single rendition (of dozens) I had in Chengdu that didn't have them, in sometimes alarmingly copious quantities.  Now that fairly fresh Sichuan peppercorns are readily available here, I don't know why one wouldn't use them.  The dried green peppercorn version (available on Amazon) makes an especially nice variation.
Made this and was very glad it was enough for two meals because I ate it two nights in a row it was so amazing and yummy and easy to prepare!! WINNER! Thank you!
i used thin udon noodles, and extra peanut butter in place of sesame paste (my peanut butter was particularly tasty, made from dark roasted peanuts). tasted great!
Loved this recipe. Replaced noodles with spaghetti squash and soy sauces with coconut aminos. Gluten and soy free.
Where do you get coconut aminos?
What kind of noodles would be a reasonable gluten-free option for this great recipe?
rice noodles
Anything!!  Zoodles, as someone already mentioned, chickpea pasta and the tons of other non-wheat pastas out there.   Black bean pasta I've bought.  Interesting stuff.
Added shredded, roasted chicken breasts and scallions. Also doubled the sauce. Could stand to be a bit sweeter and spicier, but good nonetheless.

Terrific recipe.
Added an extra teaspoon of chili-garlic paste.

I made these sesame noodles yesterday hoping that the recipe was the same as Hua Yuan in Chinatown that was my favorite restaurant in the 70's and 80's. Although they were fairly good, they did not resemble those incredible noodles that I can still taste today. They were exceptional in flavor as well as the consistency which was not as pasty as these were...hopefully one day I will find the recipe.

I, too, had visited Hwa Yuan restaurant on E. Broadway many times before it burned down.  The cold noodles were always worth the trip from NJ! Tang Tang (76th and 3rd) has about the same recipe and the cold noodles there are very close to the Hwa Yuan recipe.  Did you ever find a cold noodles recipe that was close to what we are all looking for?
Made the mistake of adding the sauce hours before guests arrived and chilled.  Don't.  The noodles soaked up all the sauce and we had dry, albeit tasty, noodles.

I add white and black sesame seeds, and drop the peanut butter/peanuts.
Also, I sub the sugar with sweet soy sauce.
A great base recipe!

I make this at least once a week making little tweaks here and there. I usually use more sesame paste because I love the flavor. I vary the noodles, sometimes add vegetables. Very easy to customize to your own personal tastes.
Super easy and tasty.
Made this with Zucchini Noodles. I added mushrooms and green onions and shrimp on top. Delicious.

Easy enough to make but it wasn't very good. I was disappointed with this recipe especially since it had so many good reviews. The sauce tasted very pastey  and in need of some salt or something else. I followed the recipe to a T. Not sure why it was so yucky.

Great recipe!
Very forgiving. Used peanut butter instead of sesame paste.
Used dried red pepper flakes for spice.
Just fudge along with what you've got on hand, tasting as you go, garnish with julienned veg, minced herbs, sprouts, meats, grilled shrimp, whatever's around. Always divine!
Used 1 lb dry linguini and doubled the sauce. It was delicious!

I have made a version of this a gazillion times, usually somewhere where there is a minimum of Chinese ingredients.  It's always a hit and keeps well for leftovers.  I always double my sauce ingredients so the leftovers taste fresh.

I wanted to make this and did not have exact ingredients on hand. I improvised and it was fabulous! Can't wait to try it with exactly what is called for. I used: whole wheat spaghetti (chewy texture - perfect); sesame oil, not having found Asian sesame paste; and a bit of sriracha. I added shredded leftover chicken to make it a meal.
Add vegetables, tofu.  Bok choi works well - use lots.  Maybe snow peas, broccoli.

Sauce is well-balanced - delicious!  I used 12 ounces of noodles and would have liked more sauce, so will double it next time.  Added shredded carrots and garnished with scallion, made a great summer supper!

This is a keeper--though I use regular spaghetti and lots of julienned  cucumber, carrots, and bell peppers. The flavors improve overnight in the fridge so it is great to make ahead, especially for a party or a pot luck/buffet. A pound of spaghetti makes 6 very generous dinner portions and is perfect for summer.

I used whole-wheat “Asian” noodles from my local supermarket in Helsinki and they were really nice with this.  The rough surface of the noodle picks up sauce and adds a nice texture.  Topped with matchstick cukes, ripe quartered cherry tomatoes, shredded lettuce, and prepared crispy fried onions. I subbed sambal oelek for garlic-chili paste and lime juice for rice wine vinegar so flavors veered SE Asian but a savory, sweet, creamy winner in any case!  Will make again!
Went all-in and added a touch more garlic chili paste; made with udon and the family loved it.
This was the most delicious meal and it came together in 15 minutes.  I didn't have sesame paste so I added toasted sesame seeds. Yum!

Claiborne and Virginia Lee's The Chinese Cookbook for 2 to 4 servings, from 4 ounces of fine egg noodles, calls for: 1/4 cup sesame paste 3 T brewed tea or water 2 T hot oil (Optional, but if you want spicy, it's got to be there) 3 T light soy sauce (that is, the regular kind) 3 T red wine vinegar 2 tsp sugar salt 1/4 tsp MSG (optional) [this recipe dates from 1972!] 1/4 cup peanut oil 2 T chopped garlic 1 T sesame oil

Sauce is delicious. Couldn't find sesame seed paste so tried to make it in the food processor. It's old; perhaps blades in newer ones get closer to the bottom of the bowl. I ended up using 3 T white sesame seeds toasted dark then muddled them with a pinch of salt until cool. Added about 3/4 t of toasted sesame seed oil, muddled. Then added enough olive oil to make a paste. It was actually easier and less frustrating than the food processor. Or maybe the frustration with the fp made muddling fun.
I remember the noodles I used to get in the 80's.  GREAT after too long a night.  ;). Think next time I'll double the sauce.

I was hooked on these noodles in the 80s too. Once I ate so many I was really sick. I moved back to UK shortly after and have never been able to find a recipe that lives up to my memory of them. I’m hoping this will be the one!

All you have to do is boil up some noodles ( a thicker dried spaghetti works great if you don't have fresh noodles), spoon some condiments into a bowl, and mix it together! This is so easy and super delicious. Plus, it's infinitely customizable with what ever ingredients you have on hand.
Fresh or dried linguine works well.  Try to get the Chinese Sesame Paste, if you can. We eat this regularly; so yummy!

Did not have Chinese sesame paste--but had regular sesame paste you use for hummus and it was great. I served with grilled chopped chicken and lot's veggies for toppings--cabbage, cucumbers, carrots... Everyone can put what they want on.
