I gave made this dish many times, and it really is an easy weeknight dish ans very flexible and adaptable to what's on hand. It makes a terrific base for leftover roast chicken or roasted vegetables or raw vegetables in the summer.

I used tahini instead of Chinese sesame paste and I used chile crisp for the spice.  I couldn't taste much of the peanut butter or sesame (am I supposed to?) but the whole thing was kind of addictive.

I have made a version of this for years.  I use cold black tea instead of chicken stock.  It works well and makes the dish vegetarian friendly for covered-dish picnics.  Scallions make a good garnish, too.

This is delicious! I use a blender instead of whisking. I have made it without ginger when I didn't have and it's just fine like that too. I double the sauce so I have extra for drizzling on other things. Tahini works fine in place of Chinese sesame paste.

Double the sauce ingredients (maybe triple next time?)  
Add more garlic & ginger
Less rice vinegar (a bit too tangy)
Maybe equal parts sesame paste & peanut butter?

Used 1 cumber, 1 red pepper, carrots, bean sprouts, and 5 spice pressed tofu.

Very fast but don’t have all the ingredients on regular basis. Note on sesame paste: “Mother of Vegan3 years ago
So I didn't have sesame paste, so I did what I always do... googled "How to make sesame paste". thank you tasteofhongkong.com, for the easy recipe! Ingredients 200g toasted sesame seeds plus 5-6 tbsp extra virgin olive oil. Blend. Added the rest of the ingredients, and delicious.”
Thank you for this. It will save me a trip to a Asian supermarket when I need to replace my current jar.

I love cooking and eating but my pandemic eating habits have caused a spike in both  my cholesterol and blood sugar so I've been doing lots of modifications and adding tons more vegetables to everything. This is the first time I've used this fantastic sauce (using only a 1/4 of the sugar) over shiratake noodles adding a bunch of roasted cauliflower, Kitchn's Crispy Sesame Lime Tempeh and tons of cilantro to the cukes in the recipe. It was outstanding - will make again.

Been making this great sauce forever but lately I've found it difficult to find sesame paste, even in Flushing. I bought crushed toasted sesame seeds and blitzed it with some sesame oil. Problem solved.
What exact noodles were used? Thank you
This recipe is an instant classic in my household. Turned out absolutely perfect. We cooked tofu and bok choy (with scallions, ginger and garlic) separately to round out the meal.

Made 4/20/24. Used TJ wheat cooked noodles. No sesame paste so subbed Almond butter, hot sesame oil per suggestions. 1.5 sauce. Also added radishes, cilantro, green onions. Added extra garlic hot sauce, maybe 1 heaping tablespoon. Watch spicy level for Jim. Added 1 lb. Cooked Shrimp, which I heated up briefly in hot noodle water.

This might be my favorite recipe I've ever used from the Times. I make these so often my family gets sick of them, even as they love them too. I typically add chopped green onions and/or cilantro. But it's also great without them. And the cucumbers for that matter. Best part is how fast it is to make these and that I always have everything on hand to make it.  And using a pound of noodles means plenty of leftovers. My pasta dish staple for a long time now!
I added sliced scallions and made crispy fried tofu for some extra protein. It was delicious!

Super tasty even with what I had on hand. Tahini worked fine. Fly by Jing chili crisp was plenty garlicky without adding the extra garlic. Delicious with plain spaghetti and finely julienned veggie remnants from the fridge. If I had a full pound of pasta, I would double the sauce. I only had about a half a box. Fried some tofu cubes and set out veggies, herbs. tofu, lime wedges, peanuts, noodles already tossed in sauce, and extra sauce. Folks assembled their own noodle bowls. Will make again.

Craig Claiborne published Barbara Tropp’s version of “smooth and zesty sesame sauce” on Sept. 2, 1981 - check your archives as it doesn’t come up as a Cooking search. I have been making multiple batches of this sauce every summer ever since. And there’s no peanut butter - just sesame paste. Nothing, though, will replace that first taste of cold sesame noodles at Empire in the summer of 1979.

I hate to give a rating to a recipe I didn't follow exactly but I was forced by the app so gave it a 5. It was very tasty, but not as good as with peanut butter. 
Changes:
-Because of peanut allergies I substituted mostly cashew and a bit of almond butter instead of the peanut butter. 
-Eliminated sugar since it was sweet enough from the rice vinegar sugar. 
-used lower sodium soy
-soba noodles 
Hoped to have leftover sauce since I made less noodles but I recommend making more sauce.

This was scrumptious. I doubled the sauce and marinated extra firm tofu cut into cubes in half the sauce and shrimp in the other half. I coated the tofu with corn starch, reserving the sauce, then fried it in neutral oil until golden brown and drained it on paper towels. I wiped out the pan and fried the shrimp in its sauce for 2-3 minutes. I tossed the noodles with the sauce and the shrimp and dressed it with cucumbers, sugar snap peas, sliced scallions, and the fried tofu. Chef's kiss!

According to the "tip," Chinese sesame is made of toasted sesame seeds and tahini is made of plain, untoasted sesame.  However, the ingredient list on the jar of tahini that I bought from Amazon, Baraka Unsalted Tahini Sesame Paste 100% All Natural, says "INGREDIENTS: 100% PURE ROASTED SESAME." (Says it in French, too.) I can't say it's the same as Chinese sesame paste, but it's very good.
Sooooo good. Hooray! Don’t overspice, follow recipe except use less than a pound noodles. Bought at Manila market - local fresh egg noodles see photo. Made in SF
I added shrimp to make this a one dish meal.  Chicken would be good as well.  Next time, I will also add chopped scallions for a little zip.

Loved the sauce first time around, although we weren't able to find chili oil or Chinese sesame paste (used tahini instead). We used the fresh pasta as directed, but 2nd time used soba noodles and preferred them.
When it says sesame oil does it mean the toasted kind?
Meh. Pretty under-whelming after trying Hetty Lui McKinnon's Vegan Dan Dan Noodles with Eggplant. I am not a vegan but am obsessed with the Dan Dan Noodles recipe.

I've been making this for years. It's never a miss. I double (maybe triple?) the sauce & have only once used Chinese sesame paste rather than almost-always-on-hand tahini. Didn't seem to make a difference. I add two tbsp of hoisin based on another recipe. I use whatever veg are fresh & handy. Today, that was Persian cucumbers, edamame, matchstick carrots, Maui onions, cilantro, slivered water chestnuts. I've served it with grilled shrimp or tofu with some of the sauce on the side for dipping.

This recipe has now been a staple in my family for years. We love to triple the recipe and use the sauce on all kinds of noodles and veggies during the week. My kinds particularly love this sauce with Korean Rice Cake noodles.

I just made this and it is delicious. Added slivers of carrots, celery, cucumbers, peppers, scallions and some micro greens. Basically everything in my veggie drawer. Also tossed in some cooked chicken. Substituted sesame oil and tahini for the sesame paste. I will definitely make this one again and again.

Easy, delicious, perfect on a summer day. Followed the alternate directions provided for using tahini rather than Chinese sesame paste, served sprinkled with fresh chopped scallions and, on the side, paper thin cucumber slices pickled in rice wine vinegar/sugar brine. The dish is easy, delicious, and refreshing on a hot summer day.
Do you know how many types of noodles the Chinese make? Do you? Are you able to specify what KIND of noodles are required, or is that just too much to ask?

Delicious addition to rotation. Next time will combat sour note by using correct sesame paste instead of tahini & making sauce day before per commenter Maggie's suggestion. Good calls: 
1) Tripled the sauce for double the fresh udon noodles. 
2) Added lots of veggies: roasted eggplant sticks (in oil & smidge of pomegranate syrup); carrots & red pepper in matchsticks; ~1 lb each snow peas, mung beans separately blanched & shocked. 
3) Served warm by heating the sauce.

In our currently sultry clime, I'll be making this mañana with tahini, etc., as suggested. But should autumn ever come, allowing us to search a bit farther afield for real Chinese sesame paste, is there any likely source ABOVE Chinatown?*

*By way of thanks in advance, this cringe-worthy pun: What did the chef at the Latino-Chinese restaurant sing as he wokked? "Sesame, sesame mucho..."

It turned out even better than it read--without sesame paste. In fact, even with tahini and without peanut butter, it emulsified perfectly (and stayed that way). The flavor and mouth feel were excellent.
I DID use fresh Chinese egg noodles, however, and have to add that 5 minutes seems FAR too long for anything REMOTELY chewy; I'd go for 2 minutes MAX.
For those that can't do penut, I've always left it out and added more good quality Greek or any middle eastern Tahini (seseme paste) and added a touch more sugar.
I added the chicken from another recipe Cold Rice Noodles With Grilled Chicken and Peanut Sauce by David Tanis and then some red peppers.
Could not find garlic-chili paste but Whole Foods had garlic-chili sauce which worked. Substituted green pepper for cucumber.

I was addicted to Hwa Yuan noodles in the 80s and have been searching for good ones ever since.  I've always been disappointed, but these are the best I've had since the restaurant closed.  Thank you Sam!
This recipe didn't really work for me, despite following it exactly. The amount of sauce was too scant for the amount of noodles.
How odd, my batch made enough to two generous meals and I am no skinny bird! Then again I did not measure exactly.... but it was awesome!

Dana, I found the same thing happening, but I think it was because I made the dish two hours ahead of time.  When I first tasted it, I was blown away, but when it came time for dinner, the sauce had soaked into the noodles and everything felt dry and gummy.
Make sure you're using 1 lb. of *fresh* noodles, not dried. If you start with 1 lb. of dried noodles, you will end up with too much noodle and not enough sauce.
I found this to be bland.
Easy and delish! Throw in some meat for a complete meal.
This recipe was the easiest one I have made so far and almost as good as Tyler Florence's.
Any suggestions for gluten-free, corn-free, soy-free noodles? :( Serious allergies here. Thanks.
Rice noodles.
Many options. Read the labels.
Delish and easy to make!  Added a little bit of Sriracha as well

This is one of those recipes where you have to adjust the flavors to your idea of what sesame noodles should be. Luckily that's easy to do. I significantly increased the amount of peanut butter and sesame paste because the takeout noodles I loved had those flavors more strongly than the recipe calls for. I only had dried lo mein noodles and 1 lb of those makes a lot of noodles, had to increase amount of sauce!
Used tahini in lieu of Chinese sesame paste, and sriracha + crushed garlic in lieu of chili-garlic paste.  Mouthwateringly delicious dish.

In a hurry, so tossed the sesame oil, coconut aminos subbed for the soy sauce (soy allergy), rice vinegar, 2 tbsp toasted sesame seeds, chunky peanut butter, a couple of chunks of crystallized ginger subbed for the ginger and sugar, 2 garlic cloves, and 2 tsp Thai Kitchen red curry paste in my blender smoothie cup - whirred and tossed over 9 ounces cooked soba noodles. Worked very well in a pinch overall. Would definitely use the blender and the toasted sesame seeds again! Fresh ginger next time
This is also excellent when adding chopped scallions and grilled or broiled chicken tenders to make a complete meal. Also discovered the same issue with dried vs. fresh pasta. Makes a lot!!!
I used 1 lb. rice noodles (ho fun) and used tahini and toasted sesame oil. It tasted great but needed double the sauce! I also garnished with red pepper and scallions, cause that's what I had.

I doubled the sauce, and added some chicken thighs for protein. I also used lots of extra chili-garlic sauce (Sriracha, actually) It was okay, not amazing, the night of, maybe 3/5. It was noticeably better after sitting overnight in the fridge, and then eating it cold – 4/5.

Doubled the sauce and used 1 lb. fresh rice noodles - which are great since you just dunk them in boiling water for 10 seconds and rinse. Great recipe - next time I'll add shredded carrots and cukes or try it on zoodles.

Yum!  So happy this recipe took me to the local international grocery store finally to find the Chinese sesame paste.  It would be great to identify the brand used however as I was overwhelmed with at least 5 different ones on the shelf there.  I went with the one that had peanut rather than soy as the other ingredient.  Now I can't wait to find other uses for it.

I used Tahini instead of sesame paste sauce and Sriacha instead of chili-garlic. I also used roasted sesame oil in the first coating of the noodles. I think there is more vinegar than necessary   - it had a slightly bitter taste, but the freshly ground peanut butter was the key!
I made these tonight and they are very close to the ones I remember from Empire Sichuan. The noodles I used were too thing, so I will use the suggestion of a thicker spaghetti next time!
Are these supposed to be served cold, hot, room temp>?

I prefer room- temp.  Once refrigerated until cold , they are not as tasty.  I make a super simple version using chunky peanut butter, one reader supplies a good one below  and leave out  the garlic, sesame past, sugar, sesame oil. Personally, I prefer less complex recipes.   PS I add salt to taste. Noodles. natural peanut butter, rice vinegar, soy, chili flakes-, salt, cucumber. or in place of the chili flakes, a prepared chili paste one can also use to make dry sautéed  green beans w/ pork.
I surreptitiously "borrowed" my mother's car to go from eastern Queens to 40 East Broadway for their sesame noodles. These noodles are definitely on point.

I used 1lb cooked bucatini because Chinese egg noodles were difficult to find in my area. Next time I would increase the Chinese garlic chili paste for a spicier version. My husband wanted slightly more smooth peanut butter in his.

This dish has gone into heavy rotation at our house. Whether cooked according to the recipe or adapted to mood and larder it's always a winner. Used chili sesame oil when I didn't have chili-garlic paste and it was still good. We do like two tablespoons of peanut butter and another teaspoon of rice vinegar. No problems with the sauce getting less silky when made before times.

I am NEVER ordering sesame noodles again! This is so simple and the results are amazing! The last time I had sesame noodles this delicious it was 1986, lol!
My only note is that, like others have stated, if you make this ahead of time, do not add the sauce to the noodles because the sauce just gets too absorbed in the noodles and it loses it's silkiness.

Made as written per Sam's tip, though used 12 oz package dry soba noodles I had on hand (the right amount for the sauce), added some chives and some PB powder for more PB goodness - delicious, and certainly delicious enough to stave off a New Yorker's craving for this dish in cold-sesame-noodle-deficient southern California!

I was out of pasta :( so I used  spiralized zucchini lightly steamed.   No sesame paste and used red pepper  flakes instead of the chili garlic paste since the peanut butter has plenty of fat in it already. I served it with a light salad of butter lettuce, shaved carrot curls, and cucumber slices with a fat-free homemade Asian dressing. This is a lot like Bittman's recipe for cold noodles in peanut sauce.

Meh.  Missing something.  I wasn't sure about "Chinese rice vinegar" and had black Chinese rice vinegar.  No Chinese sesame paste but all the tahini I found was with roasted sesame seeds anyway.  Otherwise, to the T.  Added some shriracha to see if that would help.  Salt, no.  TJ's umami paste.  Nothing brought the flavor I was hoping for.  Maybe Japanese rice vinegar would add the sour.  Not sure.  I'd like to try again but not sure I won't look for another recipe.
What kind of noodles could I substitute in order to make this a vegan dish?
Chinese rice noodles or cellophane noodles are worth checking out.
Used soybutter instead of peanut butter to accommodate my kid with nut allergy and used rotini instead of Chinese noodles - turned out great - my kids loved it!
Garlic flavor is very strong. Cut it back unless you like this flavor.
Love this recipe! We make it all the time now in my family. We often use regular semolina spaghetti or brown rice spaghetti and both work well.

This so good - and easy. We often skip the cucumber and add lots of steamed vegetables - like red bell pepper, snow peas and sliced Brussels sprouts - which makes this a great one dish meal. Even with a full pound of gluten free pasta we found there is plenty of sauce to make these additions work well.
How can you say this dish takes 10 minutes t prepare when it takes longer than that to boil water????
If you are gluten free, you can substitute Barilla's GF spaghetti or linguini. Just have to watch the noodles more closely when cooking so they don't become too soft.

Since I am not experienced in cooking Chinese, I did strictly according to the recipe.  Since we loved it so much, the next time I tried to double it and it was terrible! I  am not even sure where exactly mistake was made.
I added roast chicken and used udon noodles. Delicious. I should have made more sauce for the amount of noodles and meat. I heated it, but agree that it could be served cold.

Very nice recipe to improvise from.  I did the sauce as the water was boiling and let it simmer a bit (added a couple splashes of white wine to make up for evaporation) while it was cooking to get the flavors to blend a bit.  And I doubled the sauce (or, more accurately, cut the noodles in half).  Excellent result.  And I suspect that it would be at least as good tomorrow – if there was any left.
The two changes I made: I used cider vinegar instead of rice vinegar...didn't have any...and added cayenne. It is wonderful!!!!
Is there any difference between Chinese sesame paste and tahini?

from the tip section of the recipe:
"The Chinese sesame paste called for here is made of toasted sesame seeds; it is not the same as tahini, the Middle Eastern paste made of plain, untoasted sesame. But you could use tahini in a pinch. You need only add a little toasted sesame oil to compensate for flavor, and perhaps some peanut butter to keep the sauce emulsified."
(i used a 1:1 tahini sub and didn't need to add anything else, but i've never tried the sesame paste so ymmv.)

i came to this recipe via smitten kitchen, so following deb's lead (but mostly bc i wanted to use up what was in the fridge) i used rice noodles and subbed tahini for sesame paste. skipped the nuts bc i don't care for them and added some shredded chicken bc i had it in the fridge too, as well as torn basil/cilantro. a very good lunch! (i also cut my cukes into half moons for time's sake but would like to try the sticks sometime.)
https://www.instagram.com/p/BUclkCWgkON/

just noticed the recipe called for sesame oil, which i don't keep on hand. i used toasted sesame oil. i wonder if those that weren't wow'd by the recipe would have benefited from using the toasted variety. i also used brown sugar instead of white. (the recipe doesn't specify one or the other.)
Served with seared ahi. Really good.

Added toasted sesame seeds as garnish.  I skipped the noodles and served the sauce over a  bag of frozen thawed Chinese style stir fry veggies.  I also added some fresh cubed pineapple and mango and some chopped red bell pepper.   Fantastic!  Raided the stash of unsalted roasted unshelled peanuts that we keep around for the squirrels. Sorry squirrels but thanks!
needs more sauce, or a fine pasta
Sesame paste is also tahini paste just use sparingly as both are big flavours!

With a peanut-allergic child in the family and no peanuts or products in the house, I improvised to make a peanut-free version.  Sunflower seed butter (produced in North Dakota) can be substituted for the peanut butter.  And lacking lacking Chinese-produced sesame paste in my Asian larder, I substituted an American-produced tahini made with roasted sesame seeds.  While not identical to the don don noodles of fond memory from Chinese restaurants, the result was just as savory and satisfying.

Love this on weeknights (or any night, really). To bulk out the toppings I fried some tofu in soy sauce and gochujang and I will do that forever now. Also added avocado, which I would be open to doing again.

Made recipe to the letter with the exception of the sesame paste (used tahini). Sauce stayed emulsified and was really delicious. Upon tasting the sauce alone, I was worried it may be too salty; it was not. Once combined with the noodles it was perfectly balanced. I added some extra chili oil for heat and didn't regret it. Definitely will make again; maybe with some tofu and steamed broccoli on the side to make this a more complete meal.
Made it with soba noodles (buckwheat) for less wheat (still in soy sauce). Super delicious.
Soba noodles! Great idea.
I thought these were even better the next day, served at room temp - the sauce was mellower, the noodles firmer. Yum!

Foolproof hack: one pound dry noodles, double the sauce. 

It’ll feed a small army and yet! You’ll be disappointed 48 hours later when the leftovers have disappeared from the fridge.

I made this using 3 tablespoons of peanut butter and no sesame paste, just because I didn't want to buy sesame paste. It was still great! I also used very thin udon noodles, again just because that's what I had. Very very yummy!
this is seriously a perfect food. i added scallions and chopped peanuts which was a quick and delicious upgrade. i’ve also made this using tahini instead of sesame paste- still good!

This is one of our go-to comfort dishes.  We cut back the sugar to just over a pinch without any loss of flavor, and use crunchy unsalted peanut butter because that's all we will use.  We nearly double the sauce to pasta ratio, giving the dish a protein boost.  It makes a great dinner for guests when paired with Mark Bittman's roasted salmon, some Chinese-style eggplant and a cucumber salad.  Leftovers are great right out of the container - like takeout, but better.

Had most of the main players in my pantry/fridge. Used linguine because that is what I had and tweaked a couple of the other ingredients here and there to approximate what is called for, mainly 3 TBS course peanut butter instead of the sesame paste. For me this is a wonderful dish. Loved the interplay of sweet/fire/acidity. Went down well with a local hoppy IPA too!

I love these! Perfect for cucumber season--and they make me nostalgic for cold sesame noodles I ate at Empire Szechuans in the 1980s--though those were much oilier. I don't search out sesame paste but just use a little extra pb and thin the sauce with a splash of the warm noodle water if necessary.  If you use cruncy pb, you don't even need the chopped peanuts! For a complete meal, I top with cubed tofu baked with soy, sesame, and ginger. I also use at least a full cuke.

These takeout noodles had some minor flaws, but overall, were very tasty. We do have a couple suggestions that we found made a big difference. Use WAY less peanut butter! The peanut butter was very overpowering and took away from the whole Asian palate. Maybe adding more soy sauce would balance it out. The cucumbers added a really nice touch to the whole dish. It really left room for improvements and improvisation. For example, adding sesame seeds to garnish it off instead of peanuts.
Can you substitute sun butter for peanut butter? I’m allergic to peanuts!
Of course!

This dish used to be so common on Chinese Restaurant menus, but not as much these days. It’s nice to be able to make it at home and this recipe is very delicious. I have made it with peanut butter only (no sesame paste) and it’s just as good.  I use chow mein Udon noodles from Nanka Seimen which provide the silky texture I remember.

I did not have Chinese rice vinegar but a mix of white wine vinegar and Kirin worked just fine.  Also to make it more kid friendly I lowered the amount of ginger and garlic and served the chili sauce on the side.

I used natural peanut butter for this (as in only ingredient was peanuts) and I actually think a more skippy/jif type of PB would taste better. Was missing the peanutty sweet taste I love in these noodles in Chinese restaurants. This version was a little more focused on the spicy chili, garlic, and sesame paste in terms of taste. Maybe my fault bc I used tahini w a touch of toasted sesame oil instead of Chinese sesame paste.
Tahini really isn’t a good substitute for sesame paste. If you use natural peanut butter (I usually use Smucker’s), add a little more.

I make this about 2-3 times per month. Great Asian markets in Pensacola, so ingredients are rather easy to find. I am originally from NYC; this recipe is restaurant/take out quality. Costs less than $3-$4 to make. However, no 2 batches ever taste the same. But, who cares?! Delicious!

Too hot with chilli paste
A little too much sauce 
Made with egg noodles from T&T
Needs cucumber and peanuts
My husband loved this! Easy too
I see from the stars how everyone loved this recipe but my family did not. Even with fresh noodles. Said it really did taste like takeout
Easy & delicious.
Substitute extra peanut butter or Tahini if you don't have any sesame paste -  can add tofu or cooked meat  -  carrots zuccini also work well
Double sauce, more noodles,  add shredded zucchini and carrots

Well, I made this with rice linguini noodles. If I ever make it again, I think I'd use normal spaghetti noodles. Not sure I like it, though. It's just kind of peppery. I couldn't find sesame paste and so used tahini but I did the rest as written.
Very nice. A little dry. Probably should use more oil. Wasn’t sure if sesame oil was to be toasted or not so used some of both.

been to asian shop today but they didn't have a sesame paste, so I've made some from scratch, I dry toasted unhulled sesame seeds, put it in the mortar, few drops of toasted sesame oil, some smooth peanut butter, spoon or two of lukewarm water – perfect! made a great dish, recomend.
These are quickly becoming a weeknight staple in our house.

I adore this recipe. I use fresh lo mein style noodles that are available in the Chinese markets where I live in Chicago. The brand available in the refrigerator section is pre-cooked, so I don't boil them. I just whip up the sauce and stir it together.

Delicious! I found the tahini-like sesame sauce sold in jar at an Asian market. It has a richer toasty flavor than tahini.  I tossed the cucumber in a dressing of rice vinegar and sesame oil. Wonderful combo!

Chinese sesame paste is tough to source. My well stocked Asian grocery had only one brand and the manager told me sesame sauce was similar. I agree with advice to serve immediately after dressing. I chilled a batch and the sauce turned grainy. Flavors of this recipe are fantastic. My husband insists he'll never order out this dish again if I'll keep making it.

I have made many versions of this from various recipe sources, and this is the best one. I doubled the sauce ingredients, except for the sugar, and made only 1/2 pound of pasta. There was a lot of sauce, be we always have leftovers, so keeping some sauce separate from the noodles worked out really well, as the next day noodles can be too dry. If I made the whole pound/box of pasta, I would probably triple the sauce amounts. I used an Israeli brand of tahini that was delicious.
LOVE this recipe, even using tahini, but it took me over an hour to assemble it all, not 10 minutes.  Come on now.

This recipe is a staple - perfect comfort food.  Double or triple the sauce - it keeps nicely in a glass jar in the fridge.  *Do not sauce all the noodles at once unless you’re certain they'll be eaten in one sitting* - if you do and have leftovers, they will be gluey the next day when the noodles have absorbed all the liquid.  Add anything....some of our faves: steamed broccoli, bok choy (the bitterness is nice), or snap peas; baked or sauteed tofu. More toppings: cilantro, grated carrot, lime.
Delicious and simple to make! I added more garlic and ginger and used some dry noodles I found at whole foods. I substituted Tahini for the sesame paste.
The sauce definitely needs a day to sit. I used buckwheat soba noodles, and they were perfect. Thinking of adding a fried egg and some kale for a full meal!

Take a ½ c of noodle water and soak a jasmine teabag in it. Steep until assembling the sauce. Add tea and a pinch of Chinese five-spice powder. 
Love this dish with lots of finely sliced green part of scallions.
This sauce is always a hit on noodles but it’s also an amazing glaze for baked salmon.

Read many comments before making this and did the following:


Doubled the amounts of garlic and ginger.
Halved the added sugar.
Added about a teaspoon of sriracha and some hot chilli oil at the end to give it some added kick
Added some shredded rotisserie chicken and some black sesame seeds.
Used tahini instead of sesame paste.
Was delicious!

(I also made twice the recipe for a pound of dry Chinese noodles.  There was about a cup left over of the sauce)
Came out very dark, i would use 1 tbs of dark sesame paste & 2 tbs. of peanut butter (I like PB).

Yum!!  I used tahini and added toasted sesame oil as mentioned in the tip.  I also cooked small broccoli pieces at the end of the cooking time on top of the pasta.  Used some spicy sesame oil as well so the added cucumbers was a wonderful contrast to the heat  also added cilantro and some scallions as garnish.   A keeper!

Delicious and perfectly modifiable.  Added steamed broccoli, sautéed mushrooms and grated carrot to make it healthier and more filling.  Copied one of the users who blended toasted sesame seeds and EVOO to make the sesame paste, but used canola instead since it was on hand and I wanted a milder oil.  Grapeseed and light olive oil would work as well I’d think. Also found a delicious chili onion oil/mixture from Trader Joe’s that I subbed in for the chili oil Mr. Sifton used.  Addictive.
My family loves when I make this dish. I find a couple of tablespoons of chicken broth helps thin the sauce and spread it evenly among the noodles.

This is an easy and a very forgiving recipe - important when sheltering in place. I had all the ingredients for the sauce, although my ginger was a little sad looking. I didn't have a cucumber so added broccoli, chopped green pepper, scallions and a little cilantro instead and it was delicious.
Important tip: 3 and a half tablespoons of soy sauce is way too much. It makes what would have have a tasty sauce become a salt bomb. Reduce, and save your palate!

Get a similar "feel" by sub thick (or standard) spaghetti for noodles. Sub toasted sesame oil for the paste (don't omit the p'nut butter, smooth or crunchy). Add cayenne pepper if you don't have chili-garlic paste. And use thin, crunchy vegetables to garnish
I multiply the sauce recipe many times (I use a stick blender), and add a fairly minimal amount of the sauce to the noodles when they're still warm. Chill the undersauced noodles until you are ready to serve, and then mix in extra sauce!

For those with GERD, put a hint of ginger and garlic and forget the chili-garlic paste.  This is incredible with Japanese Soba (buckwheat) noodles, or Korean fresh buckwheat noodles (expect a lot of starch with the fresh noodles - cook in more water). Three bundles of soba and one half recipe of sauce feed two or three twice.
When using dry noodles, use only 3/4 of the pound package as one pound of noodles will absorb the sauce too much.
omitted sesame paste, subbed red wine vinegar, 1/2 t dried ginger; & pinch cayenne for chili-garlic paste. served with green beans, scallions, & cilantro in addition to peanuts & cucumber. Great!
Perfect!  So delicious!  I hope I remember cilantro next time!

A forgiving recipe, I believe.  Used toasted sesame oil anyway - isn't that what the recipe intended?  Had rice ramen that we wanted to use, leftover shrimp and sweet peppers (raw) -- mostly veggies but that's ok.  Used 'blistered' peanuts - which were extra crunchy. This was a throw in the kitchen sink type of cook today.  All peanut butter as I don't even know a place where I live that would have Chinese paste  Taste the sauce/dressing - I didn't - for me needed more heat.  Next time.

I made these once, followed the recipe to a tee and it was awful. This time around, I took my mortar and pestle, pummeled a thai Birdseye chili with some garlic, added tahini, lime juice, brown sugar, the sesame oil and then peanut butter, a and finally 2TBSP of rice vinegar and 1 TBSP soy sauce. Mixed noodles in with sauce anc topped with crushed peanuts. The sauce was rather thick so when it came time to eat, I poured a cup of hot water on the noodles and tossed one more time. Perfection.
This is crazy good on fresh ramen.
Question: is the sesame oil in the recipe *toasted* sesame oil?
Toasted sesame oil I believe.

Toasted sesame oil is more expensive but has more flavor so that is what I use. Toasted sesame oil is now available at most grocery stores but regular sesame oil is only available at Indian and Asian grocery stores. Of course, you can order on line.

The Chengdu way to cook the cold noodles is to boil fresh noodles al dente, drain, spread the noodles on a huge cutting board, and fan the noodles to cool while applying sesame oil on the noodles generously. In the 70s, it would require two persons to cool the noodles: one person to fan the noodles  and another person to toss the noodles. Now, you just need an electric fan to blow directly on the noodles. Once the noodles are cool and well coated with oil, then add the seasonings.

What is "plus a splash?"

2 tablespoons sesame oil, plus a splash
I would guesstimate less than a teaspoon.

One of my favorite dishes - and good for socially-distanced outdoor dining! Just curious - is the "sesame oil" mentioned up front in this version NOT "toasted sesame oil?" I have not found the non-toasted variety to have enough flavor to bother with, especially in a strongly-flavored recipe like this.
Absolutely delicious!  And easy!  We topped the noodles with quickly sautéed shrimp.  Leftovers, if any survive, are great cold.  Thanks for another keeper, Sam!

First time I made this my know it all husband told me it wasn't Chinese food (and he should know as an Aussie committee to China) and he wouldn't eat it. Two
Twenty years later, our daughter was charmed at the thought of her 2 fav foods, p'nut butter and spaghetti together. In summer/winter, we eat it cold/warm. We add snow peas, Savoy cabbage, broccolini, almost any vegetable imaginable and chicken.  Sometimes I do boil up too much pasta and make a lot of sauce; it can be enjoyed for days.

My parents immigrated to NYC from Taiwan, and I grew up going to Hwa Yuan. This dish is a classic & every one of my aunties had her own version. I make mine with an extra hit of vinegar, and have chopped peanuts, peeled and seeded cucumbers cut into matchsticks, and chili paste on the side.  It’s important to use Chinese sesame paste.  Tahini has a completely different taste.

i didn't have chili-garlic paste in the pandemic shutdown, so i used penzey's szechuan peppercorns and ground them a bit.  also no creamy p.b., used my superchunk p.b.  made the sesame paste with a mortar and pestle from some toasted sesame seeds .  all turned out with a very fresh taste.  very tasty with some blanched asparagus (from the garden, the only fresh vegetable i had on hand.)  i found it to be a delicious recipe.

Made it last night and served it with soy ginger salmon. Didn't have sesame paste so just added 1 T more sesame oil and 1T more peanut butter. All we had in the house was whole-wheat pasta, so I boiled about 1/3-1/2 box of the spaghetti noodles. We have cilantro growing in the back yard, so we tossed some in, along with some sliced green onions.  Seems like a very flexible recipe. Delicious--will make it again (and again)!

Amazingly I had most ingredients in my pantry! I used udon noodles and tahini rather than sesame paste. It was fast, easy and delicious. I served with scallions and sprouts sprinkled on top and sautéed spinach on the side. Sadly no leftovers.

This dish came together so easily and immediately entered the weekly rotation. I used Tahini paste from Trader Joes rather than Chinese paste and it was super. It was also vegan!!! I had rice noodles on hand and they were delicious--it also makes the dish gluten free! Point: if you've got to make a big meal for a dietarily tricky bunch, this might be ideal. As long as no one has a nut allergy, of course...

Cold noodle using Sichuan peppercorns and sesame flavoring among other flavor dates back centuries in China. Recipes are availble from multiple sources that predate the opening of this restaurant.  Perhaps the most notable is the "economy sauce" in Chinese Gastronomy by  Hsiang Ju and Lin Tsuifeng Lin.
Used 10oz of udon noodles and 1.5x sauce.  Lighten up a bit on vinegar.  Used tahini and LOTS of crunchy peanut butter (6-8 tbsp).  And tahini - couldn’t find sesame paste in Clayton GA!
Added one tsp of soya bean paste, based on advice from a professional Chinese cook. Really enjoyed these noodles!
This was delicious. Used tahini for the sesame paste, otherwise followed the recipe and it turned out great. For us it was three servings.
I doubled the sauce recipe to keep on hand for future meals. This was a big hit with my family.
I plan to do this, as well.  Adding fresh beansprouts would be taking this recipe to the next level.  Great side dish for a picnic.
I used udon noodles and added sautéed chicken and steamed asparagus. Yummy! And I was all out of peanut butter so used almond butter.

Made sauce exactly as written, using my new favorite ingredient, chile crisp! This sauce is delicious. Next time I might add a little more chile sauce but that is personal taste. Doubled the quantity, which made exactly enough for a pound of good quality dried spaghetti noodles, about 8 servings. Topped it with sliced scallions, radishes, red bell pepper, cucumber, half a hardboiled egg. Forgot the chopped cashews, drat. But there will be leftovers tomorrow, yay!

I agree with  other posts - double the sauce recipe.  We love this.  I have made this twice without the Chinese Sesame Paste (could not find it in the stores), but then used tahini (which I could find) on my third attempt.   Much better with the tahini.  Probably would be even better with the Chinese Sesame Paste.   This has definitely become part of the rotation.

Raw tahini put into a small sauce pan and stirred constantly for 5 minutes over medium-hi heat toasts up golden brown-must watch carefully as can burn quickly. Used dried linguine. Great meal using noodles as side for roasted tofu and stir-fried Chinese greens.

Knockout recipe. I made a few additions that worked great and added punchy flavor, texture, and variety: shaved purple and white carrot, a handful of spiralized butternut squash, 2 tsp of Japanese Rayu chili oil, a full tablespoon of Lao Gan Ma chili crisp, sprinkle of black sesame seeds, and instead of plain peanuts, I used Huang Fei Hong spicy numbing peanuts, which were a stellar addition. Also agree that after being dressed it didn’t hold up well next day. Will reserve dressing next time.

Made a dressed down version of this with what i had - dry ramen noodles and tahini - everything else was in my pantry. Delicious! Don't pass up this recipe due to lack of ingredient availability!
1/2 recipe of sauce good for 2 servings ramen noodles
Very tasty, but not enough sauce for the noodles...very dry unless you increase the sauce requirements.
Wonderful, simple, flavorful. I’ve made this with both chow mein type noodles and vermicelli noodles and both turned out fabulous.

Used 1 lb dried thick spaghetti (Isola, a favorite), tripled the dressing and so happy I did. What a delight. I didn't need all of it for the recipe in the end but I plan on using it again for a smaller version of the noodle salad. Used a mandolin for radishes( 1 bunch pusa desi,) and carrots(1 large) Chopped scallions (4) and (1)cucumber de-seeded. Tahini as substitute. Addicting! Garnished with cilantro and roasted peanuts. Will be making this again and again and again. Really versatile.
Wonderful recreation of the fabulous Tang Tang cold noodles I remember so well from the Upper East Side. My only twist was using cucumber,  carrot and red peppers cut into thin strands for garnish.
What kind of noodles I assume not traditional pasta ?

I also found this confusing & as a relative "newbie" I had no idea where to even begin.... from skimming thru the comments, people seem all over the place in what they used; presumably a sign  of a forgiving recipe :) Did you find success with anything in particular?
Rose brand Chinese noodles are fantastic for sesame noodles.

Yum! Easy to put together and tasty. I couldn't find the Chinese sesame paste so the splash of toasted sesame oil with tahini as a sub worked great. I picked up noodles off the shelf but I kind of wish I got the fresh noodles.

Even with the substitutions, great dish.
3 1/2 Tablespoons soy sauce?  Salt flavor overwhelms everything else.

Delicious recipe!
For the Chinese sesame paste I substituted 2 tablespoons tahini plus an additional 1/2 tablespoon sesame oil. I added some chopped scallions to the cucumber and peanut garnish.

this is one of my favorite easy recipes for a college kitchen. but, because i'm in college, it's a little more ratchet when i make it. i use spaghetti noodles, tahini and add the toasted sesame oil, rice wine vinegar, and olive oil instead of the normal sesame oil. still turns out gas, and i usually have everything on hand. 10/10 from me and my roommate

1 tablespoon tahini 
2 tablespoons peanut butter

Phenomenal. I crave and make these noodles regularly.
 I make them alongside boneless chicken thighs that I marinated and roasted.
Nothing to change.
Really good! I screwed up and forgot the soy sauce (doh!) but it was still very good. And super easy.

I make this multiple times per year. It is easy and delicious, and far less fattening than the ones you get from a restaurant. I add a bit of white pepper for additional heat. I use the Chinese rice noodles (they are clear) because they are authentic, cook quickly and have fewer calories. You can add any fresh crunchy veggie if you don't have the cucumbers or scallions. A must-have part of any repetoire.
No garlic chili paste, but I used garlic-chili sauce and that seemed to work. Also, no peanuts or ginger, but this is delish! Easy to make. Served it with lime garlic shrimp.

OMG this dish is the bomb!  I've been on the hunt for decades for a recipe that would even come close to the cold sesame noodles I once had in NYC.  I think this could be it.  I used toasted sesame oil instead of regular sesame oil, I also topped with thinly sliced scallions & minced cilantro.  I'm going to make a big jar of this sauce to have on hand!

This has been in steady rotation since the first time I made it. Tahini works in place of Chinese sesame paste. Great way to use up any veggies that can be shredded— peppers, carrots, zucchini, etc.— to make it a full meal.

I use to always order this at the Hua Yuan restaurant on 40 E. Broadway in Chinatown, NY years ago.  It was the best that I ever had.  I've travelled to China (Beijing and Shanghai ) but I never saw it on menus.  Living in Orlando Florida, which has a fair number of Chinese Restaurants, it seem that none of them make this dish (I asked).  While this recipe is not exactly what I ate at Hua Yuan, to me it brings back the flavors that I loved. Thank you Sam Sifton for feeding a craving.

I'll take the blame in that I didn't rinse the noodles bc I just completely forgot. But I didn't really like these noodles. I used tahini and I feel like that was a mistake. It was really overpowering. Anyway... not terrible, super fast to do, but just not what I was looking for, I guess.

I have made this for over 30 years. I used to find the sesame paste at Kam Man in NY's Chinatown but starting long ago I could only find the smoked sesame paste which is really strong and of course, smoky. I didn't like it so I switched to creamy peanut butter alone. It works well. All ingredients mentioned in the recipe are similar to what I use. Any of it can be adjusted to taste. The only additions I use are two T of very strong tea and finely chopped green onion (scallion).
These are the most authentic sesame noodles. The sesame paste is a must get and it transforms the dish to restaurant quality. Order it online if you can’t find locally.

I doubled the sauce & made with dried spaghetti noodles because I didn’t have time to make it to the Chinese grocery store. This recipe is flavorful and great for a vegetarian meal and perfect for summer. I toss the noodles with sauce while noodles are still slightly warm & reserve a touch of pasta water to thin out the sauce as needed. An easy recipe to tweak to make your own (I prefer more sesame oil & toasted sesame paste so I heavied up there) Great foolproof recipe.
Terrific, very flavorful, all diners agree. Followed recipe exactly as written, then let people add grilled salmon or chicken. Yum.
Larkin not a fan.  Mike doesn’t like amount of ginger
added cauliflower, bok choy and onion stir fry to the noodles. Tahini in place of sesame paste. Turned out well!
This was quite wonderful, just like takeout. I used 1-1/2 Tbs peanut butter and I used Tinkyada Brown Rice Pasta, and a side of steamed broccoli
Absolutely excellent as recipe directs.
I used 1/2 pound of whole wheat linguine and the sauce was barely enough. I would double the sauce with a full pound of noodles. I also added a dash of hot chili oil. Really good.

Was trying to use up some rice noodles (think pad thai noodles) which soak but need to be sautéed to fully soften. I took a gamble and threw the softened noodles and mixed sauce in a pan with about half a cup of water for about 3 minutes, then chilled them a little. Success! The sauce is a great recipe.
I used exactly 1lb fresh noodles and I definitely could have used more. Made a lot of sauce. Very tasty.

We one and a half Ed the sauce, using tahini and no peanut butter. We used half the sugar that it called for and used immersion blender…Barilla thin spaghetti and put one tablespoon toasted sesame oil on after rinsing after 5 minutes cooking…was delicious! Served seared tuna over the noodles

Make this all the time! Sesame paste is hard to find so we often sub 1 tbsp pb and 1 tbsp sesame oil and it works out fine. Also add chopped cilantro and scallions. Wegmans frozen lo mein noodles work great here.
I added some finely chopped scallions.
Love this and it’s hardly authentic but Korean Bimibap chile sauce is delicious in it, especially Mother In Law’s brand, sesame flavor.  Yum!

I left NYC 31 years ago, and I've been searching for sesame noodles ever since.  I used to eat them nearly every day.  This recipe is fantastic!  It's easy and quick.  I couldn't find frozen or fresh spaghetti or linguini, so I used dried.    The results were great, although I'm going to keep searching.  Finally, a taste of home that I've been missing.
Used two packs of udon. Tahini worked fine for the sesame paste.

Great recipe. Too much garlic for me. Next time, I'm going to try using just a bit of fresh garlic, and a source of heat that doesn't include garlic, OR just use the chili garlic paste with no fresh garlic. Also thinned out the sauce with some water which made it much easier to toss and eat.

I really liked this! I used fresh thin egg noodles, which were a bit thinner than the ones pictured. I did up some of the sauce ingredients to taste, more peanut butter, sugar, chili. Next time I would use a chili-garlic paste and have chili crisp (Lao Gan Ma) for serving, as I found its subtle spiciness got lost in the sauce. For those of us who can get what is listed, a specific item for the noodles and chili would be useful, as I am sure these really alter the character of the dish.
Delicious! Used my 9 1/2 oz. pac of dry soba noodles and the full sauce recipe here   juice of 1/2 lime and some minced scallion. Made a lot of tasty noodles!
Seeking recommendations for a p'nut butter substitute -  for folks who are deadly allergic.
Sunflower seed butter is a great substitute.

This recipe was so good and even better the next day. I added some shredding carrots and also drizzled Mike's Honey Hot to add some heat and extra flavor. I bought fresh noodles from the Asian market and it made all the difference compared to dried noodles. So delicious and easy to make!

We used buckwheat Soba noodles and tahini instead of the regular noodles and special Chinese sesame paste.  It was delicious, a real crowd pleaser with the kids! Served with some roasted Brussels on the side and was a hearty enough vegetarian meal
Great with tofu and bok choi.
Used tahini and it worked beautifully. Delicious cold the next day with cucumber and chopped peanuts.
I make this on a weeknight and add shredded rotisserie chicken. Perfect, easy, delish.

I listened to a bunch of commenters saying to double the sauce, and ended up with double the sauce I needed. Just follow the recipe, the proportions are right unless you like your noodles goopy and drowning. I thought the recipe was fine, but lacking in something, I'm not sure what. I did use low sodium soy sauce so I had to add a good pinch of salt and I'd use a fat pinch of chile flakes next time, too. Spaghetti worked fine but a fatter noodle like udon would be better.
I thought the same thing the first time I made it. I'd recommend tripling the crunchy chile oil. It really brings it to life.
Could you serve this dish hot?
Yes but I prefer it room temp with cold julienned cuke and oven baked crispy tofu

This really hit the spot. Followed the recipe except used tahini with toasted sesame oil, tamari sub for soy sauce and sriracha sub for chili paste and + a thinly sliced green onion to the mix. As one who can't eat wheat, I've never been able to order it in restaurants, but this was yum. Used fettucine width rice noodles for myself and whole wheat spaghetti for everyone else and both worked great. It's helpful to reserve a small amount of the pasta water to help mix the sauce with the noodles.
Double the sauce.

Hi there!
This is a delicious recipe, but there are a few things I would note/recommend.
First, finding sesame paste is HARD HARD HARD!! So, you may need to order that ingredient online ahead of time. Or give yourself extra time to check multiple stores.
Second, I am tripling the chile crisp oil for extra zing. I am using Mr. Bing brand and I find that this really brings the fun flavors forward in a rewarding way.
Tahini works fine and is available in many grocery stores.

I made this with hearts of palm “noodles” and my child, boyfriend and I all loved it.  We use the Festival cans we get from Trader Joe’s, rinse them and dry them.  They need more sauce because hearts of palm take in the sauce more than the pasta or rice noodles would, and their natural water content dilutes the flavor a bit.

So I sent a picture of my finished meal to my mother, along with the recipe and called out some of the things I changed. She replied with “so you didn’t follow the recipe lol” and I cracked up because no one who comments on these recipes ever follows the recipe entirely. And these comments make me laugh and teach me new foodie things all the time :)
Anyway, I did 50/50 rice noodles/raw zoodles, tahini instead if Chinese sesame paste, no garlic (I know, I know), fresh corn & fresh herbs. Loved it
Subbed maple syrup and regular tahini.  Fine.  Good, easy, figure out how to add more veggies.
Delicious and my favorite recipe.  I could have eaten the whole thing myself.  Next time though I will add a little more peanut butter.  Thank you NYT.

Delicious! My husband and son couldn’t get enough! I added sautéed chicken, green beans, and broccolini to make it a full meal, along with a spicy cucumber salad. SUGGESTION: Double the sauce—it takes no extra time and it can be used later; I’ll use the extra this week with a shrimp & rice dish.
I used Bucatini noodles, as that was what I had on had.  Made the sauce as is.  To round out the dish, I added a raw veg; red pepper, radish, carrots.  For protein, I added seared Ahi tuna.
I added parboiled broccoli to the final noodles with the chopped cukes and scallions to serve. And, served with bbq tofu. Excellent!!
I’ve made this many times, most recently added blanched bok choi (in the boiling water before cooking noodles) and sautéed ground pork.

These were astonishingly good, and I couldn't even get my hands on sesame paste. I'll be making them again as soon as I have some - or maybe even before! For all my other grad students out there denying themselves takeout to save money - these will absolutely scratch that itch. Pro tip: make enough on Thursday to enjoy them later in the weekend; you won't regret it. They're delicious eaten cold right out of the fridge!
Careful on noodle time. Recipe gives no indication which type of noodles to use and 5 minutes is not enough for some (like spaghetti, which I learned the hard way).

This COULD be a solid dish if it was tasty. The sauce recipe really falls short on flavor. The number of user notes that discuss modifications to the recipe as written should be a giveaway. I feel like a wasted so much time trying to make this taste decent that it was an entirely different dish. More acid, more balance , more peanut butter blah blah blah .

This is as close to what I remember in the 80's eating at Hwa Yuan in NY Chinatown.  I've made this many times and it always tastes authentic. Use Chinese noodles or Italian spagetti, it always comes out great  Add chopped scallions and sliced zucchini and you have a great main or side dish.
I don't remember seeing Chinese noodles in my supermarket, so I looked on-line at Amazon.  The best price I could find is $4 per package!  I'll start with spaghetti and go from there.
Should the peanuts be salted or not?

I had all the ingredients on hand except for fresh pasta so I used dried linguini and otherwise made the recipe as specified. My cucumber was fresh from the farmer’s market. The balance of flavors was exquisite. Next time, I’ll add chopped scallions and stir fry some broccoli for a side. I’d also like to try it with mixing in some sautéed ground pork. A perfect August day dinner.

These noodles were a party hit! I have to say that using bucatini for the noodle part just moved them up a notch- and after chilling overnight, I let them come to room temp and tossed with sesame oil. The other thing I did was serve these with a bowl of shredded chicken, sliced scallions and chopped peanuts ( all separate on the side)
So many people requested the recipe while slurping on them!!
The lazy person's version of this is great too. Powered ginger, eye ball measurements, substituting white vinegar. No garlic - oops! Kind of a food proof mix of yummy ingredients.
Sesame paste was black and tasted burnt and overpowering in the dish. What brand/kind of sesame paste should I be using?

Make the sauce ahead of time to let it season.  I couldn't find sesame paste but Tahini worked just fine and I also used 9 ounces of fresh spaghetti.  After reading other comments I cut the sugar to 1 tsp.  It was a perfect side dish for two along with scallops.  I didn't use all the sauce but will add it to the small portion of leftovers.  This recipe will be added to the regular rotation.

Made as written.  tasted the sauce before adding to the noodles and was sure I wasn't going to like it - bitter, too much red pepper and ginger, etc - but once over the noodles  the balance is there.  best to sauce the noodles shortly before serving as they absorb the sauce and taste dry otherwise.  Served with lots of other veggies on the side that could be added.  if you are using dried noodles you will need to double the sauce.
I added a teaspoon of ground Szechwan peppercorns.  Gives it a nice zing.

Made this today with 1 lb of de cecco fine linguine and doubled the sauce recipe.  The sauce was not coming together mixing by hand so I used my immersion blender and it turned out perfectly.  I tossed the noodles with sesame oil then all the sauce and put it in the fridge for later.   It’s now been in the fridge for a couple hours and it’s not gloppy at all.  I’ll top it with the cucumber and peanuts when I serve it as a side with dinner.
Definitely would double the sauce next time, and add about 60% of it initially and then save the rest for the leftovers, which really dry out. But the sauce is magic. I could drink it.
Start by reducing the rice vinegar in half. Add more to taste. We needed more peanut butter and sugar.
Easy summer dish. Made exactly as written. Topped with julienned Cukes, radishes and green onions. Make sure to use sesame paste and not tahini.
Good and easy. I added ground pork.

Just tried these for dinner tonight, with some shredded poached chicken mixed in. These are pretty good. Didn't have any sesame paste on hand, so I took Mother of Vegan's advice: toasted some sesame seeds, ground them, and mixed in some olive oil and sesame oil. Used linguine as the noodle, and a few teaspoons of Sriracha sauce for the heat.
Cilantro tofu peas green onion
Edamame green onion tofu

Shorty Tang's restaurant was our go-to place in the sixties and seventies and I was able to get the original recipe.  j It goes with very well with bbq ribs and is a nice substitute for carbs in any meal.

Just had two bowls for lunch and it's superb. After reading the comments I went with one pound dried fettucine and twice the sauce. Just plain delicious. And it is an improved riff on a recipe I made for years that was very popular with teenagers in the house - the noodle and marinade portion of Chinese Noodle Salad with Eggplant in The Greens Cookbook by Deborah Madison with Edward Espe Brown. The peanut butter and peanuts add a nice mellowness.

I tried this with the Chinese sesame paste, but I like it better with tahini. 

Maybe need to try a different brand of the Chinese sesame paste, but tahini felt lighter, fresher.

Keep the sauce and noodles separate in the fridge as well as the accoutrements in a separate container. I keep all the greens together as one. I have made a variation of this dish for years. Cilantro also plays nice with Sam's adoption of this one. Yes, folks can get upset with altering this recipe but it's fine. Just make sure your sauce is correct! It keeps in the fridge for good while so double or triple the sauce. Trader joe's shelf stable Thai wheat noods are great in addition to soba.
I’m allergic to peanuts can I add more sesame paste or leave it out?
@Linda Houser you can do you!

This makes a great ‘jar scraps’ recipe for your empty peanut butter jars! Instead of whisking in a bowl, add all other ingredients to a large, finished butter jar, close the lid and shake, then scrape the sides down to get all the PB.  I add a little hot pasta water to the jar, then close and shake again to fully mix in the PB. Add the drained noodles to the jar and shake one last time to get them coated in the sauce, then dump it out to serve! For reference I split all ingredients (except 1 lb fresh noodles and cucumbers) into 2 empty jars of PB and use 2-3 instant ramen noodle packets.

It is well worth a trip to an Asian or Chinese grocery to get the Chinese sesame sauce and noodles. Even dried Chinese noodles are different (tastier!) from American style pasta. Chinese sesame paste is called zhima jiang (芝麻酱) in mandarin, which helps if you want to find it in the store.

Twice the amount of peanut butter, at least.  
One 8-oz package of dry chinese style noodles makes approx 1-lb cooked.

I used udon noodles, similar in looks to linguine. After cooking, chilled them with cold water, drained well, then added sesame oil as suggested, until I was ready (about 2 hours later) to assemble all components. It all worked out well and was tasty!
The sauce is handy for other Asian dishes:  stir fries, dipping sauce for fresh spring rolls, thinned for a dressing on bowl type meals.

Wow, was this luscious. Just wonderful. I often with “cook” Asian sauces prior to pouring over dishes, but decided not to and I am so glad I didn’t.
This is a keeper. Easy, delicious, and great for a crowd! A no-fail dish

We always try to keep some sort of noodle in the fridge for easy eating during the week, especially for quick work lunches or teenager “fourth” meals. This is our house’s favorite fridge pasta. I normally use spaghetti noodles because that’s readily available to me. If we’ve recently had a party and I have them on hand, I use honey roasted peanuts, which are great for kids’ palates (and for me with a little extra spice added to my serving). I’ve made this with cashew butter and cashews, and those also work well. It’s forgiving, delicious, and a household favorite.
Notes to self: If using dried whole wheat noodles, make 1.5x-2x sauce. Scallions and 3x the cukes are a must. Serve more veggies on the side, and store all veggies separately.
Delish. Used tahini instead of sesame paste, and honey instead of sugar.  At the end I threw some egg, garlic scapes, and corn, on top, and it all worked brilliantly.  Will be making again.

Fantastic recipe. Great for larger meal planning (or leftovers - also stays well). You can make the sauce earlier - get dressed - then make noodles and impress guests. Easy, tasty, I mess around with peanut butter and tahini and substituting for Chinese paste - aways a hit.
Wonderful when weather is 100 degrees out. Great flavor even when having to substitute with tahini.
10 minutes?! I’ll give you 25 if you’re working fast. No one gets this on the table in 10!

very good. And I'm Chinese American. I made the sauce by first frying ginger and additional green onions,  then garlic, adding all the other ingredients,  but cutting down on soy sauce (1Tbsp), vinegar (1 Tbsp), sugar (1,tsp), and adding 1 tbsp oyster sauce. Definitely a keeper.

This recipe was easy and tasty so I'd make again. But next time I'd reduce the peanut butter from 1/4 cup to maybe 2-3 tablespoons because the peanut flavor was a little strong for us. We used organic peanut butter so maybe that makes a difference? I'd also recommend heating up the sauce before pouring over the warm noodles. Ours was a little cold when served without that step. I also added sesame seeds for garnish as well as sauteed shrimp as well as the sliced cucumber.
This was fantastic! Worth the drudgery of grating ginger. Used Chinese rice vinegar and 1 1/2 teaspoons sugar. Chinese sesame paste is worth seeking out.

Thse are not what you think they are as compared to more traditional style sesame noodles. They have more of a bitter taste, less of a buttery and more of a sesame flavor, but not the type of sesame that leaves a comforting taste on your palette.  Not a fan.  I would not recommend them.

Eating these noodles in the UK in 2025 was as close as I've come to time traveling back to 1980, specifically the Empire Szechuan on Broadway and 97th. https://www.nytimes.com/1985/02/02/nyregion/about-new-york-on-the-upper-west-side-the-szechuan-empire.html Thanks for the trip, Sam!
@Tabitha (UK)those were my favorite. Loved the one in the Village
Used dried spaghetti I had on hand and only 1/2 lb to compensate. The noodle to sauce ratio was perfect.

Did not have sesame paste so substituted Tahini -- and used a ration of 1:1 with tahini and peanut butter. The whole family enjoyed and my only mistake was doubling, not tripling, the recipe so we could have leftovers!

almond butter is mo' fun.  tahini is fine with, as noted, a bit of toasted sesame oil.
Cayenne & regular pasta or soba are fine too, as others have noted.
Unless you're making it for the Emperor in Beijing.
Home cooking is fun, not a competition.
Unless your hosting a dinner party on the Range Rovered north shore or the UES
The NYT printed this recipe several years ago. I’ve been making it ever since. Delicious.
What kind of dried noodles exactly?

I made this recipe this afternoon using 8 Ozs. of spaghetti rigati. I halved the entire recipe. Sautéed a 1/4 of a Savoy cabbage sliced thinly in sesame oil before draining & adding to the pasta along with the cucumber & sauce. I also toasted 1 Tsp. of sesame seeds before adding some to the serving I ate.
If this were a little healthier, I think I would eat it every day.  Fast and easy on days I need a little comfort food.

I followed one recommendation (make 2x the sauce) and rejected another (don't use 1 lbs. of dried noodles) and that combination worked great. I think every recipe I've attempted for sesame noodles end up slightly dry and light on flavor, but doubling the sauce on this one and using the full pound of dried noodles worked out great (could even have bumped up with even a little more sauce)

Couldn’t find sesame paste but found “jam” and worked fine. They tasted great. I did notice the difference between the jam vs tahini and see why it’s not the same. I used the Trader Joe’s Thai wheat noodles. Shaved cucumber with mandoline peeler to make it faster. Paired with some sautéed garlic butter shrimp and sliced baby Bella. Don’t skip the cucumber. It contrasted the nuttiness nicely.

You can make your own sesame paste right in your home! That's right, in the comfort of your own kitchen folks. No need to search for store bought sesame paste ever again.  All you need is a food processor and a bag of toasted sesame seeds. Blitz until a paste forms and add some sesame oil if it doesn't liquefy after 5 minutes.

Delicious!  Additions and changes second time around:  Double the sauce but not the chili oil or sugar.  Sauté scallions and mushrooms ahead of time, set aside, and add at the end along with mukimame (any protein would work).  Serve with lime, chili oil, and toasted sesame seeds along with the peanuts and cucumber.  It would be fine to make this a day ahead and let chill overnight.
This is a great peanut sauce. I substituted almond butter and still got a savory sauce for my noodles.

Did as one reader suggested in a similar recipe and added one pkg soft silken tofu to the blender with all the other sauce ingredients.  To compensate so the sauce wasn’t diluted I used: 3T sesame oil, 4T soy sauce, 2T tahini (could not find sesame paste) 2T peanut butter, 2T ginger, large garlic clove, 3t chili crunch, kept vinegar & sugar the same. Poured over soba noodles and topped w cucumber, peanuts & scallion. Had plenty of sauce to freshen leftovers,  or make another batch!

I have made these exactly as the recipe specifies. I have also tossed in:

- asparagus or green beans or greens,  chopped and added to the pasta for the last minute or so of boiling
- chopped cashews or. walnuts instead of the peanuts
- leftover shrimp or chicken
- chili crunch (Lao Ga Ma!)
- East Asian crispy onions/shallots as a topper (I throw these onto almost everything. Dirt cheap in a big bag at my closest Asian supermarket.) 

And it always comes out so delicious! Thank you, Sam Sifton!
Looking for the nutritional info for this dish...am I missing it? Sounds so tasty but I monitor intake of fats and other things, and rely on the nutritional information to assist me.
Directly below the column of ingredients is an "i" icon beside "nutritional information". If you click on or roll over that icon you will display the nutritionals.

I don't often attempt Chinese recipes, but this was too easy to pass up. Thanks to all the previous notes, I felt I couldn't go wrong! Used spicy peanut butter, lots of cucumber, less sugar. Will add more veggies next time.

This is good, less gloppy than some other recipes I've tried. I used the only Chinese noodles in the store -- they're dry and like angel hair. Next time I'll use a pound of regular spaghetti. Also added cucumber and scallion.

Excellent!  The neighborhood Chinese grocery had the sesame paste.  Next time I'll try it with Sichuan peppercorns.  Surprisingly, my wife did not find the noodles too salty (we used angel hair, which our old Chinese delivery place used in this recipe)

It was tasty, but need more flavor.  Next time I'll probably make half again as much sauce and add a little heat" to it.  We ate it as a main course, adding 1/4 pound of shrimp and that was just the right amount.

Made this with much improvising... And it was great!
I used Japanese wheat noodles, olive oil, tahini, almond butter, and Thai red curry paste instead of egg noodles, sesame oil, sesame paste, peanut butter , and chili-garlic sauce. I just made it the second time today, because it makes for a great lunch!
Can anyone recommend an adaptation for families that must avoid peanuts?  We can't ever trust restaurant noodles any more, so it would be wonderful to be able to make them at home. Thanks.
My daughter doesn't eat peanut butter, but when she bakes and cooks recipes that call for it she substitutes sunflower seed butter.  The taste isn't exactly, the same, but it's still very good.
really delicious peanut sauce.  i used it on chicken too.
Very good recipe but I would increase the amount of sauce by half; as is, noodles were a bit dryer.
Really good. I used Udon and they worked perfectly. Doubled the sauce so I can just throw it on noodles again next week. The cucumbers are a really nice touch - crunchy and refreshing.

Thank you for sharing this recipe, my boss injoy it..
It is perfect for vegetarian dinner.. God bless you all..

After living on the UWS for 20 plus years and dining on Empire's Cold Noodles too many times to count, I moved to Boston and despaired of ever having cold noodles again. Thank you Sam for the recipe! It is perfect as is. Yes, you can adjust the tahini, peanut butter, chili paste etc to taste, but it is unnecessary in my opinion.
Would somebody please give me a brand name for the Chinese sesame paste?  I can't find it anywhere - even Amazon doesn't seem to have anything that seems to be the right thing.  Thanks.
i use LuBiJu Chinese Ginglli paste that I get from Amazon.  Good luck.  This sauce is also good on broccoli, or zucchini noodles or probably cardboard for that matter.
Lian How Brand 100% pure sesame paste. Purchased at H Mart in Niles, Illinois. This is a product of China.

I just used the workaround described in the note: tahini instead of Chinese sesame paste, and *toasted* sesame oil instead of plain. Worked perfectly, and now I don't have to acquire a new exotic ingredient. Good luck!
I have picked up all these ingredients in most of the Chinese grocers in our Chinatown hers plus learned with the help of my wife interpreting
Is there a vegan substitute for the Chinese egg noodles?
can it be served hot?
Warm, yes. I think I used soba noodles--wrong choice. Thinner noodles would probably make it more moist.
Yes. I made it hot - drain the noodle, let it cool just a little (like, 60 seconds), and throw them right into the sauce afterward. I didn't want to have cold noodle on a cold day. Works great still.
Would other nut butters do as well? I have a friend with the peanut allergy.  I'm thinking almond, cashew, hazelnut...
I think it'd be cool with cashew.

I use all sesame paste for the same reason.  Just don't use black sesame paste; it looks awful. I measure it out first and use a little hot tea to melt the paste. Then add the rest of the ingredients. I like the taste better and the texture is fine.

I wouldn't use tree nut butters as a sub. If you read the labels on tree nut butters, most of the time they're made on equipment that processes all types of nuts and peanuts which is obviously a big problem for people with severe tree nut/peanut allergies. My child has a severe cashew and peanut allergy (ANA). I sub SunButter which is made from sunflower seeds. The flavor is almost identical to peanut butter and the consistency is also a very close match. Definitely ask your friend.
A little salty.  Next time I will cut back on the soy sauce.

Or get a different soy sauce.  Trader Joe's aka Trader Ming's is more nuanced than, say, Kikkoman, which is saltier.  Not necessarily better, just different.  Maybe not so authentic but we like it with our Chinese street dumplings here in Elmhurst.
Can you skip the peanut butter entirely???☘
Of course you can. It just won't taste the same.
This is fantastic! I used the tahini w/roasted sesame oil tip and like this recipe far better than any carryout version I've had!
Love this!  I did make my own chili garlic paste per Mark Bittman's recipe and it turned out great.

I  make this recipe often using home made tahini instead of the sesame paste.  Also, I have a whole set of very attractive ceramic Chinese dishes like those in the photo. However, I never use them as they are reputed to use a glaze containing lead.  Perhaps the ones which look like them made from plastic are ok.
You can get kits to test your pottery for lead. And maybe you can actually use them for food service.
Read the bottom of your plates. (Some say decoration only.) or serve in glass bowls set on the pretty plates.

I made this exactly as is (dried egg noodles, though) and loved it! one of my favorite Chinese dishes is cold sesame noodles, which is hard to find outside of NY or, at least in the NW corner of the country. Now, i know how to whip them up at home...so stoked!
Any ideas for how to make this less spicy? I don't know if I made a mistake or what, or maybe my palette is just weak, but this came out nearly inedible for me!

Elizabeth, how about adjusting the chili garlic paste to your taste?  That's the ingredient with the "heat". The recipe calls for 2 tsps but why not start with 1 tsp, taste, and add or subtract from there? HTH.
Can tahini be substituted for sesame paste?
Did you read the "tip" at the bottom of the recipe?
Yum!!
i stir fried a bunch of vegetables to add to the noodles and made a whole meal. Fabulous.
Can anyone tell me how many ounces or tablespoons 200g of sesame seeds are?  Thanks!
I think if you google you will find a conversion table somewhere.

Followed the recipe almost exactly, except didn't have "garlic-chili paste"--and was cooking for "mild" 9-year-old & spice-loving husband, so just served it with chili-sesame-oil for the adventurous to drizzle at will. Quantity of sauce to noodles was about right as written, seemed to me! Thanks for the recipe, Sam!
Great recipe! I've made it with whole wheat thin spaghetti before--it's what I had on hand in the pantry--and it's still delicious.
Cooked this recipe exactly as listed except used tahini. Was fantastic! Just like the ones I'm NYC Chinese restaurants.
Love this recipe! Made it on board our sailboat Indy...works great.

If you live near an Asian market that carries pre-cooked wheat noodles you can make this even faster.  If frozen, thaw by pouring hot water over them. I typically warm them up from the fridge too. I ran out of chili-garlic paste and used Thai red curry paste instead, tasted great.
At my local Wal Mart, I found Ka.Me brand Hokkien stir fry noodles. They're "fresh cooked and ready to serve". Not egg noodles but pretty good.

One can also use RAMEN   Remove from liquid in the cup and add the sauce. (made with out the sesame paste, and sugar) and adding soe salt.   Super fast and simple and adds protein (peanut butter, add to taste) to the noodles.

I have access to a large variety of Asian food here in the north of Boston/Lowell MA area. I'm following a gluten free diet (i know! I thought "Gluten free" was sheer baloney, but it does seem to help me after all). The type and number of Asian noodles is staggering. Can anyone please recommend a brand name/generic type of Chinese or Japanese rice noodle? Thanks.
I have used Notta Pasta and cooked JUST until al dente with good results.  Some rice noodles just get mushy.  Good luck
You may have luck with fresh rice noodles... Increase the veg and thin the sauce....Or sweet potato noodles

Used ponzu sauce instead of soy. Doubled everything, tripled peanut butter and rice vinegar, and added cayenne pepper. Kept the sauce separate from noodles, then tossed them and sprinkled slivered raw carrots and cabbage (what I had around). It still doesn't come near the silkiest sauce, though, dreamily remembered from my early adulthood from Sung Chu Mei on Hudson Street in the Village. Sesame paste is readily and cheaply available form jet.com: https://jet.com/search?term=sesame%20paste

Excellent. I didn't have any chill-garlic paste and it was still good. I sautéed onions, red pepper, yellow pepper and threw in a bag of bean sprouts at the end. I then added a small amount of the sauce to the veg mix. Each person could then add the desired amount of vegetable to their noodles. I also chopped green onions to garnish. T&T sell various brands of sesame paste. Next time I will double the sauce given the extra vegetables.
This is a keeper.
Great!!!

This is super tasty! But needs a little modification. I use tahini and split the oil. I do 1/2 toasted and 1/2 regular sesame oil. I use 1 whole tbsp of chili-garlic paste (I love spice!) and low-sodium tamari. Serve with sliced red bell pepper and cucumber. My only complaint is this is dying for some acid! I serve it with lime wedges and cilantro sprigs.

To change it up, I have also skipped the crushed peanuts and added toasted black and white sesame seeds. Either way it tastes great!
I'm allergic to peanuts and all legumes.  Almond butter works just dandy.

Used the idea of this recipe to help clear the fridge before Thanksgiving. Made several substitutions, even used up a bowl of cooked ditalini in lieu of the noodles. Served with sesame seeds and cucumber sticks. Would make again.

I love sesame noodles - it was the way I judged a new Chinese restaurant.
Most unhappily I've grown into a severe sesame allergy. Can anyone suggest a modification of the recipe without sesame? (Yes, I know, I know, it's CALLED Sesame Noodles. Still...)

Sure.  Omit the sesame oil and paste.  Roast some crushed peanuts or cashews.  Try adding a small amount of almond or cashew butter to round the flavor.   Nut fat is nut fat...Balance the options you have out there...Good luck

I've made this and a less authentic version, depending on who's going to eat it and what's in the pantry/fridge. Use balsamic vinegar instead of the rice one called for, it gives a different depth to the dish. I add carrot julienne or shreds and scallions thinly sliced on the diagonal, greens and all. It's a well loved and requested potluck, picnic and easy party dish, can be prepped and dished up in a flash.

Acccording to Wikipedia (Emphasis added):

"Tahini /tɑː.ˈhiː.ni/ (also tahina /tɑː.ˈhiː.nə/ or /tɑː.ˈxiː.nə/; Arabic: طحينة‎‎) also known as Ardeh (Persian: ارده) is a condiment made from <b><i>toasted</b></i> ground hulled sesame seeds."
