I think the difference with the chinese version is that the hulls are left in, whereas tahini, raw or toasted, has the hulls removed .
This recipe is delicious! I found it to be more yummy without the peanut butter however.
The fresh ginger overpowers the other flavors. Cut the amount in half to 1/2 tbs.
Yummy, authentic.  I used LO MEIN egg noodles and added SRIRACHA to taste to get the heat
Made this but substituted maple syrup for sugar as a matter of choice and also didn't have sesame paste so I added 1T sesame oil.  It was excellent!

I did make this with tahini in a pinch and it was still great. I ambitiously tried to get my 4 year old to eat it, and his reaction was priceless. I luckily had set aside some plain noodles, which made for a meal we could both enjoy.

Prepared this this evening with grilled chicken.  I used soba instead of egg noodles and it was wonderful.  The sauce was a bit too salty for my taste so I added a couple of table spoons of honey.  I also added some shredded carrot and green onion as a garnish.  I served it hot but think it would also do well cold.  Definitely making this again.

Used Thai Rice Noodles. If you use dry noodles, prepare to double the sauce. As 'Meg' pointed out, it serves an army. Made our own sesame paste using the link shared by 'Mother of Vegan'. It turned out great.

Whoever thought of cucumber and sesame noodles as a combo is a genius.

I used tahini because I didn't have sesame paste, next time I will try the other cooks' suggestions of blending toasted sesame seeds and olive oil to make some sesame paste. I combined garlic and red pepper flakes to make my own garlic ginger paste. I used Soba, Japanese wheat noodles, as that is what I had in my pantry. I topped the dressed noodles with chopped spring onion, sesame seeds, and crushed peanuts.
This is a quick and delicious dish!

I found this a bit bland. Even the kids weren't totally thrilled with it. I've amped it up with some spring onions and chili and will probably serve with thin sliced Korean style meat. I don't think it needed the sugar either. Basic idea is good but meh.
Used vermicelli pasta. Look for fresh Chinese noodles
This is the perfect recipe for a hot summer day!
What have people with peanut allergies done to make this recipe?
Skip the peanut garnish. It isn't necessary, and not all Chinese restaurants serve this dish with them.

I made this last night for our picnic today. I use Nanka Seimen Golden Dragon Egg Noodles (dried) and they work perfectly. I usually pack diced rotisserie chicken, diced duck, red and green bell pepers as well as green onion in little boxes for folks to select from. Someone asked about peanut allergies and all I can say is not every dish is for everybody. Perhaps a little more sesame paste.
So good.  We like ours very spicy so I added some hot sesame oil.
I just made this for a picnic over the weekend. I make this recipe all the time but this time I used Radiatori shaped pasta and it worked wonderfully. Add-ons on the side for folks to choose from.

Substituted and left out quite a bit because of allergies: no sesame paste, no peanut butter, no toasted peanuts. Used a toasted butternut squash seed oil, which is a wonderful, nutty, robust flavour for those who are allergic to sesame. Topped with well toasted pepitas and just lived through a not-so-well emulsified but really tasty noodle dish.

I'm new to Asian cooking. Please clarify when I use "toasted" sesame oil versus not toasted. Am I correct that often recipes state only "sesame oil" when meaning toasted?
What is required in this recipe? Thank you!
In Asian cooking, it is ALWAYS toasted sesame oil. It's never for frying...just for flavor. Peanut oil is usually used for frying.
This is now one of our favorite "go to" dishes.  I used tahini and a little sesame oil rather than the sesame paste and also used chunky peanut butter. So good!

I used one package (9.5 oz) plus a tiny bit more of soba noodles (what I had on hand). Used toasted sesame oil and (tried to) make sesame paste as per a reader's suggestion (I should have made a larger batch, it would have processed better). Added green onions (great!), shaved cabbage (green; was good), julienned carrot, and red pepper (raw) (in addition to the cuke). Turned out great; my husband and 11yo son loved it. Will definitely make it again!
Great recipe!

I learned how to make a version of this from a Chinese native years ago before Chinese ingredients were widely available. You can get a similar "feel" by substituting thick (or standard) spaghetti for Chinese noodles, substituting toasted sesame oil for the paste (but don't omit the p'nut butter, smooth or crunchy), adding cayenne pepper if you don't have chili-garlic paste, and using any thin, crunchy vegetables to garnish, including beansprouts, fresh cilantro, etc.

Def up the sauce (I did by 50% and was too much), jalapeño, scallions, cilantro for garnish. Served with seared ahi tuna filet and was quite lovely.
Loved these so did the family. Better than most I get these days in restaurants. Definitely a "keeper." So easy.

Has anyone tried these without the peanut butter?  I thought the peanut butter flavor overpowered the sesame.  Still very good though! Topped with shredded carrot, red pepper, cucumber, cilantro, and scallions.

This was good but I need to make some adjustments for in future. I bought fresh noodles and cooked them to the package directions but they were a bit overdone (mushy). What type of peanut butter do you recommend? I grew up with a relatively organic "just peanuts and salt" PB made by Ox Heart in Oswego, NY. Will a Trader joe's smooth brand work, or do you recommend a processed product like Jif (that is very smooth)?

I've modified into a main course by 1. This was a success. Thank you to the person who recommended using the stick blender.  I would add though, that if you do this, make the sauce in a smaller bowl so you don't get splashed. Very dry for with much pasta the recipe called for. Not the right sauce to pasta ratio. Better versions of this simple sauce out there.

It's great if you take it out of the fridge about an hour before serving. This allows the peanut butter and sesame paste to warm up a bit and become more liquid. (They will have congealed in the fridge, causing the noodles to stick together in a big mass). Sprinkle with a small amount of hot water and toss carefully to separate the noodles a bit and redistribute the sauce. Also, it's best if you store just the noodles and sauce, and add the cucumbers and roasted peanuts just before serving.

Are Chinese sesame paste and tahini the same thing?
No, they’re not. Chinese sesame paste is made from toasted sesame seeds. For some reason, some imported toasted sesame paste says “salad dressing” on the label. Tahini is not toasted.

It was delicious! i did not have Chinese sesame paste or Chinese rice vinegar. I subbed in tahini and Japenese rice vinegar. It reminds me of this Singapore Noodle Salad recipe I've made for 20 years but much less labor intensive. This recipe is a keeper!

Hi everyone - can you tell me what about this dish makes it not Vegan?  I am cooking for a vegan friend and am woefully uneducated about the finer points  of eating vegan.  Also, if you have a substitute for the non-vegan ingredient it would be greatly appreciated!
It's vegan as long as the sugar you isn't processed with bone char.
Make sure the noodles don't have egg in them.

I LOVE sesame noodles. Unfortunately, our daughter has a severe peanut allergy so I've taken to making this and other dishes that call for peanut butter with almond butter and substituting peanuts with cashews or almonds.   It's not perfect but it works. She still can't eat them due to processing methods but the chance of cross contamination is significantly reduced.

This recipe is fantastic, I made it last night for the first time. I will say that 16oz of noodles is too much. You’re safer with between 8-12 ounces. I used Chinese noodles and there was not enough sauce to healthily cover everything, and I don’t even mean generally. The amazing flavor was lost because of the disparity btw sauce and noodle portion. But I will make again and adjust!
This is just incredible. Used almonds and it’s great.
Perfect and easy recipe! To make very easy homemade noodles yourself, check Taku Sekine recipe here (it is sooo good!) https://www.instagram.com/tv/B_koLQIIzFJ/?igshid=imbdlq4oyij8
Any chance anyone has made something like this without peanuts, for a peanut-allergic person?  I know that's a big ask.

If the person is allergic to peanut butter, and tolerates sesame, I would just use 3 TBS of Chinese sesame paste. I think it tastes even better! You can garnish it with toasted sesame seeds or cashews if they tolerate those.

This was delicious and so easy  Used tahini instead of Chinese sesame paste, and did not have chili-garlic-paste.  I doubled the sauce and it was perfect.  Also, added sliced radishes to the cucumber salad.  Terrific!

Tracking down real Chinese sesame paste is worth the search for sure. Tahini, peanut butter, etc. are alright in a pinch, but it just doesn't taste authentic. If you can find it, I would use 3 TBS of it rather than mixing it with peanut butter. I also highly recommend doubling the amount of sauce. If you have leftovers, which is unlikely, it can't hurt at all to add fresh sauce as well.
Do yourself a favor and double the recipe for the sauce. You WILL want more. Guaranteed!
Can tahini be used instead of the Chinese sesame paste?  They sound the same and I've got lots of tahini on hand.
Have been reading the comments.  Many people used tahini (including the comment below yours).  I plan to use it as well.
For those who are concerned about peanut allergies, the recipe works very well with almond butter.

I have been making some variation of this for the last 30 years and it always makes me (and eaters) happy.  I coat the still hot noodles in a little roasted peanut oil, toast sesame seeds and thinly slice scallions to garnish (along w/ the chopped peanuts), use crushed red pepper in the sauce, use tahini, finish w/ starchy pasta cooking water to thin the sauce just before coating.  Spaghetti will do! Any and all thinly sliced raw veggies (the crunchier the better).  It's infinitely expandable!

Great recipe!  I tried to use the soy sauce and vinegar sparingly, however ended up adding the full amount.  Added an extra tablespoon of creamy peanut butter.  The dressing is absorbed quickly;doesn't require additional sauce (to my taste).  I didn't have sesame paste; added tahini instead.   Garnished with  chopped scallions.  This is an easy recipe to assemble as the sauce does not require cooking.  Served immediately or after marinating overnight, this dish is delicious.
Subbed a tablespoon of peanut butter and a tablespoon of sesame seeds for the sesame paste.  The fam loved every bite.
Very tasty and easy.  Used a bit more peanut butter and sesame oil and a bit less ginger and garlic.

Great recipe! I love this dish. I would add a bit less Rice Wine Vinegar. I also added more salt, toasted sesame seeds and chopped scallions, and chilled in the fridge for 20-30 minutes before serving.

This was amazingly good! But learn from my experience: I picked up some frozen "Chinese Style Noodles" from my local Asian market. I didn't realize they were frozen pre-cooked noodles rather than frozen fresh noodles. They only needed to boil for 1 minute, according to the package. I boiled a pound, but ended up with a very small amount of noodles. I ended up cooking another pound to make the noodle:sauce ratio right. It worker out great, but you'll definitely need 2 lb if using pre-cooked pasta

When I want a quick dinner, this sauce is my go-to recipe.

I always:
- Cut soy sauce to 2 tbsp
- Increase sesame paste / peanut butter to 4-5 tbsp total
- Omit sugar
- Add vegetables (usually spinach or broccoli)

Other substitutions depending on what I have on hand:
- tahini for Chinese sesame paste
- all sesame paste or all peanut butter
- cayenne / crushed red pepper for chile-garlic paste
- powders for fresh ginger/garlic
- any sort of noodle or pasta
6.4oz of dry rice linguine =  lb cooked. The 6.4 was calculated and then estimated on an analog scale. :)

Made this five times since reprinted in NYT a few months ago. Family loves this and I like the fact that all ingredients except the pasta and cukes can be assembled in advance.  The first four times (late winter) I made it as a hot pasta, (reserving and adding pasta water as needed). As I made it the 3rd time, I finally got around to getting the Chinese sesame paste, but I think the first versions with standard Tahini plus a bit of toasted sesame oil made for a cleaner tasting sauce.
This was so salty. I had to add more peanut butter and sugar to mitigate the salt. In the future I will reduce the soy sauce by a tbsp. and instead add chili-garlic sauce.

Really loved this recipe. It was reminiscent of the cold Sichuan noodles that I love. I used spaghetti noodles, and did roughly a half pound instead of a full pound. Per the recommendations of others still kept the full amount of sauce so it was doubled. Didn’t have chili oil or chili garlic paste so subbed red pepper flakes, which I sprinkled on top of each individual plate of noodles.
I subbed tahini for the sesame paste, and a pinch of cayenne for the chili. Turned out great! Very flavorful, and the ginger wasn't overpowering.

This is very tasty. I agree with Meg, I also used dry noodles, Linguini because that is what I had, and I probably used 2/3 of a Lb. In addition to the Cucumber spears I sliced a few scallions into thin rounds ( white and green parts) and roasted Sesame seeds to sprinkle over the noodles before serving.
I also substituted Tahini for Chinese Sesame paste( have to look where I can get that). All in all, I will definitely make this again, as I love  cold sesame noodles.

A bit over 6 ounces of dry soba will yield 1 pound cooked.
(Ridiculous that this isn’t in the recipe.)

This was great!  Couldn't find a takeout place near me that had this favorite menu item from my childhood.  Admittedly I whipped up a simplified and smaller version of this, but the general ratios seemed a 95% match for what I remembered.  I did not have Chinese sesame paste; I used tahini, and I also didn't have the toasted sesame oil that the tip suggests one use in this case. 
 Still great. I'll definitely save this recipe as I imagine the potential for modifications/additions is limitless

I use DeCecco Bucatini al dente and I'm telling you -- springy, chewy, little bite to it, yum. Highly recommend making the sauce as written and cooking half the pasta instead. This portion makes for a great WFH lunch for two. You may have some sauce leftover after coating the pasta, but the sauce is so tasty. Dip vegetables in it, gyoza etc.
Would it be good to add basil, mint, and scallions to this? I ask because I need to use these herbs up, and I assume it would be good, but... could it become overpowering?
Just the thing to come to on a blistering hot day.  Quick too.
What is a chili crisp?

Great question. Hope you see this answer. It's a condiment/ingredient of chili oil and chili flakes. It's delicious... This is the brand I use https://www.laoganmausa.com/ https://www.nytimes.com/2020/06/30/dining/chili-crisp-ice-cream-sundae.html

I hybridized this with another recipe, making sauce and noodles as directed but sauteing the whites of a bunch of scallions with some ground pork and adding that into the toss with the uncooked green parts of the green onions.  I also substituted some hot sesame oil to give it a kick.

I prepared the sauce and noodles pretty much as directed - I used tahini but I have some very dark sesame oil to make up for it.  I also subbed out a couple teaspoons of the sesame oil for hot sesame oil.  The biggest change ups: I substituted a bunch of green onions for the cucumber and added 1/2 lb of ground pork.  I cut up the white parts of the green onion and sauteed them with the pork, and chopped up the green parts to just mix in.  I waited for the pork to cool before tossing it all up.
Made with black bean spaghetti noodles, adding cooked, shredded chicken, roasted broccoli and carrots (all cooled). Side dish becomes single dish dinner. Great sauce recipe.
go to an Asian market and buy the packets of fresh udon noodles...they are my favorite for a dish like this.
Perfect just the way it is! Used tahini because I didn’t have sesame paste.... fabulous! Fresh noodles 400 grams, so sauce ratio was perfect.

I wasn't able to find frozen or fresh soba noodles so had to use dry, but it was still delicious. Followed the recipe as instructed minus the sesame paste and it still turned out great. As an easy, summer recipe it tics all the boxes in the sense that you do not have to turn on an oven and it's quick to throw together. Happily adding it to my recipe rotation.
We have made this twice in the dreadful California heat wave/smoke. The second time, we added chopped cilantro and a few spoonfuls of cooked corn - delicious!
Sesame paste = Tahini. Just FYI.
But sesame paste uses toasted sesame seeds, so the flavor is very different.

I added a teaspoon of crushed Szechuan peppercorns, which added a great flavor that I have tasted in cold noodles as served in some restaurants. I just dry roasted them in a pan and then crushed them with the mortar and pestle. Adding "hacked" roast chicken to the top really added to this becoming a complete meal.
So delicious and easy. Had too much sauce. Used 1 lb of noodles. Could use 1 1/4 or 1 1/2 lb noodles. Used tahini instead of sesame paste. Perfectly fine. Don’t need sesame paste

I've made this with cheap spaghetti and fresh udon noodles, both just fine. I only have tahini, so I use 1T more peanut butter (I like the added sweetness because I have a sweet tooth). I think more rather than less cucumber is called for. This makes 5 servings for me as a main course, especially if served with an additional vegetable (I live alone and eat it throughout the week - lasts well).

Such a wonderful weeknight noodle! I like a looser sauce and very intense salty/sour/spicy flavors so I let it rip with the soy sauce, vinegar, and chile paste. Eyeballing the sauce will not break anything irrevocably. Maybe I'm a philistine but I think the tahini + toasted sesame oil is a perfectly delicious substitute and more likely to be found lurking around my pantry in rural Ohio.

The sauce came out a little too thick for my taste, I even added a little more sesame oil. Since it started out thick, it became even thicker once I saved it for leftovers. Maybe add something to loosen it up. 

Don't skimp on the sugar or else the vinegar is a bit too powerful.

I used dry udon noodles, which apparently already had a lot of sodium added to them, so I wished I used less soy sauce, but that was on me.

Overall, I thought this was good, but I probably wouldn't make it regularly.

I just made this without some of the ingredients (rice vinegar, chili paste, garlic, cucumber, and ground peanuts), because I didn’t have them. I used brown rice noodles. I fried some firm tofu and tossed it with the noodles. It turned out really well. Great, easy, budget-friendly weeknight dinner! I’ll be making this again.

Had this for the for first time last night. Very good and very easy. The only suggestion I have is to use less ginger. We love ginger but the amount in the recipe made it the primary taste. While this was not a real issue since it was still very good, I feel a little less ginger would go a long way.

I added dry fried tofu and stir fried broccoli with a bit of onion to make a full meal. The sauce was good but I think it had a bit too much soy sauce. I used tahini instead of the paste it called for so that could be why.
Kept the sauce but changed basically everything else! Added fried tofu, shiitake, bok choy and used korean buckwheat noodles. Delicious!!

Shocked! I was shocked by the blandness of this recipe, having ordered sesame cold noodles in NYC through multiple times. Not enough sesame paste, not even close to the amount of peanut butter which yields fab flavor. Where’s the umami? Where is the flavor that drives people to order this take out dish over and over? This recipe is a crime against a great takeout dish! This is not even close to the recipes it claims to be a tribute to.
maybe you used a pound of dried noodles?

A good base recipe, but way too sour. I'd halve the vinegar. Also, I used pre-cooked lo mein noodles, which got very soggy after 5 min. They really only need a minute or two in warm water.

On the advice of other reviewers, I made 1.5x sauce and lightly dressed the noodles and added fresh sauce to individual portions when eating. This prevents the sauce from getting grainy.
One of the best tasting recipes.  Will keep the recipes  to follow. Sincerely Hope
Can I use tahini in place of sesame paste from an Asian food store?

Might others share their thoughts on "noodles" for this recipe? I don't know what my grocery store carried pre-COVID but currently the only fresh pastas they offer are filled & the only frozen are wide egg noodles, certainly neither of which seem right here!

https://smile.amazon.com/Organic-Planet-Traditional-Noodles-5-Pounds/dp/B004LA278S/ref=smi_www_rco2_go_smi_g4368549507?_encoding=UTF8&%2AVersion%2A=1&%2Aentries%2A=0&ie=UTF8 I get these at my local “health food” store and enjoy them

Delicious. Served with roasted tofu and broc and raw cucumbers on top. I made a lot of extra sauce.
I used cashew butter because I had no smooth pb. Added more sesame oil. Als used sweet potato noodles homemade from an Asian vendor at my farmers market. Big hit.
Next time make with regular pasta.

It’s actually TOASTED sesame oil. For me, I add dried chilies and toasted, ground Sichuan tribute peppers — they’re simply out of this world (Mala Market for ALL your Sichuan needs: https://themalamarket.com/collections/all). Thin-sliced scallions tossed with the sauced noodles, julienned cucumber and roasted peanuts on the side.
I add a bit of sherry to the mix and cut the sugar a bit. Love these noodles!
For those who prefer broccoli florets, here's a perfect use for the stems--matchstick them as you would the cukes!

This was excellent! I subbed dried ramen noodles for the spaghetti, which worked perfectly fine in a pinch. They're also really good cold the next day; just need to leave some sauce on the side to mix in, along with a bit more sesame oil to unclump them.
Hwa Yuan made the absolute best version! This brings back many happy family memories of eating out in NY. (I moved from NY in 1988 and am now in Canada.)

I used 8 oz. of dried fettuccine, added cubes of crispy tofu for protein and red bell pepper and scallions for crunch and color. Quick, easy to prepare in the time it took to boil the pasta and tasty.

At my house this made 2 generous servings.

Used some Sambal Olek as spice addition - excellent. Served with quick steamed snap peas, stir-fried gai lan, raw thin-sliced peppers, green onions, and left-over Bo Ssam roast pork. Allowed everyone to mix their own ratios. Relatively quick dinner with ability to modulate the meat:vegetable:noodle ratios.

Use 3/4 box whole wheat spaghetti noodles
Serve with chopped cilantro, scallions, carrot, cucumber, red pepper
Baked pineapple sriracha tofu goes well too (Start Simple)

I’ve been given a pasta,  I forgot how utterly delicious dish is. I’m diabetic & I had to be careful so I use red lentil pasta. Successful!! I didn’t have any garlic paste so I used Sriracha in a pinch ( yummy!) I also used sesame tahini for the sesame paste. I used dried red lentil spaghetti pasta and only used a quarter of the box. I topped the pasta off with Finley shaved cucumbers which I  then cut into match books pieces. Topped it all off with fresh cilantro . It was fabulous!

I used roasted sesame seeds and 5 tsp olive oil.  Tried to grind in a mortar & pestle as well as a blender and it didn't really make a paste, just made oiled sesame seeds.  But, when added in together with all the other ingredients, I thought had nice flavor and crunch.

this was delicious. I used black chinese vinegar, and probably a little extra garlic, ginger and chili crisp because I like spice. The sauce on it's own was a little funky, probably from the black vinegar, but once it was mixed with noodles and fresh veggies (I added cucumber, carrot, sprouts, cilantro and green onions) it was perfect. I loved an earlier suggestion to let people top their own noodles, including various proteins, but it was great with just veggies.

-It goes on picnics too. Side additions: Shredded duck or chicken, tofu, cucumber, sweet red pepper, hot pepper, scallion, jicama, and so on. Keep them all about the same size with shredding or julienne.
-Make 50% more sauce.
-Use 1 lb fresh noodles. Less of dried.
I made this with regular Barilla linguine, and a little more sugar. It was amaaazing!!
I used dried noodles and doubled the sauce, saving some of it to add later when serving.  The sauce seems to benefit from some time to blend and mature.

Made pretty much exactly as stated with Korean frozen noodles I had in the freezer. It was delicious and will definitely make again and again with different vegetables as suggested in others’ comments. But overall this is better than actual takeout peanut noodles I have recently enjoyed.
More PB.  Add some pasta water?
Can I substitute tahini for sesame paste? Isn't it the same thing?

@Lorraine Ross, No, not really. Tahini is made from raw sesame seeds and Chinese Sesame Paste is made from TOASTED sesame seeds, so they are not interchangeable…unless you add quite a lot of roasted sesame oil to the tahini.
I added baked tofu with chili garlic sauce on top and sliced green onions.  Very good.
The dressing is phenomenal. Do NOT ruin it by using buckwheat udon noodles.
Had some kelp noodles I needed to get rid of, and the sauce held up really well and totally coated the noodles. Topped with some avocado and it was a perfect, quick no-cook dinner.
Toasted sesame oil or untoasted ? My guess is toasted, but why not just specify?
@Nancy M. Yes, that king of imprecision is so annoying!

This came out fantastic! I didn’t have Chinese sesame paste so subbed tahini. As was suggested when using tahini, I used toasted sesame oil instead of regular sesame oil. I also used 3/4 pound of dry noodles instead of a pound. Everything else was followed as per the recipe. Cannot wait to make this again.
Terrific recipe. I cooked the pasta with broccoli and it was delicious. Everyone loved it.

Made this last night with adaptation for Chinese sesame paste because I couldn’t get any. I even used fresh linguine noodles from Bay cities Deli in Santa Monica. The dish just didn’t match up to anything I’ve had in the past. We didn’t even keep the leftovers. I can’t believe all the positive reviews, but maybe I just like a stickier nutty sauce on noodles.

Made with 1lb dried bucatini. Doubled the sauce. Stir fried garlic and ginger in neutral oil for about a minute then added toban djan and fried for a bit longer, until the oil turned red. Used half the amount of sesame because of the addition of extra neutral oil. Instead of cucumbers and peanuts, I added rehydrated wood ear mushrooms and scallions while tossing the sauce into the pasta (along with a bit of pasta cooking water). Was really good!
I love this on soba noodles, hot or cold. It is wonderful.
this is SO yummy!
Next time I will cut down the soy sauce. I used low-salt soy and felt it was too salty and the soy flavor dominated the others.
Add tofu
Frozen noodles at our Asian Market are in servings of a 1/2 pound per person, so for four people, you need two pounds.

If you're wondering if tahini is just fine: YES. I will get the Chinese variety next time I stop at H-Mart but in a pinch tahini is a perfectly suitable sub. I also added white and black sesame seeds and thinly sliced scallions in place of cukes (just because my husband and daughter don't like them). Used fresh buckwheat ramen noodles. Delicious!

I think I already left a review on this one many moons ago but I MUST say this is single handedly the best recipe I've cooked from the NYT. It's so good, and easy to take to-go for busy schedules. I omit pb and sesame paste and use tahini with a little water just in case it's a little thick. Delicious every time!!!!

Made this for the kids tonight and the texture of the silky sticky sauce to the chewy noodles was perfect. However the amount of hot chili paste in recipe made this dish extremely spicy. And we like it hot! Less is more and heat can always be added to taste at the table.
Now a family favorite. Yum!
Made more sauce than called for because I like them to be over sauced!
Loved this! Halved the recipe and it still made four servings. Toasted some sesame seeds because we didn't have any peanuts. Don't skip out on the cucumber! Adds crunch and complements the flavor.
I used Chinese black vinegar and the sauce had a definite sour note, should I have used the clear rice vinegar??

Okay I was very silly and doubled down on the conversion. Made this AS WRITTEN (Like 1x) but I used half the sugar and did half black vinegar/half rice vinegar. Prob did more garlic than needed and adding some crunchy garlic to taste.
What type of peanut butter?
You decide, but reduce sugar a little if you use sugary PB

made this without the peanuts or cucumbers, subbed tahini for peanut butter  (somebody had the last of the peanut butter for lunch) and skipped the chile oil (didn't want it to be too spicy). Still fantastic. Bought more peanut butter for next time.

We have a soy allergy in our house so I only used tahini and coconut aminos. I added chicken and topped with cilantro, mint and lime juice. I doubled the recipe for 2 lbs of fresh noodles. I think next time I will triple it for better coverage of the chicken. Such a keeper!
It didn't have much flavor
less vinegar

I have cooked this several times the past month and made several alterations to the recipe.

Instead of 1 pound fresh noodles, I use a half-pound of dry Italian pasta.  Cook to al dente.

The original recipe had too much salt.  I cut it roughly by half, to 1.5-2 tablespoons, low sodium tamari.

I did a direct 1-1 substitution of honey for sugar, which was fine.

When I was short on time, I used a teaspoon (5 grams) of dry alium.

Add a pound of sliced chicken cooked in sesame oil and serve!

This is so very good. This was my second time making it so I just played it by ear, without measuring. I need to restock my chili crisp supply, or make some, but a hefty dose of red pepper flakes covers it, and next time I’ll try the chili crisp with it.
Fantastic.
Has anyone tried this with sun butter?  I think my kiddo would love it for school but it’s a nut free facility.
This recipe is in heavy rotation at our house. I add peppers, mushrooms, shredded cabbage, and chicken or tofu to make it a meal.

I made the sesame paste in my mortar and pestle (1/4 cup toasted sesame seeds, heaping tbsp evoo yielded right amount); shaved zucchini strips in place of the cucumber; used regular rice vinegar; 1/2 lb dried spaghetti. This meal was so tasty I feel rather perky now
Excellent recipe, except for the missing note to double the sauce.  Otherwise, it will be a chaste, Quaker noodle dish, untroubled by savor.  Can these really be Shorty’s proportions?

Good recipe. Roasted the garlic with the noodles in stir fry. Would double the garlic and ginger next time.

Roasted sesame seed tahini was great, So look for that instead of raw if you are unable to find or make sesame paste. 

Will add scallions, carrots, cilantro and lime next time.

Delicious.! I followed the recipe verbatim.  Added shredded chicken from a Whole Foods rotisserie chicken -- which yielded tons of meat -- matchbox carrots and scallions.  DeCecco dried/boxed linguini.  So easy and a huge hit!  As always, thanks Sam Sifton!!!

I prepared this using 6oz dried fettuccine rice noodles. I made the amount of sauce in the recipe.  It served two as a main dish. Topped with sauteed bok choi and mushrooms, some silken tofu as well, it made a complete meal.

Delicious but under-seasoned. I doubled the sesame paste, peanut butter, ginger, garlic, chili crisp and sesame oil. I used 1 pound of dry linguini and I added tiny cubes of extra firm tofu for extra protein. Served it with a salad. Definitely a hit with the family.

With all due respect, I question this recipe's New York provenance. I was eating this in restaurants on Yung Kang Jie in Taipei during the 1970s.  It is wonderful and a delight on a hot summer day (and every summer day in Taipei was steamy).  As Dan Bradford suggested in his comment, the extra sesame oil in the sauce is a welcome addition. Enjoy!

Magnificent!  Used 1 lb dry whole wheat spaghetti, which equals 1.5 pounds fresh noodles.  Guided by general suggestions to increase the sauce, I tripled it. Didn't have sesame paste, so used a substitute of 1 part tahini to 1/4 part toasted sesame oil.  Served wit lots of veggies, cilantro, and lemon wedges.  We loved it

One pound of dry pasta …1.5 times the sauce recipe.    …and double the sauce recipe wouldn’t kill ya either.

Made with a double patch and tripled the sauce.  On an annual vacation with friends and knew it would be a hit so I reserved some of the sauce for leftovers the next day - good move!  Topped with chili crisp too.  Ordered the sesame paste online and it made a big difference from other recipes I've followed.
I followed the recipe as shown, but it seemed very vinegar heavy. I like the vinegar, but it seemed to overpower all the other flavors? Did anyone else have this issue?
Nellie doesn’t use sesame paste and sometimes uses honey in place of sugar.
Try with actual ingredients.  Tahini is a little bitter

Best Sesame Noodle recipe!  I happened to have all the ingredients in the house. I did use Barilla Linguine which has a nice bite.  Actually better than Take-Out.  Served at room temperature. I make extra sauce and top with toasted Sesame Seeds rather than peanuts. This recipe is a definite keeper.
Made 09/14/22. Danny and I LOVED it!

Made this tonight.. used spaghetti and sesame oil in sub of sesame paste. It was delicious! My husband and I really enjoyed it and will definitely make again. Next time I think I may add some chicken!

If you for some reason have harissa instead of chili oil like I do, that works as a great substitute (you might need to dilute with a little water or olive oil for desired consistency). Awesome recipe, excited for tomorrow's leftovers.

Delicious!  I’ve been craving Chinese food and finally made this. Used dry lo mein noodles, added par boiled broccoli, green onion and match stick carrots. Had tahini so I used that and toasted sesame oil. So glad I finally made it. Will absolutely make it again!
Great as a make ahead! Store the sauce and noodles separately, toss the noodles with sesame oil out of the fridge, and add water as needed to thin the sauce.

this is real, real good. excellent dish.

I used dried Japones chiles from a Mexican grocery store; they were what I had in the cupboard already. I still started light with the application and then brought the XO to the table so we could add more if we liked.
I use the smokey korean pepper flakes - it is a good substitute.

The tiniest dried peppers (1 inch long) are most likely bird's eye chilies. The chilis in the recipe (Japones, Tien Tsin or cayenne) are a little bigger, about 2 to 3 inches long. They will most likely just be labeled "dried red chilis" in Chinese markets, so just go by size. Hope that helps!

The recipe is for XO Sauce itself.

XO "sauce" is a misnomer. It's simply a versatile, highly savory and spicy condiment. Read the Wikipedia article for a brief history. David Chang's "Momofuku cookbook" (which might be in your public library) has a good recipe - there's no hard-and-fast rules about what goes into it, as long as the end-result tastes good - make do with what you can get conveniently (and what you like/tolerate).

It's plenty spicy and savory but the consistency came out more crumbly than sauce.  I'm considering pouring it into a tall glass with another tablespoon or so of oil and taking an emersion blender to it.  So far, I'm not calling this a repeater.

I didn't have the dried seafood, but I really wanted to make this for some eggplant, so I used canned smoked oysters, chopped fine.  Tasted delicious.
What do you use XO sauce for and how do you use it.

Apparently, baharat is "A Lebanese blend of 7 spices including paprika, pepper, cumin, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom and nutmeg. It's a bit darker and more intense than Ras el Hanout but still works well with meat, poultry, seafood and vegetables. Best substitute for Baharat: Equal parts paprika, cumin and cinnamon OR ground cumin."
The baharat I found in a middle eastern store (Sadaf brand) contained only cinnamon, cardamom, cumin, ginger, and cloves.  It worked well with the other spices in the recipe.

This is absolutely delicious, but I'm so surprised at how many instructions are missing—even after so many readers commented on it! Dice chicken thighs to 3/4" pieces; if you're adding livers, deduct the amount from the 2-1/2 pounds worth of thighs, or increase amount of spices; cut the livers into quarters; add the livers 3 minutes before the end and make sure all sides of them have been seared.

When I sauté onions, before I put them in the pan, I cook them in the microwave with a little oil for a few minutes.  It cooks them faster and more evenly than pan-only cooking, and should speed up the caramelization.
Allspice is a good substitute for baharat. You can get baharat in a Persian food store but it's not worth buying for one recipe...allspice is really close.
And what is "baharat"?  did I miss an explanation?
It's a spice. I found it online, 1/2 a cup in a glass jar for 5.99.

It's a middle eastern spice mix. https://www.amazon.com/Baharat-Spice-2-0-Zamouri-Spices/dp/B000FVMQJC/ref=sr_1_2_sspa?hvadid=241654452953&hvdev=c&hvlocphy=9021430&hvnetw=g&hvpos=1t2&hvqmt=e&hvrand=14534989039247324680&hvtargid=kwd-546588205&keywords=baharat&qid=1553271655&s=gateway&sr=8-2-spons&tag=googhydr-20&psc=1

A quick online search shows that baharat is a blend of spices, including cumin, coriander, cloves, cardamom and more. I'm sure you could order baharat online or at a specialty shop, or combine the spices to make it yourself.

Two points:
1.	In Israel, the dish is called Me'urav Yerushalmi  or Meorav Yerushalmi (Jerusalem mix).  Lots of articles are available, including a Wikipedia entry.
2.	Just about every family's baharat is different and, in Israel's open air markets, every spice shop's baharat is not only different but may differ with each batch. Mix your own, play with the flavors, and freeze what you don’t use for the next time(s).

Just ordered Sahadi's Baharat online--used to mix my own, but with amazon's free shipping, why not let laziness prevail?!
Am still confused, tho', by the stated cooking times--underdone thighs, overcooked livers, and inevitably chewy hearts don't have much to recommend them! Perhaps a little judicious "staging" would help even experienced cooks: I'd start with the hearts for at least 15', add the thighs for about the same, and the livers last--till still a bit pink inside.
I was wondering about the coo,I got times also.  Thanks for posting.
The only way the thighs would be cooked in that time is if they were cut into small pieces.

We loved this recipe but found that we needed at least 3 red onions to caramelize to give the chicken the taste and texture it deserved. Next time I’m planning to use at least 4 to 5 inions. It’s true that caramelizing the onions takes the longest amount of time in this very easy recipe but it’s worth the extra effort. We also seasoned the chicken with the spices and let it sit in the refrigerator for a few hours and I think that helped to marinate the flavors.
What is baharat?
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baharat you can find anything on google.
https://www.amazon.com/Sahadi-Baharat-Syrian-Seasoning-ounce/dp/B018WNSK30/ref=sr_1_3?keywords=Baharat&qid=1553281144&refinements=p_85%3A2470955011&rnid=2470954011&rps=1&s=gateway&sr=8-3
How many chicken hearts and livers to use? Suggestions? And what is the red sauce in the photo?
Harrissa
Looks like harrissa
Harissa
What is baharat and where do you get it?
https://www.bonappetit.com/recipe/baharat-seasoning
Google it - easy to find a recipe

Recipe for making baharat: https://www.allrecipes.com/recipe/259981/baharat/ Amazon shopping option: https://www.amazon.com/Baharat-Spice-2-0-Zamouri-Spices/dp/B000FVMQJC also, http://www.google.com. Go there, type in your question, and you may just get a correct answer. :)
This recipe actually says how long it will take to carmelize onions. Kudos!

The printed recipe in The Times Magazine section says to cut the chicken thighs into nickel-size pieces. It also says you could use 1 1/2 lb. of thighs plus 1/2 lb. of chicken hearts and 1/2 lb. chicken livers.

Tasty - reminiscent of Sam's famous shawarma recipe. We served with Israeli salad, and the bright, herby acidity was a perfect counterpoint. Pilaf on the side as well. https://www.allrecipes.com/recipe/208878/israeli-salad
Does any one know what the condiment in the small bowl is? It looks tasty! I see no mention of it in the recipe.
I believe it’s harissa.
I believe it is a Middle Eastern hot sauce called schug, which is made with cardamom and cumin seeds, salt, olive oil, hot red peppers, cilantro, garlic, and ground cumin
What amount of liver and hearts to pair with the 2.5 lbs of chicken thighs?
Where are the livers and hearts in the recipe?  How much? Shouldn’t it be listed as “optional” with the rest of the ingredients? Please correct.
I would say cut up the chicken before cooking if it's going to get done in a few minutes.
Are we supposed to cut up the chicken? I can't find it in the recipe.

Right--it's not explicit (and should be) but Step 2 says to mix the chicken pieces with the spices and then Step 3 says to spread it all out in the skillet in an even layer. Plus the cooking time is brief, so I'm guessing that you cut up the  raw chicken thighs in one inch or so pieces as prep.
Odd recipe since you have to read the article for more explicit instructions.  The recipe should be re-edited to include the article's instructions.

A very tasty chicken dish!  Only change I made was using harissa in place of bharat,  which I didn't have.
Served with a green lentil casserole.
Hey, that’s an idea! Good to know is one lives in a place where no baharat is available.
When do you chop the chicken?
What size should the chicken pieces be?? The recipe doesn’t say to chop them, but it reads like you should
The pieces look bite-sized in the photo.
"They mix chopped chicken thighs in with the hearts and livers, but as Solomonov said when I called him to talk about the dish, mixed grill doesn’t require the use of offal."

Alon Shaya's baharat (SHAYA, p.387) is 6 heaping tablespoons whole allspice berries,  1t. whole black peppercorns,  1/4 t. whole cumin seeds, 48 cardamom pods, 28 whole cloves, 2 small dried rosebuds, 2 T. ground cinnamon, 2 t. Aleppo pepper, 1 1/2 t. finely grated nutmeg.  Keeps 6 months in the freezer,  he says.  

ISRAELI SOUL doesn't list a baharat recipe. Ottolenghi's JERUSALEM (p. 299) leaves out rosebuds and Aleppo pepper. Different proportions  too.

All yum.

Not only are the chicken livers & hearts not on the ingredients list (marked "optional"), but even without them, are chicken thighs going to be cooked through after a 2-minute sear plus 7 minutes' additional cooking? Also, the livers may be overdone and the hearts slightly under....

All I can say is...where has this recipe been all my life? Perfect. Served with basmati, hummus w/olives and Israeli salad with chopped mint. Next time I’ll add livers and try it with a flat bread. Agree with another cook here - I doubled the red onions. Just delicious.

cut the chicken into bite size pieces before putting in pan?
7 minutes is enough to cook it through?

1 tablespoon black peppercorns
1 tablespoon cumin seeds
2 teaspoons coriander seeds
1 teaspoon whole cloves
½ teaspoon cardamom seeds
1½ tablespoons paprika
1 teaspoons ground cinnamon
¼ teaspoon ground nutmeg
https://www.daringgourmet.com/baharat-middle-eastern-spice-blend/comment-page-1/#comments

I used this recipe too. I found it at https://www.daringgourmet.com/baharat-middle-eastern-spice-blend/ Dry roast the whole spices and 3-5 minutes and let cool completely; then grind them with the last 3 ingredients. Despite some common ingredients, the final mixture is really much different from garam masala, or any other single spice.

I went to my local halal grocer & asked for “baharat” & the owner was confused, because in Arabic, “baharat” means “spice” (he said). Ha! They carried a product called “shawarma meat spice” by Al Salam brand that seems about right. It contains allspice, black pepper, cloves, coriander, ginger, cardamom, dry lemon zest, and cinnamon.

I add a drizzle of olive oil to the seasoned chicken to help diffuse the spices, then I let it sit for several hours or overnight. I let it temper at room temperature for an hour or so before cooking. Once it starts to cook, it's essential to NOT stir it too soon; let the chicken brown and caramelize in the pan. After this happens, it usually takes just a few tosses to finish cooking the chicken so that it's not overdone.

I've got very good pre-made hawaij (an Israeli/Yemenite spice), and I've made a very similar dish with it.  

Is hawaij close enough to baharat that it would taste pretty much the same?  Is there something I could add to the hawaij to change it up for this recipe?

This was a huge hit.  Served with cucumber-parsley salad, pitas and Harrissa.   I used Ottolenghi's recipe for Bharat.  Seemed like a lot of effort for carmelizing one onion though, so next time will use more onions and prepare in the oven.

I like learning about ingredients from other cultures, but even Google wasn't much help with fenugreek. If I go looking for it, should I try a Middle Eastern grocery? I hate to order online until I know if this is something I will use more than once. How essential is this to the taste of the dish? And if I can't find it, is there anything I can substitute?

Try any Indian market or Penzys.  Easy to find at both.  You’ll be able to find both the seeds and preground varieties for 2-3 bucks.  Penzys will probably be a smidge more but their stuff is better quality.
I get it at an Indian spice shop. I've also substituted ground celery seed for fenugreek when I can't find it.
It smells like maple syrup to me.
I ordered this in Jerusalem—no thigh meat, just hearts, livers and gizzards.  The waiter said to me, “you’re an American, you won’t like this”—oh, but I did!  Think i’ll Make for dinner tonight.

You can order the spices directly from Sahadi’s. I get most of my spices from them as well dried and preserved lemons.
If you don’t live too far away visit the Brooklyn Museum then go to Sahadis.

Make the baharat by hand, the fragrance from the grinding (use coffee mill if necessary) enhances the entire meal experience. You can also use a Turkish salad or another varied salad to add variety, and the pickles are a must!!
What pickle variety would you recommend?
The recipe calls for optional hearts and livers, but doesn’t say how many. Like the next guy, I like my chickens with a big heart, but it’s important to be precise.
The article says "a couple."

Made last night.  Delicious!  Had to go to 3 stores to find fenugreek and baharat, not even Whole Foods had in stock.  Finally found at Armenian grocer, which is always less expensive anyway!  

I decided to season chicken about four hours prior to cooking.  I think it really helped flavor.  As others mentioned, chicken takes closer to 30 minutes to cook.  Wonder if Sam was slicing raw thighs before cooking?

Served with Israeli salad, pita, and homemade hummus. Highly recommend!

I made this for 2, using 1/2 lb of swordfish instead of chicken, and it was wonderful.  I soaked the fish chunks 10 minute in milk before tossing them with the spice mix and cooked them 4-5 minutes.  I served the meal with Israeli Salad that featured toasted pearl couscous, Mina brand harissa (mild), and pitas.  Something wonderfully sweet happened every time the fish, salad, pita, harissa, and onions were all in the mouth at once.  I will absolutely caramelize a whole big red onion next time.

I made this dinner again, using salmon cubes which were available as kebabs from my local grocer. It was even better than the swordfish!  I used one large red onion for the two of us, which sufficed, barely.  More onions next time, for sure.
What's Israeli salad? Please provide a summary of ingredients.

Commonly Israeli salad is made up of diced cucumber (English/seedless variety work best) and tomato, dressed lightly with olive oil, salt and pepper. Onions and parsley are sometimes added but are not necessary. It's really flexible so feel free to adapt to suit your taste.
Israeli salad: as described by other reader but must have lemon juice in it!
Cucumber and Israeli Couscous Salad (this site).

I made this tonight as written using mostly thighs but also some chicken liver (don't care for hearts and probably couldn't find them in my supermarket anyway).  I cut up the thighs before adding the spices.  The grill was really delicious, the spices worked well together, and the meal did remind me of food eaten in Israel.
Delicious — Cut chicken into very small piece so it cooks evenly , then the rub.  Red onions are great - put too much olive oil and it burnt.  Cook very low.
Baharat is Arabic for a mixture of spices.
And you can get it ready made, if you don't want to buy large quantities of expensive spices for a little bit of spice blend, at Kalustyan's.  If they don't have it, it doesn't exist.

Made with skin on bone in thighs.  Delicious but had to put in oven to cook chicken through.  

Make extra carmelized onions! Half an onion is not enough! 

Also, replaced baharat with a teaspoon of paprika and a little extra of everything else; replaced fenugreek with Greek oregano...I mean, they both had Greek in the name... 

Let me reiterate: very delicious!
Sorry,Scug,Harrissa,and Baharat are not interchangeable as suggested by some notes. Schug and Harissa are very HOT spicy sauces. Baharat's heat is much more subtle

I once bought a package of grocery store chicken labeled "hearts and gizzards."  At home I found one pound of gizzards and one, counted ONE, heart!  Life is better now that I and my butcher know each other by name.  She saves chicken hearts and feet for me and I bring her treats from my kitchen.

We're Jerusalemites who miss the real deal and so I tried this. Verdict:
In Jerusalem, chicken spleens are traditionally included. They're missed in here, but IDK where to find them in the US anyway. I didn't take the onions out of the pan-pushed them to the side when I put in the meat and then sauteed them with the meat at the very end-which is done in Jlem. Subbed turkey 'tenders' for chicken thighs b/c that's what I had. Added in hearts and livers well after meat so that they don't overcook

The reason that it is hard to find chicken spleen in this country is because it is illegal to sell them. Not surprisingly also for lack of demand. If you happen to find a grocer who does so, by all means go for it. Or buy a plane ticket.

I thoroughly disagree with the writer who said allspice is a good sub for Baharat spice mix. The mix is fun, interesting and lively. Good with Turkish Pide. Totally worth making or buying and using with many other dishes over time. Great to expand our western palates.

Instead of the chicken hearts (and / or the chicken liver) I also substituted with calf liver. However, staging is important . The calf liver pieces need to get into the pan only after the chicken meat is done, and should be fried only for a couple of minutes, still maintaining some pink hue inside.
What is the brownish/reddish condiment served with this dish in the picture?
I had a pound of ground turkey that I subbed in for the chicken, mixing in the spice mix for a bunch of merguez-like fresh sausages. It was good! And fast, once the onions were done.
I have made this several times and it is a family favorite. Do you have recipes for the lentils and salad that are in the photo?

I made this tonight on my cast iron griddle set on the grates of my old standby standard Weber grill. And used two green garlic bulbs in the hummus because they're available now at the farm market up the street. Made a lettuce salad with a bit of red onion, sliced mango, and a vinaigrette. Served as recommended with sliced pickles and pita bread. The family was ecstatic!
Adding a very small pinch of baking soda (it doesn't take much) quickens the Maillard process for caramelizing onions. Will save some time on a hurried weeknight.
I am thinking of making a Lenten meal of this, substituting chunks of salmon or swordfish for the chicken and (of course!) reducing the protein’s cooking time.  Any thoughts?
It will be delicious. You could even use tofu. These spices make everything better.
I was so excited for this, a real taste of Israel... so disappointed.  The high point was the chopped greek salad (now called Israeli, I guess... all good) hummus and pita.

Henry,
I have eaten Greek salad in Greece and Israeli salad in Israel. Both delicious but  equating the two is like saying a dump truck and a motor scooter are the same. In Greece delicious fresh vegetables in olive oil  and lemon each piece of vegetable bite size. In Israel the salad is cut very very small each bite made of multiple pieces of each of the vegetables.
This was very delicious. Served with rice, spinach and plain yogurt. Will try with the liver next time for sure!
Followed recipe exactly and found the result lacked interest and “zing”. I love Moroccan food, so thought this would be pleasing. Won’t make again. Usually love Sam’s concoctions.

For a different version: Follow the steps 2 and 3 but instead, use a pot big enough to hold the basmati rice. Add water (2 1/2 cups for a cup of rice). -25 minutes until rice is done. Add some pine nuts (slightly grilled). Serve hot. Could be accompanied by Greek yogurt.

The Israeli salad in the photo has vegetables cut up into pieces which are too large.  A true Israeli salad cuts the tomatoes, cucumbers, onions, into tiny pieces.  Olive oil and lemon juice are a must. Salt and pepper, too.

I made the recipe with only chicken thighs. It was easy to follow and the results were luscious!  I think it worth buying the baharat and fenugreek spices on line to get this amazing flavor profile. 

I didn't have pita on hand, so I scooped up the chicken and onion mixture on sesame seed buns. The squirt of lemon at the end adds a lovely acidity to the spices and the crunch of a pickle and/or Israeli salad provides a great texture contrast to the chicken mixture. This recipe is a keeper!
I would smear some Labneh (Israeli Yogurt) drizzled with olive oil and either dukkhah or Zatar on the plate

Possible substitutes for fenugreek are curry powder or fennel seeds. Substitutes for baharat are garam masala (1:1) or a blend of cinnamon, cumin, and cardamom to get some of the prominent flavors in the blend.

Baharat is easily made from common spices: black pepper, coriander, paprika, cardamom, nutmeg, cumin, cloves, and cinnamon. I toast the whole spices lightly in a dry fry pan, then grind in a coffee grinder that is dedicated to spices only. There are many regional variations of baharat, many of which include additions like dried mint or fenugreek leaves. 

Not sure I would use curry powder as a substitute for fenugreek seeds. It has a very different taste.

I used to be wary to say the least of ingredients like chicken hearts. My husband even more so. Then we went to Zahav (Solomonov's flagship restaurant), and now I dream about his buttery Turkish hummus topped with chicken hearts, which happened to be the seasonal topping our first time there. While we haven't been as lucky subsequent visits, Zahav never really disappoints. Quarantine day dreams of that hummus and their heirloom tomato season "Israeli martini..."

I decided to use up sliced mushrooms in the fridge and coated them with the spice mix before sauteing them in the skillet.  I also didn't have baharat, so I Googled garam masala and noticed that several of its spices overlap with those in baharat, turned out great.  I cut the salt in half, just to see how high the salt level is at midpoint, I ended up keeping the salt to 1 teaspoon.  Great recipe for changing up lunch or dinner!
I've made this recipe several times and my family loves it.  I ordered Baharat online.  Delicious.
His cookbook “Israeli Soul” is a must for every cooks collection!
What is the reddish sauce looking stuff in the bowl at the bottom? It looks like there's pepper seeds in it?
What kind of pickles? Sweet, dill, gherkins, cornichons, bread and butter?
Could one make this with lamb?

I loved this dish, but a warning, the whole house still smells like baharat after several days.  It smells good while you are cooking it and while you are eating it but it lingers for days.  Maybe I will take my cast iron pan out and cook it on the grill next time :)
serve with salad (not as sandwiches)

This is absolutely delicious. I made it with only about a pound and half of chicken thighs, since that's what I had. I made my own baharat from a recipe I found online - easy to do. Used yellow onion instead of red, but otherwise followed the recipe exactly. I served it with basmati rice, homemade hummus, and creamy cucumbers, but next time I'd skip the rice and creamy cucumbers and go with pita or naan, and a green salad. It's a keeper!
I find the offal is a necessary ingredient. All in all this recipe lacks flavor (due to incorrect timing and a amount of spicing). Also why with the skinless boneless...takes the life out of it.
Made with bone in skin on thighs—browned 3 minutes/side and finished in 400° oven for 20 or so minutes.

Note the notes:  need more onions and the note re cutting chicken thighs
re Baharat from 
Eli W2 years ago
This is what I found on Wikipedia:

A typical recipe for baharat is a mixture of the following finely ground ingredients:

4 parts black pepper
3 parts coriander seeds
3 parts cinnamon
3 parts cloves
4 parts cumin seeds
1 part cardamom pods
3 parts nutmeg
6 parts paprika

Made this last night and it was great!  I ordered Baharat, fenugreek and Israeli pickles off of Amazon because I couldn't get them locally, but they came next day so that was easy.  I read in some of the reviews that there wasn't enough spice, so I used 1.5 times the spices and it was amazing.  I also made the 5 minute Hummus, heated up pita in the oven and made a side salad and it was a fantastic meal!  I will definitely make it again!
Does anyone have a recipe for the hot sauce pictured? (I assume that's what it is?)

I made a few changes while following the cooking instructions. Ingredients: Instead of the listed spices, I substituted 4 heaping teaspoons Za'atar seasoning, 1 heaping teaspoon dried Oregano, and a scant teaspoon sea salt. I added three medium-size Yukon Gold potatoes, chopped in quarter-size chunks. When the chicken and potatoes were almost done, I added a half dozen red, orange and yellow mini-peppers, cit into bite size rings. Served it over rice with a generous squeeze of lemon.

This has been a "GO TO" company dinner for years now.   I worry that it will be deleted from the NYTimes Cooking page someday.   I make my own baharat spice mix.  Over the years I have learned to caramelize the onions in advance (like a day or two)  -- then just add them to the chicken at the end.  Go easy on the oil -- I use less than called for in the recipe.   I like to serve it with roasted vegetables,  hummus, & bahatmi rice.   Perfectly delicious -- gets rave reviews every time.

title: Salmon With BBQ Sauce and Hot Peppers Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: Not usually into no recipe — like the mind-clearing concentration a recipe takes — but this was very good. Salmon perfect at 10 min. Brushed with some barbecue sauce for baking. Added some partially pre-baked very small potatoes to the sheet pan. No barbecue sauce on hand, so tossed together & heated tomato paste, ketchup, water, a little chopped onion, 1 minced garlic clove, worcestershire, red wine vinegar, lemon juice, mesquite smoked paprika, hot sauce & the jalapeno & butter Sam added. Yum.

Chipotles en adobo (canned, commonly available) lend themselves especially well to this idea since the adobo is of similar makeup to many jarred barbecue sauces. Be careful to start with small amounts of both the adobo and the chipotle. A little of each goes along way and it is easy to overwhelm the salmon.

Cook for 8 minutes max 
OR
pan sear:  1 minute skin side up in hot skillet then turn and 6 minutes in oven at 475
I was out of BBQ sauce, so I made my sauce from ketchup, rice vinegar and smoky paprika, plus the pickled hot peppers and butter that Sam suggests. Came out great!
Salmon loves mesquite-flavored BBQ sauce.

This (using up that last third of those Costco MIlano Salmon) with Trader Joe's Harissa Salsa along with Sam Sifton's No Recipe Smashed Potatoes with bacon, shredded brussel sprouts and cheddar...perfect dinner for 2 (especially if one of us is allergic to tomatoes and most bbq sauces won't work)
Added Zuni pickled onions - fabulous.
This was easy and very delicious. I cooked exactly as written and can’t wait to make again
Excellent. I started with great salmon and this simple sauce compliments it nicely
This is one of my favorite recipes. Easy and delicious.
With a high-quality BBQ sauce, no hot peppers are needed. Or desirable.

title: Rotisserie Chicken Salad With Greens and Herbs Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: This is similar to Nigella Lawson’s Roast Chicken Salad (w/ Spinach) found on this website. She includes a recipe for lime & olive oil dressing with salt which is delicious.
A favorite easy, quick "recipe", with endless variations.  I add walnuts or almonds for protein and crunch.  Tasty and filling.

The problem with store bought rotisserie chicken is the salt content. It's usually way too much. Then they add dextrose to a gelatinous liquid filled with other dreadful additives to camouflage was what never a quality chicken in the first place. Make your own when it's convenient and then use it for this nice no-recipe recipe.

@Anne I was surprised how easy it is to make a rotisserie style chicken in the air fryer. Olive oil on the chicken, a spice mix all over the chicken, 40 minutes at 350, then flip it over for another 20 minutes and boom, rotisserie chicken.
Do yourself a favor and get your bird at Costco ... NONE BETTER!

My family calls this "on a bed of," as in, "dinner is On a Bed Of." 

Good use for whatever's in the fridge/on the counter that should be used up, with any meat or poultry that's leftover or purchased. Extra points for creativity.

Has anyone ever wondered about the safety of store bought rotisserie chicken which is placed -hot- into plastic containers and then kept warm under heat lamps. I have never been able to get an answer to this.
@Beth   temperatures are regulated and checked. It is safe.
@CJ Temperature was not the question. Plastic was.
Super easy! Added parmesan cheese and currents to give it some tartness and sweetness.
Made this tonight! Went with half arugula and half spinach, added toasted pumpkin seeds for crunch. Super easy and great way to use up the last bits of scallion and cilantro in the fridge.

we used to do a similar thing for pre-service dinners at a restaurant where I worked in the 80's. We mostly had salad made with lettuce, rocket and other delicious greens with tomato etc grated beetroot carrot or whatever and we put a soft boiled egg on the top for protein - sunflower seeds. the garlic is important. A drizzle of olive oil. A shaving of parmesan. Yum.
Re Costco rotisserie chickens, I've stopped buying them. Costco has been called out for refusing to adopt humane chicken raising or processing.
So good!  Added a little bit of anchovy to the dressing, which was terrific. Leftovers made a delicious sandwich on a bun, with mayo, to take to work.

You're in my head. I opened this email while eating leftover rotisserie chicken chopped in the chopper mixed with apple, celery, and the remains of a bag of broccoli slaw. With a little mayo and a lot of spinach on a piece of white toast. Nothing fancy but comfortable and delicious. Rotisserie chicken is the best cooking cheat there is.
Made this tonight — rotisserie chicken for the flesh eater and white beans for the vegetarian. Added feta and toasted sesame seeds. Delicious! A homemade baguette with butter was a nice side.
I made this with chicken for the bf, and without for me, and it was delicious both ways. I doubled the cilantro because cilantro is life.

Gosh I love this. It is so obvious, and such a nice reminder how great simple quick things can be. As I am famous for staring down leftover roto chicken wondering "what next, my bird?", this is good. A good basic as Sam wrote it  which, as others have noted, is open to endless riffs, spins and variations.  Thank you.
I love to add pineapple
Love this non-recipe. Add cherry tomatoes, sliced radishes and crumbled blue or Gorgonzola cheese - takes this salad to a new level.

I love adding crumbled feta or goat cheese, cherry tomatoes, and crushed blue corn quinoa chips for crunch. I usually do a combination of whatever herbs I have on hand, basil, cilantro, and dill all work great!

Pardon the departure from the original, but I use butter lettuce, cucumber, avocado, tomato, and chicken, with Green Goddess dressing (I use Primal Kitchen)...serve with scallions on the side for adding as diners prefer...
I call this Salade du Fridge, and it’s a staple meal in my home!

I do a version of rotisserie roasted chicken salad with arugula, a sprinkle of Parmesan, toasted pecans (dumped on the greens while warm so they sizzle and wilt the arugula a touch), then dress it with salt, pepper, lemon juice and olive oil. So good. So fast.
This was one day of the best (and easiest) salads I’ve ever eaten. Will be one a regular in our house.

Used the surprisingly velvety breast meat of a rotisserie chicken for Julia Moskin’s Best Chicken Salad. Heaven on a plate for dinner one night and stuffed into a tender pita for lunch the next day. Yum.
At this time of year my garden is overrun with mint (who's isn't, right?) so I substituted mint for cilantro.  Delicious

We had a Mary’s chicken from the home rotis, just the breast.  It morphed into a salad with greens (mostly arugula), tomatoes, avocados, olives and feta for a fabulous meal.  I put a little oregano and toasted cumin in the vinegarette.

Perfect. Used tandoori rotisserie chicken, very flavorful. Toasted some almonds, rough cut, and added for crunch. Would use more arugula / less cilantro. I was surprised by the use of lime instead of lemon but it was nicely piquant!
This is great. We added some grainy mustard to the dressing for an extra kick. It was great. Will definitely add it to our usual rotation.
Sliced sugar-snap peas would not be amiss. Remove any side strings first.
I do one with mache (spell check wanted to make that machetes) and whatever herbs I'm in the mood for....avocado......and dress it with sea salt, pepper and meyer lemon olive oil. so simple and good.
This is good as is, but a handful of toasted walnuts would take it up a notch.

Really enjoyed this salad! Added Persian cucumber, chickpeas and leftover cooked brown rice and it was perfect for dinner. I also added a little cumin and grainy mustard to the dressing to give it more depth. 

Great for a weeknight dinner!
Use Garlic Expressions Dressing
I added some toasted walnuts for crunch and depth of flavor.  Excellent!
Realized that I didn't have cilantro after I began to  gather ingredients, parsley worked just fine with a pinch of coriander. Good quick salad!
I tore up some basil and mint I had in the garden and added some feta. Yummy!
I did the same thing. I love mint and cilantro together. The cheese makes it a little creamy.
I added chopped mint and goat cheese. Fantastic flavors. I had some arugula and herbs leftover and served it as a side salad; still perfect!
Made this salad without the chicken as a side salad.  Added a little bit of feta cheese.  Super tasty!
Make this with toasted, skinned hazelnuts, raspberries or seedless grapes, romaine, or mixed greens, and bits of Gorgonzola or similar cheese. Toss with a raspberry vinaigrette.

It’s also delicious to put shedded chicken, some greens, salsa, avocado, green onions, tomatoes (basically this salad) etc. rolled up in a flour tortilla for a cold sort of instant burrito. Nice for no cook, cold summer dinner.
This was so quick and easy for a busy weeknight. I threw it together and consumed it in under 30 mins. So filling, too!

Sam’s “no-recipe” recipes are always good. I never buy rotisserie chicken (many reasons), but I’m adding this to my file of recipes for leftover chicken, which I often have, particularly since I no longer freeze cooked chicken. When I make a whole (rotisserie!) chicken the two of us eat it for a couple of days more until it’s gone. All I freeze is the stock I’ve made from the carcass. Frozen cooked chicken dries out too much, and it tends to get lost in the freezer.
I can’t be the first to suggest that a nice, crusty baguette would go well here, can I?

I've made a similar green salad with chicken at least once a week but with a few differences:
1) The whole bird will feed us two adults for 6 meals at least!
2) I will mix it up salads depending on the season; a) a crisp sliced apple, feta or blue cheese with toasted walnuts, b) shredded carrots, cherry tomatoes and fresh corn on the cob or c) roasted sweet potatoes and cauliflower with curry/cumin seasoning sprinkled with raisins and pumpkin seeds.
I couldn't find pears in season, so I substituted Nectarines -- it was fantastic!

4 thighs,1.33 lbs-done in 20 min:185 degrees
1 lime @2tbs; 1-2 tbs OO
Great, quick recipe.  I'll be making this regularly, varying with shrimp or tuna as a replacement for the chicken.  Also, herby mixed greens instead of arugula make a nice alternative.
DELICIOUS

Just perfect! Healthy, easy, great for weeknight or weekend and great for parties. The suggested blend of flavours, which is my favourite, works perfectly. Sometimes I substitute the juice of a whole lemon for lime juice and add vinegar preserved capers, particularly on hot summer days. The recipe works perfectly even if you remove the skin of the chicken (to avoid the skin‘s fat and high salt content).
So simple and so delightful! Received high marks from my husband, who can count on one hand the salads he would enthusiastically eat for dinner.
The best no recipe recipe with endless opportunities! I add chopped almonds as suggested by another reviewer. This one never fails.
Whole Foods has rotisserie chicken without the  added salt and preservatives.
I used the leftover tahini dressing from the quinoa bowl and it was incredible!

I made this with a chicken (actually a fowl) I had used for making chicken soup. Worked great. Mixed greens, sliced tomatoes, red onion, salad stuff plus the avocado. Squeezed a lime over the mix, added cilantro and oil. Delicious dinner from leftovers.
Add Olives
My husband says this is the best salad he's ever had. I used basil olive oil with juice of one lime and a minced garlic clove. Chicken was stove top cooked whole chicken.

An effortless and cool salad. This recipe made my usually a bit sad rotisserie chicken night feel kind of chic. 


I added honey to the dressing and sprinkled with maldon salt and nutritional yeast (as a dairy free sub for the parm everyone has been suggesting). 

Enjoy!
I would make this into a wrap, yum! Maybe make the dressing a little thicker so it doesn't leak out of the wrap easily.
I thought you had an idea how to turn an overly salty chicken into something edible.
This is a once a week dinner for us. Also helps our diet. It's sort of an obvious perspective, but the freshest ingredients really make a difference.
This was fantastic! I minced the garlic in my dressing & added a little black pepper and balsamic vinegar. It made a very tasty, and easy to make, dinner.
I love the DeTox Chicken Soup Recipe...I know I'll like these too!
NYT has recipes so locked down, I cant even send them to use in my recipes. Sometimes I cant even see ingredients it’s so complex.

Improvised a Julia Childish vinaigrette, which was nice; next time I will move more into a miso-sesame-lime thing and lean on the scallions. For this round, though, dried thyme, thin-sliced radishes, and roasted cubed sweet potato were nice additions (03.03.26). Also thinking about coriander, chili, turmeric, mustard, curry powder.
I find store bought rotisserie chicken to be way too salty. I broil mine own.
I make this with smoked trout or a poached chicken breast rather than store-bought rotisserie chicken.  Poaching only takes a few minutes and, to me, makes for much tastier chicken.

I often do a version of this. Sliced up roast chicken breast, avocado, chopped up cucumber for crunch, sliced red onion, and lots of any kind of lettuce/aragula. (Iceberg lettuce works very well.) With a vinaigrette of 3 tablespoons olive oil to one of balsamic vinegar. Very popular in our house. Healthy too. :-)

"Arugula" in U.K. (and Commonwealth countries) = "Rocket"
I had no idea what you were talking about until I googled it...
I added fresh mint and cubed mango and it was absolutely delicious! Great summer salad!

This no-recipe has been a game changer for me. I make it cruelty-free by using Beyond brand frozen seasoned vegan 'chicken' pieces, adding garlic powder and paprika, it cooks up in under 10 minutes. I do the rest as described in the no-recipe, subbing sliced onion for scallion, plus the optional avocados. This dish is restaurant worthy IMO, I've had it (& loved it) almost every night for the past week, can't wait to try some of the other no-recipes (and make them cruelty-free too!).  Thank you!

title: Soft-Boiled Eggs With Anchovy Toast Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
How is this delicious NOT a recipe (just in paragraph format)? 

Ingredients:
1 stick of butter
1 tin of anchovies
Etc. 

Method:
1. Let the butter soften on the counter while you assemble the other ingredients: a tin of anchovies, some garlic cloves, a shake or two of smoked or regular paprika, a wee splash of lemon juice, and...
2. Rinse and mince the little fish, mince the garlic, and fork everything together into the butter to taste.
3. Then slather toast with the result and serve it with...
How long can you store the anchovy butter?

This rocks: made it this morning. But, anchovy butter needs nothing more than finely-minced anchovies (I use oil-packed, and I didn't rinse them) and butter. Lemon juice, garlic, etc are distractions. You could gussy this up with capers, etc, but it's fine without garnishes. Maybe a little black pepper ...

My go-to Thanksgiving brunch. Not filling enough to ruin dinner. I'm not sure why this isn't in proper recipe form:

1 stick butter, softened
1 tin anchovies
2 garlic cloves
1/4 tsp smoked paprika
salt, as needed
Good quality bread, toasted
Poached egg

Blend or smash together or use your grubby fingers to do the work. Spread over good quality toasted bread and serve with a poached egg.

Stupid good. I realized I had both a tin and a tube of paste and since this requires rinsing and mashing up the fish -- I grabbed the tube. I used old (close to too old) no knead bread and 3 (old) garlic cloves, grated on a zest-er. I'm not good at eggs so I steamed. I did not wait. Dang! Only one egg, and one piece of bread is needed. Stupid good.
As a non-American that is not butter-obsessed, I used olive oil instead and made more-or-less a chunky paste. Scrumptious!

Amazing! The mix of crispy, salty, beady, fatty goodness with umami makes for quite a meal.  I adjusted the anchovy butter recipe because my fillets would not puree.  I sauted them in olive oil with a few cloves of garlic and some red pepper flakes.  I added the lemon juice, chilled the mixture, then blended it with the butter. Yum!
Sounds great! I just used a chef knife to mince my anchovies. I left my butter out for a long time so it was very soft and easy to mix with a spoon.

My household consists of me. This is a great recipe because you can shape the butter into a log about an inch wide; slice it into quarter-inches; and freeze those slices (wrapped in parchment or waxed paper).
All you have to do is remove one slice from the freezer while you prepare the egg and toast. By the time the egg and toast are done, the slice has softened enough to be spreadable. 
No bacon or ham or sausage (or scrapple, I'm Pennsylvanian) necessary; this delivers plenty of umami.
Ok ok hear me out. Smear the anchovy butter on good bread and then pan fry... insane.
I used the drained anchovy oil to cook my eggs over easy for an added bang.
This anchovy butter is truly greater than the sum of its parts. I can eat it on ANYTHING.

I've been enjoying a similar yet also delicious recipe since I was a kid. It's just the butter and the anchovies on toast with scrambled eggs. We don't rinse off the anchovies and throw them in with the tin's oil. Here, I didn't like the addition of the lemon juice or paprika but the garlic was a nice touch. But that's just my own personal preference.
I added capers as someone suggested...yum!

Cannot recommend this enough. While prepping the butter (deleted paprika, added fresh dill), did a quick 2 egg poach, cracked 2 eggs into boiling water—> remove w slotted spoon. Toasted the bread, slathered the butter, eggs on top. A stupendous 10 min addition to Saturday morning.

I made this with brown butter and threw the anchovies into the butter as it was browning. Stir well with a fork and the anchovies melt into the butter. Once it’s cooled and solidified slightly, mix in garlic, lemon and took someone’s suggestion to add mustard.  Topped with a soft boiled egg, pepper and red pepper flakes.  This was delicious, easy, will make again. Looking forward to trying other variations. I forgot the smoked paprika but will use next time.

What a great idea! Happened to have a can of sardines when I read this non recipe and so tried it with a baked potato and a side of veggies without the egg. I loved the freshness of the lemon with the sardines and how well the butter tempered the sardines. I love liverwurst and although this flavor was completely different, it had a similar consistency.
This was DELICIOUS

I started to make this when I realized I didn’t have anchovies. Was super sad but mixed up the butter with some lemon, salt, garlic, and paprika anyway. I’ve been really into soft-boiled eggs now that I’ve nailed down the right method for me (shout-out Budget Bytes) and was happy to mash those little beauties onto the butter-slathered toast. It was ridiculously good. Looking forward to my next attempt with anchovies. I bet it will be even better.

I love anchovie toast, just never thought to put an egg with it.
This is a to try.
1 stick butter -- how many bread slices will this cover?
