Oh yeah. Also, I just take a stick of butter and melt it in a pan and then add the anchovies and let them dissolve with the help of mushing them with a fork. Then spoon the melted anchovy butter on the toast. Just warm and melt any leftovers the next day.

You definitely won’t need additional salt. I think maybe a tin of anchovy was a touch too much. I made it the morning of and chilled it in the fridge for a half hour maybe less. Delicious, very decadent for breakfast keep in mind. Would be good with a side of arugula salad.
You could also serve eggs with bagna cauda.

For the perfect egg with a soft yolk and fully cooked white: steam it right out of the refrigerator for 6.5 minutes, then run it under cold water. Steam about 20 seconds longer if you don’t want a runny yolk.

This stuff is seriously or even frivolously good. Had it with poached eggs on grainy bread for breakfast then for lunch in a sandwich with chilli sauce, sliced steak, cheese, roasted red peppers and caramelised onions on Turkish bread that I toasted in the sandwich press with a little olive oil for crispiness. Am now thinking about adding somewhere for dinner...
Beyond delicious. I just put butter, paprika, grated garlic, and oil tin of anchovies (drained, not rinsed) in a bowl and smashed with a fork until creamy. So simple and so special.
I made it. Awesome.  In true Jamaican style I added a bit of scotch bonnet to it.  Moved it up a notch.  Also tried it with mushrooms.  But the soft boiled egg is the real thing.
Alternatively, pound garlic w salt in a mortar, add softened butter or high quality Spanish ev olive oil w anchovies and pound to a paste then slather on toast.
OK to use sardines instead of anchovies?
No. Not OK. It needs to be anchovies. If you put smashed, tinned sardines on your toast, then you must dribble good vinegar on top (cider vinegar, for example.) This is what they do in England.
I just made this and it's insanely delicious. I put all ingredients but butter in a mini food prep. Mixed them. Then added butter and mixed more. Insane.
I like to do this with rolled Anchovies with capers. My family hates it, so I wait until they're out of town. Red pepper and fresh black pepper is required. Fresh herbs a lux garnish.
Absolutely perfect. I used olive ciabatta for toast.

I used a molcajete to blend the ingredients together which worked nicely for getting the garlic and anchovies really squished in with the butter. This is magic in the mouth.  I had it with an easy sunny side up egg.  Toast in the oven for a round two sans egg.  Just so delectable.

Phenomenal butter. Truly dangerous how good it is. My husband is literally angry I told him to try it early because it's so delicious he wants to just make it his entire lunch, rather than using it for breakfast tomorrow as planned. 

If you like umami, anchovy, and rich, acidic, savory in general, try this.
Adding a touch of dijon mustard to the butter takes it to the next level

Added avocado. Melted the butter and wisked the anchovies & garlic then let that set back to slurry state while prepping eggs and avo.. chopped some arugula for bitter and color on top. Love all of Sam’s not-recipes-recipes for the license it moves us to exercise…
Used chia seed bread lightly toasted, spread to the very edges with my own Crème d'Anchois. Dusted lightly with smoked paprika (pimenton) and scattered with chopped cornichons. Very tasty.
For non-Americans, a “stick of butter” is 113 grams.

I love this flavor combo. If you want an easy way to cheat I use anchovy past and a micro plane for the garlic and just mix like crazy. Sometimes I don’t even bother with the egg because it’s so good on simple toast.

1) take a few spoonfuls from your butter crock and spread it onto a shallow bowl
2) mix some anchovy paste into the butter. 
3) spread the new concoction onto some slices of sourdough bread and toast them in the oven, or alternatively in a buttered cast iron pan, which is how I prepare it. 
4) poach a few eggs. (You know how to do this part, right?) 
Place poached eggs onto buttered and toasted slices of bread. ENJOY!
How many anchovies are in s tin? I buy a big jar.

I have no idea as a buy a big jar as well.
I would suggest 3 or 4 and then add until you like it.
About 12. Tins contain 2 oz.
I haven’t made it yet but will poach the eggs— easier and less messy than either soft boiled or scrambled.

What a treat to discover anchovy butter! This is my new favorite thing. I make it with anchovy paste (which I think gives it a smoother flavor than when I make it with anchovies), a little microplaned garlic, and a dash of white wine vinegar. Served on toast with a poached egg on top is heaven to me.

I have legitimate cravings for this. I use anchovy paste, because it’s easily stored and saves me some prep work. Otherwise, I follow the other suggestions of this non-recipe. Soft-boiled egg is the way to go. This is part of my weekly rotation and makes for a great slow & lazy weekend breakfast.
Anchovy paste is the bottom of the barrel, really. Get good anchovies in a bottle. Please

This was an easy, delicious recipe that I will repeatedly make going forward. I added chopped, cooked broccoli rabe for an added crunchy, nutritious element. It that’s not your favorite, sautéed spinach is a more mild alternative way to add some greens and get an extra nutrient in the recipe. Enjoy!
Unbelievable...just the sort of savory bomb that i adore. Ate it on Ken Forkish’s Saturday white loaf-toasted. A breakfast i will remember.
Unclear from the directions: Should I re-chill after mixing together, or allow to remain soft on the counter?
This is gloriously simple and delicious. Currently eating it at 3 p.m. -- no need for it to be limited to breakfast!
This is incredible as is. Or you could switch out the bread for pasta. With or without egg. Phenomenal.

Delicious. I used the whole tin of anchovies and didn't rinse the fish before roughly chopping. I minced the garlic but microplaning probably would have been easier. Was generous with the mix-ins of parsley, paprika, pepper. Definitely go for the soft boiled egg on toast... But I also can't wait to use this butter for pasta!!
This looks terrific, will definitely try this...  Anyone ever sub avocado for butter?

OK, made it today, this is an awesome dish.  My daughter hates anchovies and also loved it.  I used ciabatta bread (got at Sam's Club) and broiled/toasted it for about 1.5 minutes on high.  Perfect dish, thanks so much!!!  Tomatoes on the side were yummy with this.
Thanks, Sam! Made this yesterday for lunch.The best on a snowy day, though probably excellent in the middle of summer as well.
I skipped the prep and made an anchovy brown butter that I spooned over fried eggs, flecked with paprika and parsley. Special.
Really good, but ultimately, just butter some toast and lay some anchovy filets down on it and save yourself some time.

I didn’t want to take the time to make anchovy butter, so I warmed the anchovies and their oil in a saucepan until they disintegrated, and used that to dress some arugula. I put the arugula on top of my buttered toast, and soft boiled eggs on top of that. Delicious!

SO Yummy!! I followed the recipe and soft boiled the perfect jammy eggs. I happened to have crispy pancetta in the frig so I sprinkled that over the anchovy toast split the egg & layed that on top then fresh chopped parsley from my garden. Can't wait to make it again
Why would I rinse anchovies? I’ve never done this.

I just had this for lunch. And yum! I made the compound butter the night before. Had a tin of Wild Planet anchovies which looked more like sardines. No matter, I followed the suggestions, put the contents into a glass jar in the fridge overnight. The next day, I put the butter into the microwave for a few seconds to soften it up. I was just going to slather on one piece of toast and top with one fried egg, but it was so good, I caved in and had seconds. Easy peezy!
Way too salty!

Was initially grossed out by the idea, but once I gave it a shot I was pleasantly surprised. I don't know if it needs the added salt but it didn't hurt.

Overall it was easy to put together and tasted delicious.
In the UK, this is a version of what is called "Gentleman's Relish."
I LOVE the use of the word, "Sporty!"

title: Kale Salad With Cranberries, Pecans and Blue Cheese Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: I like massaging the kale gently with the dressing before adding other ingredients. It's not quite as raw and easier to eat.

I do this too! I was just going to suggest this to the reader who cringed at raw kale when I saw your note. I just gently massage olive oil through the leaves and it tenderizes it nicely. Then I add some fresh lemon juice and some roasted garlic.
If life is too short to massage dressing into the kale, then it is definitely to short to go through all the steps and clean up of blanching it.

Life is too short to massage kale.

With a tangy dressing and some sweet and crunchy additions (see the no-recipe above), you won't need to get intimate with your kale. 

If you dislike raw kale (why are you reading this?) just blanch it first: throw chopped or torn kale into boiling water for 30 seconds, drain it, drop it on a kitchen towel, mix with dressing asap.

I use a combination of currants, toasted pine nuts and grated parmigiano reggiano.  Use a food processor to pulse kale (not baby kale) into small bits.  Sometimes add finely chopped broccoli florets.  One of my favourite salads; even non-kale eaters will eat this.

For the vinegrette, mix in some almond butter to the mustard, olive oil & lemon juice. It balances the raw kale (I used dark green lacinto), and you never remember you are supposed to hate it. My husband who has to have his arm twisted to eat salad, ate my second day serving. 

Toss whole salad with it, don’t drizzle.

A great idea I learned in CA several years ago - whether you hand rip or chiffonade whichever type of kale you prefer... you massage it thoroughly with half a mashed avocado (adjust to a whole if you are doing a very large quantity) then squirt lemon juice to prevent browning and voila you have tenderness and dressing all in one. Then add your favourite fixings combination as Sam Sifton suggests and season to your taste. Bonus: a great treatment for the hands before you rinse them off.

I made little balls of goat cheese and rolled them in toasted pecans and added them to the salad. I made the dressing with 1/4 cup olive oil and 1/8 cup lemon and orange juice combined, 1 tsp of Coleman's mustard (prepared, not dry), 1 tablespoon cranberry sauce, salt and pepper. Delicious.
easiest way ever to "massage" kale: I throw smaller pieces on my kitchen counter and use a rolling pin on it! (just a couple of passes per leaf is fine).

As a kale grower, we eat raw kale only in the winter when it’s sweet and tender.    Ripped up kale leaves, orange or apple pieces, some toasted nuts or seeds , maybe avocado (me) or onion slices (him) with whatever nice dressing you have or can make quickly.    It’s a treat every time we eat it.

Raw kale can be off-putting - or "ick" as another commenter put it. But it gets a lot better if you slice it and then rub it with a bit of salt to get it to wilt.  The more you rub, the more it wilts.

Make sure you buy organic kale.  Kale is one of the moist pesticide laden foods - coming in as #3 of the worldwide dirty dozen annually listed by the European Working Group.  People think they are eating healthy but kale it's super bad:  "Nearly 60 percent of kale samples sold in the U.S. were contaminated with residues of a pesticide that the Environmental Protection Agency considers a possible human carcinogen, according to EWG's analysis of 2017 Department of Agriculture test..." Google it.

I make this with dried cranberries, currants, and almonds, and with a honey-lemon dressing. The finer I chop the kale, the more it releases its own juices into the dressing. Kale can seem dry to the touch, but there's juice in there.

I find that whether the kale needs to be "massaged" to be tender depends on the variety of kale I use.  Curly kale always needs a massage.   But Red Russian Kale is more tender and good without that extra step.  Tuscan kale is also more tender than the curly variety. The dressing, if done a bit in advance also softens the texture, and yet does not wilt the kale.

Thin sliced kale massaged with olive oil and salt. Blue cheese. Apples. Pecans toasted med/high heat then some syrup poured in and sprinkle with c cayenne and salt. Cooled on a plate. Dijon Mustard, olive oil, red wine vinegar salt and pepper.

Sauté kale in a bit of chopped baccon first. Or Blanche it 20 secs, strain out into ice water, drain off water, dry kale on towel. Raw or un-steamed kale is nasty. In my humble opinion. Maybe add in fresh or sauteed fennel? A sliced hard  boiled egg?
Salad with character!
Dress the salad at least 20 minutes before you plan to serve.  The vinegar will 'cook' the kale making it softer.  Leftovers are delicious over hummus or smashed avocado on bread.
I just have to say that the "life is too short to massage kale" comment was hysterical. I'm trying this recipe just because of that.
Just use baby kale - it's mild and tender.

Delicious salad.  I would like to add that for years, I've been putting about 4 or 5 naked leaves of kale torn off the stem into the food processor.  Hitting pulse ~ 3 times is pretty perfect for bite sized pieces that are already massaged with the motion and blade.  Simply do this as many times to process as much kale as you want for your salad.  Done this way, the machine does a wonderful job cutting and "massaging" the kale.  I've done both Tuscan and curly kale this way.
Better if you cook the kale, serve with all the goodies mentioned, and you can call it whatever you want. Raw kale ick!

I discovered baby kale in a trader joes salad mix - it was great.  I even tricked my hubby into trying it and the man who "hates" kale was pleasantly surprised at this wonderful new leaf I discovered.  : )

Reference previous note about using food processor to cut/massage kale:

Be sure to empty after pulsing each batch of 3 or so leaves.  Otherwise already processed kale on the bottom gets cut into mush! I learned the hard way when I first began doing this.
Chilled red wine???? Never!! Chilled white wine OK.
You don’t know what you are missing! Not chilled chilled, but pleasantly cooled is lovely!  Try it!

Actually, red wine should be served at wine cellar temperature which means 55 to 60 degrees which is quite cool compared to 'room temperature', generally considered to be 68 to 70 degrees. I always open a bottle of red, and stick it in the refrigerator for a bit before serving - especially in the summer.

Think of kale ceviche.  I prepare the kale early in the day or the night before, put it in a zip lock bag and add some vinegar and/or lemon juice to “cook” or soften the kale prior to the actual salad prep.
I made tempeh croutons for this. It was surprisingly delicious and both a little kid and toddler loved it.
I read that eating kale raw yields the best nutrient benefit and massaging helps to release those nutrients and to help make it more digestible.   Thanks for another great kale salad recipe.
Goji berries are great here also.
Made with kale from my garden. Swapped feta for blue cheese. Really good!

I've found that putting ~ 1 1/2 big handfuls at a time of kale stripped from stems into my large food processor and pulsing until it is a comfortable mouth size, it is automatically "massaged" just the right amount.  If you put too much kale in at once, the bottom greens will grind up too small before the top parts make it to the blade. You must be careful not to go too far or you'll make mush of the kale.  I started doing it this way long ago when kale salad first became "a thing".

This salad is good with roasted chopped walnuts, covered with a small amount of maple syrup. I used goat cheese instead of blue cheese. The homemade mustard dressing is a good idea. This is a tasty, fresh summer meal. A garlicky pasta goes well alongside it.
I make a similar salad with roasted butternut squash. Makes it a meal!
I agree about the value of massaging the kale. Takes less than a minute, is kind of fun and easy. I do it before I add any dressing or anything else. Relaxes the kale, just like massages relax us!

Two recommended substitutions -

Roasting nuts in mikes hot honey instead of cayenne/maple syrup

Swapping kale for Trader Joe's cruciferous crunch

Absolutely delicious. This is how I love to approach salads. Once you have the  "recipe" of base salad, salt, savory, sweet – it's fun to riff. Followed this one pretty closely though...toasted the pecans in maple syrup and sea salt, used dried cranberries and finely chopped my kale. Crumbled Point Reyes blue cheese topped it off. I over-dressed it...the only adjustment I would note for the future.
Try SMOKED blue cheese like the organic by Rogue Creamery for a total treat!

I followed the suggestions, and it was a very tasty and satisfying salad. The pecans were to die for (one tablespoon of maple syrup for a cup of pecans, 375 for about 11 minutes). But first, make sure you can handle raw kale. I discovered that I did not. I will spare you the details.
I love this salad. It’s my go to. Everyone I’ve served it to loves it as well. No changes although I can see adding different fruit, nuts, cheese. It’s a winner
Don't want to massage your kale? Strip it from the stems, put it into a big zip-lock bag, and roll it with a rolling pin a few times. Much quicker.

“I’ve been making big kale salads recently, with dried cranberries, lardons of bacon, dabs of Brie, cubes of Honeycrisp apple and sourdough croutons, all slicked in mustardy vinaigrette. Sometimes, to gild the lily, I’ll fry a chicken-thigh schnitzel to drape over the top. This is a statement meal, and a great way to get in a last round of brassicas before switching to spring’s lighter lettuces.”

— Sam Sifton

(NYT Cooking newsletter 03/31/23)

Used juice from ~1/2 lemon to massage kale. Toasted 1 cup pecans (375F for 10 minutes). Tossed with 1T maple syrup & 1/8t cayenne. Added 1/2 package Mt. Vikos feta & generous helping of dried cranberries. Dressed with left over mustard vinaigrette ( /- 4 or 5T). Sprinkled with salt & pepper. Ate with Z’s True North bread. What was left in the fridge transformed into a satisfying salad.
Grow your own kale: it's even better in the Fall.
Life’s too short to eat kale!

Made it with finely chopped and then massaged kale, dried cranberries, feta cheese, maple walnuts (made from recipe I found on the web), and my usual dressing of rice vinegar, olive oil and grey poupon mustard. We found it quite satisfying. Added some left over steak to hubby's. Served with warmed and buttered naan.
I’m vegan.........what could I add to replace the blue cheese for a tang like that?

YUM.  If you slice your raw kale reasonably finely (like the width of fettucine) and then toss it with the acidic dressing recommended by Sam Sifton, or some other dressing that has lemon or vinegar, the kale will wilt but still have some crunch.  Cooked kale won't have crunch.

We chose a stilton as our blue cheese, which wasn't as creamy as, but was more pungent than, a standard blue. The pecans toasted with maple syrup and cayenne were amazing - I went a little heavy on the cayenne...
How did you toast the pecans with maple syrup? In the oven or on a stovetop?

i made mine with chopped green grapes, gorgonzola, and added tahini & inglehoffer’s stone ground mustard to the dressing. sweet and spicy pecans from trader joe’s and their pre-made croutons as well. (definitely massaged the kale) amazing and so easy!

I like blue cheese but wouldn't buy the cheese just for this salad. I've used feta in this kale salad and it was good. I can use the leftover feta in other dishes that blue cheese doesn't work in other recipes.  You can also use an herbed feta cheese too. I toast the pecans without the maple sugar or red pepper and it's just as good.
I chop the kale with a good sharp knife and then mix in grapes, goat cheese, avocado, nuts (whatever I have), then dress.
What does everybody think would be a good substitute if making for family with members that dislike blue cheese? Maybe goat cheese crumbles?

But HK...it takes only about 10 seconds to massage the kale, and this brief task tenderizes it and makes it even more flavorful. It's still raw kale, and it is even more delicious than just tossing it in the bowl.

Lauren,

Soft or firm feta is delicious with kale.  We love it with dried cranberries, dried cherries, or bite sized Granny Smith apples.  I nearly always add toasted slice almonds for more crunch.
works with steamed broccoli rabe or fresh escarole or endive, Belgian or regular plus a little radicchio.  I find kale kind of boring

By cutting kale into thin threads, it renders it tender and easier to eat. I add chiffonaded kale to many types of salads, such as tuna, egg, chicken, caesar, lentil. Also, to potatoes boiling for smashed potatoes or soups, also to a pan i'm scrambling eggs in.

I use lacinato kale (aka dinosaur kale).  I remove the fibrous stems and massage the chopped leaves with some coarse kosher salt for a few minutes to soften it.  It will turn a darker green.  I add chopped apple, red onion, raisins or dried cranberries, sunflower seeds, feta cheese.  I use a good olive oil, some red wine vinegar, a little sugar for the dressing.  It is so good!

As soon as cool weather vegetables are available in Ohio I go buy as many little, interesting looking, greens as I can manage--spinach, lettuce, kale etc. They usually grow fast and easily. The other day for spinach lasagne I picked ten cups (as recommended by another commenter) of mostly the kale and spinach but also a darker lettuce and made the lovely lasagne. I didn't do anything but pull out the thicker stems. I rinsed and lightly dried the greens--not so important as with frozen.

A great idea I learned in CA several years ago - whether you hand rip or chiffonade whichever type of kale you prefer... you massage it thoroughly with half a mashed avocado (adjust to a whole if you are doing a very large quantity) then squirt lemon juice to prevent browning and voila you have tenderness and dressing all in one.  Then add your favourite fixings combination as Sam Sifton suggests and season to your taste.
Bonus: a great treatment for the hands before you rinse them off.
or use baby kale, not as tough, still yummy

This was fabulous! I didn’t expect much but I kept commenting how great it was. I made a mustard vin with 1 rounded tablespoon Dijon, 1 1/2 tablespoons red wine vinegar, 1/2 garlic clove grated, salt and pepper, and about 1 tablespoon lemon juice. I made the maple syrup and cayenne roasted pecans as suggested, and used kale, seasoned herb croutons, dried currants, soft crumbled blue cheese, and pickled red onions. Served with chilled cotes du rhone. Wonderful.
I, too, "massage" my kale as Lynn-Marie suggests. Super easy to do.  Takes 15 seconds.  It  makes a nice difference to just soften it up a bit, if that's what you like which I do!
if you chiffonade your kale it should not need to be massaged.
I really appreciate cooks who can just know how to mix up ingredients. I still like to have a detailed recipe. I see the Wednesday no recipe and think yum but would never leave my comfort zone!

I recommend first striping the leaves off the stems, using just the leaves in the salad and discarding the stems. This makes the salad more of a pleasure to eat, as the often hard and pithy stems can be a hindrance to enjoyment (and perhaps part of the reason why many people do not like kale).

Kale salad is one of favorite winter salads, after a friend brought one to a holiday dinner. My version is very similar to Sam's, but I usually sub pomegranate arils for the cranberries or currants, since my husband buys them all winter. I have variously used toasted pecans, walnuts, pine nuts or slivered almonds, and always add grated parmesan cheese. I just made one to take to a buffet party tonight; it's perfect since kale doesn't wilt like lettuce and the dressed salad keeps well.

I ordered a salad with fried sweet potatoes  at my favorite hang out and when it arrived I was horrified that it was all kale.  I ate it and loved it because the kale had been marinated in something that made it edible to me.  With that said I will try this with some trepidation but my vegan friends say, "well, of course, you marinate it or rub it down with something or other"  Silly me.

I consider it a perk we have kale available year round here in Costa Rica and l make a big mixed green salad at least once a week so we have plenty for a couple of lunches. Thank you Sam for promoting this great vegie.
It's deliciousfinely chopped and added to a pot of beans as well! Another near weekly staple on our menu.
Easier way to massage your kale: stick your hand in a plastic bag and massage away. Or, put the entire salad in a gallon bag and massage from the outside, store the leftovers within, serve the rest.
"Massaged" lacinato kale with 1/2 mashed avocado and salt as someone recommended. Added juice of 1/2 lemon plus a bit of EVO. Toasted pine nuts, dried cranberries and 2 clementines. Yummy.

I never “just use what’s in the fridge, “ not confident that it’ll work out well. But that’s what I did with this. While I’d purchased some locally grown (hoop house) kale, everything else was stuff I had on hand:
-lemon juice leftover from earlier in the week
-subbed 1/2 package Mt. Vikos feta for blue cheese
-remnants of some mustard vinaigrette 
-pecans & dried cranberries are pantry items
The result -eaten with the final third of loaf of good, country bread- was surprisingly satisfying.
Just put the kale in a bag and smash it with a wooden meat tenderizer. Takes 30 seconds.
Delicious!  I added arugula, tarragon leaves, Bartlett pear, Roquefort, cinnamon maple candied pecans, and a few Craisins.
Lay some delicious turkey meatballs on top and call it Thanksgiving! Very low stress.
Add honey to dressing?   Read the other comments.
Of course chilled red—yes!

I added roasted butternut squash, avocado and some griled tuna. Amazing! Took another reader's suggestion and put the kale in a Ziploc and ran it over with a rolling pin- this worked well (I have arthritis). Five stars!
Kale likes a "deep" massage! This salad holds up well the second day.

The best way I've found to increase the tenderness factor for kale is to de-stem it and slice the leaves very thinly. Gather a pile of the leaves, ball them together tightly in one hand and slice  in about 3/8" strips. Kale varieties can vary in texture and I adjust the slicing width slightly to get the correct mouth feel. Quick, easy, adaptable!
Love this salad and have made it a zillion times….it actually isn’t bad the next day for lunch!  I use Feta instead of blue cheese.

Take the kale and put in a ziplock bag and squeeze air out before zipping. Use a wooden mallet to bang on the bag. You can see color change as you tenderize (not pulverize). Then chop and eat. Its perfect this way

title: Smashed Potatoes With Bacon, Cheese and Greens Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: Taking a cue from Helen, I added the strips of bacon to the same sheet pan for the last 20 min of potato roasting :-)

Amazing, thank you so much. My new go-to meal for up to four people. I used diced ham, heavy French cream and goat cheese. Microwaved the potato. Used a very hot oven and got crunchy sides and bottom and soft interior. I'm now working on how to give it the additional pep to make it sing. Perhaps a tiny bit of jalapeno peppers sprinkled on the potoato?

I made this on a Friday night when my husband was out of town and I was wrangling our 2-year-old and 4-year-old wild children. I cooked the bacon in the oven and then swapped the bacon for the potatoes. Added green onion to the suggested no-recipe and it was excellent. Super easy. I had everything in the pantry. Felt like a treat.
Very nice! I followed the non-recipe recipe. Next time I think I’ll add some lemon juice and capers. And an herb of some sort!
Nearly perfect. The missing ingredient is smoked paprika.

These were great.

Here's a tip for the smashing part: if you flip the cooked spuds skin-side-up first, then smash, the potatoes "keep together" a bit better and are a little easier to scoop off the mug bottom if it sticks.
Oo... and here I am the proud owner of a cabbage. This and roasted cabbage wedges (oil, garlic, vin) in lieu of greens on the taters?  Hmm...

Loved it.  It's a little tricky to smash the potatoes....mine wanted to stick to the bottom of the cup and some of the browned surface was lost.  Maybe Pam on the cup next time?  No harm done, it was a delicious weeknight success.

This is a hit in our house! if we want to be fancy we use arugula, Gruyere, and pancetta.  Don't forget to make a few extra to pan fry in the morning under a nice yolky fried egg. The crunchiness after frying them in the cast iron is fantastic.

I have been making smashed red potatoes for years!  I use the medium sized ones, bake in the oven until fork tender, then arrange on a sheet pan and smash each one with a potato masher. I tope each one with butter, S&P, and some garlic powder.  Sometimes I add grated parmesan cheese, then broil until they are crispy and smell wonderful.  I will add bacon next time!
So easy and delicious! I made this last night and it was awesome. Toppings were spinach coated in olive oil, bacon, mashed & salted avocado, green onion, & pepper jack cheese.

The whole family LOVED this meal & requested it again soon!  To bump up the greens, I wilted 3 large handfuls of baby kale in some leftover chicken stock I had in the fridge. Just squeeze out excess broth with tongs before assembling.
Do the bacon first then stir the bacon grease into the potato’s before they go in and add liquid smoke.

Perfection in a potato! No need to try to beat or improve on this tho there are indeed any number that of ways to spice a spud. This concoction is all easy harmony. The only thing I might do with the avocado is to mash it first and make it more of a spread; our slices kept capsizing!
Thanks!  This was delicious, fun, and easy.

Oh dear lord, I love this. So easy and so satisfying. I've made this for dinner but really love to have it for weekend brunch -- while it's in the oven, it frees me up to poach eggs or make omelettes or just sit around in a haze drinking coffee.

As good as twice baked, but easier, and good way to use up gold potatoes which are more perishable. Interesting technique with the greens, avoiding precooking. Glad I did not use parchment - easy to scrape up the nice melted cheese from the pan. Green onions, hot sauce, cherry tomatoes all good accompaniments to cut the richness. My potatoes were irregular sizes and my pan was a mass of greens and cheese rather than neat portions, but that was not a problem.
Love this simple technique to make the smashed potatoes in one pan. They were fluffy and crispy, a super fast and easy.
Very easy to make and it was fun to smash the potatoes. I just had small ones on hand.
These were SO GOOD and SO EASY.  I called them potato nachos.  It just sounds better than smashed potatoes.
We had prosciutto instead of bacon. Good!

This is delicious and easy. I used spinach, gruyere, and pancetta. I first toss the spinach in a  bit of olive oil. For the non-meat eater I left the pancetta out and add smoked paprika as somebody else suggested.
Good! Just put olive oil on top
Everybody loved them!
I’ve used a small piece of parchment paper on top of each potato when smashing with a potato masher. No mess!

title: Rice and Beans With Extras Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: Before you do anything: cover some thinly sliced red or white onion in vinegar (I like cider here), and pinches of salt and sugar. Pickled onions will only improve as you consume the leftovers.

Good tip. I've been doing this with radishes, lately with the many variety we get in our CSA. I sometimes use rice wine vinegar, add a dash of toasted sesame oil and/or a few mustard seeds. I do it about 30 min before I start a meal.

I don't usually have orange juice on hand but I usually have canned mandarins. They add a nice orange essence to a dish. They might make a good sub for the juice, the wedges break apart and add nice little juicy bits.

My take on this dish:
To the onion and garlic saute, add:

Canned tomatoes, drained, keep juice to add as needed
Pinto beans drained, canned or made fresh
Canned mandarin oranges
Oregano
Cayenne pepper

Stir into rice. Top with cilantro. Lime & avocado on side. Good w white fish.
Lime juice works for pickling red onions, too.
I freeze good fresh oj in an ice cube tray, then save the cubes in a bag in the freezer for recipes like this
I sometimes add a splash of vinegar to bean dishes. Sherry vinegar is especially good for this purpose.

Hard to believe something so simple could be so delicious!  It's worth a run to the grocery store if you don't happen to have OJ on hand; it adds a surprising depth of flavor that shouldn't be missed.  I made this just with black beans and rice.  Salsa, cilantro and a squeeze of lime, a couple of corn tortillas heated up on the comal...heaven on a plate.

Thank you for this. A fine reminder for a quick supper with ingredients generally in my pantry. I only added minced chipotle in adobo to the bean & onions. A quick salsa with mango, avocado, jalapeño, lime and basil. Delicious, thank you!

Very good. Would never have thought to add orange juice, but it really works. Used garlic, onion and cumin, black beans, and chunks of roasted tofu. Served with avocado and tortillas. It’s quick and tasty. Easily modifiable for spices, heat, protein etc.

Black rice and beans appears a few times a month in our weekly rotation. For those of you with an instant pot...I put dried beans in with water as I wait for my coffee to percolate. Set it for 22 minutes + 10 minutes natural release and it is all done before I leave the house. I drain the beans before I leave inthe AM. Or this can all be done night before, of course.

I use half for one dinner and freeze the rest for the next dinner. Beans taste better and then I'm in control of the sodium.

I took the advice of someone here - making sure I absolutely add the OJ. I actually ran out in the middle of cooking to buy orange juice. I am SO glad I did. The OJ with the cumin make this dish. I have found that it is necessary to wait until the juice has simmered down into a syrup (as the recipe indicates) before adding the beans. I rushed once, and the taste difference was noticeable. Don’t skimp on the cilantro and lime...and and the jalapeños if you like spicy. Heavenly!!

Believe it or not, the closest thing I had to orange juice in the house were fresh peaches. I diced one up and used in place of the OJ -- cooked them down a little but didn't let them get to the dissolving point. The sweetness worked well with the other flavors.
I didn’t have orange juice, so I used beer instead to make a nice glaze. Dark beer makes it nice and chocolatey but any will do.

I added scrambled tofu to the mix, and paired it with cilantro lime rice. It was actually quick (unlike some recipes that say 20 mins and really mean an hour), and very low-effort for high results. Would recommend adding chipotles, salsa or some other sauce with strong flavor. Otherwise, great recipe!

As I don't have OJ around, I keep a can of frozen OJ concentrate, spoon out what I need for any recipe. I can also dilute it as much or as little as I want.

Recipe looks easy and delicious.
I topped with some crisp lettuce and shredded sheese... very flavorful for such a simple recipe.  Quickly  warm tortillas in a hot cast iron pan for soft corn-rich mouthfuls.

Suggested modifications:
1. canola or vegetable oil for the diced onion (olive oil flavor lost so no point)
2. add diced red pepper along with the onion
3. no syrup is necessary, depending on the other spices you add
4.  just add the can of black beans to the diced, cooked onions/peppers
5. spices:  really, to taste.  cumin, yes, but also possibly turmeric and pepper, but the main spice to add liberally is powdered chipotle.  Personally, I like a liberal dash of smoked paprika
@Nick N.  I choose EVOO for the nutritional and anti inflammatory benefits which canola and vegetable oils lack.

I'm glad that I don't have to choose between Sam Sifton's writing or his recipes. Both are terrific.

My favorite appliance is our rice maker. If you are busy or tired, the rice maker makes the rice portion of a recipe a snap. Instant rice is a travesty.

Enjoy Sam's version of a solution to coming at home when you are exhausted. Or your own version. But first, put the rice in rice maker, turn it on, take a shower, open a beer, and then start cooking. The rice will be ready when you are.

I’m interested. I have had rice from a rice cooker several times, and still haven’t found that it is as good as when you make it in a pot. It’s not real hard, a cup of rice to 2 cups of water. Am I missing something?

Delicious. This answers the question of what’s for dinner on a night when your resources are stretched thin. We use Goya black beans because they are so much better than other canned varieties. We add a chopped poblano to the pan with the onions. And since we almost never have prepared orange juice around, we use fresh juice from the oranges or tangerines in the fruit bowl.

Frozen OJ concentrate is a great freezer pantry staple. U can take it out of the cardboard tube and keep in a storage tub in the freezer. I’ve always been able to scrape off a spoonful or 2 for recipes like this or vinaigrette’s without having to defrost the whole container. The ‘ concentrate’ has a lot of the water removed so you get good rich orange flavor.

I have made this several times now, and have found it to always be very good.  Sometimes add a chopped red pepper with the garlic and always the orange juice. That  is magic. The addition of pickled onion in is good, as is some vegan burger crumbles.  Oh and cilantro, always if you like it.  And  it is very nice in tacos for breakfast or lunch the next day......talking about soft tortillas here, not crispy, though I guess that would work ok for lunch. Thank you for the quasi recipe, my favorite.

Mine is a general suggestion that I recently discovered. My wife likes brown rice and I like basmati on occassion. I recently discovered that you can cook these 50:50 togethed in a rice cooker and they come out perfectly (water maths 2:1 for brown and 1.5:1 for basmati, 1.75 cups of water for each cup of the hybrid rice). My fussy kids like it too. Extra fiber and absorbs some sauce - bonus/game changer.

add can of black beans to diced, cooked onions/peppers, 
spices: cumin, possibly turmeric and pepper, add chipotle, dash of smoked paprika, oregano; 1-2 tsp frozen of oj, jalapeños, cilantro

serve w avocado, salsa, rice, flour or corn tortillas
Yes,  I can do this! Added andouille sausage and did the orange juice. Delicious! It was nice with some roasted sweet potatoes and brussel sprouts too.

Why drain the black beans?  Chick Peas, yes, but black and red beans - never.  The liquid has a lot of the color and flavor.  Leave the liquid in.  To make it a Cuban black bean recipe, saute onions, bell pepper, garlic, cumin, bay leaf, a squirt of red wine vinegar, salt and pepper to taste, add beans and simmer for 20 minutes.

I agree with you...and try it with about a dozen big green olives chopped up and added with the onion and pepper. a cup or so of chicken stock helps lengthen the simmer and further tenderize everything.
I made it with mango juice and it was delicious
I like this approach. It's very literary; recipe as action.

Planned on rice and beans for supper tonight and voila, this recipe appeared!  
I used fresh grapefruit juice because I didn't have fresh orange and incorporated the chipotle, as some wise person suggested.  
Go make this! 
And, use Sam's formula to make it your own!

If you have the time, cooking dry beans before hand improve this a hundredfold. If you have a crock pot, simply start the beans on the stove top and boil vigorously for 10-15 min, then put them into the crock pot at the appropriate heat setting.
I really enjoyed this! I added a can of chipotle peppers in Adobo with the orange juice and let that cook down a bit before adding the beans. Magical flavor! I'll be making it again.
Follow the directions to a tee however... I mix the rice with the beans, cover with quality cheddar, and throw under the broiler until bubbling ! Enjoy !
Just so good-- even better the day after! Added a jalapeño into the mix with the onion and garlic. Hot sauce is essential for some acid.

I use the Can’t Miss Rice recipe replacing water with chicken stock. I use ground turkey instead of beef. Sliced jalapeños, jarred salsa and canned black beans round this dish out nicely. It’s easy to make and very popular in our home.

Definitely going to give this a go, as my kids' favorite meals are of the 'rice and beans' variety.  I'll be making some changes to make it vegetarian though, so probably replace the main protein suggested with seared tempeh, or even forgo all together and just add veg!  It's summertime, so probably tomatoes, peppers, and some summer squash of some kind; maybe some kind of hefty, dark green to up the nutrition factor.

It was all fun and games until the orange juice showed up. That was not for me.  I prefer to keep this dish salty/savory.  But so many options to build on this.  I added a few grape tomatoes, and pressed the mix with Monterey Jack into a fresh tortilla.  Fabulous (except for the OJ essence).
Four medium sweet potatoes chopped in chunks, one can black beans, cilantro, lime juice, red pepper flakes, onions and garlic caramelized, salt and pepper. Great!

We often serve black beans like this as part of a burrito bowl...short grain brown rice + beans + toppings (cheese, quick pickled onions, tomato, lettuce, avocado and sometimes roasted sweet potato). Simple, cheap and tasty supper. The addition of orange juice simmered down was delightful and I will totally do this again the next time we have burrito bowls.

I loved this! I don’t eat meat for breakfast or lunch, so I added diced sweet potatoes and spinach instead. I also increased the OJ, added chipotle powder and a splash of red wine vinegar. The addition of orange juice was inspired. So easy, so filling, so yummy!

Made this with red beans and instead of the meat roasted small piece of cod roasted with some cumin sprinkled and put it on top of the rice bean combination. I keep no OJ in my house but the juice from one small orange was more than enough. This was hit and super easy
I have twin 4-year-olds. Fast weeknight dinners that everybody likes are worth their weight in gold! This is one!

I have a new Zojirushi rice cooker. I love it, but I don't always want "soft" rice. Sometimes I want each grain to be separate and distinct. I just bought some red rice, and just for fun decided to try cooking it on the Quick Cook setting. One cup of red [or brown] rice in two cups of water, 45 minutes cooking, perfect. When it was done I added a can of small white beans. Seasoned all this with herbs and olive oil. YUM!

Before you do anything: cover some thinly sliced red or white onion in cider vinegar, and pinches of salt & sugar. Pickled onions only improve with age!

Add chopped jalapenos after onions cook a little to add lovely spice & crunch.

Also add chipotle powder &/or Spanish sweet paprika
This was delish!  Added Sriracha and topped with advocado. Orange juice is a must!  Easy quick dinner!
Added some avocado as a garnish. Fantastic!
I have ever kind of bean but black in my pantry right now. Has anyone tried pinto? Other options for me to substitute are red kidney, garbanzo, and cannellini.
I used the leftover beans over fried eggs for breakfast
Really wonderful. I added leftover roast lamb and served it with bottled guacamole salsa. Great with quick pickled radishes.
2 cans black beans, 1/2 pkg veggie ground, chipotle powder, 1 can tomato’s. Nice basic recipe to mix things up.
Take it a little further and simmer with some jarred sofrito and green olives! Sounds weird but it’s 10/10!
How many ounces are in the "big can" of beans?  I don't know what "big can" means.
I used two 15.5 cans for two servings and leftovers.  Sometimes we have to go with our "best guess" & it usually works out.

This was the easiest of holiday weekend meals.  I substituted hot dogs for the other meat and threw in a can of Rotel tomatoes.  Served with papaya and a green salad. I’m going very economical with ingredients this year.
Used pineapple juice and added a chipotle in adobo sauce. Rave reviews from the family and super easy!

I made this last night. Delish!   Used Oregano,  cayenne, cumin, bl pepper, garlic, cilantro as in the directions.  Subbed No-Salt Vegetable Broth for the Orange Juice.   The addition of the mandarin oranges and cayenne really perked up the traditional Beans & Rice dinner.  Added a few squirts of lime at the end.  Sooo Good!  No need for the tortillas.   Will be making this again and again.

Had some delicious marinated pork tenderloin left over from making Tacos al pastor. It was a superb addition of the spicy meat chunks to the bean mixture, added extra cumin and a can of diced tomatoes. Yum!
Made a version of this (although will do it Sifton's way) and have always added a fried egg on top.  A little more work and another pan but it it works.

I make a "no-cook" version of this that is great for hot nights, side dishes, or leftovers.  In a bowl add diced/chopped cilantro, onions, serrano chiles, tomatoes, avocado and marinate in lime juice - all to taste.  I do warm/heat up the black beans to soften them, then drain, add some lime juice to the beans and mix them in with the rest of the ingredients.  Serve hot, room temp, cold...pretty versatile.

I've made this a few times and it's always delicious. This time, I added grated zucchini before it spoiled with the onion. It took longer to cook because of the extra moisture but turned out divine. Also, I recommend around 3/4 cups of juice. This time, I used mango-orange juice.
Made as suggested, but threw in about 20 pieces of diced pepperoni with the onion. Adds some nice color as well as flavor. Will serve with roasted butternut squash quesadillas.

I love these prompts. I used a teaspoon of molasses plus water and lime juice as a sub for OJ. Used less onion and added onion powder and chopped green pepper. I rinsed the overly salty canned black beans. Served over brown rice with baked eggplant sliced topped with a shredded Mexican cheese blend and topped with hot sauce. I will make this again.
Do Not skip out on the OJ !!! adds critical flavor
My mom just instructed me to make these beans and bring them over to her house for mother
If you visit South Korea you will get a recipe about rice food.
I’m allergic to oranges. Any substitution suggestions? I’m still looking for a good answer.

I'm thinking maybe fruits that are otherwise good paired with peppers, onions, garlic and such in salsas - like pineapple or mango, but I'd avoid juices or nectars that are sweetened and even if just using the fruit or juice would also up the acid (such as simple, white vinegar) in order to balance it.

I am allergic to lime and lemon and often sub OJ in recipes.  Presumably, one could do the opposite. I usually add juice to our tastes; and generally use more OJ than the amt lemon/lime the recipe indicates.
This was quite good!

Used this a guide, but added Mexican oregano (MUST be Mexican), Serranos, couple chopped tomatoes, chorizo with a splash of lemonade (didn’t have OJ). Topped off cilantro. I love these no-recipe recipes. Give me more.
No meat. Add tsp minced jalapeño, cilantro. Serve with green onion, cilantro, sour cream.
Great.  Used 2 regular cans of beans and 6 ounces ground turkey.  Served with tortillas, rice, cilantro and avocado.  Use more orange (tangelo) juice.  Forgot the lime...
Didn’t have cumin so smoked paprika stepped in. Didn’t have OJ either, but mango juice was great.  Yum!

Sometimes it needs to be a even simpler!  Canned black beans, a little dried onion and cumin, served over freezer microwave rice- add turmeric for color and taste. Drizzle olive oil and balsamic vinegar over this. If you have an avocado, dice it on top.  Fresh tomatoes? Dice a bit. Squeeze with whatever citrus juice you prefer. 10 min meal.

This is a good dish and I've made a variation.  I sometimes add a fried egg  on top if I want another "extra."  The pickled onions from APK sound good. Great addition, Sam Sifton, I like these no-recipe recipes.

"Then add a big can of black beans (drained, please)"
Thanks for tip.
How much water with black beans when coooking 22 min and natural release?

And for those of you who, like me, are either lazy or rice-challenged, replace the cooked rice with a Seeds of Change grain pilaf (7 Grain or Brown Rice and Quinoa are my favorites) which cook beautifully in the microwave in 90 seconds.   Whole Foods and Fresh Direct carry them.  Oh, and you can forego the tortillas and put a fried egg on top as well.

I’m all for this except for the orange juice! In the spirit of open-mindedness I followed the “recipe”, but as I predicted, it was too sweet for me. I wonder if there is something else that could add another dimension of flavor without that flavor being sweet? Thoughts?
This is one of my favorite go to dishes. I have made it numerous times, although now I tend to omit the onion and add a side of sliced avocado. It's easy and delicious.

I followed this recipe just as it is and added beyond meat sausage- taken out of the casings as we are vegetarians. So delicious. Has been requested again for this week. I also did the sheet pan sausage dish with the beyond meat sausage and it was also a big hit.
Thanks for all the great tips.  In our house, adding jalapenos after the onions cook a little is a must!  adds a lovely spice and crunch.  Either with or without seeds depending on your tolerance.
Excellent with kielbassa!

I added to this recipe softened sun dried tomatoes and chipotle in adobo sauce. After all was cooked I threw in some greens to wilt (arugula but spinach would be fine or add in during cooking some kale or swiss chard). Don't forget the lime!
what size can of black beans? How many ounces?
We use the 28-ounce can of Goya beans. It makes enough, with the rice and toppings, for supper for three of us with a scoop leftover for your breakfast quesadilla the next morning. :-)

I ran into problems, but all's verrry well that ends well.  For starters, either I overdid the cumin, or the veggie sausage I tried turned grainy.   I tried to counteract it with more OJ, then olive oil, then I gave up and put a half can of tomato paste in because I knew the paste would bring the elements together.  Of course that meant I had to add water. And plain Goya black beans aren't as soft as the kind that come in Goya soup. So I cooked it for a while. It was yummy.
If I were to add ground beef, how much would you add? And would it already need to be browned? Or would you brown it in the pan, then proceed to add the cumin etc? Thank you!
Threw this together with some smoked jalapeño sausage. Really tasty! I might increase the orange juice next time--I think the citrus flavor kind of disappeared into the dish a little too much.
Yummy! We ate with a pork chop that had a great tomato sauce with onions on it which added a lot. Also used lime on it.
Loved this! Yes to pickled shallot and jalapenos, and warmed tortillas on my gas range.

Super easy and satisfying. I also added a few tomatoes (following a reader’s suggestion). Included the OJ. Served with cilantro and avocado pieces. Sam Sifton’s description of muddy and fragrant is right on!

Loved this - simply delicious. OJ was very nice in the beans. I also added chopped red bell pepper to onion mixture. Pickled red onions were fantastic addition. Our extras also included thinly sliced jalapeños, shredded cheddar, thinly sliced radishes, cilantro leaves, avocado and sour cream. We heated tortillas for scooping but if you want to skip the carbs you won't miss them, serve over fine shredded iceberg or red cabbage or dish up right on the plate.
One thing I like is starting with chunks of thick cut bacon to start and then cooking the onions in the grease. Also scrapping up any burnt bits off the bottom of the pan for more flavour.

“Mom, sometimes the simplest food is, like, the best thing ever.” 
Used chorizo, a couple of oranges off my neighbor’s tree and followed the guide. If it works for my teenaged son, it works for me!
Good with bacon
I wouldn't try this again. I found the taste of OJ overwhelming. There's nothing really special about this dish.

We made this exactly as described and we all loved it! We used 2 links of mild Italian sausage.  Perfection!

I was skeptical skeptical about the OJ but it definitely worked!

Like most recipes (excepting baking), this one points you in a general direction and gets you thinking about different favor combinations (Cumin and OJ for instance). 

I made this with hot italian sausage (had it on hand), added a can of tomatoes (before the OJ). It could be good any number of ways. As Priya Krishna is wont to say: Pick your own adventure. 

In the end, it's just fuel. This was pretty good, but if you made it differently it's still going to be good.
Added link sausage that I cooked down in the pan first. Also added a can of Rotel. Great no-recipe to bring in your own touch and taste.

I used a Walla Walla sweet onion, .5 pound bulk chorizo and 1 can of pinto beans.  Added the juice of a whole orange, just delicious against the spicy sausage.  Served in soft flour tortillas with sour cream and chopped iceberg lettuce to cool some of the heat of the chorizo.  Roasted zucchini on the side.  So easy and so yummy!
Orange juice, chipotle en adobo, picked onions, lamb or beef, Mexe beans, half squashed, cilantro, avocado, feta cheese.
Made as directed and added some pickled red onion thanks to another review.  Delicious!
Perfection.

Don’t omit juice 
Add cilantro stem garlic onion 
Then meat (chicken thigh and sausage) 
Cumin chipotle powder smoked paprika 
OJ
Black beans roasted tomato 

Serve w avocado and chopped salad
The spiced oniony sausage (Italian-style turkey) and bean mixture was just delicious. We found tortillas de trop. Otherwise this was a great way to eat beans and rice.

This was delicious. Stuck pretty much to the suggested items. The orange juice added a sweetness and nice flavor. Threw in a can of diced tomatoes. Served with pickled jalapeños, squirt of lime, splash of Tabasco, and of course chopped cilantro.
If you are like me and have love/hate relationship with cumin, try coriander seed powder, fennel seed powder and/or garam masala.
I usually use 1/4 to 1/2 of the amount of cumin called for.  Same thing with thyme.  Those flavors and smells totally overwhelm me in large amounts.

Sam’s no recipe recipes never fail to please. My 5 year old loved this w/o hot sauce and my husband and I loved it with. Added 1/2 lb of ground hamburger. Used orange juice and loved the contrast of the beef/bean mixture with the hot sauce. Served with lime wedges, cilantro, and Cholula. We will make this again. Pro tip, buy frozen microwaveable rice. Makes this a one pan meal.
needs a little heat!  sprinkle on a little cayenne to add some warmth which will also open your taste buds to the other ingredients.
Throw in a poblano or bell pepper or two with the onion as well!
Ridiculously good.. I added beer w the fresh orange juice and some thyme before.. other than that. So good. Made mine vegetarian to serve with jerk chicken. Could have eaten this as my whole meal.
Made 1cup rice with 1 cup water and 1 cup orange juice. Meat was andouille sausage. Good.

Sauteed half a chopped jalapeno pepper with the onion and garlic, then added two chopped chicken chorizo sausages. After heating those up I added about 2/3 cup OJ, a splash of Rioja, and LOTS of cumin. While the beans were simmering, I stirred in the juice of two limes. To serve, I had fresh chopped cilantro and pico de gallo. Dee-lish! And easy!
Made it! Was good. Might add green peppers next time. Ate it with homemade flour tortillas
Curious about the "drained, please"—in another recipe I use you put the liquid in and then boil it down

I cook rice often, and use Craig Claiborne’s method - easy, always perfect, and simple to clean up after. Preheat the toaster oven on “bake” to 400. Put a scant tablespoon of butter in a small baking pot (mine is a LeCreuset Dutch oven)

I've made this twice now! The OJ adds a lot of flavor. Most recently I chopped up some leftover pork tenderloin and added to the recipe for additional protein, and it worked great! Next time I may try to really caramelize the onion to add more natural sweetness. Goes really well with carnitas (and rice of course).
This was delicious!  I did add chopped green pepper while cooking and fresh corn at the end.  I’ll make this again!

title: Grilled Cheese With Jalapeño, Tomato and Fried Egg Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: My family never used butter.   Cut the fat and taste the cheese and jalapenos:  lay an even number of bread slices in broiler, topped with a few slices of cheese and the additions.  Broil till melted and maybe a touch bubbly and burnt, checking often.  Then flip slices together and broil till top is toasted, then flip and toast bottoms.  Crisp, easy, can be done for four or more sandwiches at once, and no butter.  NB: I love butter, but prefer to enjoy it in essentials, like hollandaise

OMG!
I was just going to write this recipe this way; then read yours!  My mom used to make them this way (I’m going back to the 60’s, here) and called them toasted cheese!
She put slices of bread, no butter, on aluminum foil in the broiler and put a slice of cheese and a slice of tomato on top of each;  broiled until the tomato melted into the cheese and the cheese melted into the bread (a few minutes) and ‘presto! Enough sandwiches for the family!  I think we ate them open-faced.

It’s been a long tine since I had these and will try them again with better bread (whole wheat, not white) and maybe some herbs!
Thank you!
BTW, adding an egg, a la a Croque Monsieur, is a brilliant idea.
Oh my Gosh, this was the best grilled cheese I ever had!  Made it exactly as written.
I love grilled cheese best when cooked on a cast iron skillet.
Looks delicious but how do you eat a sandwich with a fried egg sitting on top of the sandwich?

So we weren't the only family who did them in the broiler :)  And yes, the aluminum foil is essential because the cheese sometimes drips out.  If you make them with rye bread, the cheese will definitely run out, so use less cheese - it's so good on rye...

Neighbor kids loved them our way and pestered their mother's to make them the way we did.  When you can feed four children all at once, it is a lifesaver.
I'd add some avocado slices to this!  Love these "no recipe" recipes!

Would skip the pepper. Tried it and face was on fire and couldn’t taste much after that. Also I made this in a sandwich maker and topped with the egg fried separately with salt pepper and tasted great

I love a fancy grilled cheese, so I expected this to be good. But wow, this exceeded expectations. Ford Farm Coastal Cheddar and Kerrygold Dubliner Cheddar, tomato and sliced jalapeños in between layers of cheese on sourdough: a new favorite.
Pam, OMG! Your comment brought back a memory from over 65 years ago. My mother made the same toasted cheese and tomato but with CHEESE WHIZ! Thanks for the memories!!

If you replace the jalapeno with a bit of the "sauce" part of a can of Chipotles in Adobo sauce, you can spread it (thinly!) over the whole piece of bread to get more uniform spiciness that has a richer flavor profile than the jalapeno.
Hey Sam. Just wanted to make a moment to say I enjoy your writing as much as your recipes/non-recipes.
Sam, I do too!
@John F. Totally agree! Will follow the recipe and the protocol of walking Ziggy then bed! Thank you!!!

Thanks Sam. It’s my son Joe’s 17th birthday today. There is a pandemic. My larder is a bit slim. I’m trying to keep my business running from home. People are stressed. All I could think of was the comfort of my Mom’s sloppy joes, but I’m tired. You saved me. Sloppy joes, biscuits and a green salad.  Voila!

It’s beautiful. I could cry. 

Thank you.
@Tavis Dockwiller just hopped in to say your writing is beautiful.
You can also substitute lentils instead of ground beef
Or canned black beans
Yum! We use riced cauliflower!

I used ground turkey.  Even though it’s ground from thigh and skin, there was almost no fat. I liked that the dish wasn’t too sweet and am glad Sam used Worcester sauce instead of ketchup.  Also, we’re wimps, so I omitted all the hot stuff.  Aren’t we lucky to have the NYTIMES?
Yes we are!
I would cook the meat separately so I could drain the fat. Then add the already cooked vegetables, tomatoes and seasoning.
good idea;  the amount of grease for 1  1/2 lbs of 80/20 ground beef would be alot.
A dash of ketchup or bottled barbecue sauce wouldn't hurt, adding a bit more sweetness than the Worcestershire sauce alone can provide.
A favorite. I add a smoky kick with a generous spoon of dried chipotle, and add sweetness and depth with a pour of Port to boil off while it thickens.
I use ground turkey and while including some of the other items mentioned, I also add apple cider vinegar, brown sugar and honey! Yum!
I grew up eating sloppy joes with a sweet taste. Glad to see your additions.
Acid & a bit of sweetness is a must in sloppy joe!

If you want a recipe, look at Marion Burros "healthy" sloppy joes on this site. She prescribes mustard and a touch of molasses along with tomatoes in various forms and Worcestershire. It's delicious and much like what you remember from childhood. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/4203-sloppy-joes
I agree with you on Marion Burros’ recipe - delicious and a big favorite of our family....
I enjoy serving this on warmed corn tortillas and a sprinkling of grated cheddar.

I often base what I will make for dinner on what I have on hand that I need to use—frozen ground beef and frozen pretzel buns, a half cup of homemade bbq sauce, with fig balsamic and Siracha replacing suggested Worcestershire and hot sauce. I cooked it twice as long as Sam suggested, so I would have time to fix anything that was off, and also to thicken it as I had no tomato paste. I made this tonight for March Madness and am sure it is why my team eked out a win. Fabulous.
Using the word "glug" repeatedly does not make it a no-recipe recipe.
But it does make it fun to read.

I gotta tell y'all. A Sloppy Joe recipe without brown sugar and ketchup is just not going to be a Sloppy Joe. I found the pureed tomatoes plus the tomato paste to be too tomato forward, and I am tomatically inclined. I love a no recipe recipe, but this is not the flavor that I was looking for.
Roast a few cumin seeds, mortar and pestle, and hi it with that. Adds a great savory note.

The sloppy Joe recipe your Mom served in the knotty-pined basement "rec" room when hosting the cheerleaders. Now I use plant based burger.  
Sloppy Joe
2lbs ground beef
1 onion, chopped
2 tbls vinegar
2 tbls white sugar
1 bottle catsup (12-14 oz)
1 tbls. Worchestshire
1 tsp dry mustard
2 tbls lemon juice
1/2 green pepper chopped
Sauté the onion til translucent  Add  the green pepper, sauce  a bit longer.   Brown the ground beef with the added sugar. 
Add remaining ingredients and cook 30 -35
This beats my mother's recipe for "BBQ Beef:" hamburger and Campbell's tomato soup.
Your mother must have gone to the same cooking school as mine. She never met a can of Campbell’s she couldn’t repurpose.
In my house growing up these were known as Messy Missys. I’m sure I have no idea why. Except for the title these are spot on as I remember them.
Me, I would drain the fat before proceeding to cut down on greasiness.

This recipe has a more complex flavor than the one I've made all my life that uses catsup and mustard, etc. I like this recipe better, perhaps because I'm not a kid anymore and my adult palate is more developed. It isn't as sweet, but still has a bit of sweetness. I pulsed Cento San Marzano Whole Tomatoes to a small-chunk texture. Delicious. It takes a bit longer to put together but is worth the effort.
Brown and drain the ground beef remove from pan. saute veggies. add tomato paste. return beef to skillet. cuts the grease dramatically

Hey all,I just want to say how fab it has been to use this amazing app over the last year!im in NZ and whilst we had a short & very sharp entire country lockdown last year the ripple down effect has been immense.
We import so much and yet gave away the ability to produce here in our own country.
Folk are getting used to living a more simple life.
For me these recipes are like having a surprise every day!
Today I will preserve more tomatoes,Beets & sweet peppers..so grateful for this app X:)

Welcome aboard, and great to have you here :-)
May you have many happy hours using the NY Times cooking app,
and taking joy from the simpler things in life. 

All the best to you-stay safe!

Any way you make your sloppy joes, they are equally delicious spooned over a freshly roasted sweet potato. Top with shredded cheese, chopped scallions, even a little dollop of sour cream. Best way to roast sweet potatoes (quick) while you make your sloppy joes - cut scrubbed potatoes in half, slather cut side with olive oil, salt and pepper. Roast cut side down on non stick pan at about 400 till soft, gooey and caramelized. Much better than that ole bun.
Good - but could use more zip next time.  Plan to add more tomato paste and hot sauce to my version.
Made it with ground lamb. A delish step up.
Has anyone ever made this with ground chicken?
What a brilliant idea, thank you!
I add 1/4 cup sweet pickle relish. Don’t knock it until you have tried it.
2lbs lean ground turkey, chopped tomatoes and tomato sauce, over sourdough with a bit of grated cheddar. Absolutely great.
Very good, very comforting. Also forgiving, you can taste as you go and adjust, add, whatever. I used a generous amount of gochujang since I was low on tomato paste and would absolutely recommend.

Cooked the beef and then removed it and sauteed the veggies in the fat from the meat.  No other oils.  Returned the beef to pan and began experimenting with with tomato paste, tomato sauce, catsup and S&P until I was satisfied with then flavors.  Definitely serve on hot dog buns.

I had my first sloppy joe as a child when my mother took me to Rike’s Department store in Dayton, Ohio, early 50’s.  Prominent in their version was French’s mustard, along with onion, green pepper, catsup and chili sauce.  It is my standard to this day.  Nobody else mentioned mustard.
"Walk for the dog and bed".  Thank you for the smile.  I needed that.

Made a healthy version, and still very tasty: 96/4 ground turkey + lettuce wraps. Paired with a vinegar-based cole slaw. Looking forward to making again in the summer and pairing with fresh corn on the cob.

For those that love this recipe but need a shopping list: 
Olive oil
- 1 small onion, diced
- 2 celery ribs, diced
- 1 jalapeño, diced 
- 1 red pepper, diced 
- 3 cloves garlic, minced 
- 1/2 lb ground beef (80/20)
- 3-4tbsp tomato paste (or ketchup) 
- 1 cup canned tomatoes, pureed (or leftover pasta sauce) 
- Worcestershire sauce 
- Vinegary hot sauce
- Buns
@Bex *1 + 1/2 lb ground beef ;)

I had to eliminate some of the ingredients for dietary reasons, but these were delicious. However, instead of a list of ingredients to follow, I found it more difficult finding them in the non-recipe recipe. Having looked up "glug" and "knob" the other day just to know the actual measurements of these words, I am much happier. I know there was no "knob" in this recipe.
I like glug and knob.  Perfect descriptions!
Added some leftover Memphis bbq sauce in the fridge, and I’m glad I did!
Liked it!  I felt like I needed extra Worchestershire sauce than described for taste.  Easy and good family dinner meal!

I’m used to sweeter sloppy joes from my childhood so I added three tablespoons of brown sugar and about two of Dijon mustard and it was delicious. Sprinkled with cheddar cheese and served with hamburger dill pickles on sesame seed buns, toasted.

Made this and added smoked paprika and some tamari sauce to the mix. My daughter decided it was missing a flavor layer, she asked to add some cinnamon. Turned out she added a Chinese 5-spice. You should try this! Very, very tasty.

Also, I used ketchup instead of paste.

I love seeing everyone's additions. I added smoked paprika and skipped the jalapenos (just wasnt in the mood for spicy), and mixed in some chopped frozen spinach to get my greens in. Turned out great!
I like to rotate this "recipe" in once a month, but I use Vegetarian Crumbles. The tomato base hides it well and it's an easy Vegetarian dinner!
Made this with green lentils and generous glugs of red Cholula. Such a great hearty meatless Monday

use ketchup instead of pureed tomatoes which imparts a sweeter flavor: 1.5 lbs of hamburger, one chopped sweet onion, 1 cup of ketchup (or more to your taste), 2 T. yellow mustard, 1 T. of Worcestershire sauce, 2 T. vinegar, 1 T. brown sugar, 1 T. celery seed. Brown meat and onion, drain fat, add the rest of the ingredients and simmer.

These were so. So. Good. We even made the broccoli because Sam said to and it was just perfect. I ran out of Worcestershire sauce and ended up making my own - wow. Doing that from now on! Thank you Sam, for making quarantine not so terrible.
I made a vegetarian version using steamed green cabbage chopped up into half inch pieces and it was delicious.
I love a Sam Sifton no-recipe recipe. I haven’t had a sloppy joe since I was a kid, and these scratched an itch I haven’t felt for a long time. So, SO delicious.
It is SO wonderful to see sloppy joe's without ketchup and spicy not sweet. Thank you!
I added a few tablespoons of brown sugar and a teaspoon of regular mustard. This is the second time I have made these and this version is amazing.
This was delicious! I was not excited about the ground turkey I had thawed- but this non-recipe turned it into a delicious dinner! Thank you Sam!

Oh. Should have done as I usually do and look at notes. I think a little mustard might have done the trick. Could’ve done a few more glugs of Worcester maybe? Like the idea of a wee bit of molasses. This is gonna be good next time!
Oh. And. Fake ground beef. Cuz trying to imitate mama in a veggie world.  Although mama would totally have been down with black beans.

This is such a simple, but wonderful recipe. 
My husband (who is not originally from the US) had no idea what in the world a "Sloppy Joe" was. After using this wonderful recipe (exactly as is), these have quickly become a favorite at our house..Thank you, Sam. Your recipes and your writing never disappoint:-)

Making these tonight.  Looking through the comments and the one from Tavis kind of got to me.  It’s almost a year later, pandemic still in full force and I’m hoping that she’s hanging in there.  I bet she could use some sloppy joes again right about now.  Hang in there Tavis.  This too shall pass.
my grandmother made these with a can of tomato soup, brown sugar and mustard.  love the memories💕
couple tblspns brown sugar

I made this last night, following Sam’s no recipe recipe. Having added tons of “glugs”, it was still wanting. I then added a small amount of Dijon mustard, maybe a Tbsp of dark brown sugar, and some good chili powder. Covered the pot, and cooked on low around thirty minutes. Result was creamy, spicy, delicious.

The no-recipe recipes are among my favorites, because I like to experiment and I rarely make something the same way twice.  I made this one last night.  I used ground turkey and lots of garlic.  I wasn't at all sure how much Worcesteshire we'd want, but I kept glugging!  I was appreciative that the result was not sweet.  Oh, and I think i used at least 1 1/2 jalapenos and lots of hot sauce.  Very yummy comfort foot!  Keep these no-recipe recipes coming!
Made this up basically as written. Used turkey meat. Added anchovies paste and Dijon for depth. Cayenne pepper, chili powder, sweet paprika

Made up basically as written. Added anchovy paste and Dijon for depth. Cayenne pepper, chili powder, and sweet paprika for spices. Very yummy on either a sweet bun or lettuce wrap. Made my fiancé happy to have a childhood favorite for dinner (though slightly elevated from the Manwich he grew up with).
I just followed the recipe in the notes provided by Dee K and it was perfection. A total hit and its now my go to forevermore. Thank you Dee!

The potato bun is the right call here I like the bun squishy though. I cut up a good watermelon, put a jar of dill pickles on the table and some Fritos scoops. I used 2 lbs of ground beef, one lean and one 80/20. Followed all of Sam’s other advice, but I used only a small amount of jalapeño. My 6 year old ate everything on the plate including the crumbs. My husband had seconds. We will make again.
Needs a dollop of brown sugar, otherwise excellent.
Great recipe but it lacked the sweetness I’m used to. If it’s something you miss too a bit of brown sugar or molasses will do the trick
I add black beans to up the protein even more!
Add brown sugar for sweetness, a little salt, and definitely double (at least) the tomato paste and puree. I added Sriracha which gave it a nice boost of flavor and spiciness level.

Added tbsp dark brown sugar
3-4 tbsp Worcestershire

Glug for glug this recipe beats hands down any other dinner idea I could have come up with on my own after a long week of travel. I used a puréed can of original rotel in place of “tomatoes”, and a glug of baby rays bbq because sugary bbq sauce solves a lot of problems in my house. I added celery leaves with the meat because this non-recipe didn’t have instructions   I forgot about red peppers with my adhd until after I started cooking but didn’t have any anyway. A-total-win-4-me-2-day :)

Chili sauce instead of canned tomatoes/and/or ketchup

Added about a teaspoon of garlic chili sauce instead of jalapeño and hot sauce
I will make any recipe that calls for "quite a few glugs." Of anything.
I want to live in Sam Sifton's world. Made this tonight, easiest recipe ever. One exception was a red onion, it was the only one we had.
My go-to accompaniment to sloppy joes is mashed potatoes and buttery fresh corn sliced off the cob.

Delicious.  **Not sweet at all,** and we really appreciate having a non-sweet option for such a great comfort food dish.  Thank you Sam!! 
We used 1 lb of beef and needed most of a can of crushed tomatoes to get desired consistency.  A little squirt of mustard and a few slices of pickle were nice on the bun.  Maybe coleslaw next time.  Took Sam's advice and steamed some broccoli which was a great addition.

It’s delicious.  And…
Please just list the ingredients so we can add them to our grocery cart. Please.
Very good. A little greasy, may want to drain the beef fat after cook. Add seasoning like you would to normal ground beef. Lots of Worcester is good here.

You mentioned that, "my partner is always happy about sloppy Joes, including when I use ground turkey, crumbled tempeh or red lentils instead of beef." Could you tell us more about how you change the recipe for the  bean option?
I add a splash of dill pickle juice at the end… tangy.

I stick pretty close on this one except for using 85/15 chuck and draining some grease off.  Plus whatever I'm feeling like that day, maybe mustard, maybe ketchup, maybe celery seed.  We all know how to add sweetness to this if we want it to taste like that recipe Mom clipped from Better Homes & Gardens back in the day.  While I liked that sort of food when I was a kid, I prefer to remember her in other ways!
Was going to make burgers, but then saw this no recipe recipe. Super easy. Served with french fries to mop up any sauce on the plate.

If someone could bold the ingredients so we could see them better while shopping that would be wonderful! I seem to always miss the tomato puree, so we have to find ways to improvise. But I realized that adding a “note” with the groceries listed is the way to go. Never miss anything now! Here they are, in case it’s helpful. 

2.5 pounds 80% beef
Purée tomatoes
Tomato paste
Worcestershire sauce 
Jalapeño
Red pepper
Celery
Onion
Garlic
Potato buns
@holly that’s 1.5 lbs beef
Looks a lot like a recipe to me!
I have an easier way! Leftover taco meat and some ketchup. Try it!
My mother used to make Sloppy Joes fairly often in the 50s and 60s. It had a sweetness to it but I don’t recall how she did it. I should have paid better attention! What do you suggest?

A recipe from my childhood (1960s) used ketchup instead of pureed tomatoes which imparts a sweeter flavor: 1.5 lbs of hamburger, one chopped sweet onion, 1 cup of ketchup (or more to your taste), 2 T. yellow mustard, 1 T. of Worcestershire sauce, 2 T. vinegar, 1 T. brown sugar, 1 T. celery seed. Brown meat and onion, drain fat, add the rest of the ingredients and simmer.
a tablespoon or two of light brown sugar

The versions from my ‘50s midwestern youth always used a TB or two of brown sugar along with catsup. That gets cloying fast.  I use honey in smaller quantities and it still imparts the touch of sweetness we remember.
What is a “glug“ of olive oil
a large tablespoon
2 dashes or half a smidgeon
Enough to coat the bottom of the pan.
I made the no recipe as suggested. Eyeballing ingredients, tasting as I went and it was perfect. I used a brioche bun and served with sweet pots and corn. All the starches!
Served this on super soft and yeasty homemade buns with celery leaves and a smear of mayo. SO GOOD.
Ive made this with ground turkey also.  You might up some of the spices to taste.  Always yummy.
2 lbs beef, whole can of paste, whole can of crushed tomato,

I followed the recipe, though doubling the ingredients for my large family. When tasting it before serving, I felt it needed some barbeque sauce. It was just the ticket--added a little sweet (but not too much) and some smokiness to the mix. Yum!

I used lean beef, just added a bit more oil.  For the puréed canned tomatoes, I used San Marzano passata.  
Easy and oh so good.

My new recipe for Sloppy Joes ... Thanks

Just made this with a few alterations. I didn't want to use up my few cans of tomatoes (saving those for pasta) but I had some ketchup and chili sauce in the fridge from olden days when I made a shrimp cocktail . . . anyway, it was fabulous replacing the tomatoes with that. And no red bell pepper so I just left it out. Comfort food for the covid19 house bound these days.
Delicious! Made it vegan by substituting beyond ground beef. Otherwise, no subs and enjoyed it a lot!

My favorite sloppy joe recipe is much simpler. Brown ground beef and onion, add cup of bread crumbs (actually tear up a couple pieces of bread in small bits). Mix cup of ketchup, 1/4 cup of sugar and 2 T of Worcestershire sauce together.  Add salt and pepper and mix into meat mixture.  Adjust sugar to taste (I like it sweet and tangy.) Simmer a bit. Done.
