Soooo good my kids and grandkids ask for it regularly. Got the recipe from an Amish lady in Indiana back in the 70s

I made sloppy joes as a kid with chopped onion, garlic, hamburger, ketchup, a little water and Campbell’s chicken gumbo soup.  Recipe from soup can. It was delicious on toasted buns with melted cheddar cheese.
I added a healthy pinch of salt and some ground black pepper. The dish was kinda bland without it.

Since this is a non recipe recipe, I feel no guilt over my edits. Added gochujang (1 tbsp) with the tomato paste. I added both once the veggies were soft and cooked a little until they darkened. I deglazed that with apple cider vinegar. I also used 2 tbsp of brown sugar because sloppy joes that aren't a little sweet make no sense to me.
It's too oily.  Pour off some of the fat after browning the beef. Otherwise great.

[ketchup instead of pureed tomatoes] 1.5 lbs of hamburger, one chopped sweet onion, 1 cup of ketchup (or more to your taste), 2 T. yellow mustard, 1 T. of Worcestershire, 2 T. vinegar, 1 T. brown sugar, 1 T. celery seed. Brown meat and onion, drain fat, add the rest of the ingredients and simmer.

and stir and sizzle until it is well browned, about 10 minutes. Bring the heat down a bit add tomato paste — say 3 tablespoons, or 4 — and let it get toasty before adding 1 cup+ Puréed Canned tomatoes

Ron and Mark -- hamburger and Campbell's tomato soup was how I was taught sloppy Joe's in 6th grade Home Ec in the very early 1960s. I've never tried the recipe that way since I was a child, although I have thought about it.
How many do we think 1.5 pounds of this will serve? Enough for 6?
Added some green chile for a kick. So good!

Substituted ground lamb for beef and jarred roasted red peppers for fresh. In true Northern fashion (I support referring to the "upper Midwest" as the "North" because its less pejorative and more geographically accurate) we added a slice of melted American cheese to the toasted bun. It was a hearty dinner perfect for cold winter nights.

So good! I used a pasilla pepper instead of red bell, added spinach as it cooked to make it a little healthy, drained some fat, and added a little anchovy paste like someone suggested. I added a good amount of Worcestershire, as I like less sweet and more savory. Had it with white cheddar on a potato bun. Way better than the cheap stuff I ate as a kid.

Made with a mixture of Beyond Meat grounds and FieldRoast Chipotle vegan sausage - so sautéed the onions first, then added the “meat”, and last, all the other ingredients. The pinch of cloves really makes a difference. Really a simple, tasty meal.
ketchup definitely for that traditional flavor -the cloves and sweetness tilt it toward a non-spicy BBQ  savor.

Chili sauce is the essential tomato element, in my opinion (formed in childhood, of course) . . . plus onion, garlic, Worcestershire, and served open-faced so blobs of it don't end up on the table or my lap. Soft brioche rolls are nice, but that's a recent addition.
Used a pound of Impossible Meat and added a few shakes of liquid smoke. Divine.
Awesome

This is very good as-is, but I like to add a lot of ketchup and plain yellow mustard and a little sugar to make it more like my childhood favorite. Excellent stuffed into halved, baked peppers as an alternate to sandwiches.

YUMMM!!!
But...
In our house, it ain’t a sloppy joe without ketchup, a dash of cider vinegar and spoonful of brown sugar!!
Subbed this with impossible meat to make vegetarian and topped with banana peppers. Makes for a delicious summer dish

Enjoyed this, quite tasty - our modifications were:

0. Omitted the celery, because I forgot
1. Used 1.5lb of fresh ground brisket, most of the thick fat cap removed
2. 14.5 ounce, can of san marzano tomatoes
3. Some good splashes of Tapatiio for the hot sauce
4. Added some water after simmering for a bit, to get it a bit more "sloppy"
5. Added a good shake of onion and garlic powder, as well as medium hot chili powder and cayenne pepper

Add more tomatoes to make it sloppier, next time

Thank you Sam.  Your no-recipe brought back memories of raising my children  and serving this their favorite dish in the USA. Now I have the privilege of living on the island of Sicily and making this dish using ingredients available in this part of the world. Today I introduced the dish to  my ex-pat and national  family members who had the local baker make the perfect bread to go with their new go-to dish. All family member, young and old loved it.

Used finely chopped chicken instead of beef. Added carrots in with the vegetables..reminded me of a pasta sauce. Didn’t have canned tomatoes so I used a couple table spoons of ketchup and water to make it saucy. I also added a tablespoon or so of molasses. This gave a deep, sort of sweet taste that I knew as sloppy joes growing up.

It is finally getting a bit cold in the Houston area. For some reason cold weather means sloppy joe time, but as a vegetarian I've not had that in a while. Cooked this tonight with a package of impossible ground "meat". This satisfied the urge and was actually better than the sloppy joes from a sauce packet that I grew up with.
I have loved Sloppy Joes since 1st Grade at Memorial School in East Brunswick, NJ. Still the all time best. These are close, thanks Sam.
"a walk for the dog and bed". Always a pure bliss to read your recipes. And a pure bliss to devour them.
A side of broccoli?

What I love about this is you can just “use up” whatever you have on hand - the whole can of tomato paste, the whole can of puréed tomatoes, “glugs” of Worcestershire to taste. The only addition was a couple of glugs of my cheap Friday night red wine…let it simmer until everyone got home for dinner… and it was a hit across-the-board!

As this recipe is meant to be improvised upon by the cook, I really can't complain. I like the addition of celery, because I am always okay with hiding an extra vegetable. However, a sloppy joe should have a bit of sweetness. I added a bit of ketchup and about a tablespoon or so of brown sugar. Also a squeeze of yellow mustard. Otherwise, a solid no-recipe recipe.

I have always made sloppy joes without a recipe but my latest batch was especially good.  Made with equal amounts of ground beef, ground turkey and pork sausage.  I added sauteed  onions, celery, garlic and bell pepper, some leftover BBQ sauce, ketchup and a can of condensed tomato soup. Seasoned the mix with chili powder, cumin, Slap Your Mama,  Siracha, and red wine vinegar.  Serve topped  with thin slices of sharp cheddar cheese and a few potato chips.

Made this with fresh cherry tomatoes, chopped into quarters, but otherwise followed the instructions except cut it in half.  Really easy and quick. Left out the hot sauce but used red pepper. Easy Sunday meal.
I made this with Beyond Meat (vegan alternative-protein). It made for the best sloppy joe. Big day for this Medwesterner.

Sloppy joes are fun to make especially if you improvise! Tonight we used red onions, finely chopped guajillos and anchos, a little molasses, Worcestershire, chili powder, and some dry mustard and it really tuned out good.  Can’t wait to do it again :)

Great base recipe that you can tweak to your tastes. 

I added some nduja instead of the jalapeño and some smoked paprika to bring out some depth. Could also use cumin.
Liquid smoke is a good addition

This was very good and last time I added dried mustard, which helped. Tonight, i put a little yellow mustard on the finished portion and it was great. I always about double the tomato paste, it adds a lot.
Great weeknight stand by.

Delicious! It won over my wife who only remembers sloppy joes as a too-sweet school food from her childhood. She does not have a sweet tooth. She scarfed it down. I made it pretty much as written but added some things, some I saw suggested and some not. Added paprika and mustard to the filling, topped it with dill pickle slices and avocado slices, then a bun onto which cheese had been broiled. I would do it exactly this way again.
Really Sam? Steamed broccoli? Fries and cole slaw says me, but that's a small quibble with a great non-recipe. thx

I made as written, but used ground turkey instead of beef and served on a brioche bun. It was absolutely delicious! It made a nice amount, too, so we enjoyed it for leftovers a couple of times. A nice addition to our weeknight repertoire!
Cut salt by 1/3
Why does everyone write reviews with their own recipe - that’s not what this is for

This is the method I use for everything except for baking. I check several recipes, then mine becomes a synthesis of variables from different sources, and a lot of "me!"
When others ask for the recipe or for me to make it again, I tell them that I've no idea......
Would love to see more like this in the future.
Added brown sugar to taste…as is, way too tomato-y…absolutely needed some sweet…also used DrPragers fake meat…served w/side of slaw & slicked pickles…righteous. Love Sam’s non-recipes!

This week week while caring for a new mom who is vegan I made it with lentils. It was wonderful. 

These exceptionally adaptable no recipe recipes are fantastic.
Excellent. I also left out orange peel as per comments. Served with carrot sticks for a truly retro dinner.
My family and I are eating this right now. It’s delicious! I had to do without the red bell peppers. Great weeknight meal!
This is extremely good with ground chicken, which I would never normally recommend. Insanely good.

Great stuff! Tastes like the sloppy joes of childhood! I added a little brown sugar, bbq sauce, apple cider vinegar, ketchup, and swapped carrots for the red pepper. Added water and then simmered for a good long while! My kids loved it.

1 cup of tomato sauce would not be enough, I used a large tin to up the slop factor and was heavy handed with the Worcestershire. But it was actually very good and so much easier than I would have thought. It was a big hit as well.
Made this for two young kiddos so swapped out the spicy elements and added a sprinkle of sugar to counter the metallic bite of the tomatoes. My eldest is now a big fan!

Didn’t have puréed canned tomatoes, so used diced - and the fire-roasted ones.  Left out the jalapeño and hot sauce but added a few dollops of Calabrian chili crisp at the same time as the tomato paste…
Love this!  Exactly how my grandma Ella taught me how to cook ❤️

Love this!  Reading the text was like standing in the kitchen with my Grandma Ella as she taught me to cook.  Ground elk, yellow pepper, red onion, served on Brioche slider buns w/a side of Cole slaw & black pepper potato chips 😎
I added tomato sauce as it needed more moisture, skipped the jalapeño and let people add their own hot sauce.

Cooked today. Went on the family’s repeat list. Only substitution was canned diced tomatoes rather than puréed. Just a what we had in the pantry decision. See the comments about cooking meat separately and then adding to the already prepared vegetables, think it could help with draining off excess fat and liquids, but given the results this evening not sure worth adding an additional pot to clean. This is a great one dish option.

I like a recipe that is not a recipe, because this is how I cook most of the time. I used 2lbs of meat, and one 14 oz can of tomato sauce, and it was just the right amount of sloppy. Also added about a tablespoon of brown sugar. Great stuff!

Thank you for the sides note about broccoli - great vegetable balance!  Made this last night for the Taylor Bowl, really enjoyed it. Glad this was not a recipe, as that means that when I dumped the hamburger in after the tomatoes it still worked out; the meat broke up gratefully easily.

Holy smokes! What a great way to stretch your culinary skills by making this a “non-recipe”. I added this, I added that and before I knew it, BOOM! Sloppy Joe! The easiest dinner I’ve ever made and the family chowed down like I was trying to steal their food while they were eating. I’m never buying canned again.

I love this no-recipe method and result.  Made many times with all the no recipe
type variations you could imagine...whatever is handy.   THANK YOU!!
Not sure what those are, but they ain't sloppy joes . . . More like spaghetti. Just add pasta ... toast the buns, rub with garlic. Sprinkle everything with Parm ... LOL ...
When I was a kid we made it simply with browned ground beef and a bottle of chili sauce…the sweet, ketchup-like stuff, not spicy.

We add a generous tablespoon of Woody’s Barbecue Cooking Sauce to give it a lift and at times just use a taco seasoning mix to make taco sloppy joe burgers. Definitely the chopped onion, one medium onion to a pound of meat or whatever. Some grated cheddar is usually good and served with cole slaw.
First time making a sloppy Joe and loved it. So so good!!
I can’t explain why except that my mother did it and I like it like that - I use a coarsely chopped  green pepper. Maybe the bitterness of the pepper contrasts well with the sweetness of the sauce.
Very good takes longer than it says to make
Excellent recipe that allows for modifications if needed. I used ground venison but otherwise cooked as written. Super flavorful and not a lot of work
For those who want a touch of sweet...we topped with bread and butter pickles.
Good flavor but way too greasy for our tastes. Might try again with 90/10 vs the recipes recommendation for 80/20.

Impressively easy recipe that is blessedly brainless and wonderful to make. I ended up with a mix that reminded me of what my mom used to fix from the good ol Manwhich can, but more to my tastes now.

I omitted the pureed tomatoes and substituted about ¼ cup water with a pinch of chicken bouillon, and threw in a couple of pinches of brown sugar and parsley. I ended up serving it over a nice pile of orzo because I didn't realize I was lacking bread (this was a really last-minute dinner idea), but it still worked really, really well.
Adding some smoked paprika and ground ancho chile help round out the flavor
Even more delicious the next day
This turned out amazing. I didn’t haven’t celery, so we used celery seed and some celery salt and wow, it worked out so well. This reheats beautifully.

I make these all the time and just use whatever peppers I have on hand (often poblanos and jalapeños, but it’s been habanero a time or two, and tonight, I used Fresnos). But tonight, when I went to grab my tube of tomato paste from the fridge, I discovered I was all out (SACRE CERISE!) So I used harissa paste instead and hooooooooo boy, was it good!!

Thanks for this. I experiment a lot with my sloppy Joe recipes, but one thing is for sure: NO KETCHUP ALLOWED. adding ketchup makes it way too sweet and cloying. I do almost always add yellow hot dog mustard to mine, and then squirt more mustard on top when serving.

Tloved this recipe!! I took liberties and substituted a couple of the tablespoons of tomato sauce with chili sauce and added about a tablespoon of light brown sugar ! I also didn't have a can of tomato puree so l roasted a few vine tomatoes in the oven, peeled skins and threw em in the pot. It was amazing! Loved the "no-recipe" recipe concept !
This looks great. I’ll add a pinch of horseradish near the end for a little kick.
Excellent. Followed the no-recipe as written. I’ve made it a few times now and appreciate no ketchup. I’m calling this sloppy Sam’s, no offense to the author who I suspect is very tidy ;)

Really out of my comfort zone to just “make it up” LOL, but this was delicious. Made enough for two hungry people on a really cold night.
2 slices bacon
Medium onion
10 baby red potatoes
2 carrots
Kosher salt and black pepper
Smoked paprika (which I think made a HUGE difference)
1 C frozen corn
4 C fish stock
.5 C white wine
.25 C heavy cream
8 oz cod
Don't cut the raw fish into chunks. Immerse the entire fillets in the liquid; they'll break up naturally as soon as they're cooked through.
try with coconut milk (full fat) really, really amazing...with some crushed lemongrass...
Every time I try to use coconut milk in soup, I end up making Tom Ka Gai. Must be a one track tongue.
Amazing & yes speedy! Started with jar of anchovies & its oil, garlic w/the other stated ingredients, bottle of clam juice & water. Perfect for this sloshy night in NY.

Excited that this turned out so well as I don’t cook w/ fish much. Started by dissolving 2 anchovies into olive oil & butter. Added onion, carrot, celery, potato, red pepper flake, smoked paprika & blk pepper. For liquid, used Pinot Grigio, chicken broth & clam juice. Thickened w/ half & half. Added .75lb tilapia & canned minced clams. Rough chopped 15 pre-cooked shrimp into bowls before ladling in stew. Voila! Fish stew. Served with garlic toast. Fam loved it. Does into reg rotation. Thx, Sam!
That's a pretty good recipe for someone who says she doesn't cook fish much!  Next time, throw the uncooked shrimp in at the last minute.
I've made this for years and love to throw in a good lot of chopped parsley at the end along with lots of black pepper. Cod is good.

Wow am I a chef? Used cod + bacon + leeks + carrots + potatoes + veg stock  + clam juice + paprika + slices of hot pepper + a few dashes of Worcestershire for depth. I cant wait to make this again and impress my bf whos not my bf
I’ve got the same kind of bd (wink)
For yellow color as shown in photo, add 1 t turmeric. Instant yellow magic plus hint of added flavor.
@pix The smoked paprika adds good color.

2 slices bacon


Olive oil
Onion
Carrot
Celery
Potato
Corn 1 cup
Thyme stems
Bay leaf
Pimenton
Pepper

Clam juice, water, white wine. 1:1:1

1/2 cup heavy cream

Cod
White fish

Chopped parsley

I started off without a recipe for my version of fish chowder. Main difference: I add cooking sherry to the onions and bacon (yes, I'm reckless), no carrots in mine, and I add the potatoes once I've got a broth going. Otherwise, pretty much the same dish ... easy and oh-so-tasty. I splurge though and use pricey shell fish in addition to cod or halibut. It's perfect for a chilly morning, noon, or night :)
A bottle of clam juice mixed in with water and white wine works great.

Made this using white miso for broth (my go-to for any broth/bouillon) , added some red pepper flakes, and dressed it at the end with cilantro and lime wedges.  Used good quality frozen cod fillets.  Fantastic.  Will add the 'basic' chowder to our regular meals!
Chicken broth. I add thyme and sage for true New England chowder.
I would leave out the carrots. that would introduce a flavor I don't want.
If you poach the fish first, remove the cooked fish from the water and cook the potatoes in the fish cooking water, you'll have a much more flavourful chowder. Might require another pot but worth it.

For liquid I used David Tanis's shrimp broth from NY Times (https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/12914-david-taniss-shrimp-broth.) Added a bit of diced red bell pepper to veg, next maybe half a jalapeno diced as well. For fish, Trader Joe's frozen cod pieces (bargain priced, already chopped to appropriate size, and you can throw them right into the soup without thawing first -- cooking will do that for you.) Soup was excellent

This is amazing. I've made it probably five times now. I've used fresh bluefish, fresh porgy, and frozen barramundi just depending on what's on hand. Agree with others not to slice the fish ahead of time as it comes apart on its own. Instead of bacon, I've used dried chorizo which works great. Smoked Paprika, garlic, onion, potato, carrots, white wine, chicken broth, heavy cream and when I'm feeling luxurious, saffron.

Great recipe. Thanks also for the advice from the readers!
Used a can of anchovies in olive oil (net weight 50g) for the initial "sofrito"    and coconut milk (400 can) plus a bit of water, just enough to "rinse" the can.  
For the fish I kind of overdid it:  4 smallish hake fillets,  one monkfish tail I found sulking in the freezer and two fistfuls of clams.
Next time I'll look for the lemon grass and will probably add one two chili peppers while dissolving the anchovies.

Excellent recipe!  This recipe is so user-friendly. I used 2 of everything; butter, 2 pieces of bacon, shallots, carrots, celery sticks, 6 baby potatoes, half of a fennel bulb. I added about a half teaspoon of smoked paprika, and a cup of organic frozen corn. I also added saffron, thyme and bay leaf.
My fish was based upon what I could find in the frozen section of a mid-west supermarket, and so I used 2 haddock filets and 4 of flounder. 4 cups of fish stock and 1 of dry white wine. Delicious!
A little clam base or lobster base does wonders for this soup. That's the not-so-secret ingredient that many restaurants that will go to great lengths to hide.
You add the fish last. Fish cut in small chunks takes only 3-5 minutes to cook at the end. Excellent and so easy!

Used cod and didn’t cut it in advance - when the fish was cooked I gently broke it into chunks. I didn’t use carrots but did add a pinch of saffron. Also used white wine and broth but too much - stick to 2-3 total cups of liquid. Added fish sauce at the end for some zing. It came out well.

In the spirit of this no-recipe series, I tossed the fish & prawns in turmeric & a little salt, then quickly browned a little before adding to the soup.  It's from a Malaysian fish curry recipe that I really like.

Made this according to no recipe, used shrimp instead of fin fish...so good. Can add pepper flakes for a bit of heat. Sam Sifton's no recipe stuff is so great. No food snobbery here...thanks for that.

Very similar to the no-recipe chowder I make, minus the carrots. Here's one thing I do differently, though.I crush some of the cooked potato to give the liquid a bit more body, before adding milk or half and half. Once the fish is cooked, ladle chowder into a bowl, float a smidge of soft butter on top, dust with paprika (letting some hit the melting butter) strew in parsley. Comfort in a bowl.
P.S. If you have time, try briefly salt-curing fish, washing off salt before the fish goes in.

I made this with bacon, leeks, one small parsnip, smoked paprika, flour, vermouth, clam juice, milk, thyme, halibut, fresh parsley. I never say this about my own cooking, but, I gave myself one Michelin star for this soup.
Do you cook the fish first, or does it cook in the liquid?
It cooks in the liquid. I used 1-inch sea bass chunks and 5 minutes is about right. Just test the fish for done-ness before serving.

I have been making a no-recipe fish and/or seafood chowder for many years. I usually add some red pepper and some celery along with the other vegetables mentioned. But the secret is to use lobster stock base from Better than Bouillon.  Not only does it add great color but the taste addition is amazing. It’s rather expensive but a little goes a long way. You can make shrimp bisque with it or lobster bisque too.

This turned out very delicious. I like how you can personalize it. I used a can of cream corn instead of dairy to thicken it. I also put diced fennel bulb and 2 T of chopped kimchi in it. Quick, easy, healthy and adaptable to ingredients on hand.

Really excellent no-recipe recipe.  Don't skimp on the liquids.  I used a combination of seafood stock (3 cups), dry white wine (1 cup) and even a little water (almost a cup).  I was also generous with the vegetables.  Cod worked well.  So good....and will do it again soon.
The bacon adds a wonderful layer of complexity to the flavor profile of the chowder. I also highly recommend using seafood stock and white wine for the liquid portion of the soup.

I add a can of evaporated milk after the potatoes are cooked and before putting in frozen cod or haddock.  A favorite Auntie always used frozen fish in chowder because she said it held together better than fresh.
We prepared this just as noted with a very fresh snapper filet, skinned and cut into chunks.  It was delicious!

I used all the helpful cooking notes and made it with olive oil, butter, anchovies, turmeric, smoked paprika, pinot grigio, clam broth along with onions, jalapeno, frozen corn, carrots, heavy cream and salmon. It was a beautiful color from the turmeric/corn and paprika/salmon combination and absolutely delicious.

Because I’m a Mainer I prefer the virgin version of fish chowder without corn, carrots, celery, bacon etc. I think all that overpowers the delicate flavor of the fish. Just butter, onions, potatoes cooked in clam juice and Old Bay. Then add whole fillets cod (the best fish, however I always feel guilty using cod because it has been overfished). Add evaporated milk or cream and milk. Let it stand for a while or refrigerate overnight it tastes better. My family has loved this chowder for 40 years!

This is one  of my favorite Sam Sifton no-recipe creations. I made it  many times during the COVID shut-down, when I was learning to stock and cook from the pantry rather than shopping daily. Perfect vehicle for frozen haddock fillets (added whole ) frozen corn, potatoes slightly past their prime. I used bacon and butter both. Best version used two bottles of clam juice, water, and a slosh of dry vermouth because I had no wine. Smoked paprika not a must, but really peps it up. Oyster crackers

The weather outside is frightful, but this chowder is so delightful. Made this more or less according to the “instructions,” using nitrate free pre-cooked bacon from Trader Joe’s and white wine. We used skinless salmon and the flavors were outstanding. My picky eater husband gobbled it up. The only sound was the delicate dinging of spoons on soup bowls. Delicous!
P.S. Used monkfish.
I prefer cod. I heard red snapper is also good. I tried tilapia in fish stew once and it was too flat (as in thin), and I didn’t like it’s color. Avoid it.
A sprinkling of mashed potato flakes for thickness??
Julia Child recommends clam juice as a substitute for fish stock.
I always use clam juice! I never have fish stock around and clam juice works great.
I added sliced fennel and chopped dill - delicious! (Didn't add corn.)
I was just thinking about adding dill!

The accolades are warranted. I used a couple of methods common in the Maritimes of Atlantic Canada, one Yukon Gold potato diced and one russet potato grated which almost disintegrates into the soup while the starch provides body, along with one (obligatory) tin of evaporated milk. For liquids, one tin Bag Harbor fish stock, vermouth (Dolins, Yum) and topped off with water, used celery instead of carrots, Cod and smoked haddock. Not overly rich, in fact one of the best chowders we've had.
Do you cook the fish first or put it in the chowder raw and it cooks in 5 minutes?
Just put it in the chowder a few minutes before serving. If it sits in the soup too long it will fall apart —and you don’t want that, unless it’s the next day’s leftovers!

Absolutely delicious non recipe!
My variations- added celery, vegetable stock and a pack of powdered coconut milk.
Devine! Already thinking about the next time i make it....

I’ve made this twice: once with cod and shrimp,and once with cod and scallops. I used good quality seafood stock and white wine for the liquid. Both times, it was great. I think that smoked paprika adds great flavor. I’d live to experiment with salmon. We think it’s s delicious weeknight meal, and even better the next day.
Works great with medium size chunks of fresh salmon. Delicious. Also try a small piece of something like smoked cod or haddock.

I have another method to offer. When I poach salmon, I do so in tomato juice and wine with herbs, onion , etc. after the salmon is poached, I strain and save the poaching liquid (in the freezer usually) and when I want chowder I defrost the liquid and then make a more “Manhattan” version of the impromptu chowder similar to this one. 

Enjoy!

I accidentally grabbed almond milk instead of chicken stock and just unloaded the carton. The result was shockingly delicious!

It kind of makes sense since almond milk is basically protein and water too. It makes a lighter version of chowder which we adored. We are going to use almond milk for this recipe going forward.

I grew up cooking with my mother on Saturday mornings. She was a wonderful cook, and always cooked by feel; never really followed a recipe. When friends would say "that was wonderful, could I please have the recipe?", she would just make it up, and write out what she thought she had done. Then, according to my sister, when it didn't work, they thought she was trying to keep her recipes secret. No-Recipe cooking is completely natural; it's not good to get uptight about it.

Made this last night and added some Cayenne and Saffron...at very end added some more butter! Didn't have any white wine so added some beer althought I think white wine would be better. OMG! It was the best and just downloaded every recipe online. Cooking just how my mom cooked. Love it!

Made this tonight and I am so impressed with myself! 
End of jar of bacon pieces, pat of butter. 
2 small/med onions
4 smallish russet potatoes
4-5 aging carrots
cup of frozen corn
Can of “seafood stock,” cup of cheap white wine, water
Salt, pepper, bay leaf, smoked paprika, dash of turmeric, bit of dried thyme, garlic powder
A pour (1/3 or 1/2 C?) of cream
Zap few seconds with immersion blender
One pound frozen grocery store flounder
12 oz defrosted shrimp
12 oz defrosted bay scallops
So good.

Using this loose recipe with canned clams makes a quick but surprisingly good clam chowder. Use the clam liquid from the can and the best stock or wine-whatever you have on hand for a quick but substantial lunch.

Cherished Grandma recipe: everything up to the fish cut up in smallish pieces.  Layer bacon, cook until grease starts to render, add a layer of onions,cook until translucent, layer potatoes, add clam juice and/or shrimp stock and water to barely cover potatoes, cook until tender.  layer fish fillets (haddock or cod or pollock, or what you can get, on top.  cover.  cook until fish is almost done.  add milk, pepper.  dinner for a crowd in one pot, and the most comforting thing you will ever eat.
I always use evaporated milk in fish chowder, fewer calories and the chowder does not break if you have leftovers to reheat. I have made a similar chowder for about 40 years.

Great to see this recipe at breakfast time, best time to eat fish chowder. Old Maine trick: instead of cream, add a small can of evaporated milk, Much healthier and utterly delicious. Oh where oh where are the beloved Nabisco Crown Pilot crackers when we need them!
Should the fish be already cooked before adding?
No need to pre-cook the fish. The heat of the chowder will take care of that. Pre cooking or boiling the fish will over-cook. Good rule of thumb for any seafood chowder.
No, the fish chunks will cook quickly in the hot liquid.
No, the fish should be uncooked when added to broth at the last minute. It cooks very quickly, depending on how thick the filets are.

I saute onions, celery, potatoes to crisp; add chicken broth and thyme 'til veg are cooked through, last three minutes pieces of cod, which will disintegrate on their own.  Serve with chopped parsley and a dollop of butter, sour dough bread or saltines.  Always a hit.

I had a fennel bulb so I sliced it thin and cooked it with the onions. Added two minced garlic cloves along with the paprika. Used water and a cup or so of white wine for the broth. Monkfish for the fish. Easy, quick, and delicious.
What gives the broth its beautiful yellow color in the photo?
@Amy if the food stylist made it the way Sam talked about it, the carrots and the smoked paprika would add some yellow. Alternatively, if you added a touch of saffron or turmeric, that would do it.
Had no bay leaves or smoked paprika. Substituted tarragon and regular paprika and cayenne. Delicious!

I have made this two or three times now and getting comfortable with a no-recipe recipe is so confidence boosting! Plus, this is so quick and delicious. We love it. This time I used cod and shrimp. Last time I used cod and bay scallops.

Looking forward to trying again, with a better understanding of how to cook. Used some old Riesling I had in the fridge and a little bit of water for the broth, and all I can say is I should have used less wine.

To "kick it up a FEW notches" I added smoked whitefish (sturgeon) from my local Jewish Deli to the chowder base and for the final few min, 2 raw, peeled, tail on, Jumbo Shrimp per person...just long enough to cook through before serving...oh what a meal!
I hit it with a bit of Crystal Louisiana-style hot sauce at the end and that was the difference for me.

This is my favorite NYT recipe/no recipe.  I love that it's different every time.  I usually add some shrimp for additional color and flavor.  I do something different every time, and it's always delicious.
The smoked paprika really makes this dish.
Best use for leftover grilled fish that I know of.
Made it sort of Spanish style with tomato base, yellow bell pepper, peas, potato, and one 6oz haddock filet and maybe a dozen shrimp I had lingering in my freezer. Delicious.
I added red pepper flakes and used tilapia. It was utterly delicious.
Also used bay leaf!
I use leeks if I  have them and add a pinch of saffron.

Don't cut the raw fish into chunks. Immerse the entire fillets in the liquid; they'll break up naturally as soon as they're cooked through.

maybe coconut milk in place of milk/cream

"Surely you must have water."  Not true.  Some states are still allowing water shut offs during the pandemic.  For more information about your State and who to register your complaint for this despicable behavior, go to Food and Water Watch.  We need everyone to be able to wash their hands.

Just tried this with Salt Cod (bacalao) - yes the salt dried cod.  Just happened to have some that had been de-salting for a couple of days.  Use less water, more milk, more potatoes to thicken.  Proportions just as one would guess to provide desired thicker or thinner broth.    Worked beautifully when mixed with some pre-cooked mussels and small fish fillets.
Nick 
St. Catharines ON, Canada

Add chopped ginger with the onion.
Substituted "fish" with frozen seafood mix [chopped Squid, Octopus, Clams, Mussel & Shrimp]. 
Added extra shrimp.
Few spoons of oriental fish sauce [anchovy extract] provides more pungent aroma.
Used whole milk instead of cream to keep it healthier.
Tried with and without the white wine. I would skip as it made little difference to taste.

Tried separately with coconut milk, galangal/ginger and kafir lime leaves.  Very similar to Thai Tom Kha soup.

This was delicious. Did bacon and butter, homemade fish stock, potatoes and carrots, bay leaf and Spanish smoked paprika. 20 minutes in the IP. Added in a big can of salmon, cream and white wine. Simmer simmer simmer. Served with big hunks of homemade French bread. Every single bite was sublime and it was easy peasy. Slurp slurp slurp.

Also, first time making my own fish stock. WHY HAVE I NEVER DONE THAT BEFORE????

Third time I’ve done this and finally motivated to say THANK YOU SAM for this brilliant recipe. 
We had no carrots so a rib do celery and half an orange  pepper did the trick. So Versatile. Cheers from Rural Suffolk in England.

Made this tonight with some nice cold-smoked Atlantic cod (I’m located in Newfoundland and can get it locally.) I didn’t have any white wine so I added a splash of vermouth which worked fine. I served it with a batch of flaky homemade biscuits and it was as good as anything out there.
You can also substitute evaporated milk for cream (gives you a little creaminess without as much fat, and is shelf-stable).

A can or two of clams with juice is a great substitute for fish stock. 
Sake is also a really delicious liquid to add, instead of, or in addition to, wine. 
Fennel and celery fit in with the carrots. (I can't eat onions, so fennel or celery my usual substitutes.)
Chopped parsley and dill stirred in at the end -- yum.
I have made this dish at least a dozen times, each effort using what was at hand.  Try as I might, it always turns out wonderfully.  Relax, the non- recipe will bail you out.

So good and so easy.  Essentially used up leftovers.  2 baked potatoes, a few baby potatoes, end of a bottle of white wine, a cup of corn, the remainder of a sweet and red onion, 2 cloves garlic, 2 cod fillets from the back of the freezer (thawed them first) and a can of evaporated milk… was delicious.  Smoked paprika makes a big difference.
I always use Old Bay in my chowder... did someone else mention this already??

Great non-recipe that helped me deal with about 8oz of leftover cooked Hog Fish. At $30/# I didn’t want to throw away. I followed the suggestions closely, using chicken broth and bacon. I added about 2/3 cup leftover mashed potatoes (why not?) as a thickener. The fish was added at the last minute to only reheat. This is such a great idea to use delicious broiled or sautéed fish.  Very tasty!

A great fridge clear out recipe (particularly useful before leaving for a trip). Freeze what’s left over and reheat upon your return when you’re too tired to cook but want homemade. You really can’t go wrong with this.

Similar to the chowder I grew up eating as a kid, on the north shore of Lake Superior. Frozen cod was the protein of choice, which was odd as we lived on this ginormous freshwater sea. No matter. I'm definitely going to make this and do my own riff - I'm thinking a touch of curry would make it that much more warming.

Very good and so EASY to throw together. My version was probably light on the fish, tho, only used about 6 ounces of frozen cod, but it was just right for chowder for two. Probably used a bit more than "a flash" of smokey paprika, but we like that. Took commenter's advice and used the bottle of clam juice. I think this is one of those forgiving recipes, and you can tinker with it. Thanks, Sam Sifton.

Skipped the bacon, and went a little Rhode-Island style and added summer savory (a bit like thyme).  I think a combination of canned clams and frozen cod would work well here.  Don't skimp on freshly ground black pepper, either.

I've been making this regularly and it's really delicious. Here in the UK, I can get 'fish pie mix' from my Fish Guy, a mix of Cod, Haddock, Hake, Pollack, Salmon & Smoked Haddock chunks so that's what I use, and I usually throw in some extra vegetables just to annoy my vegetable averse partner!

Olive oil/butter
1/2 large onion diced
3 carrots sliced
3 Yukon potatoes large dice
Shiitake mushrooms quarters
Swiss chard/dragon kale wide strips
S & P, ~1/2 t. smoked paprika
Whole carton veg broth + ~1 c. white wine
Small bunch thyme, 2 small bay leaves
3 glugs whipping cream
6 cod filets cut in large pieces
Good splash of white wine at end--a MUST.
Quick and SO GOOD!

Unparalleled! So easy and good, I keep making it and feeding everyone I know. Smoked paprika is essential, don’t skip it as that’s what gives it depth and kick. Didn’t use chilli pepper due to guests allergies, but might do so in future. Made with cod and a bought medley of shrimp, squid, and mussels. Used home made chicken stock. Sam, this is your best recipe yet!

Saffron for sure, as well as the Pimenton later, but anchovies and two crushed cloves of garlic cooked low and slow with fresh cracked black pepper to start. I’ve totally gone in for a top shelf vermouth with cod and salmon. And, by all means the best celery available. Fresh dill to finish.

I have made this stew twice now, and both times it's been amazing! I varied it a bit, but salmon and lots of smoked paprika featured in both batches. I made it for may parents last night for St. Pat's Eve, with salmon AND cod. Just perfect. It's a keeper!

Olive oil
Anchovies
Celery
Onion
Yellow bell pepper
Potato
Jalapeño pepper
Ginger
Garlic
Paprika
Turmeric
Curry leaf
Kaffir lime leaf
Coconut milk
Vegetable stock
Halibut
Shrimp
Salmon
Lime juice
Fresh coriander

Ended up being kind of a Thai fish chowder, inspired by previous commenters. Very, very tasty. Quick! Will repeat while I still have a stock of curry leaf and lime leaf. A tiny bit of shrimp paste might be a nice addition. Excited to make other variations.
Fun dish to freestyle with. Applied a lot of the recommendations. Will do again in the future, and will definitely mix it up.

When I make gefilte fish for Passover, I strain and freeze the cooking liquor in different sized containers, then use it for chowder or cioppino.  Once, the label fell off in the freezer, and I mixed it with chicken broth for company.   Enough said....

Love the open endedness of this recipe.
Used seafood stock that just made from frozen shrimp shells I had been saving (finally!) Carrots, potatoes, white onion, fennel stems, cheap Pinot Grigio, frozen shrimp, frozen bay scallops, frozen black cod,heavy cream, bay leaves and smoked paprika-- all was going well but I thought it needed something more so added a bit of anchovy paste and in the end stirred in some wasabi fumi furikake (a Japanese rice seasoning) and that really made it!
Thanks !!
I used tilapia, shrimp stock and half & half. Delicious!

This was AMAZING! I added about four slices of fennel really thinly cut. Pinot Grigio. OMG it was so good! I used baby potatoes - the golden ones. Chunked up tilapia. Cup of frozen corn. Onion. Carrots. Paprika. The rest just like the paragraph (won't call it a recipe because there is no list!) and it tasted so light and wonderful. Put a few splashes of 2% milk. This was heavenly. The flavors all blended together in my magic Dutch Oven. It was filling, but light. Sam, you really gifted us all!

Brilliant. 

Onions, bacon bits, smoked paprika, white wine, chicken stock, peas, frozen corn, salmon, smoked haddock, cod, creme fraiche and fresh thyme. Delicious and ready in 15 minutes.

I love this no-recipe recipe — it's exactly the way I like to cook. We started with onion, celery, and tiny potatoes, and used Better Than Bouillon lobster broth. The smoked paprika is delicious if you have it, especially if you're not using bacon. Fresh corn was a fantastic addition, too. We topped the chowder with chopped vegetarian 'bacon' and fennel fronds. Served with crusty bread, as recommended, plus a side of Smitten Kitchen's avocado-cucumber salad.
Delicious!  Added clam juice and can of chopped clams to bacon, potatoes, onions, celery, wine, fat free 1/2 & 1/2, frozen cod, lots of Old Bay (you gotta have this!) and fresh corn.
Clam broth for the water is a basic add- and no carrots if you are a New England chowder purist. Loved everyone’s riffs on this basic recipe though.

Great non-recipe. I used pancetta,butter, leeks, potatoes, carrots, celery, fennel, garlic. Had no
fish stock or white wine, so only water. A pinch of saffron, chipotle powder, splash of fish sauce. 
Finished with cream then chunks of red snapper. 
Delicious!

Easy, delicious, and adaptable. I used what I had on hand:

—bacon fat and butter
—quartered small red potatoes
—diced sweet potato
—sliced fennel bulb
—sliced leeks
—fish stock
—white wine
—cayenne
—saffron
—heavy cream
—two packets of Wild Alaska pollock quick cuts
—Bar Harbor canned surf clams
—heavy cream
—a few splashes of pernod
Have made this many times. So delicious, fast and easy. Also has my all time favorite recipe line, “Surely you have water.
@Felicity Frisbie I've quoted "Surely you have water" so many times!

Excellent. For a two-person portion, I used two waxy potatoes, one carrot, an onion, and one ear of corn. Used Japanese tuna dashi packets with 500 ml water for stock, and about 300 g of sea bass. 

Skipped the bacon and used milk but it was still plenty tasty. Suggest seasoning to taste again before adding the fish. For moderate appetites the amount is more than enough without bread etc.
Made this tonight using a little bit of everything mentioned above and it was fantastic...tasted like something we would have had in a great restaurant.

Omitted bacon and corn.  Added a handful of chopped smoked king salmon just before the liquids and another handful at the end after the raw fish, plus a handful of fresh basil.  Used halibut for the main fish.  Used vegetable stock.  Otherwise followed it as stated.  Very good!

This was so yummy! I threw in some long-overlooked salmon that was starting to reach "freezer-burn" status. I also used a combination of wine, water and chicken broth.  We topped our portions with some shredded parmesan and sopped it up with some chewy rustic bread. A perfect meal on a cold winter evening.
Added baby bok Choy and used almond milk and water...even better the second day
Doubled the corn instead of using carrots - So good!

Fabulous! Butter, shallot, garlic, carrots. White wine and chicken stock. Smoked paprika, frozen corn, fresh parsley, bay leaf. Salt and pepper. Splash of fish sauce. Whole pollock. It broke up when cooked. Splash of whole milk. 30 minutes. Dinner on the table. Outstanding.
I made this tonight.  Used broth wine and half and half.  Tasted great but it curdled. Why.
Perhaps you added cold milk to a very hot liquid? The acid of the wine could also curdle the half & half. There are lots of good tips online to prevent this. Good luck!
Wait till the end to add the cream. Then don't let it boil.

I am in Florida a few months each year and have fish odds and ends that I've put up in my freezer and some high quality jarred clam juice and canned clams I can add. So I can work this with so many combinations and come out golden every time. Really appreciate it—tops in my go to list.

I made this last night.  I used salmon and tilapia, and added a bit of turmeric as one reader suggested.  Wow!  It was fantastic.  Will definitely make this again.  It is so easy to make on a work night.  I had a can of fish stock on hand, and also used white wine to make up the difference.

It was an inspiring photo and description.   
I used fluke, Nantucket bay scallops, and wild shrimp. All the liquids suggested: white wine, chicken broth, and water, then seasonned with bay leaf, herbes de provences, thyme, and a pinch of cayenne. Carrots, celery, and potato made it chunky. Milk and cream turned it from soup to chowder. Finely chopped fresh parsley at serving time added a tasty & colorful finish.
20 minutes.
I'm all for no-recipe recipes, but it seems that in addition to fish-per-person, veggies per person would also be useful. Handfuls surely change depending on if you're cooking for 2 or 8.
This is great. Used cod, andouille bacon from whole foods and paprika/turmeric which gave it a needed kick, a bottle of clam juice, and 3 splashes of cream. A really filling soup!
Substituted roasted Poblanos instead of red peppers. Topped with homemade Salsa Verde, Grilled Shrimp, Avocado, Scallions & a couple of toasted Tortilla strips.  Lovely!!!!

I like the free approach of this, and these "non-recipe" recipes...  it's liberating.  I winged it and it came out great - common sense to adjust as you go. Not a fan of carrots in chowder, so left those by the road. Cod, scallops and clams - yum!!
Made this with a can of coconut milk and some cabbage that was hanging out in the fridge. Also used the jar of anchovies as per Mae's suggestion. Delish.

Made in instapot. 1 onion,carrot, stalk celery, 3 bay leaves 2 cloves garlic.
1 C Clam juice, 1/4 c wine, 1 C water 1 C milk.
Added fish, more milk and 1/2 c 1/2&1/2. Yummmm

Delicious.
Made this for 4 people and the potatoes soaked up much of the liquid so kept adding.
Used:
 2 bottles clam juice
1 1/2  cups chicken broth
½ cup vermouth
heavy cream
½ packet SAZON seasoning
grated fresh ginger

Yellow potatoes are ideal—they release more starch than reds to thicken the soup (dice them pretty small), but they don't dissolve like russets. I've always added a little smoked fish, though the smoked paprika suggested here would probably be simpler. (Note that traditional New England uses salt pork and not bacon—but I like the smokiness (I don't eat bacon).) I've generally used a mix of milk and clam juice and no water.

I made this with bacon, potatoes, onion, carrot, smoked paprika, hot paprika, dried thyme, bay leaf, three cups of homemade chicken broth, 3/4 of a cup of white wine, 2/3 cup of heavy cream, a can of clams in their juice, and two filets of frozen cod which I let cook in the soup at the end for about 8 minutes. 

Absolutely delicious.

My secret weapon for soups fishy and savory: powdered daishi. Worked like a charm here in place of the fish stock. The smoked paprika is a revelation, especially for those of us who don’t eat bacon but miss its smoke factor. I’m kind of regretting spring is near as I want to put this delicious soup in my weakly rotation.

My riff on this - sautéd bacon, onions, potatoes and turmeric; splash of white wine, one bottle of clam juice, and enough broth to cover the potatoes; cook. Added a glut of cream, whole fish (cod) fillets, then shrimp at the very end. Kicked it up a notch by dry-brining the cod ahead of time, for 20-30 mins - this kept the fish tender and flavorful. Success.

This is probably violating the spirit of chowder, but I use this with leftover fish from a previous meal.  Slip the fish into the chowder last, just long enough to heat through--no cooking required in this case.  Finally, a way my family will eat fish they've already seen at one dinner.  And it's delicious!
Pandemic bread-making here—NYT’s Artisan loaf to have with this fish chowder. This meal tonight is going to be divine......

I made this combining elements of a few of the suggestions and Sam Sifton's guide as the basis. Left out the carrots. I added a pinch of Poudre de Fée (Fairy Dust) that I purchased in Paris, the chowder was really good. everyone loved it.
I'd love to know more about the "poudre de fée", sounds interesting. If we ever get to France again.......
Does salmon work well?
YES! Salmon in this is glorious!

I've been making a version of this for years, ever since my first Joy of Cooking  college graduation gift. I often put minced or whole baby clams in, too, along with the liquid in the can. And I cook it from the start in milk with a glug of cream, or half and half, and with the fish of any stripe in there, too. Celery is a nice addition. It all tastes incredible from the minute it's done, and even better the next day. This is a family favorite.

I like a thick chowder, so I make a roux with the onion and celery, olive oil and butter. When the flour has cooked long enough to lose its raw flavor, I add the wine, and let it simmer. Boil the potatoes and carrots in a large pot with enough water to cover, then at a simmer add the fish. While the fish cooks stir the cream or milk (a can of evaporated skim milk works well) into the roux, and scrape it into the pot with the veg and fish. Finish with nutmeg, Tabasco, and parsley to taste.

It was in the paper today so of course I had to make it today.  My husband doesn't eat pork, so we did butter, chicken broth, white wine and water, carrots, onions, corn, potatoes and sole, with bay leaf, a little cayenne and smoked paprika.  Delicious.  I can see making a chicken chowder in a similar way.  I love recipes that are a method, rather than measurements. We'll be having this again.

Thanks Sam - this couldn't be better timed. I knocked up a batch of crab shell stock last night and scored a bag of frozen corn earlier this week (good on the cob stuff is difficult to get here in England). Didn't have a plan for either. Now however, dinner is decided! Cheers
Though this is a can't miss non-recipe, I believe it really benefits from the addition of bacon as an indispensable ingredient.

I come from generations of Atlantic seacoast dwelling people on both sides of my family, who regularly ate fish fresh from the dock. Chowder is a staple. This is the recipe!  Clam chowder is made the same way except you add clam juice and I skip the peppers.

Just saying: thank you, fish and tomatoes don't get along.

This is definitely a KEEPER and the perfect recipe for a cold weekend in VA! 
started with basic instructions (using bacon and butter!) and added-
-chopped celery, shallot, and 4 cloves garlic with the onions, carrots, corn and potatoes.
- used seafood stock, a little sherry and 1 cup white wine
- while cooking added fresh cilantro, L & P, and saffron (had on hand) more paprika, and few dashes of spice from cabinet-can't remember!
- Cod worked well... might try shrimp next time!
Tasty. I used 1 piece fish (snapper) and  5 large shrimp. Added kernels from 1 cobb of corn which I think gave it a nice sweet flavor.  Used  water and white wine and only 1% milk.
Excellent.  Made it with catfish since that is commonly found here in the Deep South.  Used Clam Base & water.
This made the best clam chowder! The smoked paprika makes all the difference.

Loved reading the notes and coming up with my own variation: turmeric, lemongrass, saffron, mushrooms, fish sauce, full-fat coconut milk, added a little leftover rice at the end instead the potatoes. Used a little salt pork instead of bacon. Fish: scallops, shrimp, cod, all thawed first from frozen. Topped with cilantro. Yes, a little like Tom Ka Gai soup.

Even made with water, with no wine or any kind of stock on hand, it was excellent. Added a generous amount of half and half and seasoned very well. Definitely make again and again.  I could eat this for breakfast and probably will. Delicious!

Confession - I am not a cook. This has been my favorite quarantine cooking adventure! The devil-may-care approach encouraged me to give it a go. I had to sub sweet potatoes & shrimp, which turned it into something delicious & unexpected.

Fish chowder is scary enough without a recipe, so I threw one together for anyone that’s intimidated by this recipe. 

Brown butter and brown 4 slices diced bacon, 2 small onions. Add 3 large red potatoes and 1.5 ears corn. Then 6 cups Chicken broth and 1/2 c white wine. 

Then add paprika, turmeric, parsley, salt, and pepper to taste. 

Add 1/4 cup half and half and simmer until potatoes are cooked. 

Then add 1.5 pound catfish and 2 cups chopped shrimp and simmer till cooked. Add lemon juice!
Delicious.  Added a little celery, some clam juice, white wine.

Actually used Maui Blanc - pineapple wine. Fish Chowder ala Lahaina

Easy to make and easy to riff on. That's what makes Sam Sifton's suggestions so great. It gives you a good starting off point. 
Added the corn cobs to the stock to help starch it up a bit. Added tomatoes (world's smallest San Marzano, grown in the shade in a paint bucket). 

I suggest getting good bay leaves to have on hand. They make a world of difference in so many dishes.

One of the best dishes I have ever made!  Skipped the bacon, added celery and a bouquet of thyme and Rosemary. For fish I used scallops, shrimp, and cod and 2 ears of fresh corn. Used half and half, seafood broth and white wine for base. Absolutely delicious! Served with garlic bread.
I can’t wait for Sams no recipe book. This is a great concept. I follow this recipe with whatever fish I have. It’s always great.

I use anchovies a lot, and never thought of it in my fish chowder—thanks commenters. I don't eat bacon so I often add a bit of smoked fish to my chowder, though smoked paprika or chipotle works too.

For some extra body beyond the cream, I'll sometimes remove a bit of the cooked potatoes and puree them. Or if I'm feeling especially lazy, add a little instant mashed potatoes toward the end—my go-to soup thickener, since it never tastes raw.

Love this recipe! Perfect match with how I cook. Reminds me of the pumpkin soup recipe based on a Myers Briggs interpretation of personality types here https://www.capt.org/products/examples/20014HO.pdf Thanks!
Great link--thanks!
A trick I learned that really enriched the flavor is to simmer the fish in the milk

I made a huge batch and froze plenty for later.  Used halibut, cod, rockfish, scallops and chopped clams.  The hot paprika was a winner. Used a stick of butter and 3 slices of bacon, fish stock and white wine -- plus clam juice and fish sauce and added more spices including plenty of fresh thyme and left off the milk/cream.  Yummy!!  Will make another batch in a couple weeks using shrimp instead of the scallops.
Threw in a splash of vermouth at the end, excellent!

This was simply amazing and so easy!
I chopped up 2 pieces of thick sliced applewood smoked bacon and browned it in clarified butter. Added diced onions, celery, garlic and potatoes and sauteed until the onions and celery were soft. Added smoked paprika, coarsely ground pepper and some Old Bay. Added some chardonnay and then chicken stock and after the potatoes were soft I smashed a few of them against the side of the pan, heavy cream and then catfish and parsley flakes. Served with beer bread!

Adding to the chorus of enthusiasm after making this for the first time yesterday. Used 3 pieces of bacon, 5 smallish potatoes, 3 small carrots, and just one small onion; had to use the whole 4 cups (32 oz) of seafood stock so as to have enough room for all that plus defrosted Icelandic haddock from Trader Joe’s, along with 1/3 cup white wine, 1/4 cup heavy cream, and some saffron with the smoked paprika. Made NYT beer bread to go with! Everyone loved it, including the 8-year-old.
Use clam juice and wine for broth

Cook:
3 slices bacon (remove and pour off 1/2 fat)

Chop and add to pot- 3 Yukon gold potatoes, 2 carrots, 1 celery stalk, 1 onion

After about 6 min add 1 minced garlic clove, 1/2 tsp smoked paprika, 1 tbl Old Bay seasoning, salt and pepper to taste

Add 1/2 C white wine and stir well. Add 4 C chicken broth, thyme,1 fillet of the cod and diced bacon. Simmer covered 30-40 min

Sauté 10 shrimp separately and add with remaining 1b fish to chowder

Cook 10 min uncovered. Added cornstarch to thicken

This was fantastic—a new favorite. Miss my grandmother’s fish chowder, and this was close. My variations:

2 tb olive oil instead of butter
3 med. carrots
3 med. celery
1 yellow onion
2 red potatoes 
1 c. Trader Joe’s frozen roasted corn
1/2 tsp pimenton dulce
4 c. (1 box) low sodium chicken broth
1/2 can full fat coconut milk
Tilapia (since I had some in the freezer)

So good. Looking forward to experimenting more in the future.

So appreciated all the other comments, which gave me the courage to try this without a recipe! It’s amazing. I especially appreciated the suggestion of Turmeric which is transformative. This is a new favorite!

Not a note writer per se, but this chowder deserves praise. Made for a wonderful, easy lunch on an icy day. We used haddock, bacon, carrots, potatoes, onions and seafood stock. Smoked paprika and crushed pepper flakes are a must!
Make enough to save for the next day; it improves! And a bit of cayenne will wake things up. Our favorite chowder recipe calls for it.

For 2 generous servings:

3 tbsp butter
1 onion
2 carrots
2 Yukon Gold potatoes 
2 cloves garlic
1/2 tsp dried thyme
1/2 tsp smoked paprika
1/8 tsp cayenne
Salt & pepper 
3/4 cup white wine
1 cup clam juice 
1 cup water
1 cup frozen corn
1/3 cup heavy cream
8oz mahi 

Really good.
I added some turmeric for colour and also because it's good for us!  It was all very delicious.

This turned out great! Here's what I used:
Cod (about 6 ounces)
Sockeye Salmon (about 6 ounces)
Pee Wee Potatoes (about 2 cups)
Rainbow Carrots (2)
Celery/Onion (about 2 cups)
Pancetta (3 ounces)
Fish Stock (28 ounces)
Dry Vermouth (half cup)
Heavy Cream (half cup)
Saffron, Smoked Paprika, Bay Leaf, Flat Leaf Parsley
Put the fish in whole and let it break apart after cooking.

I have to say, I'm in the minority here, but I made this, and it was, well, terrible. Didn't taste like any chowder I've ever eaten. Was just thick and muddy. My wife had a spoonful, and for the first time in my 7 years of cooking for her, said, this is terrible, and went and got leftovers, not touching another bite. I'm a pretty good cook, and I generally know how to riff and improvise, but I'm never making chowder without a recipe again.

Made this last night because we tried to save some food from our freezer, but not everything stayed frozen.  So, a 2-lb bag of thawed cod with a small piece of salt pork that also didn't stay frozen, lots of potatoes, carrots, onions, and celery, corn, a huge glug of white wine, water and the suggested seasonings made a lovely dinner, even for my husband who was not thrilled when I told him I would make a fish chowder for dinner.  Of course, in Maryland, we add Old Bay.  Tasty!

I had leftover sheet pan breaded tilapia with potatoes and onions. So the chowder was pulled together so quickly. I had several slices of prosciutto and substituted that for the bacon and used leeks for the onions. I used excellent clam sauce I had in the freezer. I followed another's suggestion of adding turmeric to give flavor and beautiful yellow color. Brought this to a simmer then last minute added the roasted tilapia and onions. Served with crusty bread. A definite hit!
Made a curry spin with peas instead of corn, curry and turmeric instead of paprika, and coconut cream! Delicious will try again!

Love this recipe...such a great starting point. And thanks to Lenore’s note, I used 1/2 can of full fat coconut milk instead of dairy, and the result was fantastic. Also added celery to the onion-carrot soffrito, as well as a quart of vegetable stock, Trader Joe’s frozen roasted corn, and a pound of their frozen cod. No bacon or butter...kept it pescatarian. Just fantastic.

This has become a favorite recipe. I love the “ no recipe” concept—my favorite way to cook! I don’t measure anything. I use onions, carrots, corn, potatoes, plenty of smoked paprika. Usually cod, but I often throw in some shrimp too. White wine, vegetable stock. Maybe bacon, maybe not.  Maybe milk, maybe cream, It’s always great.

My favorite way of doing this is to start with a pot of water and milk, simmer the fish until it is no longer transluscent, then set the fish aside. Continue with the sauteing of the vegetables, add a little flour, then slowly add the simmer water until it all thickens a little. When the vegetables are soft, add the fish, and heat until it's all warm and delicious.

darn good!
made it with cod, but used no fat (no butter, no oil, definitely no bacon), still tasted great. For the cream, I used a splash of Silk heavy whipping cream (even though it does have some coconut and safflower oil), a splash is not going to clog my arteries any further ... right?

Will make again, for sure.
Added saffron

A previous chef used the term "sloshy night" and this is a perfect meal for a night like that.  A good bottle of white wine and this delicious stew.  I added baked lobster because it was valentines, and some ginger to the fish stock, wine, and cream.  Cooked the cod in the broth, added some smashed small roasted red potatoes. Bacon and paprika added some smokiness.  Some lightly toasted thick cut bread to slop up all the broth I couldn't get on a spoon.  Delicious.
I have made this many times because it is my most requested dish, varying herbs etc. I don’t always make it with bacon, but always add smoked salmon for a richness of flavor.

Edited for ease of use
1/4-1/2lb fish fillets/person 
Dice a strip or two of bacon 
Grab some butter
Add to Dutch oven med-high heat 
sauté diced onions, carrots and potatoes until onions translucent.
Add salt and pepper and smoked paprika.
Corn on the cob or a cup of frozen corn. 
Fish stock?
White wine? 
Water. 
Add liquid so the potatoes are covered. add a bay leaf and simmer.
reduce the liquid by 1/3 
Splash of milk/cream heat to thicken
Cut fillets, stir in gently, serve with crust bread
I would definitely recommend adding fresh dill!
I dust the cooked vegetables with a few tablespoons of flour before adding liquid - I use milk to make New Englandy chowder.  The flour thickens the soup nicely

Used a can of fish soup stock, 1/2 cup of white wine and some chicken broth and water
Butter, one large onion, 2 carrots, 2 good sized russet potatoes,  
1.5 # of red snapper
Chopped fresh Parsley and 1/2 cup of cream  at the finish
Some fresh thyme cooked in the broth
Salt pepper, smoked paprika
Was quite a good way to eat fish.

Made this last night and it was so good! I used celery, leeks, potatoes, roasted corn, and some diced pancetta, as well as some homemade veggie broth. Didn’t have white white but used sherry instead. I loved the smoked paprika - it’s not something I’d think to use in a fish stew but it was delicious!
Bravo! Amazing guide. I added extra wine & water to make a more substantial broth. My husband ate a huge bowl. Make sure you have baguette on hand.

I made this with sea bass I caught several weeks ago.  I used chicken broth, pinot blanc wine, a little cream at the end.  I had some pancetta in the fridge so I used that and the one piece of bacon I had.  Diced the onion, carrot and small potatoes and sautéed them with the meat.  I  through in some cooked seasoned rice towards the end along with some chopped spinach I had.  Seasoned with salt, pepper and smoked paprika.  Served with crusty bread.  It was wonderful.  I need to go fishing again!

I made this with tilapia fillets and left out bacon due to dietary restrictions. I added 5 garlic cloves and dried thyme. I used a combination of white wine and vegetable broth for the stock. I like the suggestion from another reader to add flour to thicken. I avoid dairy, so I tossed in some cashew cream instead of dairy cream. A squeeze of lemon at the end helps to brighten things up. If you have fresh herbs, especially parsley or chives, they’d be a nice addition here.
Bottled clam juice works as well as fish stock and better than water.

Dissolve 4 anchovies in 4 TBS butter, cook a few minutes.
Add chopped onion + pinch salt, cook some more.
Add chopped carrots and chopped potatoes.
Add 1/2 tsp ground thyme +1/2 tsp paprika + 1/4 tsp tumeric, cook some more.
Add 1/2 cup vermouth or white wine, reduce almost completely.
Add 32 oz seafood stock (eg Kitchen Basics), cook uncovered ~25 mins.
Add 1 cup frozen corn + 2c hot whole milk.
Taste and adjust seasonings.
Add 1 lb chopped cod, simmer until just done.
Parsley garnish.

My roommate demands I cook this EVERY SINGLE WEEK in the winter. Per person ratios (allows for leftovers): 
1 large potato
1/4 onion 
2 carrots 
2 celery ribs 
2-3 strips of turkey bacon 
1/4 cup uncooked rice (this will thicken it)
1/2 pound fish, e.g., tilapia, cod or haddock
Both sweet and smoked paprika as well as pepper to taste
Enough low sodium chicken broth to cover everything

Optional: shrimp, chopped up herb stems, lemon juice on top at the end, sliced garlic, dill, parmesan.

Made this with some canned clams and skate that had been in the freezer longer than I will admit.  Started with bacon, added onion, potatoes, and lots of smoky paprika, salt, and pepper.  Added white wine to deglaze the pot, then added homemade chicken stock, water and a bay leaf.  Let that bubble, then added heavy cream, clams, skate, and sliced scallions.  I added more liquid as it cooked down because I didn't want stew.  Definitely a keeper.

Was a fan of this -- used 3 cloves garlic, 2 large leeks, 2 potatoes, corn, veg stock + water + vermouth, also added old bay for a more seafood taste. Reduced down for about an hour prior to adding canned clams + flounder chunked into 1'' pieces. Finished with black pepper.
Made with coconut milk, lots; butter; left-over black cod; and left-over corn. Yummy!

This was so fun! At first I was annoyed there was no recipe, but then I just went with it. This version was sooooo good!!! Here's what I used:
- onions, carrots, fresh sweet corn and fingerling potatoes
- 2 hot italian sausages (was all I had and seriously made the dish)
- haddock filets
- dry sherry to taste
- chicken broth
- water
- half cup of heavy cream
- 2 bay leaves
- fresh thyme
- smoked paprika
Very unusual and absolutely delicious! I will be making this spicy version again.

this sounds amazingly similar to Norwegian fish soup, down to the butter, potatoes, fish stock and milk.
Fiskesuppe.
Really good. Used bacon and cod. Needed heat. Added Cholulu because that is what I had. Tabasco would have been better.

Yes, this works. Sam supllies the basics (that you really already know) and I trusted my instincts! I added some white miso, which I stole from another NYC recipe and it turned out rather good. Excellent!

We loved this recipe!  I'm an excellent recipe cook.  I can take a recipe and adjust, add, subtract, and substitute.  This was a great opportunity to have a little structure and push me a bit outside of my comfort zone.  It turned out delicious, wasn't fussy, and boosted my confidence!  Always love reading the comments/suggestions from others.  So helpful!
superior no-recipe clam chowder modeled on these instructions, with leeks, potatoes, chopped surf clams, cream and a handful of raw bay scallops added to each bowl just before serving

Even easier, buy "Imagine Potato Leek Soup" as a base and add the rest minus the potatoes or add a couple of steamed or microwaved potatoes chunks for texture. Add carrot slices and wine/stock as needed. Simmer till carrots are soft- 20 minutes.  I use haddock, frozen is fine for this. Serve with olive oil garlic toast. Inspired by the bouride served at Cafe Des Artistes in NYC so many years ago.It was unforgettable. It had saffron in it as well. Drizzle with olive oil!!
Excellent. I love just throwing things into a pot. I used white wine and chicken broth with left over sushi fish (yellow tail and salmon) and with the pimenton--terrific.

To any other noob out there, maybe salmon isn't the right choice on the first time out.  My broth was delicious (peppers, onions, carrots, potatoes, 4 oz of chicken broth with the water -- my cooking wine had gone bad, so I skipped that -- and I added a little bit of sherry vinegar.) But once I added about half the fish suggested here, it was a lot, lol.  If I were to do this again with salmon, which I won't, I would add probably 1/16 of what Sam suggests here, because it was, uh, flavorful.
You can leave out the milk/half and half and have something like Rhode Island clam chowder. To thicken you can mash some of the potatoes. I have also used potato starch to thicken chowder.
I didn’t have fish stock so I used two cups clam juice, 1 cup white wine, and water.

This was great. Didn't use onions, did use carrots and celery. Cod, a can of clams, miso broth, clam juice, and white wine for the broth. Added some collard greens. Paprika and turmeric. Coconut milk instead of milk or cream. Next time will add more clams and some shrimp.

Made this for the first time tonight. Used 6 baby red potatoes, 3 carrots, a small onion, 2 cups vegetable stock, 1 cup white wine, about half a bottle of clam juice, and half a pound of cod. Also threw in some thyme and parsley. Wife and I both loved it. Next time, maybe fewer potatoes, more liquid, and maybe a pound of fish.
Only thing I did different is that we were given a pound of fresh wax beans from a friend's garden, so I used those instead of corn.  Came out great!  Thanks for the no-recipe recipe, Sam!
Not enough broth for me. I will try increasing liquid next time. Perhaps to 1/2 inch above vegetables? Or 6 cups, maybe even 7.

I have tried several variations of this soup and it truly is customizable! Everything but the kitchen sink kind of deal. I have made this as suggested by @Lizhobbins but i have also made this with fish sauce and gochujang (spicy) for those cold winter nights. My only suggestion is salt towards the end instead of as you go. I’ll post some recipes of my favorite variation if anyone is interested. Happy Cooking :)

This is exactly the way I like to cook, and we only had to pick up fish and cream. We had a bunch of fun things already in the fridge. Next time, I will use bacon. Thanks to everyone for mentioning. Not sure how I could have missed the bacon! And I will skip carrots. It’s true that they add a sweetness that you don’t really want. Instead we’ll use fennel. 

We used carrots, celery, onion, a smidge of garlic, a pinch of smoked paprika, homemade crab stock (that was perfect and so flavorful!), fingerling potatoes, a few chopped green chard leaves, semi-dry white vermouth, heavy cream, and snapper. 

My Swedish partner declared it a triumph! We will definitely make it again!

Nice based to riff off of. I used coconut milk as advised by another commenter and went for an Asian variation, added a small chili pepper, a splash of fish sauce and fresh corn kernels sliced off the cob - very good!

Oh, this is a keeper for sure. A bit of bacon, onion, celery, carrots worked well. And a terrific use of the white fish “cut-ups” packs I get with my Alaskan seafood subscription. Very rich - just a generous cup with a crust of bread made for a very satisfying meal.
I got fine particles after adding milk.  Was it wrong to have allowed the milk to boil?  I tried it with tilapia, which almost disintegrated upon boiling; salmon and halibut worked well, though.

yeah, the no-recipe "recipe" says to reduce the heat to a simmer. so when he says let it "bubble along" he means simmer, not boil, but i can see how this can be confusing. (i hope that didn't come off sarcastically, just wanted to help :)) just let it simmer gently so dairy will stay creamy and the fish will stay in tact.
Excellent and easy. Added red pepper flakes and that was a good idea. Also thyme.

This recipe is quick and easy. I used chicken broth and white wine. Also threw in a small chopped jalapeño pepper, as we like our food spicy, and the smoked paprika. I skipped the chopped bacon and simply started the dish with high-quality olive oil. A great recipe on a cold, snowy night in winter.
Tossed in a couple extras to clean out the fridge...couple handfuls of fresh spinach, handful of spicy green beans. Delicious. Love this unfussy, stuff you have on hand anyway recipe.
I made this with cod and a combination of clam juice and white wine and it turned out really well. I would definitely make again while omitting the corn (just a personal preference).

I was nervous about this recipe - it seemed too easy and off-the-cuff.  I need not have worried.  The flavor is amazing for so few ingredients.  I did make it a point to find fish broth, started with the bacon and used heavy cream for thickening.  At the end I added about1/4 cup of chopped parsley.  The fish I used is known as trigger fish - a very firm, bony, but flavorful variety that we had caught last summer.  This recipe is a keeper!

Ahhh this recipe was so good!! I did exactly as instructed but added a couple of cloves of garlic and a shallot or two. The fish stock I found was way overpriced so I used clam juice instead, still very good. I also added in a couple of sprigs of thyme I had on hand while the soup was simmering. Also, probably overdid it slightly on the wine as it had a kinda sweet flavor, but still tasted delicious!!

This was indeed speedy, but oh so tasty!
Thank You Sam and readers commenting.

I had some of the ingredients, but not all .... so I riffed as per readers suggestions.

Used cod, butter, onion, clam juice/water/white wine combo. Added 3 previously cooked potatoes, and a big squirt of lemongrass paste, and S&P of course. Delicious!

It's not Sam's no-recipe, but close enough. Can' wait to try again with ingredients called for.
a fine non-recipe
Cook bacon until crisp, then add onions. Wegmans seafood stock is really good. Added some cornstarch slurry to thicken at end.

Fantactic. Started with shallots and scalloped red bay potatoes in olive oil. Red pepper flakes, salt, pepper, cayenne pepper. White wine. Heavy cream. Then fish. Then added cannelloni brand and Italian parsley.

I used chicken stock for the liquid, plus 2% reduced-fat milk, and they both worked well. I had some frozen cod chunks, which I threw in at the end. They quickly thawed and broke apart in the broth. This soup is very easy and tasty.
Just reviewing all the notes, if you are looking for a very good potato soup just drop the fish, an either or recipe!  Thanks.
I usually have a can of clams in the pantry, what do you think about throwing them in (instead of clam juice)?

Here in Maine you leave out the carrots and paprika. It's also traditional to make Chowder with canned milk. Sound horrible? It's delicious. And if a real Mainer is making it, there's a 95%  chance it will be made that way. Rendered salt pork bits are made available to sprinkle on your chowder. And for some (not me) fist-crunched oyster crackers go in just before eating. I suspect the canned milk custom evolved from island living where cows might not have been available.
Easy, delicious, I had clam juice some prawns and rockfish....this is a must cook!

1st attempt: soup, used clam liquid + coconut milk, used cream to thicken -- will add white wine and use heavy cream next time. vegi, used onion, celery and okra. okra gives the soup a slimy texture, it seems to help thicken the soup, but maybe not for everyone. To avoid the sliminess, avoid cutting into the pod when trimming the stem and add towards the end before fish and clam. I used rockfish this time, will try tilapia next time. Add heat and serve w/ lime wedge.
Made it exactly as directed with good ingredients, then threw it away because it was tasteless and unsalvageable. An expensive waste of time.
Delicious

Used package of gravad lax
 from Ikea instead of fresh fish. It added a good salty note, and the dill was a lovely flavor.

Made a similar non recipe for years substituting shrimp for cod and adding whatever colorful veggies were available...zucchini, cherry toms, red pepper. The must have is a pack of Goya Sazon for color and flavor. Great for a company for dinner starter.
Add fresh or frozen cauliflower.....wonderful

My father was from Maine. My mother was from Wisconsin. This chowder is very similar to what he used to call his “dowry”, along with a recipe for real Maine baked beans.   I’ve been making both to rave reviews for my whole adult life.

About how long will it take to cook after I reduce the heat?  “Until liquid has reduced by half” is such a frustrating instruction, even for a loosely goosey non-recipe.  I end up dipping in a wooden chopstick and making a pencil mark.
Would dashi be a good stock?  Miso or vegetable better?

In the UK it is easy to find smoked haddock which can replace 1/4 of the cod and all of the bacon. The smoky taste makes this an amazing soup. A bit of fresh (not smoked) salmon adds a nice flavour. To be honest I often use a bag of frozen British "fish pie" mix which is mostly cod and 1/4 part each of salmon and smoked haddock or pollock.  Works perfectly.

Easy "recipe" to follow and delicious. Chicken stock with a splash of white wine works well as a liquid, better than water. Regular paprika is good if you don't have smoked. But cream or half and half is better than milk for sure. Add celery to veg as well.
Delicious and easy. But it took a long time for the carrots & potatoes to cook at a simmer. Consider cooking them at a bit higher heat.
Made this tonight. It was delightful! Used tilapia and added a red bell pepper, some shakes of Old Bay, and several dashes of liquid smoke. Delicious!

Easy.  That's the way I like things.  If you aren't afraid to wing it and judge from how the veg, potatos, cream/broth is doing - this is an easy recipe.  Follow how the dish is coming along and you will do great.  My wife and teen-agers really enjoyed this during the Lock Down in Oregon - Corona home cooking time.
About how long does it take? Wondering specifically how long it takes for the liquid to reduce. I am thinking about 30min?
