Wish I'd crisped the bacon first; otherwise very good. I used shrimp broth (saved from another recipe),added chard and kale fresh from garden plus a squeeze of lemon. And some chiles (because I like everything spicy.....

When I was 19, I lived in Melbourne Florida and I was on a very tight budget. My boyfriend and I would go to the Indialantic River with our clam rake and get buckets of cherrystone clams. I'd make chowder every week for just a few dollars worth of vegetables, potatoes, milk and such. So good and so cheap and easy.
Don’t forget Maine’s favorite: Old Bay Seasoning
Maryland! Old Bay is from Maryland!

Base: butter, bacon, chorizo, canned anchovies
Then added: onion, celery, carrot
Simmered in: white wine, water
2nd simmer: heavy cream
Filet used: monkfish
Topped with: cilantro (don't have parsley) and a squeeze of lemon to cut the fatty mouthfeel

Would skip the chorizo, add less butter and heavy cream next time...but very tasty.

I made it last night and it was easy and absolutely delicious, but I strongly recommend adding garlic (like 5 cloves), celery, parsley and cayenne pepper to round out the flavors.  For the liquids, we put a combination of veggie stock, milk, sour cream and white wine.

Tried this today and it came together fast. I felt comfortable enough to substitute with ingredients I had on hand - onions, carrots, celery, potatoes, a piece of halibut, a small piece of rockfish, butter, white wine, some better than bullion lobster seasoning, canned corn and a half cup of heavy cream. Seasoned with salt, pepper, park and a bay leaf. I'd lighten up on the salt next time and use less of the bullion mixture. But still, very tasty and my very first chowder.
Delicious! I added jalapeños, bell peppers, fresh cilantro & oregano, coconut milk and garlic.
This was terrific using about a pound of cod. I did add the leaves of three stalks of thyme before serving, but otherwise followed the non-instructions.

This was great inspiration. I used vegetable broth, chopped a small spicy pepper, onion and lemon grass for flavoring then added potato, coconut milk, ling cod and salmon.  The fish poaches and falls apart. The salmon skin gave some extra flavor while cooking, then was easily removed once the fish was cooked.  Yum.  A thousand and one ways to delicious.  Also, great way to use some fish that may have been in the freezer too long.

During the time of Covid pandemic quarantining, we are having the groceries delivered and maybe that's why the frozen salmon I ordered was too "fishy" when I cooked it by itself. I decided it might work better as part of this chowder. The only other "fish" was frozen shrimp. But it turned out delicious!! For fish stock, I used a cube of shrimp bouillon (caldo de camarrón) from the Mexican section of the grocery store. Try it; it worked well.
I love these no-recipe recipes!

This was delish and easy. Used pancetta as a base and added some bacon grease to the oil to sauté the veggies.  Use about 1.25 lb of cod and lots of Yukon golds and generous hit of smoked paprika and fresh thyme. Added cream at the end and garnished bowls with 1 T of Greek yogurt.
We absolutely loved this! Made with some tilapia fillets from Publix and this is definitely going in the regular rotation!

I made this without fish (I know - but lockdown/necessity...) and it was still super tasty with bacon, onion, potato, carrot, fish stock, a lot of corn and (on balance too many) jalapenos. Was also out of milk (asian household - almost never have it in!) so stirred in a bit cream cheese. Looking forward to making again with fish!

Used white wine and clam juice for the liquid. If only I’d had some bacon or smoked paprika! But alas, my cupboard, in this time of Corona, is lacking. It came out delicious nevertheless and reminded me of the chowder my mother used to whip together in minutes.
When I openned the fish, it had gone sour, so I subbed a can of chopped clams.  Otherwise, I made it pretty much as written. It was a major hit!

I'm a big soup fan, and chowder is one of our favorites.  I sautéed mira poix with a Tablespoon of butter, dashi for the fish broth, a bay leave, smoked paprika, salt, pepper, dash of cream, fresh corn, about a cup of broccoli slaw and fresh cod.

2 slices bacon
Medium onion
10 baby red potatoes
2 carrots
Kosher salt and black pepper
Smoked paprika (which I think made a HUGE difference)
1 C frozen corn
4 C fish stock
.5 C white wine
.25 C heavy cream
8 oz cod
Also used fresh stripped corn cobbs in the broth, then added the kernels in with the fish.

Loosely followed this plan using the leftovers from a mixed seafood boil. 
Sautee diced red bell pepper, carrot, onion, and celery alongside of your leftover cut up andouille sausage
When the onion is translucent, throw in the corn (sliced off the cob) and potatoes (chopped up fairly small)
Mash it around, and add pepper and smoked paprika
Pour in the leftover boiling liquid
Simmer and let reduce 
Pour in some cream and let it thicken a little
Toss in your seafood! I used mostly shrimp.
Delish!

Like the open nature of this recipe... to that end: added chopped fresh greens at the end (garden chervil, garlic chive leaves, italian parsley...). Nice to have a little green in the bowl, and the freshness of the greens adds a v. nice note, offsetting the richness. Also: no fish but lots of scallops, so went with those -- needless to say, fabulous. No cream, but sour cream stirred in after it went off the flame -- just as good as fresh, and maybe better.
this is great. Easy, tasty, healthy. Use 2% or full fat milk. Throw in some baby spinach. Probably all sorts of other vegetables would work. I used half pound of scrod and it made enough for 4.

I'm glad to now have this recipe in my back pocket. Quick, versatile, and delicious. In the spirit of a non-recipe (and because I didn't have everything to make it as written), I did some substitutions. Leek and shallot instead of onion, sockeye salmon chunks and frozen/thawed shrimp for the protein, and half/half and a little sour cream  for the dairy, and it came out tasty. Happily had the leftovers for dinner a few nights in a row.

My spouse likes chowder really thick. So I make a roux with the onions and bacon fat, and add a can of evaporated skim milk to make a thick cream sauce. After the potatoes and carrots are cooked in your choice of liquid (for me it’s milk, white wine, and water) I scrape the cream sauce into the pot and add the fish. The fish poaches as the broth thickens.

just made this and I love it! And my mom, who “doesn’t like fish that tastes like fish” loves it too!
I sautéed 2 sliced leeks, 1/2 an onion diced, and two stalks fine dice celery in 1 T bacon fat. White pepper. Deglaze w/dry sherry. Add peeled diced red potatoes and a small bottle of clam juice plus twice that amount of water and a can drained corn and about 1/3 c leftover bacon, minced. A glug of sherry, a cup or two of chicken stock. 3 fish filets, a (drained) can of clams. Two glugs cream.

Wow!  Made this tonight - stuck true to formula.  Used 3 cups seafood stock and 1/2 cup half and half.  Used  tiny potatoes, 2 carrots, half an onion, frozen corn, added leftover green peas.  Used two Des Allmands catfish filets - cut into chunks - salt and peppered them.  added 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika  but could up that i think.  Perfect.  Husband ate it over rice. Definite repeater.

Delicious, easy and fast. Made it with cod, chicken stock and a few slugs of half and half at end.  As someone noted, a little green would be a nice contrast and a scattering of chopped parsley before serving does the trick.
Don’t bother cutting the fish in chunks before adding to the pot. It will do that naturally as it cooks. A winner.

Made this yesterday! First rain of the season in Santa Cruz, CA! Perfect for a soup. I loved this recipe and so did my husband who is from Maine so I felt a little nervous making a fish chowder for him. I like how you have the base but then can improvise to your whim and on hand ingredients. So thank you everyone for chiming those in, is always helpful to me.

Well, this no-recipe recipe was fun!  FYI-for the broth I used water, white wine, vermouth and a splash of soy sauce!  Added a few tblsp of heavy cream and a bit of milk at the end.  When cooking was done, I let it rest for 5 minutes or so.  We've made this a number of times now and it's so good to have a quick fish chowder recipe.

Delish!!!
Added 1/2 tin of anchovies early on. That helped broth develop fishy taste. Also added I can of clams at the same time I added fish. 

Served with home made drop biscuits

Great, fast weeknight dish.  Used Yukon gold creamer potatoes and peas instead of carrots.  Used rock cod for the fish.  No fish stock and would have used clam juice but was out of it so just used homemade chicken broth.  This is the kind of dish that you can modify over and over depending on what you have in your refrigerator and pantry.
A tiny bit of liquid smoke really transforms this -I use fish and sauce leftover from Ina Garten's mustard roasted fish recipe, new potatoes from my garden, chicken broth, bacon, onions, hot pepper

We used vegetable stock, and added red peppers & creamed corn (from another comment) and it was fantastic.  The only thing I'd do differently next time is saute the bacon until crisp and crumble on top along with a little parsley just before serving.

This was delicious! I added the bacon and used vegetable stock, lots of dill, garlic, jalapeño and a bit of a slurry to thicken it up. I measured nothing, which I simply loved. Just felt it out. For the fish, I added two hot smoked salmon fillets I needed to eat. It’s a totally different texture than lox, and more like regular chunks of salmon fillet but very smoky, which added a good depth of flavor.

So good and simple. I used some odds and ends that had been sitting in the freezer - a few scallops, half a bag of shrimp, a single filet of cod. Used veggie stock and clam juice, with a little white wine vinegar to add a bit of acidity.

I've made this chowder many times and it is wonderful.  In fact, I am about to make it again.  Since we are away, the recipe is not with me so I am here on NYTimes Cooking to print it out.  Thank you for being there for me! My no-recipe recipe included butter but no bacon, onions, carrots, potatoes, salt, pepper, smoked paprika (but not too much), frozen corn, fish bouillon and vegetable bouillon, water, splash of vermouth, milk and a splash of cream,  and haddock.

Outstanding. 
Used 3 or 4 small red potatoes, 2 medium-ish carrots, 1\2 small onion and 3 strips of bacon, 2 cups fish stock and 1\2 pound haddock. Instead of the paprika, I used Penzey's "Seafood Base" (made with clams, shrimp, lobster and crab!). 

On the second round I might cube rather than dice the potatoes so they can be cooked a little longer. Diced, they cooked quickly enough that there wasn't much time to reduce the stock without the potential for everything becoming mush.

I made this with hake, smoked salmon and bay scallops. When it came time to put in the milk, I discovered that all I had was 1%. I added some butter to enrich it a bit. Veggies were good. Flavors and textures were interesting. Good non-recipe.
Very nice. Used boxed seafood stock (circa 2 cups) and white wine (1 cup) for the liquid, and threw in some frozen shrimp along with the fresh cod at the very end. Easy and delicious!
I have done similar chowders but instead of only water, I reduce the water and use coconut milk or evaporated ( not condensed) milk.
I add a cup of heated non-fat half and half. Towards the end, I add 2 scrambled egg yolks and a pat of butter and heat it all together. I serve with chopped parsley or chives on top.
does the type of fish matter? I have tilapia...
The non-recipe calls for FISH. Use whatever you have/like. You don't need permission from anyone.

Didn't have any fresh fish, but had some smoked salmon that needed to be eaten - so made smoked salmon chowder (skipped the bacon) and threw in some frozen langoustine tail meat. Used fennel and onions for the base, so delicious!

I made this for the first time and followed the recipe, using up leftover shrimp shell stock (ala David Tanis), potatoes and frozen corn. I augmented with a bit of white wine and store bought fish stock. I used one lb. butterfish. It was delicious...even though I forgot to add cream!

Wonderful chowder! Started with a piece of smoked salmon and put in with onions, carrots, potatoes and turnips. Added smoked paprika with salt, pepper and bay leaf. Cooked about an hour, then added cod and cream.
I was shocked to find I didn't have an onion, but it was awesome anyway.  I used fresh salmon and a little whipping cream, which I added after I filled my bowl so it wouldn't curdle or something.
Made with 2 cups each radish, parsnip, celery, onion, chicken stock; 1 cup bell pepper, white wine; about a cup of diced smoked salmon trim, heavy cream, lard.

Used LizHobbins guidelines below but didn't use bacon since it was a Lenten Friday, for spices used 1/2t thyme, 1/2t smoked paprika and 1/8 t cayenne.  Used another suggestion for 1 cup white wine, 1 bottle (8oz) claim juice and 1 c water.  Used two pieces of frozen tilapia that i thawed and added uncooked for the final few minutes and 1 can chopped clams. Finished with 1/3c heavy cream.  Very good and perfect Friday meal with a salad and bread.

This recipe is easy and awesome! I was afraid that I overdid it with too many vegetables, but it turned out great. Kept it simple my first time and used cod and Yukon gold potatoes with seafood stock, onions and carrots, and did the reckless thing and included butter and bacon (why not?). We enjoyed it on a cool Sunday night with biscuits. Young son gave it a 10/10. Perfect.
I made this twice with hake, bay scallops, shrimp and smoked salmon. Also potatoes, carrots, celery and mixed vegetables. Delicious. And easy
I decided to add Patis (fermented fish sauce) and I used far too much. I will try again without it.

This is a nyt classic, up there with its plum torte recipe! I avoided the cream & added crushed tomatoes, veggie broth, wine & can of beans to fish.  Is easy, very tasty & wonderful for sopping up sauce with great bread.
Wowee. Made as directed with pre-diced pancetta and onions, Bar Harbor canned fish stock. So delicious!!  I'm hooked!

Easy and quick.  Delicious as is.  Used the potatoes cubed, fresh corn, carrots, onion, seafood stock and white wine.  Only addition, chopped celery.  Also added chopped parsley and celery leaves at the end along with the heavy cream and a clog of vermouth.  Will make it again.
Especially nice with smoked haddock.  (transplanted NYSer living in UK)
10/2/21 made very good

I cook onions and a bit of celery until soft but not browned. Add broth of choice, potatoes sliced, thyme, salt and pepper. When potatoes are cooked add fresh uncooked fish filet of choice, here it is haddock, it will flake in a few minutes. Add milk with bit of cream and heat. Add a flake of butter, adjust salt and pepper, before serving. If you have excellent fish very fresh you may not want to confuse the delicate flavor.
This is delicious and very easy.  I’ve riffed many ways on it.  Yum yum yum.
Had shrimp on hand, so I made it with that, and it was delicious. No bacon, and I didn’t miss it at all. It’s great to have an easy to memorize fish chowder recipe in my back pocket now.
Also puréed half the soup to make it extra creamy, and we loved it.

Had double the amount of fish I ordered (instacart error) & decided to make this spur-of-the-moment for dinner (2 people). Half small vidalia onion, 4 tiny  potatoes, 4 mushrooms, butter and olive oil, water, dry white wine, plenty of black pepper, sumac, smoked paprika, bay leaf and mint. About 1/2 c. frozen roasted corn from TJ's, 0.7 lb tilapia. At end added about 1/4 c half&half. Next time I'll remember celery. Excellent.

Would this still be good without the milk/cream?
Has anyone tried it?
Thanks!
Absolutely!
It would not be chowder without milk/cream.
If you want it "creamy" without adding dairy, use a stick blender to mush up some of the potatoes.

I've been making a version of this for years, and thought I'd made it up myself.  I always add some cumin, along with the bay leaf, but I might try smoked paprika next time.  I also use Better than Bullion veggie broth (the fake chicken kind).  My version just has onions, potatoes and fish and no dairy.  It's really really good.
Is there any particular reason that this has to be made in a Dutch oven?
You can use any stock pot, but be sure it speaks Dutch.

Celery with the carrots, onions, and potatoes, then a big splash of white wine to clean the pot. Can of green chiles (or half a can if they aren't mild), clam juice (2 bottles) and enough water, 2 10oz cans of baby clams , 1 can diced tomatoes, no milk....So good. This was my first No Recipe Recipe at the NYT and right up my alley. Couldn't get cod or haddock, so had to settle for tilapia. Will make again.

I made this last night with a package of frozen mahi mahi. I added some vegetable broth cubes, Old Bay seasoning and adobo to the soup for more flavor. When everything was bubbling, I added the semi-frozen slabs of mahi to the chowder and just broke it up in the pot. Before serving I added slightly less than a 1/4 cup of coconut milk. It was delicious!

Fast, delicious, satisfying. Used combo boxed chick broth and cheap white wine 1:1. Used a bit more than a splash of half and half and used 1# fresh cod. Tempered 1/2 n 1/2 by warming it in microwave and ladling a cup or two of soup into cream before returning all to pot. Cut cod into small pieces as directed--done in 3 mins. Adjusted salt and hit the soup with a lot of fresh black pepper. Felt like I could eat the whole pot by myself!

March 2022. Made this with adding leeks first, then carrots, parsnip, gold potatoes, then garlic and spices: turmeric, urfa biber, smoked paprika. Added homemade
Rotisserie chicken soup, 1 c or more white wine, some water, bay leaf, fish sauce, anchovies. When veg cooked, added, chopped dill and chopped parsley, also labne that first gets tempered with hot soup slowly. Then added back in to soup. Also check salt content. Really delicious.
Sensational for so fast. Left out the creme inadvertently but the broth was still to treasure. Added an extra bay leaf.
This is incredibly easy and delicious.
Anyone know if you can freeze the leftovers of this seafood chowder? Wasn’t sure if this would reheat well or not.

We loved my version of this chowder. I used bacon fat to fry the onions, plain water, frozen grilled corn, and hot Spanish smoked paprika. And I added some kale so that I wouldn't have to cook a green vegetable. We ate it with home made skillet corn bread. Thank you for the idea and guidance.

Really good fish stew.  Based on earlier comments, I added an anchovy to the veggies and, at the end, celery leaves, parsley and a little diced red bell pepper for color and taste. I made a mistake and added the milk without tempering it, so it curddled slightly. But, it was absolutely delicious. I'll certainly make it again.

Delish! Omitted the bacon, and found it needed a bit of salt to bring up the flavor. Veggies: celery, onion, carrots, corn, waxy potatoes. Seafood: 1# cod and 9 fat shrimp. Used the shrimp shells to make a quick stock w/ wine and water. Added a splash of fish sauce and a hit of tumeric and thyme. Went for it with 1/2 pint of whipping cream. We were short a person for dinner, and the lucky 14yo called dibs on the leftovers for his lunch, so I guess that's a good meal. Thanks for the idea, Sam.

Made on a weeknight with tilapia. Use the large stockpot to have enough room. Half and half separates a bit while fish cooks, add at the very end. Carrots add nice sweetness. Don’t need as much fish with all that starch.

Blend for a few seconds with an immersion blender / and or potato masher to thicken up the soup a bit and give it a chowder consistency. Don’t overblend, just do it long enough to purée a portion of the soup and stir it in.

Used our left over broiled fish (our first wolf fish and a piece of white fish), confidently followed many of your suggestions.  It WAS a quick prep, good use of food on hand and delicious!  Thank you, Sam!  I just read several of the comments and will try other suggestions in the future...anchovies, etc!

I used a mix of halibut, salmon, shrimp, and small scallops.  Definitely use bacon and butter, fish stock and a half cup or so of dry white wine.  All else the same.  AMAZING, i was literally sopping up the bottom with the crusty baguette.
made twice = just great!
Good! I used canned clams and wine for liquid.
Small diced tomatoes and sprinkled over the top before serving. Added nice acidity.

I added a packaged of shredded coleslaw mix, turmeric and smoked paprika with a can of full fat coconut milk and BONUS just bought a lemongrass plant so added some of that for flavor too. Can’t wait to see how it turns out!
This was much too sweet for me!  I used leeks - so that could have been contributed. Some lemon or hot sauce at the end helps -  but overall a sweet combo of flavors in this chowder.
Make sure to let bacon get more on crispy side.
Curry was a happy addition.

I agree that this is totally amazing!  I love the fact that it encourages - nay even requires - creativity.  I added some lovage to give it a slight celery vibe.  This will definitely be part of my standard repertoire with a different twist each time according to mood, garden, and pantry.

Oh my goodness!

Bacon
Onion
Carrots 
Potatoes 
Sauté a bit

Clam Juice:White Wine:water
A splash of Dry Madeira
Salt, Pepper, Smoked Paprika, Thyme, Bay Leaf

Simmer until potatoes are tender

Add fish/shrimp
Adjust seasonings 


Heavenly!




H

Really out of my comfort zone to just “make it up” LOL, but this was delicious. Made enough for two hungry people on a really cold night. 2 slices bacon Medium onion 10 baby red potatoes 2 carrots Kosher salt and black pepper Smoked paprika (which I think made a HUGE difference) 1 C frozen corn 4 C fish stock .5 C white wine .25 C heavy cream 8 oz cod

Was looking for something easy and special for Christmas Eve.  Made a few adjustments to Sam's recipe — coconut milk instead of cream.  Added rich mushroom stock to clam juice, white wine and water.  Enhanced the cod with shrimp.  Just potatoes, no carrots or corn.  It was delicious, layered and smooth.  Will definitely make it again.  Thanks NY Times and the many readers who added helpful comments.

We've made this a couple of times. Always, love it!!
I love the general guidelines because they give space for inspiration to change things a bit.
Can this be made with frozen fish fillet or do I need to defrost them first?
Adding frozen anything to a pot of boiling or simmering liquid is always a mistake. The frozen items will disrupt the cooking process, and unlikely with any benefit. Defrost first.

I've made a couple dozen recipes from the NYT, and this is my favorite so far. Red onion, carrots, potato sautéed in bacon fat. A cup of frozen corn. A quart of our homemade canned broth. Paprika, salt, pepper (and a dash of fish sauce for good measure). Two Bay leaves, and fresh Thai basil (because we had it) Cooked down, added half and half. A handful of frozen shrimp and four pollock filets chopped into bite sized pieces. Served with garlic toast. Delicious!
My go-to when I want to impress.  The smoked paprika makes a difference.  The recipe is very forgiving and am all time favorite!!!
I added a small can of green chilies and this really helped with flavor.
Really good.  I added some red pepper, served with cornbread.

Excellent; added a jalapeño to the mix for a little heat against the creaminess. Was truly excited to eat leftover for lunch the next day, something not usual for fish leftovers in our household. Was even better after sitting overnight.
Easy, salmon, cod. Shrimp, tilapia all good

When I make fish chowder, I add some kombu (kelp) - a piece that's maybe about 2"x3" - with the liquid. Let it cook a bit and soften, then remove and dice (medium-fine) and add back. Adds a great hint of the ocean that compliments the fish really well.
Does anyone have any thoughts about using saffron?  I remember loving a fish soup with most of these ingredients as well as saffron.

This is one of our favorite NYT recipes.  It's different every time.  For this week, I used quite a bit of cod, added a few shrimp (I find that they add nice flavor and a bit of color--I cut them up), some corn, lots of carrots, a bit of milk, seafood stock and white wine.  The smoked paprika, as some have said, is key.  I never cut up the fish before adding, as flaky fish just falls apart during the cooking.  This is a nice healthy meal, with a salad.  Love the no-recipe recipes!

My thanks to Liz Hobbins who put down a road map on this one.  Sam's directions were clear, and with Liz's suggestions in terms of quantity, this was a breeze and so delicious.  This recipe is now on rotation at our house, and is much anticipated on our 'Soup Sundays'.  Great to prepare and enjoy while watching football.
There's no recipe!!!

Anne, it states right at the top... this is a no recipe recipe, something that Sam does every once in a while. Read the intro! If you really really need a list of ingredients, hit the "most helpful" button. The top response has a good ingredient list. It has over 760 "is this helpful" up votes. Enjoy your fish chowder!

I make a similar fish chowder, but omit the bacon and milk  to make it vegetarian and more like Rhode Island chowder. If I have it, I use the liquid from jarred gefilte fish for the base or other fish liquid. If not, water and perhaps a little white wine. Delicious.
Where is the recipe?  All I'm seeing is a headline, a photo, and comments (??)

No fish stock? Try this. Add about 4 cups of water to a pot big enough that it's about one inch deep. Add a rack if you have one, (not critical). Steam a dozen, (or more), little neck clams until they open. Use the steaming liquid for fish stock, (clam broth). Add the steamed clams at the very end to the chowder just to warm them up.
I love a recipe that gives the basic idea and leaves me on my own to check the fridge, freezer and pantry.

For the win! Onions, carrots, Fennel bulb sliced thin, canned corn, kale, smoked paprika, salt, pepper, red pepper flakes, thyme and dill...white wine, a dollop of yogurt since I didn't have milk. No potatoes bec of the carbs. Excellent cod was perfect! Next time, anchovies or coconut milk but not both...so good and easy.

Amazing recipe -family loved it!
The tips to add clams in juice, bacon, cream, butter, and a little anchovy were helpful.  Added extra hot paprika, pepper, cayenne and a little cayenne, too.  Used spaetzel instead of potato.
Loved it.  Great comfort food.  Great with garlic bread!  THANKS NYT.

I pretty much threw the kitchen sink at this: bacon and butter (yum) diced red and yellow pepper, 4 cloves diced garlic, chopped potato, frozen peas toward the end (had no corn) and leftover cooked cod from the night before. I used white wine and water. Amazing and gone very quickly.
Used Burlap and Barrels “Smoke n Herb” seasoning, lobster bullion, white wine, carrots,onions,red peppers, potatoes,

I grill the fish filets whole briefly to give them a char and then cut them into rough chunks. I also use roasted corn kernels to punch up the smokey flavors. I recently discovered culantro and have started adding it to a variety of dishes. It's butch cilantro.

This also makes excellent “runny” potato soup.  Add some diced carrots/celery, more potatoes, extra milk, maybe a handful or two of cheese. My Kansas born, Depression raised grandma would add in a tin of oysters to the delight of her adult children at Christmas.  My dad quickly learned to substitute the oysters for Velveeta if he wanted to avoid a riot from us brats born in the 80s.  I now make it (sans Velveeta to appease my dairyman husband) when I need a reminder of what home tasted like…

Yes what kind of fish?  Don't different fish cook at  different rates based on thickness, fatty or not?  Are there fish you definitely shouldn't use?  

This is why I'm a baker.
Coconut milk is a great addition instead of milk or cream.
My mother taught me that it's not chowder if it doesn't include diced celery.
A great inspirational recipe. I used homemade fish broth I’d made from prawn shells so everything complimented each other.
Followed the recipe as is. Added some red pepper flakes and Jalapeños to get some heat but found it to be just okay

Thank you for making this easy! Done in 1/2 hour, this was a treat. A couple of shallots, some shrimp broth from the freezer, to which I added about 1/4 C. of flour, a can of octopus from Spain (Octopus was a surprise... I don’t speak Spanish.), fish sauce, anchovy paste, corn, potatoes, red bell pepper a handful of spinach, parsley and black pepper at the end. Oh, I used monkfish because that’s the best for chowder. I did cut it into chunks and they held together nicely.

It’s the end of a long 48hours providing care to a husband recovering from shoulder surgery, two preteens and a dog and I could not have coped with a recipe (and failing to fulfill it) so this was a dream. Used a bag of frozen fish pie mix, tin of corn, and the potatoes, spring onions and celery hanging out in the veg drawer. Couple of slices of bacon from the freezer. Spoonfuls of cream leftover from making ice cream for a dinner party. Feels like it was meant to be!
I loved this “recipe”- turned out so well for me! Lots of complements from my guests. Shows how versatile chowder can be depending on what you have in the pantry.
I've made this a few times to great success. My husband loves it. I use coconut milk, smoked paprika and a little cayenne. It's always great.

Four bacon slices —crisp before adding veg

Add lots of smoked paprika 
Pinch of cayenne

A little instant seafood soup base, not too much, it's salty. Korean or Chinese have heat.
Noilly Prat vermouth, keep it on the fridge door.
Any or all of the "fines herbes" for garnish. Parsley, chives, tarragon, chervil.

I’ve made this with cod twice in a week to clear out the freezer. The open approach and excellent results make me feel like a better cook!  We don’t do potatoes so one had a fennel base with onion and the other kale with leeks. Common elements were chicken stock, white wine, half and half, chorizo, plus chives, basil, scallions and parsley on top. The next variations will lean Asian, so thanks everyone for those tips.

Use 5 quart soup pot for double recipe:

4-5 pieces diced bacon plus 2 T butter
2 large red potatoes
6 carrots
1 large vidalia onion
4 cups water and 4 t chicken bouillon
1 bay leave
dash of garlic powder
Salt and pepper
1 cup cold whole milk w/2T cornstarch dissolved
1-1.75 lb fish

Simmer covered for 30 mins; add milk mixture until a bit thickened; add fish without cutting it up.
made this with leeks, raw fresh corn, carrots, vegetable broth, oat milk, cod, & tarragon, beyond delicious

Whoop! Sautéed leeks, carrots and some pancetta. Threw in the clam juice, white wine, Added half and half some turmeric and paprika...then some clams, some shrimp and halibut. So good! I am no longer afraid...lol....of making a fish soup.

I used Tilapia, leftover Fennel stalks and fronds instead of celery, onion, carrot, Better'n Boullion chicken flavor, and added miso and thyme sprigs in a sort of melding with Kay Chun's Miso Fish Chowder.  No bacon, forgot to add cream and didn't miss it--the broth was delicious and dippable.

I’ve made this a few times, it’s DELICIOUS and wonderful and feels so decadent but not heavy. Get whatever nice fish your fish monger has and let your senses and pantry/fridge guide you through the rest.
Used leeks instead of onions.  Highly recommended.

A perfect light dinner.  Found frozen halibut; used onion, carrots, anchovies, bay leaf, generous smoked paprika, several glugs of dry white vermouth, a can of chicken broth and a dash of milk.  Really tasty and very quick to cook.
Needs MORE flavor!
Great recipe!! I used fish sauce, turned out great.

To eat this at multiple meals, cook just enough seafood for a meal in the soup minus the milk. Heat just enough milk for that meal and add it directly to the dishes. Then you can re-use the soup to cook new fish for a later meal.
Made it essentially as written. Added 12 mussels along with the fish. Added an ear of corn. Used mostly clam juice, some  water and wine.  Dill at the end. Delicious!

title: Steak Tacos With Pineapple Salsa Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
I marinate with olive oil lime juice garlic cilantro and soy. 

The oil gives it a nice char while the rest give the meat and great flavor for the tortilla

No marinade, just salt, pepper and butter,  and the broiled steak was just...meh. Also the pineapple I bought was less than spectacular. I think this no-recipe recipe has a lot of potential to be fantastic but would be better executed during the summer months.
Use cotija cheese. Or pork and marinate differently
Cube the steak when done, so much easier to eat in tacos. Make sure to salt sufficiently, add lime juice to salsa. We used flank steak, needed more like 4 min/side
Be careful when using fresh pineapple with meat. It can turn meat to mush very quickly, a lesson learned the hard way when I added diced fresh pineapple to a pork quesadilla!
Served over thin strips of fried tofu with onions and spinach. Added a dash or two of cayenne and ancho chili powder and lime juice to the salsa

Grill pineapple slices before or along with the steak. Then chop and add to the taco with cotija cheese, lime juice, jalapenos and cilantro.  A little Tajin on the grilled pineapple make it even better. Or make the salsa with grilled pineapple.

Marinated 1lb of skirt steak with soy sauce, canola oil, minced garlic, cumin, and chili powder for a few hours. Grilled and diced up. Pineapple salsa was delicious and simple - just some fresh pineapple cubed with cilantro. Served meal with other taco fixings. Make this!
Tried it with entrecôte - very good!. Second time with pork - even better. I think pork and pineapple go together very well.
I whipped up a quick marinade with olive oil, lime juice, soy sauce, garlic, cilantro, and honey. Seared the steak in a cast iron skillet. Super easy and quick meal!

First time commenter after trying dozens of NYT recipes over the last 2 years of membership. Steak was almost inedibly tough as written. My husband’s response: “What I’d expect from NYT cooking.” We’ve been so underwhelmed by “recipes” like this and many others that try to be “fancy” when the family just wants classic good cooking. If you attempt this, definitely marinate it… or better yet find an actual recipe instead of whatever this is. Once again wondering, “Why am I paying for this membership?”
Held the jalapeño and both my children under age 5 loved this. Grilled corn and worked perfect as a side. Highly recommended.

Didn’t notice the direction to broil the steak and simply pan fried it. Delicious with the pineapple and jalapeño salsa. I suspect if I had broiled the steak it would have been less appetising as other comments have indicated.

title: Almond and Dried Fruit Pilaf With Rotisserie Chicken Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: The dried fruits and almonds are normally sauteed briefly before being mixed into the cooked rice so that the dish is not dry.

When I make this, I add a couple of teabags—chai or your favorite spiced tea—to the water when I add the rice. This perfumes everything without the grit of adding ground spices and you can pull them out easily at the end.
Such a great idea! Thanks for the tip.
This sounds very cool! I must say though I never suffer from grit of ground spices; I just turn over the rice a few times and blend in and it is fine.
Sam, are you cooking with aluminum pots and pans?
Replace water with chicken broth for more intense flavor
400° oven 1 cup rice one and a half cups of liquid 17 minutes
I will try! Does this give separate grains texture and if so, what kind of rice and covered or not?
This is a delicious go-to recipe in our house.  But to up the ante - Use chicken stock instead of water, and steep a large pinch of saffron threads in the stock before adding to the rice.

This is my go-to recipe for leftover rotisserie chicken. We usually eat a half Costco chicken one night, and then the other half is refrigerated, then meat is shredded/chopped and added to rice mixture when (apricots slices) fruit and almonds added. Also sometimes use sautéed mushrooms with the onions, and add baby spinach later on so it wilts when the whole shebang comes together. VERY good.

This idea would make a great tweak to Craig Claiborne's baked rice, in which you soften some chopped aromatics in a tablespoon of melted butter in a small pot (I use my littlest Le Creuset Dutch oven), mix a cup of raw white rice into it, stir in a cup and a half of warmed water or stock, and put it in a preheated 400 degree oven for exactly 17 minutes. Then you'd proceed according to these directions - no sweat and no appreciable mess to speak of.
I diced my rotisserie chicken and added it to the pilaf.
Used some garam masala asking with the onion, apricots and some unsweetened coconut (soaked in a little bit of water while the rice cooks) plus toasted slivered almonds to stir in. Delicious!
I would love to know how much and what kind of no piquante curry to add to this.  Does anyone know the proportion of curry to rice?
This has no curry in it.... instead it has *currants*.
Curry would be great and so would currants...how about both?!
Very good
I sometimes replace some of the water with mango juice.
This is also nice with chopped dried apricots and dried cherries as a substitution. For the almonds I like to use Trader Joe’s  toasted slivered almonds.
Really delicious when you stir in some of the chicken juices from the roasting pan before serving.

title: Steamed Mussels With Tomatoes and Chorizo Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: yes, but, once you've cooked everything, ALL the mussels will be dead. in a good way. you know, because you've cooked them. *before* you cook them, toss any that have broken shells or are open and don't close when you tap on em. those are dead in the bad way. if you have some that are closed when the rest have opened during cooking, leave those in the pot-they might just need a few extra mins. if they're still closed, pry them open and eat them. sometimes they're stubborn or they got frozen-nbd.
Solid, classic approach. Definitely add a lot of butter and you're good to go.
I love to add a bit of cream at the end.

I feel that cream mutes the flavors. I like the briny flavor of the mussels or clams on their own. Spanish chorizo or Portuguese chouriço are interchangeable and ready to eat. Mexican chorizo is raw and must be cooked before eating.
Easy and delicious. i added a can of good quality chopped tomatoes along with the cherry tomatoes for more sauce. I will be making this again.
No chorizo in the house so used about half a pound of bacon instead.  Added garlic, shallots and a can of Rotel along with a good slug of white wine.  Delicious!
I bet this recipe would work really well with clams too.
Added some white beans for some added texture. Delicious.

This turned out fantastic. Used about a half pound of ground chorizo to two pounds mussels, and it had enough kick on its own that more chili flakes weren’t really necessary (this will depend on your chorizo, so adjust accordingly). Shallots would do nicely here and I’ll likely use them next time in place of garlic. Chopped a fair bit of parsley but was so hungry I forgot to garnish with it—whoops!

Great series. I love fresh mussels and often grab a bag. I doubt I have cooked the same recipe for decades. 

Mussels just require moisture and heat and lend themselves to an almost infinite variety of improvised recipes.
What kind of chorizo should be used?  The soft Mexican kind in a tube or the more hardened, dry Spanish variety?
In the photo it looks like the hard Spanish chorizo.
The text says to cube the chorizo which would be impossible with Mexican chorizo, so Spanish chorizo is the right type.
Great!  I used 1/4 lb. of mild Italian sausage, otherwise prepared as suggested.  A 2-lb. bag of mussels and some garlic toast were more than enough for a meal for two people.

to two pounds of mussels, i used a third of a pound of raw chorizo and sauteed until crisp, three quarters of a cup of white bordeaux, c. three quarters cup of finely diced tomatoes from the garden, four cloves finely chopped garlic, and served with crostini rounds brushed with olive oil and lightly salted. vorzüglich!
Added shallot, half a jalapeño, a little saffron infused chicken broth and 2 TBL French butter.
please don't eat mussel's you've pried open unless you cook them after you pry them open.  Been there, done that, it wasn't pretty.
Used linguiça (no chorizo on hand) and a bottle of pilsner beer instead of wine. A dose of red pepper flakes. Very good. Would be better w/ a more robust beer.

I used too make this long ago at a San Francisco Tapas restaurant "Sol y Luna". We used a latin lager beer instead of the wine. Chili flakes are a good option too and grill the rustic bread for more smoky flavor.

Ingredients:
frozen cooked rice, a cup or so per person
frozen diced vegetables 
meat, such as chopped BBQ brisket, pastrami, ground pork, bacon, roast chicken
2 eggs
garlic, chopped
ginger, chopped
scallions, chopped
soy sauce and sesame oil (about a 3:1 ratio) make 1/8 cup of sauce for two

I work as a nanny for a preschooler and have been making this for my little girl for a long time. Like the others, I find bagged frozen veggies to be a Godsend. I also throw in some salad mix--there are so many non-lettuce choices--cabbage, broccoli, carrots, and yes, always onion. I do use scrambled egg, to add protein (or tofu). Then some sesame oil and soy sauce. Authentic? Nope. Delish? Yep.
Adding the salad mix is a great idea too! Gonna steal that, with your kind permission...

This is what Chinese moms do with leftovers. I don't bother with the ginger or garlic, though I'm sure it makes it more tasty. Scallions are enough for me, and it saves me the chopping time. My mom taught me to cook the eggs before throwing in the vegetables with the rice. But really, the best thing about this recipe is that cooking order doesn't matter. When I've served it to non-Chinese people, they are impressed and I am embarrassed since it's thrown together in 15 minutes from leftovers.

Chopping is hard for me (arthritis) so I am always up for easy “cheats” - I keep jars of garlic & ginger in my fridge. They’re pre-chopped and stored in olive oil, I believe. I’m sure there’s some other preservatives in there - but it’s worth it to me to not have to chop those two things ALL THE TIME since I put them in everything. Someone else also mentioned that Trader Joe’s sells FROZEN cubes of both.. perfect to toss into anything! I’ll definitely be checking that out, too!
Who knew something so simple could be so good? Having made it once I better get the concept of "throw in anything you have." I added some chopped peanuts on top for some crunch and loved that.

Overnight in the fridge rice, crumbled up by hand before you start (super important)... Very hot oil, I use peanut, in a non-stick skillet.  Dump in rice and push down into oil.  Once it's browning, do a "fold in" turn for all of it, then let it brown again.  Dump in your veggies... fold in again. Here, it starts to steam so get it "crunch y" beforehand.  Stir as needed to not burn... add in soy sauce, stir.  Well in the middle and eggs to cook them.  Plate it up... Yummy!!!

For those of you who believe that chilling the rice produces resistant starch, you are correct.  However, once you heat it up again, the resistant starch disappears, and it converts back to regular starch.  Just thought I would share that to clear up any confusion.

Disagree. I'd be curious to know where you got your info...? "Cooling rice after cooking may promote health by increasing the amount of resistant starch it contains. One study compared freshly cooked white rice to white rice that was cooked, refrigerated for 24 hours and then reheated. ... They found that eating the cooked then cooled rice led to a smaller blood glucose response." https://www.healthline.com/nutrition/cooling-resistant-starch https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/26693746

For my young adult children (and any newbie cooks):
1. Need a wok? Nope, a frying pan will do.
2. Cook the frozen veggies first? IMHO, a microwave thaw is a good idea.
3. No garlic, ginger or scallion? No problem! Some powdered spices work (or not). I used a half of a yellow onion in place of the scallions.
4. For meat, use the leftovers of Melissa Clark’s wonderful and simple salt & pepper roast chicken!

Finely diced onions.  Start with that every time, if you can.  Stir-fry along with  your meat.  Great use of leftover bacon fat, too, (use instead of oil). How do the calories go down when chilled? Also what is resistant starch compared to regular starch?

Kaya, resistant starch doesn't react as much in the body, so the glycemic response with cooked/chilled/reheated rice (or pasta) is lower -- a good thing. Contrary to what another posted here, reheating doesn't change resistant starch back to nonresistant starch. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/m/pubmed/26693746/

I put in the rice earlier so that it gets nice and crispy on the bottom. Chinese sometimes deliberately let the rice cook until stiff and brown, then scrape it up and mix it in. It's a special crunchy treat. I also toss in slivered kimchi at the end for zest.

Readers should note that the ingredients - ALL of them - that Sam listed are entirely substitutable and optional, except for RICE. By definition, "fried rice" requires rice and that it be fried. Anything else that goes in is optional. You don't even need eggs. And you certainly DON'T need soy sauce to make fried rice. In fact, soy sauce is NOT needed to "make something Chinese" (I hate that term "Asian". Asia is a BIG PLACE with myriads of cuisines)
Indeed, who could throw in the same bag with a straight face Philippino, Thai, Japanese, Korean, Cambodian cuisines ???

I didn't own a wok for years (silly me!) and just used a large skillet for stir fry cooking.  I believe a skillet is superior to a saucepan because it is easier to keep turning over the ingredients in an open skillet as opposed to a deeper saucepan.  Constant stirring of these dishes is necessary for even cooking and to prevent scorching, hence the term "stir fry".

I've been doing something like this for years, usually with leftover vegetables or onion and peppers, plus some soy sauce and sesame oil. Frozen rice?  No way.  Get yourself a rice cooker.  It only takes 15 minutes to cook a perfect rice. Jasmine, Japanese rice, none of that parboiled stuff.
I think he means rice that you have already cooked, like leftover rice that you store in the fridge or freezer. Fried rice is always made with 'old' or leftover rice from previous meals.

Or simply cook your rice in a covered pan.  I measure of rice to 2 measures of water.  Boil the water and then add the rice and stir.  Turn the heat down as low as it will go (to whatever setting your stovetop calls this), cover, and let sit covered for 20 minutes.  Pretty much perfect rice with no need for a special appliance.
Yep. I always used frozen rice ( that I cooked myself) when making fried rice. It’s somewhat drier and absorbs flavor much better.

Leftover rice is great, but fresh rice is just fine, if the mood suddenly strikes.  Start with the rice first in the pan, dry it out by frying it with a little oil and let it develop some crustiness on the bottom. Then add your frozen or par-cooked veg and seasoning (just salt, no soy here is how I grew up eating it).  Make a well in the middle and crack in eggs. Scramble. When they're nearly set, mix them into the rice/veg to coat everything in eggy goodness. Pepper. Drizzle chili oil. Serve!
I also like frying the rice first and moving it up in the wok to let it sit in the lower temperature areas while I fry the other ingredients. This way you get crispier rice.

For those that struggle scrambling eggs in the well of a hot pan, If you don't mind dirtying another pan, just scramble the eggs and mix them in separately.

Best egg texture: lightly scramble them in a bowl, and cook them in the wok quickly before anything else, almost like an omelette, folding over only once or twice so they're just barely cooked through. Remove to a plate, then add them in at the end and break them up with your spoon.

I also like to add a little mirin or rice vinegar to my sauce to balance the salt.

I don’t usually write notes but this is so good and easy I had to write one!! We make this any time we have leftover meat - it’s especially good with smoked turkey and brisket. Hearts of palm and bamboo shoots also add flavor and texture.

To David’s concerns about bacterial spores in leftover rice: I’m a retired medical microbiologist, and yes, rice harbors a spore forming bacillus that, when growing, produces a heat stable toxin. AND bacterial food poisoning can happen with many foods, not just rice, with similar spore-forming bacteria or others. If you follow USDA storage requirements, keeping food either cold (under 40*) or hot (over 140*), cool leftovers quickly and refrigerate within an hour, they are safe to eat, rice too.

I made this many times during the early days of the COVID lockdown when my husband and child were home during the day. You can do it in a wok or a cast iron or other heavy pan.

I've always made this off-the-cuff, with leftover rice and whatever I have in the fridge.  But I add the eggs after I have added the rice and stirred it around for awhile.  Then you just crack the eggs right on top of the rice and quickly mix it in, stirring till the eggs are cooked dry and coat everything.  Great for breakfast!

Question..I love rice but I've been reading how leftover rice almost immediately starts producing spores making it something you should not reheat or save. Do any of you have those concerns about using leftover rice for recipes like this?

Never heard this. Many cultures keep a warm pot of rice in a rice cooker for days. I don’t do this but can tell you many times I have refrigerated or frozen leftover rice and never had any sense that anything was spoiled.

If you do a Google search under 'Leftover rice" bacteria you'll find lots of articles that basically say, "uncooked rice often contains spores (cells capable of reproducing quickly) of Bacillus cereus — a bacteria strand that can cause food poisoning — that can survive when rice is cooked." So I am wondering if any of the cooks on this site have any info or know how to get around this.

I love fried rice so I am in the habit of making extra rice whenever I make it. Does keep in the fridge for a week or so, freezer for longer. Just this week I bought a pineapple with no particular plan in mind, then I saw a package of Aideles prepared pineapple teriyaki meatballs. Presto- add garlic, shallots, ginger some misc veg hanging about in the fridge.   It was very yummy

This was so easy and a big hit with the fam! Thanks, Sam! 
All I had was toasted sesame oil. I was afraid that might be too strong so I cut it with olive oil in the sauce. When you say "sesame oil" do you mean toasted or not?
Made this for dinner tonight with a bunch of leftover veggies! Really good. Served with a stir fried cabbage from left over slaw blend. Thanks for the guidelines.

Used what I had -- kernels from two ears of fresh corn, shallots, fresh ginger and garlic, frozen broccoli and cauliflower, leftover rice.  Quarantine smorgasbord!  Flavorings: tamari, sesame oil, Thai sweet red chili sauce, Vietnamese chili pepper sauce.  Three eggs.  Chalula at the table.  Wonderful!
I made this tonight without meat (we didn't have anything suitable) and I sprinkled some sesame seeds on it before serving. Quick and easy! Sunflower seeds would work too.

Great, quick and super easy to follow! Its easy to feel accomplished afterwords only to realize how easy it was, and then be pleased about how easy it was. And how tasty! I love the no recipe, recipe format because it makes it very easy to tweak. Eggs, leftover meat (or not), frozen veg and sauce is the staple, but I like using fresh ingredients when I have them and putting a Thai spin or different flavor profile to it. Tons of fun and always enjoyable, as breakfast, lunch, snack or dinner.

I adore this recipe. I used baby bok choy and a variety of local mushrooms, along with boneless chicken breast that I'd velveted and cut into small chunks--I stir fried the chicken after the vegetables and put it aside as well until the end when everything went back into the skillet.
So easy and delicious

I used leftover hoisin glazed pork belly I had braised the day before as the meat base as well as some of the leftover hoisin/honey/vinegar/ketchup/soy sauce marinade as the sauce, very tasty! I also added fresh Korean peppers (the ones that when dried become gochujang powder or paste) with the scallions. I cooked the rice the day before and spread on a sheet pan to make less sticky.  Finally threw in some peas and soy/seasame oil towards the end. Very easy and unctious no recipe 10 minute meal.
Made it using chicken, mixed veggies, garlic, rice, soy sauce, scallions, and sesame oil. Everyone liked
Loved this simple guide for a quick evening meal! Easy clean up after is a plus too!
I prefer his other version just "Fried Rice" - the instructions work better to brown rice separately than vegetables. Also, this version always ends up greasy for me but the other version does not.

I've made this many times. It's always been delicious; in fact, it's my husband's favorite meal. I like to use leftover pork and organic frozen vegetables. Following this non-recipe as written has worked the best. Bring it very hot to the table, crowned with bright green scallions.
Some acid would be good too. And fresh herbs at the end.

This is so freeing and absolutely delicious! I am 65 years old and admittedly grew up in a meat and potatoes American fare household but how did I miss this??!! Going on the weekly rotation for a while varying it by type of rice, veggies, meat, etc. Thank you Sam Sifton!
Followed the template and added cooked salmon to the mix, which goes very well with fried rice. Also a seeded Serrano chile. Excellent dish!

Love this formula and make it every week. Not having sesame oil I used tahini as a substitute in the sauce with great results. Leftovers, if there are any,  are so delicious, even eaten cold straight out of the fridge!

Thanks for this!  Brings me back to a great family who ran the food concession at our small university campus for many years.  One day the special board highlighted "Himalayan Rice."  Always the adventurous eater, I asked Ted what was in it.  It was the leftovers of the week on fried (grilled?) rice.  It was great and became a Friday staple.  We do the same at home decades later - although on our own schedule.

Be sure mise en place is ready, this goes really really fast.  Added cashews at end.  Careful with the sauce, I added too much.  For cup of cooked rice, measure sauce in teaspoons, not tablespoons.  Next time remember tofu.
Aidan liked it, use beef next time in addition to ham, more soy sauce,
Great way to use up leftover egg yolks or whites from baking.

My husband use to make this all the time but he’d start out with bacon and use that to sauté everything. Cut up leftover meats from previous meals and pretty much the same as the rest as far as veges rice and eggs . I can never make decent rice so a stop by the local Chinese restaurant works for me. This is such a delicious way to use up leftovers. Waste not want not…
I used only one egg, that was a mistake.
Add some oyster sauce to the sauce mix. Really kicks it up a notch. I save the rice we get with Chinese take out in the freezer for this use. Works great.
Sometimes I have seasoned rice and some leftover sashimi fish from ordering in Chirashi. It makes excellent fried rice along with the added veggies.

I make this all the time. Make the rice in your rice cooker or  instant pot a day ahead and store in the fridge. Do not use freshly made rice!!! This is crucial. My best versions include bean sprouts. Bok Choy is excellent when you have it.

Whatever ingredients you have are fine (though cold cooked rice is a must), but I use a different sequence: cook eggs (soft!) first, and remove from the pan. Then, in order, protein, veggies, rice. Stir-fry until the rice is well-separated and hot. Then soy sauce, sesame oil (a little goes a long way!), sugar and white pepper. Stir-fry until the color is even, add back the egg and chopped scallions. Stir and serve! A splash of Shaoxing wine before you add the rice is not bad.

I make this often for my family, but I generally use whatever fresh veggies are in season at the farmer's market. 
We do fried eggs on top for our protein. 
And a sprinkling of sesame oil is nice.

I make this a lot. For me the trick is saving leftover chopped ingredients or chopping a little extra through the week. Then it all comes together quick. I add lots if garlic, of course eggs, and sprinkle corse salt at the end (trick from my dad). Makes for an easy kids school lunch too.
Made this as suggested but with shrimp instead of meat and added some fresh cayenne pepper and Thai basil -- wonderful!
Cabbage, spinach, chard, etc., are also good additions, if you  have them on hand.

I use a 2:1:1 ratio of soy sauce, ginger paste, and hoisin, then dress it at the end with a light drizzle of sesame oil. And I always just soft-scramble some eggs first and remove them from the pan, because I've never been able to master the well. *shrug*

I confess I am always bemused when I see “recipes” for fried rice.  My mom made it with rice, leftover veggies, sliced hot dogs and soy sauce. I make it the same, but with left over meats or bacon, plus more flavorings. My husband’s mom, Chinese, did the same. One day I will try a recipe.  But for now, thanks for giving us all permission to use whatever’s around. Oh, and good call on cooking the eggs in the center before mixing them in. Thats how you get pieces of egg.  Thanks.

I oil a wok and then remove the oil, and then use duck fat for the actual stir fry. Also, add fish sauce and half a teaspoon or so of MSG, which, as any fan of Uncle Roger knows, is the King of Flavor!
Increased the sauce by about a third.

This is an excellent recipe and has helped me easily use up various vegetables and protein, meat or tofu, and it never disappoints.  I follow the cooking technique to the letter as well as the basic ingredients of garlic, ginger and scallions, with the 3 to 1 ratio of soy sauce to Sesame oil. To the sauce I add some siracha and a little sherry or masala wine.  The other vegetables and the protein can be varied. Try it: if you like fried rice this will be a go to in your recipe box.
Use lots of fresh veggies that needed to be used from the fridge. Didn't use any meat but added roasted pumpkin seeds at the end. Threw in a bit of fish sauce as well. This was so easy and delish!

2 slices cooked bacon, chopped
2 cups cooked rice
2 cups veggies - I used 1 cup broccoli, 1 cup peas/corn
2 eggs
Sauce & rest as written 
Juiced a lime
I found it needed a little acidity so I added some rice wine vinegar.
I'm all about cleaning out the fridge and cut up some chicken and mushrooms to stir in, liked other stir fry recipes that suggested adding shredded cabbage, gave it more bulk and made it healthier!
Chopped up bologna is a great addition. Not really a fan of the stuff in a sandwich setting, but crisped up in fried rice, it becomes one of my favorite ingredients!

Thank you for an easy to follow and execute fried rice recipe!  I have tried many and this is the first one that works. Also, being gluten intolerant I can’t easily find Chinese food that is gluten free since soy sauce is customarily made from wheat.  I can make  recipes, however, by using tamari soy sauce which is made from soy.  For some reason Chinese restaurants here do not use tamari sauce.  I am not sure why as it has the same flavor and qualities as soy sauce made from wheat.
So delicious.  We sometime sub out other ingredients but this is perfect as is!  Started cooking Asian food during lockdown and found that it’s so easy and super fast.  We have become experts.
This is a great recipe, but a couple of tablespoons of rice vinegar at the end really made it stand out. My favorite cheat was using a bag of cole slaw (just pre-shredded cabbage and carrots).

Ironically, I followed this "non recipe" pretty closely. As directed, I made a sauce of 3T soy sauce, 1T sesame oil, and a tiny dab of hot gochujang. I used a couple of leftover pork chops, diced small; a large heap of leftover roasted veg (cauliflower, onions, and bell peppers); and one sad ear of corn I found languishing in the fridge. Sure enough, random as all that was, the end result is delicious and will be even better with some chopped roasted peanuts on top. Thanks, Sam Sifton.
Needed some oomph.  Added some Schezwan seasoning that perked it up.  Lots of bowls for all the different add ins:-)
Great kid food!

Yum, yum.  I used vegan sausage, half brown and half wild leftover rice, then followed the instructions.  What a delicious result.  It was served with roasted salmon and a cool cabbage slaw.  Another winner.  Thank you, Sam.!
Also added some rice vinegar during cooking process.

Didn’t have pre-cooked rice or the time time to prepare rice so I used couscous. Ready in 3 minutes, flavor of lo mein or ramen noodles and the soft, fluffy mouth feel of rice. Plus it soaks up more of the garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil sauce!
This is good for what ails you!  Wow--so simple, fail-proof, and absolutely toothsome!

Made last night. If you have schmaltz (chicken fat!) in the freezer like I did, use it for the neutral oil. I also do the "gold over silver" method for the beaten egg, drizzling it over the rice as you stir fry everything. Real easy!

This was really good.  I didn't use frozen veggies except for peas at the end. Sauces included items he suggested plus chile bean sauce, fish sauce, black vinegar, Chinese rice wine, the usual suspects.  I added frozen baby peas at the very end just let them get warm from other ingredients.
This came out great. I used 1/3 cup of soy sauce though and it was a bit salty for my taste. Use caution on sauce and pour in slowly tasting as you go!

I used what I had on hand: leftover take out rice, an uncooked boneless porkchop, onion, red pepper, celery, garlic, ginger, big handful of cilantro. No soy sauce so I used ‘umami joy sauce’ and sesame oil, 1 egg and I topped it with spicy chili crunch: SO good!

Would appreciate advice on how to freeze rice. I tried this with long grain white rice cooked 1:2 with water on the stove. I let it cool on the counter then put the whole pot into the freezer overnight. At cook time it was a solid brick and not at all what the recipe promised about helping the grains to separate. I've considered spreading it thin on a parchment-covered cookie sheet but if it's that much work I wish they'd say so. Any other tips?

title: Roasted Fish With Ginger, Scallions and Soy Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: I have lived on Cape Cod since 1970,and have been blessed to live near the Chatham fishing fleet.My favorite way to prepare fish,whether sole,cod,scallops,striped bass or whatever has just come in,is to saute on high heat in a bit of olive oil on moderate high heat.Place fish on plate,deglaze pan with vermouth,add a bit of butter,add chopped scallions,or chives,or fresh dill or parsley(or all),put fish back in pan for a minute,squeeze fresh lemon,and serve.
My husband cooked this for us last night using cod. He added a little miso and sake and it was so good, I wanted to lie down and scream. He loves no-recipe recipes.
Sandwich? I'm thinking fish tacos!

Used salmon instead of white fish. Excellent!!  Cooked 3min, added sauce, 4 min more.  Just barely cooked enough.  Careful not to overcook.

Used 2tbs soy, 2 tbs Rice wind vinegar, 4 tbs soy, ginger (minced, grating failed miserably), sesame oil, garlic, scallions.

Go easy on salting the fish - the sauce is very salty.

Definitely - rice is an excellent side.  (Required, actually!)
#Tom, miso will add an umami punch to the sauce. When you’re looking for a depth of flavor, miso is a great tool to have at your disposal.
does it matter whether it's white or red miso?
@Rugby Cook TJoes sells a pouch of white miso that I keep in the fridge for these suggestions, as well as salad dressings — and more.

I’ve started roasting fish fillets directly from frozen, using a similar sauce. I just let the frozen fillets sit in a roasting pan with the sauce on top while the oven preheats to 425. I cover the pan with foil and roast for 5 mins, then remove the foil and continue roasting until the fish is done. Based on this recipe, I’ll try holding off on the sauce until after the 5 minutes under foil.

I used walleye pike and this was delicious! I poured the sauce onto the fish and baked for about 10 mins.  perfect!  started some broccolini a bit earlier with a little of the sauce and it all finished together.   I served with rice.  Used a ratio of about 5T soy to 2T mirin rice wine with a splash of sesame oil, some togarashi pepper flakes and miso to the marinade. Grated 1 garlic clove and a chunk of ginger as well.  
This is a make again recipe for sure!

I have this same recipe in my kitchen arsenal with a few twists.  I often use a fatty salmon, julienned scallions, and my secret (I'm only sharing with you) sauce is tempura sauce with which I add a little water, sugar and hot sesame oil.  We often eat it over white rice garnished with cilantro.  My 8-year old always asks for seconds!

this was great.  I used local farmed rainbow trout that looks and tastes like salmon.  Needed a bit more time than suggested but the result was wonderful.  Next time I will add a bit of miso to the sauce, reduce the soy sauce and up the ginger and garlic.

#Bicka - I recently bought two kinds of miso just because. They're still in the fridge unopened! So, what does miso add to the recipe? Taste? Texture? I know I could google it but best from an aficionado. Thanks!

#Tom - Use the white miso to make a simple miso soup and that will explain the wonderful taste it adds. Red miso is better with stronger sauces, e.g. tomato sauce, rich stews, etc. The white is for milder sauces, e.g. bechamel. 

Simple Miso
4 cups water.
1 tablespoons nori (or wakame seaweed, shredded)
3 Tb miso.
3 green onions (a.k.a. scallions, chopped)
1 8 oz. pkg firm silken tofu (cut into 1-inch cubes)
Optional: dash soy sauce (omit for a gluten-free soup)
OR - You can buy instant pkg.
Try not to ever buy farmed fish......bad for you and the environment.

This recipe was delicious! I used 5 tbsp soy and 2 tbsp rice wine vinegar and added 1 tbsp white miso as well. Roasted the fish for 10 minutes as they were thick fillets and accompanied it with oven roasted bok choi, a wonderfully easy weeknight meal!

My husband eats food, but doesn't love food, if you know what I mean. He LOVED this. I used seasoned rice vinegar, sesame oil, olive oil, ginger and soy sauce. And he couldn't stop talking about how good it was. Definitely going into the regular rotation!
2 min cook is not sufficient...I did 5-6.

He says fluke--thin fillet? White fish. Could be correct for that and not
for the fish you choose to fix.  Says "until done".  ;o)

I love this recipe.  As suggested, I added sesame oil, used both grated and powdered ginger, and added a little hot sauce.  Sam's right about using "lots" of scallions.

I have one request.  The "no recipe" concept works fine, but I would appreciate a simple list of ingredients to check that I haven't forgotten something.  I don't need quantities or additional directions, but I'd love a checklist.  Thanks!
Miso = umami

I don’t cook fish often because I live in a land-locked area and am never certain exactly how fresh is the fish available in my grocery store. This recipe was good, easy and my husband loved it. I used halibut filets which were about 1” thick. To determine the right amount of cooking time I cross referenced this recipe with another NYT Cooking recipe specifically for halibut. Ten minutes was the recommendation and it seemed right. Served on top of couscous. Next time will double the sauce.

I learned a long time ago, when visiting a Colorado friend, that frozen fish is the freshest (unless you’re getting it literally right off the boat). Most fish sold in the meat department in stores has been previously frozen anyway. When fish are caught on large fishing vessels out in the ocean, they are flash-frozen. Frozen cod is wonderful, and a heck of a lot cheaper than buying it at the counter.

I used about 2 parts soy sauce  to 1 part mirin. I think I will try 1 to 1 next time. Added a big spoonful of miso, which was a great addition. No need to salt the fish as noted by someone else.  Served with brown rice and roasted broccoli glazed with the sauce. I put the broccoli in a few minutes before the fish. Used cod. This would be great with salmon or chicken thighs. I keep peeled 1-2” pieces of peeled ginger in the freezer—next to the chopped garlic.) It makes these recipes so easy!

Tried this two ways, one just as written...great. Then, I had no ginger or rice wine/miso so I used what I had: pureed lemon grass / garlic, with soft butter as a rub, then some lemon juice and the zest added to the soy/saki for the finish. Yum

I agree with another commenter, the fish should be raw or totally cooked, not somewhere in between, but I'm a rank amateur so that's just my opinion.  The combination of miso and soy was good but a bit too salty, so while I would use both again, I would be spare with one or the other.  The other note from a rank amateur is that it seems completely necessary (and therefore probably goes without saying for others) to pat the fish completely dry.
Soy sauce and miso double up on saltiness.  If adding miso, reduce soy sauce.

This is very similar to a recipe from the late Byron Ayanoglu for salmon that I have been making for so long it’s a no recipe recipe. It calls for lime juice instead of vinegar and always sesame oil. The original recipe was for poached fish but I have also made it in foil pouches on the bbq. Just put all the sauce ingredients in the pouch. This is my one fool (me) proof recipe. I’m a one hit wonder!

Made this tonight with Trader Joe’s baby bok choy and a salmon filet.  Sautéed the bok choy in small amt of oil, added the salmon and seared on one side, then glazed all with soy/sherry/ginger/garlic/brown sugar mixture.  Added sliced scallions and served with rice.  Really tasty, quick, healthy meal.
I was unclear about the timing. Only two minutes in the oven? I left it in for several more minutes and checked frequently. It was delicious.

This is delicious.  I used parchment paper (425 degrees max) to make clean-up easier.  I used black sea bass, which is my new favorite white fish.  I really think that one could cook this dish in a pyrex dish, at 450 degrees (or 425).  I have cooked bluefish this way for years, with success.  No need for pre-heating the pan, either, or for waiting to put on the sauce.  Well, we can all experiment! Fish does continue to cook after being taken out of the oven.
I am so jealous! I have not had bluefish since moving to the West Coast.
What does it mean to put “neutral oil” on the pan? Can I use olive oil?
It usually means not olive oil. Canola or something flavorless. These oils have a higher "smoke point" than olive oil so they won't burn on a hot stove or oven.
Grapeseed oil is a good go to oil.  Flavorless and is good at high heat.
But if you like olive oil and don't mind the way it might taste with the Asian flavors, there's no reason you can't use it.
I wonder if this would work better as a marinade as opposed to a sauce.
In my opinion, in my experience, fish is too delicate to marinate. A coating is more than adequate

Wow, this is really good for something so simple to throw together. I made the sauce with ginger, garlic, scallions, soy sauce, a dash of red wine vinegar and a few teaspoons of kimchi juice from a homemade batch- the kimchi juice added depth, funk, and spice- so delicious. Make extra sauce for serving!
I serve it with miso rice, which adds some extra umami. Just mix a couple tablespoons miso paste into the water before adding rice.
I also added the miso and it was amazing!  I also cooked longer because I was using a thicker cod filet.

Not sure why some commenters said they used rice wine vinegar rather than rice wine which Sam is suggesting like shaoxing, mirin or sake, because vinegar is different. Of course this is a no-recipe so people can use what they like. I added white miso, lots of green onions, garlic and a tiny bit of toasted sesame oil, then finished with cilantro leaves. No need to salt the fish, as the soy will take care of that. This was delish!

I know this sounds a little bizarre, but I added diced ripe red plum to the dish (thinking of the tradition of Chinese plum sauce), and its sweet/sourness was killer with the acid/tang of the soy and rice wine in the sauce for the fish.
Simple and delicious.  A basic go to.  But Sam--seriously---2-3 minutes to cook the fish?  Was that a misprint?

I have to think it's just part of the modern trend where people don't cook fish all the way. I like my fish either cooked through or raw like sashimi but not done rare or medium rare, so I cooked mine all the way through. I don't think it matters for this recipe – – cook the fish how you like it.
If he was using Fluke it can be pretty thin and might cook in that amount of time in that hot of an oven on a hot sheet.
Salmon: cook about 10 minutes total, finish in broiler if necessary
Fish needed more time than expected.  Used Alaskan Sole.  Double the sauce - you’ll be looking for more.

Cod. 1 Tbsp white miso,  ~2 Tbsp Soy,  ~3 Tbsp rice wine vinegar,
Grated ginger
About 20-25 min @ 425-450
Also scallions, garlic ramps, if on hand

Thick cod.  1 Tbsp white miso,  ~2Tbsp soy sauce (tamari), ~3Tbsp rice wine vinegar,  grated ginger 
Scallions and garlic ramps if on hand
20-25 min. 425- 450 degrees
I guess there’s no need of salt, since the soy sauce is pretty salty. I added some lemon afterwards because I missed the acid, so next time I’ll add before cooking.
I used 5 tbsp soy and 2 tbsp rice wine vinegar and added 1 tbsp white miso as well.
Made this into fish tacos. Tossed some of the sauce into cabbage slaw, piled it atop the fish set into warm corn tortillas, and added a few chopped peanuts for extra crunch. Delicious.
I made this with halibut that was pretty thick so it actually ended up cooking for around 12-13 minutes. Served with bok choy as shown in the picture and it was amazing!

This is so simple but wonderful. I used small cod fillets, and did add garlic to the sauce. Sautéed tatsoi while it roasted, and drizzled with sesame oil, and served it all over jasmine rice. I normally rely on recipes, so this was a delicious validation that I kinda know what I’m doing!
Do not salt the fish! It came out too salty. I would try it again but less soy.
The soy sauce ran off the sides and really burnt on to the sheet pan.  I didn't paint it on because how do you paint ginger, scallions, garlic?  Mine was pretty think.

Used for barramundi with 2:1 soy sauce/shaoxing cooking wine. Dash of sesame oil, chili oil, mirin. Heaping pile of grated ginger (easier to grate when frozen) and thinly sliced scallions. 3-4 tablespoons of minced garlic. 

Cooked for 5 minutes pre-sauce and ten minutes after to get it fully cooked through. Delicious. 

Maybe a little too salty - I made enough sauce to coat and form a shallow pool in the pan. Definitely didn't need that much.
I have made this at least four times; the most memorable was halibut but it’s just such a delicious way with fish. I add baby bok choy to the pan just before it’s done.

If the day is too hot for cooking in the kitchen in a 425-degree
oven, I use an outdoor grill with a roasting pan small enough to
sit off the heat source, i.e., indirect heat.  Use the direct heat source
to grill eggplants with miso (nasu dengaku).   Works and tastes great!
Also, I make a miso dressing and dress plain grilled eggplants without
any further cooking - no indoor-style top-heated broiling.
Served on top of leftover black rice from takeout. I chopped a head of broccoli into it and zapped it in the microwave and plopped the roasted fish on top. I used dark/thick soy sauce.

Kind neighbor shared some fish he caught here on the Central Coast of California so I tried this no recipe recipe knowing my teenager who doesn’t love fish does love soy sauce so maybe, just maybe. He enjoyed it as did his friend who stayed for dinner and had told me earlier she had never eaten fish before (???) I had a backup in case the teenagers didn’t like it but they both ate it all! I subbed white wine vinegar bc I didn’t have rice wine or sherry. The house didn’t smell fishy either! Win.

I had frozen breaded Cod fillets from Whole Foods. I tried this recipe to see if I could turn them into a special treat.  It worked!  I browned the fillets in olive oil for about 4 minutes on each side, removed them from the pan and added a little more oil, 2 chopped scallions and 1 chopped garlic clove, then added about 1 1/2 tablespoon of soy sauce and some finely chopped ginger, heated about 2 minutes then added back the fillets for about 4 minutes. Served on baby spinach.  Delicious

We do a similar dish, weekly, with salmon, but first marinate the fish in the no recipe sauce (usually ginger, garlic, olive oil, chilli pepper flakes, smoked paprika, soy sauce, bit of honey, lemon juice, lime juice). Cook on stop top in cast iron skillet for a few minutes and transfer the skillet with cover to the oven for a few more. Serve with basmati rice and broccoli. One of our favourites.

This is so good, and so simple! I believe that Sam's no-recipe recipes tend to expect the cook has enough culinary experience to make adjustments as needed. I follow the rule of thumb to cook white fish fillets for 10 minutes per inch of thickness at 400 F, but since Sam's direction is to heat the pan at 425 F, the fish didn't need as much time. I ended up cooking on heated pan for 3 minutes, adding sauce, and roasting off for another 5 minutes. Perfect!

Super tasty and very easy prep. I used basa filets that I had in my freezer.  I mixed 3 cloves of garlic, 2 Tbsp minced ginger, soy sauce, 1 Tbsp white miso, a spoonful of Fly by Jing chili crisp, and a ton of scallions.  Did not season the fish with salt and pepper.  Roasted for 15 min at 425 along with some seasoned baby bok choy (a little oil, minced garlic and oyster sauce). Paired with some multi grain rice to soak up the incredible sauce. Def going into the weeknight rotation!
We really dig this non-recipe with any whitefish. We make extra sauce, which is great with baby bok choy or broccoli. Served with rice.

My ideal recipe: fast, healthy, delicious, minimal cleanup. I used branzino direct from the freezer.
