Rinsed, dried, rubbed with oil alongside baby bok choy on a sheet of parchment, which I slid onto the hot pan. Left unseasoned, since the sauce is plenty salty, Cooked 10 minutes, slathered with sauce, and cooked another 10. Perfection. Next time I will double the sauce so I have extra to spoon on top while eating. Had time while it cooked to whip up Eric Kim’s peanut butter Parmesan noodles.

I made this for a dinner party the other night.  It was quick, delicious, and impressive looking.  The main secret is having very fresh fish and not overcooking it.  The sauce is light enough to let the fish shine through, but flavorful enough to make it interesting.
I'm a bit unclear on what this means: "a few tablespoons of soy sauce for each one of rice wine or sherry." I hope someone can explain this....Thanks!
It's a ratio - for each tablespoon of rice wine or sherry, add a few tablespoons of soy sauce. You can just mix to taste but its a starting point.
My interpretation: mix them at a  ratio of 2 or 3 Tbs. soy sauce to 1 Tb. rice wine or cooking sherry.
it means, to make the sauce, for every two to three tablespoons of soy sauce add one tablespoon of rice wine (or sherry) until you have enough sauce.
145 internal temperature. No scallion or just for garnish. Parsley or ??? better

Easy and satisfying. It worked well for me with salmon fillets, after 4 or 5 minutes of baking, to flip them skin-sides up for a couple minutes, before a final 3 or 4 minutes of skin-sides down after first brushing on the sauce. The sauce yielded a nice glaze result made by slowly adding soy sauce to a red miso base (a generous tablespoon measure), mashing mixture with a fork to a thinner consistency, then stirring in a bit of shrimp paste, ume plum vinegar, grated ginger and finely-diced onion.
Great dish. Made with John Dory.

Great concept, great flavors. Liked it except too salty. I did 1 T soy sauce and 1 T rice wine with scallion and ginger (for two small-med filets) and would do less soy sauce in future. I can’t imagine using more than 1 T soy sauce for each Tbsp of rice wine/sherry, but I know my flavor preferences.

I thought the ginger was undercooked/raw since there was only 1 minute of cooking time as prepared in accordance with this recipe.  Im going to turn to frying or chinese steaming methods the next time i want a dish with this flavor combo.

prepare fish,whether sole,cod,scallops,striped bass or whatever has just come in,is to saute on high heat in a bit of olive oil on moderate high heat.Place fish on plate,deglaze pan with vermouth,add a bit of butter,add chopped scallions,or chives,or fresh dill or parsley(or all),put fish back in pan for a minute,squeeze fresh lemon,and serve

My husband cooked this for us last night using cod. He added a little miso and sake and it was so good.
I cooked this with petrale sole and reduce oven to 400 for about 10 min. and was perfect; topped with some cilantro and scallions, very good.
Made this with cod and since I didn’t have rice wine or sherry, I used some balsamic vinegar with the soy sauce.  Nice treatment and tasty result!

I used grouper, so It had to cook a little longer due to the thickness of the fish. Otherwise, I made this as written (using sesame oil in the sauce) and we enjoyed it very much. Great weeknight dinner with little prep required.

I love Sam -- I think he's brilliant, and I'm on the same page with him regarding no-recipe recipes.  But I thought this was just OK and I don't think I'll make it again.  If forced to do so, though, I would add a small amount of sweetener to balance the very strong taste of soy sauce.  I opted for Rice vinegar in the sauce -- maybe using sherry would have done the trick for me.

Add garlic
Sesame oil
Made this tonight with red snapper. Not great. Maybe I just used too much ginger/scallions for the amount of fish. Also too salty.
Fabulous recipe and a nice change from the usual butter, lemon, spices combination we usually prepare.
Delicious. I used mahi-mahi and added sesame oil and generous black pepper. Served with brown rice and salad.

This is a simple, delicious recipe. I made it with Rock Fish filets and used a sheet of parchment paper for easy cleanup.  I subbed tamari to make it gluten free.  Will definitely put this one in the monthly rotation.
Use a microplane rasp to grate the ginger. This recipe is great with cod.
I made this with petrale sole. It was delicious. Next time I will add a little bit of sichuan pepper. Roasted snap peas make a great side.

Made this last night for dinner with fresh Mahi Mahi. Started with 3 Tbs Soy Sauce, 2 Tbs of Sherry Cooking Wine, 1 Tbs Rice Vinegar, juice from 1 whole Meyer Lemon (because I had one), 1 tsp Sesame Oil, 1 tsp of Siete, 1 garlic clove minced, a ton of fresh ginger - because it's my fave - maybe a 4' piece grated, Pink Himalayan Salt, Cayenne Pepper. Cooked longer to be more well done. Served with rice and oven roasted asparagus. Family loved it. Next time, I will add Miso!

This was delicious and simple. I used garlic, ginger, scallions, chili crisp and a bit of sesame oil with the soy and rice wine vinegar.  I had grouper in the freezer, but this would be great on many types of fish. 

We used the extra sauce as a little dip for scallion pancakes I had in the freezer.
Great no-recipe, my favorite kind. So easy to adjust. The flavors are great. I’d love to know how adding chili oil works—I’ll try that next.  Hoping for even more no-recipes!
Delicious and sooooo easy.  Used fresh caught bass from the nearby lake.  Will hang on to this no recipe recipe for when we go fishing again!

I used about 2-3X more mirin than soy, grated ginger, garlic, & sesame oil.  Peppered fish and a smidge of salt from my grinder.  Other comments made me wary of salting the fish AND using soy sauce, good to consider.  I followed the 2 min suggestion, but fish was 3/4 translucent, only opaque on outside - thick fillet of halibut.  I brushed it again after another 6 min in oven, total roasting time roughly 8 min.  Added more fresh green onion after plating.  Wish I had used more grated ginger.

Used cod -- served with polenta (cooked in chicken stock). Didn't add salt -- even with low-sodium soy sauce, it was rather salty for our taste. Our fish was thicker, so the roasting took a little longer. We liked it.
Made it with salmon instead of a white fish.  It was great, but required more cooking time.
Thanks to the person who posted the link to the Monterey fisheries site.  That will be helpful.  Always appreciate hints.

SO delicious and I love the freedom to play - I put some Gojuchang in the dressing and it sang!
Served with Kale Sautéed with Ginger, Garlic and Chili oil. So yummy!
I think Sam is the king! This technique is perfect -I've  made it many times -this time with a side of fried rice (sort of) with lots of vegetables and chinese sausage (junk food for sure but tasty.)
Used sole fillets. Was dead easy to make and the finish didn’t take long at all so make sure everything else is ready to go before you start it. Reheated well the next day for lunch.

Liz ( 2 years ago), thanks for sharing Byron's recipe, which includes tamarind concentrate.  Tamarind adds a nice fruity, tart flavor that works well with soy and ginger/garlic.  It's an ingredient in most Pad Thai recipes. It will complement the fish nicely. If using tamarind, I would 
suggest lime juice, not vinegar. All other ingredients will complement nicely. The tamarind will also give flavor with out adding sodium.
Baking takes more like 25 min
So delicious and easy. A go-to from now on.

title: Ham and Cheese Pasta With a Handful of Peas Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
Loved this. To the ham, I added onion and minced garlic. I also zested a lemon into the pasta for brightness. Next time, I may add breadcrumbs to the top and set it under a broiler for crunch.

Years ago, I was working on my mac & cheese recipe and was advised to shred my own cheese, as pre-shredded cheese has a coating that affects creaminess. With that in mind, I shredded a block of Swiss for this recipe and added a large splash of pasta water, so I didn't encounter an issue with "globbing.
@Erin, my cheese didn’t melt well at all.  My husband enjoyed this but i was “meh” because the cheese didn’t coat the pasta or the ham.
The Swiss is a mistake, it globs together into a solid mass taking with it all the peas and ham. Gruyere as suggested by Peggy is the way to go, or a mixture of both.
This was SO. GOOD.  I used a mix of cheddar and provolone cheeses (because that's what I had) and deglazed the pan I cooked the ham in with a splash of white wine to scrape up all the browned bits.
I’m hooked on the “no recipe” recipes!  This was quick, easy and tasty!  Added a minced shallot and one garlic clove to ham.  Might do a small dollop is Dijon next time!

Based on what was in my pantry & fridge, diced up several slices of Easter ham, chopped some fresh asparagus, & had handful of diced onion. Threw it all in a hot skillet with a little olive oil & butter. Boiled the last a half of a box of rigatoni while shredded a half cup of gruyere & a half cup of good white cheddar. Deglazed skillet with a little pasta water & tossed all together while piping hot. Topped with black pepper and grated Parmesan. Delicious, fast, & easy. Hit with the fam.

Have made this twice now, once with Swiss, the second with Jarlsberg.  Love the taste of both, but the melted Jarlsberg is much easier to clean off of plates and utensils. Swiss seems to adhere itself much more and takes some elbow grease to remove. Also appreciate the mild, nutty flavor of Jarlsberg. Thinking of trying this with asparagus tips instead of peas.

Delicious! I used shredded Swiss and I added garlic and lemon zest, I decided it could use another veggie so I put in some cauliflower, I also used pre cubed pancetta that I found at Trader Joe’s and rigatoni instead of shells because I already had it in my pantry. I prepped ahead and then baked at 400 until it bubbled. I think you could use any veggies or meat, I may try with cubed chicken, broccoli, and red pepper next time :)

I made this to bring to lunch this week. Super quick and cheap! I used a ham steak from the deli and cut it into cubes. I also added some chopped Gouda cheese to it to make it extra cheesy. This is my new meal when the choice between pizza delivery and time are in competition.

This is what my family calls an ONA. O never again.,,
This left big gaps in making a good casserole. Things. Just.  never. melded. 
The cheeses never got fully incorporated.  I used jarlsberg and guyere  I cooked the pasta and added the organic peas. And pasta H2O.  Plus I added a killer chopped green onion to the ham.  Put in a little pepper flakes. 
Fogetabiutit    
Who are these peeps who cook and send in these recipes. Jezuz  H Christ. They need to come and eat in my kitchen in Midwest.
@Kay same experience
I used to make something similar years ago but used cream and Parmesan cheese to toss with the pasta and peas. It worked out well. I like this variation.

If you’re lucky enough to live where the grocery stores carry country ham steaks, use one of those! (I buy a bunch when I’m in the right part of the country and pack them in a cooler for the trip home—they travel/freeze well)

I’ve loved this no-recipe since it first appeared. Tinkered with it several times too and it still tastes wonderful. The only suggestion I have is use the best quality Swiss or gruyere cheese - this makes a big difference!
When there is no melted cheese (had the Swiss cheese globs), the whole thing is dull but probably child-friendly.  This no-recipe recipe has the feel of grandma's way to use up leftovers.

When I make this, I use butter, chicken broth, cream or half and half, and Parmesan from the NYT Pasta Primavera recipe. Add the cheese OFF HEAT and whisk, whisk, whisk before you add to the shells, ham, and peas. Don’t forget the garlic and red pepper flakes. https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/2369-le-cirques-spatti-primavera

Made 3/4/19 - good, not great, pretty easy. 16 oz of pasta, 4 serving bag of peas and one ham steak. Could go down to 12 oz of pasta. I used mostly gruyere and a little Jarlsberg totalling about 1.5 c maybe a bit more then added about 3/4 c Parm. Added salt and pepper at the end to bring out more flavor.

What a delicious, fast winter meal! I used Asiago cheese as it was what I had on hand. Melted well if given time to heat up and some big stirs. I also added garlic powder and some thyme and chives I needed to use up. Serve with red pepper flakes. 

To make it a little healthier, do a half the cheese with two large dollops of Greek yogurt with nutritional yeast. 

To make less healthy, use bacon instead of ham add the pasta water to the grease, combine with pasta instead of the butter pads.

Just wonderful. Simple and fun. I used a terrific locally made raclette (I should go look for the name); superb. I also added some fresh garlic, sauteed at the end of browning the ham, because, why not? Next time, I think I'll add a bit more cheese. But not too much.
Next time I'll stuff the shells. Or maybe sprinkle the cheese on top and blend it in.  Even though I spread it out in a huge bowl, the cheese melted into globs and stuck to all the peas.
Meh. Not bad, not great. Shoulda just made a quick bechamel for the cheese, used half cheddar half gruyere, never melded right. Flavors were good, will give it another go.
Dang. This did not turn out. Not sure why. Cheese (Jarlsburg) turned into clumps despite stirring/pasta water/ butter. Not great. Please advise!

I had the final leftovers of a spiral ham, and needed some kind of recipe to use it up. My choice of cheeses was limited. I used ricotta, which I was sure would mix with everything, and rigatoni which I had on hand. Because I often share my meal with four dogs, I kept enough for myself, and used the rest as a topper for their kibble. Because I’m sharing, I avoid garlic & onions. I gave out their portions then added Parmesan cheese, baby tomatoes & half a lime to my portion. 
All the dogs absolutely gave it 5 stars! I give it 4, because it’s a flexible tasty meal for using up left-overs. Thanks for a useful recipe! (Saved under “good to know!”)

This is the second time NYT has done me dirty with a cheese sauce. I don’t know how other people are getting this Swiss to melt, mine turned into a huge stubborn clump. A few spoonfuls of pasta water is not enough to turn cheese into creamy sauce!! Maybe everything has to be super hot? Stirred super vigorously? I don’t get it.
This is reminiscent of the tuna noodle peas I ate growing up ( and still do when I am feeling forlorn).
The Jarlsberg didn't work for me.  The meal was "saved" with mozzerella and parm.
Made this yesterday.  Very good BUT very heavy!  I added mushrooms and some halved cherry tomatoes, but it was still very heavy and filling.  Husband loved it, tho.

Good enough as written. I used Jarlsberg, which clumped a bit, and added sun dried tomatoes and a couple glugs of half-and-half. Still could have used a something more to loosen it up. Lemon zest and juice would have been a good idea, too.
Delicious! Used orecchiette pasta, and Raclette cheese. Yummy!

I never write reviews but this was so lousy that I just had to. Agree with Kay, never again. A waste of perfectly good ingredients. Simple, yes. But barely edible and terribly plain, food for the sake of eating and nothing else. Cheese clumped together, poorly integrated ingredients, very bland. I don't understand how this "recipe" made it into the Times and how can it have the 4 star rating?? Not a peanut butter lover but I'd prefer a PB sandwich. Come on Sam Sifton, 2 thumbs down.
Used half Jarlsberg, half cheddar...very tasty. Extra water helped give the right amount of moisture.

My husband and I have made this recipe twice now and really like it for an easy workday lunch. We can make a batch on Sunday and have a ready to heat " warm pasta salad" for lunch the next day or two. I was surprised by the truly negative comments, not my experience. Finely grated softer Swiss cheese blended in a bit at a time made the ribbony strands as described, and don't forget a splash of pasta water. We also added a glug of olive oil.
Easy, weeknight go-to after a long day at school. Cheese clumps a little, but doesn't matter when you're tired and hungry!
I made this with a cave aged Appenzeller Swiss and it came out clumpy and stringy. Haven’t tasted it yet. No takers in the family.

Delicious, and light. I used Gruyere (my favorite cheese) and it did clump somewhat, so I didn't get a creamy sauce despite lots of stirring. Seems like Swiss or Jarlsberg would do the same thing. Either way, this was delightful.

New cook here. Everything was going fine but then the cheese - Jarlsberg - just became a couple of big glops when I added the pasta and ham rather than smooth ribbons around the pasta. What did I do wrong?
Don’t expect the cheese to be like a béchamel sauce; it is just melted in. Stir slowly, don’t forget a bit of pasta water, and you can avoid globs.

Made it with exact ingredients.  When it was still at the tossing in the bowl phase, it seemed as if perhaps I used too much cheese, because it would not coat the pasta.  I added more pasta water and when that didn’t work, I heated about 1/4 cup half and half to see if it would help it blend.  No luck.  I was out of things to try, hungry, and ready to eat it any way at all.  Miraculously, all the stringiness and globs just seemed to fit right in with each bite.  Couldn’t tell it wasn’t blended.
This ended up being a glob and mess, I used Jarlsberg.  I like the concept, but I think a softer, easier to melt cheese is needed instead of Swiss.

A fine quick dish. A couple things: I had an oddball hybrid cheese on hand (branded “Fontiago”, ostensibly a cross between Fontina and Asiago. Do NOT use that; turns gummy and gloppy, not creamy. That said, there’s a Swiss hater in the house, so I’d say Gruyère or Comté will be my next try. I started with diced Virginia ham, added sautéed diced onion and two minced cloves of garlic after browning the ham in butter. Added diced 1/2 a red bell pepper when I tossed pasta in cheese, before the peas.
Globby.
The flavor was good but the cheese never incorporated, I didn’t use pre-shredded Swiss but it still melted in chunks and wouldn’t smooth out.
Swiss is a big no, it congeals into big lumps.  The flavor was just fine so might try again with another cheese and onions cooked with ham would be nice
My good Swiss cheese ended up in a big ole glob, and adding the pasta water only made that worse. The flavors are good, but the cheese situation is problematic.
Loved this recipe but used havarti instead of jarlsberg.  So creamy and so delicious.

I don’t know what kind of Swiss cheese you used for this recipe, but the Swiss cheese that I used - a block that I grated myself - turned into a big clump in the middle of the bowl with pieces of ham and some peas stuck to it. Melts like a dream? More like a nightmare. Ruined my dinner.
same! I was just going to make a cream sauce and wish I would have. the cheese clumped and felt like a waste. @David
This sounded so good, I tried it tonight.  I used Jarlsburg as that was all that was available in my store.  The cheese softened into globs.  I was expecting it to coat everything.  Any suggestions?
Maybe the pasta water will help deglob?
I also got globs.  Not crazy about this recipe.  Maybe sprinkling the shredded cheese into the pasta as you stir it would be better than putting it on the bottom and hoping it mixes well.

I plan on trying this in a few days, but I don't plan on purchasing a block of good, expensive cheese simply to melt into pasta. I'm going to use, (no sniggering please), a can of Campbell's cheddar cheese soup that's been sitting in my pantry, unloved, for quite some time. Many, but not all, are having problems with the cheese 'clumping', or worse. I figure, well, the canned cheddar's already 'melted', mixed with a bit of milk. Does this sound agreeable?
Super simple, but be sure to get some nice sharp swiss.
I am sorry, what are the measurements of the various ingredients... I am getting vague measurements and do not know what is needed from this posting/recipe.
Nancy Ann, this is a no-recipe recipe so not measurements given. Just use your skills and imagination. Earlier, Jason, gave the measurements he used, so adjust his to your kitchen.
These recipes are made deliberately without any measurements: a no-recipe recipe. :)

This no-recipe is easily done in a microwave! You have to make each ingredient one at a time, so you have to work quickly, but with shredded cheese, mixed veggies, and carefully cooked ham steak, this can be done in 10 minutes or less.
Trying to empty out refrig before leaving on vacation.  Had a bit of corned beef left over and used that instead of ham.  Worked great!

I had some serious challenges with the cheese chipping together. I spread it out in a large shallow bowl, and all the Swiss cheese just clumped together in big wads. I even tried returning it to the pan with the remainder of the cooking water to no avail. You’re probably better off melting the cheese in the reserved pasta water on it’s own first, and then tossing everything else in and finishing on the stove.
Used aged swiss.  Not worth the effort.
Are used pecorino Romano, and the recipe came out just as described. It was delicious, and I’ve made it twice in the last week.

This is what my familia calls an ONA... oh never again.  It was just not right.  Consistency was off, the cheese never melted correctly.  This needed to be a casserole that was properly constructed and just not thrown into a pot or bowl and expected to act accordingly.  It had great ingredients and a good premise.   
BUt it did not come together correctly. 
I used Jarlsburg and guyere and I incorporated pasta water. Plus had  a nice ham slice and organic peas,  disappointing at the very least.
Hotdish. Needs dairy like cream or cream of ____ soup, and crunchy on top, like bread crumbs or potato chips or...corn chips! Now that's the Minnesota way!
With well browned onions and a couple cloves of garlic chopped fine this could be a great dish. Without them the ham & cheese is too bland to carry the day.

Large shells seemed to be too big for me so I used smaller Orecchiette instead. Sauteed ham with diced shallot and vermouth but next time will use more ham. And perhaps more than a cup of grated cheese. Used Jarlsberg and that worked fine. A quick and delicious meal with a side salad.
Worked with cheddar and mont jack, threw in mushrooms, kidney beans, wilted super greens in addition to the ham and it worked! So fast!
Plan look better forgot about it put me down there in life so you know
I have made this for years, using tortellini and parmesan cheese. Always a hit!
This is how I always make my pasta dishes:)

Made this last night.  1# orrechiette, about 3 c diced leftover ham, half of a 1lb bag of tiny peas, and probably a cup or so of full fat ricotta.  Generous amounts of pepper - perfect way to use up leftover ham and ricotta!

Love cooking this way, with whatever I’ve got on hand.  Used some Canadian bacon I had, grated some gruyere which I find more melty anyway, chopped up some fresh baby spinach and tossed that in at the end.  Super simple.
I made this with leftover Christmas ham; freezed it for church funeral luncheons!!

The first time I try a recipe from NYT, I make it as written. Any amending would intrude on later versions. 
This idea stands, unammended. Well, some garlic, maybe....!

Bland as is.  Needs lots of garlic sautéed with the ham.  Used 8 oz of diced ham; should have used 16 oz.  Adding white wine to the ham at the end of sautéing would also add more flavor.  Substituted asparagus for the peas.  Worked well.
Ran with the whole no-recipe part and did it with little scallops. Delicious!

I made this with shredded gruyere.  Also started with two slices or bacon (in 1” slices, added one shallot thinly sliced and two cloves or garlic before adding the butter and diced smoked ham.  Excellent!
Try putting a cooking lid on the bowl. It helped me melt the cheese

This was so good. I sauteed some shallots with the pancetta (no ham) and grated parmesan, and a good cheddar topped with lemon zest for the win. 
As other shave said do not use pre-grated cheese b/c it clumps. 11yo gives this 2 thumbs up.
I substituted chicken sausage for the ham. Delicious!
I added a can of diced tomatoes; great for flavor and color.
I like to use a glob of creamy Gorgonzola instead of Swiss. And pancetta in lieu of ham. Same principle, better melting and flavor.

This was such an easy and delicious dish! I was a bit concerned since the hubs loves his carbonara, but he raved about it. Adding the pasta water is a big, important tip. Added scallions and pepper as garnish.
I so love these "no recipe" recipes.  They just work!

Delicious with farfelle; I used Goat Milk Gouda for the cheese. Added onion and garlic as suggested, no peas so used green beans, cooked in pasta water for last 3 minutes. Delicious. I used about 9 oz pasta, 1-1/4 cup cubed ham and about 3/4 cup grated cheese with the 1/4 cup pasta water. Stirred well, was a bit globby but got caught in the folds of pasta so worked out fine. Topped with Daiya parmesan shreds. Keeper recipe, super quick, less than an hour to prep and make.

An easy and delicious dish. I used left over ham from Easter (was in freezer) . I sautéed 1/3 of a large yellow onion, added cubes of ham, 1/4 C of white wine and at the end added grated pecorino. The peas were with the tubular pasta. I used smaller amounts because it was just for two.
Delicious use for leftover holiday ham.

21 May 2023

Well, a non recipe, eh?

Fresh peas in the pod, skinny French green beans, cherry tomatoes, and Parma ham from the marché; fresh tagliatelle, crème fraîche, heavy cream, butter, and gruyère from the grocery. Salt and pepper from the pantry. Don't measure anything. Heat it, combine it, eat it. 

Excellent.
Jarlsburg did not melt. Why not? Bummer
This was divine, we did add a little parmesan on top and it was great!

Did this as a single serving meal. Made a bit of bechamel that while cooling, added a heaping tablespoon of whole grain mustard. I added cracked rosemary to the diced ham and deglazed the pan with a splash of white wine. Mixing some reserved pasta water was key. The acid in the mustard and sweet peas created great balance.

I think the secret to the cheese not clumping is to use enough pasta water (I used 1/2 cup), mix it all together very quickly while still very hot, and serve right away. 

I used fontina, which was perfect, and extra large shells cooked al dente.
What about using flour in the butter with some milk and pasta liquid to blend the cheeses?
Excellent and easy. I used 2 cups of Swiss and it paid off with a pound of pasta.

Perfectly fine.  A one (iron) skillet meal.  Used garden grown broccolii w/ garlic & shallot sauteed in olive oil.  Removed and wiped out the skillet. Browned the ham chunks in butter.   Removed.  Added pasta and boiling water.  Cooked til almost al dente.  Returned other ingredients plus paprika.  Added jarlsberg.
Make a bechamel.
Annie especially likes this.
No reason to not make a béchamel- cheese otherwise will not meld. Made delish sauce with an onion Gruyère and finished  with grated Romano.
Easy and delicious as written, or note the many good suggestions below. Perfect for self or close friends. Truly comforting.
Added lemon zest and a splash of lemon juice, as well as a dallop of Greek yoghurt at serving. Used broccolini instead of peas and whole wheat rotini. Lots of parmesan. Super delicious.
This is similar to one of my “no recipe” favorites with ham, a little olive oil with minced garlic and a dash of red pepper, chopped fresh parsley or basil, chopped walnuts or pecans and blue cheese.
Thank you, James Beard, for the original recipe.
What a waste of a nice piece of Gruyère.  I should have trusted my instinct to make a bescmel with it.  Instead I ended up with a gloppy mess that tasted fine but was not very appetizing.
This was the perfect recipe for using up leftover Thanksgiving ham and vegetables like green beans.
I also had an issue with the Swiss cheese not melting and suggest either a different cheese or a béchamel sauce. Also for a whole box of pasta would use more cheese.
i found this reasonably good as a hot mixture, but better as a cold salad.  in any case, I used 2-year swiss and didn't have the problems with texture that other cooks mentioned.

Oh delicious!  Thanks S.S. and Commenters.  Per you all, I splurged on two 4 oz slabs of BF ham at the deli and a chunk of Gruyere. Used 1/2 pound of shells since I like more manavelins than pasta.  Sauted cubed ham in unsalted butter with shallots and a minced garlic clove.  Be patient with this, I went for the browned crispy bits shown in the photo.  Pasta water is absolutely essential, save more than you need.  Yes, do lob in another large pat of butter and a handful of Parm.

Great recipe for the night you get home from vacation and don’t want to go to store!!! Only thing I didn’t have was the cheese specified so I used goat cheese. Delicious and next time I’ll grab Gruyère because I think that would be amazing. You need to make this if you can.
Have made something similar with marscapone. Delicious. And melted.
I cooked this for a church gathering.  We all loved it!  They thought it was a high scale meal!
A delightful read, as always, Mr. Sam.  And who knows!  I might actually make it.

This sounds wonderful, but given how much trouble I’ve had in the past with the cheese sticking to just about everything BUT the pasta, I think I will try it like one of the other commenters suggested: dump the pasta, ham, butter and peas into a greased baking dish, top with cheese, and bake in a hot oven until it bubbles.  I can’t wait to try it!

I have two teenagers who’re both incredibly busy, so meals are a challenge. This recipe is a God send when we want the warm comfort of Mac’n cheese but don’t have quite enough time to make it. I make this as is but substitute a good sharp cheddar because that’s what we all love.
Used half cup of parm as that was what I had and the dish was super easy and delicious!
Made with Gruyère cheese and meal tasted good with some added lemon juice and garlic.   The problem is it was a disaster to clean up.   I wouldn’t make it again.

title: Grilled Broccoli With Soy Sauce, Maple Syrup and Balsamic Vinegar Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
It would be nice to also give an actual recipe for those who might need it.  For some folks, even a 4 ingredient recipe might be daunting.  Something like: 
-2 tbsp soy sauce + 2 tbsp balsamic + 1 tbsp maple syrup + 1 tsp oil-- or something like that, for those who might need a little more rails around them.  

It would make a good cheat sheet for those of us trying to cook off the rails a little, but who are worried we might be far off the map.

Chrissy - you got it!  May be add 2 tsp. oil.  You are on the rails, don't worry.  This is one of the 'recipes' you'll fiddle with the outcome until a better roasted broccoli recipe comes along.  But this one is preeettyyy good.
Agree with Sage55 ... you've got this, Chrissy!

"It would be nice to also give an actual recipe for those who might need it."

But that is exactly the point: you *don't* need it.

(If, by chance, you really do, there are tens of thousands of recipes on this app, and only a couple hundred no-recipe ones. You can definitely find a strict recipe for broccoli, if that's what you're after.)
Is it wrong to start eating the smaller florets right out of the pan while waiting for the larger ones to cook? Do I have to share?
I love these no-recipe recipes but it would be SUPER helpful if you formatted the text (bold the amounts and ingredients). Very hard to skim while planning/cooking.

Fantastic recipe! Add some garlic chili sauce, and served over rice. We were fighting over the last bits. I will make again tomorrow night, this time adding sliced red peppers and cubes of medium firm tofu to make a full meal, and will serve with a wedge of lemon. I made enough sauce to have roughly 1/4 inch of sauce on bottom of pan, in which I tossed the broccoli a few times during roasting. There was a bit left that we scooped over the rice.

Try roasting broccoli at 450 degrees for 8- 10 minutes with a splash of olive oil, and garlic and onion powder. Perfect. (2) 2 T balsamic, 2T soy sauce, 2 T maple syrup (3)  Added a bit of fresh ginger paste to the balsamic-soy dressing. Made a generous amount and had extra to pour over my rice. Topped with garlic salt as well. Yum!

This reminds me (vaguely) of David Chang's fish sauce vinaigrette, which is the best thing to happen to vegetables ever. My slight variation on that is: 2 T fish sauce, 1 T water, 1–2 tsp rice wine vinegar or lime juice, 1 or 2 grated garlic cloves, 1 T maple syrup or sugar. Toss with burning hot roasted veggies or a pan full of charred, sautéed brussels sprouts, cauliflower, or broccoli immediately after turning off the heat. So addictive.
Try balsamic GLAZE, vs vinegar and skip the maple syrup. Perfect!
Best broccoli ever! And so easy. Tossed it like a salad, using just enough sauce to get it moist but not soggy. Baked on cookie sheet at 450for about 20 minutes. Delicious!
450 for 20 minutes was too long for our taste.  We suggest you check it sooner.
2 T balsamic, 2T soy sauce, 2 T maple syrup
We used 2 T soy sauce, 2 T balsamic vinegar, 1 T olive oil, and 1 tsp maple syrup. Grilled until charred and tender. Sprinkled with salt and some freshly grated parmesan. Delicious.
try balsamic glaze instead of vinegar and skip the maple syrup

I do this all the time, it's great! I like to pre-heat a cast iron pan in a 450F oven to get a good char on the broccoli. Fresh chopped chilies work well in this, as do toasted nuts to top it with (walnut or hazelnut are good).
I always like things less sweet than most recipes call for so I used 2T Balsamic and Soy Sauce and 1 TEASPOON of maple syrup and it was still far too sweet for me.
Look for Bal-Soy sauce. Made in Italy. Combo of soy sauce and reduced balsamic. Love this to cook with and to splash on. Great on broccoli.

I just made this and it is not a recipe of any sort. I enjoy cooking & am happy to "improvise" as Mr Sifton suggests, but he offers no guidelines on how much marinade to use per head of broccoli ("equal parts" implies some sort of measurement as a starting point). No guidance on cooking time. Having to toss food is costly and wasteful and certainly does not save me time.

"it is not a recipe of any sort"

It says, very clearly, that it's a no-recipe. If you don't want to use it, it's very easy to find a prescriptive recipe. Those of us who like mucking about a bit in the kitchen also pay a subscription fee, and it's okay if ONE recipe a week is designed for us.

Is it just me or anybody else doesn’t like no recipe? Then again I always follow exactly the first time recipe I respect the chefs on this site that 
is why I subscribe-  there is a picture of Julia and I on my refrigerator  really appreciate  the time and passion of these food authors -

I like the idea of an inspiration rather than a set of instructions. I cook from recipe boxes every week night and you have follow them to the letter, as the ingredients are all sourced for you. Now here’s a chance to experiment and add my own flair and preferences to the dish, as suggested by Barry.
Add pre-cooked crumbled/chopped-up bacon!
before or after cooking broccoli?
Try roasting broccoli at 450 degrees for 8- 10 minutes with a splash of olive oil, and garlic and onion powder. Perfect.
No need for the maple syrup. Otherwise tasty and easy .

Delicious! As a home cook in learning mode, I find comfort in following a prescriptive recipe, but I look forward to more no-recipe recipes. It felt liberating not to feel bound to strict set of ingredients. I enjoyed improvising with what was in the pantry without worrying that a substitution would ruin it. (My pantry's variation: cauliflower, orange marmalade, extra red pepper flakes, less balsamic. Served on top of rice & chickpeas, topped with lemon juice and scallions.)
Sounds delicious!
Added a bit of fresh ginger paste to the balsamic-soy dressing. Made a generous amount and had extra to pour over my rice. Topped with garlic salt as well. Yum!

This was on the menu when I visited my son and cooked dinner with him. He invited a few friends over including a neighbor, a young man whose wife was away on a 6-month military deployment. He said, "Ma'am, that was the best meal I've had since my wife deployed." About a week later, my son called. His friend told him, "I was dreaming about your mom's broccoli. Will you get the recipe for me?" A definite winner.

Grilled two skewers of dry scallops and an aluminum tray with the sauced broccoli. Together with a simple tomato cucumber salad, one of the simplest, fastest and most delicious grill plates we’ve had. The broccoli was a star. Will be trying more no recipe ideas. Thanks

Turned our to be a BIG hit even though I forgot the red pepper flakes and sesame seeds at the end, and my husband did not like the look of it as it came out of the oven.  The taste won him over! Roasted on a baking sheet at 400F.

I am always dismayed when I read complaints about  recipe that is called "a no recipe recipe", no surprises.  Having said that, I did equal parts of the ingredients as listed and the broccoli was wonderful and tasty.  Don't be afraid, go gently into that dark night of cooking "no recipe recipes"
I did not know broccoli could taste this good.
very good but easy on the vinegar
We amost never use white rice — quinoa or wheatberries are healthier grains, with a lot of protein.
Hey good about the healthy grains. I almost never use them, prefer basmati or jasmine rice once a week.

I've been making a much fussier version of this from a recipe that calls for marinated tofu, roasted with broccoli and tossing all together in a "pesto" of ginger, tamari and sesame seeds. It's delicious but this really sounds like the weeknight dinner I've been looking for. I'll keep the tofu for protein but eliminate the pesto. I'll also be on the lookout for furikake.
Used a simple aluminum tray... like those used for pre-made meals. Could also use heavy duty foil.
Why do you recommend broiling vs. roasting in the oven?

Definitely take the notes of others- I also would recommend cooking at 450 for 15 no more, and use foil. 400 for 20 min is probably fine too
I added additional garlic and ginger to the sauce, then sprinkled with toasted sesame seeds when they came out.

Request to have again! Broccoli not really soft enough but crisp. Florets have crispy smokey flavor  Make sure grill is hot. Best thing: sauce left in bowl.... put in sauce pan. Add some corn starch and garlic and onion powder and margarine. And more oil and let simmer till fairly Thick. Great sauce over broccoli and rice. Maybe try blanching broccoli to get it soft throughout, prior to grilling. Also try one broiler as well. Needed lots of broccoli for two dinner size portion with rice.
This is terrible! Not good at all. Don’t waste the ingredients on it. Yuck-o! Did I mention it’s bad? The char with the broccoli with the combination of the sauce. No bueno.

What is gained by making this a "no-recipe recipe"? There are non-negotiable ingredients (so important they're in the title) -- why not list them? There is a process -- why not write it in steps? There is obviously a desirable sauce:broccoli ratio -- why not share the measurements? Presenting a recipe in an unstructured, unreadable way makes it harder to improvise, not easier (what we educators call "misplaced rigor"). Anyone wanting to experiment won't let a standard recipe get in his way.
I use this same marinade for flank steak. Why didn’t I think of the veggie version. I do use equal parts of soy, vinegar and syrup.  Add garlic as desired.
Do you need a special grill pan to do this on the grill or do just put down a sheet pan?

Made this as directed, but it was too sweet-and-soury for my taste.  Won't make it again.  I actually like broccoli fairly plain, with a squeeze of lemon.  Tried this on a lark.  This sweet and sour recipe might be good to tempt kids to eat broccoli, but in my experience nothing works for that (that I'm willing to try at least, like dumping melted chocolate on top of the broccoli).

We liked this, but I did leave out the maple syrup.  There is a limit to how sweet I want broccoli to be.  The flavor is a nice balance of sweet and tart.  The balsamic really stands out.  A spritz of lemon juice might be nice.  I roasted it instead of grilling or broiling, but I'd like to try grilling it when the season rolls around.  
I think this would be really good on some short pasta.

It's winter in Canada so grilling is not an option for now. But when I tried this under the broiler, I found that either the flower parts got too charred or else the stems were too under-done. I think I'll have to cut the florets into much thinner pieces than I have been doing. 

Any other suggestions?

Consider roasting the broccoli. Preheat the oven to 400 F/204 C. Put the broccoli in a large pan so it is single layered (spread out), and roast for about 30 minutes (check them at the 20 minute mark they may be done).
I marinated a thinly sliced chicken breast in this combination and it was great!

Lessons learned after roasting the broccoli at 425 for about 20 minutes: definitely line the baking sheet with foil, and don't pour any extra marinade over the broccoli before roasting. I ended up with large pools of burned marinade stuck to the foil, big mess. Sesame seeds on top is a very nice addition.

This is great, I’ve made it a few times, with and without the sesame and chile. Just make sure it’s not sitting in too much liquid or it goes soggy. 

Makes a meal with a bit of tofu cubes added after you’ve cooked the broccoli. 

It’s next level awesome if you can get hold of purple sprouting broccoli.
Two tbsp soy and basaltic for 3 broccoli crowns. A little maple awesome. Amazing

"No recipe" recipes is how my grandma taught me how to make sweet and sour stuffed cabbage to die for with a mystery ingredient even you would never guess!!! And, her beef and barley soup so thick the spoon stands at attention. A handful of this and a handful of that... yada yada. I have 3 lbs of beautiful short ribs in my freezer I plan on preparing with a dry rub, then seering and then into my slow cooker with the same sweet and sour ingredients as the stuffed cabbage. Yummy
Broil high or low? How long?

I often change recipes anyway but I am not fond of no- recipes recipes.  The point is to see how someone else makes a delicious dish. If I want to experiment off of it, I will, as will others. I like reading what other people think of the recipes and how they change up the same recipe in the comments but I like having a common ground people go off of.
Wow! look like delicious but where’s recipe? Should i guess or predict it?

The very first paragraph is:

"This is a no-recipe recipe, a recipe without an ingredients list or steps. It invites you to improvise in the kitchen."

2 tablespoons each of balsamic vinegar and soy sauce plus 1 tablespoon of honey did it for me. 

Make the above mixture in a bowl and toss it with some florets in a large mixing bowl. Spread them on a baking sheet and set the oven to 450 for 20 minutes and let the magic happen.
I must have done it wrong but it came out soggy from the oven grill and not good.  Put it on a rack on the pan?

This reminds me of that mushroom recipe that tastes like bacon - that one we have in weekly rotation and the touch of smoke makes them addicting

I am going to add this broccoli recipe in too
Okay so don't use 1/4 cup for every 1 part... I used 1 and a half florets and that was waaaay too much. Flavors were outstanding though! Also added miso paste which was perfect.

Did this in the wok. Used sherry vinegar instead of balsamic, otherwise same ingredients. Threw in a little leftover roasted cashews and a little leftover coconut basmati at the end, sprinkled with a little sesame oil. Was a really tasty quick meal. Next time I think I will try the balsamic (or rice) vinegar instead, but it was still nice.

This was lovely. I used 2Tbspoon soy sauce, 2Tbspoon balsamic vinegar and 1Tbspoon maple syrup, and a tiny bit of groundnut oil. I also added a bit of minced garlic and red pepper flakes, 20 minutes roasting at 200 Celsius. I added the florets to a baking sheet with chicken legs, 20 minutes before the chicken was done, and had some rice with it. Yummy

My husband does not like roasted vegetables. So I steamed broccoli with a little water and butter then added the sauce when tender. Stir fried for a short minute. Sprinkled chili flakes and sesame seeds. Yum!
Adding cloves of garlic to the mix could only make it better! :)
Good. After cooking I tossed with Trader Joe’s chili onion crunch.

2tb soy sauce
2tb balsamic
2tb maple syrup
2tb Olive oil

Terrific side dish. We tossed the broccoli with 2 T of soy sauce, maple syrup, and balsamic vinegar and drizzled it with neutral oil. We then roasted it at 450 degrees because it was pouring outside so the grill wasn’t an option. My family loved it and ate every bit of it. Definitely a keeper!

For those recommending 2 Tbsp soy sauce - 2 Tbsp balsamic vinegar -- for what amount of broccoli florets is that appropriate?
Can one use frozen broccoli?
Also, if this isn't a recipe, then why do I have to pay to access it?

Broccoli crowns were on sale at my market, so I made this dish for supper last night and enjoyed it thoroughly.  I cut the broccoli into small and medium floret clusters but left as much thin stem as possible on each cluster.  I grilled the broccoli on the top of the stove in a grill pan, and I served it over rice (as recommended by Mr. Sifton).  I'll definitely be making this dish again.
Not sure if I can ever eat broccoli any other way from now on. Truly SCRUMPTIOUS!!

I made a mountain of this and all but a tiny bit was eaten by my table of 6 - kids included.  I found that the balsamic and soy sauce combo was genius and did that at a 1:1 ratio.   The maple syrup was a nice balance but kept that 1/4 in the ratio.  I roasted mine at 400 for 20 min. I was surprised how much of the marinade was taken in by the broccoli (where does it hide??) and remained after baking to keep it very juicy.  So you may not need as much marinade as you think.  Enjoy!
There is a grilled broccoli recipe without the maple syrup if you need more details.
This is a great "no recipe" recipe!

Broccoli florets
Oven to 450.
2 T of balsamic
2T soy sauce
1teasp maple syrup
Garlic chili sauce to taste
Spread in flat pan with little oil

Can be done over rice.
Roast for 10-15 min.
I pile the grilled broccoli on top of white rice, add some of the sauce to the bowl, and enjoy a vegetarian meal.  It's easy and delicious.
Add a healthy dose of freshly grated ginger to the balsamic-soy-maple combo -- brightens it up and great on all roasted veggies.  I use it as a dressing on my buddha bowls - sooo easy!
Does not really need syrup. Used a little grapeseed oil. really good!

Sauce: 2 T soy sauce, 2 T balsamic, 2 T maple syrup, 1 tsp oil; minced ginger.
Roast broccoli at 450 degrees 8-10" w/ splash of olive oil, minced garlic, and onion powder. Top w/ garlic salt. 
Add garlic chili sauce. 
Full meal: add sliced red peppers & cubed medium firm tofu, serve w/ lemon wedge.
I did equal parts soy, balsamic and syrup.  Way too sweet. Try 1 soy to .75 balsamic to .25 syrup next time.  Add red pepper flakes

Add some garlic chili sauce, and serve over rice. 
Add sliced red peppers and cubes of medium firm tofu to make a full meal
The dressing was flavorful but it overpowered the broccoli.  Might try again using less, or diluting with a little water.
Pretty good.
Used the 2 T each method, swapped balsamic glaze for the vinegar and syrup, added some cauliflower, and grilled it.  Loved it!
Much better roasted than as written. I poured the sauce all over the broccoli   garlic and roasted at 450 for 35 min. They came out crispy instead of soggy like with pan frying.

Following the suggestion to improvise, I used fresh asparagus, added slivered almonds, and put under the broiler until it looked done. Served over saffron brown jasmine rice, garnished with chopped egg and red pepper flakes. Delicious and easy.
This did not work out well for me. The broccoli was a bit dried out and and way to strong n flavor due to the balsamic vinegar. I don't doubt this could be good if prepared well.
Absolutely delicious as a raw broccoli salad too!
Good without maple syrup.  Do not overcook.
Although expensive, the furikake is OH SO Worth it

Cut good size spears length-wise after trimming the ends and grilled that way. It took about 9 minutes over no-longer very hot charcoal. no risk of falling through the grates. Added flaky salt at the end but forgot the pepper flakes. Easy and delish with grilled rack of lamb. Cooked the broccoli while my lamb rested.
is very nice

Didn’t care for this at all. I used two tablespoons each of soy sauce and balsamic vinegar and one tablespoon of maple syrup, with maybe half a tablespoon of oil, and marinated it for maybe ten minutes. It had very little flavor. I guess I could have marinated it for longer, but I wouldn’t bother making it again, as Ali Slagle’s recipe with mustard and red wine vinegar is far better and just as easy.
Easy and tasty. Added mushrooms and birdseye chilis. Took 14 minutes in the broiler. Flipped about halfway. Added a little more sauce at the end.

made this with 3T balsamic, 3T golden mountain sauce (pretty similar to soy sauce but with more of a rounded flavor?) and 1 heaping T of maple syrup, plus some ginger paste, garlic powder, and onion powder as per one of the comments. baked at 400F until dry. my kitchen smells incredible, would recommend for that alone

2 -3 heads of broccoli cut up. Used 1/3 c soy and 1/3 c balsamic, avocado oil  to coat and a good drizzle of maple syrup. Gave it a good shake in a bowl w a lid to coat every piece. Spread coated broccoli in single layer using tongs on a parchment covered baking sheet and put into a 425 degree oven for 10 minutes then broiled for 1 minute to crisp up. Pulled from oven and sprinkled with red pepper flakes and toasted sesame seeds. Flavor packed bunch of broccoli!
425 for 25 mins. For 1 head of broccoli, 1 used 2T of balsamic vinegar, maple syrup, olive oil and liquid aminos instead of soy sauce. Delicious.

title: Quick-Broiled Pork Chops With Peanuts and Gochujang Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody:
It helps you progress as a home chef.
Nudges you in a direction an encourages you make it your own.

Really liked the ease and taste of this dish.  I think next time I will brown the chops on top of the stove in an iron skillet on the first side, turn off the heat and flip to more gently finish the other side.  Added the orange zest to the sauce.  Served with chunked rainbow carrots and lightly blistered shishito peppers.

Really good and pretty easy.  Went with the ingredients as prescribed since I’m new to Asian cooking.   Watch the peanuts, they burn quick.   Go heavy on scallions and have lots of rice ready.   Paired with Brussels Sprouts tossed in extra sauce.  Fun St. Patty’s Day feast.
OK, so how is this different than a recipe, except using words like wisp   , so we have to guess?
I think the idea is for for more imaginative cooks to envision different takes on this dish.

We liked this for an easy dinner. Also didn’t have gochujang so used chile-garlic sauce. I broiled the chops another 4 minutes on the second side and that was too much. Should have been just 2 minutes.
Reduced sauce before broiling pork chops.  Made with cauliflower rice as side dish. Worked well together.
Ok for those of us who don't eat mammals - wondering if this would work with pan cooked and thinly sliced chicken breasts - Korean schnitzel if you will...
I haven’t made this, but it sounds to me like it would be terrific on chicken. Also, I’m thinking chunks of zucchini would be tasty broiled alongside the chops and coated in the sauce.
could I substitute Siracha for the Koren sauce. Its not something I'm likely to have in the house.. thx

Sriracha is significantly spicier than gochujang and gochujang has more of a fermented umami depth to it. So rather than a straight substitution, it might be better to mix sriracha with miso and maybe some tomato paste.

Gochujang is fermented, so you won't get the same flavor from soy sauce.  You might try sriracha + miso or fish sauce  to add the umami flavor the sriracha is missing.  Gochujang keeps a long time, and is getting easy to find in the grocery store though.

My first recollection of seeing these no-recipe recipes and I love it. No rules. Be creative. Any kind of meat or any kind of sauce works. For me it's switching out chili powder for gochugaru (Korean pepper flakes) and Shaoxing instead of Mirin. (It's much cheaper...lol) Maybe sprinkle a bit of rasped ginger on top.
This flavor profile was so good!  The orange takes the whole thing in a beautiful direction!!  Next time I’d like to try it with sautéed pork medallions, increase the amount of sauce and reduce it.
yummie!

Yummy indeed--and since the whole no-recipe idea is that we "wing it" (with chicken breasts, perhaps, when "pigs [don't] fly"), toss peanuts & sesame oil in a small bowl with sliced scallions, top the almost-done meat with the sauce, and then top it all with the peanut-scallion mixture--watching like a hawk (sorry!) to prevent it burning--another minute should do.

Hot coal fired grill for 4, tossed in sauce, back on for 3..
Super good!

First time I used too much mirin. Was ok, but too sweet. Second time I used less mirin & it was much better. Served with rice, steamed green beans & roasted yellow squash. Today, I chopped up leftover pork & veg then tossed it with rice, peanuts, scallions & last drops of sauce as a rice bowl. So good. Did not cook chops under the broiler. Used my usual method: heat skillet in oven @450F. Brown 1st side of chops on stovetop on med-hi about 1 min; flip & finish in oven for a few min until done

Great flavor profile on this dish.  Broiling the chops seemed an extra step, so I browned them  in a screaming hot pan (the same pan used for the peanuts, wiped with a paper towel) and then deglazed with the orange juice.  Then I just made a pan sauce by adding the gochujang and mirin.   This will be on repeat.

This was a hit at my dinner table. I had the butcher slice six thin pork chops, almost a half inch thick. Didn’t have Gochujang, used sambal oelek which is a cupboard staple for me. What’s a wisp and does that matter in a no-recipe recipe? I had a Honeybell tangelo and no oranges in the fridge. Two tablespoons of sambal oelek to most of the Honeybell’s juice and several shakes of mirin made for a tasty sauce. Too thin, next time, less juice. The leftover spiced peanuts made for a nice snack.
This is a perfect recipe for sous vide cooking.  That approach allows you to use a thicker chop (but, only if you want) and still have juicy meat and the exotic/spicy flavors of the sauce.

I should have preheated my broiler longer--just under 5 minutes was not enough, so the chops took longer to cook. I also should have salted more thoroughly than just a sprinkle. I didn't have gochujang or orange juice on hand so I used sambal oelek and pineapple juice. The final result was tasty and would be even better with a hotter broiler and more salt. I will try this again.

Made this for my family. I could not get thin chops so after grilling I seared them then poached them in a large skillet with the sauce to ensure they were cooked through. Serves with jasmine rice and a bottle of Riesling.
Tasty made as directed. I served it with a side of watercress namul and sticky rice. Even better as leftovers when chopped up and made into fried rice and topped with the peanuts and scallions.
This is really good with lightly sweetened homemade stewed apples.

I made a "variation" of this. I didn't have peanuts so I used almonds, they turned out so good and crunchy. I also didn't have OJ or mirin so I used some rice vinegar and Worcestershire sauce instead. It turned out really amazing! I love the broiler method, so quick and the chops were so juicy still. Definitely going to make this in some form again!

This was so delicious and I’m very proud of my first no-recipe recipe! I broiled the pork chops in a pre-heated cast iron. I think next time I’ll brown them on the stovetop to get more color and then finish them in the broiler. I couldn’t find chops that were super thin, but I just used whatever they had at WFM and they worked just fine. I served them with broccoli roasted with sesame oil and soy sauce. This was an easy weeknight meal and was even executable when I was a zombie after work.
We love it, we use cashews.
Use roasted sesame seeds if you don't have or want peanuts.

Made exactly at outlined, obviously with my judgements on sauce measurements, but we absolutely LOVED THIS. So simple yet full of flavor and beautiful presentation (I did add cilantro along with the scallions for garnish). Definitely going into rotation. Thank you, Sam Sifton, for making weeknights simply delicious.

title: Peanut Butter Sandwich With Sriracha and Pickles Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: Day 1 of the Coronavirus lockdown. My roommates and I stumbled upon this recipe with low expectations. We just made this sandwich once with PB, sriracha, and thinly sliced dill pickles. It was so good that we made it again immediately after -- this time with thicker cut dill pickles, sambal, red onion, and PB. It was even better. THIS SANDWICH SLAPS.
What kind of pickles...sour or sweet?

A revelation! Let's face it: for adults, the strength of classic PBJ (for hiking and such) is convenience. But PBJ is too sweet, even with marmalade. THIS is an adult sandwich and just as easy.  Don't forget the tamari as suggested.  And next time, to achieve the "next level", I'm adding a delicate mist of---wait for it----fish sauce. (I know, I know.  But I can't wait.  In fact, I think I might just
Any update on the addition of fish sauce?

Tried the fish sauce: interestingly, this sandwich has such strong flavors as written that I could hardly taste it.  So, solely in the interest of upping the oddity, I will continue to include fish sauce.
Bread & Butter Pickles are the way to go. I have tried them all, and the combination with peanut butter is surprisingly delicious!

This sandwich reminded me of  Vietnamese flavors so I tried country white bread toasted with lots of peanut butter, thinly sliced cucumber, and a handful of cilantro.  Wow, it was delicious!   Sorry to brag but, hey, I am sharing my discovery with you in hope that you will like it too.  Next time I might add a bit of basil or mint and a squeeze of lime.  BTW,  I love NYT's cooking recipes.  Thank you for many yummy meals.

As a kid we ate pb & pickle sandwiches because that's what was in the cupboard. We also ate brown sugar & bean sandwiches, saltine cracker cereal, and cocoa made from Nestlé Quik, hot water & evaporated milk.

PB & my Dad's Bread-n-Butter pickles. PB and Bacon.  The kids at school laughed at my lunches - little did they know what they were missing.  Then there is the old 'cocktail spread' standby - PB mixed with ketchup.  That's turning the clock back.  Still delicious!
@NHStormie If I didn’t know better, I’d think you grew up in my house.

Did a taste test comparing PB (Jif) & bread & butter pickles to PB & kosher dills, same amount of sriracha added to each.  Both were tasty and crunchy, but had too much sriracha (my fault!), the heat dominating the other flavors.  Sample with kosher dills was salty and sour and mildly sweet.  With B & B pickles, it was sweet, salty, with an end note of sour, a more complex flavor mix. Next time I’ll put just a very small dab of sriracha with the PB and stick with the B and B pickles.

My dad used to do a peanut butter and bacon sandwich - you broil it until the little slivers of bacon are done and the pb is cooked and brown on top. Heart-attack on a slab of bread, I know, but insanely delicious. And, for me, nostalgic.
I grew up with peanut butter and bacon sandwiches! Soooo good!
Toasted whole grain bread + natural p.b. + sliced pickled jalapeños = :-)) !
I could see that being amazing, I once tried an appetizer of whole pickled jalapenos stuffed with a mix of pb and chutney. I dream of them still.
Peanut butter and pickle sandwiches on toasted sourdough! 3 generations ate it in our household. My parent grew up in the bay area in california. Never sweet pickles only dill pickles here 😊

I made this today for lunch - it did not disappoint. I like regular peanut butter and pickle sandwiches, but this took it up a notch. So many flavors in every bite! It will definitely go into my rotation for lunches. I used bread and butter pickle slices.
I have been making peanut butter and gochujang sandwiches since I saw Mr. Sifton mention them once in an article.  It's really outstanding.  I can't wait to try the addition of pickles.
Can we get a definitive answer on what kind of pickles?
I would imagine sour pickle- as he states sweet and sour and I don’t see any other ingredient being sour
My long time fave for this combo is TJ’s sliced dills (from the jar) and crunchy PB on rye toast. Don’t knock it ‘til you’ve tried it. Now I MAY try adding a dash of hot sauce.

Either Sweet & Sour or Sweet, preferably Sweet & Sour. The best is to cut the pickle into chunks big enough to add a pop and explosion of pickle against the other tastes and textures. Dill, we never did. But, I see folks do.
Try adding a little fresh cilantro in there, too. Yummy

This was such an unexpected pleasure. I made this sandwich using two loaf ends, Maggi seasoning sauce, Cholula hot sauce, and Trader Joe's hot and spicy dill pickle chips. The pickles provide satisfying tooth to the sandwich while peanut butter holds ingredients and flavors together. The layered salts compete for the palate's attention, as one makes another shine and then again. To the readers asking about the type of pickles: seriously, choose that which will make you happy.

Dear Sam:  without an ingredient list it's hard to tell...are those sweet gherkins you're using?  I can be convinced to try this either way, but want to try the "correct" version first (at least)!  Thank you -

He mentions in the narrative that the sandwich combines "sweet and salty," and I think the pickles are the only ingredient that could bring the sweetness.  So I think those are sweet gherkins in the photo.
I grew up eating pb and tomato sandwiches.  I don't know where this originated from but it's something my parents ate and served.  It's quite good.  Try it.
I grew up with this, too. So delicious! Always reminds me of home and Mama.
Me too! It was from my French Canadian grandmother: pb, mayo, tomato, lettuce. Yum!
We eat this with our chosen family at the beach every year. Thick slabs of perfectly ripe tomatoes with freshly ground crunchy peanut butter and some mayo or hot sauce or whatever!
My husband courted me years ago - 35 to be exact with just such a sandwich  when he met me for lunch at work.   His was PB, red onion, mayo and sprouts - not one but two - on wheat. I was smitten.
I grew up on peanut butter and sweet relish sandwiches - they are still a favorite - and also love peanut butter and pickled jalapeño.  I can’t wait to try this!
Add jelly and red onion to that pb and jalapeño sandwich!
I was skeptical, but my first bite made me a convert! I'm definitely adding this to my rotation. Next time I won't skimp on the soy sauce, either! Delicious!
Reminds me of my dad’s favorite: peanut butter, mayo, onion and lettuce. I think I need to try both now.

I have been eating PB, thin sliced onion and Mayo for years- desert hiker sandwich- PB for energy, Onion for crunch and Mayo to help it all slip down...
often elicits an eeww, but try it!
My father used to make those! I loved them. Everyone else said EEWW, but the flavors go remarkably well together.

We do an "adult" peanut butter sandwich variation we saw in NYT years ago. Treats peanut butter like a vegetable spread. Wheat toast, natural peanut butter, cucumber, pickle, mild cheese like havarti.

Just stumbled upon this recipe last week and brought it up with a coworker. Some other people overheard and before I knew it my casual mention of the recipe spiraled into a full-blown lunch hour with 10 coworkers where we tried different variations on this sandwich. The result? Everyone loved it!
A peanut butter & bacon sandwiches are a family favorite. Might just try a splash of sriracha...
A different, somewhat Thai, savory pb sandwich is: natural pb, sliced cucumber, grated carrot, sriracha, some mint and/or cilantro.

Sliced in half then toasted a multigrain baguette. Sweetened crunchy pb on one side, light smear of sriracha on the other, decent layer of sliced bread & butter pickles, and few leaves and stems of coriander on top of the Pb. Drizzle of low salt soy on the side with the sriracha. Yum.
Extremely delicious and simple in the best way. If adding pickle seems too outside the box, try it with cilantro first. It's fresh and savory, a perfect mouthful.

peanut butter and sriracha is great, but adding Western pickles is disgusting.  I could, however, see this with Indian pickle; I would recommend an Andhra-style lemon or lime pickle. Try Priya brand lime if you do not make your own.
Better have a side of pepto with you

I call it the Sad Sifton.

I make it frequently.
I was totally open minded and tried this. It didn’t blow me away.  What am I missing? I used crunchy PB, dill, Pickles, sriracha, and a dash of soy sauce. It was fine but just ok.

@Chris 
Sam’s recipe says “sweet & sour” pickles. Dill was ho-hum but bread & butter   sciracha & a dash of soy or tamari is a game changer

I just pulled Melissa Clarks Anadama bread out of the oven, shmeared crunchy peanut butter, bread and butter pickles and way too much sriracha (which is perfect for me!), sat down and tucked in. There was so much flavor in the short list of ingredients. Thank you Sam Sifton, for this amazing no-recipe recipe.

This recipe reminds me of a favorite chip-dip consisting of 2 parts peanut butter & 1 part hot salsa (from a jar, not fresh). It’s best made & consumed alone, as an alternative to ice cream at midnight.  Not kidding!
This was amazing. I added cilantro as was suggested by a fellow commenter and it was the most thrilling sandwich I’ve had since quarantine. Thank you, Mr. Sifton. Thank you.
Really good with chili crisp. That crunch...
I make a very similar kind of peanut butter toast with gochujuang, and nobody believes me when I say it's delicious. I'll have to try adding the pickles!
@Rachel I make the same and often add cucumber slices for coolness and crunch. Pickles are not far off, but they’re not as crunchy and may be a bit too salty for pb & gochujang.
Surprisingly simple, and delicious.  It's become my "go-to" snack.  Equally delicious on crackers!
I'm assuming you use sweet pickles?
Dill pickles are the best!
Dill.

Definitely dill! My husband introduced me to this combination when we were poor graduate students. Sweet pickles make the sandwich too sweet for my taste. Out of curiosity, I looked up bread-and-butter pickles (sweet) to find out why they're called B&B. Depression Era ingenuity yet again. Bread, butter with sweet-and-sour pickles.

We always had peanut butter and cucumber sandwiches. The cool brightness of the cuke cuts nicely the warm sticky PB. 
Also had sardine and pickle sandwiches, on rye, of course. I loved them, but they weren’t popular in the school cafeteria. Sigh.

I once made a peanut butter and chipotle sandwich by accident - I keep some chipotles en adobo in the fridge in an old jam jar, and I wasn't paying attention. The result was savory, salty, smoky, and just a little sweet, though a bit spicy even for me.
I have made PB and siracha sammies w/pickles for years!!!! Dill pickles, not bread and butter pickles.  Also try PB, thin sliced cucumber and kimchi!
I make a few variations of this. My favorite is PB, sriracha, cilantro, and green onion with a squeeze of lime. Yum!
Try the peanut butter, siracha and pickle relish on a hot dog. Amazing!

There isn't much I won't eat and very little I don't like.  I went into this with an open heart, mind, and mouth.  This does not taste good.  It is a strange concoction. 

It cannot be untasted.

Delightfully repulsive! So, I had to try it. Plus I especially love sambal olek! Results? Something to add to my "refrigerator-empty-what's in the pantry" repertoire. Made it with a drizzle of tamari, rather than soy sauce. To assuage any doubts (terrors, actually) about it's nutritional value, here's a few points: 1) peanut butter provides protein; 2) tamari is fermented from soy, so it's probiotic; 3) pickles--um, cukes are veggies, right? And, if you use enough sambal olek, it'll cure a cold

I grew up in North Dakota and ate many many peanut butter and pickle sandwiches (usually with a layer of plain potato chips for a little more crunch)! To this day I love the combo, but, despite my love of Sriracha, had never thought to add it. So delicious! Now, this is my new favorite combo!
delish!

I'm really glad to see the two most recent comments. I was excited to try this and made it last week, and found it "meh." After re-reading the enthusiastic reader comments, I thought maybe my first impression was off. I just made one today, and I have to say I dislike this sandwich so much that I will never have it again. Clearly, it's an individualized taste.

Definitely delicious; definitely calorie worthy. Saw this an had to have it. I made this with Dave's 21-Grain bread, PB, Sriracha and topped with Wickle's Pickles - spicy and sweet. I'm eating it right now with my forehead breaking out in that 'sriracha cold sweat'.  Calling this PBS&P.
This is up there with your Grilled Cheese and Kimchi sandwiches.   A new standby in our house.

Peanut butter, candied jalapenos, shredded purple cabbage and carrots, pickles if you want them, cilantro - scads of it, tamari and fresh lime juice on toasted bread. that's the hot ticket right there. Sure, add your choice of meat if you're a carnivore, but it's totally not necessary. Mix your tamari and lime juice with the peanut butter before building the sammie if you want. You'll figure out how best to layer the ingredients to suit yourself. No rules!

I’ve been eating PB  and dill pickle sandwiches all my life- it’s my favorite, but never thought to add sriracha. Will try it.  I can’t imagine it with anything other than dill pickles or maybe bread and butter, but definitely not sweet pickles.
Each bite is a holy note. Or, wait! Make that unholy.
Also recommended: peanut butter and kimchi sandwiches.

I grew up eating pb and dill pickle sandwiches, and I've always loved the sweet and salty mix. I often take pb (always crunchy!), gochujang, and sliced baby cuke sandwiches to work for lunch. Sweet, spicy, crunchy deliciousness!
Petition to rename this “The Umami.” This ruled. If using unsalted peanut butter, it’s worth adding a little more soy sauce to bring the salt level up.

I've been a peanut butter and onion (+ sometimes pickles) sandwich eater for decades. I always try to explain to the skeptical that it's got the vibe of a spicy peanut sauce. Chili-garlic crisp is another great addition. So glad others are discovering the joys of the savory peanut butter sandwich.
I used to make peanut putter, lettuce and mayonnaise sandwiches for me and my Dad when he was divorced and being a bachelor. I felt very grown up and they were delicious.
I have eaten PB and Tomatoes to this day (I'm 76).  Also PB and sliced Banana. I must try Bread and Butter pickles.
I’m obsessed with the sandwich. My favorite version is made with crunchy natural PB, lightly toasted seeded bread, a drizzle of soy, and Wickles (a spicy bread and butter pickle). So. Good.
A new favorite, open-faced on well-toasted chewy dark bread, using real PB that you have to stir. But why just a spritz of sriracha? Squirt it on liberally. And B B pickles are the way to go.
This sandwich is attaining cult status at my work. Recommend unsweetened peanut butter and dill pickles. Don't skimp on the sriracha! I use toasted sprouted multigrain bread for extra heft.
Nicky Dover.  Thanks for the laugh during these stressful times.  I almost fell off my chair laughing.
@Thea Koshman, I couldn't agree more re: @Nicky Dover's hilarious comment!
A sardine and a slice of green mango or apple make this even better (thanks, Vilayvan!).
I made this with Kimchi instead of pickles. Delicious!

My husband and I approached this food experience with considerable trepidation.  After one bite, we were pleasantly surprised.  But I felt something was lacking.  Another reviewer noted thinly sliced red onions, which was the key.  

Experiment results:  toasted multigrain bread at room temp worked best.  We agree to disagree on bread and butter vs. very vinegary dill pickles.  Guess it depends if you like sweet or sour.  

To quote another reviewer:  THIS SANDWICH SLAPS!!!!

Ok, just made this and I don’t know how it’s good, but it IS! 
I used Famous Dave’s Signature Spicy Pickle Chips which are a bit sweet and bit spicy. Dill would work I suppose, but I think this sandwich needs that bread and butter sweetness. It’s a non-recipe though so that’s why things are somewhat ambiguous. You do you. 
Don’t forget to toast the bread!

Oh my goodness, this is so delicious and satisfying! Chunky Teddy’s PB, hometown summer squash pickles (a variation of bread and butter) and a generous slather of Sambal Oolek. I use my favorite GF bread (there are some good ones available now). Man, what a weird combo but unexpectedly delicious! Thanks, Sam!
This is my new favorite food. I shared a photo with some people at work though and they were so grossed out they threatened to turn me in to HR

There are a lot of folks in the comments asking "what kind of pickles?", and that misses the point of the "no recipe recipe". 

Personally, I don't like sweet pickles, but dill pickles were awesome on this. You might choose bread & butter pickles. Or crunchy peanut butter. Or Sourdough bread. Or add onions. Or do all the other things that people usually do with recipes, which is to say: make them your own.

The pickles are up to you, my friends. The pickles are up to you.
Added fish sauce and a little MSG. Boxed cabernet to finish it off. Arrest me.
Try it with Almond butter!

Sweet gherkins, crunchy peanut butter, low sodium soy sauce & sriracha are definitely the way to go. The sweetness, the crunch, the spicy and saltiness remind me of an Asian spicy peanut sauce. Absolutely oddly delicious.

Peanut butter and dill pickle sandwiches were a family classic growing up but I found it lacking as an adult.  I realized the the type of peanut butter made the difference.  Jiff or Peter Pan containing sugar was the perfect amount of sweetness to balance the dill pickle.  These days I use non-sweetened natural peanut butter so the slightly sweet bread & butter pickles work better.

I have been enjoying peanut butter and pickles -- always sweet -- all my life.  Yes I am from Alabama, and am almost 79 years old.  Now I am more likely to do it on Ritz crackers instead of bread, and my favorite pickle for this is the hot sweet pickled jalapeños from Trader Joe's.  The beverage of choice is always ice cold Coke Classic.  My favorite lunch when there are no good tomatoes. That's another story.
i have been eating p.b. and dill pickle sandwiches on toast my whole life (I don't like p.b. with jelly or honey).  adding the sriracha made for a nice zip.
For me, it has always been natural style crunchy peanut butter on toast with chopped black olives.  Sometimes with the addition of lettuce.

I have made this so often that I now crave a deconstructed snack plate:

Thick and crunchy sweet pickle rounds, topped with a dab natural peanut butter, topped with a pea-sized squeeze of sriracha (or chile crisp).   (Salt the stack lightly if the PB is unsalted.).
Add mayo. Cuts the stickiness of the peanut butter and adds tang. And ground black pepper.
I make a very similar sandwich, using gochujang instead of sriracha and soy sauce. The gochujang adds a similar combination of spicy, salty and slightly sweet, and spreads really easily on bread.

Made this with Big Spoon Roasters Crunchy PB, NYT homemade chile crisp, diced cornichons, and a little red onion.
Spectacular
my ex husband used to make this with jalepeños instead of pickles much to my disgust. sent him this recipe to give him the satisfaction of being right for once.

This might be credited to the south but I grew up eating peanut butter and pickle sandwiches in southeastern PA in the 1950s. Just plain peanut butter, a thin sliced Jewish pickle fished out of the pickle barrel at the grocery and soft squishy white Stroehmanns bread.

Yes, I was skeptical but went out of my way to buy B&B pickles to make this. Glad I did, it was very good with unexpected smokiness. Really surprising complex tastes…don’t leave out the soy sauce. I  will definitely put this into my rotation of breakfast toasts next to my other favorite PB   squeeze of lime   drizzled Sriracha   fresh cilantro toast.
Also great:  peanut butter and pickled jalapeños!
The flavors together were interesting, to say the least.  Not bad, but not my favorite either. Would I make it again? For sure, in a pinch, but this will not be my go-to sandwich.
Wowza! Ran out of bread & butter pickles so substituted Wickles Relish   the recommended tamari drizzle.
I grew up in Nebraska on this sandwich- minus the Sriracha, but am excited to add this new flavor to the mix. The only other thing we added was a thin layer of mayo- this can't be beat!

I made something like this sandwich by accident a few years ago, and it was pretty great. I was going for a regular PB&J, but the J wasn't what I thought it was. I have a color vision deficiency, so I sometimes confuse things that would be obvious to other people. In this case, I grabbed some leftover whole chipotle chiles in adobo that were hanging out in a used blueberry jam jar, and before I knew it, I had a peanut butter and chipotle sandwich. Eye-wateringly spicy, but delicious.
The best of this recipe is all of the reader comments. Does anybody else read the recipe, think I’m going to make that, and then go right to the comments? I knew this was gonna be a good one.
Peanut butter with hot tomato chutney from Nervous Nellie's Jams & Jellies ftw!  But I will try this too!
We've been doing this for DECADES! Peanut butter 'n pickles.
Me, too. Always sweet pickles!
PB,onion,sardines plus pickle and condiments of choice
Had this two nights ago, rather late, without the pickle. I know what I'm having for lunch.
Peanut butter, lettuce and mayonnaise on sourdough. My go-to sandwich whenever the larder is lean.
I used to love this combo as a child. I had forgotten! I see one in my lunch soon!
Peanut butter, bacon and pickles!
So true!
