I think it must’ve been sibling rivalry but as a child my wife would make a peanut butter and bologna sandwich with miracle whip.  The kids sometimes had contests to see who could make the strangest sandwiches.
I didn't have any pickles, so I substituted apricot preserves i had sitting in my fridge, and that sweetness added a great balance as well! really satisfied my salty and sweet teeth!
My fav is peanut butter with cold crispy cucumbers! Yummy
This past Thanksgiving I had my first pickle & pnb sandwich at the challenge of my fiancé. It was good, but I can't say I'd eat the whole sandwich. But maybe I will with Sriracha! ;)
Peanut butter toast with sambal oelek has been a go-to snack/breakfast for me for years!  Can't wait to try adding a pickle!
I love open face peanut butter and kimchi on toasted whole grain bread! The best!

I've been relishing crunchy almond butter, smashed avocado and sliced pickle 
sandwiches for a couple of years,and love them. I like to use Wickles pickles, which have a habanero bite to them, and sprinkle some of the juice over the avocado and almond butter. The best. I had no idea that this is a thing in the south...when I describe them to others, they usually say 'ew'.
Wow wow wow!  Don't skimp on the pickles and sriracha.  Too good for kids!

We grew up eating peanut butter and ketchup sandwiches which I needed to retry recently. It was good and this sounds delicious too! Pickle doesn't sound too bad either but we were from the north, French Canadian roots.

Peanut butter, Pappy’s bread and butter pickles and Hellman’s. I think on whole wheat toast?  Sounded weird to my classmates in a very un-southern elementary school, but one of my favorite sandwiches growing up. Thanks for her memory and inspiration!
Dare I say [gag] and disagree with everybody else? ;-)
Peanut butter, sliced tomato and miracle whip. Yum!
I like to think I invented peanut butter (crunchy) and kimchi sandwiches. On buttered toast!
Made this exactly as described using our homemade dill pickles and home-baked multigrain bread. The pickles are much like Claussen's kosher dills. Liked the taste but found it far too salty.
I went with sliced kosher baby dills and it balanced wonderfully with the sweetness of Jiff.

I have tried this a few times this week. I prefer when the ingredients are applied one at a time, rather than mixing the soya and sriracha with the peanut butter before applying to the toasted bread. When applying individually, the pb gets melty. The consistency changes with sriracha. I used sweet gherkins, and egg twist rolls.

I’m on board with everything about this sandwich. Unfortunately I found it to be underwhelming for all the hype. I’ll probably mess around with it more, maybe trying different pickles or bread, or subbing in gochujang for sriracha. Also, maybe it’s better with a regular peanut butter instead of natural, no sugar PB?
It’s definitely better with sweetened peanut butter and good crunchy kosher dills (I love Clausen Hearty Garlic).

I was really excited to try this because it's so "weird," but man it really just did nothing for me. Was one of those times where I just tasted all the individual ingredients but didn't understand why they were together. Maybe need to try it again.
Not sure, but for peanut allergic crowd, would ALMOND or CASHEW butter work as well?
Yeah, it’s a no from me dawg
Yes! My favorite pb sandwich is with pepperoni and baby spinach. Addictive.

This is my most favorite sandwich from long ago when my mom would fix this for my school lunch.  Everyone else thought it was bizarre, but I loved it!  I use dill pickles for this, but sweet work just as well.  Can't wait to try it with the toasted bread,  sriracha and soy!  Thanks for championing an often misunderstood sandwich!
I’ve made this for years. Peanut butter and kimchi is also good.
I invented this in the 1990's as a post workout routine.  Specifically it consisted of blackened pumpernickel rye toast, peanut butter and Sriracha.
As a kid my mom always made me peanut butter, sweet bread and butter pickles, and Miracle Whip sandwiches.  Still one of my favorite comfort foods.
Put peanut butter and onion on a BLT made with caraway rye toast.  The L makes the sandwich unstable and adds no flavor and can be left out.
Yes, I can see this!  Sort of a thai flavor profile, to me.  I would sprinkle cayenne or red pepper flakes rather than sriracha, but that's my preference and probably splitting hairs.

My mother's side of our family has been eating peanut butter and sweet pickle sandwiches thru at least 4 generations. I insist on chunky peanut butter, others creamy. I like them with sweet pickles that were canned with hot pickles. Best snack ever.

So your finally catching up with me! Good on ya!
My favorite peanut butter mix is:
grill or fry a hot dog (the more burn the better)
toast a hot dog bun,
add a layer of peanut butter to the bun
drop in the dog
add a little more peanut butter to your liking, and then
(drum roll please)
Chop some olives stuffed with pimentos and add to the top.
Viola!
You'll be amazed at the complexity of flavors. And I guarantee that no one will want to take a bite out of your dog.

The favorite sandwich in my family was peanut butter, mayonnaise and lettuce. I've been eating it as long as I can remember. The contrast of peanut butter and mayo is amazing and the lettuce adds a satisfying crunch. I am definitely going to try pb and pickles for lunch today.
Definitely use dill pickles and 12-grain bread. Sambal and soy take this over the top!

Dill pickles. The picture looks like dill pickles and the sweetness comes from the peanut butter and the sour from the pickles. I grew up on PB and dill pickle sandwiches and find them more appealing than with sweet pickles. I will be trying this and similar variations in the future.
As I grew up in the 70s, my dad and brother added sardines to this sandwich mix, sometimes a little mayo too.

I did trust you and, despite being a life-long peanut butter hater, I throughly enjoyed this snack. I would suggest sambal badjak as an even better choice than sambal oelek or over-the-top sriracha. Spicy piccalilli also works.
Been eating peanut butter and pickle sandwiches for years. I add lettuce and mayo. Next time I'll add sriracha as well.

I *love* seeing this recipe and thanks. Several years ago I tried a peanut butter-infused foie gras at now-shuddered 'Tailor' in Manhattan. Cut to 2013, a man with these ingredients: Loaf of Arnold's Multi-Grain Bread; Spicy Crunchy Cheetos; Teddie's Chunky Peanut Butter; Stop 'n Shop Yellow American Cheese; Butter chip pickles; Hunger. Grilled stacked cheese. How I had thought to whip out the Sriracha, mere centimeters away. Next time!

I made this with toasted bread, spread with creamy peanut butter, homemade sriracha, Vlasic Bread and Butter slices and a drizzle of soy sauce - sublime!  I was prepared to hate it but both I and my spouse really liked it!!!
Mayo and jam. It's begging for mayo and jam!
Easy snack, tasty enough. Used dill pickles and crushed red peppers since I had no B and B pickles or Siracha.

Wow! This sandwich was something I never knew I needed - now I'm addicted. Made as suggested and used cornichons because that was the only pickle type we had in the fridge. Still worked, but can't wait to see what a sweeter pickle would bring. Everyone who loves peanut butter sandwiches should try it.

Stumbled on this just last night and couldn’t stop thinking about it. Used some homemade B&B pickles and used Trader Joe’s Chili Onion Crunch- open face style toast- very unexpected YUM! Definitely recommend a chili crisp for some crunch in addition to toasted bread
My favorite summer dinner. Great with lemonade. I know you're a skeptic, but I'm including it anyway, in hopes that one day soon, you'll give it a try.
I was taught years ago by a college roommate to make with dill pickles. SO good. I am sure that any pickle will be delicious. Going to try with bread and butter pickles next time.

I mixed the peanut butter with some soy sauce, rice wine vin, and fish sauce and it was perfect! Used bread and butter pickles, topped with a bit of sriracha, sesame seeds, scallions. Will definitely be making again!
Made this once with grilled chicken breast and no pickles.  I would recommend chicken thigh the next time. Definitely adding B&B pickles for a more complex flavor.
This is my new favorite thing! Used bread and butter pickles and crunchy peanut butter. It’s incredible!

have eaten pb and dill pickle sandwiches my whole life.  the comments inspired me to try bread and butter pickles (and sriracha!).  sriracha - great, bread and butter - too sweet.  i never like pb&j or pb and honey, either.  but it's always good to experiment.  pb and aged cheddar on sourdough toast is great, too.
I grew up in Ohio eating PB and mayo sandwiches.  Didn't know it was 'weird' until I was all grown up & living in New York!  It's still my favorite & I would totally add bacon to that.
LOVE PB and pickle sandwiches, with either bread & butter or dill pickles -- both good!  Or any pickled veg -- thinly sliced radishes, carrots, daikon, turnip.  PB is also terrific with kimchi.
I used Frank’s instead of sriracha because I didn’t have any on hand! It was still really good! I would have added another pickle slice or two and a bit more franks.
This has become a goto solution for me.  Really a fan and definitely endorse dill pickles.  Adding red onion sounds like something to try next time.

I've always loved (crunchy, for me) peanut butter and SWEET pickles.  Interestingly, it's been a recent topic of conversation.  Most people haven't heard of that combination, so it's such fun to find this "non-recipe" and to read everybody's notes.  I'm intrigued by those who have used dill pickles and liked the sandwich.  Also, the idea of adding sriracha or soy is interesting to me.  I'll have to try that!  Thank you, everyone!

Interesting that the link is from “weird” lunchtime sandwiches. Weird is the reaction I get whenever I describe my childhood lunches of peanut butter and...
We used regular dill slices—the trefoil shaped ones from a jar. We also broiled bacon on top of the peanut butter, and crushed potato chips worked nicely as a topping. Pimento stuffed olives, sliced and arranged over the peanut butter were a favorite.
My wife swears by peanut butter, jelly and summer sausage.
It reminds me of an on-trend version of my grandfather's favorite - Peanut Butter, Bacon, Ketchup and Lettuce on toast.
Ahh, I fondly remember the sweet pickle and peanut butter sandwich from my youth 70 years ago . Proof that the long term memory survives!

Growing up, our family dubbed this as "Mud and Alligator steak sandwich." As an adult (for a really long time now), I now specify Clausen's dill pickles and siriracha sauce mixed in with the PB.  Guess we kids would have added "rooster" somewhere in the title.
amazing combo! i used sweet bread and butter pickles but any pickle would be great

Just made this, and zoo-weeeee mama its a good one. I added bacon and grilled it grilled-cheese style with a little butter. Will definitely make again and again. My wife saw my face when I told her how good it is, and she said you look very happy. She was right...I was very happy.
My mom made these with her dad’s bread and butter pickles, iceberg lettuce and mayo. Yum!  Will be updating the family recipe to include sriracha mayo. Now to dig up Pappy’s pickle recipe...
this is oddly good - I've been making it on Wasa crackers, with dill pickle slices.
Oh wow, just tried it - used dill pickles and sprouted wheat bread.  Thank you!
YUM. Excellent idea. I used a dill. Perfect
I did not love this. It was more odd than good.
Don't do this with whole grain bread - do it with delicious toasted sourdough!!
Amazing!!  Reheating some smoked brisket and had some bread and butter pickles to serve with it. While waiting on the brisket tried this on a few Ritz crackers as snack.  Will do again and again.

I love this sandwich. When I make it, I like to add a slice of Swiss cheese and some bacon. First I cook up a piece of bacon, then I assemble the sandwich. I put the sandwich in the skillet and toast it using the bacon fat.

I was curious to try this, and then I liked it so much I made two back to back! With dill pickles it’s pretty much a salt bomb, so next time I’ll buy some bread and butter pickles... that’s right, I’m going out of my way to make it again.
A favorite of mine when pantry options are low; peanut butter, (dill) pickles and mayonnaise. You need a thick layer of each. Don't be scared, try it!
Absolutely DELICIOUS! Thank you Sam!

Try this combo on a hamburger!  It was really good. I went light on mayo on one side, light peanut butter on the other, dill pickles and a drizzle of sriracha.  I’ve tried the peanut butter sandwich before and liked the flavor but always felt slightly ill afterwards ... just couldn’t mentally get around it, haha. But adding the burger made the whole thing pull together for me & mentally more palatable. So if you’re also squeamish but want to try the flavors ... add a plain beef burger!
It sounded so awful that I just had to try it. This actually was REALLY yummy!

Like most commenters I was INCREDIBLY skeptical, but I really enjoyed it! I used honey peanut butter so it was a nice sweet and salty combination.

TOTALLY recommend this!! At least try it!

Definitely use bread and butter pickles. In my first experiment I tried bread and butter pickles first, followed by dill. The dill overpowers the flavors. I have prepared this numerous times since my first try two weeks ago. Love it! Actively promoting the recipe to disbelievers

This recipe shouldn’t work and at first I thought it was a parody. After reading many of the hundreds of 5 star reviews I took the plunge...
Delicious! Sweet, sour, crunchy, umami goodness in toasted bread. I’m off to make another!
I was super skeptical of this but my instinct told me it could be good, and it was delicious! I used gherkins, peanut butter with chia seeds, sriracha and a dash of soy sauce.
What kind of pickles, dill or sweet?

B&B vs Dill? Why ask Sam? Folks, this is explicitly a no-recipe recipe so try it either way or both then decide what YOU like. I’ve loved PB and TJ’s sliced kosher dills for years but in the interest of science I now plan to try PB and B&B.

Delicious and nutritious; I have this regularly for lunch. I sometimes replace the Sriracha with Lao Gan Ma if I want a little more flare. The chili oil and the peanut butter work well together. I've also found celery is a great substitute for the pickles if you're out or want something a little less salty.
This is my new favorite sandwich. Made it with gochujang and it was so, so good.
I made this with homemade dill pickles. It was much better than expected. I would definitely take this over a PB&J any day!

Is what I thought with a satisfied nod when I tried this strange combination. Delicious, and takes on its own flavor as a sandwich (more than just peanut butter and pickles).  I’ll pocket this for some easy pantry lunches for weekdays to come.
A perfect blend of spicy, salty, sour, and kinda sweet. Would never have guessed that this would work!
We tested this with multiple pickles and Wickles were by far the superior choice.
10/10 recipe. Add some red onion and DOUSE it in siracha. I like to scrap some bacon fat that I have leftover on the bread while it is still warm.
I use cholula instead of sriracha, I prefer the savory heat on this as opposed to sriracha a sweet heat

I finally got around to trying this recipe with ingredients I had on hand, and was surprised at how wonderful it is! I used freshly ground peanut butter, sliced bread and butter pickles, and gochujang on a gluten-free baguette. The pickles add a nice crunch. I would definitely make this again (and again), and look forward to trying the less-sweet original recipe.
Toasted Sourdough, crunchy peanut butter, sriracha, mint, cilantro & chunks of dill pickles. Fantastic. Gonna keep experimenting on this one.
I recommend pb with garlicky kale on whole-grain toast -- fab.

I would like to try this I have been making peanut butter and tinned sardine sandwiches for sometime, unconvinced my family and friends thought it a novelty for attention and then there is this recipe. Thank you, I'm not weird!
Tried this on a leap of faith. Turns out it's too delicious for words. This is fast becoming my go-to pantry snack/light meal!

I’ve always liked my pb sandwiches either sweet or savory, and have branched out to pb and caramelized onions or apples once in a while. My father turned me onto the pickles and hot sauce method that this recipe touches on, which works really well, but now I like pb and pickled jalapeños with drizzles of honey and hot sauce.

I had forgotten what a deliciously surprising and surprisingly delicious  sandwich this is. I've made it with dill pickles, bread and butter pickles (including the delicious thinly sliced strands of pickled onions) and gherkins. Next up - pickled beets? That sticky smooth texture of peanut butter begs for a bit of acid. And that pickle crunch is so satisfying. And the sriracha holds my attention. No mindless sandwich eating here.
I did a riff on this.  Ritz Crackers, creamy peanut butter, bread and butter pickle and a dot of hot sauce.  BOOM. this combo kind of overwhelms every taste zone at once   Crave it!
Sooo good! This really works with pickled anything, dill or sweet, any hot sauce or sambal. Add some cabbage or sprouts for more veg/crunch & cilantro. Yum thanks!
I thought I would hate this...but then I made one for a snack one day. Peanut sauce with pickles about sums it up. I couldn't stop eating it.

I grew up eating PB and Kosher Dills (cuz I ate too many garlic dills and of course smelled like garlic) but I crushed potato chips into the mix! Delicious! I now add hot onion! I think I originated chips on a Sammy, lol! Been eating them for almost 60 years!

When I was a kid, one of my favorite snacks was peanut butter toast with taco Doritos. Later I added pickles. A coworker told me about Sriracha with peanut butter, and compared it to a Thai peanut sauce. Reading all these comments, I feel validated.

Have done PB and pickle sandwiches with mustard (bacon optional) for years'n'years. The best part is serving to inexperienced friends, whose eyebrows and noses go all crinkly when you describe it. Something about pickles and PB. Yet they invariably experience enlightenment with the first sandwich.

I thought I might be the only one who grew up on peanut butter and bacon on toast.  I still make it somtimes...so delicious!!....also I've had peanut butter and mayo, PB and tomato...but not pickles!  I'll use the jalepeno dills that I love, and maybe soy. won't need the siracha.
Seriously one of the best things I've eaten lately, including among some high faulting' restaurant entrees. Had it for snack dinner tonight, and I'll be having it again tomorrow for lunch.
I’ve been eating PB and dill pickle sandwiches for 20 years and I’m happy that they’re being popularized.
I have been making peanut butter and pickle sandwiches for years! My kids thought I was out of my mind, but no matter the variation, it's really great!

Embarrassingly so good I ate it for breakfast, lunch, and dinner today. I used "bread and butter" pickles that are super crunchy and added a lot of sweetness. I may try gochujang in place of the Sriracha and crunchy dill pickles since the gochujang has more sweetness than Sriracha. This combo is SO good.

A great pantry meal or snack! I used siracha, gluten-free soy sauce, Wickles Pickles (spicy + sweet), and crunchy Jiff on Carbonaut toast. The point, at least for me, is to use what I have on hand rather than copy someone else's pickles or bread. I expected to throw it away but didn't!
I’ve been devouring pb and the hottest salsa you can find open-faced sandwiches for decades! Try it.
Extremely good!

My first attempted this was very good! I’m looking forward to adding more pickles and a little more Sriracha next time.

Based on a similar NYT article several years ago, I put a bunch of pickled jalapeños on a peanut butter sandwich. It’s a staple for me now; this sandwich seems to be a slightly more complex version of that.

I've been eating these for decades (since the 1960's) - It started out when I was about 10 years old and put pickles on my PB sammy cuz I love pickles! In about 3 years I added hot Chile sauce (Thai style). My recipe: natural PB, Dill pickles sliced, hot Chile Sauce (I've use one from my local Korean store), a dash of curry powder spread on some lovely, slightly toasted dense bread. YUMMO!!

Crunchy PB, preferably Teddy’s no salt extra crunchy. Wickles pickles, sweet and hot. Bacon, fried crisp.  A couple of thick slices of ripe tomato and a shmear of mayo. Sub banana for the tomato for a sweeter vibe. These are a family tradition of long standing.
I found this combo on my own on a desperate evening when I needed dinner quickly and was sick of ramen and egg dishes. Fast, relatively healthy, and quite delicious! Try it.

PB and pickles sandwich was a lunch bag favorite as a kid, always on mom’s homemade bread. On a lucky day there might also be a small bag of leftover potato chips, which if they weren’t smashed already, my sister and I would crush and add to the sandwich filling. Salty and crunchy, unctuous, and sour, nothing wrong with that.
This changed me. I eat it all the time now! I do open-faced on toast, and always bread and butter pickles! It’s so good!
OMG. Brilliant! Enjoying many variations for breakfast. Breakfast of champions!
I usually start my day with a PB and banana sandwich, so eager to try a lunch version. Didn't have sriracha but used chile crisp, delicious! Will now try the variations suggested in the comments.

This is delicious! I recommend using bread and butter pickles. Great idea for an appetizer!  After it's prepared, cut into four pieces and plate it. Intense and complex. Will make a great conversation piece.  And for those who overdo the sriracha, keep Kleenex nearby.
As a teenager, 60 years ago, my favorite after school snack was peanut butter/pepperoncini sandwich. Loved it!

Who knew? This is so delicious! I’ve been singing its praises ever since I (skeptically) tried it. Ignore your presumptions and just try it. I only had smooth pb and I’m going to get chunky just for this.
Add sliced apple and sauerkraut or kimchi!
I am 70. I have been eating pb and bread & butter pickles since I was a kid. Love the added crunch and touch of sweetness.

This sandwich was great! I skimped too much on the sriracha and would also increase the soy sauce.  I had dill pickles.  My wife had B&B with hot honey instead of sriracha.  My mother ate pb and dill pickle sandwiches, adding when possible leftover slices of pot roast Or Thanksgiving turkey. Thanks Sam Sifton!

I finally tried this, but with a twist. For pickles, I used Wickles relish (sweet with a kick) and then I used Momoya kimchee base instead of sriracha purely because I love it. So tasty and umami-laden, I will do this many more times!
For a little snack, I used Ritz crackers, creamy peanut butter, and zesty bread and butter chips. Didn’t love it with the sriracha, but the pickles have kick
This is *outrageously* good. I will be making this a lot.
Instead of pickles add hagelslag!
Yum! Sweet and hot pickled jalapeños for the win! On toasted wheat   sriracha   soy sauce.  And peanut butter of course. I think I’ll add iceberg next time.

I’m not crazy about pickles. My best friend, who lives in another state, loves them. We FaceTimed making and eating this sandwich for lunch. She thought it was fantastic. I thought it would be fantastic without the pickles. Peanut butter and sriracha - brilliant!
Regarding the dill vs sweet pickle issue, I have a middle ground. I use dill and then drizzle a small amount of honey, which always goes good with Sriracha, anyway.
To bring this to the next level - make it as a hot dog instead of a sandwich.
Was a little suspicious at first, but open to trying. Result — Sooooo goooood!!
I have been making this for years and still asked the question if I am pregnant. Pro tip- add mayo to one side of the bread.

Growing up in Nort Carolina in the 69s we used super crunchy home made bread and butter pickles. Also loved peanut butter with leftover bacon from breakfast and leftover sage flavored breakfast sausage patties with jelly.
People didn’t believe me that this was good until it was in NYT. Used to eat this backpacking w Tapatio.

I live in NZ and I’ve always found PBJ sandwiches too sweet (and soggy) and PB alone in toast is too ‘meh’.. but in NZ we have Vegemite and a thin scraping of that with PB in toast and it is perfect- Vegemite is a yeast extract with a very strong flavour of umami - so I can see this pickle and PB sandwich being a winner in my books.
I have been making and eating PB&Pickles sandwiches since 1977. I might have to try Cholula sauce instead sriracha.

I am 88 years old and have been eating peanut butter  and pickle sandwiches since I was 6 years old.  But the pickles are sweet or bread and butter pickles. As an adult I began toasting my bread... unless I am too hungry to take the time!  The pickles not only add flavor but texture (moist and crunchy) to an otherwise gummy sandwich.  Ditch your PB and J for PB and P... It works with saltines as well (flaky and crunchy).  Delicious, nutritious and expeditious!

title: Miso-Glazed Eggplant With a Bowl of Rice Recipe
author: Sam Sifton

comments.commentBody: Cut 1 large eggplant in half & then into 1-inch thick steaks. Roasted for about 30 minutes. Flipped once; will flip 2-3x next time. Broiling sauce = 3T miso   3T toasted sesame oil   3 T rice wine vinegar   1.5T soy sauce   black pepper. That amount of sauce allowed liberal application on the steaks. Broiled for 5-8 minutes. Served over brown rice with scallions.

My mother does an Iranian version of this for me with saffron rice and roasted tomatoes. My absolute favorite meal. So yummy and healthy!

I have to try it with sesame seeds!
Yum! I want your mom’s recipe!
I made this with coconut brown rice (cooking the rice with a cup of coconut milk and some minced ginger) and topped with pickled ginger. Delicious vegan dinner!

I had regular eggplant so halved lengthwise, turned flat side down and cut again lengthwise into fillets & scored thicker ends criss-cross. I'll cut my fillets thicker, 1", & score one side of each this way in future. (I do this with halved Japanese eggplants and it'll be good for this too.)
I added ginger to the mix and always will. It elevates this dish to simple but sublime. As do scallions and toasted sesame seeds.
I got distracted and things got a bit toasty, but that turned out ok.
I sauteed it in an iron skillet and it was good too.

I'm all for the semi-improvisational approach, but those who haven't had this dish before might be better served by consulting Martha Rose Shulman's recipe, on this site, for "Miso-Glazed Eggplant." Alternatively, you could try googling "nasu dengaku." What you add to the miso, and in what proportions, actually matters a good deal, and you're also going to want to heat that miso mixture on the stove before painting it on the eggplant. (You need to melt the sugar Mr. Sifton forgot to mention.)

I hate to wait, so I make some basmati rice in a rice cooker, portion it into half pint containers, and freeze it. The microwave has it hot and ready in ~3 minutes or less. I see people throwing left-over, cooked rice from the take-out in the garbage. Makes no sense. There's always a use for it somewhere.
I added some hoisin sauce (about a quarter teaspoon but I didn't measure) to the sauce that I sauteed the eggplant in. It made it taste less sharp. I'd definitely do it again.

I'll cut my fillets thicker, 1", & score one side of each this way in future. (I do this with halved Japanese eggplants and it'll be good for this too.)
I added ginger to the mix and always will. It elevates this dish to simple but sublime. As do scallions and toasted sesame seeds.

A simple and very scrumptious. Absolutely, délicieux! Served with a bit of red pepper crushed for some heat. Make sure to bias cut eggplant not too thin. I prefer a thick cut but not too thin.

Bon appétit!

Cheers,

This is easy and delicious, and tastes just like it does at a restaurant. I used regular eggplant cut in halves and cooked it for about 35-40 minutes with three or four flips, adding more oil halfway through, before I added the sauce. After adding sauce, it cooked for a little less than 10 minutes before it was done. I added pickled plum to the rice I ate it with to add some flavor there too. This would also be great supplemented with a sushi roll. Will definitely make again!
I used red miso (will try to find white miso for future cooking). This was tasty and cooking the eggplant before broiling was a good tip I will add to my toolbox!
I’d like to add tofu for some protein.

I'm all for vegan cooking and love the NYT commitment to promoting more vegetarian recipes . More attention to nutritional value would be appreciated: the protein in this dish is derived from a few sesame seeds and the recommendation to serve over brown rice. Amending with 'for a complete meal, add cubed tofu or legume of choice' would still make for a fantastic no-recipe recipe. If you're otherwise getting animal protein, it's okay as is.
So delicious!!! I made extra sauce and brushed it on some tofu to broil as well. Served with scallions and sesame seeds. Also added ginger to the sauce, as was recommended in comments.

Ok WOW. I struggle with eggplant but this was SO good. I could only find the large ones so I cut it length wise into 4 pieces, and let it sit with salt for 30 minutes before cooking. The sauce was delicious! I added some Yuzu I had in hand, for some heat. I poured it on top afterwards too. Used toasted sesame seeds and scallions over rice and massaged kale to make it a full meal. WOW will definitely make this a regular.
Delightful simple meal after weeks of travel and indulgence. Reminds you that rice and vegetables are always here when you get back.
This was perfect as is, and I added some tofu to the pan for protein.
This works equally well with small zucchini or summer squash.

Yum! I’ve been trying to make something like this for years. I lived in Japan for 4 years in the early 90’s and glazed eggplant was one of my go-to’s, but I’ve never found it done right in the states and never got close enough in my attempts. This is the real deal. Even my eggplant-hating wife loved it.

Followed no-recipe pretty closely but added splash of ponzu. Now the only issue will be finding Japanese eggplant, which my local Whole Foods never has.
I have brown miso.  Is there a substantial between white and brown miso?
This was great! The eggplant was terrific & we made enough for BBQ chicken.
Please explain how to cut the Japanese eggplants on the bias? Is that lengthwise or crosswise? I’m not sure.
diagonally

Ok so I did this no recipe recipe last week - and had some sauce left. So tonight I bias cut a Chinese eggplant, and cubed half a package of TJ tempeh. Warmed some oil & browned both. Then I dumped in the leftover sauce, a splash more soy & a few cups of raw spinach. When it was wilted, added some leftover brown rice & got everything nice & hot. Served with sesame seeds and a chopped scallion it was AWESOME. My eggplant hating meat loving husband asked if I’d make enough for him next time :)
I really enjoy a lot of not recipe recipes but this was a total bust. Ugh. Stir fried broccoli and other veg to round out our meal and so glad I did, the eggplant was so unpleasant.

I usually like not recipe recipes but this was a total bust. Stir fried broccoli and other veg to round out our meal and I’m glad I did. The eggplant was just ick, and I usually love eggplant. Bummed.
can you use red miso instead?

Absolutely. I substitute red miso for white all the time and it works great in marinades. It has a stronger flavor but holds up to robust seasoning. It also may have a higher salt content. Check the packaging if this is a concern.
Skip the oil,  I just basted the "steaks" with a concentrated miso soup from paste and air fried until golden brown,  about 7 minutes
3/23/24. Made.  Pretty tasty.  Not sure worth doing again. G seemed lukewarm.

I’ve done potatoes accordion style before and the recipes suggested laying 2 chopsticks alongside the spuds when cutting to keep from cutting all the way through. That might work perfectly for this sausage.
In addition to being delicious, you can also teach diners about archispirostreptus gigas, the giant African millipede.

Re: millipedes.  We grill ours on skewers separated with cicadas (in season).  The plentiful legs get nice and crisp and are best eaten like corn on the cob.  The cicadas add a tempting, crusty flair with creamy centers.
Leftovers go into our legendary Vermin soup. Do take the time to skin and bone the mice.  
Serve with a sprinking of recently deceased crunchy cockroaches. 

Sorry, everyone.  It's just that kind of day........
@Erica Halaburda Fabulous!
I'll try this but that was truly the most frightening looking picture I have ever seen, especially after watching a video of a Chinese Luna Moth's life cycle.
Millipede was the first word that came to mind when I saw the picture.
I agree and I used to Janowicz as a last name.

This looks strangely good. But put mine on a bed of sauerkraut (onions browned in good lard/bacon drippings, bit of caraway). German mustard, Bavarian pretzel on the side.

But you know, that sausage looks like something that slithered out of those 70s era cookbooks--the ones that use gelatin molds for everything. Why have plain old kielbasa when you can hasselback it and set it in aspic? And for that perfect retro touch, add sliced pimento-stuffed olives for eyes. Serve on a bed of lettuce. :)
Good Halloween dish. Looks like something Tim Burton would serve.

I must have sauerkraut with kielbasa. It’s a rule!
Roast under the sausage. Yum yum!
And, if there are leftovers, they go in the scrambled eggs next morning!

Made and served with Julia Moskin's Hasselback potatoes, both fabulous and used one pan, too. Gulden's for the mustard, and put the leftover mustard/apricot preserves on the table for extras. The combination of the glaze and the many cuts seemed to allay the greasy mouth feel I've always not cared for with kielbasa. Cabbage sliced in thick (1") rounds and laid on same pan for last 10 minutes was perfect too.
The cabbage was a great addition--thanks!

I invited my mom over for dinner last night and served her and my husband this strangely good recipe. All three of us loved it. 
 I didn’t have apricot preserves  so used the peach jam that I had in the fridge, mixed with spicy brown mustard. 
On a separate sheet, I baked at the same time several slices of stale Chiabata bread which I had smeared with some butter. Piled high with the pepper underbase, it tasted like a fine bruschetta. And yes, I let the kielbasa crisp up a little.
Made it exactly as written and served with a side of potato pierogis. YUM. Definitely a keeper. Will make again.
Same here!!
This was really tasty!  I added a couple of sliced yukon gold potatoes.  My husband loved it!
I’ve made this often enough that I now keep apricot preserves in my refrigerator. I don’t mess with this “recipe.”  It’s too good as is.
Is the preserves /mustard mixture shattered on everything or just the kilbasa?
I put the mustard/preserves sauce on everything.

YUM. Used Dijon mustard and Kirkland honey. Added some asparagus I had on hand. I tossed the veggies in some onion and garlic infused oil I had leftover from making sour cream & onion dip for the Super Bowl. Served it with warm French bread.

Absolutely delicious. The peppers and onions were delightfully sweet. I used orange marmalade with whole grain mustard for the glaze, and roasted baby potatoes and Brussels sprouts on a separate sheet pan, which were a nice addition to the meal. Adding this to my list of keepers.

I made this with 2 links of kielbasa and added some small potatoes with the onions and peppers.  I didn’t have apricot jam so I made a Dijon mustard and honey mix to put over the sausage at the end.  I almost left it out but happy to have added it because a few minutes under the broiler at the very end caramelized the kielbasa deliciously.  Loved by children and adults alike.
I did the roasted potatoes too! So good!
Cooked with cabbage and potatoes
Delicious! Made with andouille sausage and lime-cilantro rice and crystal hot sauce which complemented​ the apricot glaze.
Love this! Also tried with chicken apple sausage and put apples and onions instead of peppers. Easy and tasty
Aside from looking unsettling, it's, well, gimmicky. Slice it thicker all the way through then you get the crispy bits all over the slices and it far easier to serve. Add potatoes and there you go.

This is comfort food to the nth degree. It has become our go-to supper when we just cannot go on. I substituted red pepper jam for the apricot. It gave it a little kick that left a pleasant afterglow in my mouth.

I just did this. No peppers, because COVID 19 but I added potato skins because I had some in the fridge. Fat kosher hot dogs because I am kosher. Made one serving, because sheltered at home alone. 

 I thought it was simply delicious. I will totally do it again. The hot dog curled and separated like the picture. The onions got a but jammy. The potatoes got a bit crispy.

The only bad thing is that I ate fast because I enjoyed it. I guess not such a bad thing.

This came together just like it was posted online - I scored a frozen kielbasa while grazing through the freezer instead of venturing out. 

My college freshman's eyes lit up at the prospect of the whole meal - the rest is April 2020 history. This will be a backup plan for years to come - Perfect example of why I love reading what to cook this week w/NYTimes and Sam Sifton! You make us home cooks look good! 

Cheers!
I have had this preparation with Jewish soft salami beef sausage as an appetizer.  Really good.
So yummy and easy. Added Brussels sprouts but they were not a good addition on day two. Try steamed greens like Sam suggests next time. But very salty. Get sausage in Hamtramck next time.

This is quite good.  I used kielbasa from the local Polish market (Golemo’s Market in Worcester, MA).  I used two peppers and two medium onions, but otherwise cooked as directed.  I basted one half of the kielbasa three times, heavily, with the preserves and mustard.  The other half I basted twice lightly.  We preferred the lighter basted side, as that side of the kielbasa was more crispy.

I’ve made a version of this for years now, but never thought of adding the mustard and apricot glaze. It added a lot of flavor! I love learning new tips like that to add to my back pocket inventory of no recipe recipes.

Delicious!  Made this with a skeptical husband. Used Major Grey Chutney because that was on-hand - and wowzers!  Super Yummy. Husband was most impressed and probably our new "I don't know what to have for dinner, let's do the really good Kielbasa thing again"

I love polish sausage (kielbasa).  This was good and a change from how I usually make it when I’m not going the sauerkraut route.  I saute a variety of bell peppers, onions, and thinly sliced red potatoes in oil then add the sausage sauté till browning, then a bit of chopped garlic and a sprinkling of red pepper flakes.  Serve with warm baguette.  Simple and delicious for weekday or Sunday dinner.

So good!! Some addl tips:
1) burn the skin so it’s crispy
2) add sriracha to the basting
3) make extra jam+mustard for dipping
4) eat with boiled cabbage or sauerkraut to cut the fat&saltiness
I didn’t have peppers, so I made this with Brussels Sprouts and onions - and substituted honey for the apricot jam in the glaze. Still came out great!

LOVED this! So quick and easy! Used turkey smoked sausage with apple butter and spicy brown mustard glaze. Veggies on hand were asparagus, cherry tomatoes and a shallot. Finished off with a quick broil for some color. Amazing!!!

This was so easy and delicious. Great on a cold night when I really didn’t want to spend much time in the kitchen. I used turkey smoked sausage. Added Yukon gold potatoes. Don’t skimp on the mustard sauce.
Good advice on the mustard sauce. I did skimp, as my husband isn't a fan of mustard, only to have him "oops" finish off the extra mustard/jam combo!

Simple and tasty. I didn't have any preserves so I mixed dijon and raw honey instead. Won't blow your mind, but it's a great platter to put out for a party or  a small dinner. The veggies on the sheet pan were my favorite part—make sure to let them caramelize nice and good in the oven!

I really enjoyed this take on kielbasa. I had both kielbasa and a homemade apricot jam in the fridge so I made this. I also roasted baby potatoes, whole mushrooms, onions and baby zucchini with it. Tip for cutting hasselback style, I put a wooden chopstick on the cutting making it even board aside the sausage and only cut as far as the chopstick allowed making the cuts of an even depth without accidentally cutting all the way through. I will put this in my regular rotation using other sausages.
Added quartered yellow baby potatoes and cooked them with the onions and peppers for twenty minutes before adding the kielbasa. Super easy and super tasty!

I have been making Kosher salami hasselback style for years. Mix 1Tlbs each of yellow, Dijon, and honey mustard and orange marmalade. Shmear the mixture all over and between slices. Roast in a 375 oven for 30-45 minutes until brown and a little crispy. Serve as an appetizer with party rye and sliced Swiss cheese. Always a big hit at parties.
Added some sauerkraut to the pan at the same time I added the kielbasa. No notes otherwise, it's a phenomenal recipe.
Is this using fresh kielbasa or smoked? We're close to Greenpoint Brooklyn and have access to both from the Polish Butcher shops.
It looks like smoked.  Fresh is lighter color.

I don’t know. If this is a fresh kielbasa, I’d wager that’s not enough cooking time. So, as another responded posted, clarification as to a fresh or smoked ring in this recipe. Also if it’s smoked, and you cut into the ring before baking, you’ll risk drying it out. If you’re going to do it this way, I’d suggest putting the smoked coil into a deep skillet, fill half way up the coil with water or cheap white wine, cover, and bring to a simmer for about 10 minutes. Then add it to the platter.
I added broccoli and some jalapeños to the red and yellow peppers and onions. Nice change for kielbasa from sauerkraut. Husband liked the crispy sausage top. How do I add a photo of mine?
I also spritzed the veggies very lightly with balsamic vinegar.

The slicing was a bit much for me.  I just heavily scored the top and sliced 3/4 way through into 4 servings.  Made butter braised savoy cabbage for the side.  Added thin sliced red potato w/the sweet peppers and onions.  Mustard on the side.

Suggestion:  Lay it between a pair of chopsticks.  They will act as a stop and keep your knife from cutting all the way through.  It works well with potatoes, and in summer with tomatoes, into which I tuck fresh mozzarella and basil and drizzle with balsamic vinegar.
added canned sliced potatos. crisped them and the sausage. delicious
Holy moly this was good. Made it exactly as Sam suggested, though served it with mashed potatoes on a very cold Minnesota winter night.
Mashed potatoes are a great idea--thank you!

This turned out to be easy and delicious!  I used a sweet onion and red and green peppers.  After reading some of the notes, I decided to make the sauce with honey and mustard and served it all over mashed potatoes, perfect comfort food for a cold (-22F) Montana night dinner!
This turned out perfectly.  I also added an Anaheim Pepper in addition to the Red Bell Pepper.  Next time I’d like to try a “Jalapeño Mustard” with this recipe.  Perfect weeknight dinner.
I added quartered, small Yukon Gold potatoes, Brussels sprouts and a sprinkling of Aleppo pepper to the pan.  This was a delicious and speedy meal and my finicky, teenager gobbled it up.

We loved this.  We did not have apricot jam, but did have a sweet and slightly spicy pepper jam.  We combined that with stone-ground mustard and added some thinly sliced potatoes to the vegetable mix.
Where do you purchase Hasselback kielbasa?
Any kielbasa.  Hasselback is the technique of making thin slices without cutting all the way through.

Oh my goodness, this was sooooo good!  I took this opportunity to clean out the veggie drawer, fennel, zucchini, leeks, garlic scapes, onions, peppers of all colors, it was incredible. Definitely going onto the holiday rotation!
This is on regular rotation at my house now. It’s fast and easy to have the ingredients on hand. Faster than take out for a busy night and really good especially with great bread.
I have to say that I find the readers' comments to be both informative and entertaining in this, as well as other, recipes.  Thank you all!
Sausage and Peppers, robbed for your pleasure. Ok but seriously, I made this for lunch today; along with pickles, spicy mustered, bread, and egg salad I threw together. Husband and kids enjoyed it.
We've made this several times now.  Great weeknight meal.  I like to use the bags of mini peppers - a little easier to prep and a little sweeter.

A note to Mr. Sifton.  I used honey and red peppers with the Hasselback kielbasa and dijon mustard, cooking on top of the stove and it was so delicious!  Poetry goes well as a dessert along with a little candlelight for this prosaic but great dish.  Thank you!
Cooked as suggested. Loved the dish. Thought the sauce added a lot. Next time I make it I will at least double the peppers and onions.

I made this last night with a glaze comprised of honey and spicy brown mustard, and cooked the sausage atop lots of peppers, onions, sauerkraut, (as many others suggested), and potatoes. My husband was floored by the flavor and thinks I am a genius. This is the perfect recipe for a crowd, and I will be making it again and again. Thank you, Sam Sifton!

I spread boiled cabbage leaves on tray with recipe's suggested vegetables. I added corn, chopped brussel sprouts & whatever extra I had on hand to create a pad for each kielbasa.   After baking I served each with a dollop of sour cream.
I used a glaze of apple cider jelly and Dijon - the kids have asked for it a second time!!
Fun idea. Tasty and easy for "times like these".
25 minutes at 425 sounds like just about the right amount of time to roast some chopped cabbage wedges, too.
Used orange marmalade and dijon mustard to baste. Delicious!
That was so easy and SO good! I didn’t have the preserves so I used a teriyaki sauce for some sweetness. It was really good and my teenage son loved it and can make it himself. Bonus!
In Sweden I ate it with slices of tomato stuck between the cuts. ‘Oven baked Falu sausage’ it is called.
Use apples and Brussels sprouts
Made with roasted red potatoes, peppers, onions and tomatoes. It was amazingly divine!!  Have to admit our kielbasa was homemade which added to the perfect recipe.
This is an outstanding recipe. We've made it many times, and it always comes out beautifully.
This recipe is memorable because it is insanely GOOD! Why oh why, is "simple" (usually) so bloody good?

We've made this a few times, and we have it with butter lettuce to wrap scoops of meat and veggies.  I've even passed this along to some plant-based friends with adaptations with Japanese eggplant and zucchini in lieu of the kielbasa.  Great quick dish.

We basically love everything Sam Sifton suggests, but this one is a zero for us.

Followed the recipe exactly, and all we could taste was a greasy aftertaste of kielbasa.  The taste dominated our taste buds, and even though the peppers and onions were fine, the star of the meal wasn't.

I know everyone else seems to have loved this, and we were prepared (and wanted) to love it, too, but wow, after 3 or 4 bites, we just threw the rest of it out.

does anyone have any ideas like the "no-recipe" for Kielbasa above that i can use leftover cajun spiced andouille sausage in?  Sam, would this kielbasa recipe work with it?  Or no?  What do you recommend?  I'm making gumbo but had to buy more andouille than I will ever use, and I don't want to waste it.  I am looking for something waaaay simple too.  I'm feeling lazy....

I think it would work. The flavor profile is similar.

Success would all be in the timing, which would depend on how dry your andouille is. The basting step would help even out differences, and allow you to check the sausage and observe if it is getting too fry and starting to burn.

If by leftover you mean already cooked, it probably just needs 5 or 10 minutes to warm up.

If it's getting too hot, cover with foil.

This is a wonderful looking dish. I'm going to try it.

Slice 1/2"rounds,slide on water soaked sewers alternating with sweet peppers,onions sliced into 1/8,white mushrooms,  red tomatoe quarters,yellow squash rounds. Spray with Evo and grill until tender but still firm. Brush French bread with garlic butter and grill slightly. Enjoy w/red wine or beer.

Use the left over andouille in a shrimp and grits recipe: stir-fry onion, green pepper, sausage, shrimp. Remove from pan. Make red-eye gravy in same pan, reduce. Return onion, peppers, sausage, shrimp to gravy pan. Combine. Serve over white hominy grits.
Made this this evening. Simply delicious! Great complex flavors starting sweet, then savory, then salty. I’ll make this again. Looks really pretty too.

Very simple and easy to pull together on a Friday evening; used Maille Old-Style Mustard and I'm embarrassed to say Smucker's sugar-laden preserves. Resonated with my German roots. Hit the spot, but nothing to write home about--very much bachelor food.

Overall, just too heavy and processed and greasy--I found the roasted peppers better than the kielbasa, and a mess to clean up--Smucker's preserves just too cheap, commercial, and sugary alongside a cheap, greasy, commercial kielbasa--this is total supermarket/Walmart food, akin to getting cheap fried chicken from the supermarket. Give me vegetables any day--this was enough to drive me back to vegetables.
Bad sausage is never a good idea. Locally or farm-made charcuterie is often not hard to find. It makes all the difference.
It sounds like you bought an inferior sausage.  I've not had that problem unless I think about needing something cheaper.  Cheap is not good.  It's cheap for a reason.
We put Parchment paper under it, EASY!! cleanup!
Sat in my stomach like a rock--ugh.

This was great. Used yellow and orange pepper with a Vidalia onion, and mixed apricot preserves with stone ground mustard. Cook till the sausage starts to get a little brown and carmelized on the edges. Yum!
I don't see the recipe nor the directions on how to make this. Am I the only one?
It's a no recipe recipe.  The description is the recipe.
It is a “no-recipe” recipe!  Read through it and you will understand how to do it.
You have to click on the word More + then it drops down with them.
Too easy and too good. A keeper!
Here's a dumb question: Does one peel the kielbasa before it cooking for this recipe?

No, the skin is left on and in fact helps to keep the accordion shape of the sausage.  BTW I made this with elderberry preserves from Ikea and with a gochujang sauce called K-Mama out of Minneapolis (you could improvise with other preserves and spicy sauces) to rave reviews. Repeating the menu today upon request!
I prepared this on the grill in the yard when we had people over. Put the sheet pan on the grate while getting the grill to temp and proceeded as prescribed. Big hit as a starter for our picnic.

I loved this meal - super easy and unpretentious ingredients which make it easy to make on short notice. My only comment would be that the basting mixture was thick and coated the kielbasa more like a frosting then a glaze. I added vinegar to help thin it out and I wish I had done that from the beginning. I served with broccoli and garlic bread (all fridge items), but do think it would be lovely over a bed of butter lettuce. Will definitely be making again.
I made it just like the picture; good and easy.  Even picky daughter liked it

Does anyone know the origin of the moniker "hasselback" in cooking? I've made the potatoes and liked them, but wondered what the connection with the name and technique of slicing is? Or did the recipe/technique originate in some place by that name?  Thanks!

I think it’s the same as a Swedish dish named ”Hasselback potatoes@ (tou do the same thing but with potatoes instead od sausages) it comes from the famous restaurant Hasselbacken located on the Island of Djurgården in Stockholm: https://hasselbacken.com/en/restaurant/#
A last minute decision that went very well. I sliced some redskin potatoes and threw them in. A quick finish under the broiler worked great. I will make this again!

I had some kielbasa that I wanted to cook and searched for a recipe, finding this one.  I really riffed on it, base on what I had on hand.  My vegetables were onions, potatoes, and corn on the cob.  My basting mixture was a mixture of some Raspberry honey mustard I found in my pantry and Apricot liqueur to thin it out.  The results were fantastic.  This is a great way to cook kielbasa, regardless of what you mix with it.
This was a nice change from plain kielbasa. I have made hasselback potatoes before and this prep was easy. The preserves over the top just adds to the goodness
I addition to the peppers and onions, we roast baby red, white and purple potatoes. Oh Yum!! Has become a classic favorite now!!
Where, oh where is the list of ingredients???
It's one of Sam's non-recipes which is really fun because you can improvise. And it's all right there, just read. It's a fun way to cook.
Delicious!  But this is Arizona, and I like heat, so next time I will add a jalapeño or two to the veg mix.

Just what the doctor ordered last night. Local purple, yellow and orange peppers, onions and cherry tomatoes added for a just a few minutes at the end.  All shoveled into a toasted sub roll for a very satisfying and easy-to-prepare meal at the end of a long, long day.
I used honey and mustard (allergy to stone fruits) and didn’t have a baguette on hand so served it with farro....easy and delicious weeknight dinner.

Used 1 tbs whole grain mustard and 1 tbs of a smoother,spicy mustard,plus 2 tbs of the apricot preserves. We liked the glaze a lot but next time I might thinly slice and fry the kielbasa first,for more crispy brown goodness. Or just slice it all the way through so the slices will brown on the sheet pan .(losing the millipede,alas). Or slice it before the last 10 minutes of baking (hard to baste slices,so baste before). Also I used standard grocery store kielbasa. Next time would buy better.
was delish!  next time I will roast 1 1/2 peppers per person.  They shrank alot.
We love this recipe, and make it often. We find that the smaller the slices of peppers and onions are, the more melt-in-your-mouth delicious they become.
Fresh or smoked kielbasa?
Can you freeze kielbasa? When you are ready to cook it, how long does it need to defrost?
I bet you can, as long as it's vacuum-sealed, but that's the way mine came and I ended up cooking it well within the "use by" date. I had planned to freeze mine, but it had a very long "use by" date!
I love the idea of this recipe but once I got the image of a pill bug stuck in my head it was all over.  But don't worry, you're still OK with me Sam.
Ignore the rhetoric and make it! It's wonderful!
Made this last night— substituted raspberry jam as I am allergic to apricot. Used peppers, onions, and purple cabbage as the base. Phenomenal!

This may be a very photogenic dish, but the roasting of of an overly sliced kielbasa, simply allows all the juices to drain and evaporate.  Better yet, roast whole, and enjoy the juicy flavor it was designed to deliver.
You may have cooked too long or not basted enough?  (Just guessing).

This was SO good and super easy.  Got some short Polish sausages and glazed with a Dijon and peach preserve mix, with a mix of bell peppers and zucchini.  Next time I'll serve it with rice as well, and maybe glaze the veggies.

Made this last night (4/20/20) with perogi.  My family was very unsure when I told them I was making kielbasa "a different way", but we loved it!  I will probably make it this way from now on!  A perfect blend of sweet and hot.  I used Mister Mustard and apricot preserves (just a cheap store brand of preserves).

This was delicious.  I used turkey kielbasa and added 2 baking potatoes that I peeled and sliced into chunks and about a cup of sauerkraut that I had leftover.  With a green salad, it was a perfect light dinner.  Easy and perfect for quarantine meal planning!
We made this using a ring bologna we had in the freezer. It was really great! We will definitely do it again!
This was stupendous! I doubled the recipe and added potatoes. Everyone loved it!

Made this tonight, using a yellow pepper, a yellow onion (not sweet), and a pork kielbasa. We're sheltering-in-place, so wasn't going to go out for apricot jam, so used what was in the pantry: locally made Republic of Jam's (Carlton, OR) Apple Pie Bourbon (ingredients: apples, sugar, Bull Rum Bourbon, lemon juice, pie spices, pectin) and Grey Poupon mustard. Served with a 3-cheese tortellini, with Parmesan shavings. Delicious and a big hit with my husband. Will definitely make it again!

Used homemade Guinness mustard (whole grain/dijon mustards, stout, minced shallot, apple cider vinegar, turmeric, brown sugar, hint of roasted cinnamon) which paired perfectly with the preserves and thankfully wasn't too sweet. Added a thin cut russet potato to extend the dish. There weren't many leftovers, but the caramelized onion, crispy potato slices, and roasted red pepper that weren't eaten made a welcome addition to a baked asparagus frittata the next evening.
The chopsticks worked perfectly for slicing the kielbasa. Dinner was delicious with the kielbasa and smashed potatoes tossed with grated Parmesan.
Delicious!!!

Loved this. Did it with kielbasa on top of an orange bell pepper and a couple handfuls fresh green beans. And my baste was just straight up maple syrup. Totally would make this again. Wanna try to see if I can get the timing right to make it with potatoes. And I wanna try honey and spicy mustard as a baste too...
Onions and peppers were delicious. Next time I will triple the amount of both, and maybe add some Brussels sprouts to the mix.

This was a home run. I used potatoes, butternut squash, red peppers and onions, and basted with a honey-mustard combo. I probably baked it a little longer than indicated but it’s easy to eyeball for doneness. I found the hasselbacking to be pretty simple - cut through once, no big deal.
Agree this is a keeper. Used turkey kielbasa. No apricot jam handy, used honey with the mustard, some homemade ciabatta ... lovely. I liked the sauerkraut idea a few folks mentioned, next time!

I've made this and it was delicious. Will try with just hot italian turkey sausage and see how it turns out. I did baste it a couple times more, but the apricot/mustard mix is absolutely delicious. Might experiment with a different fruit jam next time.

Great easy dish.  Adding thinly sliced new potatoes made it a one dish meal.  Red onions came out great in this dish.
I shook all veggies and potatoes in a mix of olive oil, paprika oil, Herbes de Provence, salt and pepper and it gave the dish a lovely flavor.
So good. I had fresh kielbasa and this recipe worked great (just needed a little more time in the oven to reach the right temp).
I have made this repeatedly and love it. I add a few shakes of red pepper flakes to the mustard and jam mix to give it a little heat.
This is a wonderful recipe. As a baste, I use a jalapeno pepper jelly. It has become a regular in our household.

What a wonderful, easy winter tray-bake!  The mustard/jam basting sauce really elevates the sausage, as does the slicing technique. Vegetables used: Savoy cabbage, broccoli, red onion, red bell pepper (clearing out my veg bin). This is great, flexible dish.
I made this for my family of 7, and when I went back for more the kielbasa was gone.  The 7-year-old said, sorry mom, but the popular meat is gone.  Goes well with sautéed kale and toasted rolls.
I made this the other night. Came out drier than kielbasa roasted not sliced. I don’t think it was worth the effort
This gets 5 stars for fun and taste. The note about the peppers and onions on baguette was on the money. Great recipe for a crowd, too!

Made this tonight. Added some "cabbage steaks" to the veggies, and put some already boiled mini potatoes in the pan when I put the kielbasa in. It was a great way to use a local kielbasa, and my generally picky kid went back for seconds. I do like the idea of this and a baguette. Will make again. I winged it on the sauce based on pantry stock. Mustard/TJs Asian Sesame sauce/maple syrup. I basted every 4 minutes.
7/2 and above.used honey. Basted 3 times.
Make this as much for the veggies underneath as the kielbasa itself - the fats of the meat blended with the glaze is an exceptional feast.
Made this with red peppers, assorted onions.  No glaze, but put Robert Rothchild’s sesame honey mustard on afterwards - very tasty.
Combine this with the technique in NYT's sheet pan pierogies recipe -- wonderful.
No apricot jam in the fridge so I used a store-bought barbecue sauce to which I added some dark brown sugar.  Served with homemade baguettes.   Delicious!

Second time making this—didn’t have any bell peppers this time so subbed 4 average sized Yukon gold potatoes peeled and cubed 1”, soaked in cold water 15 min then dried and tossed with onions, olive oil, salt & pepper. Put in sheet pan 10 min before adding sausage then cooked 30 minutes (basting 3x). DELICIOUS and the potatoes were perfect. I think this sub will be my go-to!
Cooked it, fabulous! Buy the best kielbasa you can find. I used Holmes Smokehouse Pecan Smoked. Wow, just wow! Didn’t think my wife would eat it… she had seconds.
Instead of adding apricot preserves, I used a honey mustard sauce and it worked perfectly. To aid in slicing the kielbasa, I set two popsicle sticks on either side.

I made this once as suggested and it was *amazing.* This time I didn’t have any peppers, but I did have a zucchini so I made a mix with zucchini and onions. instead. I added some rosemary while they cooked. I changed up the sauce to orange marmalade with a touch of mustard, thyme, a splash of rice wine vinegar, capers and a touch of horseradish. It was wonderful. 
(It’s still best the way it’s written, though.)
I made this with potatoes and onions and keilbasa and then used apricot preserves and honey mustard. Turned out amazing! Even my 3 year old and 11 year old enjoyed it.

This has made it into our weekly rotation for dinners. Super simple and it's easy to add to or subtract from. I usually go for honey mustard and fig preserves for glaze over a bed of peppers and onion, served with brown rice.
I made this primarily because I thought it was funny. Used pepper jelly and mustard for the glaze. It's so good, it's almost funny how good it is!
Totally Devoured!

Too soft, but try on b que. bet would taste great,
!

I have made this a few times and it’s always been delicious.  I have added chopped kale massaged with seasoned olive oil once. I added diced potatoes another time. It  has been great every time. 
It’s definitely an easy no recipe recipe.😊
Use apples and Brussels sprouts
This was a positively scrumptious, easy midweek meal.  Served it atop miso mashed sweet potatoes(not yams!).  Thank you, Sam Sifton, for another no-recipe wonder!!

This is one of my easy go to recipes to feed the men in my family. I accidentally grabbed the orange marmalade at the store last night instead of the apricot jam (store brand, similar packaging). Used the marmalade and which turned out fine.

Finally got around to making this, and it was absolutely delicious. And it was easy. Don't pass on the glaze, I used greengage plum jam with mustard and a splash of rice vinegar to thin it. I didn’t have apricot. It worked.

It won’t be a one time wonder.
Made this for a potluck using 2 kielbasa. Easy and delicious.

After using this preparation for the first time, my wife and two daughters haven't let up about when the next time will be. Not long, me thinks, and there will be two Kielbasa on that tray. Next time I'll let the Kielbasa roast 10-15 minutes before applying the prescribed glaze, in order to let the skin and edges develop some crackle. And that glaze, uh, yeeesssssss. I used a fig preserve, which was delicious, and will use 2/3 mustard to 1/3 preserves next time, as it was a touch too sweet.

I think it looks lovely and I must say that it is the recipe that convinced  me buy a subscription to NYT Cooking.  It is  the recipe I pass along most often and has made me a Sifton fan for life because most of us are just trying to throw a meal on the table that tastes wonderful in a hurry.  Tonight I was trying to come up with something that qualified as baseball food...and then I remembered this (My state made the NCLS)-I plan to serve it with French Potato Salad.  YUM!
I’m thinking Shishito peppers and onion are the perfect tray mate for the kielbasa.
I’ve made it a million times. I change the vegetables, there’s usually potatoes. It’s so forgiving, the leftovers are great — it’s excellent.

I'm getting a little bolder in my cooking. I made this tonight, added a jalapeno - with seeds, a couple of garlic cloves and used orange marmalade with Dijon to make the basing sauce. I had a few slices of a crusty loaf of homemade bread as the simple accompaniment. My husband said to add it to my "do it again".
This was really good! Entire family liked it. Super easy and quick to make and clean up was a breeze.

This also works well without the peppers and onions - just cooking the Kielbasa on a sheet pan and basting it with the mustard/apricot preserves for 25 minutes. I used turkey kielbasa and served it with broccoli Mac-n-cheese. Quick comfort food!
So easy and absolutely delicious...sadly, no leftovers!!

How long should it take to cut up the sausage?! The recipe sez to let the vegetable roast while I do this step but if it’s my first time using the recipe, I don’t want them the vegetables to roast for an unspecified amount of time…

Awesome.  Some of the fat from the Kielbasa leaked out to the peppers and onions on the sheet pan bringing some of the jam/mustard with it; really enhanced the peppers and onions.  Next time 3 full peppers and one onion.  I didn't know what kind of mustard to use, so I used a 50/50 blend of yellow and Dijon mustards (because that's what was in the fridge), but I saw a suggestion to use Guilden's and I think that would be perfect.

Surprisingly stunning result - I was not expecting this level of flavor and deliciousness. I cooked it in the air fryer - 400 degrees for 10-12 mins. No need to fire off the stove in the heat of the summer   quick and easy meal = win-win!

Good and easy. I would cook the onions and peppers longer than the time it takes to slice the kielbasa so they have a better chance to caramelize. Might just make in skillet next time (so oven doesn't heat up the house). I used coarse Dijon for the mustard.
I assume this is intended for smoked/ready to eat kielbasa? Paisan’s in Brooklyn sells fresh kielbasa. Any idea what would need to change to use this technique on that product?

I did this with Turkey Kielbasa and smoked sausage.  Used orange marmalade and spicy mustard.  No onion, several different fresh picked garden peppers sweet and mildly spicy.  In a big cast iron on the grill.  Served with Brioche hot dog buns.  Fabulous!
Love this recipe.  It's a regular in our rotation.

We triple the amount of veggies, adding potatoes, Brussels sprouts, and whatever the choices at the farmer’s market suggests. Tonight we're adding in celery root! We use Schmalz brand kielbasa, and it's at least a monthly dinner in our rotation. So, so good.
Added potatoes and cabbage

13/10 - So easy. Very delicious. And a REAL quick meal. Some of those other “quick” meals are like 45 min of prep before 20 min of cooking. I keep Kielbasa on hand now. I was always wondering what I should do with it before. I used used honey and whatever mustard I grabbed.
didn't have apricot preserves, so I did apple butter   ACV   spicy brown mustard and it was incredible. also didn't have bell peppers, so I used carrots, so good!

Of course I messed with the recipe. Since using  stoneware pan, I did not preheat it. Cooked veg with included sliced potatoes and yellow squash for about 15 minutes before stirring and adding kielbasa. Then followed recipe and cooked for another 20 minutes with sauce made from a sweet Thai chili sauce/dijon. Excellent results!

I wanted to love this but didn't. Maybe because I grew up w/saurkraut as my main kielbasa accompaniment? Loved the peppers and onion in the marinade, just agree with the person who said the kielbasa flavor was overwhelming and it was a bit too sweet.
This is a keeper. We often make it after a sauna and eat it next to the fire with some good wine. Thanks Sam!
A few minutes at 450 at the end does not hurt but you have to watch it.
Love this recipe! I use GF Teriyaki instead of jam. It is great with onions. peppers and pineapple. Delish for all who have tried it!
